MeaningHint: To remember the meaning of <kanji>One</kanji>, imagine yourself there at the scene of the crime. You grab <kanji>One</kanji> in your arms, trying to prop it up, trying to hear its last words. Instead, it just splatters some blood on your face. "Who did this to you?" you ask. The number One points weakly, and you see number Two running off into an alleyway. He's always been jealous of number One and knows he can be number one now that he's taken the real number one out.
ReadingHint: Make sure you feel the ridiculously <reading>itchy</reading> sensation covering your body. It climbs from your hands, where you're holding the number <kanji>One</kanji> up, and then goes through your arms, crawls up your neck, goes down your body, and then covers everything. It becomes uncontrollable, and you're scratching everywhere, writhing on the ground. It's so itchy that it's the most painful thing you've ever experienced (you should imagine this vividly, so you remember the reading of this kanji).
ReadingMnemonic: As you're sitting there next to <kanji>One</kanji>, holding him up, you start feeling a weird sensation all over your skin. From the wound comes a fine powder (obviously coming from the special bullet used to kill One) that causes the person it touches to get extremely <reading>itchy</reading> (いち).
MeaningMnemonic: Lying on the <radical>ground</radical> is something that looks just like the ground, the number <kanji>One</kanji>. Why is this One lying down? It's been shot by the number two. It's lying there, bleeding out and dying. The number One doesn't have long to live.

MeaningHint: More simply put, you can just know that this kanji is the double of one. It's two lines, that's not all that hard to remember!
ReadingHint: Imagine asking to borrow the two knees of a stranger in the street, just so you can count to two. They're confused at first, but then they present each knee to you in a loving sort of way. Yep, that's two knees alright.
ReadingMnemonic: How do you count to <vocabulary>two</vocabulary>? Just use someone's <reading>knee</reading> (に), and then their other knee. There — two knees!
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji <kanji>two</kanji> and the radical <radical>two</radical> are the exact same things. Therefore, you know the kanji for two already!

MeaningHint: If you know your radicals well, this kanji will be a breeze!
ReadingHint: Picture nine-year-old you, lining up nine cookies and nine cucumbers. You dip a cookie in milk and bite into it, then dip a cucumber in and take a bite. Nine bites each until they're all gone. Ah, you miss being nine.
ReadingMnemonic:
Although this kanji has two on'yomi to learn, the pronunciations of those two on'yomi are very similar, so that should make them easier to remember.

When you were <kanji>nine</kanji> your favorite foods were <reading>coo</reading>kies (く) and <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう). Yum yum!
MeaningMnemonic: Nice! This kanji is the same as the radical that looks just like it (down to the meaning, even!). The radical is <radical>nine</radical> and the kanji is <kanji>nine</kanji>, making the meaning of this kanji really easy to remember as long as you know the radical first.

MeaningHint: Make sure you know your radicals well and you'll be A-Okay on this one!
ReadingHint: How unbelievable is it that someone got <kanji>seven</kanji> percent on a test, especially if you try to cheat? Imagine the girl you loathe the most from school, and imagine her cheating on the test (it's important it's a girl, because of the "she" part in <reading>she chea</reading>ted (しち). Then, imagine your happiness when she's super upset about getting seven percent on it. Serves her right, obviously.
ReadingMnemonic:
You know this kanji means <kanji>seven</kanji>, so let's use that to remember the reading too. <kanji>Seven</kanji> is a lucky number, usually, but this time it isn't so good: 

<kanji>Seven</kanji> percent is all she got on her test after <reading>she chea</reading>ted (しち) on it.
MeaningMnemonic: Lucky for you (as is the case with many of the lower-stroke kanji), this kanji is the same as the radical you've already learned. The radical that makes up this kanji is <radical>seven</radical> and the kanji (which is exactly the same) is also <kanji>seven</kanji>. So, if you know the radical, you know the meaning of the kanji as well.

MeaningHint: Know the radicals, and you shall know the kanji.
ReadingHint: Imagine how angry you were about the policy. You'll have to come up with something to be angry about yourself (pick something that would actually make you angry, like maybe you're only allowed to wear cut off short-shorts jeans at Nintendo now), but if you pick something good, you'll definitely remember the two readings for this. Just remember you're working at Nintendo, and the policy has to be something about jeans. Then, imagine yourself as the only person complaining and everyone else in their dumb jeans (or lack-thereof) shaking their head at you because you're being a fool.
ReadingMnemonic: Only one <kanji>person</kanji> at <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん) complained about the new <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) policy, and that person was you.
MeaningMnemonic: Remember how the radical version of this kanji meant <radical>person</radical> because it looks like a person with their arms down and their legs spread out? Well this is a <kanji>person</kanji> too!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine lights turning on and flashing, and an entire audience inside. You're at a game show, and the announcer announces the neeeeew tent in his announcey voice. Feel the surprise. Feel the happiness. Feel the excitement. A beautiful new tent all for you!
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out, after you <kanji>enter</kanji> the tent (remember how the person in the radical is entering one?), you're given a <reading>new</reading> (にゅう) one! Why? No idea, but hey — it's new!
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>enter</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>enter</kanji> as well.

MeaningHint: Count the fins out in front of you, all the way from one to eight. It'll help if you actually do this. Now smell each fin, and think "Wow, I have to smell all eight of these?" Yes, you do.
ReadingHint: You've asked him why you can only put eight fins at a time in the hatch, but he never wants to answer. What could be down in the hole that the hatch covers? Why is there a hatch at all? What's with numbers and hatches being mysterious?
ReadingMnemonic: After you count and smell all <kanji>eight</kanji> of your fins, it's time to put them away. You open a large <reading>hatch</reading> (はち) and put them in. This is where you keep your fins, always putting in eight at a time because that's what the boss wants.
MeaningMnemonic: This isn't exactly the same as the <radical>fins</radical> radical, but it's close. Sometimes this will happen (where we have to use a slightly "modified radical" version of the original), so it's good to understand that now, early on. In this case, we have fins. How many fins? <kanji>Eight</kanji> fins. How do you know that? Because you caught four fish with two fins each.

MeaningHint: If you know the radical, you should be feeling fine… No, you should feel powerful!
ReadingHint: Know anyone named <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき), even a famous person like Ricky Ricardo or Ricky Gervais? Imagine that person's face on the monster the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers are fighting. Now, imagine the Power Rangers <reading>lock</reading>ing (りょく) him up. He looks much less scary once he's locked up. Phew.
ReadingMnemonic: Who has the most power of anyone? It's the <kanji>Power</kanji> Rangers. They are battling their arch enemy <reading>Ricky</reading> (りき). They defeat him and <reading>lock</reading> (りょく) him up, so that he can't hurt anyone ever again.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are the same. Both are <kanji>power</kanji>.

MeaningHint: If you're familiar with Christianity, they have a cross and ten commandments. Cross = ten, you see?
ReadingHint: Picture the jewels on this cross. Maybe draw it out on a piece of paper so the visual really sticks. Each cross is really a symbol for different sets of ten things, and in this case, it's ten jewels.
ReadingMnemonic: This cross in particular has <kanji>ten</kanji> <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう) on it. Two on each arm and two in the middle. That's ten jewels!
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>cross</radical> is the symbol for the number <kanji>ten</kanji>. A good way to remember this is to think about all the religions that have crosses and also important sets of tens.

MeaningHint: This one should be pretty self explanatory! Even easier, you can look at this kanji and see it has three straight lines. You know what one looks like. You know what two looks like. So... this is three!
ReadingHint: Picture Santa-san sitting at his desk at the North Pole, eating cookies, and marking one, two, then finally three lines next to names. Once the third line appears, the kid doesn't get presents anymore.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Three</kanji> is the number of chances you get with <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん). He has a list, and when you get to three "naughty things" lines, that's it. You're on Santa-san's three strikes and you're out list.
MeaningMnemonic: This kanji is made up of the ground radical (which also happens to be the kanji for "one") and the radical for two. Combine <radical>one</radical> and <radical>two</radical> together and what do you get? <kanji>Three</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Focus your imagination on how body parts should be buried, not above ground like this. Sure, finding a toe is a little strange, but finding a toe above the ground? Downright silly.
ReadingHint:
Try to remember Joe, the local farmhand. He is a recurring character that will come up anytime the reading for a kanji is じょう.

He is a very big man, and he wears farmer's clothes. It's important to imagine your version of Joe as very big, because it will help us to differentiate him from Jo-Anne, who is the character associated with the similar, but shorter reading (and character) じょ. Although Joe will develop as you go through all the じょう reading kanji, for now, just imagine him as a big, kinda slow dude, who works for a local farmer. Also, he only has nine or fewer toes, apparently.
ReadingMnemonic: Of course when you find a toe <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, you want to know where the toe came from. When you examine it, you see a name written on there. This toe belongs to the local clumsy farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう).
MeaningMnemonic: You find a <radical>toe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. It's weird, because it's <kanji>above</kanji> the ground, not where toes belong.

MeaningHint: Where's that toe again? Below the ground. Maybe it's your toe. Imagine digging it out and thinking, Ugh, why does it have to be under the ground like this? So irritating. Will I be able to sew it back on in time to save it?
ReadingHint: There's another useful on'yomi reading that just isn't useful enough for you to learn now. That reading is げ, but we're only going to worry about that much, much later when you have to learn one or two vocab words that read 下 as げ. That's the problem with really common kanji — too many readings.
ReadingMnemonic: You keep digging in the ground for the toe <kanji>below</kanji>, but instead you hit something hard and metallic. You try to dig around it, but it's huge. Turns out it's a <reading>car</reading> (か). Keep digging until you unearth the car below. (Inside, you find the toe you were looking for.)
MeaningMnemonic: There's the <radical>ground</radical>, and there's a <radical>toe</radical> just <kanji>below</kanji> it. Yep, you know this means "below" because the ground has a toe below it in this kanji.

MeaningHint: If you learned your radicals — which the WaniKani system forces you to do (unless you're cheating) — you'll already know the meaning of the kanji.
ReadingHint: You just can't forget all the cool things about kanji learning he said from that mouth. What a great mouth こういち has. How are the lips of his mouth both so skinny and fat at the same time?
ReadingMnemonic:
Do you remember seeing the construction worker こういち earlier in the 工 mnemonic? If you haven't yet, not to worry!

For kanji with こう readings (there are a lot of them!) we will use the character こういち every time. He's one of the people who started this website, so we'll do our best in the mnemonics to embarrass him as much as possible. Anyways, remember that whenever you see こう, think こういち.

Everyone agrees, the <kanji>mouth</kanji> is <reading>こう</reading>いち's best feature. You stare at it lovingly as he whispers sweet kanji readings to you.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> radical and the <kanji>mouth</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: So, if you know the radical, you know the meaning of this kanji as well.
ReadingHint: When you look at the big guy in your head, imagine him covered in tie dye head to toe. Think about how ridiculous it looks on him, especially since he's such a big guy. Shirt. Sweatpants. Hat. Shoes... it goes on and on.
ReadingMnemonic: There's this big guy (that's how you remember the radical, remember?), and he's wearing a huge <reading>Tie Dye</reading> (たい, だい) shirt. Because he's so huge, the tie dye shirt is also super huge. Everything about him is pretty big, but the tie dye stands out more than anything.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>big</radical> radical and the <kanji>big</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji as well! How swell.
ReadingHint: Whenever the reading じょ comes up, we will use the character Jo-Anne. She's quite a short woman, which is to help us differentiate じょ from じょう (who is the giant, oafish farmhand Joe). じょ is a smaller lady, じょう is a giant man.
ReadingMnemonic: When you think of a <kanji>woman</kanji>, think of <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ), though she usually goes by just "Jo."
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>woman</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>woman</kanji> are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. If you are, then you're studying your kanji as well!
ReadingHint: Be sure to imagine this vividly. The more absurdly you can imagine it, the more likely you are to remember the reading, <reading>san</reading>.
ReadingMnemonic: Think about <kanji>mountains</kanji> talking to each other, calling each other by their names and adding the Japanese name-ender <reading>san</reading> (さん) to each of their names. "Hello, Everest-<reading>san</reading>." "Oh hi, Fuji-<reading>san</reading>."
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mountain</radical> radical and the <kanji>mountain</kanji> kanji are the same.

MeaningHint: So, if you know the radical you know the kanji as well.
ReadingHint: Imagine standing by the river, watching all those cars floating by. They start off really dirty, but the river washes them all clean.
ReadingMnemonic: You're standing next to a <kanji>river</kanji> that's actually used as a <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>wa</reading>sh (かわ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>river</radical> radical and the <kanji>river</kanji> kanji are the same as each other!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals and you'll know this kanji's meaning.
ReadingHint:
If こういち as a construction worker somehow doesn't make your legs tremble, that's okay. Just make sure you take this story to a ridiculous enough level to construct a memory in that brain of yours. Be mad at him for not following construction site regulations — where's his shirt, shoes, and pants, and why is he using the bulldozer like that?!

Alternatively, say you really like what you see (you're going to have to imagine a much better-looking version of こういち's face, though). You and こういち make eye contact a little too long. He nods, puts his cigarette out, and takes off his hard hat, letting his long Fabio-esque locks flow. You beckon him over and he comes. What happens next is up to you and your own imagination. My guess is you have a very deep conversation about kanji and the best ways to learn it.

Anyway, when you see 工 in a kanji, it will often take the こう reading. Make sure to remember that, because it comes up quite a bit!
ReadingMnemonic:
Every time a kanji uses the こう reading (there are a lot!), we'll use the character こういち. He's one of the people who started this website, so we'll do our best in the mnemonics to embarrass him as much as possible. Like this:

You see some <kanji>construction</kanji> in front of you. There, standing shirtless (only suspenders and a hard hat) is <reading>こう</reading>いち, looking back at you.
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>construction</radical> radical and the <kanji>construction</kanji> kanji are the same!

This kanji also means <kanji>industry</kanji>, which is what construction is, if you think about it. It's all part of the construction industry.

MeaningHint: This kanji also looks a lot like the kanji 力. To avoid getting them confused, remember that this one is missing the little nubbin at the top, because 90% of the power comes from the sword to cut your enemies down (which is why the sword kanji is 90% of the power kanji).
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to travel to とうきょう with your katana. Airport security is not happy with you trying to take a katana on the plane, so you get a full cavity search. I hope you don't have any other swords hidden anywhere.
ReadingMnemonic: So now you have this sweet <kanji>katana</kanji> in your hands and you're swinging it around like an idiot. Feel free to make whooshing noises as you swing it around. To become a master katana wielder, you decide to travel to <reading>とう</reading>きょう (Tokyo). とうきょう has the best sword and katana schools in the world, so it makes sense to travel to とうきょう.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical and the meaning of this kanji are almost the same! The radical is <radical>sword</radical>, but we're learning Japanese, so can you name a Japanese <kanji>sword</kanji>? That's right, the most common is a <kanji>katana</kanji>, which happens to be the meaning of this kanji as well.

MeaningHint: As long as you know the radical dirt, you know the kanji dirt.
ReadingHint: Dogs and dirt just go together!
ReadingMnemonic: Who spends a lot of time in <kanji>dirt</kanji>? <reading>Do</reading>gs (ど) do! Dogs dig in dirt, they roll in dirt, sometimes they try to eat dirt (which isn't so great).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dirt</radical> radical and the <kanji>dirt</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: As long as you know the "thousand" radical, you'll know this kanji as well!
ReadingHint: Problem is, as they all tumble down the slide the change falls out of their pockets, so there's a thousand times a thousand cents scattered all over on the floor under the slide.
ReadingMnemonic: Remember the thousand people going down the slide on a cross in the radical 千? Turns out each one of them was carrying a <kanji>thousand</kanji> <reading>cen</reading>ts (せん). Go ahead and try to calculate what a thousand times a thousand cents would come out to. It's quite a lot, actually.
MeaningMnemonic: This kanji is the same as the radical you learned, so it means <kanji>thousand</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Do what you're meant to do. Study those radicals!
ReadingHint:
Young people love staying up the entire evening doing youth-people things, and it's really obnoxious for us curmudgeonly folk.

Note: All the ゆう readings will use the word "youths" as a keyword for the mnemonic, so bear that in mind for any future ゆう reading you'll encounter.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>evening</kanji> is when all the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) come outside to play.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>evening</radical> radical and the <kanji>evening</kanji> kanji are the same. If you know the radical you know the meaning of the kanji.

MeaningHint: So, if you know your radicals, you know your kanji as well! This won't last forever (where radicals are exactly the same as the kanji) so enjoy it while the kanji are still small and simple!
ReadingHint: The child (sheep) runs away and you chase after it. Every time it's about to turn a corner it looks like a sheep again, but whenever you yell and point, it changes back into a child. You're the only one who can see this sheep child!
ReadingMnemonic:
The main reading here is し, so to remember it we'll use this mnemonic:

There is a <kanji>child</kanji> standing in front of you. You look closely at the child but something seems wrong. You rub your eyes and when you open them again, there is a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in front of you. A sheep?! You yelp, blink again, and it's back to being a child. Are… are all children really just sheep?
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>child</radical> radical and the <kanji>child</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Knowing radicals sure is nice, huh?
ReadingHint: You read that right, shougun is written with a long う sound. Make sure you spell it right from now on!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>small</kanji> kanji would be the perfect gift to give to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). The Shougun loves little things, and the <kanji>little</kanji> kanji represents all of that.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>small</radical> radical and the <kanji>small</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: As long as you know the radical street, you know the kanji street.
ReadingHint: Mrs. Chou is a character you'll see a lot throughout WaniKani, anytime the reading is ちょう like this one. A lore will develop around her, so the more vividly you can imagine your Mrs. Chou right now, the easier things will get later. In general, she's a mean, evil woman, so as long as you imagine her within those parameters, you're good. And, when I say she is a "street-woman" in the mnemonic, I don't mean she is homeless. She has a nice, middle-class home that's not outside of her financial means. What I'm saying is she wanders the streets looking for evil things to do. These are HER streets. So whenever you see the street radical, know that Mrs. Chou is probably going to show up!
ReadingMnemonic: So you're walking down this <kanji>street</kanji>, and off in the distance you see the silhouette of somebody. A lady. Big, black curly hair and red eyes. It is the street-woman, Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical that look like this are exactly the same. They are both <kanji>street</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It's not quite a <kanji>circle</kanji> though. You just need to add a <radical>drop</radical> of liquid to the edges to smooth it out to become a full circle.
ReadingHint: Obviously if you "don't know this reading" well enough, you should watch some more Maru videos. I'll understand... just make sure you come back someday, okay?
ReadingMnemonic: Do you know about the adorable cat Maru? If not, you should check it out. May seem like a distraction, but if you come back knowing the reading for this kanji, then it's totally worthwhile. When you think about a circle, think about Maru all rolled up in a little circle, being super adorable. Don't tell me it doesn't melt your heart just thinking about it. Alternatively, you could also look at this kanji and think of Maru inside of a box, with his little tail sticking out of it. Whatever works best for you, just get yourself to "Maru."
MeaningMnemonic: What's something with <radical>nine</radical> sides? Just add a <radical>drop</radical> and you'll have something that's almost a <kanji>circle</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself placing the barbs in front of the slide, thinking about how talented you are. It took years of training to perfect this talent. But it was worth it. Everyone on the playground respects you now.
ReadingHint: Cyborgs have brain implants and cool bionic limbs, so they're basically the most talented people around.
ReadingMnemonic: But then people start to say that no human could have so much <kanji>talent</kanji>, that you must be a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). Are you really a talented cyborg?
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>ground</radical> you put <radical>barb</radical>s at the bottom of a <radical>slide</radical>. This is your special <kanji>talent</kanji>. When you go down the slide, you fly right past the barbs like some kind of daredevil. Any normal kid would land on the barbs and get hurt, but you have this talent.

MeaningHint: Make sure you're studying your radicals, otherwise you might find yourself in the middle of a kanji-learning problem.
ReadingHint: Imagine the fear and shock you'd feel after finding out it was Chewbacca who shot the middle of your mouth (and head). Touch his fur. Look really close at his weapon. Give him a sniff, too. Just so that memory never fades away.
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading for this kanji, we use the word Chewbacca to pull up ちゅう in our memory.

If you remember back to the radical 中, a stick was stuck in the middle of the mouth of something. Let's say it was your mouth, and when you look up to see who did it, there stands <reading>Chew</reading>bacca. He's even doing his Chewbacca yell. And it isn't a stick in your mouth, it's an arrow from his bowcaster. It just so happens Chewbacca's bowcaster looks just like this kanji too. Go figure.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <reading>middle</reading> and the kanji <kanji>middle</kanji> are both the same. So if you know one, you know the other.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, friend-o.
ReadingHint: One, two, three, four, five—GODZILLA IS HERE GO GO, RUN!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Five</kanji> is the number of buildings <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) will knock down. Oh, look! There he goes now.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>five</radical> radical and the <kanji>five</kanji> kanji are the same.

MeaningHint: Imagine this beast doing various crazy things, the crazier the better (to help you remember). Make sure all these things are associated with the devil and the devil's number (<kanji>six</kanji>), so you can remember the meaning of this kanji.
ReadingHint: Sing along with the monster, if it helps you to remember the reading of this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic:
Whenever we need to remember the reading ろく we just use the word <reading>rock</reading>. 

So, think about the devil and the devil's number. What is often associated with those things? Why, <reading>rock</reading> (ろく) and roll is. Imagine the lid-head fin-legged beast suddenly blasting out rock music in the middle of his destructive mayhem.
MeaningMnemonic: Just imagine a beast with a <radical>lid</radical> for a head and <radical>fins</radical> for feet, running around looking as insane as possible (really imagine it!). When it runs up to you, it shows you the top of its lid-head. On it is written the numbers 666. Why? Because it's a spawn of the devil, and the number <kanji>six</kanji> is the devil's number.

MeaningHint: This kanji can mean both "yen" and "round"! It's OK if you can't remember both meanings. But it might help to think about how round the yen coins are inside this person's head, which can also be pretty round…
ReadingHint: Picture these round えん in your mind. Maybe look some up online too, just to be safe. See how round those yen (えん) are?
ReadingMnemonic: What does the word <kanji>yen</kanji> sound like? It sounds like <reading>えん</reading>. Why? because back in the day, Japanese used to have a "ye" sound, but now it doesn't. Instead, it's an "e" sound, which is why yen = えん!
MeaningMnemonic: Here we have a <radical>head</radical> with a <radical>lid</radical> in it, near the top. Imagine the top of someone's head being closed by a lid. Open that lid up and peer inside... it's full of <kanji>yen</kanji> coins, the currency of Japan. Jackpot! And all those yen coins have a nice <kanji>round</kanji> shape, naturally.

MeaningHint: If you know your radicals well (and you should!) you know the meaning of this kanji well, as well!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself in line to get into heaven. You're super worried, because you're not sure if you'll get in. You heard there was a one in <reading>ten</reading> chance... those aren't good odds. Feel nervous in your imagination. Feel worried. Feel the pressure as you get closer and closer to the gate... will you make it?? Ten percent!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Heaven</kanji>... nowadays, people aren't all that good. Things have gotten bad. That's why the acceptance rate to get into heaven is only <reading>ten</reading> (てん) percent!
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>heaven</radical> is the same as the kanji <kanji>heaven</kanji>, making this an easy kanji to memorize.

MeaningHint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. It won't be forever that radicals and kanji overlap so nicely like this!
ReadingHint: You try to shake your hands off, but it's no good. The televisions are stuck, and the volume won't go down. You have no idea how you got these televisions on your hands. All you know is that you want them off right this instant.
ReadingMnemonic: To remember the reading て we will use the word "television." You have great hands. Go ahead and look at them. OH MY GOD. You have little <reading>te</reading>levisions (て) on your hands. They are playing the dumbest show you can think of, too, both hands at the same time.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>hand</radical> radical and the <kanji>hand</kanji> kanji are identical in looks and meaning! That should make things easy!

MeaningHint: If you can't remember the radical for writing, maybe you could try… writing it down?
ReadingHint: Imagine writing a big X (or even a big 文) on all your buns, then showing them to the bun monster. Watch as he turns and goes at the sight of the writing, all disappointed. Not today, bun monster.
ReadingMnemonic:
This kanji has more than one important reading you need to learn! But don't worry, here's a mnemonic to help you:

You always put <kanji>writing</kanji> on your <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん) to keep them from getting eaten by the bun <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん). What kind of writing? Usually it's just an X to show the monster these buns are off-limits. (In fact, it kind of looks like the X part in 文. Hmm…)
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>writing</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>writing</kanji> as well.

MeaningHint: The radical for this one is easy too, making the meaning for this kanji super easy as well!
ReadingHint:
This reading will be easier to remember if you know of Friedrich Nietzsche... so do your research and learn about him if you don't. He's a pretty historical dude. Imagine the sun totally pissed off, too. It makes a grunting noise as it builds up a sun beam to blast him off the face of the earth.

Also note that there's another reading worth learning as well: じつ. The reading can be remembered by thinking "<reading>Gee! Two</reading> readings?" Because, seriously, two readings? Too many...
ReadingMnemonic:
There are so many on'yomi for this kanji, but since this kanji is so important and common, you'll end up learning them all eventually whether you want to or not. For now, you're only going to have to learn one of the readings, and that one is にち. 

The <kanji>sun</kanji> is so hot. It focused its rays to take out one man... and that man is Friedrich <reading>Nietzsche</reading> (にち), famous philosopher. He philosophized one too many things about the sun, so the sun decided it was time he paid.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> radical and <kanji>sun</kanji> kanji are the same, huzzah! Also, if you want to remember <kanji>day</kanji> as well, you can just think about how a day is the time it takes for the sun to cross the sky.

MeaningHint: This should be EZPZ as long as you know the radicals already!
ReadingHint:
Use the げつ reading when this kanji means "moon", in words like "moonlight". Use げつ for counting months, and saying things like "last month and "next month", too. Go for がつ in the names of months, like "January".

Don't worry too much about memorizing this pattern, though. You'll be getting lots of practice soon!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>moon</kanji> really <reading>gets</reading> (げつ) to your <reading>guts</reading> (がつ). Staring at the moon for too long will give you terrible indigestion, so be careful!
MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>moon</kanji> kanji is made up of the <radical>moon</radical> radical. This kanji is also used to mean <kanji>month</kanji>.

MeaningHint: So, how are those radicals doing? Do you know them well?
ReadingHint: Feel the shame, feel the irritation. Make yourself want to run away from those dumb trees that are mocking you.
ReadingMnemonic: You’re walking through the trees, looking at them. They look ominous. Suddenly, you trip and fall. The trees start to <reading>mock</reading> (もく) you. Even the mocking birds mock you. It’s terrible. It’s painful. You’re being mocked as you pull yourself up off the ground.
MeaningMnemonic: This is another kanji that is the same as the radical. Both <radical>tree</radical> and <kanji>tree</kanji> are the same in looks and meaning.

MeaningHint: So as long as you know the radicals, you know the kanji.
ReadingHint: I know that was a long one, but try to imagine all the different bits. Try to feel the feelings (cold, smell, etc) too. The more you create in this fake memory (which you’re using to remember and associate water with すい (swing)) the more likely you are to end up remembering enough to make it happen. Especially when it comes to the senses part, the more vividly you imagined it, the more likely you are to remember the on’yomi.
ReadingMnemonic: Imagine a lake (i.e. a whole bunch of <kanji>water</kanji>). When you think of water, you should try to think of this beautiful lake. It’s sunny, the trees smell nice (imagine a tree smell if you can – the more senses you use the better!) and on that tree there is an awesome tire <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい). You get onto the tire swing and you are swinging back and forth. The water is right in front of you, and you are able to use the swing to throw yourself into the water. It’s a lot colder than you expected, and you come back to the surface, gasping for breath. The water gets into your mouth and you taste it. How strange, it’s very, very <reading>swee</reading>t. Imagine the sweetness of the water and how weird it is (really really sweet, this water is).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>water</radical> radical and the <kanji>water</kanji> kanji are the same.

MeaningHint: If you know the radicals then this one is EZPZ.
ReadingHint: Oh no... Oh no!! If you don't have a <reading>car</reading>, imagine what it'd feel like if your car just exploded and burned down. If you do have a car, imagine your own car in this situation. It should be a terrible feeling, if you imagine it well enough. So, when you think <kanji>fire</kanji>, remember the fear you had when your car was given to the flames. Now you have to walk everywhere, sad :(
ReadingMnemonic:
Whenever we need to remember か, we use the word <reading>car</reading>.

For this kanji, when we think <kanji>fire</kanji>, we ought to think of something on fire. What's on fire? It's obviously your very own <reading>car</reading> (か)! Oh Goddd NOOO!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>fire</radical> radical and the <kanji>fire</kanji> kanji are the same as each other.

MeaningHint: Soon there will be very few kanji/radical-same combos ... so enjoy it while you can.
ReadingHint: P.S. Don't actually let your dog eat noodles, at least not with marinara sauce. Disney didn't show you how that Lady and Tramp date probably ended — unromantically, with indigestion.
ReadingMnemonic: Every <kanji>dog</kanji> likes to <reading>ea</reading>t <reading>noo</reading>dles (いぬ). Like that movie where the dogs share a noodle and end up kissing, right? So when you see 犬, just imagine how those two eat noodles.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dog</radical> radical and <kanji>dog</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: Hopefully you're a king of radicals... because if you are, this kanji was super easy for you!
ReadingHint:
You need to be polite when you talk to the king and add "Oh" to the beginning, otherwise the king will be pretty angry and bad things might happen.

We're going to use the 王 kanji for all おう mnemonics from now on. So make sure you always remember to say おう King!
ReadingMnemonic: When you see the <kanji>king</kanji> you need to say, "<reading>Oh</reading> (おう) King! Oh King, oh no, oh why are you looking at me like that? Oh King... you aren't going to chop off my head are you? OH NO!"
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> radical and the <kanji>king</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: In your imagination, really feel like your life depends on getting out. The important part is the feeling of "needing to get out of the mountain pass." If you have this feeling, you'll be more likely to remember that this kanji means exit, because that's what you want to do when you see this kanji.
ReadingHint: Focus on the surprise of realizing you're being hunted while trying to find the exit to the mountain range, then focus on the next part, where you get shot. View it in your head as the hunter shooting you. Powww!
ReadingMnemonic: As you're trying to <kanji>exit</kanji> this mountain range, you suddenly realize that someone is actually hunting you. What? Make sure to feel that sudden realization. It's crazy. Then, you find the exit and run towards it. Sadly, the hunter is waiting there, and he <reading>shoots you</reading> (しゅつ) through the leg, knocking you down.
MeaningMnemonic: There are two <radical>mountains</radical> in front of you... and probably a bunch more around you too. You are in a pass, and don't know how to get out. You are frantically looking for the <kanji>exit</kanji> before you freeze to death. The most important thing is to find the exit from this mountain range - your life depends on it.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at a narwhal from the front. No mouth. Now look at it from the right. Mouth!
ReadingHint:
Imagine a bunch of youths walking in a group and looking at everything from the right. You remember when you were a youth and you used to look at everything from one side too.

Note: All the ゆう readings will use the word "youths" for the reading mnemonic. You can think of these "youths" as a group of younger people who cause a lot of the problems in this world.
ReadingMnemonic: How did we know to look from the <kanji>right</kanji>, though? Because the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) figured it out. Youths think in ways that older people usually don't. That's why they can see the swirls on cereal, hear the music behind noise, and of course, look at things from the right side.
MeaningMnemonic:
<radical>Narwhal</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>s are on the <kanji>right</kanji> side of their head. In fact, some people didn't think they had mouths at all because they weren't looking from the right. Rookie mistake.

See how the "mouth" radical is to the right of the "narwhal" radical? That should help you remember that this means <kanji>right</kanji>.

MeaningHint: If you can see this kanji as a kind of square, you should be able to remember it means "four" as well, since squares always have four sides.
ReadingHint: What's better than a four-sided sheep?
ReadingMnemonic: Now that you have <kanji>four</kanji> sides, you look like a four-sided animal: the <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Look at yourself in the mirror and see an adorable four-sided sheep looking back at you. Imagine yourself with perfectly square wool. (You can only see four sides because mirrors aren't 3D. So you're a flat, cartoonish sheep.)
MeaningMnemonic: Imagine contorting yourself in such a way that your <radical>mouth</radical> now has your <radical>legs</radical> inside it. You've bent your body in all the wrong ways to achieve this. In fact, if you look at yourself, you look like a square now... You're just a person with <kanji>four</kanji> sides.

MeaningHint: Picture a bunch of narwhals constructing an underwater ice village. They're all working away with their left hands.
ReadingHint: If you're right-handed, imagine trying to use these left-handed saws. It flips and flops all over until you drop it. But if you're left-handed it feels just right!
ReadingMnemonic: In their <kanji>left</kanji> hands, the narwhals all hold <reading>saw</reading>s (さ). That's because the ice for their homes is already there, so they just need to saw away at them to make the shapes they want. Narwhal construction is actually very simple.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Narwhal</radical>s do <radical>construction</radical> with their <kanji>left</kanji> hands (or flippers, I guess). That's because all narwhals are left-handed!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo! And good luck with all the meanings (sorry sorry sorry).
ReadingHint: Just think about books and how you use them to hone your skills. Or read a book about honing things. Like swords.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Book</kanji>s are one of the best ways to <reading>hone</reading> (ほん) your intellect, you know?
MeaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>book</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>book</kanji> as well.

Take note that the second, third, and fourth meanings of this kanji are <kanji>origin</kanji>, <kanji>real</kanji>, and <kanji>main</kanji>. You can figure this out by thinking about how books mainly have their origins in real life.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking, staring at the ground until you come across a line that you're supposed to stop at. If you stop, the big "correct" sign appears, chiming a happy tune.
ReadingHint: Perhaps a slightly unorthodox weapon for a ruler of medieval Japan, but you won't complain about a saber from the Shogun himself!
ReadingMnemonic: You did the <kanji>correct</kanji> thing, so here's your reward: a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) from the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) himself!
MeaningMnemonic: There is a line on the <radical>ground</radical> so you <radical>stop</radical>, making a giant sign pop up that says <kanji>CORRECT</kanji>! You obeyed the rules of the road and did the correct thing. Good on you.

MeaningHint: This kanji looks just like a king bouncing a <kanji>ball</kanji>, no? If not, imagine the kanji itself bouncing the drop (the <kanji>ball</kanji>) over and over again. Kanji are surprisingly playful like that.
ReadingHint: Try to remember Tamara the ball girl. She'll come up anytime the reading for a kanji is たま, and usually she'll be involved with some kind of ball or round object. That's her job, after all.
ReadingMnemonic: When he's done playing with it, the king gives his <kanji>ball</kanji> to <reading>Tama</reading>ra (たま), the resident ball girl, for safekeeping.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> has a big <radical>drop</radical> of liquid in his hand... but then he bounces it! The drop was really a <kanji>ball</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Just know your radicals, and you will know your kanji as well.
ReadingHint: Smell the tacos. Touch the tacos. Taste the tacos. This is definitely a very strange rice field.
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading た, we use the word "taco."

You're in the rice field... but when you lean down to examine the rice more closely, you see that it's not rice that's growing, it's a bunch of little <reading>ta</reading>cos (た), blossoming out of the ground.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>rice paddy</radical> radical and the <kanji>rice paddy</kanji> kanji are the same, making this one super easy to remember, at least meaning-wise.

MeaningHint: Study your radicals hard! Most important thing!
ReadingHint: Imagine two teams (black and <kanji>white</kanji>), wearing their team colors, working really hard on hacking the iPhone. It is a huge contest, and people are watching with a lot of tension, to see who will hack the <kanji>white</kanji> iPhone first. Feel that tension, and see the colors separating them so vividly. The colors are important, and the excitement of the winner holding up the <reading>hack</reading>ed iPhone is too.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>white</kanji> team was the first to be able to <reading>hack</reading> (はく) the <kanji>white</kanji> iPhone.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>white</radical> and the kanji <kanji>white</kanji> are the same.

MeaningHint: Well... you'll know them both as long as you studied your radicals, young grasshopper. If you didn't, then you're just a young grasshopper who needs to study their radicals.
ReadingHint: Of course, feel the pain of your eye being gone... and struggle to get it back. You eventually give up because the sheep is too fast, but then you come up with an idea. What if I call out back to the sheep in its own language? Go ahead and cover one eye and start making "MEHHH" noises to see if you can get the sheep to come back to you and give you your eye. It's up to you whether it works or not, but be sure to feel frustrated by its reaction, or happy by it. Either one works.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>eyes</kanji> are important to you, right? Sadly, one of your eyes is missing right now, because a sheep grabbed it and pulled it out, and is now running away from you, yelling his sheep call.... "<reading>Mehhhhhh</reading> (め)!"
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>eye</radical> radical and <kanji>eye</kanji> kanji are the same as each other, so now you know them both.

MeaningHint: If you're keeping up with your radical studies, you're also keeping up with your kanji studies!
ReadingHint: Imagine the doormen being extra straight… too straight in their standing. On their uniform is “The Ritz” written in fine lettering, and as you walk past them, they stand up straight and say “Welcome to the Ritz!” Whatever you do, while you’re thinking about it, make sure you think about how they stand up so straight, and associate it with these doormen, who work, specifically, at the Ritz. If you effectively imagine all this, you should be able to remember this on’yomi. Go through it like it’s a movie in your head!
ReadingMnemonic: Think of someone who has to <kanji>stand</kanji> up straight for you. These people are pros in hospitality. These are the doormen at the <reading>Ritz</reading> (りつ).
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>stand</radical> and the kanji <kanji>stand</kanji> are the same thing, so now you know them both.

MeaningHint: If you can get to ten thousand different pieces, then that means your sword is absolutely perfect. If your sword dulls before you get to ten thousand, you have to throw it away.
ReadingHint: Carefully draw your manga onto these ten thousand leaf pieces. Maybe you even use your sword again to give them some textured details. You're so creative and talented!
ReadingMnemonic: Now that you have <kanji>ten thousand</kanji> pieces of tiny leaf paper, you decide to turn them into a <reading>man</reading>ga (まん)! Each little leaf piece will become a panel.
MeaningMnemonic: You cut the <radical>leaf</radical> with your <radical>sword</radical> into <kanji>ten thousand</kanji> pieces!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Go outside right now and do something to save the condors. If you don't do something right now there won't be enough time left!
ReadingMnemonic: Right <kanji>now</kanji> the <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) are in danger! Not next week, not next year, right now! Haven't you heard that deforestation and pollution are killing all of the condors?!
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>now</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>now</kanji> as well.

MeaningHint: And, you have no idea how to get them out. They're <kanji>inside</kanji> for good, and it's freaking you out. As in, there's a little person in there, running around, controlling your brain. People think you're crazy, but you know better... He's <kanji>inside</kanji> there, doing evil things.
ReadingHint: Imagine him holding the knife out, threatening to stab your brain. You’re in a conundrum here. If you don’t take him out, you’re in trouble, but if you do try to take him out, he might stab you in the brain with his knife. Try to think a way around this situation, focusing mainly on his knife.
ReadingMnemonic: You know this kanji means <kanji>inside</kanji>, so you can keep thinking about the person that's inside you, running around. You finally discover him, and try to take him out, but before you can, he pulls out a <reading>kni</reading>fe (ない) and threatens to cut you if you do anything irrational.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>head</radical> has a <radical>person</radical> <kanji>inside</kanji> it!

MeaningHint: Picture the fins falling onto the sword, and being divided into many parts. And if those fins represented hours, they’d be divided into minutes. Makes sense, right?
ReadingHint: This is how you feed your people: neatly divided, delicious buns. Picture these delicious buns divided into perfectly shaped parts. Yummy.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that the fins have been cut into neat <kanji>part</kanji>s, it's time to cut something else: <reading>bun</reading>s (ぶん).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>fins</radical> that fell onto my <radical>sword</radical> were sliced into many <kanji>part</kanji>s. Following this logic, this also means <kanji>minute</kanji>, which is part of an hour, and <kanji>divide</kanji>, which is the action of splitting something into many parts.

MeaningHint: Take these seven swords and swing them around. <kanji>Cut</kanji> everything in your path, including yourself. Then, think about how dumb it was to try to use seven swords at once, and then feel the pain of the cut you gave yourself. Ouch, ouch ouch.
ReadingHint: Just make sure you think about how you need to give them away in <reading>sets</reading>, and not anything else. This is the most important thing to think about in your head as you're trying to establish the mnemonic for your kanji reading memory. It's gotta be sets.
ReadingMnemonic: Because having seven swords for yourself is too much, you decide to take the swords and divide them up amongst pairs of people. You <kanji>cut</kanji> them into pairs, and give <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) to everyone who needs them in the village.
MeaningMnemonic: If you have <radical>seven</radical> <radical>swords</radical>, there's only so much you can do with them. The main thing? Probably using them to <kanji>cut</kanji> something.

MeaningHint: Picture all the kids out sliding on the slide at noon during their lunch break. It's hot and dry and they're very happy.
ReadingHint: Watch in horror as Godzilla's giant foot crushes all the kids and their slide at exactly noon. You'd better go...
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Noon</kanji> also happens to be the time <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) decided to attack for the same reasons: it's dry and kids are using the slide.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>slide</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> at <kanji>noon</kanji>. The sun is brightest at noon, so it dries the morning dew off the slide, making noon the perfect time to slide.

MeaningHint: Imagine that narwhal sitting there. It waves its flipper and says "hi friend" to you.
ReadingHint: Your narwhal friend goes on to warn you about all those youths, and how they will try to take away your friendship with their good looks and young ages.
ReadingMnemonic: "Hi <kanji>friend</kanji>," says the narwhal. "I must warn you about all the <reading>you</reading>ths." (ゆう)
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>narwhal</radical> sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> is your <kanji>friend</kanji>.

MeaningHint: We've fattened you up with lots of juicy radical knowledge, so this one should be pretty easy.
ReadingHint: Picture this fat person curled up all cozy-like on their Japanese futon. They look so content! Have you ever slept on a proper Japanese futon, by the way? We're talking premium, cloud-grade comfort — luxurious enough to make you feel like a real fat cat.
ReadingMnemonic: Imagine a <kanji>fat</kanji> person who loves to sleep on <reading>futo</reading>n (ふと).
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji is the same as the radical, so this means <kanji>fat</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine a small slide (how could you not, after you see the radicals?). So few people can use this thing, really, but there are a few little ones who can fit on it at least.
ReadingHint: Imagine this in your head. Even the few people who fit on the slide are struggling to get past the Shogun. Meanwhile the Japanese secret service is trying to make sure no one gets close to the shogun while he slides. A few people are crying. They want to slide, they want to leave… It's chaos.
ReadingMnemonic: Another reason so <kanji>few</kanji> people can use it is that the <reading>Sho</reading>gun (しょう) is trying to go down the slide. We're talking a grown man who's way too big for this little slide, so a few people have to wait behind him — it's a slide traffic jam.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>small</radical> <radical>slide</radical>. The thing about small slides is that <kanji>few</kanji> people can use them. Not everyone can fit their bum in a small slide — only a few small ones can make it down.

MeaningHint: Imagine taking a stick and pulling it back into your bow. Imagine how hard you have to pull. This thing is really strong!
ReadingHint: Imagine using all your strength to pull on this bow. Imagine the heat rising in your body. There's sweat dripping down your face. It's so hot, but you have to keep pulling!
ReadingMnemonic: When you <kanji>pull</kanji> the bow back, your body strains and you feel an immense <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) rise inside of you. You keep pulling and the heat grows hotter and hotter.
MeaningMnemonic: If you have a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> you need to <kanji>pull</kanji> the two back together to shoot it. If you can't pull it, you won't be shooting anything.

MeaningHint: Also, make sure you know the radicals by heart, it'll help you to learn more effectively, just like with this kanji.
ReadingHint: This is when I start kicking you in the shin and you imagine feeling the pain of getting kicked there. As I kick you in the shin, the stress is getting too much for your poor heart inside your shin. Poor shin, and poor heart.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>heart</kanji>, where is it stored in your body? Most people thought it was in the chest, but in reality, your <kanji>heart</kanji> is stored in your <reading>shin</reading> (しん).
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>heart</radical> and the kanji <kanji>heart</kanji> are the same. Rejoice and let your heart be free!

MeaningHint: Time to open the door to the reading part of this mnemonic.
ReadingHint: Feel the pain of your toe going away. Think about the other side of the <kanji>door</kanji>, where your <reading>toe</reading> is probably rolling around after it blasted off your foot.
ReadingMnemonic: You open the <kanji>door</kanji> and immediately get it closed back on your <reading>toe</reading> (と), literally causing your toe to explode.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji for <kanji>door</kanji> are the same!

MeaningHint: The only direction you need to go in is forward, it's always the right way.
ReadingHint: Close your eyes and picture your home in your mind. Can you tell which direction it is from where you are?
ReadingMnemonic: What <kanji>direction</kanji> do you always know? Which direction do you always want to go? <reading>Ho</reading>me (ほう). Something about your home always stays with you, no matter how far away you go. So if you're lost and you don't know which direction to go, just think of home!
MeaningMnemonic:
The radical and the kanji for <kanji>direction</kanji> are the same! Huzzah!

There is also another meaning too, though. This can also mean <kanji>way</kanji> as well. Think of it this way: When you move in one direction in your life, you are developing your own way. "The way of [your name here]."

MeaningHint: To distinguish this from the "noon" kanji (午), try thinking of the bit poking out of the top of this kanji as a tuft of hair on the cow's head — a cow lick, if you like.
ReadingHint: I hope you know what わぎゅう beef is, otherwise this will be a difficult one to learn.
ReadingMnemonic: Have you ever heard of わ<reading>ぎゅう</reading> (wagyu) beef? That ぎゅう is this ぎゅう.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji for <kanji>cow</kanji> are exactly the same! That makes it easy to remember, so long as you know your radicals well enough.

MeaningHint: No, I am not your father... but this kanji / radical set is!
ReadingHint: Hopefully you know who they are. If not, think about your father laughing like this: ちちちちちちちち〜！
ReadingMnemonic: Imagine your father is <reading>Cheech</reading> (ちち) from Cheech and Chong.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji for <kanji>father</kanji> are the same.

MeaningHint: Just make sure you're studying your radicals, otherwise it won't be as simple as I'd hoped.
ReadingHint: Smell the <kanji>fur</kanji> as you <reading>mow</reading> it down. Feel it flying everywhere. It's giant, so it must be pretty intense. Make it more intense to help yourself to remember the reading of this kanji better.
ReadingMnemonic: With <kanji>fur</kanji>, whisk your imagination to a land where there are stalks of fur all around, just like a big tall grassy field (except instead of grass, it's fur). You're surrounded by the black fur, the wind is blowing. Imagine yourself touching the long fur and smelling it. What does it feel and smell like? Now it's time to get rid of it. There's just too much <kanji>hair</kanji>! To get rid of it, you'll need to <reading>mow</reading> (もう) it down with a lawn mower. Imagine yourself starting up the lawn mower, and taking it around, mowing down the hair. Hear the sound of the mower in your head (they're loud and distinct). The important thing is to imagine yourself mowing everything. Key is to think "fur → in the fur → get rid of the fur → mow the fur," in that order. That'll get you to "mow," which will help you to remember the on’yomi.
MeaningMnemonic: <kanji>Fur</kanji> and <radical>fur</radical> are the same as each other, so the meaning of this kanji should be simple. This can also mean <kanji>hair</kanji>, too, though more like furry hair.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine the fluffy wool of the sheep, stopping you from moving forward. You can't push past it, no matter how hard you try.
ReadingMnemonic: You have to <kanji>stop</kanji> because there is a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in front of you. You try to walk around the sheep, but it moves to stop in front of you again. Every time the sheep stops you stop too.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>stop</radical> radical is the same as the <kanji>stop</kanji> kanji.

MeaningHint: You go outside. There's ice everywhere. It's freezing cold. Yep, this is definitely winter.
ReadingHint: Your pronunciation might be a little weird. But that’s okay. People still love your presents, and the way you say "fu’ you!"
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Winter</kanji> is also the time of year you give out presents: "One fu' you, one fu' you and… one <reading>fu' you</reading> (ふゆ)!"
MeaningMnemonic: You’ve got a <radical>winter</radical> with lots of <radical>ice</radical>. But doesn't any winter have lots of ice? Exactly. That's why this is just <kanji>winter</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Hold on to that radical knowledge! It'll be the north star that guides you through your studies.
ReadingHint: Yep, those things are called keytars. And for some reason, in the north, everyone has a keytar. They're basically obsessed with them. North = keytar. Quite simple.
ReadingMnemonic: You go north, and notice that everyone has a keytar (きた). You know, those keyboards that people hold and play like a guitar?
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji is the same as the radical — it means <kanji>north</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the triceratops on its side, then imagine it getting up. When it stands up normally, half of its body is soaking wet still. It's a half-n-half triceratops right now.
ReadingHint: Imagine him using his blaster to shoot the poor triceratops in half, perfectly down the wet-dry line of its body.
ReadingMnemonic: "You're half wet and half dry! I will cut you in <kanji>half</kanji> with my laser blaster so you're proper again," a voice says. It is  <reading>Han</reading> Solo (はん), the anti-establishment guy who hates when things aren't even.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>triceratops</radical> is lying out to <radical>dry</radical>. But it's so big, and it's lying on its side, so only <kanji>half</kanji> of it gets dry.

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!
ReadingHint:
Imagine a 子 looking at old things. A record player. A Game Boy. A cassette player. Kids just don't get old things.

Note: When you see the (古) radical in a kanji, there's a good chance it will use the reading こ.
ReadingMnemonic:
<kanji>Old</kanji> things are misunderstood by the <reading>子</reading>s (こ) of the world.

*Note:* Come up with a 子 character you can use for all the こ reading mnemonics going forward. This child will show up many times throughout your mnemonic journey, and it will help if you imagine the same character the whole time.
MeaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>old</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>old</kanji> as well.

Note: This "old" does not refer to people. Think of it as "old things" instead.

MeaningHint: Doesn't this kanji look like a machine mouth? Now wouldn't you want to keep that private from the people you know?
ReadingHint: Imagine being so afraid that people will find out about your machine mouth that you have to dye it to match a normal human mouth. Oh, when will this discrimination against machine-kind end?!
ReadingMnemonic: To make sure they can't tell what's <kanji>machine</kanji> and what's human, you <reading>dye</reading> (だい) the mouth to make it look like skin. You find dye the same shade as your normal mouth and just slap it on there.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>private</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> that's a <kanji>machine</kanji>. But you have to keep your machine mouth private because it would scare all the non-machine people around you.

MeaningHint: After imagining your evening toe overheating, rush outside to fix it. It's the best way to prevent fire toe, which is way worse. In the evening it's nice and cold outside too, so feel this on your skin, and feel the cold air on your toe, which is starting to get some relief.
ReadingHint: If you don't know Guy Fawkes, you should do a little reading up on him to make this mnemonic make sense. Also, Anonymous People wear a Guy Fawkes mask, if that helps any.
ReadingMnemonic: You go <kanji>outside</kanji> (you're not supposed to be out there at night!) and you're accosted by a couple of guys. Then, all of a sudden, <reading>Guy</reading> (がい) Fawkes jumps from the roof above you and takes them out, then starts running. "Guy Fawkes!" you yell after him!
MeaningMnemonic: You're known to get the ailment <radical>evening</radical> <radical>toe</radical>, which is when your toe catches on fire after getting really, really hot (imagine this happening, ouch!). The only way to fix it is to cool your toe down, and the best way to do that is to go <kanji>outside</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture this towel with the name of a city on it. Any city you like. Maybe it's your city!
ReadingHint: Imagine a city that has a sheep everywhere you look, as far as the eye can see. This city was built on sheep, don't you say a bad word about sheep when you're there.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>city</kanji> must have a lot of <reading>shee</reading>p (し) because, besides the manhole lid and the towel, all you can see are sheep. There are sheep everywhere. It's a sheep city.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a manhole <radical>lid</radical> and a <radical>towel</radical>, both with the name of a <kanji>city</kanji> on them. Decorative manhole lids and souvenir towels — two things cities love to slap their names on, especially in Japan.

MeaningHint: You want your private canopy to be wide so you can sprawl out under it as much as you want. No one's going to come in and tell you to close your legs or sit up straight. So it can be as wide as you like!
ReadingHint: Picture yourself lying under your wide, private canopy pretending to be a super hero on the ground. You're stretching a nice, wide super hero pose, like you're flying through the sky.
ReadingMnemonic: You like being <kanji>wide</kanji> because it makes you feel like a different person. But not just any person, like a super <reading>hero</reading> (ひろ)!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>canopy</radical> you use in <radical>private</radical> is very <kanji>wide</kanji>.

MeaningHint: See how the vertical bar is broken and your window frame is all messed up now? Why would your mother do such a thing?! Her hand is bleeding too…
ReadingHint:
You thought your mother was going to explain or apologize, but instead she's pointing and saying, "Haha," over and over.

Yeah. That sounds like my mom.
ReadingMnemonic: Suddenly your <kanji>mother</kanji> points her bloodied finger at you and laughs, "<reading>Haha</reading> (はは)!" She keeps laughing, haha haha haha, over and over. You feel even more alarmed.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone breaks part of your <radical>window</radical>. Who did this? It was your <kanji>mother</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Eat your yogurt. (Gogurt doesn't count, don't @ me.)
ReadingMnemonic: Today, your <kanji>task</kanji> is to eat <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). It's nutritious, it's delicious, and I'm telling you to do it, so you have to. A task is basically just something you have to do, so that's what this is.
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>task</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>task</kanji> as well.

MeaningHint: Just know your radicals and you'll know this kanji!
ReadingHint: Imagine the arrow as a human-sized living thing with legs, arms, and clothes, though. Strangely, it’s able to walk around even though it's an arrow. It has some kind of agenda. Make it a bit evil, if you can. What a strange thing…
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading や we use the word “Yankees” (like the evil baseball team, go M’s!).

What is this arrow doing? It’s following around all the <reading>Ya</reading>nkee (や) baseball players, stalking them, waiting to make its move (I’ll let you decide what its “move” is).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>arrow</radical> radical and the <kanji>arrow</kanji> kanji are the same. Sort of a "straight" shot to memorizing this kanji, you know?

MeaningHint: More people can see this character since the kid is on the roof.
ReadingHint: Imagine the child holding up the letter yelling at everyone who passes that the "J" stands for "Jesus." And yes, this kid just loves the Big Lebowski.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>character</kanji> that is being shown is "J," which stands for <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). The kid wants everyone to see this <kanji>letter</kanji> and <kanji>symbol</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> <radical>child</radical> stands upon the roof, holding up a single <kanji>letter</kanji>, a single <kanji>character</kanji>, a single <kanji>symbol</kanji> for the world to see.

MeaningHint: If you already know the radical, this one's a freebie. That should make your day a little brighter, hm?
ReadingHint: You might also say "Ah!" when you come up with a bright idea, like in those light bulb moments.
ReadingMnemonic: What do you say when you see something <kanji>bright</kanji>? "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" Like when you open the curtain and the sunshine hits your eyes, or someone shines a flashlight right into your face. Ahhhh, so bright!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bright</radical> radical and the <kanji>bright</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Study your radicals and you'll read good.
ReadingHint: Imagine having someone's life in the palm of your hands. It's up to you whether you protect it with your saber or... well... do the other thing sabers are for.
ReadingMnemonic: You can save <kanji>life</kanji> or take it away with your new <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). This is the saber you got from doing the correct thing (remember?), and now you need to figure out how to use it with this life. So what do you do?
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical and the <kanji>life</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals and therefore know your kanji. It's just that simple.
ReadingHint: Whoops, maybe you shouldn't throw stones through windows or you'll see people's sexy times.
ReadingMnemonic: You throw a <kanji>stone</kanji> through a window... and, now you can see something very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>stone</radical> radical and <kanji>stone</kanji> kanji are the same thing!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals!
ReadingHint: Genji's origin story is pretty important when it comes to mnemonics and Japanese literature. He's going to keep popping up on your kanji journey, so make sure you remember him!
ReadingMnemonic:
The <kanji>origin</kanji> of everything can be found in <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). Genji is the name of the main character in the world's first ever novel, "The Tale of Genji." 

Picture him as a rich, pretty boy who cares a ton about his looks and gets along really well with all the ladies. He's also really great at poetry, which helps the lady situation.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>origin</radical> radical and the <kanji>origin</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: It's all these generational differences that make the world go 'round!
ReadingHint: Hold the saber up high and pledge to protect the world for all of the generations to come! Then swish it around and make buzzing sounds with your mouth.
ReadingMnemonic: It's your job to protect the <kanji>world</kanji> and every <kanji>generation</kanji> with your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It was given to you to uphold correctness, and you'll pass it on to the next generation when it's time. Then they'll watch out for the world when you're gone.
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>world</radical> radical and the <kanji>world</kanji> kanji are the same, but there is another really important meaning you need to know: <kanji>generation</kanji>. 

Think of it this way: the world is just made up of many different generations of people, plants, and animals all getting along together.

ReadingHint: Picture this master of water controlling the droplet as it flies around inside these chutes, like he's solving a difficult puzzle with it. Astounding!
ReadingMnemonic: Do you remember back to the radical mnemonic, where the <kanji>master</kanji> is floating a water drop above his head? Imagine back to there. Once you do, the most amazing thing happens. The master sends the drop of water into a <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ) and it goes careening through an intricate series of them.
MeaningMnemonic:
The radical <radical>master</radical> and the kanji <kanji>master</kanji> have the same meanings!

This kanji also means <kanji>main</kanji> because the master is the main person of a dojo or household or business.

MeaningHint: Imagine different groups of leaders — they're all dressed nice and normal. Then, off to the side are the leaders of bears, cats, alligators, and narwhals... They're the other ones, no matter how you look at it. They are in their own group, and they are considered "others" because society won't accept them :(
ReadingHint: Just imagine the "others" shaking their heads at these taco worshipers. Seriously, what the heck?
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading た, we use the word "taco":

Then there's the <kanji>other</kanji>s of the others. These are the <reading>ta</reading>co (た) worshipers. They're really strange.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>alligators</radical>... she's no ordinary leader, that's for sure. She's not a politician, she's not a queen... She's one of the <kanji>Other</kanji> Ones, a group of strange, eccentric people who become leaders of animals.

MeaningHint: Picture how ridiculous this beggar looks, taking copies of his forehead with the ground copy machine.
ReadingHint: This particular shaman's specialty is being able to turn any surface into a copy machine at a swirl of his little shaman wand. Picture him running around in circles, doing a little dance and chant, and then the ground suddenly turning into one giant copy machine.
ReadingMnemonic: This ground <kanji>copy</kanji> machine can only be the work of one person: a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ)!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>forehead</radical> of this <radical>beggar</radical> is on the <radical>ground</radical>. Except it's not exactly the ground, it's a giant <kanji>copy</kanji> machine, and he's making copies of his forehead!

MeaningHint: Keep your private dirt in the past. It's better for everyone.
ReadingHint: Imagine physically, literally burying your past 子 self. If you hide it in Little Kyoto, no one will ever be able to dig it up.
ReadingMnemonic: To remember the reading きょ, we'll use "Little Kyoto." This will differentiate きょ from きょう, which is regular-sized Kyoto. Get it? You decide to bury all this private dirt from your <kanji>past</kanji> in Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). That stuff is from when you were just a little <reading>子</reading> (こ) and you don't like to think about it.
MeaningMnemonic: If someone tries to dig up <radical>dirt</radical> on your <radical>private</radical> life, tell them no thanks, that stuff is in the <kanji>past</kanji>. Some things should stay in the past. Not everyone needs to know everything about your life and your past.

MeaningHint: What are these numbers? What do they mean? Think over it for a while, get frustrated, and try to figure it out. You can't, though, so you probably won't ever get inside.
ReadingHint: Grandpa ごういち keeps saying numbers as you free him from the prison. Just one after another. Numbers numbers numbers. Hopefully old man ごういち isn't crazy.
ReadingMnemonic:
"You want to know the <kanji>number</kanji> to the hatch?" yells an old voice from inside. You say "yes," and the voice gives you the numbers and you're in! You go down into the prison, where there is one prisoner: Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> in the <radical>ground</radical> (in our case, it's a hatch), and inside is some kind of <radical>prison</radical> you can't quite get to. If you've ever seen Lost, this will sound familiar... on the hatch, there's actually a series of <kanji>number</kanji>s that you don't understand.

MeaningHint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji as well! How swell.
ReadingHint: Next time you put a belt on, make sure it's in the exact center of your body. Heck, try it now. And don't forget to say "Ohh!" as you do it. It'll keep the reading for this kanji front and center in your mind.
ReadingMnemonic: Remember that guy from the 央 radical fastening his belt across the <kanji>center</kanji> of his body? This guy always secures his belt at the center — not slightly to the left, not slightly to the right, but dead center. His precision is so mind-blowing that everyone watching can't help but say, "<reading>Ohh</reading>!"(おう).
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>center</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>center</kanji> are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Imagine carefully taking off your horns and laying them flat on a paper towel. You have to smooth them out so they're as flat as possible.
ReadingHint: Imagine carefully laying a flat layer of hay on top of your flat horns. You feel really at peace and... you're tired now. You decided to lie on top of the hay and take a nap while you wait for your horns to dry.
ReadingMnemonic: Being <kanji>flat</kanji> isn't enough, so you cover the whole thing in <reading>hay</reading> (へい). Hay absorbs water, so you add a flat layer of it.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>dry</radical> your <radical>horns</radical> you need to lay them <kanji>flat</kanji>. If they aren't flat they'll always be a little damp somewhere and you'll never have a moment's <kanji>peace</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine: it's just you, your fingers, and the wild street. It's hitting time!
ReadingHint: Now imagine your dad saying, "Don't do a hit!" over and over at you. "I'm sorry dad! But the streets were calling me!"
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>hit</kanji> one chump after another, suddenly your <reading>da</reading>d (だ) comes marching out of your house. Uh oh. Dad caught you hitting, which, honestly, isn't a very nice thing to do, so he has every right to stop you.
MeaningMnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> to the <radical>street</radical> to <kanji>hit</kanji> some people! Street fingers are for curling into a fist. And that street fist wants to do some hitting!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself adding that drop. What is it exactly? Something pretty potent, that's for sure. Now go ahead and touch the block of ice... wow, it's really cold — and only from one drop, too. Feel the burn on your finger from where you touched the <kanji>ice</kanji>.
ReadingHint: How did this happen? Was it your fault? Imagine the situation that would get Corey frozen like this.
ReadingMnemonic: When you think of <kanji>ice</kanji>, I want you to think of someone frozen with ice. Do you know anyone named <reading>Corey</reading> (こおり)? Anyone famous named Corey, perhaps Corey Feldman? Imagine this Corey person frozen solid with ice.
MeaningMnemonic: Add a <radical>drop</radical> of something magical to <radical>water</radical> and suddenly it turns into big block of <kanji>ice</kanji>, just like that.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself with a cross inside your mouth, saying all kinds of humble things. "Oh no, I'm not that good." "Oh no, my mustache doesn't look that great."
ReadingHint: Imagine mowing your neighbor's lawn while you say humbly how bad you are at mowing lawns. What a good neighbor you are.
ReadingMnemonic: As you're saying all these humble things, you're also <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) your neighbor's lawn. Your neighbors love you so much, because you're so humble, and you mow their lawn for them.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>cross</radical> inside your <radical>mouth</radical>, so the things you say are very pious. Everything you say, you <kanji>say humbly</kanji>.

MeaningHint: If you know your radicals, you know your kanji too.
