ReadingHint: Imagine someone with a huge head, washing dishes. When I say huge, I mean huge. She turns towards you with her big head and says “Hi, I’m Sarah” and you see her name tag, which also says Sarah. She is Sarah, the dish washer. The plates she holds up next to her head pale in comparison, even though they're the biggest pieces.
ReadingMnemonic: The person washing all the <kanji>dish</kanji>es was named <reading>Sarah</reading> (さら). She was named that because of her large head, which was shaped like Sarah Jessica Parker’s head (very big).
MeaningMnemonic: The <kanji>plate</kanji> kanji and the <radical>plate</radical> radical are exactly the same, like two matching pieces of china.

MeaningHint: It's raining, so you lend Totoro your umbrella. As a thanks, he gives you a present wrapped in bamboo leaves.
ReadingHint: Thanks for the new ray gun, Totoro!
ReadingMnemonic: So what did you receive as a <kanji>thanks</kanji>? You open it up, and there's a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun inside!
MeaningMnemonic: What do you get if you lend a <radical>spirit</radical> your <radical>umbrella</radical>? <kanji>Thanks</kanji>, of course! Especially if that spirit is Totoro, the spirit of the forest.

MeaningHint: The best thing to do with a tree is rest under it. Picture the leader resting under the tree with his eyes half closed.
ReadingHint: Don't email me saying cucumbers don't grow on trees. Cucumber trees exist. Look them up and then rest easy knowing I'd never lie to you.
ReadingMnemonic: As he <kanji>rest</kanji>s, he suddenly gets hungry and pulls a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) off the tree and bites into it. A good rest isn't complete without some crunchy tree cucumbers.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> is next to the <radical>tree</radical>. What is he doing? He's leaning against it, <kanji>rest</kanji>ing.

MeaningHint: Try to imagine the previous person — they must have been absolutely filthy to leave all this dirt behind. Why'd they have to go down the slide ahead of you?! If only you could've gotten here before them…
ReadingHint: If you look really far off into the distance ahead of you, you might still be able to spot this dirty, slide-ruining centaur. Hurry up and go after him before he gets on another slide!
ReadingMnemonic: Then you realize: the <kanji>previous</kanji> person on the slide must've been a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Centaurs track dirt everywhere 'cause they're always galloping around in the wilderness.
MeaningMnemonic: If you go down a <radical>slide</radical> and get <radical>dirt</radical> on your <radical>legs</radical>, it must have been the fault of the <kanji>previous</kanji> person, aka the person who went down the slide <kanji>ahead</kanji> of you. They should've cleaned off their dirty legs <kanji>before</kanji> they went down the slide, but they didn't, and now you've got dirt all over your legs too.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself going to the area you chose, and watching "Evening Mouth" speak. There are thousands of others there, wanting to see the giant floating mouth in the night sky. "Evening Mouth" then says "Everyone, what's my <kanji>name</kanji>?!" and everyone shouts back "Evening Mouth!" in unison. "Evening Mouth" has certainly gotten really <kanji>famous</kanji>.
ReadingHint: You know what they say. April showers bring May Flowers. What do <reading>May</reading> Flowers bring? They bring “Evening Mouth,” baby.
ReadingMnemonic: So, when does “Evening Mouth” appear? Turns out, “Evening Mouth” only comes out to talk in <reading>May</reading> (めい). You know this because it’s just starting to be spring, and when you look around (you’re watching “Evening Mouth” again) you see some spring flowers come up.
MeaningMnemonic: What is the <radical>evening</radical> <radical>mouth</radical>? Imagine a mouth that's up in the night sky. It's just there, talking, and only comes out at night. It only appears in one area of the world. (What area would you choose? Imagine yourself going to that place to see the "evening mouth.") And it appears only one month out of the year (more on this in a second). The "evening mouth" is the most famous thing in the _____ area (this is the area you choose). He's so <kanji>famous</kanji> that everybody knows his <kanji>name</kanji> ("Evening Mouth").

MeaningHint: Last year was a close call and you ended up shooting the cow's horn, which is why the cow radical looks a little strange here. Can you see how the cow radical fits underneath the gun?
ReadingHint: Nendoroids are Japanese plastic figurines. (Look them up if you need to! And yes, there are cow nendoroids.) It probably won’t be easy to make a cow-sized nendoroid this year, but better than having to shoot a real one.
ReadingMnemonic: It seems everyone has caught on to the fact that you deliberately miss every <kanji>year</kanji>, so this year you make a <reading>nen</reading>doroid (ねん) decoy in the shape of a cow.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>gun</radical> and a <radical>cow</radical>, which must mean it's that time of the <kanji>year</kanji>! Every year on New Year's Eve, you get asked to shoot a cow, as per tradition. You really don't want to though, so you deliberately miss every time.

MeaningHint: Imagine the sun coming up slowly, over the horizon. It then lines up with the cross, and at that moment, your alarm goes off, waking you up. You check the alarm clock, and instead of displaying the time, it just says EARLY and FAST on it, flashing in big red letters and alternating.
ReadingHint: But you get your soul up early and head to the church fast anyway, because you're doing all of this for your soul in the first place!
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the そう reading we use the word "soul."

When you're forced to get up <kanji>fast</kanji> and <kanji>early</kanji> you can feel it in your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). You're so tired and it's so early, your soul cries out, "Please don't make me go this fast this early. I'm gonna leave your body if you keep this up!"
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> is above the <radical>cross</radical>, it's time for some people to go church — which is always way too <kanji>early</kanji> on a Sunday, am I right? And if you want to get to church early, you need to be <kanji>fast</kanji>, otherwise you'll be too late.

MeaningHint: Touch the energy, catch the spirit ball. Is it solid or gaseous? Does it tingle or burn? Try to imagine it out. Then, yes, go ahead and sell that valuable energy treasure to a power company or something.
ReadingHint: You take the key and open another treasure that's nearby. Open it up and see what's inside. Imagine something spectacular. Thanks, energy key.
ReadingMnemonic: As you reach inside the <kanji>energy</kanji> <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball, you feel something hard inside. It's a large <reading>key</reading> (き) that gives you access to another treasure.
MeaningMnemonic: These days <radical>energy</radical> is the greatest <radical>treasure</radical>. Not gold, money, or anything like that. Imagine finding some in a literal treasure chest. As soon as you open it, out bursts the <kanji>energy</kanji> in a kind of <kanji>spirit</kanji> ball.

MeaningHint: Imagine flying toward the sun to see a hundred leaves growing together in the shape of one big leaf. You want to go closer!
ReadingHint: Imagine hearing Goofy guh-hyuck over and over after you save him from being stranded on the sun. How would you feel being stuck in a cramped ship with laughing Goofy and his hundred leaves? Oh, you thought he would leave those behind? Nope, they saved him so you have to take them back too. Happy travels!
ReadingMnemonic: You fly closer, counting all one <kanji>hundred</kanji> leaves when you hear a strange sound. You take off your space helmet and hear, "GUH <reading>HYUCK</reading> (ひゃく)!" You know that laugh... it's Goofy! On top of the hundredth leaf is Goofy laughing and waving around. He must have built this leaf tower to get your attention.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>leaf</radical> growing out of the <radical>sun</radical>! You fly out into space to check it out and when you get closer you see that it isn't just one leaf, it's a <kanji>hundred</kanji>! That makes sense, you wouldn't be able to see just one leaf on the sun from where you were on earth.

MeaningHint: Just make sure you know your radicals!
ReadingHint: Really take as much bamboo as you could possibly hold. Take some bamboo, then take some more, and take more… It’s free after all.
ReadingMnemonic: Today is free <kanji>bamboo</kanji> day, so just <reading>take</reading> (たけ) all the bamboo you want.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bamboo</radical> radical and the <kanji>bamboo</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: This is why it's good to make sure you know your radicals.
ReadingHint: Think about how it tastes. Think about how it feels in your throat. Let it get caught on your teeth and around your tongue. It's a weird feeling having thread in your mouth, and you want to use that weird feeling to remember you're going to <reading>eat</reading> it.
ReadingMnemonic: You have some <kanji>thread</kanji>... what's the first thing you do with it? You take it and put it in your mouth, and start sucking it down like spaghetti. You have decided to <reading>eat</reading> (いと) the thread.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> radical and <kanji>thread</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

MeaningHint: Study those radicals hard to make sure you know your kanji as well!
ReadingHint: This is sort of a stereotypical singer sort of image. Make sure you concentrate a lot when you're listening to your mimimimiiii tone. In fact, why don't you do it out loud right now - make a little fool of yourself. It's fun, and it'll help you to remember!
ReadingMnemonic: Put your hand to your <kanji>ear</kanji> and sing... "<reading>Mimi</reading>mimimimiiiii!" (みみ) and listen to your tone with your ear.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>ear</radical> radical and <kanji>ear</kanji> kanji are the same in looks as well as meaning.

MeaningHint: It's times like these when you gotta hope you're studying your radicals!
ReadingHint: Imagine little words that say "mush" flying out from under your foot as you mush the poor thing. What does a "mush" sound sound like? You'll have to come up with something unique to yourself, and focus on it to remember this reading.
ReadingMnemonic: It's an <kanji>insect</kanji>! Get it! You squish it with your foot - what sound does it make? A <reading>mush</reading> (むし) sound.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>insect</radical> radical and <kanji>insect</kanji> are the same in looks as well as and meaning.

MeaningHint: There is a whole group of people out there who are measuring these trees. Imagine them asking each other "do you think this will be a good tree for our village center?" and other people nodding in agreement or disagreement as they measure.
ReadingHint: Look at the mural on the side of this newly constructed village center. So many trees! Visualize the whole process of the tree becoming a building, as you reflect on the message of the mural.
ReadingMnemonic: They take all the wood back to where they're building their <kanji>village</kanji>, and once they construct the village center they paint a <reading>mura</reading>l (むら) on the side of it. The mural is of the forest where they got the trees from, to honor the village's origins.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone's taking <radical>tree</radical> <radical>measurements</radical>… Why are they taking measurements of trees? Because they're planning to cut them down to make their <kanji>village</kanji>.

MeaningHint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji too! Man, kanji are easy sometimes.
ReadingHint: Picture the man watching as the first light of dawn tints the sky pink and orange. Time to get to work, man!
ReadingMnemonic: When you think of a <kanji>man</kanji>, think of <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), because that man has to get up at dawn to power his rice paddy every day.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>man</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>man</kanji> are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Think about it, a single farm wouldn't need a whole street. Just paths would do. There is a street next to this rice paddy, so there must be other people, which makes this a <kanji>town</kanji>.
ReadingHint: Remember imagining Mrs. Chou in great detail in level 2? Time for some of that work to come to fruition. Imagine Mrs. Chou walking down the street of the town towards you, ready to make your day bad. She breaks things as she walks towards you, then knocks you down for walking around in her town. She says, "Wherever there's a street, you'll find Mrs. Chou." Then spits on your back as she walks away.
ReadingMnemonic: You go to visit this nice little <kanji>town</kanji>. But then, there she is... Every time you walk down the street, the street-woman Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) arrives to ruin your day.
MeaningMnemonic: When you see both a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and a <radical>street</radical> in the same place, you have the makings of a whole <kanji>town</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Your leader is a nice guy who wants there to be flowers for future generations to enjoy. That's why he's painstakingly planting all these flowers with his spoon. Imagine watching him dig hole after hole with his little spoon just to plant some flowers for everyone.
ReadingHint: You and everyone else are free to enjoy the flowers too, but… Just make sure to get out of the way when Hannah shows up. These flowers are really for her.
ReadingMnemonic: Secretly, the leader is actually planting all these <kanji>flower</kanji>s for <reading>Hannah</reading> (はな), a girl he has a huge crush on. Everyone thinks the leader's a nice, selfless guy, but really he's just trying to impress Hannah.
MeaningMnemonic: All these <radical>flowers</radical> were planted by your <radical>leader</radical> with nothing but a <radical>spoon</radical>. Now the whole place is absolutely full of beautiful <kanji>flower</kanji>s. Flowers everywhere.

MeaningHint: Make sure you're studying your radicals. If you do, you'll know a lot of kanji as well, almost magically!
ReadingHint: The struggling to see bit is pretty important — make sure that you slowly see a little better and better as the person comes closer, but it's difficult the whole time (struggles are good for memory). Then, it starts to become more and more clear, and you start saying "that can't be <reading>me</reading>. No way it's me..." When the person comes up to you (the person, being you) you learn about parallel universes and other fabulous things. Still, the whole time you can't stop thinking about how "this person looks just like me... no, this person is me!"
ReadingMnemonic: You see something. It's walking towards you, but you need to see better. When the person comes close, you realize "That person is <reading>me</reading> (み)!"
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>see</radical> and the kanji <kanji>see</kanji> are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: Shellfish are shellfish, you know?
ReadingHint: Imagine rowing your kayak out into the ocean to collect shellfish. Imagine the color and shape of the kayak. Feel the spray of the salt water and the taste of some fresh shellfish, right out of the ocean.
ReadingMnemonic: To get some <kanji>shellfish</kanji>, all you have to do is hop in your <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) and get some. Shellfish live in the water.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>shellfish</radical> radical and the <kanji>shellfish</kanji> kanji are the same! It can be just the <kanji>shell</kanji> part too.

MeaningHint: What a lucky ducky — as long as you know your radicals, you'll know this kanji's meaning as well!
ReadingHint: Think of あか as being some sort of alias for "red." That's why it's pronounced あか in Japanese, AKA "red."
ReadingMnemonic: This kanji means <kanji>red</kanji>, <reading>AKA</reading>, also known as, あか.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>red</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>red</kanji> are the same thing!

MeaningHint: Same kanjis and radicals make Jack a happy boy. You should also learn the other meaning as well, though, if you can.
ReadingHint: Just think about your bare feet. Think about them too much. Feel how cold it is on your feet. Now, put on a sock to make it feel better. Ahhh, so nice!
ReadingMnemonic: What do you wear on your <kanji>foot</kanji>? You wear a <reading>sock</reading> (そく).
MeaningMnemonic:
The radical for <radical>foot</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>foot</kanji> are exactly the same.

That being said, there's also another meaning you should keep in mind, which is <kanji>sufficient</kanji>. If you think about it, one foot should be sufficient enough to walk around with if you hop.

MeaningHint: Make sure you study your radicals... though don't do it while driving.
ReadingHint: The more fantastic you make this car, the better. Also, feel the surprise of a shaman getting out of this car. What a cool dude that shaman is.
ReadingMnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading しゃ, we use "shaman".

There's a <kanji>car</kanji>. It's the nicest car you've ever seen. Exquisite, beautiful. Wouldn't you be surprised to learn it belongs to a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ)? Well it does. This shaman charges a premium for his shaman services so he can afford fancy cars like this.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>car</radical> and the kanji <kanji>car</kanji> are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Actually, it does make it easy — the radicals make kanji wayyy easier, no?
ReadingHint: Be sure to feel irritated at that person. Think in your head as they're telling these dumb "not" jokes how much of a fool they are... and how much of a fool you are for sitting here and listening to them.
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember ふ we'll use the word "fool."

Think of those jokes, where someone says something, then ends with a loud "<kanji>NOTTTT</kanji>!" Someone is doing that to you, over and over. What is that person? That person is a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ).
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>not</radical> and the kanji <kanji>not</kanji> are the same thing! That makes it easy... Not!

MeaningHint: Imagine the leader samurai doing things. He's doing more than anyone else, no one can keep up with what he's doing.
ReadingHint: Picture the leader samurai doing lots of sheep things. He's shearing sheep. He's feeding sheep. He's doing everything you could possibly do, because he's the sheep samurai.
ReadingMnemonic: He was <kanji>doing</kanji> things with <reading>shee</reading>p (し). He is the leader of the sheep clan, and he was able to do more with sheep than anyone else. That's why he's the leader.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>samurai</radical> didn't become leader by sitting around not taking action. He got to where he is by <kanji>doing</kanji>.

ReadingHint: Imagine yourself laughing at Ana while you point at her, continuing to jeer as Ana struggles to escape the hole. You’d help Ana out of the hole, but you’re too busy laughing at her.
ReadingMnemonic: <reading>Ana</reading> (あな) fell into a <kanji>hole</kanji>. Laugh at Ana and her unfortunate misfortune of having fallen into a hole like a ninny. Haha, Ana – you’re stupid and clumsy!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>hole</radical> radical and the <kanji>hole</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Spectacular!

MeaningHint: Picture the treasure reflected in the drop of water. It's marvelous!
ReadingHint: Picture his home from the outside. It looks like a normal, small castle. But then you walk in and it's a treasure trove of... well, treasure!
ReadingMnemonic: All of this <kanji>treasure</kanji> is kept in the king's <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). He can't let other people see how much treasure he has, or they'll get mad. Or worse, ask him to share some with his people. Yuck. The treasure stays at home.
MeaningMnemonic: Under his <radical>roof</radical>, the <radical>king</radical> stares into a <radical>drop</radical> of water containing his greatest <kanji>treasure</kanji>. Just kidding, the king has way more treasure than that. The drop of water is just reflecting what's around him: mounds and mounds of treasure, stacked all the way up to the roof.

MeaningHint: To help this mnemonic along, literally imagine your older brother as someone that's just a giant mouth with a pair of legs walking around. It talks and talks and talks, too, irritating you. Feel the irritation specifically at your older brother. He's older than you, so you can't beat him up... yet.
ReadingHint: Hmm... don't you want to go to きょうと someday? Maybe you should have sent your brother somewhere less cool.
ReadingMnemonic: You wish you could box your loud-mouth <kanji>older brother</kanji> up and send him far away to <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is super far away from where you currently live, so just ship your older bro off so he can never bother you again.
MeaningMnemonic: Who's someone who's just a <radical>mouth</radical> with <radical>legs</radical>? That's your know-it-all <kanji>older brother</kanji>, who won't shut up about anything.

MeaningHint: Picture the leader of the ceremony out back, unsure of what period or age they're in, while the substitute leader walks in and stares at them in shock.
ReadingHint: Now you're out back with the original leader and the substitute leader, trying to dye the color of this stupid shirt to match that one.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>substitute</kanji> leader is wearing the wrong color clothes, so you have to <reading>dye</reading> (だい) them, like they did in the olden <kanji>age</kanji>s.
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>leader</radical> for the <radical>ceremony</radical> can't come so you need a <kanji>substitute</kanji> to <kanji>replace</kanji> them ASAP!

This kanji has another meaning too. The leader can't come to the ceremony because they forgot what <kanji>period</kanji> of time it was. If they think they're in the wrong <kanji>age</kanji>, they won't come because they're trying to lead some monkeys in the ceremony instead.

MeaningHint: Imagine sliding down a slide made of rough, gnarly tree branches. Their bark digs into your skin and pulls it right off of your body. Okay, it's painful, but it's a small price to pay for having such beautifully exfoliated skin.
ReadingHint: Your skin usually protects you from the heat, but imagine how raw it would be after you shed the old layer all at once. It's kinda vulnerable right now, so take care of it.
ReadingMnemonic: Without your rough old <kanji>skin</kanji> to protect you, you feel the full <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) of the sun now. It burns… it burns! Yeah, you better protect that fresh new skin from the heat.
MeaningMnemonic: You go down a <radical>slide</radical> made of <radical>branch</radical>es and your <kanji>skin</kanji> scrapes right off — ouch! Lucky for you, it was just your old outer layer of skin, and now you've got a fresh shiny layer. Yay for exfoliation! You're not sure what to do with this big, floppy layer of old skin, though…

MeaningHint: This may seem traumatic, but actually your father is surprisingly into it. Imagine him mixed in with all kinds of other things. It's important to note that this kanji is about things being mixed in, not so much about the action of mixing. That's another kanji.
ReadingHint: "I love mixing in with potatoes and tomatoes," your father こういち states. "Turn up that temperature. I like to mingle with a bit more tingle."
ReadingMnemonic: With a <kanji>mix</kanji> of your father and other ingredients <kanji>mingl</kanji>ing in your cooking pot, you lift up the lid... your father pops up. Your father is <reading>こう</reading>いち. He stares back at you with a big smile.
MeaningMnemonic: You put a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>father</radical>. But, he won't taste good alone, so you <kanji>mix</kanji> in some other ingredients. They <kanji>mingle</kanji> inside the pot with him.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: "I met my soulmate in a kayak," said Koichi.
ReadingMnemonic: What better way to <kanji>meet</kanji> new people than to start <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい)! Get into your kayak and just float out into the water. You'll eventually bump into someone else in a kayak and boom, you're meeting someone new!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>meet</radical> radical and the <kanji>meet</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Absorbing light is natural for reptiles, and a round one can absorb even more. Imagine this strange, round piceratops basking in the warm rays of light shining down from the sky. What a rare sight you've come across.
ReadingHint: Imagine many different (but also kind of the same) こういちs attacking and throwing spears at the piceratops. Little do they know that it's fully charged up after absorbing all that light, and it unleashes a bright flash of energy, rendering all the こういちs momentarily blind and allowing it to escape.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch this majestic beast absorbing <kanji>light</kanji>, suddenly several dozen feral <reading>こう</reading>いちs burst out from the wilderness, spears in hand. Oh no! Those こういちs are hunting this rare piceratops!
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>triceratops</radical> in the distance. But, it's odd. It's perfectly <radical>pi</radical> shaped (that is, circular, like a pie). Some kind of piceratops, if you will. The reason they are this shape is because they want to absorb as much <kanji>light</kanji> as possible. They evolved to be round so they don't have any corners casting a shadow.

MeaningHint: Pick up the mustache, hold it up next to your face in front of a mirror. It's unbelievable, but it's the same in every way, down to the last hair.
ReadingHint: If it helps, imagine hundreds or thousands of doughnuts all around you with the same exact mustache. First of all, it's terrifying to be surrounded by thousands of mutant doughnuts. Secondly, it's really weird they all have the same mustache as you.
ReadingMnemonic:
When we need to remember the reading どう, we use the word "doughnut".

While this is already strange enough, you notice something stranger. Over a couple feet away is a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう) who also has the <kanji>same</kanji> mustache as you (and the one that fell on the ground). The doughnut looks at you for a moment, then scampers off. This doughnut must be a mutant because normally doughnuts don't move.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mustache</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>. Upon closer inspection, you see that the mustache on the ground is exactly the <kanji>same</kanji> as yours!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself counting and counting smaller and smaller mouths inside of each other, recording the number of times you can find a mouth inside another mouth. Dig deeper and deeper until it feels ridiculous how small the mouths are getting. Be sure to focus on the counting and the number of times you find one. Now, how many mouths is this times two, because your mouth is like this too.
ReadingHint: Your kayak is slashing and dipping through the revolving mouths, going down and down more times than you can count!
ReadingMnemonic: If you're having a hard time wrapping your head around the infinite number of <kanji>times</kanji> these mouths <kanji>revolve</kanji>, just picture yourself in a little <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) going around and down through the mouths.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>mouth</radical> inside a <radical>mouth</radical>. But if you look even closer (not actually, but we're pretending), you can see another mouth inside that mouth... and another one inside of that one! It keeps going and going. Can you count the number of <kanji>times</kanji> there's a mouth inside a mouth? This kanji also means <kanji>revolve</kanji> too. Think of each "time" a thing happens, it loops back around and comes back to the beginning, revolving around again another time.

MeaningHint: If you accidentally think "a lot" or something similar, that's okay too. It's the basic meaning that counts, and all these things are pretty close, though eventually you want to get to the point where you know that this kanji does in fact mean <kanji>many</kanji>.
ReadingHint: The tacos pelt you and knock you off your feet. Hard shell, soft shell, meat, lettuce, tomatoes, cheese... it's terrible. And there are so many of them, flying from the sky to drown you in tacos. This is absolutely absurd (and you should think that too, so you remember the reading).
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading た we use the word "taco."

Let's start with the kanji meaning: <kanji>many</kanji>. You go outside in the evening (because that's what this kanji is made up of) and are surprised when you're hit by <kanji>many</kanji> <reading>ta</reading>cos (た) flying from the sky. For some reason it's raining tacos, and of course you have no idea why.
MeaningMnemonic: How many <radical>evenings</radical> are there? There are <kanji>many</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a triceratops. Now picture Wolverine. An equals sign between them. A little arrow pointing at the horns, an arrow pointing at the blades. And a big flashing CORRECT sign above them. Need I say more?
ReadingHint: If you're lucky, you might see Wolverine fighting a triceratops in the middle of Shibuya Crossing in Tokyo. Heroes and monsters are always fighting in Tokyo, right? At least that's what my anime tells me.
ReadingMnemonic: Because you got the answer <kanji>correct</kanji>, you win a free trip to <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Congrats!
MeaningMnemonic: What superhero is most like a <radical>triceratops</radical>? …If you said <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you're <kanji>correct</kanji>! Why is that the <kanji>right</kanji> answer? Because the triceratops has three horns, and Wolverine has three blades coming out of his hands. It's obvious, isn't it? But you knew that of course.

MeaningHint: Make sure you get every one, and don't miss any of them. Actually search around in your imagination for any more windows, because you want to stress it's important you hit each and every window with your gun.
ReadingHint: Now you need to shoot every one of the mice that came through those broken windows. You can't let any survive. You have to get every last one out of here!!
ReadingMnemonic: Now that <kanji>every</kanji> window is broken, you feel pretty stupid because <reading>mi</reading>ce (まい) are pouring in through <kanji>each</kanji> one. You've made a way for mice to get into your house. That wasn't smart.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>gun</radical> and you see some <radical>windows</radical>. What do you think you're going to do with this gun? Of course, you're going to shoot the windows... <kanji>every</kanji> single one of them. <kanji>Each</kanji> and every one.

MeaningHint: Aren't radicals nice? As nice and as fundamental as… rice. They make things so much easier, if you've been studying them.
ReadingHint: Imagine helping to bulldoze the rice into the bay, watching it fill it up, then pile high. A bay isn't the best space for rice storage, but it'll do. The important thing is that you think you have way too much of the stuff, so the only way to get rid of it is to dump it in a large body of water, and that large body of water is a bay.
ReadingMnemonic: A ton of <kanji>rice</kanji> is grown in <kanji>America</kanji>. And yet you never see rice in American cuisine. So where's all that unused rice going? Actually, it all gets dumped into a big <reading>bay</reading> (べい). They have too much rice and no place to store it, so they've started using bays to store it.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>rice</radical> radical and <kanji>rice</kanji> kanji are the same in looks and meaning. This kanji can also mean <kanji>America</kanji>, maybe because America is covered in rice or fields or something?

MeaningHint: Have you been studying your radicals? If so, this'll be easy!
ReadingHint: Feel the honey in each feather, taste it trickling over your lips... And go ahead and plummet towards the ground (because you were jumping off a building to fly when someone doused you with honey). At least it's a tasty way to go, no?
ReadingMnemonic: You've gathered <kanji>feather</kanji> after feather, like some kind of wannabe birdman or birdwoman, and now you're ready to fly. But as soon as you're about to take off, someone dumps <reading>honey</reading> (はね) on you, making your feathers all goopy.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>feathers</radical> radical and <kanji>feather</kanji> kanji are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

MeaningHint: Imagine him in his coffin, changing from a begging position to a stereotypical "thinking" position. His hand is on his chin, eyebrows furrowed. Focus on the beggar's thinking. This is a pretty abstract idea to imagine, so it will be important to create a vivid picture in your mind's eye.
ReadingHint: Then, the beggar realizes that he is こういち. Here he is begging in a coffin, and still he only thinks of himself.
ReadingMnemonic: What does the beggar <kanji>think</kanji> of? A thought bubble appears near his head. In it is a picture of <reading>こう</reading>いち! He's thinking that if こういち were here, he'd save him. Oh, if only こういち could save us all!
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>coffin</radical> there's a <radical>beggar</radical>, doing his begging. But it doesn't work, nobody helps him. So, he just begins to <kanji>think</kanji>: Why am I in this situation? How could I have avoided it? How will I get out? Think, dammit, think!!

MeaningHint: Study those radicals, and perhaps we'll eat some meat tonight.
ReadingHint: If you don't know anyone named Nick, then just imagine it's Nic Cage. Picture him as he chews that succulent meat, maybe even closing his eyes in gastronomic ecstasy. Yeah. Go for it, Nic. Enjoy that meat.
ReadingMnemonic: You know who loves <kanji>meat</kanji>? <reading>Nick</reading> (にく). You probably know a Nick — imagine him eating some succulent meat right now.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>meat</radical> radical and <kanji>meat</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

MeaningHint: Radicals have self. Kanji have self. Kanji and radicals can sometimes be the same thing... so do they really have "self"? Only way to find out is to study both of them hard!
ReadingHint: You can feel free to do some introspection here.
ReadingMnemonic:
When we need to remember じ we use the word "Jesus."

Who said you should always look at your <kanji>self</kanji> before tossing stones (or whatever) at others? <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) did. So, make sure you look at your own self before being a jerk.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>self</radical> radical and <kanji>self</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

MeaningHint: Color me pink! This kanji's super easy if you know your radicals.
ReadingHint: You can even have someone ask you about your ear... "Is your ear okay? I think it's the color blue..."
ReadingMnemonic: You already have <kanji>color</kanji> on the mind because you know the meaning of this kanji, so think of something colorful about yourself. Imagine your <reading>ear</reading> (いろ) being a weird color... green, purple, red, whatever. Just make it really bright and standing out.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>color</radical> radical and <kanji>color</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

MeaningHint: If you know your radicals well enough (and you do), you'll know this kanji.
ReadingHint: Remember, whenever we see the reading こう, think of こういち, a founder of WaniKani (who loves gyoza). We'll keep it consistent, so over time you should develop a character around こういち that you learn to love or hate.
ReadingMnemonic: "Oh, did he <vocabulary>go</vocabulary> again?" When <reading>こう</reading>いち is not around, that's what people often say because he goes to the <reading>gyo</reading>za (ぎょう) restaurant almost every day. こういち just loves gyoza so much, you always know where he is when he is not home.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>go</radical> radical and the <kanji>go</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know all those radicals, it will make your life more pleasant.
ReadingHint: Imagine the dusty, ramshackle towns in the Wild West where you find your niche. You set up shops with big signs that say SNAKE OIL. Congrats, you've cornered the snake oil niche in the Wild West!
ReadingMnemonic: You go off to the <vocabulary>west</vocabulary>. It's the Wild West, even. There aren't many people out here, making it easy to find a <reading>niche</reading> (にし) for your snake oil business.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>west</radical> radical and the <kanji>west</kanji> kanji are the same. Isn't that nice?

MeaningHint: Think of our president (or your prime minister, or dictator, or whatever) and imagine this person appearing on television one morning with a big ol' lip ring. Feel surprised by it. Just say "What??" over and over again. You want to have the feeling of "what" in you when you imagine this — that'll help you to remember this kanji's meaning.
ReadingHint: Imagine this leader calling the nanny over. "What is it?" asks the nanny. "Naan!" shouts the leader. What would that leader do without their naan nanny? What indeed.
ReadingMnemonic: This leader with the lip ring wants one thing. <kanji>What</kanji> is it? A <reading>nanny</reading> (なに) who can make <reading>naan</reading> (なん). The leader loves naan because it's soft enough to eat with their lip ring on. And a nanny needs to make it because the leader can't do anything on their own.
MeaningMnemonic: Our <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>lip ring</radical>. Wait, our leader has a lip ring? <kanji>WHAT</kanji>??

MeaningHint: Imagine looking down at your body and thinking, huh, I wonder what all that junk is? Oh well, I'm sure the leader has it all figured out in that book of hers.
ReadingHint: Picture the leader, reading a book, putting a tie on her body. Look around at you and your family and your neighbors. You don't know what your body meat-bags do and you don't know how to tie a tie. Womp womp.
ReadingMnemonic: The leader's <kanji>body</kanji> book also taught her about something very body specific: how to tie a <reading>tie</reading> (たい). You can't have a tie on your body, no no. Those are for the leader and her smart body.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> has a <radical>book</radical> all about the human <kanji>body</kanji>. Knowing about bodies is a privilege. One that only leaders get—it helps them control all their subjects, like you.

MeaningHint: Imagine the leader taking his key to open up a safe that has a button to make other keys for the underlings. No key is as great as this key, though.
ReadingHint: Imagine the leader making the keys and dropping them one by one into a big sack. He's always walking around carrying a big sack over his shoulder, distributing the keys to his underlings and watching them saw them into shape.
ReadingMnemonic: The leader <kanji>make</kanji>s all the keys and puts them in a <reading>sack</reading> (さく) to distribute them to the underlings, who then cut them into shape with a <reading>saw</reading> (さ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>key</radical> is the key that <kanji>make</kanji>s all the other keys. It's sort of like the one ring to rule them all, the one ring to bind them... except with keys.

MeaningHint: Scientists have spent decades trying to figure out what the diagram of two massive drops and treasure in your mouth could mean, but to no avail. Imagine the pain of having to spend your entire life with a mouth full of two massive drops and treasure… poor you.
ReadingHint: Now you’re stuck at this zoo with your diagram mouth full of two massive drops and treasure. Scientists think this can't be it, so further research will be required. Your diagram mouth of drops and treasure isn't off the hook quite yet. Just a few more years, spent at this zoo! I promise!
ReadingMnemonic: After decades of analyzing the <kanji>diagram</kanji>, scientists found out it leads to… a <reading>zoo</reading> (ず)?!
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>mouth</radical> full of two massive <radical>drop</radical>s and… <radical>treasure</radical>?! Upon closer inspection, they appear to form some sort of <kanji>diagram</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine him and the other samurai with their flags on sticks, waving them around shouting things like "We want a voice!”, “Give us a voice!”
ReadingHint: Imagine your surprise at seeing this large protest of 子 samurai, shouting for their voices to be heard. Really make sure you can hear yourself going “You’re a 子, eh?!” in your head.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to join the samurai’s protest for more <vocabulary>voice</vocabulary> but immediately notice… they’re all <reading>子, eh</reading> (こえ)?!
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>samurai</radical> with a <radical>flag</radical> on a <radical>stick</radical>. On his flag is the symbol 声, the kanji for <kanji>voice</kanji>. Why does he have that there? He feels like his Shogun isn't giving him and his fellow Samurai a voice, so he's brought a flag with the word "voice" written on it to protest.

MeaningHint: If you don't have a younger brother, just pretend you do for now. Imagine him really vividly. He is a stereotypical little boy, playing dress-up with horns and a bow and arrow, and he's going down a makeshift slide held up by a stick.
ReadingHint: Imagine the bright red, blue, green and purple dye in your younger brother's hair. He's put so much on that he's dyed half his face too. He's going to be in trouble when he gets home!
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>younger brother</kanji> loves playing dress-up so much that he's now started <reading>dye</reading>ing (だい) his hair all kinds of colors.
MeaningMnemonic: There is someone wearing <radical>horns</radical>, holding a <radical>bow</radical>, and going down a <radical>slide</radical> held up by a <radical>stick</radical>. What type of person do you imagine? I'd say someone who's an immature boy. That would be your <kanji>younger brother</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture all the different shapes this hairy lantern could take in your mind.
ReadingHint: Picture the shape of this hairy, glowing cake.
ReadingMnemonic: You stare at the <kanji>shape</kanji> until you finally recognize it: <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! Well, it's hairy, glowing cake, but it's still shaped like a big slice of cake.
MeaningMnemonic: You're walking at night when you discover a <radical>lantern</radical> covered entirely in <radical>hair</radical>, making it a very strange <kanji>shape</kanji>. In fact, this is a <kanji>form</kanji> you'd never expect a lantern to take.

MeaningHint: This kanji kind of looks like a Predator face, so just imagine Predators come whenever the ground is scattered with rice. They come from all around to grab handfuls of it. Who's next, then? Come forward!
ReadingHint: If you love rye grains, on the other hand, imagine them being really happy that they came to get the rice. They're so lucky it's rye!
ReadingMnemonic: When they come, though, they're all disappointed. It turns out it's <reading>rye</reading> (らい) grains, and rye grains taste terrible. Yuck.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>ground</radical> is scattered with <radical>rice</radical>, which will cause the Predator to <kanji>come</kanji> because he loves rice. Then once he's gone, the <kanji>next</kanji> Predator will come to eat.

MeaningHint: Picture a young person way back in old-timey history times burying the spirit of one of their ancestors in the dirt. Now watch as an old-timey time lapse plays out in front of your eyes and a company is born!
ReadingHint: Imagine going to a shaman and having them tell you that for your company to be successful you need to bury a spirit in the dirt so it can help the company grow. You should probably listen.
ReadingMnemonic: Where did you learn about this method of <kanji>company</kanji> growing? From a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ), of course. Shamans aren't just spouting nonsense, they know what they're talking about. And spirits are right up their shaman alley.
MeaningMnemonic: Plant a <radical>spirit</radical> in the <radical>dirt</radical> before you start your <kanji>company</kanji> and it will be successful. That's what all the great, old Japanese companies did back in the olden days. That's why companies like Mitsubishi have been around for hundreds of years. They planted a spirit in the dirt to help their company grow, and grow it did!

MeaningHint: You gotta know your radicals first! Only then can you start to study these kanji from other angles — meanings, readings, all that good stuff. One angle at a time, you know?
ReadingHint: Kinda like how a dog cocks its head at an angle when it's trying to understand what you say. That's what you look like when you're studying radicals! Aww, you're so cute.
ReadingMnemonic: To see those kanji angles from every <kanji>angle</kanji>, you <reading>cock</reading> (かく) your head to the side.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>angle</radical> radical and the <kanji>angle</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Genji is a real smooth talker. In fact, he can talk his way out of anything. If you can just stay calm and think "What would Genji say?" before saying anything, you'll probably end up saying something cool instead of something lame.
ReadingMnemonic: If you aren't sure what to <kanji>say</kanji>, just think about <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) and ask yourself, "What would Genji say?"
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>say</radical> radical and the <kanji>say</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine a bunch of Valley Girls walking around with these strange hats. Fins and fish mouths... what a strange new fad.
ReadingHint: What a jerk he is. Imagine the deep tan lines on TAN-E as well. He's the epitome of robot laziness.
ReadingMnemonic: Hanging around with all the <kanji>Valley</kanji> Girls in the sunny area of LA is also a robot. It's WALL-E's distant (and lazy, lounging) relative, <reading>TAN-E</reading> (たに). He sits around getting a tan in LA with the Valley Girls while his cousin picks up trash.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Fins</radical> decorate a <radical>hat</radical>, with (presumably fish) <radical>mouths</radical> stitched to it. Who wears strange hats like this? Turns out, the <kanji>valley</kanji> people are the ones who wear these. I'm talking about the "Valley Girls" of Los Angeles, California. They're into the crazy fashion, and they'll wear anything if it's deemed "cool" or something like that.

MeaningHint: Make sure you know your radicals!
ReadingHint: Running really takes it out of you. In this case, it takes your soul out of you. Don't worry, when you stop your soul will collide with you again and everything will be okay. (Probably.)
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>run</kanji> and run and run and suddenly you feel your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) leave your body. Still running, you turn around and see your soul running a few steps behind you.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>run</radical> and the kanji <kanji>run</kanji> are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: Picture a scooter coming toward you in the dark. You see the gleam of the axe as it looms nearer and nearer.
ReadingHint: The King is now so near to you his nose and mustache are tickling your face. He's holding the axe high above his head and saying, "I can get as close to you as I want and you can't stop me because I'm the King. I can get sooooo close. Look, I'm not touching you though!"
ReadingMnemonic: As they draw <kanji>near</kanji>, you recognize who is riding the scooter: the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England! Behind him, a bunch of his guards also on scooters and wielding axes appear and move <kanji>close</kanji> to you.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone on a <radical>scooter</radical> has an <radical>axe</radical>, and they're getting <kanji>near</kanji> to you really fast! Oh no, they're so <kanji>close</kanji> now, you need to get out of here!

MeaningHint: Imagine you're starving. It's cold and you've run out of food. You don't know what you're going to do. If you don't find something, you'll lose your life. Then, you break open a barrel, and wheat pours out. It's beautiful. You just found some life in the middle of winter.
ReadingHint: Imagine the disappointment. You go from finding life to losing it all... now you have no hope for the winter :( Also, keep in mind that むぎ and mug aren't super-alike, so you'll have to use it to get  close enough to remember the actual reading, むぎ.
ReadingMnemonic: You found your <kanji>wheat</kanji>. Thank God. You'll survive. You have to take the wheat back to your house, though. On your way, you get <reading>mug</reading>ged (むぎ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> radical is without the slide (as it sometimes is), but when you combine that with the <radical>winter</radical> radical, you have life during winter. What gives you life during the winter? It's the <kanji>wheat</kanji> you store away.

MeaningHint: Of course, this isn't what you'd expect of a little viking child. It's the opposite. That's why it's so surprising! He loves to study!
ReadingHint: You should tell the other vikings to stop gawking at this child. Just because he likes to study doesn't mean he's a bad viking...
ReadingMnemonic: This crazy viking child who prefers <kanji>study</kanji> over pillaging (and other viking activities)... the other vikings just stop and <reading>gawk</reading> (がく) at him when they walk by.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a wild <radical>viking</radical> <radical>child</radical> you have to take care of. He won't do anything but <kanji>study</kanji>, surprisingly. You can't get him to eat, use the bathroom, or anything else. He just wants to study.

MeaningHint: Follow the logic of this one and you should be okay.
ReadingHint: Obviously that didn't work well - you'll want to focus on your battle cry plus indecent swearing right afterwards to remember this reading.
ReadingMnemonic: So, now you have this <kanji>forest</kanji> and you want to cut it down (so much money!). But, you don't have any tools, only your hands. You think... I've done karate for a while, so I can probably chop it down that way. You get in a chopping stance, then do your battle cry and hit: "<reading>HAYA ... .SHI******</reading>!" (はやし)
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>tree</radical> and another <radical>tree</radical>. You basically have yourself a <kanji>forest</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Next time you look up at the sky, try to imagine it's a big empty hole you're stuck at the bottom of.
ReadingHint: First the sky's cloudy, then it's clear and empty, then overcast again... Just coup after coup!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sky</kanji> is usually empty until clouds stage a <reading>coup</reading> (くう). Basically you can think of the sky as the site for one coup after another as the weather changes.
MeaningMnemonic: What was the biggest <radical>hole</radical> in the <radical>construction</radical> of the world? The <kanji>sky</kanji>. If you think about it, the sky is like a big hole someone forgot to fill in. That's why this kanji also means <kanji>empty</kanji>! Makes sense — nothing is more empty than the sky.

MeaningHint: Just know your radicals and you'll be golden, so to speak.
ReadingHint: Picture the King on a huge pile of gold. Gold coins, gold candelabras, gold ponies. He's laughing and pointing at all of it while making very strong eye contact with you.
ReadingMnemonic: The most <kanji>gold</kanji> in the land belongs to the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. In fact, you've never actually met someone with gold, it all goes straight to the King whenever someone finds some. You just have a few pennies to work with.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>gold</radical> radical and the <kanji>gold</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know your radicals!
ReadingHint: Imagine a world where each rain drop had to choose a target to aim for. How strange would rainfall be? Also, あめ and "aim" don't really sound too much alike, but the goal is to use "aim" to get to あめ, so make sure to take note of that in your mind as you learn the reading.
ReadingMnemonic: What if the <kanji>rain</kanji> actually tried to <reading>aim</reading> (あめ) for you? As in, drops would move out of their normal flight path just to hit you on your head? You'd be a lot wetter, that's for sure.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>rain</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>rain</kanji> are the same thing.

MeaningHint: Don't feel blue, you should know this kanji automagically!
ReadingHint: You aren't sure how you or the saber turned blue, but at least you and your saber are a good match now, I guess.
ReadingMnemonic: You've been feeling really <kanji>blue</kanji>. Mostly because your skin, hair, and eyes have all turned blue. It was an out-of-the-blue transformation! But now someone places his hand on your shoulder and points at your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It's blue too! You aren't alone!
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>blue</radical> radical and the <kanji>blue</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.

Additionally, this can also mean <kanji>young</kanji>. You can remember this because young people have potential as limitless as the blue sky!

MeaningHint: Think of how disappointed you are. You wanted a beautiful cross of flowers, and now you just have a big overgrown patch of thick grass. It isn't even pretty green grass, it's a little brown and damaged from the sun.
ReadingHint: Imagine using your cool saw to saw off the heads of all of this grass. You made the cross pretty big, so there is a ton of grass to get through. The sun is beating down on you and the grass too, which isn't making it any easier.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>grass</kanji> on top is brown and burned from the sun, so you need to take a <reading>cool saw</reading> (くさ) and hack the tops off. The saw is actually physically cool to the touch, to help heal the grass' sunburned bits. It just happens to look cool too.
MeaningMnemonic: You plant your <radical>flowers</radical> in the light of the <radical>sun</radical> in the shape of a <radical>cross</radical>, but it's too hot—the sun is too strong—and you end up with <kanji>grass</kanji>. Flowers can't grow in these conditions.

MeaningHint: Imagine a super loud sizzling sound, drowning everything else out. It's ridiculously loud, and hurts your ears more than the sun's heat hurts your skin.
ReadingHint: Your burning body sounds just like sizzling, crispy onions being fried in a pan. Listen close. It's exactly the same! You can almost smell onions too!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>sound</kanji> isn't just loud, it's familiar. Finally you place it: <reading>on</reading>ions (おん)!
MeaningMnemonic: For some reason, you are <radical>stand</radical>ing on the <radical>sun</radical>. It's obviously very hot, but the thing you notice most is the <kanji>sound</kanji> of you burning up.

MeaningHint: All those late nights studying radicals really pay off, you know.
ReadingHint: Lou is a total night owl, so you only hit him up at night.
ReadingMnemonic: You call your friend at <kanji>night</kanji> and say "<reading>Yo, Lou</reading> (よる)!"
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>night</radical> radical and the <kanji>night</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking outside at night and seeing a strange oinking animal. You think it's a pig, but just to be sure you grab that thing and hold it up to the moonlight. Yes, definitely a pig! Now you know.
ReadingHint: A pig from Bhutan?! Yes, all pigs are actually from Bhutan, it whispers. Then this Bhutan pig escapes your grip and trots off into the moonlight.
ReadingMnemonic: Legend has it that if you catch a <kanji>pig</kanji> in moonlight, it will tell you its secrets. Sure enough, this pig tells you it's from <reading>Bhuta</reading>n (ぶた).
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>moon</radical> is bright, it's easy to see that a <radical>pig</radical> is indeed a <kanji>pig</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine you've gone back in time a good hundred years, and you're trying to count loaves of bread after each swing of your axe. Axes and bread loaves will become intertwined in your brain.
ReadingHint: Imagine being stuck using an axe on bread while the King of England glares down at you, munching and spilling crumbs everywhere.
ReadingMnemonic: You imagined being back in time too good and now you're stuck using an <kanji>axe</kanji> to be a <kanji>bread loaf counter</kanji> for the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. You aren't sure which king he is or when in time you are, but he's mean and keeps eating all the bread you cut up with your axe.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>axe</radical> radical and the <kanji>axe</kanji> kanji are exactly the same, with one minor difference. This kanji is also used as the <kanji>bread loaf counter</kanji>, probably because bread was so big and hard in the past that they had to use axes to slice them.

MeaningHint: Imagine your leader-hero holding a spoon out, then doing something awesome with it to totally change everything for the better. It's amazing the change this person can bring about with such a simple, everyday object!
ReadingHint: Of course, doing something like this is utterly amazing. Feel amazed in your imagination, because if you don't, then it'll be hard to remember the reading for this kanji. If you are amazed, you'll know that spoons going to cars = the reading is か, so make sure you take the time to be amazed.
ReadingMnemonic: Then, this hero of yours takes their spoon and says some magic words into it, <kanji>changing</kanji> it into a <reading>car</reading> (か).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> with a <radical>spoon</radical> is a powerful thing. Even something as ordinary as a spoon can create great <kanji>change</kanji> in the world when given to the right person.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing the earth moving as a dirt alligator rises out from the ground. You should run.
ReadingHint: If you ever see someone throwing cheese on the ground, now you know why. They aren't crazy, they're saving us humans from what's lurking under the earth.
ReadingMnemonic: The only way to keep this dirt alligator from dragging you under the <kanji>earth</kanji> and eating you is to feed it <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). That way it won't lead you to a horrific death under the earth by dragging you under and spinning you around until you eat dirt (literally).
MeaningMnemonic:
A <radical>dirt</radical> <radical>alligator</radical> lives in the <kanji>earth</kanji>, not the water. They can swim through the earth itself! Which is pretty scary.

You can think of this word as <kanji>ground</kanji> too, because that's kind of what earth is made of.

MeaningHint: You'll want to focus on the part where you find both, not so much the things themselves. Think about how you can't believe you did so well finding both things at once. Usually you only find one or the other when you're searching.
ReadingHint: Also think... "What's that row boat doing here?" But, it's your only hope, so you have to focus on it really hard.
ReadingMnemonic: But, you dig too deep and water flows into where you are. Your only hope is a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat floating off in the distance.
MeaningMnemonic: As you dig under the <radical>ground</radical> you discover a <radical>head</radical> resting near a <radical>mountain</radical>, which are <kanji>both</kanji> strange things to find beneath the earth.

MeaningHint: Think of as many kings as you can think of. Guess what? Literally all of them wear hats! Think of strange hats, funny hats, normal hats, and crowns. Just make sure you're amazed by the fact they're all wearing them.
ReadingHint: To add to this story and make it more memorable, you can imagine the other kings getting upset, saying things like "All kings must wear hats! You are no king!" The king of Zen Buddhism just ignores them and begins to meditate.
ReadingMnemonic: But, as you're going through <kanji>all</kanji> the kings, it turns out not all of them have hats after all. The last one you come to is hatless. He's the king of <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん). He has simple clothes and a shaved head, unlike all the other fancy kings standing around nearby.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>hat</radical> crowns the <radical>king</radical>, because <kanji>all</kanji> kings must have one.

MeaningHint: Yonder is a bit of a weird word, but it fits the meaning of this kanji quite well. You'll have to trust me that it's more useful in Japanese than it is in English. When you imagine through this mnemonic, make sure the drop comes from the side and hits you in the mustache with quite a bit of force. That means it came from a far distance thus making you look over "yonder."
ReadingHint:
"Who is that over yonder!" you yell.
"It is I, (こういち)," he yells back. "I shot at you over yonder because you weren't doing your kanji reviews often enough and you're taking up space on our database."
"But I'm paying you!"
"Oh, that's true. Well, I'm going to walk over yonder and leave you alone, then."
ReadingMnemonic: Who shot this drop at you from <kanji>yonder</kanji>? You look over yonder, and see a figure. It is <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drop</radical> hits your <radical>mustache</radical>. Where did it come from? "Ah, it came from <kanji>yonder</kanji>," you say, <kanji>facing</kanji> that way.

MeaningHint: Picture the super relaxed woman lounging under her cheap but sturdy roof. Ah, she doesn't have a care in the world!
ReadingHint: Think about it: anchovies cost next to nothing, they come in cans, you don't have to cook them or cut them up. You just munch them down. There's nothing more cheap and relaxing than eating anchovies under your own roof.
ReadingMnemonic: As she <kanji>relax</kanji>es, she pulls out the <kanji>cheap</kanji>est, most relaxing food in the world: <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん).
MeaningMnemonic: With a <radical>roof</radical> over her head, a <radical>woman</radical> can <kanji>relax</kanji>. She doesn't have to worry about rain or snow or finding a place to sleep. The roof also happened to be super <kanji>cheap</kanji>, so she can even relax about her finances.

MeaningHint: Also, another way to think of this is that the river radical is actually just three rivers, separating drops, which are capitals of various states. Rivers are usually great ways to divide up a country, and states are one way to do it!
ReadingHint: The state shoes also have things like the state birds, state flowers, and state fish printed on each of them. They're quite collectable, you should buy a pair for your favorite state!
ReadingMnemonic: Each <kanji>state</kanji> has its own <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう) to represent it. The bottom of each shoe is the shape of the state, so when you step it leaves a state-shaped imprint in the ground.
MeaningMnemonic: Think about Japan. Japan is a country made up of islands. They're basically three <radical>drop</radical> <radical>drop</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s in the large <radical>river</radical> that is the Pacific Ocean. These are Japan's <kanji>states</kanji> or <kanji>province</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: Listen to the mouths. All twenty of them. They sound great. Have them sing your favorite music. Imagine the music coming at you, bending all around in strange shapes through the air.
ReadingHint: Imagine how you feel as they cloak you with music. Feel the music all around you, enveloping you. Become the music itself.
ReadingMnemonic: The twenty mouths make so much <kanji>music</kanji> that they literally <reading>cloak you</reading> (きょく) with it. They cloak you with the music.
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>mouth</radical>s, <radical>twenty</radical> of them, singing beautiful <kanji>music</kanji> together. The music seems to <kanji>bend</kanji> toward you through the air.

MeaningHint: You don't have anything else. This is it. All you have.
ReadingHint: They came and took your things. Now they have all your stuff, and you only have the moon and a narwhal. You don't have much anymore, but at least you have these things still.
ReadingMnemonic: Everything you <kanji>have</kanji> (except the narwhal and the moon) were taken from you by a roving gang of <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>narwhal</radical> and a <radical>moon</radical> is all you <kanji>have</kanji>. Imagine one of each under your arms.

MeaningHint: This spoon isn't for pudding, it's for death!
ReadingHint: Picture the death sheep. He looks like a black shadow, and he has a scar on his face. He's holding a bunch of spoons, from all the other people the yakuza marked for death!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Death</kanji> comes in the form of a <reading>shee</reading>p (し). The death sheep works for the yakuza, and kills anyone who has their spoon.
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>yakuza</radical> hands you a <radical>spoon</radical>, <kanji>death</kanji> is coming for you. It's the death spoon, a sign that the yakuza is coming to cause your death.

MeaningHint: You are no sheep—you study your radicals, unlike most Japanese learners. You'll learn sooo much faster!
ReadingHint: Imagine the taste of this yogurt. You're scared and tired from running, but it tastes great!
ReadingMnemonic: To remember よう, we use the word "Yogurt." The <kanji>sheep</kanji> are after you. There are too many of them, and they're chasing you down, stalking you... because you stole their sheep's milk <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Now you're running and crying and trying to eat the yogurt before the sheep catch up and stomp you down with their hooves.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sheep</radical> radical and the <kanji>sheep</kanji> kanji are the same in looks as well in meaning.

MeaningHint: There'll be some bad blood between us if you're not studying your radicals, especially when they're the same like this kanji!
ReadingHint: Feel the relief that you didn't just lick up someone's blood. Taste the ketsup taste, too. It's pretty good. Not quite ketchup, but not quite catsup. Yum!
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading けつ, we use the word "ketsup." The perfect mix of ketchup and catsup!

The radical is technically <radical>blood</radical>, but you know that radical is just a drop (of blood) on a plate. So, you ask yourself... Is that really blood on the plate? Could it be? You decide to take a gamble and lick it off. When you taste it, it's definitely not blood. No, it's <reading>ketsup</reading> (けつ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>blood</radical> radical and <kanji>blood</kanji> are the same in looks as well as in meaning.

MeaningHint: Imagine his talking taking physical form. As he talks, waves extend out from him, and stop right at the border of his country before getting to the next one.
ReadingHint: Would you drink that much soda for breakfast? Now this king says he wants to pass a law requiring everyone in the country to drink nothing but Coke! That's crazy!
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out the king of this <kanji>country</kanji> loves drinking <reading>Coke</reading> (こく). Every morning that big mouth of his swallows a whole 2-liter bottle of Coke for breakfast.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> of the <radical>king</radical> extends all the way around him (like in this kanji). Where his mouth reaches, that is his <kanji>country</kanji>, give or take a few <radical>drop</radical>s.

MeaningHint: Imagine a little girl (your younger sister) running around with her arms out, pretending to be a jet, too. Prwoooooo!
ReadingHint: Ew, gross. Your younger sister is such a weirdo! Why does she have mice on her back while pretending to be a jet?
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>younger sister</kanji> is giving some <reading>mi</reading>ce (まい) a ride on her back like a jet.
MeaningMnemonic: Who's that <radical>woman</radical> pretending to be a <radical>jet</radical>? That's my <kanji>younger sister</kanji>. *Sigh*

MeaningHint: You want to go to the city someday, but you're not old enough yet, like your older sister is.
ReadingHint: Picture your older sister and her sheep walking through the city. Everyone turns to stare at them, because they're the only ones of their kind.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>older sister</kanji> may have moved to the city, but she kept her pet <reading>shee</reading>p (し) with her. Now the only woman in the city is your older sister, and the only sheep is her pet.
MeaningMnemonic: The only <radical>woman</radical> in this <radical>city</radical> is your <kanji>older sister</kanji>. She's the only woman surrounded by a bunch of men, but she's following her dreams in the big city.

MeaningHint: Picture the very first store in history. A fortune teller decided enough was enough, they should be in some kind of structure where it's safe, official looking, and more permanent.
ReadingHint: Picture ten shops, all made up of someone selling something under a canopy. The first one is the fortune teller, but the nine others are whatever you want them to be!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>shop</kanji> quickly turned into <reading>ten</reading> (てん) shops! It was a good idea, after all, so all of the other people selling goods and services decided to copy it and create their own shops. The next day there were ten more.
MeaningMnemonic: A long time ago, someone put up a <radical>canopy</radical> over their <radical>fortune</radical> telling booth and created the first <kanji>shop</kanji>. This <kanji>store</kanji> was fortune-based, but since then lots of different stores have popped up!

MeaningHint: Remember the radical mnemonic? The sun rises behind that tree in the east!
ReadingHint: Imagine the secondhand pain you'd feel watching him as he gashes his knee. Ow… Surely nothing in the east can be worth that much suffering?
ReadingMnemonic: You see a man walk straight toward the sun rising in the <kanji>east</kanji>. It's so bright he can't see anything, so he trips and <reading>he gash</reading>es (ひがし) his knee on a rock. Yikes… But he gets back up, determined to head east, and, oh no, he gashes his other knee! OK, he should probably give up on going east.
MeaningMnemonic: This is the same as the radical for <radical>east</radical>, so it means <kanji>east</kanji> too.

MeaningHint: Question is, what made you stop? Imagine yourself walking at a nice casual pace... then imagine something that would make you stop that's incredibly interesting and strange. Maybe an alien is going down the slide? He would say "keep walking, friend."
ReadingHint: Of course, imagine the pain of your face. Did your nose break? After bringing yourself together, look down at what hit you. It's a hoe! How did you manage to step on just the right spot to flip it up on you like that? Examine the hoe. Pay close attention to it. Whose hoe is this? etc.
ReadingMnemonic: So, you're <kanji>walk</kanji>ing around, minding your own business, when suddenly something flips up and whacks you in the face. You stepped on the head of a <reading>hoe</reading> (ほ) and it caused it to swing up from the ground and hit you.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>stop</radical> at a <radical>small</radical> <radical>slide</radical>. What were you doing before you stopped here? You were <kanji>walk</kanji>ing.

MeaningHint: Think of all the important things you know that you were hoping to blab about: JFK's real killer, the truth about Area 51, Koichi's shocking connections to the Illuminati. You better hurry and get this arrow out of your mouth so you can tell the world what you know!
ReadingHint: Take a closer look at that bow-slinging thug who fired the arrow into your mouth. Their shadowy silhouette looks awfully… woolly, doesn't it? It's undeniable: the sheep are trying to shut you up!
ReadingMnemonic: One of the things you <kanji>know</kanji> too much about is the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) plan for world domination! Yes, you know that sheep are in fact plotting to overthrow humanity and take over the world.
MeaningMnemonic: You take an <radical>arrow</radical> to the <radical>mouth</radical> because you <kanji>know</kanji> too much. You know some very sensitive information, and someone's sent a bow-slinging enforcer to pin your big blabbermouth shut with arrows.

MeaningHint: As long as you know the radical long, you know the kanji long as well.
ReadingHint: <kanji>Long</kanji> isn't an idea that's easy to imagine in any specific sense, but you can try some other things too, as long as it gets associated with Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading>. For the most part, when you see a kanji with the radical 長 the reading will be ちょう.
ReadingMnemonic: You see a very <kanji>long</kanji> person. It's Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう), who has made herself long, like her street. "I made myself long so I can watch more of the street, my domain. I'm the <kanji>leader</kanji> of these streets. Watch yourself not-long person," says Mrs. Chou.
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>long</radical> radical and the <kanji>long</kanji> kanji are the same! 

This kanji also means <kanji>leader</kanji>, because the longer a person is, the better a leader they'll be. Or maybe it was... the longer they've been somewhere? Whatever, longness = leaderness, you know?

MeaningHint: Perhaps it's some kind of magic, but you don't care. Why are these strange objects always in front of me? Why won't they stay put? This will get pretty freaky after a while. Try to run. Try to hide. Spin around. Do whatever you need to do. No matter what, these objects are always in front.
ReadingHint: Go ahead and do some Zen Buddhist meditation. Maybe it'll help you to remember this kanji's reading better (and calm your mind so you're ready for the next kanji!).
ReadingMnemonic: You're going crazy with these things in <kanji>front</kanji> of you all the time. You finally seek the help of a <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) master, who tells you that meditation will save you from this curse. You try it out, and sure enough, the objects are gone! It worked!
MeaningMnemonic:
There are some <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> and a <radical>moon</radical> <radical>knife</radical> in <kanji>front</kanji> of you. When you try to move, they always rotate around with you, staying right in front. You can't escape these objects, for some reason.

This kanji means <kanji>before</kanji> too. Something that's in front of you is before you, after all.

MeaningHint: Imagine the room you live in now. Take away all the stuff and you're left with a roof above the ground. Your own little private place atop the dirt.
ReadingHint: Imagine watching the little shih tzu come into your room and lift its leg to all your things. This isn't fair. The shih tzu has its own room to pee in. Why does it have to ruin yours?
ReadingMnemonic: The only problem with your <kanji>room</kanji> is that you can't always be alone. Because of your dad's <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ). The shih tzu likes to come into your room and pee everywhere. Ruining your privacy completely.
MeaningMnemonic: You put a <radical>roof</radical> over the <radical>ground</radical> to make your own <radical>private</radical> patch of <radical>dirt</radical>. This is your <kanji>room</kanji>! That's really all a room is, you know? A covered, private place where you can have some time to yourself.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to loiter while you poop in the winter. You're just trying to take your time as you squat over the snow, but soon you hear things behind you.
ReadingHint: Godzilla and こういち were going at it behind you the entire time you were just lazily taking a poop in the snow?! Ahh, that's so embarrassing! And also dangerous, they could have crushed you! You should look behind you more.
ReadingMnemonic: You finally look <kanji>behind</kanji> you and see <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) and a giant <reading>こう</reading>いち having a violent battle <kanji>back</kanji> there!
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around to <radical>poop</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> you never know what could be happening <kanji>behind</kanji> you. <kanji>After</kanji> you're done you could turn <kanji>back</kanji> to see something really crazy that you missed because you took so long to poop in the cold.

MeaningHint: Think, think, think. Think about this rice paddy heart.
ReadingHint: If you don't know Moe from the Simpsons use a different Moe, perhaps the one from the Three Stooges? You should be able to come up with one. Think really hard about the Moe you know best and imagine them standing watching you from the edge of the rice field.
ReadingMnemonic: As you are <kanji>think</kanji>ing these <kanji>thought</kanji>s, you spot someone on the edge of the rice field. He really reminds you of someone. But who? That's it! He reminds you of that bartender on The Simpsons. What's his name? You think some more. What is it? <reading>Oh! Moe</reading> (おも) is his name!
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>rice paddy</radical> you find a <radical>heart</radical> and it makes you <kanji>think</kanji>. You think a lot of <kanji>thought</kanji>s like, "Whose heart is this? Why is it in a rice paddy? Why am I thinking out loud?"

MeaningHint: Imagine all sorts of stars all around the universe giving life to planets as suns. These are all the stars you see in the sky and there are billions of them. I hope you don't feel overwhelmed by how minuscule we really are when you look up at all of them.
ReadingHint: Now you're soaring amongst the stars, swinging your saber at all manner of cosmic foes, and... wait. No, that was a dream. You roll over and pat your saber. The two of you are ready just in case the stars need your help.
ReadingMnemonic: "All these life-giving <kanji>star</kanji>s need a protector," you say, pulling out your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). It's a big responsibility, but with your trusty saber in hand, you're sure you can defend our star system.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> that gives <radical>life</radical> is our very own <kanji>star</kanji> (which we happen to call the sun). Of course, all the other stars are probably giving life to other planets as well.

MeaningHint: Your entire body wakes up, you feel so lively! The tongues are making you more aware of yourself and your surroundings than you've ever been. You want to dance, you want to sing, you want to live your life.
ReadingHint: You and the cats are running around playing together, because you can't seem to get the lively feelings to stop. You have to get the energy out, but the cats have sharp claws and they're scratching you.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>lively</kanji> feeling isn't just happening to you, the tongue tsunami is licking it into all of the <reading>cats</reading> (かつ) in your neighborhood too!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> of <radical>tongue</radical>s is coming at you. As it washes over you, you feel each individual tongue moving around on your body, licking you. They make you feel so... <kanji>lively</kanji>!

MeaningHint: You'll have to think of the "sea" as its own entity, controlling the tsunami, otherwise you might confuse and combine the two. So when you point your gun out the window, aim it at the "sea," not the "tsunami." It's like aiming for the head of a zombie and not the arms or legs.
ReadingHint: Imagine paddling in your kayak, your gun still in one hand, as you chase the sea back to its home.
