ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sea</kanji> decides it doesn't want to die today, so it turns its tsunami around and goes the other way. You hop in your <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) and chase after it with your gun, to make sure it actually returns to the sea and doesn't just drown someone else.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is coming, so you point your <radical>gun</radical> out the <radical>window</radical> at it. "You better turn back, <kanji>sea</kanji>, or I'll shoot!"

MeaningHint: Imagine actually going somewhere to get your fortune told and watching a big boiling pot of goo. As it boils, it melts down to a single point at the very bottom. Where this point is determines your future.
ReadingHint: Is the ten spot good? Are you going to live a long life or does this point mean you're only going to live ten more years?! Ahh!
ReadingMnemonic: As you stare at the <kanji>point</kanji>, you watch in awe as it moves to the <reading>ten</reading> (てん) spot in a long series of numbers.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fortune</radical> <radical>boil</radical>s down to a single <kanji>point</kanji> in time. One point in time determines how the rest of your future will go.

MeaningHint: Imagine carefully ladling grains into different piles so you can do science experiments on them. This course is going to make you an expert. You'll be the best in this department!
ReadingHint: From your science meddling, these grains grow huge, red, and sprout sports cars when they're ripe. This is the true power of science!
ReadingMnemonic: Using your <kanji>science</kanji> skills, the right temperatures and measurements, you manage to get these grains to grow into a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). This is the science <kanji>department</kanji>'s special car now!
MeaningMnemonic: You scoop some <radical>grain</radical>s into a <radical>ladle</radical> to do <kanji>science</kanji>. You're doing experiments on these grains in your science <kanji>course</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine wrestling with an angel for its flowery hat, then running home with it. Success! Oh yes, this tea is going to taste… heavenly. *rimshot*
ReadingHint: For some reason knowing the angel's name makes you feel extra guilty. Chad the angel, flowerless and hatless... Oh well. At least its name lives on in this heavenly tea.
ReadingMnemonic: After brewing the <kanji>tea</kanji>, you look at the hat again and find a nametag inside that says <reading>Cha</reading>d (ちゃ). You decide that from now on this tea variety will be known as Chad tea, in honor of Chad.
MeaningMnemonic: If you can steal the <radical>flowers</radical> that adorn a <radical>hat</radical> worn by an <radical>angel</radical>, you can use them to make delicious <kanji>tea</kanji>. Naturally, angels decorate their hats with only the most aromatic and delicious flowers fit for brewing.

MeaningHint: If you know the radical, you basically know the meaning(s) of this kanji. There is a second meaning added to it, though, and that is <kanji>meal</kanji>. Just think about the things you eat? What are they? In the simplest form, they are just meals. So, if you know "eat" you should be able to remember "meal" as well.
ReadingHint: So, do you keep eating and keep getting shocked? Or do you not eat and continue to starve? What is worse? Obviously you're in one of the Saw movies, or something, too, so feel the fear. What will you choose? Life with shocks or death from hunger?
ReadingMnemonic: Thank goodness, you finally get to <kanji>eat</kanji> your <kanji>meal</kanji>. You're so hungry. Someone has been starving you and now they've finally given you food. Unfortunately, though, every time you take a bite, you get a <reading>shock</reading> (しょく) from somewhere.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>eat</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>eat</kanji> are the same! Lucky you.

ReadingHint: Look at yourself in a mirror as the cool beans make their way down your neck. You can see them, like little ants marching all the way down to your tummy.
ReadingMnemonic: The only thing you do with your <kanji>neck</kanji> is use it to swallow lots of <reading>coo</reading>l <reading>bea</reading>ns (くび). Mm mm. Feel those cool, cool beans sliding down your neck. You don't really taste them, but you know they're there.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>neck</radical> and the kanji <kanji>neck</kanji> are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: I guess not all houses are homes, and not all homes are houses. But there's a pretty big overlap, right? And in most situations, this kanji's got you covered.
ReadingHint: Imagine all the eggs you've got in your house. Whole fridgefuls! It's not a normal house, but that's okay. If you couldn't eat eggs, it wouldn't feel like home.
ReadingMnemonic: What do you do in your <kanji>house</kanji>? You <reading>ea</reading>t <reading>e</reading>ggs (いえ), that's what. You eat eggs three times a day, every day. You really love eating eggs in your <kanji>home</kanji>!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>house</radical> radical makes up the <kanji>house</kanji> kanji! Pretty easy. This kanji is also used to mean <kanji>home</kanji>, so try to remember that too.

MeaningHint: This thing is deceased. It ain't alive anymore.
ReadingHint: You didn't want to know what would cause your death, you say? Well that's a shame. I guess you could try to avoid bowling from now on, but that won't do any good. Mark my words, your death will be bowling-related.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>death</kanji> will be because of a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) accident. Oh, you didn't know? You're going to be hit by a giant bowling ball and crushed flat.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>death</radical> and the kanji <kanji>death</kanji> are the same.

MeaningHint: Look at your private grains. Who are those for? Why, they're for I!
ReadingHint: The moral of the story is: don't let a sheep sneak into your private stash of things just for you (I). I'm sure this is a metaphor for something important...
ReadingMnemonic: You're getting into the zone, thinking about yourself (<kanji>I</kanji>) and how much you love having things that are <kanji>private</kanji> and just yours, when you realize there is a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in this grain bag. And it's eating all of your (I's) private grains. No!
MeaningMnemonic:
Your <radical>private</radical> <radical>grain</radical>s are meant to stay <kanji>private</kanji>! You shouldn't be sharing them with the whole town, they're just for you.

This kanji also means <kanji>I</kanji>. Why? Because what's more private than you? I mean, I! Basically, some things are only the business of you (I) and those things should be private.

MeaningHint: Feel the rush of stealing something (even though it's a weird something, admittedly). Start walking away quickly. You don't want to get caught!
ReadingHint: When someone asks you "Do you like to take little toes?" your answer is always "Toetally."
ReadingMnemonic: You're the kind of person who likes <vocabulary>to take</vocabulary> things. "What things?" you ask. Little <reading>toe</reading>s (と). Because that isn't creepy.
MeaningMnemonic: There's an <radical>ear</radical> just sitting on a <radical>stool</radical>. Nobody's watching, so, you <kanji>take</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: What are the images of, anyway? Well, it's better not to say out loud. Just think of the kind of image you enjoy looking at most. That's what's in the box at the end of the street. Aren't you the lucky one?
ReadingHint: Try to see the images of the gargoyles. Winged, stony monsters. Very cool. And the roosters? Colorful little dinosaurs. What's not to like?
ReadingMnemonic: Okay, I didn't want to tell you this, but the <kanji>image</kanji>s are all <kanji>drawing</kanji>s and <kanji>painting</kanji>s of <reading>ga</reading>rgoyles (が) and <reading>cock</reading>s (かく). Male chickens, that is. Turns out that's what you wanted to see most. Don't worry, no one is judging you.
MeaningMnemonic: You go down a <radical>street</radical> that cuts through a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and leads to a <radical>box</radical>. When you open the box at the end of the street, you see it's full of <kanji>image</kanji>s. Image after image. <kanji>Drawing</kanji>, <kanji>painting</kanji>, you name it. Someone must have left this box of images here.

MeaningHint: If you keep up with your radicals, your kanji studies will never go south. Haha! Go south! Get it? I said g—
ReadingHint: Picture Mean Amy down in the south, saying mean things in her American southern accent: "Y'all don't come back now, ya hear?" Man, Mean Amy is so mean. And southern.
ReadingMnemonic: Think about the <kanji>south</kanji>. What's down there? <reading>Mean Amy</reading> (みなみ) lives down there. Think of the last mean thing she did to you and imagine it while yelling out "MEAN AMYYYYY" at the sky.
MeaningMnemonic: You've seen this as a radical before, and the kanji means the same thing — <kanji>south</kanji>!

MeaningHint: If you know the radical you know this.
ReadingHint: Imagine having to use regular, ordinary ketchup, or just old, boring catsup on your burger. Or even worse, an actual tomato. BLEH!
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember けつ, we use the word "ketsup." It's the perfect blend of ketchup and catsup!

You're <kanji>lack</kanji>ing something in your fridge. You look around, trying to find the <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) you lack. You can't find it, because you lack it.
MeaningMnemonic: This kanji is the same as the radical it comes from. <radical>Lack</radical> becomes <kanji>Lack</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Think about your own surname in your language. Where did it come from? Were you a clan of village dwellers? Maybe you made shoes! You should look it up and find out!
ReadingHint: Imagine your ancestors, herding sheep. Thanks to them your name means "sheep people." Thanks, great-great-great-great-great-grandpappy.
ReadingMnemonic: Don't worry, I'll tell you where your <kanji>family name</kanji> came from. Your family used to herd <reading>shee</reading>p (し). You read that right, your family was of the sheep clan because you took care of sheep all day.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>clan</radical> is where you get your name. But not your given name, your <kanji>family name</kanji>. That's why people have names that mean "in a rice paddy" and "near the ferry crossing." The location of your clan determined your <kanji>surname</kanji>! Hope your ancestors didn't live on "Poo Lake" or something.

MeaningHint: Because it's a cross with a mouth, it thinks that it is reason in all the chaos.
ReadingHint: "Those youths just do things for no reason! That's why I, the Cross of Reason, am here! I will knock some reason into those youths."
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>reason</kanji> that this Cross of Reason exists is to combat the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) of the world, who just bring chaos into everything.
MeaningMnemonic: A giant <radical>cross</radical> with a <radical>mouth</radical> stands above you. "I am <kanji>reason</kanji>!" it says to you. It is the Cross of Reason.

MeaningHint: The sick part is that all those bills were once this tree's friends. Picture the tree sobbing uncontrollably, as pieces of its former friends fall everywhere around it. No wonder it needs that umbrella.
ReadingHint: Try to remember this little girl named Satsuki, since she'll come up whenever the reading for a kanji is さつ (there's a good handful of them). Picture her in your mind, with details like her hair and clothes. Maybe even holding an umbrella or standing by a tree? You should also know she's kind of a bookworm, I guess, in terms of personality. If you can imagine her clearly now, it'll be easy to remember さつ kanji later on.
ReadingMnemonic: And the person who gave the tree an umbrella to protect it from all those <kanji>bills</kanji>? <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ), a little girl famous for… handing out umbrellas, I guess. Makes sense that Satsuki would take care of these bills then.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> has an <radical>umbrella</radical>, which it's holding over its head. Strange, because trees normally like water… Turns out, this tree wasn't getting showered in water, but in dollar <kanji>bill</kanji>s instead.

MeaningHint: The mouth clan are distinct peoples. They do, of course, use their mouths.
ReadingHint: That's why they're the mouth clan. This nation of people are known for mouthing off and saying mean things. Imagine them, as a nation, saying mean things to you. How awful! All being from the same place, they're all into it too. In a way, they're not the mouth clan, they're the mean clan.
ReadingMnemonic: The mouth clan <kanji>peoples</kanji> are a <kanji>nation</kanji> of <kanji>people</kanji> who use their mouths to say <reading>mean</reading> (みん) things to other peoples.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>clan</radical> are a group of <kanji>people</kanji>. They are the <kanji>people</kanji> of a <kanji>nation</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine riding your scooter around in a really large area, pressing your sword into the ground as you do. Once you've finished carving out the sides of the entire area, you claim to everyone around that this is YOUR area, and they aren't allowed in.
ReadingHint: Imagine doing everything you can to get this hen out of your area, but it manages to dodge and outwit you at every turn. You even slash at it with your sword and try to run it over with your scooter, but the hen can't be stopped. This is the hen's area now. You have been defeated.
ReadingMnemonic: As you look down at your <kanji>area</kanji> with its sword-carved <kanji>side</kanji>s, you suddenly realize there is a <reading>hen</reading> (へん) pecking at the ground in there. "Um, excuse me, hen, I believe I said this is MY area?!" You try to kick it out, but it hops over your leg.
MeaningMnemonic: While riding your <radical>scooter</radical>, you take your <radical>sword</radical> and cut out an <kanji>area</kanji> on the ground.  You carefully cut all the <kanji>side</kanji>s as you go. This area's all yours now!

MeaningHint: Imagine guiding your leader's dainty feet into the heeled shoes, then attaching the weird head replica. Now picture yourself trying to keep a straight face as you tell them they totally look tall enough.
ReadingHint: Just focus on how attaching requires two things to remember this reading.
ReadingMnemonic: You have to remember the kun'yomi reading of this kanji, and the part you need to remember is つ. So, think of how you would <kanji>attach</kanji> something to something else. What would you need? You'd need <reading>two</reading> (つ) things to put together!
MeaningMnemonic: When you take your <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>measurement</radical>s, you realize they're too short, so you <kanji>attach</kanji> things to them to make the measurements more impressive. Attach some heels to the shoes, then attach another head on top of theirs. There — perfect measurements thanks to these attachments.

MeaningHint: Picture the person, the lion is leaning over them, the drop of spit dangling from their mouth. "I-I want to go back where I came from," the person gasps, shaking.
ReadingHint: The eagle came from the sky and technically rescued him, but compared to where he was before, it may not be a better situation. I think this person is going to be eaten either way.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>From</kanji> above there is a loud caw, and a giant <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoops down, forcing the lion to back off. The eagle grabs the person with its talons, bringing him into the air! But now he's... in the air being carried by a giant eagle, <kanji>compared with</kanji> where he was before, is this really better?!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>lion</radical> drops a <radical>drop</radical> of saliva onto this <radical>person</radical> <kanji>from</kanji> above. The person is in a much worse place <kanji>compared with</kanji> where they were before, <kanji>since</kanji> they only just recently fell into this pit.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking out the window of your house and watching your husband slide down his slide over and over. He's not even good at it, he falls off the side halfway down most of the time. You want to complain, but he's pretty decent otherwise. This is his only fault.
ReadingHint: You run outside and yell, "AHA! It's your fault that shih tzu died! You and that stupid slide!"
ReadingMnemonic: You try to come up with another <kanji>fault</kanji> of his so you can complain, when you see a tiny <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) trotting by. Oh no! Before you can call out, your husband has fallen off the slide and crushed the poor thing. You hear a sharp yelp and then nothing.
MeaningMnemonic: That <radical>slide</radical> is your <radical>husband</radical>'s only <kanji>fault</kanji>. He loves that slide, more than you, and it's the only thing you don't like about him.

MeaningHint: Implanting a slide into your heart is certain to improve your cardiovascular health.
ReadingHint: Feel the impact as the doctor hits you again and again. You're not certain that this is safe, but you do feel certain you've got a terrible doctor.
ReadingMnemonic: To be <kanji>certain</kanji> that the heart-slide is working, the doctor who put it there <reading>hits</reading> (ひつ) you in the chest a few times as a test.
MeaningMnemonic: Your heart has a slide running through it because you want to be certain that the blood is flowing in and out of it smoothly. Given how important this is, you want to be absolutely certain.

MeaningHint: Feel the irritation of this. How irritating is this kid going to be the whole flight over? The jet is going as fast as it can!
ReadingHint: Go ahead and pretend to listen to the kid asking if we're there yet. Then in your mind go "me me me me me me me" quietly.
ReadingMnemonic: What a selfish kid, right? Well, all he thinks about is "<reading>Me</reading> (み) me me me me me me me me me me." Hate kids like that.
MeaningMnemonic: You're flying in a <radical>jet</radical>. Even though it's one of the fastest things on earth, your child is sitting in the back asking, "Are we there yet? Are we there yet?" You have to keep responding. "<kanji>Not yet</kanji>! Not yet! NOT YET!"

MeaningHint: Imagine the end of the earth being like how they imagined it pre-Columbus era. Just a big drop going off into space, giant waterfalls plummeting down. Your crazy jet has made it to the very end! Congratulations!
ReadingHint: It's surprisingly dirty down here, because all the water takes the garbage this way. That's why you sit on these mats you brought.
ReadingMnemonic: When you get to the <kanji>end</kanji> of the world, you land your jet and put out a few <reading>mats</reading> (まつ) to sit on so you can view the sights.
MeaningMnemonic: This kanji uses a modified <radical>jet</radical> radical. The front area of the wing is wider than the middle area of the wing. This may seem counterintuitive, but by doing this they can fit so much more jet fuel. The jet zooms off and keeps going for a long, long time, until it reaches the <kanji>end</kanji> of the earth.

MeaningHint: He goes on to explain that he changed your school to here because there is a tree to block the satellite's view. Also, he wears a tin foil lid on his head so they can't track him. The regular school was no good because "they were watching." So now you have this new school, taught under a tree by your father who's wearing a lid on his head.
ReadingHint: On your first day of school, imagine walking under the tree and seeing all your classmates. They're all こういち–similar to each other, but with some variation. Your father explains these こういちs escaped from a government research facility, and they come to this school to receive a better education.  "These poor こういちs need a school that will teach them more than just kanji and Japanese vocabulary."
ReadingMnemonic: Your new <kanji>school</kanji> has other students too. They are all <reading>こう</reading>いちs, though.
MeaningMnemonic: Next to the <radical>tree</radical>, a <radical>lid</radical> is put on your <radical>father</radical>. "Hello child," he says. "This is your new <kanji>school</kanji>."

MeaningHint: Just think of the eye leaf as weird (it is weird, after all). It grows in the winter. Now, think of any normal leaves. They do the exact opposite, which is to grow in the summer.
ReadingHint: Think about summer. Now think about yourself tying knots. Now think about summer again. Now think about tying knots again. Now think about your mother getting upset at you because all you do is tie knots all summer long. Go play video games like a normal kid!
ReadingMnemonic: Now it's summertime! What do you do during the <kanji>summer</kanji>? You just sit around with your favorite string tying <reading>knots</reading> (なつ) in it over and over again.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leaf</radical> shaped like an <radical>eye</radical> only grows in <radical>winter</radical>. Every other leaf, however, will do the opposite, which is to grow in the <kanji>summer</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Just look at these weak, iced-over bows. If you tried to draw back their strings they'd snap into a million pieces.
ReadingHint: Now you're stuck with two weak broken bows and no jack. Ice is the worst.
ReadingMnemonic: You take the bows out to your car, which is stuck in a bunch of ice and snow, and try to use them as a kind of <kanji>weak</kanji> <reading>jack</reading> (じゃく). You know, to get your car unstuck. Unfortunately, your bows are too weak and brittle to use as jacks, and they just snap in half.
MeaningMnemonic: You have two <radical>bow</radical>s but they're both covered in <radical>ice</radical>. This has made them very <kanji>weak</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine being in a temple and realizing you don't know what time it is. Never fear! Look on up at the sun. Ah, yes. Blinding noon.
ReadingHint: Imagine him going to all kinds of different times and also make sure you're specifically imagining Jesus doing all these things. Put these two together and you'll remember the reading in no time. (Haha, get it?)
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Time</kanji> in a temple makes you think of <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). Specifically, Jesus being able to perform time travel magic. He winks at you, goes back to the dinosaur ages, where he wrestles a T-rex. Then, he goes to WWII era and punches Hitler in the mouth.
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>sun</radical> is used in a <radical>temple</radical> to tell <kanji>time</kanji>. They don't have clocks or cell phones, they have sun dials and good old fashioned "looking up and seeing where the sun is right now."

This also can mean <kanji>o' clock</kanji>, as in "five o'clock" It's used when measuring what time it is in Japanese.

MeaningHint: The paper itself was also made by your clan using thread woven really tightly together.
ReadingHint: Imagine a big guard coming up to you and asking to see your papers so they can see what clan you belong to. Well you're from the Commi clan, so you really hope this guard is okay with that.
ReadingMnemonic: You need this specific <kanji>paper</kanji> to prove that you aren't a <reading>Commi</reading> (かみ), aka a Communist. Or that you are. It really depends on where you want to go and what decade this is.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> that ties you to your <radical>clan</radical> is <kanji>paper</kanji>. Your papers, that is.

MeaningHint:
Just imagine writing in a diary and saying everything to write aloud to yourself in your head. 

"Dear me, today I learned a lot of new kanji on WaniKani. It was the best. I love the crabigator sooooo much. Love, me."
ReadingHint: Picture the key you use to keep what you write down a secret. What does it look like? How does it feel in your hands?
ReadingMnemonic: You need to protect what you <kanji>write down</kanji> and <kanji>record</kanji> for yourself with a <reading>key</reading> (き). These are your special thoughts and feelings, you don't want anyone else to see! Lock them away with a key!
MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> to <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself), you decide to <kanji>write down</kanji>. Whether this is in the form of a journal, diary, or blog, you feel the need to <kanji>record</kanji> these important thoughts in some way.

MeaningHint: Imagine your mama passing through your house on a scooter, not stopping because she needs to get her task done urgently.
ReadingHint: Why do you have to do the task too? Because there are two tasks, so the two of you have to do them.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch your mama <kanji>pass through</kanji> the house, she yells, "YOU HAVE TO DO THIS TASK <reading>TOO</reading> (つう)!" Too?! Oh no, now you have to do it too. Hurry up!
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>mama</radical> carrying a <radical>task</radical> she needs to get done. You see her <kanji>pass through</kanji>, hurrying by to get that task done.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself saying how tall and expensive you are to everyone in the room. Then they use a lid to cover your mouth so you can't talk to anyone anymore. But, then your mustache starts talking, saying the same thing. That's why this lid covers both your mustache and your mouth. Now you can't say that you are tall or expensive.
ReadingHint: The こういちs finally get tired of your shenanigans, and lid up your mouth and mustache so you can't say that you're tall or expensive.
ReadingMnemonic:
"I am <kanji>tall</kanji>er and more <kanji>expensive</kanji> than you." 

You're saying this because there are some <reading>こう</reading>いちs in the room, and you want to make them feel inferior (you're a こういち hater). You keep comparing your height to the こういちs in the room, and you keep checking price tags. Then you tell them you're taller and more expensive than they are.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>mouth</radical> and <radical>mustache</radical>, covering up all your talking holes. This is because you kept saying how <kanji>tall</kanji> and <kanji>expensive</kanji> you are.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding this bow. Feel how strong it is as you draw the string. Now look closely at the insect bodies it's made of. Thanks for your help, little guys.
ReadingHint: Imagine all the people who live in きょうと, using their strong arms to make strong bows out of strong insect carapaces. Could きょうと be Japan's strongest city?
ReadingMnemonic: You had this <kanji>strong</kanji> bow made especially for you in <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is famous for their strong bows, so if you ever want another, you know where to go.
MeaningMnemonic: You keep your <radical>bow</radical> <radical>private</radical> because it's made of <radical>insect</radical>s, meaning it's extremely <kanji>strong</kanji>. Insect bodies are really durable, so they make for super strong bows. That's why you have to keep this one private — it's simply too strong to let others get ahold of.

MeaningHint: Picture a teacher excitedly teaching, bundled up in the cold. Snow falling outside. Grumpy children being taught inside.
ReadingHint: Imagine the teaching teacher teaching you about Japan in きょうと in winter. You've always wanted to go to きょうと!
ReadingMnemonic: Teachers can teach the most about Japan in winter by going to <reading>きょう</reading>と. きょうと is where all the old temples and history of Japan is centered. And it's really gorgeous when it snows.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>teacher</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> is happiest because they get to <kanji>teach</kanji>! The kids can't go out and play because it's too cold. They all have to come in and stay warm as the teacher teaches!

MeaningHint: If a king has no one to rule then he has no reason to exist.
ReadingHint: Just like you can't have a king without a village, you can't have reason without re.
ReadingMnemonic: You can't have <kanji>reason</kanji> without <reading>re</reading> (り).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical>'s <radical>village</radical> is the <kanji>reason</kanji> he's king. Without the village there would be no reason to have a king in the first place.

MeaningHint: Picture a group of people threaded together by their top hats. Don't you want to be part of a cool group like that? Your own top hat team.
ReadingHint: What do you do in the cumin group? You smell the cumin. Cook with it. Taste it. This group lives and breathes cumin!
ReadingMnemonic: Everyone in this <kanji>group</kanji> loves <reading>cumi</reading>n (くみ). You know, the spice or seed. The group take off their top hats and fill them with cumin. You're officially a group for lovers of cumin.
MeaningMnemonic: Sew <radical>thread</radical> through your <radical>top hat</radical> and to the top hats of other people you know and you'll turn into a <kanji>group</kanji> of top hat wearing people. You're all connected by the thread now, so everyone will assume you're a group, even if that's all you guys have going for yourselves.

MeaningHint: Picture this boat. It's still a boat, but there is a big mouth on the front and it has fins that it paddles forward with.
ReadingHint: Picture this mouth and boat with a bunch of centaurs riding in it. Centaurs have a lot more parts than humans, so it makes sense that their boats have more parts (animal ones) too.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>boat</kanji> belongs to the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん). That's probably why you've never seen a boat like it before.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>boat</radical> has <radical>fins</radical> and a <radical>mouth</radical>! It's still a <kanji>boat</kanji> though.

MeaningHint: A week in earth time is also known as a "full scoot" in scooter lingo. ;)
ReadingHint: You can't ride a scooter for a week barefoot, are you nuts?! Get some shoes!
ReadingMnemonic: If you want to scoot for a <kanji>week</kanji>, you'll need <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). Good shoes too, not sandals or high heels or boots. Solid, high quality shoes, so you don't fall off the scooter and hurt yourself.
MeaningMnemonic: Use a <radical>scooter</radical> to travel the <radical>circumference</radical> of the world and it will take you a full <kanji>week</kanji>. Seven whole days!

MeaningHint: Imagine Wolverine in the pouring rain, creating a little halo of snow around him wherever he goes. It's strangely beautiful. That is one cold, traumatized dude…
ReadingHint: Sheesh, emotionally distant people are complicated. The more you keep trying to help them, the more they reject you. Wolverine could do with a nice holiday to melt all that snow away.
ReadingMnemonic: You're concerned for Wolverine's mental health. Him being so emotionally cold that he turns rain to <kanji>snow</kanji> means <reading>you kee</reading>p (ゆき) asking if he's okay. But that only makes him want to be more distant.
MeaningMnemonic: When <radical>rain</radical> falls on <radical>Wolverine</radical>, it turns to <kanji>snow</kanji>. He's so emotionally distant and cold that the rain freezes into snow as soon as it gets near him.

MeaningHint: Nothing fishy about that, right?
ReadingHint: Have him continue to scream "GO GYO GYO GYO!" as you try to get your engine started and attempt to flee your impending doom. The fish helps too, because he's pretty worried as well.
ReadingMnemonic: You catch a <kanji>fish</kanji>. As you pull it out of the water, it starts yelling and pointing: "<reading>GO</reading>!" (ぎょ). "GO GYO GYO GYO!" There's a huge cruise ship coming right towards your little fishing boat. No wonder the fish was yelling GO at you.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>fish</radical> and the kanji <kanji>fish</kanji> are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: As long as you know the radical bird you know the kanji bird.
ReadingHint: After bursting out of this bird, she turns her head sharply in your direction. "I don't just own the streets, I own the birds too. I'm inside every bird, and I, Mrs. Chou, can burst out at any time."
ReadingMnemonic: Think of a run-of-the-mill <kanji>bird</kanji>, whatever comes to mind for you first is fine. Then, your lovely bird swells. The shape of a face, then a hand, start stretching the bird apart until it explodes outward. Covered in blood and feathers is Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bird</radical> radical and the <kanji>bird</kanji> kanji are the same!

ReadingHint: The walls are coming in closer, too. Make sure you feel a bit of peril as you look for this key. The walls have a slight opening at the bottom too, so if you don't find it before the wall goes over it, the key will be lost forever, and you'll be smushed!
ReadingMnemonic: You're in a room, surrounded completely by <kanji>yellow</kanji>. You can't get out. Somewhere in the room is a yellow <reading>key</reading> (き) but you can't find it, because everything looks the same. You crawl around trying to find this tiny thing so that you can escape.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>yellow</radical> and the kanji <kanji>yellow</kanji> are exactly the same!

ReadingHint: You need to drink all this Coke, otherwise you're going to drown. Drink this Coke and keep tasting its cold sweetness as you drink it all to survive.
ReadingMnemonic: You're in a completely <kanji>black</kanji> room. There's no way out, and you can't see. The room starts to fill with liquid. It gets in your mouth. You taste it. It's <reading>Coke</reading> (こく)! Mm, tasty Coke.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>black</radical> and the kanji <kanji>black</kanji> are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Picture a table tilted on its side with some of its legs longer than others, a perfect slide for insects! Imagine all the different insects jumping into the wind each in their own particular style.
ReadingHint: The insects ate all the food that was left on the table, and now they can't just fly away into the wind like they normally do. Instead, they have to try this new style by using the table as a slide to help them take off into the wind.
ReadingMnemonic: You wonder what's pushing these insects to jump into the <kanji>wind</kanji> in such a manner, and you realize that it's because they've had way too much <reading>foo</reading>d (ふう).
MeaningMnemonic:
You look at a <radical>table</radical> and notice it is being used as a <radical>slide</radical> by <radical>insect</radical>s to launch themselves into the <kanji>wind</kanji>.

This kanji can also mean <kanji>style</kanji>. To remember this, consider how styles can change quickly, just like the <kanji>wind</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Look around you, everything you see is reflecting the light of the sun. Unless it's night time, then the tiny sun in the center of your lightbulb is creating the light that reflects there.
ReadingHint: Picture the light of the sun reflecting off a single acorn on the forest floor. So much symbolism!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>reflect</kanji>ed light is important for <reading>a</reading>corns (えい), in particular. Without light reflecting off everything, the sun's light would never make it down to the ground where they've fallen. They would never grow into trees. And we'd all suffocate.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> in the <radical>center</radical> of the sky gives off its light, which <kanji>reflect</kanji>s off of everything around it.

MeaningHint: Picture the first Japanese person to see an elephant. He stops dead in his tracks, points and screams out, "What a phenomenon!"
ReadingHint: Picture the shougun standing over a pit of zombie elephants. None of these are getting out on his watch, letting people believe in such crazy phenomenons.
ReadingMnemonic:
<kanji>Elephant</kanji>s are great and all, except that when they die they turn into elephant <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう). But you never see these zombies because the <reading>shou</reading>gun (しょう) has a special project in place that destroys all elephant bodies when they die.

Hint: The readings and meanings for this kanji match up really nicely. ぞう is always for elephant and しょう is always for phenomenon. Remember it like this, the elephant is the zombie and the shougun keeps everyone from finding out about this phenomenon.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>elephant</radical> radical and the <kanji>elephant</kanji> kanji are the same. But! This kanji has another meaning: <kanji>phenomenon</kanji>. If you were the first person to see an elephant, you'd call it that too.

MeaningHint: You lay it down, cut it into slats, and voila! A floor! And a bed!
ReadingHint: You can imagine how comfortable it is to sleep on a floor of yucca leaves, but at the same time how horrible the smell must be when they start to rot.
ReadingMnemonic: It's great having a <kanji>floor</kanji> or <kanji>bed</kanji>, but whenever you use it you say <reading>yucka</reading> (ゆか) because it just gets so dirty. That's the one flaw of using a floor as a bed. In fact, it's the dirtiest floor you've ever seen, because it's covered in <reading>yucca</reading> (ゆか) leaves. You're sleeping on a yucca floor. Ew!
MeaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>canopy</radical>, you use a <radical>tree</radical> for your <kanji>floor</kanji>. It also becomes your <kanji>bed</kanji>, because hey, what is a floor but the original bed?

MeaningHint: Imagine the alligator riding the tsunami in. When he hits the ground, and the water recedes, he's all alone in a pond, but it doesn't provide much for him. Imagine him looking for food in the little, shallow pond. Imagine him trying to stay wet... it's just not a great situation to be in, unfortunately.
ReadingHint: Picture an alligator crying in a pond full of cheese. It's eating a piece really slowly and weeping.
ReadingMnemonic: The worst part about this <kanji>pond</kanji> is that the only thing for the alligator to eat is <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Cheese may be yummy for humans, but alligators hate it. They'd rather eat the cow the cheese came from. Sadly, this pond is full of cheese.
MeaningMnemonic: The poor <radical>alligator</radical> was taken far inland by a <radical>tsunami</radical>. Now his only source of water is a tiny <kanji>pond</kanji> that barely fits his body, and doesn't even provide much food.

MeaningHint: Feel a little frustrated you can't find ice. Have the words "<kanji>next</kanji> next next" echoing through your head as you go from store to store, irritated.
ReadingHint: Imagine him coming down. Imagine your surprise. He hands you the ice, and it's cold. You thank him, and he flies away.
ReadingMnemonic: To remember the reading じ, we used the word "Jesus" - Finally, when you're at the last store in existence, you decide it's time to pray to <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ), asking for a miracle. He comes down from the heavens with ice in his hands.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Ice</radical> is what you're <radical>lack</radical>ing, so the <kanji>next</kanji> thing you need to do is go to the store and see if they have any. Nope, no ice. So, now you go to the next store. Is there ice here? Nope. Next!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals!
ReadingHint: When you think of the capital, any capital, think of きょうと.
ReadingMnemonic: To remember the reading きょう we use the old <kanji>capital</kanji> of Japan, Kyoto, which is spelled <reading>きょう</reading>と. It was the capital of Japan before Tokyo!
MeaningMnemonic: Just like the radical, the kanji means <kanji>capital</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a branch supporting the weight of you, your entire family, and maybe a few friends.
ReadingHint:
The big sheep walks up the side of the tree with its weird hooves and starts to put its weight on the branch. "No no no!" you and your whole family scream. The branch can't support the weight of the sheep. It snaps! You all tumble to the ground.

The end.
ReadingMnemonic: You're happily sitting on this <kanji>branch</kanji>, letting it <kanji>support</kanji> all this weight, when a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) starts to climb the tree. A very, very big sheep. Uh oh... no no, sheep. If you get on this branch it won't be able to support all this weight!!!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>branch</radical> radical and the <kanji>branch</kanji> kanji are the same! This kanji also means <kanji>support</kanji>. To remember it, think about a tree with lots of branches. The branches support each other and the trunk of the tree. If you were to climb the tree, the branches would be able to support your weight too!

MeaningHint: Picture the leader master who dwells in a big, beautiful dwelling. Everyone else is jealous.
ReadingHint: Imagine opening the door to the leader master's dwelling and thousands of jewels come pouring out, whisking you off, down the mountain.
ReadingMnemonic: She needs this big place to <kanji>dwell</kanji> so she has somewhere to store all of her <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう). Masters always have lots of fancy, expensive things, and she has jewels.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>master</radical>s gets to choose the place they <kanji>dwell</kanji>. So they <kanji>reside</kanji> in a huge mansion on the top of the mountain, surrounded by all the other masters in their smaller dwellings.

MeaningHint: Imagine putting on your top hat and getting power from it. Then, you use that power to help someone. It's important to make the association that it's the top hat giving you the power to help that person. You're basically Frosty the Snow Man.
ReadingHint:
You can imagine saving her however you like. Perhaps she was under a car, and you used your top hat powers to help her out of that situation by lifting it above your head.

Note: Jo-Anne is the character we're using with じょ reading mnemonics. She is short compared to Joe (じょう), the giant farmhand, which is how you'll differentiate the two similar readings.
ReadingMnemonic: The person you <kanji>help</kanji> with your top hat power? It's <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ).
MeaningMnemonic: With <radical>top hat</radical> <radical>power</radical>, you're able to <kanji>help</kanji> anyone.

MeaningHint: More specifically, antibiotics. Your wound is festering pretty badly.
ReadingHint: Or, so you think. He just keeps circling and circling. Feel some hate for the eagle. Why isn't he dropping the medicine? You don't have long to live!
ReadingMnemonic:
When we need to remember the sound い we use the word "Eagle."

You need your <kanji>medicine</kanji> really bad. Then, you look up. There's an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) flying overhead. He has a package. It's medicine! You're saved!
MeaningMnemonic: You're in a <radical>cage</radical> and you've been shot by an <radical>arrow</radical>. Obviously things are looking pretty bad. You need some <kanji>medicine</kanji> if you hope to live.

MeaningHint: Imagine having all the local rulers as your buddies, all the influence you would have. You might end up with everyone wanting to be your buddy.
ReadingHint: Oh no! This coonhound mistook Wolverine for a giant racoon. (His mask kinda makes him look like one.) You better go help your buddy now. Be mindful of the coonhound though, they're good hunters I hear.
ReadingMnemonic: As you're about to embrace your <kanji>buddy</kanji> Wolverine, a <reading>coon</reading>hound (くん) tackles him off the slide, and bites him in the process.
MeaningMnemonic:
<radical>Wolverine</radical> is going down a <radical>slide</radical> and from your <radical>mouth</radical> you scream, "Hey, <kanji>buddy</kanji>! Watch it with that slide, it's really fast and you might hurt yourself, buddy!" 

You're a really good buddy to Wolverine. He also happens to be the local ruler of these lands. You make it a habit to befriend the local strongmen, just in case… No wonder you're so buddy-buddy with all the rulers around here.

MeaningHint: What's that, you say? It doesn't always have to be writing types versus measurement types? We can work together to create a better society? Nah, sorry, I'd rather it be me versus the world.
ReadingHint: Think of someone you know who's your total opposite. Now imagine you're both in ties, but opposite colors, and you're debating… or fighting in a cage match or something.
ReadingMnemonic: Whenever it's you <kanji>versus</kanji> someone else, you wear a <reading>tie</reading> (たい). Naturally, your tie is always the <kanji>opposite</kanji> color of your opponent's tie. Kinda like how political rivals wear different color ties when they debate. It's pretty silly, but at least your tie makes it clear that it's you versus them.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Writing</radical> and <radical>measurement</radical> stand <kanji>versus</kanji> one another. Writing is all about feeling and expression. Measurement is about cold hard numbers. Yep, in this world writing people are basically the <kanji>opposite</kanji> of measurement people. Which side are you on?

MeaningHint: The cape covering the mouth represents how this bureau never talks about their secrets. The flag represents your country. Think about all the cool secret things the Bureau of Secret Things must have.
ReadingHint: Now, this mnemonic isn’t perfectly close, but the goal is to get you close enough in your brain so that you can remember きょく later. If you can get to CLOAK YOU, then you can get to きょく much easier than just trying to get there from scratch. So imagine working in this bureau and getting to cloak yourself all the time.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>bureau</kanji>'s best-kept secret is cloaking devices that <reading>cloak you</reading> (きょく) with the press of a button.
MeaningMnemonic: Your nation's <radical>flag</radical> sits on top of a <radical>cape</radical>d <radical>mouth</radical>. This is the symbol for the "<kanji>Bureau</kanji> of Secret Things." It's a special <kanji>department</kanji> of your government made to keep things hidden.

MeaningHint: Imagine standing around with a weapon in your hands. It feels more like you're doing nothing, loitering around the wall, but this is how you offer your service to your country, so you have to do it.
ReadingHint: Somehow your duty has become pushing this stubborn, hairy yak away from the entrance to your country. You poke it in the butt with your weapon, but it doesn't seem to care.
ReadingMnemonic: You're about halfway through your <kanji>service</kanji>, when a <reading>yak</reading> (やく) walks by. It stops directly in front of you and grunts. "Excuse me, Mr. Yak, but it's my <kanji>duty</kanji> to escort you away from my country."
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around with a <radical>weapon</radical> because you're currently doing your <kanji>service</kanji> and <kanji>duty</kanji> to your country. You're standing guard outside, waiting just in case something will happen.

MeaningHint: Imagine a weapon in between your fingers. It doesn't matter what kind. Now feel the weight as you throw it.
ReadingHint: Imagine throwing the weapon in your fingers just before you walk into とうきょう. You can see the lights, smell the smells! Throw your weapons and your cares away!
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>throw</kanji> your weapon away because you're about to enter <reading>とう</reading>きょう and there are no weapons allowed there. There is, however, a big pile of thrown away weapons by the border, which is where you're throwing yours right now.
MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>fingers</radical> is a <radical>weapon</radical>, so you <kanji>throw</kanji> it. Maybe you're angry and you threw the weapon at a person you don't like. Maybe you're a pacifist and you threw it away so no one else could use it. Either way, when you have a weapon in your fingers you have to throw it.

MeaningHint: Have trouble with the decision. Which one? Tsunami will be big and cool, but a hook will be more discreet. How can you decide? Can you think of a neat way to decide this? You're like Two-Face deciding on how to kill Batman.
ReadingHint: Get everything ready to take your target down. How will you collect enough ketsup to make a ketsup tsunami? Where will you unleash it? You gotta decide on the ketsup logistics now.
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the けつ reading, we always use the word "ketsup." It's a special mix of ketchup and catsup (yum):

You finally <kanji>decide</kanji>. You're going to go with the tsunami... but not just any tsunami. You'll kill him with a tsunami of <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ).
MeaningMnemonic: You have at your disposal a <radical>tsunami</radical> to assassinate this <radical>person</radical>, or a <radical>hook</radical>. Which method will you choose? <kanji>Decide</kanji> now!

MeaningHint: Imagine carefully digging a hole nine times, in nine different spots, so you can do your research. Hmm, I wonder what kind of research these holes are for…
ReadingHint: Put nine full-grown cucumbers into their holes so you can research what happens next.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>research</kanji> is on <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう)!
MeaningMnemonic: You dig a <radical>hole</radical> <radical>nine</radical> times for your <kanji>research</kanji>. You should always do research with nine variables so you can compare them, and so there's an uneven number.

MeaningHint: Imagine hurtling down a slide, your inner self reveling in the joy of the moment. Then, you hit a sharp barb — OUCH!!! Suddenly, you're very conscious of your body again.
ReadingHint: Feel the sharp pain in your shin as it radiates through your body. It kinda sucks, but in a way it's reassuring proof of the physical existence of oneself.
ReadingMnemonic: What part of your <kanji>body</kanji> did the barb get stuck in? Your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). That pointy little barb dug deep into your shin and made you painfully aware of the fact that <kanji>oneself</kanji> and one's body are inextricable.
MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>self</radical> goes down a <radical>slide</radical> and hits a <radical>barb</radical>, it's a painful reminder that you have a <kanji>body</kanji>. Sometimes people think their self is just some kind of floating, disembodied consciousness, but getting a barb stuck in <kanji>oneself</kanji> while going down a slide will quickly remind one of one's bodily existence.

MeaningHint: Well, well… someone's lucky to not have to learn a new kanji.
ReadingHint: Think back on someone you met recently whose name or face you can't really remember. Hate to break it to you, but… they were a shaman.
ReadingMnemonic: If you don't know who <kanji>someone</kanji> is, odds are they're a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ). Only a shaman would have the mystical powers to obscure their own identity and pass themselves off as a mysterious, unknown "someone."
MeaningMnemonic: The radical and the kanji are exactly the same, so this means <kanji>someone</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking around at night and you need to sharpen your sword. You walk up to a stone lantern and rub your sword all over it to sharpen it. Touch the blade. Indeed, it's been sharpened!
ReadingHint:
You'll want to remember this character, because he will come up many times throughout your WaniKani journey. Ken the samurai used to work for WaniKani, but then he went rogue because he thought WaniKani wasn't being radical enough. So now he wanders the website carrying his squid friend (who you'll meet later) in a bucket.

To help you visualize him, imagine a samurai with armor made from crab shells, a helmet that is just an alligator head, and swords with crab claw hand guards. His great beard and mustache are styled in the shape of whatever kanji is giving you the most trouble at the time.
ReadingMnemonic: Why did you <kanji>sharpen</kanji> your blade? Because you are about to do battle with <reading>Ken</reading> (けん), the famous samurai warrior.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>stone</radical> <radical>lantern</radical> is an excellent way to <kanji>sharpen</kanji> anything, even in the dark. Your knives, your forks, your spoons, and even your sword.

ReadingHint: Feel the shock of this. It sounds just like a sheep, it's strange.
ReadingMnemonic: You are looking at a horse. It's looking at you. You try making horse noises at it, to get a reaction. Instead of reacting with a normal horse noise, it just makes sheep noises: "<reading>Baaa</reading>," (ば) it says. "Baaaaa."
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>horse</radical> radical and the <kanji>horse</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Huzzah!

MeaningHint: Makes sense, because a bunch of individual trees is what makes up a forest!
ReadingHint: Keep repeating this pattern over and over, continually focusing in on how you need "more" trees to make this forest. For some reason the person working with you only goes up by one at a time, though, so you have to say it a lot (it'll help you to remember, anyways!).
ReadingMnemonic: One tree? No. Two trees? Not enough. I want <reading>more</reading> (もり) trees, please!
MeaningMnemonic: This kanji is just the <radical>tree</radical> radical three times. Just based off this, you should be able to figure it out. This kanji means "<kanji>forest</kanji>."

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at the dirt. It's a distinct color and texture, and you're able to identify your location just from that alone. Now draw that line on the ground and you'll always remember this place.
ReadingHint: Remember Joe? He's our local farmhand who only has nine toes (from the 上 mnemonic). Imagine that guy explaining to you how to know your location from dirt. He may be a bit clumsy, but the man really knows his dirt.
ReadingMnemonic: Knowing your <kanji>location</kanji> from dirt is quite a feat! Who taught you that? It was <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big local farmhand. Despite being a bit weird, he can identify any <kanji>place</kanji> just from its dirt!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Dirt</radical> makes it <radical>easy</radical> to identify your <kanji>location</kanji>. Even if you add an extra line to mark the ground, it's still easy. Keep that in mind and you can go from <kanji>place</kanji> to place.

MeaningHint: Imagine a sun attached up in the sky with two crosses, which act as scaffolding, and the moon gradually fading off to the right as the morning wears on.
ReadingHint: You watch the sun scaffolded by crosses, and the moon next to it, as you sip on your early-morning Asahi.
ReadingMnemonic: Every <kanji>morning</kanji> you have <reading>Asa</reading>hi (あさ) beer for breakfast.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>cross</radical> sticking out the top of the <radical>sun</radical> and another <radical>cross</radical> sticking out the bottom. The <radical>moon</radical> is off to the right. That's because it's the <kanji>morning</kanji>, which is when the sun and moon switch over. Those crosses are making sure the sun stays high in the sky for the rest of the day.

MeaningHint:
Go through in your mind the system your farm / village uses for deciding who has to sickle on any given day. It rotates, and it's a turn-based system. So, do you all stand in a line? Is there a calendar? Come up with something interesting, focusing on your turn in the process you come up with. 

 It will be helpful to remember both the "number in a series" and "turn" meanings for the vocab coming up in the future.
ReadingHint: Try to feel disappointed by this. Strange thing to feel disappointed by, but you were a great sickler. You were going to win the great prize, too (come up with something huge!), but now you're banned for the season.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>turn</kanji>-based rice sickle system is a contest as well! But, you are disappointed to find out that you have been <reading>ban</reading>ned (ばん) from the game because of the performance enhancing steroids you've been taking.
MeaningMnemonic: You have to <radical>sickle</radical> the <radical>rice paddy</radical> today because it's your <kanji>turn</kanji>. This kanji also means <kanji>number in a series</kanji>, which is a lot like a turn. For example, you're number 3, so your turn will be number 3.

MeaningHint: Shout "PLEASE ANSWER ME!" a few times to get the idea of "answer" in your head. What you really want is an answer, whether it's bad or good, but nobody's willing to say anything because you look so terrible.
ReadingHint: With this shiny quarter in your hand, everyone is giving you their answer. Yay for bribery!
ReadingMnemonic: You pull out a <reading>quarter</reading> (こた) and offer it to whoever will give you an <kanji>answer</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: "Does the <radical>bamboo</radical> shirt <radical>suit</radical> me?" you ask... but nobody will <kanji>answer</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It's unbelievable the kind of picture you're able to paint with just the tips of threads. So much detail. So artistic. If it helps, imagine that you drew the Mona Lisa, better than the original, only using this thread tool.
ReadingHint: Tell a few more people about your picture. Have them all do the same surprised "Eh?!" reaction at your art skills.
ReadingMnemonic: You tell someone you painted this masterpiece of a <kanji>picture</kanji>. Their response? "<reading>EH</reading> (え)?!"
MeaningMnemonic: When a bunch of <radical>thread</radical>s are <radical>meet</radical>ing on paper (after you've dipped them in paint, say) they can create a masterpiece of a <kanji>picture</kanji> (or <kanji>painting</kanji>).

MeaningHint: Imagine looking over a bunch of different kinds of shellfish wrapped in nets. Think about which ones you want to buy.
ReadingHint: Imagine buying an entire car's worth of shellfish. Mmm, delicious.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to <kanji>buy</kanji> as many shellfish as you can fit in your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You keep buying until your car is completely full of shellfish before you stop. Cars are totally a realistic unit of measure.
MeaningMnemonic: Here's a <radical>net</radical> full of of <radical>shellfish</radical> that you can <kanji>buy</kanji>. You can't just buy loose shellfish. You can only buy the ones that are in the net, bagged up and ready for your money.

MeaningHint: Imagine riding your scooter down the road. You're carefully aiming the scooter's neck the right way so you don't end up going out of control and dying.
ReadingHint: Alternate ending: You do a sweet jump, go right through the doughnut hole, take a bite, and land safely back onto the road!
ReadingMnemonic:
To remember the reading どう we use the word "doughnut."

You're traveling down this <kanji>road</kanji> so fast that you don't see the giant <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう) that's rolling toward you until the last minute. You crash into it, and both you and the doughnut go tumbling down. Ahg, your neck!
MeaningMnemonic: Aim the <radical>scooter</radical>'s <radical>neck</radical> at the <kanji>road</kanji>! Scooters are only meant for driving on <kanji>street</kanji>s and <kanji>path</kanji>s, and if you go the wrong <kanji>way</kanji>, you'll probably break your own neck!

MeaningHint: "Interval" is a weird word to remember, but just think of the sun going around the earth and through the gate like clockwork. Every 24 hours it passes through the gate, and each interval is exactly the same.
ReadingHint: Picture Genghis Khan ordering his most skilled inventors and builders to create this special sun gate so they can harness the power of intervals, and all people under his rule can benefit from the accurate telling of time.
ReadingMnemonic:
This method of keeping track of <kanji>interval</kanji>s of time was invented by the great Khans of Mongolia, specifically the most famous Khan of all: Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).

Genghis Khan has had a huge impact on the world as we know it, especially the Eurasian continent of the past, so he's going to come up a lot from now on. Be prepared to get very familiar with him and his friends!
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>gate</radical> that the <radical>sun</radical> goes through at a regular <kanji>interval</kanji>. There is an <kanji>interval of time</kanji> in the sense that the sun goes through it at the same time every day. And there is an <kanji>interval of space</kanji> in the sense that it has to go through the space between the gate posts.

MeaningHint: Picture all of the different types of clouds in the sky. There are puffy white ones, heavy dark ones, and wispy thin ones. But they're all clouds.
ReadingHint: You look at each cloud and each one is a different cool mom. They're each doing different cool mom things. You suddenly don't want to look at the clouds anymore.
ReadingMnemonic: You lie down and try to figure out what the <kanji>cloud</kanji>s are shaped like. The first cloud is shaped... like a <reading>coo</reading>l <reading>mo</reading>m (くも). She's wearing high pants, and holding a kid on each hip, but she's also listening to the latest music and using slang. Huh. You look to the next cloud and see... wait, another cool mom?
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>rain</radical> <radical>cloud</radical> is still a <kanji>cloud</kanji>! It's probably going to rain on you soon though, so I hope you have a jacket or an umbrella.

MeaningHint: She has to count each grain in order to divvy up the rice supply and make it last the winter. Imagine the amount you get today — just a small number of grains. It's not a good time for you guys…
ReadingHint: Come up with a story about why your farm got sued for everything. Imagine sitting in court. Listen to the judge deliver the verdict. Be angry. The woman will be counting rice for years to come.
ReadingMnemonic: Why do you have to <kanji>count</kanji> your rice out like this? It's because someone <reading>sue</reading>d (すう) your farm and took everything you owned. Since being sued, you only have a small <kanji>amount</kanji> of rice to last the winter.
MeaningMnemonic: Give <radical>rice</radical> to a <radical>woman</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> so she can <kanji>count</kanji> it. The woman will count each individual grain of rice, then tell you the <kanji>amount</kanji> or <kanji>number</kanji> of grains.

MeaningHint: Close your eyes and feel the aura of comfort wash over you. Listen to the musical tinkling of the tree's icy branches, setting you at ease. You never want this pleasurable moment to end!
ReadingHint: Place a hanger on the rack. Feel the wave of pleasure that runs through you, and then bask in the afterglow of comfort and ease that it leaves behind. Now, go find something else to hang up on your rack. You need another hit of comfort and pleasure.
ReadingMnemonic: You want more of this aura of <kanji>comfort</kanji>, so you chop down the magic tree and build a clothing <reading>rack</reading> (らく) out of it. Then, you put the rack in your house so you can enjoy that fuzzy feeling of comfort and ease every time you go to hang something up.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>white</radical> <radical>ice</radical> <radical>tree</radical> is a magical tree that radiates an aura of <kanji>comfort</kanji>, <kanji>ease</kanji>, and <kanji>pleasure</kanji>, with its icy foliage tinkling like beautiful <kanji>music</kanji> whenever it sways in the breeze.

MeaningHint: Your problem is you weren't saying it with your tongue before. Once you start using your tongue, you're able to speak suddenly!
ReadingHint: You decided to teach a walrus how to speak because it seemed like the hardest thing to teach, with its tusks and all that. It turned out to be really easy. Although, you may have picked the Justin Long walrus, by accident. Either way, this walrus can speak! And you definitely (probably) helped.
ReadingMnemonic: If something uses their tongue, it will be able to <kanji>talk</kanji> and <kanji>speak</kanji>. To prove this, you teach a <reading>wa</reading>lrus (わ) to use its tongue, and lo and behold, it speaks to you!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Say</radical> it with your <radical>tongue</radical>. If you do, you'll be able to <kanji>talk</kanji>. You'll be able to <kanji>speak</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine being hit by the electricity. Feel the powerful electric shock pulse through your body. You're like a crazier Ben Franklin out here.
ReadingHint: Feel the indent in your face. It doesn't hurt, strangely, but it is pretty huge. Panic about this dent a bit.
ReadingMnemonic: After the <kanji>electricity</kanji> hits you, you feel your face. In it is a huge <reading>den</reading>t (でん).
MeaningMnemonic: You're in the <radical>rain</radical>, in a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, holding an <radical>umbrella</radical>. You're here because there's nothing else that's very tall around you. You want to get hit by <kanji>electricity</kanji> (aka lightning), to prove that it exists.

MeaningHint: Why do you hate this place, in particular? Come up with a great reason why to help you to remember this very broad kanji meaning.
ReadingHint: Come up with a weird reason as to why the circus is at this place you hate so much. Why did you come here with your axe? Does it have something to do with the circus show?
ReadingMnemonic: You smash through the door and finally get inside the <kanji>place</kanji>, only to find some kind of <reading>sho</reading>w (しょ) going on. It's a circus show — elephants, tightrope walkers, bears on unicycles! The greatest show on earth, but why is it at this place?
MeaningMnemonic: You're tearing this <radical>door</radical> down with an <radical>axe</radical>. You're absolutely crazy. "I HATE THIS <kanji>PLACE</kanji>!!" you keep yelling as you smash in the door.

MeaningHint: Imagine someone who looks super anti-social (and a little feral). Ask them why they're sitting on this stool inside a cliff. They reply it's because they hate people. They're anti-people. In fact, they're anti-everything.
ReadingHint: Make sure you get to the point where you pull this anti-social guy's beard off, to reveal Han Solo beneath. Be surprised... not just because he's Han Solo, but because he's real, too! Maybe you can yell out "Han! Your people need you!"
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out, when you go to this guy and pull off his beard, its the <kanji>anti</kanji>-hero from Star Wars, <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! What is Han doing here, so hidden away? He's obviously hiding from Jabba because he doesn't want to get frozen in carbonite again.
MeaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>cliff</radical> there's a <radical>stool</radical>. If someone has a stool deep inside a cliff, they are most likely pretty <kanji>anti</kanji>-society, hiding away like that.

MeaningHint: Imagine running into this samurai salesman as you walk through a sketchy part of town. He cautiously glances over his shoulder before opening his trench coat to reveal to you… all the foreheads and legs he's trying to sell.
ReadingHint: Imagine loudly saying "bye" over and over at this shady samurai. He keeps trying to tell you about how fresh and high-quality all his foreheads and legs are, but you just loudly repeat "bye" as you back away from him.
ReadingMnemonic: If you run into this samurai who <kanji>sell</kanji>s severed foreheads and legs, you should just say "<reading>bye</reading> (ばい)!" and get the heck out of there. Actually, if anyone tries to sell you severed body parts — samurai or not — you should probably say bye over and over until you can get away from them.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> has come bearing <radical>forehead</radical>s and <radical>legs</radical> that he's trying to <kanji>sell</kanji>. He thought it was a waste to leave all his slain enemies on the battlefield, so he gathered up their foreheads and legs to sell on the black market for some extra cash. Hey, no judging! A samurai's gotta sell what a samurai's gotta sell, okay?

MeaningHint: Imagine his muffled words as he tries to explain this with the cross in his mouth. Hm, better not pry further. Maybe he's trying to… X-orcize his demons…
ReadingHint: Imagine Jesus gently taking the cross out of Wolverine's mouth. What a swell dude.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>occurrence</kanji> doesn't impress <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ), who appears and gently removes the cross from Wolverine's mouth.
MeaningMnemonic: You find a <radical>cross</radical> in the <radical>mouth</radical> of <radical>Wolverine</radical>. What a strange <kanji>occurrence</kanji>. I guess he put it there, but why would he do such a <kanji>thing</kanji>? You ask him, but he says it's a personal <kanji>matter</kanji> of utmost secrecy.

MeaningHint: Smell the ground. Waft the earthy smell of history into your leader-like nostrils. What does it smell like? It smells useful. And also like ground.
ReadingHint: When you use the ground for sheep like this, your people rejoice. You're the best leader ever! You always use everything to your advantage!
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>use</kanji> the ground to raise <reading>shee</reading>p (し). When you were down there sniffing around you realized that your people will flourish best if you use this land for grazing and raising sheep.
MeaningMnemonic: A good <radical>leader</radical> can put the <radical>ground</radical>'s <radical>history</radical> to great <kanji>use</kanji>. You are a good leader. You have studied the ground and dirt of the earth your entire life. You know the history of the ground and you know how to use it to lead your people.

MeaningHint: You feel the panic upon realizing what you've just done. This is murder in the first degree. You used a terrible tool to do it, and now you must hide it.
ReadingHint: That goo is really slimy. It's disgusting. You feel sick to your stomach. Please don't throw up, there's already enough junk on the ground with the eye and fins and all.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>tool</kanji> is dripping with <reading>goo</reading> (ぐ). Goopy, incriminating goo. It's the guts of the fish you just killed and it's all over your hands too. Yuck.
MeaningMnemonic: There's an <radical>eye</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>, among some <radical>fins</radical>. What happened? You killed a fish, and now you must hide the murder <kanji>tool</kanji>. Quick, hide the tool before someone finds out it was you.

MeaningHint: You're dealing with a lot of emotions right now. You feel the pain from being cleated in the forehead, but you're also glad to have finally been accepted into the soccer club. Suppress your pain and receive this honor.
ReadingHint: Did you ever play soccer as a kid? Do you remember accepting a small juice box into your little kid hands during halftime? Apparently pros drink the same ones.
ReadingMnemonic: After they <kanji>accept</kanji> you, they give you a small <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ) box. It's to help you recover from the trauma of getting kicked. And you <kanji>receive</kanji> the juice with grace.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone throws a <radical>cleat</radical> at your <radical>forehead</radical> while you sit on a <radical>stool</radical>. You must <kanji>accept</kanji> this. Then another guy cleats you in the forehead, but you remain on the stool and <kanji>receive</kanji> it without complaint. This is some kind of acceptance ritual for the most prestigious Spanish soccer club in the world. If a cleat to the forehead doesn't knock you off the stool, you'll receive an offer to join.

MeaningHint: Imagine Japanese people with grain in their mouth. The whole country is at peace.
ReadingHint: The walruses also achieve peace by putting grain in every walrus' mouth, following the Japanese style.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Peace</kanji> is the <kanji>Japanese style</kanji>. The <reading>wa</reading>lruses (わ) living off of Japan see this, and start to emulate.
MeaningMnemonic: When everyone has <radical>grain</radical> in their <radical>mouth</radical>, you will have <kanji>peace</kanji>. That is the <kanji>Japanese style</kanji> of doing things.

MeaningHint: Think back on all the experiments and calculations you did to determine this. You tested all kinds of things — umbrellas, awnings, tarps — but ultimately you determined that only a roof would suffice.
ReadingHint: Stick your hands into your coat pockets and feel those crispy, toasty taters. You're not gonna let them come to any harm.
ReadingMnemonic: Why did you <kanji>determine</kanji> your coat rack needed a roof? Because your coat pockets are full of <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots! You can't afford to let your tater tots get rained on, so you determined they needed to be properly sheltered with a roof.
MeaningMnemonic: You build a <radical>roof</radical> over your <radical>coat rack</radical> because you <kanji>determine</kanji> that this is the best way to protect your coats from the elements.

MeaningHint: Imagine sitting comfortably on your roof as the world around you changes to spring. Snow melts and reveals all of the truths that have been obscured and hidden in winter.
ReadingHint: Gee! Two babies! Reality sure is lovely.
ReadingMnemonic: At the moment, you're trying to learn the <kanji>truth</kanji> about the deer that is coming out of the snowy woods when you see not one, but two baby deer appear with it! <reading>Gee! Two</reading> (じつ) babies! You keep looking around and realize that all of the animals seem to have two babies that they had hidden in the snow.
MeaningMnemonic: Climb onto the <radical>roof</radical> in <radical>spring</radical> to see the <kanji>truth</kanji>. The world is waking back up from winter and revealing the <kanji>reality</kanji> of what it is all around you.

MeaningHint: Imagine placing each piece of clothing you own onto the stool and carefully stamping them with a symbol of the moon. This should stop people from stealing all of your clothes!
ReadingHint: Your friend turns red and tries to pull the clothes back, "F**k you!" they yell. "No, f**k you, these are my clothes and I have proof this time!"
ReadingMnemonic: A few days later you're with a friend when you recognize the <kanji>clothes</kanji> they're wearing. You grab it, rip it off, and scream, "<reading>F**k</reading> (ふく) you!" There is your moon stamp! These are your clothes!!
MeaningMnemonic: Take the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>stamp</radical> on the <radical>stool</radical> in front of you and use it on all of your <kanji>clothes</kanji>. Now even if you sell or lose a piece of <kanji>clothing</kanji>, you'll be able to find it again by checking for your special moon stamp.

MeaningHint: Picture most of the earth's population already swimming on the tsunami that will last the rest of eternity. Your floaties and life preservers would help you through this mess. You need to swim!
ReadingHint: Picture yourself swimming in the tsunami with everyone else. You can't really talk to them, you're too busy swimming for your life, so you just shout "Oy!" to them and they shout it back.
ReadingMnemonic: <reading>Oy</reading> (およ)! Oy, stop daydreaming about <kanji>swim</kanji>ming and actually do it!
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> is going to last an <radical>eternity</radical> you better learn how to <kanji>swim</kanji> now. We'll be under water for the rest of time and if you can't swim you're not gonna make it.

MeaningHint: Imagine if cows could fly. Wouldn't you be jealous of them? I mean, if a cow can fly, any old thing should be able to, right?
ReadingHint: Imagine a thing like flying boots. Do you believe such a thing could be real?
ReadingMnemonic: You want a <kanji>thing</kanji> to help you fly with the cow so you put on your flying <reading>boots</reading> (ぶつ). Your flying boots are a pretty strange thing in themselves, but they're the only way you can keep up.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>cow</radical> with <radical>wing</radical>s is flying in the air. What a strange <kanji>thing</kanji>! You've heard of flying pigs before, but cows? This is a new and strange thing indeed! Cows with wings! What a silly thing.

MeaningHint: Flowers, like anything that grows old, go through a lot of suffering as their bodies break down. They're no different from you and me.
ReadingHint: No one can resist a good cookie. It will ease your suffering, or at least distract you for a little bit.
ReadingMnemonic: If a flower or a person is growing old and <kanji>suffering</kanji>, give them a <reading>coo</reading>kie (く).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> have grown <radical>old</radical>, so they're <kanji>suffering</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Think of the last time life gave you a kick, and how you expressed that pain to make it go away, specifically on the surface that is your face.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself saying it more like ひょう, though. ひょう man!
ReadingMnemonic: You need to <kanji>express</kanji> yourself, so you call up a friend and say "<reading>Heyo</reading>!" (ひょう).
MeaningMnemonic: Sometimes <radical>life</radical> gives you a <radical>kick</radical>, and you have to <kanji>express</kanji> yourself to deal with it! How do you express yourself? On the <kanji>surface</kanji> (of your face)!

MeaningHint: Of course, we feel terrible about forcing the clowns into their own part of town. It's not that they're lesser than us, it's just that we don't like them.
ReadingHint: Picture all of the clowns, trapped in their weird clown part of town, drinking booze and smashing each other over the heads with the bottles. You're glad you don't live in that part.
ReadingMnemonic: You see, that <kanji>part</kanji> of town is full of <reading>boo</reading>ze (ぶ). Clowns need a lot of booze to keep their charade going, so we just let them have it. But they aren't allowed in our part anymore, because they all stink of nasty clown booze.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>clown</radical> <radical>building</radical> is over in that <kanji>part</kanji> of town. We keep the clown part as separate as we can from the rest of the normal people. In fact, the entire clown education <kanji>department</kanji> and their clown <kanji>club</kanji>s have to stay in their part, they can't come in ours.

MeaningHint: Just imagine you're a car or a train and the grain is a person. It's going for a nice ride in your mouth!
ReadingHint: Now the nori is going for a ride too, woohoo!
ReadingMnemonic: You decide the grain is probably lonely on this <kanji>ride</kanji> alone, so you add some <reading>no</reading>ri (の) seaweed, so it has a friend.
MeaningMnemonic: You put a grain in your mouth and let it take a ride. This kanji has the same radicals as 和, but this time the grain is in your mouth, which is why there's little pieces of grain sticking out of the 口 part ;)

MeaningHint: Picture all of the hungry guests and customers under your roof, eating your food and sleeping on your floor.
ReadingHint: Kyaku looks just like any other customer. Picture a stereotypical "customer" type person in your head.
ReadingMnemonic: One of your <kanji>guest</kanji>s is a regular named <reading>Kyaku</reading> (きゃく) the <kanji>customer</kanji>. Kyaku is always staying over at one place or another, so everyone knows their name.
MeaningMnemonic: Under your <radical>roof</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> there are more <radical>mouth</radical>s to feed because you let <kanji>guest</kanji>s stay with you when it's cold. Some of them aren't family, so they have to pay to stay under your roof and get food. Those are <kanji>customer</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: That's the natural next progression of claiming some land. Building a roof upon it and building some shops and stores inside. You'll see why in a moment.
ReadingHint: You have yourself a whole Yankees stadium, and all it took was a roof and some shops and stores! If you build it, they will come.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>roof</kanji> covering the dirt is there so that the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や) can play baseball year round. The <kanji>store</kanji>s and <kanji>shop</kanji>s are there to sell hot dogs and Yankees memorabilia.
