ReadingMnemonic: The simple drunkard started a <kanji>war</kanji> with the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん)! This is especially unfortunate, because the centaurs are really good at <kanji>battle</kanji>s because they're all automatically cavalry troops.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>simple</radical> <radical>drunkard</radical> starting a <kanji>war</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself standing on your older brother. They push you off. So you do it again. You want to be on top, but your older brother doesn't want you to. You compete for the top spot over and over again, throughout your whole life, because that's what one does with their older brother.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself competing to stand on top of your older brother. Do you win? Do you get the one ticket to きょうと?
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>compete</kanji> with your older brother because only one of you may go to <reading>きょう</reading>と. Whoever stands on top the most gets to go to きょうと, so you must compete for a spot.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical>. Now <radical>stand</radical> on your <radical>older brother</radical> again. You wish to stand on, and above, your older brother. You <kanji>compete</kanji> over and over again, to stand atop your older brother.

MeaningHint: Imagine putting a little drop of water over a root you want to grow. Doing this is good for the plant and also good for your soul.
ReadingHint: Rowing even feels good on your arms and back. Probably just as good as all these roots will feel.
ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>good</kanji> way to get a lot of drops of water for your roots is to buy a <reading>row</reading> boat (りょう). Then just row out to the middle of the lake and plonk all the roots right into the source!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drop</radical> of water to a <radical>root</radical> is <kanji>good</kanji>. It's good for the roots to grow and live and thrive. And what's good for roots is good for our plants and us humans too.

MeaningHint: Just constructing this thing was an achievement. Feel proud like it was, well, an achievement.
ReadingHint:
Thanks こういち. Now everyone will know of your achievements around kanji learning and inside-out earth theory.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading. Remember that, because it comes up quite a bit!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>achievement</kanji> was made possible by <reading>こう</reading>いち, so of course this construction project is a giant statue of the man himself. What an achievement of a statue. What an achievement of an achievement.
MeaningMnemonic: Using your <radical>construction</radical> <radical>power</radical> you build something great. A true <kanji>achievement</kanji> built for the achievement of making kanji learning easier!

MeaningHint: Picture a temple dedicated to a single cow. All of the people who come and worship here have a special place in their hearts for the cow.
ReadingHint: You walk up to a statue of the special cow in the temple and see a collar around its neck that says "Tokugawa Moo Moo" on it. How... special!
ReadingMnemonic: This cow is <kanji>special</kanji> because it belonged to <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu, the first shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate and uniter of Japan! He's a super important figure in Japanese history. You should absolutely look him up and read a bit about him (when you're caught up on these lessons, that is).
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>cow</radical> <radical>temple</radical> is <kanji>special</kanji>. This cow must have been very important to have an entire temple dedicated to it.

MeaningHint: Imagine the leader looking around the rice paddy. She keeps saying, "Ugh, it would be convenient if I didn't have to walk around out here looking for the treasure that's going to make things more convenient for me. Ugh." Go ahead and say this phrase out loud, too, pretending to be the leader in the rice paddy. Feel the cold and the wet, making it less convenient for you.
ReadingHint: Feel the happiness that the leader would feel at this revelation. It will make everything much more convenient for her, since she's not the one doing all the manual labor. She won't have to eat out of a bento, that's for sure.
ReadingMnemonic: She finds a place to dig in the ground. It looks like there's some fresh dirt here. Finally, the treasure of <kanji>convenience</kanji> that will make this leader's life so much easier. She digs into the ground and pulls out a box. When she opens it, she finds out it's a <reading>ben</reading>to (べん). "My god," she thinks. "This really will make my life more convenient. My people can take their lunches with them now instead of coming back to base to eat, which will waste way less time!"
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> stands upon the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. She is searching for <radical>treasure</radical>. It is said that this treasure here in this rice field will make things much easier for the leader (that's what leaders really want, after all). If she gets this treasure, it will be a great <kanji>convenience</kanji> for her, because convenience is everything when you're the leader.

MeaningHint:
Picture this leader asking you (yet again) to work for them. "Can you move those heavy objects with all that power of yours?" Typical leader — as soon as they know you're good at a certain kind of work, they ask you to do it all the time. 

Note: There are a few other kanji that have a similar meaning to this one. This kanji is used in words that refer to doing work, with a focus on movement or operating something.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself receiving a doughnut as payment after you've been working. It's delicious. Doughnuts are fantastic. Taste the fresh doughnut in your mouth as it crumbles and melts on your tongue. It's the best doughnut you've ever had.
ReadingMnemonic: Where do you get the power to do all this <kanji>work</kanji>? From <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう). You eat nothing but doughnuts. If you had no doughnuts, you couldn't work. In fact, your leader pays you for your work… in doughnuts.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> makes you lift <radical>heavy</radical> loads with all your <radical>power</radical>, since this is what you do for <kanji>work</kanji>. Why do you do this kind of work? Well, you're good at it and the leader makes you do it, I guess.

MeaningHint: Here's an order: study your radicals.
ReadingHint:
Imagine an authority figure giving you this order as an official order. Ray guns don't really exist, so you think it's a pretty strange order.

Hint: when you see the <radical>orders</radical> radical, the reading will often be れい. The mnemonic will likely involve orders to get a ray gun of some kind.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>orders</kanji> are to get a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun.
MeaningMnemonic: If you know the radical meaning <radical>orders</radical> you know the kanji meaning <kanji>orders</kanji> as well!

MeaningHint: Put your hand over your heart, or on your wrist, or somewhere you can feel your pulse. Your heart is pumping blood through your body, hear the sound of your heart beat and try to picture and get an idea for how all the blood flows through your body.
ReadingHint: Picture the majestic animal that is the bald eagle. Imagine the sound of an eagle screeching as it zooms through the air, flapping its wings in the wind. It’s pretty impressive.
ReadingMnemonic: The heart of an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) is very strong. The person who knew this probably came up with the <kanji>idea</kanji> to make the bald eagle a symbol of the United States. Eagles are always flying around and doing things, so their hearts are very strong.
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>sound</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> gives me an <kanji>idea</kanji>. The methodic thump thump thumping of a heart puts me in a trance that gives me great ideas. It’s a very inspirational sound, the sound of a heart is. It gives me an idea. 

Alternatively, you could look at this kanji and see a "stand" radical and something that kind of (almost) looks like the kanji for "think." When you stand up and think, you come up with your very own <kanji>idea</kanji>s!

MeaningHint: What's your favorite food? Imagine that, as you bite into it, tiny jets are released from inside the food to attack your tongue with flavor bombs, stimulating your sense of taste.
ReadingHint: Of course, if you're vegetarian imagine the flavor of the meat being terrible. Up to you. Make the mnemonic work for your lifestyle.
ReadingMnemonic: And what has more <kanji>flavor</kanji> than a big slab of <reading>me</reading>at (み)? You love meat and its flavor nearly as much as you love the <kanji>taste</kanji> of its juice. So many good things to put in your mouth!
MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> there is a <radical>jet</radical> dropping <kanji>flavor</kanji> bombs. Every time you <kanji>taste</kanji> something, it's actually because a tiny jet has flown into your mouth to bomb you with flavor.

MeaningHint: Imagine waiting outside a temple. People are giving you looks. People think you're loitering, not just waiting innocently.
ReadingHint: Imagine adjusting your tie as you wait. Oh no, now even more people are staring at you. The tie thing is making you look even more suspicious!
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>wait</kanji> outside the temple, you can't help but adjust your <reading>tie</reading> (たい). When you wait you get nervous, and when you get nervous you fidget. So now you're messing with your tie a whole bunch.
MeaningMnemonic: People who <radical>loiter</radical> around the <radical>temple</radical> are probably just trying to <kanji>wait</kanji>. They don't mean to look like they're up to no good, but it's hard to wait without looking suspicious when you're waiting outside a temple.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself under great exertion. You're attempting to use your power to move something and it's really requiring a lot of exertion to move. Picture yourself doing this action and imagine how difficult it is.
ReadingHint: Picture this Ben in your head. Ben always uses lots of exertion to do tasks. Imagine him using an unnecessary amount of power and exertion in carrying out normal everyday tasks.
ReadingMnemonic: Excuse me, I don’t think I’ve introduced myself yet. My name is <reading>Ben</reading> (べん) and I use powerful muscle <kanji>exertion</kanji> to accomplish every task. Ben knows what Ben wants and how to get it. Remember that, kids.
MeaningMnemonic: You'll have to <radical>excuse</radical> me, having all this <radical>power</radical> puts a lot of strain and <kanji>exertion</kanji> on my body. My power comes from exertion of muscle, so please excuse me if I start to perspire.

MeaningHint: Somehow your little garden is now being used by the Yoga King to teach yoga to a whole group of random people under this canopy... you have no idea how they got back here.
ReadingHint: You're not sure what else to do, so you end up serving tater tots in your garden all day to stretchy strangers.
ReadingMnemonic: Not only is this Yoga King taking over your <kanji>garden</kanji>, but he's ordering you to serve food to his class. "You there, peasant, fetch these people your freshest <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots from this puny garden to give them strength."
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>canopy</radical> and a man called the <radical>Yoga</radical> <radical>King</radical> standing in the middle of your <kanji>garden</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to live without a heart. You couldn’t. Your blood stops flowing and your body shuts down. You would no longer be able to draw breath.
ReadingHint: Imagine smelling this smelly sock. Imagine the smelly stench of the dirty sock wafting up into your nostrils and filling your lungs with its odor. Gross.
ReadingMnemonic: You know how when people are unconscious and their <kanji>breath</kanji> slows or comes to a stop, they use smelling salts to revive them? Well a smelly <reading>sock</reading> (そく) can work just as well. Socks get really stinky and their stench is powerful enough to revive someone who isn’t drawing breath.
MeaningMnemonic: Imagining one’s <radical>self</radical> without a <radical>heart</radical> can make it hard to draw <kanji>breath</kanji>. Without a heart, you’d have no blood pumping and you would start to die. This would make it increasingly harder to draw breath and get oxygen to stay alive.

MeaningHint: Imagine coming to your senses after shooting up a block of tofu for stress relief or whatever you were doing. Yeah, you gotta get away from tofu for a while… It's definitely time for a relaxing trip.
ReadingHint: Imagine soaking up the sun on the beaches of Rio de Janeiro. Rio has its fair share of guns, but at least there's a lot less tofu around these parts.
ReadingMnemonic: So, where do you go on your <kanji>trip</kanji>? To <reading>Rio</reading> (りょ). You had to go way down south to get as far away as possible from tofu. Ahh, nothing like a nice trip to Rio!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>direction</radical> your <radical>gun</radical> is facing leads straight to a block of <radical>tofu</radical>. This might be a sign you need to take a <kanji>trip</kanji>. If you're out there pointing guns at tofu, you're probably a little… tired… and in need of a change of scenery. Take a trip!

MeaningHint: Roots are roots, ね.
ReadingHint: Picture a family of condors nestled at the bottom of a tree in its roots. Then a huge machine comes over and mows them all down as it cuts all the trees off their roots. Darn you humans! Do you have to ruin everything?!
ReadingMnemonic: Tree <kanji>root</kanji>s are the perfect roosting place for <reading>con</reading>dors (こん). Unfortunately, living on roots is also why they're always endangered. Condors can be killed pretty easily living on the ground on roots like this.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> <radical>root</radical> is still just a <kanji>root</kanji>!

MeaningHint: As the tsunami recedes, watch as all of the trash comes streaming out of the river and crowds the bank.
ReadingHint: Now Ryu is punching and kicking and tatsumaki senpukyaku-ing all of the trash into burnable and non-burnable piles. But the stream doesn't end. Now he's trying to get his other Street Fighter pals to help him beat it back!
ReadingMnemonic: Next to the trash <kanji>stream</kanji> you suddenly see <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter and he looks pissed. He's furious that so many people throw their trash in the river and it causes streams like this.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> lifts all the <radical>trash</radical> out of the <radical>river</radical> and it <kanji>stream</kanji>s out onto the shores.

MeaningHint: I’m sure you’ve heard many theories on how dinosaurs went extinct. Well, this is the real story. Imagine this poor triceratops and all his friends being extinguished by this powerful tsunami, sweeping them up into the moon where there's no air to breathe or leaves to eat.
ReadingHint: Watch the Shougun's eyes light up as an actor dressed like a tsunami crashes into an actor dressed as a triceratops and all of the lights on the stage are extinguished. Spooky...
ReadingMnemonic: This famous event where almost all life was <kanji>extinguish</kanji>ed is reenacted every year for the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). He's got a bit of an unhealthy obsession with extinguishing life...
MeaningMnemonic: A long time ago a huge <radical>tsunami</radical> swept a <radical>triceratops</radical> up into the <radical>moon</radical> and <kanji>extinguish</kanji>ed its life.

MeaningHint: Picture a clown and all the ridiculous funny things they do. Imagine the clown throwing a pie at someone's face and how funny that is. Chuckle to yourself and remember this kanji.
ReadingHint: This clown has served its purpose. Wave your hand in the air and say bye-bye to this worthless clown.
ReadingMnemonic: When the clown stops being funny, it's time to say <reading>bye</reading> (ばい). At first, the clown <kanji>double</kanji>d the leader's entertainment, but now the clown is just annoying. It's time to say bye-bye.
MeaningMnemonic:
A <radical>leader</radical> who hires his own personal <radical>clown</radical> will likely <kanji>double</kanji> his entertainment. Clowns are funny and make people laugh, so the leader decided to hire one. Since that time, the leader's amount of mirth has doubled. 

This also means <kanji>times</kanji>, which makes sense when you think about the kind of math you'll need to double something. What is A * 2? It's A doubled, first of all, but you're using multiplication. You're "timesing" something.

MeaningHint: Imagine a shellfish opening and closing its mouth, talking to you and telling you what a proud member of its family it is. It's a strange sight, but picture it in your mind anyway.
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to go to the shellfish inn. Imagine all the members-only signs telling you that you can't come in. Womp womp.
ReadingMnemonic: If you were a <kanji>member</kanji> of the same shellfish family, you'd be able to go to the special shellfish <reading>inn</reading> (いん). Every species has a members-only inn.
MeaningMnemonic: Even a <radical>mouth</radical>-bearing <radical>shellfish</radical> is a <kanji>member</kanji> of the invertebrate family. Yes, shellfish do have mouths, look it up!

MeaningHint: Imagine the mountain as small as you'd like. Maybe it's only barely sticking out of the sea, and the bird is having trouble standing on it. Whatever you do, make sure you think of it as an island, possibly owned by the bird.
ReadingHint: The Sheep Mama is a huge, majestic sheep with magical powers. She kicks the bird off the island with her hooves, and the bird squawks and flies away as fast as it can.
ReadingMnemonic: But this <kanji>island</kanji> doesn't only belong to the bird. In fact, the bird shouldn't be on this island, because it belongs to the <reading>She</reading>ep <reading>Ma</reading>ma (しま), who is like Mother Nature but for sheep. The all-powerful Sheep Mama comes to kick the bird off the island, because this place is only for sheep! All hail the Sheep Mama.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>bird</radical> sits upon a <radical>mountain</radical>. If you look at the radicals, the bird is quite large and the mountain quite small. Think of this in the mnemonic as well. The reason the mountain is small is because you can only see the top of it, sticking out of the water. On it is the bird. This small mountain with the bird on it is an <kanji>island</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine being at a moonlit festival and waiting in line for the stool and jackhammer. You don't want to eat candy apples or ride rides, you want to wait your turn to try out the jackhammer (while comfortably seated on a stool, of course).
ReadingHint: Imagine your cyborg going to this festival. Everyone here has some type of cyborg enhancement, like robotic arms, legs, or braces. Your cyborg is really excited to be around its people and play with jackhammers.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>festival</kanji> is for <reading>cy</reading>borgs (さい) only. Picture the cyborg you made getting excited that you're taking it to the festival. That's why all of the activities have to do with heavy machinery. The rides are mostly just cyborgs letting other people ride them, but it's still pretty fun.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> shines down on a <radical>stool</radical> and a <radical>jackhammer</radical> which are there for the upcoming <kanji>festival</kanji>. Festivals need activities, and at this festival, you can sit on a stool in the moonlight and use a jackhammer to make a lot of noise. Perfect for a festival!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself, standing tall in the light of the early morning. You hold your head up high, open to your bookmark, and read the name of the chapter aloud: "Chapter 42: The Most Important Number."
ReadingHint: Picture yourself in a line with other authors, standing in the early morning, waiting to read chapters from your respective books. You really hope the Shougun likes yours. If he does, you'll be famous! If he doesn't, you might be beheaded.
ReadingMnemonic: You're practicing this specific <kanji>chapter</kanji> because you've been asked to read it for the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). There will be a bunch of other authors reading chapters of their books to him too, and you want to make a good impression.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>stand</radical> at a podium <radical>early</radical> in the morning to practice reading the <kanji>chapter</kanji> from your latest book.

MeaningHint: Imagine a line of bamboo shoots arranged from oldest to youngest. They look like pipe organ pipes, or a xylophone, and now you need to count them.
ReadingHint: Now they're in descending order already, and you can count them nice and easy thanks to your dye method.
ReadingMnemonic: To remember you need to use the <kanji>ordinal number prefix</kanji> before you count these bamboo, you <reading>dye</reading> (だい) them.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Bamboo</radical>'s <radical>younger brother</radical> is counted with an <kanji>ordinal number prefix</kanji>. An ordinal number is one that represents a position or rank in sequential order (like first, second, third, etc.). We use this ordinal number prefix before counting these things in Japanese sometimes. Like with bamboo, going down in order toward the youngest.

MeaningHint: Imagine just a ton of someones and buildings all packed together in a small area. More than a village or town or city. With enough someones and buildings, you end up with a metropolis.
ReadingHint: Imagine this huge metropolis. Now imagine every building just being a toe. Tall skyscraper toes. Smaller house toes. Wide apartment building toes. And then the people walking around. They are toes, too. Toes walking around and going into toe buildings, with toe cars and toe dogs. If you imagine the toe metropolis deeply enough, it should be weird enough to burn into your memory.
ReadingMnemonic: In this <kanji>metropolis</kanji> everything is a <reading>toe</reading> (と). The someones are toes, walking around. The buildings are toes, too.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Someone</radical>s and <radical>building</radical>s, someones and buildings, someones and buildings. So many someones and buildings. With so many someones and buildings, we have ourselves a <kanji>metropolis</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a massive 1 ton weight. It’s heavy. You can’t even begin to move it without some sort of powerful machine to help you. Imagine yourself trying to move it with just your bare hands.
ReadingHint: Okay, so you don’t have enough power to move this doughnut as is. In order to move it, you’re going to have to use your superhuman power of eating. Once you eat enough of it, the remainder will be easy to move. Imagine yourself digging into this massive doughnut and taste its fresh baked goodness on your tongue. It’s mighty tasty.
ReadingMnemonic: Remember that 1 ton weight? Well, actually it’s a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう). A huge, massive, 1 ton doughnut. It’s super heavy and hard to move, but it’s delicious.
MeaningMnemonic: When something is <radical>heavy</radical>, you need a lot of <radical>power</radical> to <kanji>move</kanji> it. It’s not going to move unless you use a lot of power. That thing is heavy and unless you use all the power you got, it’s not moving.

MeaningHint: Imagine this mustache entrepreneur, standing at his merchandise stand, selling his wares. He looks quite dignified, legs and all.
ReadingHint: Now all of the merchandise belongs to the Shougun. The mustache has nothing left to sell, but at least he's rich!
ReadingMnemonic: The mustache is selling his <kanji>merchandise</kanji> so well that even the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) notices. He leans down really close to the standing mustache man's merchandise and stays very still. Then he snaps his fingers and his servants come rushing out to buy it all!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>stand</radical>ing <radical>mustache</radical> with <radical>legs</radical> is able to sell <kanji>merchandise</kanji>. Most mustaches can’t stand because they don’t have legs. This mustache, however, does. It also has a keen business sense, which is why it’s selling merchandise.

MeaningHint: You don't want a bunch of evil in your heart, do you? No, I didn't think so.
ReadingHint: Acupuncture was originally invented in Asia a long, long time ago, to stop the bad chi from going through your heart.
ReadingMnemonic: Luckily, people in Asia know how to get the <kanji>evil</kanji> <kanji>bad</kanji>ness out of your heart with <reading>acu</reading>puncture (あく).
MeaningMnemonic: In <radical>Asia</radical> they assume everyone's <radical>heart</radical> is automatically <kanji>bad</kanji> and <kanji>evil</kanji>.

MeaningHint: This guy headed your direction with his gun doesn’t look friendly at all. Hear the sound of the bowstring as you nock your arrow and prepare your shot to defend yourself. Then imagine the sound of everyone in your tribe doing the same.
ReadingHint: You remember what a bosozoku is, right? We wrote about them on Tofugu a while ago. They’re bike gang members and the “zoku” in bosozoku means tribe. Remember that article when you see this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: When he starts to get closer, you notice that this guy is a boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang member. Bosozoku means “running <kanji>tribe</kanji>” and this guy is running right at you. With a gun. So gather your own tribe behind you and prepare to defend yourself against this bosozoku member.
MeaningMnemonic: If someone is headed in your <radical>direction</radical> with a <radical>gun</radical>, nock an <radical>arrow</radical> in your bow and gather your <kanji>tribe</kanji> behind you because it’s about to get real! This man with a gun is headed your direction and he doesn’t even know what you and your tribe are gonna do to him. If he gets any closer, you’re likely to shoot that arrow right at him.

MeaningHint: Picture this tsunami rushing past the tree you managed to climb up. It's a deep one, way higher than your forehead, but you're safe here. Legs exhausted, sure, but safe and sound.
ReadingHint: Imagine all the debris floating in the shin-deep water now, bumping up against your shins. Probably hurts, considering how sensitive shins are.
ReadingMnemonic: You wait until the tsunami is only as <kanji>deep</kanji> as your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). Then you climb down the tree and wade home in the shin-deep water.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> is higher than your <radical>forehead</radical> you better move those <radical>legs</radical> and find a <radical>tree</radical> to climb, because that thing is <kanji>deep</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine the king holding a sphere in his hand, and looking at it. It's a green sphere, and that is because...
ReadingHint: Now imagine the king requesting a cucumber sphere, and holding it up to look at it. If you want, have him take a bite out of the sphere, and say "this sphere is a cucumber?" Imagine the taste of it. Imagine the smell, and its bumpy cucumber skin.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sphere</kanji> is a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) sphere. A strange sphere shaped cucumber.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> <radical>request</radical>s a <kanji>sphere</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture all those juveniles standing on top of buildings in your village, being bored, and doing juvenile things.
ReadingHint: Imagine looking up to the top of the buildings in your village. Thousands of juveniles, staring down at you and everyone else, eating doughnuts. The crumbs falling down on top of you. But, the doughnuts smell good, at least. Maybe you try to catch some crumbs.
ReadingMnemonic: Mostly, though, the  <kanji>juvenile</kanji>s are standing on top of village buildings, eating <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう).
MeaningMnemonic: Those who <radical>stand</radical> on top of a <radical>village</radical> are <kanji>juvenile</kanji>. Imagine a bunch of young people standing on the roofs of buildings in the village. That's a juvenile thing to do.

MeaningHint: All buildings have white marks that you can compare with the rest to see where the floors are.
ReadingHint: Kayaks working at desks. Kayaks that are desks. Kayaks walking around checking on other kayaks. The kayak floor is quite odd. It's odd there is a floor only about kayaks.
ReadingMnemonic: One <kanji>floor</kanji> is just all <reading>kay</reading>aks (かい). You're looking at this building, and every other <kanji>story</kanji> is normal, but that one floor is kayak after kayak after kayak.
MeaningMnemonic: Look at a <radical>building</radical> and <radical>compare</radical> the <radical>white</radical> parts. Each of those white parts is a <kanji>floor</kanji>. Multi-<kanji>story</kanji> buildings have tons of different floors that you can see from the outside.

MeaningHint: Picture the entire roof covered in ice. It's supposed to be spring right now, but it's too cold for the ice to melt off your roof.
ReadingHint: The samurai isn't doing a good job stopping the cold, and soon he and his sword have frozen into your ice roof. So now it's cold and you have a dead samurai stuck to your house. Great.
ReadingMnemonic: You can't stand the <kanji>cold</kanji> anymore, so you hire a <reading>samu</reading>rai (さむ) to come try to hack the ice off of your roof with his sword. You stand outside shivering, and watching as the samurai tries to stab the ice off of your roof.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical>, even though it's almost <radical>spring</radical>, is still covered in <radical>ice</radical> so it's <kanji>cold</kanji>. Inside the house is cold, outside the house is cold. Everything is cold!

MeaningHint: Imagine you're this someone, standing out in the hot sun. Feel the hot rays beating down on you. You're getting all sweaty and sticky from the heat now, ugh.
ReadingHint: Feel the hot rays of sunlight beating down on you from two directions. "Ah, two hot suns is more than I can take," you gasp as your body slowly melts into a human puddle of sweat.
ReadingMnemonic: One sun was <kanji>hot</kanji> enough, but then a second one shows up. "<reading>Ah, two</reading> (あつ)!" you scream. If you were sweaty and sticky from the heat of just one sun, imagine what this second one is going to do to you. Ah, two hot suns!
MeaningMnemonic:
If the <radical>sun</radical> is on <radical>someone</radical>, they will get <kanji>hot</kanji>. That's just how it is. Someone standing out in the sun for a long time will inevitably get hot. The sun is hot, and it makes you get hot too.

Note that this usually refers to hot weather or the heat of an area you're in and doesn't refer to a hot "thing."

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at the moon and seeing a crab on it and thinking: "oh, it's the crab moon <kanji>time period</kanji>."
ReadingHint: Imagine a key with a little crab moon on it, denoting the Crab Moon period of time. Then, go around and lock everything with it and watch thousands of crabs trying to get into everything. Luckily, they can't, because you have a special key that locks things so crabs can't get in. When the Crab Moon time period is over, you can use your key to unlock everything.
ReadingMnemonic: When the crab moon <kanji>period of time</kanji> is upon us, we must take a special <reading>key</reading> (き) and lock everything in such a way that crabs cannot get in.
MeaningMnemonic: A "<radical>crab</radical> <radical>moon</radical>" is a specific <kanji>period of time</kanji>.

MeaningHint: After four or five attempts, you've finally planted a perfectly straight tree. Pat yourself on the back for a job well done.
ReadingHint: After planting your tree and failing again for the 100th time, you stand there in total shock. How could it be this difficult?
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>plant</kanji> and re-plant your tree over and over, utterly <reading>shock</reading>ed (しょく) at how hard it is to plant it right. You thought planting trees was easy, so this is a real shock to you.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want a <radical>tree</radical> to grow <radical>straight</radical>, you have to <kanji>plant</kanji> it properly. Everyone knows that poorly planted trees grow all crooked and twisted. That's just how plants work. If your tree's not straight, start over and plant it again.

MeaningHint: Stop what you're doing and look into the rice box. Nope, it's definitely full of teeth. There's a grain or two of rice, but it's like 98% teeth.
ReadingHint: On second thought, I don't need to explain my box of teeth to you. Hah! Figure it out yourself.
ReadingMnemonic: Why do I have a rice box full of <kanji>teeth</kanji>? <reading>Hah</reading> (は)! Wouldn't you like to know. Hah! I definitely have an answer that isn't weird or creepy…
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Stop</radical>! That <radical>box</radical> of <radical>rice</radical> is actually full of <kanji>teeth</kanji>! I know it says "rice" on the cover, but I promise you those are all teeth and if you bite into them you'll break a <kanji>tooth</kanji> of your own.

MeaningHint: Right after the tsunami, this plate was wet, but the sun is warming and drying the plate. Imagine looking at the plate and seeing the beads of water evaporate off it as it warms up. Feel the warm steam on your face as this happens.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself frying onions on this plate warmed by the sun. Smell the onion smell as the onion fumes get into your eyes and your sinuses. It makes your eyes water as you are very sensitive to onions.
ReadingMnemonic: The plate starts to get so <kanji>warm</kanji> that you could fry an <reading>on</reading>ion (おん) on it. Who doesn’t love fried onions? That plate sure is warm enough, so start cookin’ ‘em!
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>tsunami</radical> which washes a <radical>plate</radical> out into the <radical>sun</radical>. Because it's in the sun, it becomes  <kanji>warm</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the harbor. You're the only one there. The wind is whipping violently and all of the boats docked around you are being bounced around. You can see the tsunami rising out of the ocean and coming for you and the harbor.
ReadingHint: Well this sucks. You wanted to enjoy being alone together with the tsunami and the harbor, but now こういち is here ruining your peace. He's floating around in the harbor like everything isn't about to be destroyed, sipping on a fancy drink in his inner tube.
ReadingMnemonic: As you look around at the <kanji>harbor</kanji> in your last moments you suddenly see someone in the water. It's <reading>こう</reading>いち! He's sitting in an inner tube in the harbor waiting for the tsunami too.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming and you want to be <radical>together</radical> with it by <radical>yourself</radical> (oneself) so you go to the <kanji>harbor</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the boiling hot tsunami water as it comes for you. Psh, this will be easy.
ReadingHint: You decided to live in a yurt because of the easy access it provides to hot water. They're so mobile!
ReadingMnemonic: As the <kanji>hot water</kanji> comes, you just lift up the bottom of the <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ) you live in, and it comes flowing in. All of the cups, pots, and barrels you laid on your floor are now full of hot water.
MeaningMnemonic: If there's a <radical>tsunami</radical> it's <radical>easy</radical> to get <kanji>hot water</kanji>. Just stick your hand out the window as the tsunami goes by and poof! That water's HOT.

MeaningHint: Imagine this tent of beans. Make it huge, bulging with beans! Now, get climbing. Feel the beans on the other side of the tent's wall.
ReadingHint: It's beautiful! It's breathtaking! All your climbing makes this view of とうきょう even more worthwhile!
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>climb</kanji> the bean tent, and from this vantage point you can see <reading>とう</reading>きょう in the distance.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>tent</radical> of <radical>beans</radical>. You try to <kanji>climb</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: Try to picture what this king is wearing as he comes down the slide and arrives at the bottom. He can surely afford to wear the finest garments — plus, his horns give him all sorts of options for accessorizing.
ReadingHint: Imagine the sound of horrible chalk squeaks as the king furiously sketches all sorts of crazy outfits on his massive royal chalkboard — all the while muttering "What to wear, what to wear?"
ReadingMnemonic: How does this king decide what to <kanji>wear</kanji> each day? He uses <reading>chalk</reading> (ちゃく) to sketch out his wardrobe ideas on a chalkboard. This helps him <kanji>arrive</kanji> at a conclusion faster.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>horn</radical>ed <radical>king</radical> on a <radical>slide</radical> catches your <radical>eye</radical> because you love what he's chosen to <kanji>wear</kanji>. You can't wait for him to <kanji>arrive</kanji> at the bottom of the slide so you can check out his outfit close-up.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself shooting this arrow. Imagine yourself nocking the arrow to the bow, and pulling the string back. That bean plant is not short enough, and you're going to do something about it.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself being out in the sun for so long that you get an amazing tan. Your skin feels warm all over from all the sun you were getting throughout the day. Feel the warmth coming off of your tanned skin.
ReadingMnemonic: All this shooting of arrows to make the bean plants <kanji>short</kanji> is taking a long time. Since you're out in the sun, you're starting to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). After the day is finally over, your tan is really, really dark.
MeaningMnemonic: If you use an <radical>arrow</radical> to shoot a stalk of <radical>beans</radical>, the bean plant will end up <kanji>short</kanji>. When the beans are hit with that arrow, it severs the plant in two, and you are left with a stalk of beans that is much shorter than before.

MeaningHint: It makes sense for a big open field to have been what was there before the village was made.
ReadingHint: It was sad that they had to destroy the natural habitat of the Yankees (a field) to put up the village.
ReadingMnemonic: On this <kanji>field</kanji>, the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や) used to play.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>village</radical> <radical>beforehand</radical> (what the village was before it was a village) was a <kanji>field</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine these geysers and the white water shooting from the springs under the ground. Now imagine yourself standing over one when the water shoots out. You’d get super wet and probably knocked over due to the force of the spring water. Imagine this happening to yourself.
ReadingHint: Imagine this centaur pushing its way out from the hole in the ground, plugging the water as it squeezes through the hole. Why was there a centaur in the spring? Once it gets out, imagine the centaur standing in front of the spring as it bubbles up and starts shooting water out again. This spring belongs to the centaur.
ReadingMnemonic: From the <kanji>spring</kanji>, a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) emerges.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>White</radical> <radical>water</radical> shoots out from a <kanji>spring</kanji> in the ground. Due to the force the water shoots from the spring, the water looks white with all the air bubbles.

MeaningHint: Just remember that this "suit" means "to suit you" - not the thing you wear, okay!
ReadingHint: Imagine you two standing next to each other, posing for a photograph. You two suit each other well. Grandpa ごういち with his gray beard, and you with your [well, you have to fill in this part by yourself].
ReadingMnemonic:
You need someone who will <kanji>suit</kanji> you. Someone who can <kanji>join</kanji> you on your life journey. So you call Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.

Note: For the ごう reading mnemonics, we use Grandpa ごういち because こういち is used for the こう reading. So just remember that ごう comes from Koichi's grandpa ごういち.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>suit</radical> and the kanji <kanji>suit</kanji> are exactly the same! This kanji can also mean <kanji>join</kanji> which makes sense. When something suits you well, like a hat or something, it joins together with you quite well too. Join me, my hat! You suit my head well. It just <kanji>fit</kanji>s, you know?

MeaningHint: He's already eyeing you down, loitering this close to my skin. Might wanna... back up a little.
ReadingHint: Imagine him turning from a frown to a broad smile as he smells the fragrant curry you've brought.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>He</kanji> may get grumpy if you loiter around my skin, but a well-made <reading>curry</reading> (かれ) always brightens his mood. So give him some curry.
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around my <radical>skin</radical>, <kanji>he</kanji> will get angry at you. He's my very protective friend, and you don't wanna make him angry!

MeaningHint: This version of <kanji>I</kanji> is more boyish. It's a sort of young man/boy way of saying "I," though you'll hear women use it too sometimes (it's a teensy bit tomboyish though).
ReadingHint: I am beaucoup I am. (Is that a pun? Did I do it right?) I dunno. Just say ぼく a lot and try to connect all of your approximate knowledges of languages together. Merci ぼく from ぼく.
ReadingMnemonic: You use the word <kanji>I</kanji> a lot. You say it so much, you just start referring to yourself with the french <reading>beaucoup</reading> (ぼく) for a lot. Because you love yourself a lot.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>business</radical>? Ha! That's <kanji>me</kanji>! That's <kanji>I</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself being really, really tired. Then look at your skin. It's all blotchy and sickly looking.
ReadingHint: Feel all the heat (ひ) emanating from your body. It makes you realize how very, very tired you are.
ReadingMnemonic: Why so <kanji>tired</kanji>? Your body is <reading>hea</reading>ted (ひ) up from exercising way too much. You should let that heat cool down so your skin starts looking better.
MeaningMnemonic: I have <radical>sick</radical> in my <radical>skin</radical>. Just look at it, it doesn't look too good. Whenever I have sickness in my skin, I know that I'm probably <kanji>tired</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine having your mouth bashed into a gate. It’s going to hurt. Imagine how painful this would be and how problematic it is. Imagine that the only question in your mind is how to get to the hospital as quickly as you possibly can.
ReadingHint: Monsters are scary. Maybe you’ve had a dream where you were being chased by one. Remember how scary that is when you see this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: The reason you ran mouth first into this gate was because you were being chased by a <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん). A monster in and of itself is a big enough <kanji>problem</kanji> as is, let alone smashing your mouth into a gate. You poor, hapless soul.
MeaningMnemonic: If you run into a <radical>gate</radical> <radical>mouth</radical> first, you’re going to have a <kanji>problem</kanji>. Smashing your mouth into a gate is going to hurt. A lot. The problem you’re now in is one of getting to a hospital and having your mouth sewn back on. The <kanji>question</kanji> is, how are you going to get to the hospital?

MeaningHint: Back in the old days a lodge had to be huuuuge so when a leader brought a hundred people through town they'd have a place to stay.
ReadingHint: Imagine being someone staying at the lodge as they came in and everything shook around you. This lodge would probably last through an earthquake too!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>lodge</kanji> <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) as the hundred people came tumbling in through the door. It shook, but it didn't fall over. The roof stayed firmly in place, and everyone got to sleep under it okay.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> under which the <radical>leader</radical> can house one <radical>hundred</radical> of his people is at the local <kanji>lodge</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Can you picture a tree without eyes and a heart imagining something? Contemplating? Probably not. To imagine or contemplate, a tree definitely needs eyes to observe and a heart to experience feelings.
ReadingHint: Use your soul to imagine what this tree is imagining with its soul. Is it contemplating the deep mysteries of the universe? Is it imagining where that squirrel has gone off to?
ReadingMnemonic: If a <radical>tree</radical> can <kanji>imagine</kanji>, it likely has a <reading>soul</reading> (そう). Its soul is what makes it possible for the tree to <kanji>contemplate</kanji> things, you see?
MeaningMnemonic: Only a <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>eye</radical>s and a <radical>heart</radical> has the ability to <kanji>imagine</kanji> and to <kanji>contemplate</kanji>. For a tree to imagine and contemplate things, it needs eyes to see and a heart to feel.

MeaningHint: The feeling is kind of hard to describe. On the one hand it's warm and loving, but on the other hand it's also kind of wet and slobbery.
ReadingHint: Just be careful not to let in all the Genghis Khan feelings. The confidence and motivation are good, but the feeling of wanting to wantonly slaughter your enemies is probably one you ought to repress.
ReadingMnemonic: Overall, though, it's a good <kanji>feeling</kanji> — so good that you feel like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! With this canine lovingly slobbering on your heart, you feel like you could go out and conquer the world!
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>canine</radical> puts its <radical>mouth</radical> on your <radical>heart</radical>, it's a pretty unforgettable <kanji>feeling</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture all the things you've arranged correctly ahead of winter — logs bundled for the fire, blankets for the night, and yourself, all bundled up in your neatly organized home — just as the first snowflakes begin to fall.
ReadingHint: Last winter you tripped and fell on a saber you'd left lying around. Ouch, lesson learned. That's when your passion for organizing things in bundles started. You know better than anyone the value of arranging your sabers as safely and tidily as possible.
ReadingMnemonic: But the most important things to <kanji>arrange</kanji> and <kanji>organize</kanji> are your <reading>sa</reading>bers (せい). You have an extensive saber collection that you simply must organize before winter arrives.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>bundle</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> is the <radical>correct</radical> way to <kanji>arrange</kanji> things. Just make sure you <kanji>organize</kanji> it all before winter comes, or it'll be too late.

MeaningHint: Imagine a weird sound coming from the sun. Maybe a rumble, or something like a balloon deflating? Then everything goes totally dark. Welp, guess you'll be living in the dark for the rest of your life.
ReadingHint: And because it's so dark, the anchovy taste is all you can concentrate on. Imagine that fishy, salty taste on your tongue, the little softened bones sticking between your teeth. Ugh.
ReadingMnemonic: Now wandering around your house in the <kanji>dark</kanji> you start to feel hungry, so you reach blindly for some food and take a bite. Yuck! It's <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん). You don't even like anchovies, how did they get in your house? Hmm… Dark forces are at work here…
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>sun</radical> starts to make a <radical>sound</radical>, you know it's about to go <kanji>dark</kanji>. The sun isn't supposed to make a sound, so if you can hear it, it's probably about to fizzle out or something. After it's gone, obviously everything will be pitch black. Get ready to be in the dark!

MeaningHint: Imagine what Sheep-sama must have done to become such important and distinguished sheep royalty. Some pretty amazing things, probably, especially for a sheep.
ReadingHint: Imagine playing in the water with Sheep-sama in the summer. It's crazy hot outside in the summer, so going in the water feels nice. Imagine the coolness of the refreshing water envelop your body.
ReadingMnemonic: This sheep with the <kanji>formal name ender</kanji> only likes to play outside in the water during the <reading>summer</reading> (さま). Summer is the best season for this fancy sheep. The other seasons just can't compare.
MeaningMnemonic:
By a <radical>tree</radical>, there's a <radical>sheep</radical> in the <radical>water</radical> with a <kanji>formal name ender</kanji>. That means everyone calls him Sheep-sama. No one calls him just "Sheep," since he's kind of a big deal. He's actually like sheep royalty. When he's not in his castle, Sheep-sama enjoys playing in the water by the trees.

This can also mean <kanji>manner</kanji>, as in your manner of doing things. I guess someone with a formal name ender has a different manner of doing things.

MeaningHint: Picture this majestic tree bridge laid on its side, beckoning you into heaven with its mustachioed mouth. "Cross over!" it says in its bridge voice. Do… do you just step over the mouth? Can you wipe your shoes on the mustache, like it's a bristly doormat to heaven?
ReadingHint: Hear the bridge shouting "HASH BROWNS" in its bridge voice. You stuff some hash into its mouth and it starts to chew loudly. Oh, ew… Little hash brown bits are getting stuck in its mustache. Fortunately, the bridge lets you across. Unfortunately, it turns out all of heaven smells like hash browns.
ReadingMnemonic: Actually, to get across this <kanji>bridge</kanji> you have to give it some <reading>hash</reading> (はし) browns. That's what the bridge's mouth is for. Hash browns to get across the heaven bridge? Small price to pay, really.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> must've been sent from <radical>heaven</radical>, because it has a <radical>mouth</radical> and a <radical>mustache</radical>. Like some kind of special heaven tree that talks. And why was it sent? To be a <kanji>bridge</kanji>. To be the great heavenly bridge that we all, someday, must cross.

MeaningHint: Still, imagine pulling Wolverine from X-Men out of the water with that thread, and seeing him looking kinda green. Pretty green, but he could be greener. We have to go greener! We have to make the Hulk green with envy! So you put him back in the water, again and again…
ReadingHint: These Marvel crossovers are getting ridiculous.
ReadingMnemonic: As the flailing Wolverine begins to turn even more <kanji>green</kanji>, he calls out, "HELP <reading>ME DORY</reading> (みどり)!" Me, Dory? No, not you. He's calling out to that fish from Finding Nemo, another Disney IP. "Help me Dory, I'm green!" Then Dory leaps out of the water to bite through the thread and save Wolverine… THE END. Fade to green.
MeaningMnemonic: If you tie a <radical>thread</radical> around <radical>Wolverine</radical> and dip him in <radical>water</radical>, he'll turn <kanji>green</kanji>, like the Hulk. Some kind of weird chemical reaction. But I… I wouldn't try it if I were you. You wouldn't like him when he's green.

MeaningHint: You're absolutely obsessed with pointing your thread towards the east, so all you do is practice. Practice, practice, practice.
ReadingHint: Everything in your life is a rental, so you better get to practicing. Your house, your car, your friends — all of these rentals need to be paid off!
ReadingMnemonic: You do all of this <kanji>practice</kanji> to try and afford all of your <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん). One day, all of this practice will pay for all of your rentals.
MeaningMnemonic: Pointing a <radical>thread</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> is what you <kanji>practice</kanji> every day. You're incredibly passionate about eastward thread-pointing, so you make sure to practice every day.

MeaningHint: Picture all of the people at the temple reciting poems together. You join in too, and it's a really powerful moment!
ReadingHint:
You turn to the person next to you and realize you have to say everything in poem form.

Excuse me
I'm not a creep
so hear my plea
is that a sheep?
ReadingMnemonic: As you recite a long <kanji>poem</kanji> at the temple you see a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) in the crowd. It's opening and closing its mouth like it wants to say the poem too.
MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> at a <radical>temple</radical> is a <kanji>poem</kanji>. It has to be a poem, because if you say anything else it's just plain disrespectful. All of the monks and priests at this temple have to read and recite poems all day, and if they can do it forever, you can while you're visiting.

MeaningHint: Imagine taking a gold coin and scrubbing it with some soap and water. As you scrub, you slowly see that it's really silver underneath. Dang it, silver again!
ReadingHint: Guinea pigs are to silver as normal pigs are to truffles.
ReadingMnemonic: If you want to hunt for some <kanji>silver</kanji> for yourself, all you need is a <reading>guin</reading>ea (ぎん) pig. Guinea pigs can smell silver, so get a couple and set them loose in the woods and they'll lead you straight to it.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical>, at its <radical>root</radical>, is made up of <kanji>silver</kanji>. Using silver as the root of a coin is a great way to save on gold! That's why, often, if you just scratch gold, you'll find there's silver hidden underneath, at its root.

MeaningHint: Everywhere you go, you overhear someone talking about your stylish geoduck-patterned jacket. It's the only topic anyone wants to talk about.
ReadingHint: Imagine how annoying it is to constantly be asked about the dye you used in your jacket. You'd really like to change the topic — some might even say you're dye-ing to change it…
ReadingMnemonic: After a few days, the <kanji>topic</kanji> changes from the cool geoduck design to what kind of <reading>dye</reading> (だい) you used to get those real-to-life geoduck colors.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> shines down on your <radical>coat rack</radical> to reveal your <radical>geoduck</radical>-patterned jacket, which then becomes the major <kanji>topic</kanji> of discussion all around town.

MeaningHint: Picture a public building, like a museum or a library, and look at the roof. There's a bear eating the roof!
ReadingHint: Imagine Genghis Khan signing something and giving a speech talking about how important it is that all bears are able to eat the roofs of public buildings, as a bear in the background eats the roof.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Public building</kanji>s must make their roofs available for eating because of a law passed by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Eat</radical>ing the <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>bear</radical>, because the roof is a <kanji>public building</kanji>. Because it is a public building bears are allowed to eat the roof.

MeaningHint: Imagine the horse and the shrimp sitting next to each other on a small bench. There is nobody else at the train station. It is awkward.
ReadingHint: Instead of saying "a key" he says "eh key." How strange. Imagine the station master saying it like this with his weird accent. You ask him if he has "a key" and he replies with, "What is a key?" Then you say "eh key." He totally understands you now.
ReadingMnemonic: The train pulls into the <kanji>station</kanji>. They walk up to the train doors, but they don't open. "Perhaps <reading>a key</reading> (えき) would help?" asks the station master, as he goes over to open the door to the train.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> and a <radical>shrimp</radical> are sitting next to each other. They are both at the train <kanji>station</kanji>, waiting for a train to come and take them home.

MeaningHint: Imagine a hundred million free puppies. That’s a lot. Imagine playing with all of them. You’d be pretty happy. Unless you hate puppies. Then you’d be pretty unhappy, I imagine.
ReadingHint: Picture this cult leader gathering a hundred million people into Oklahoma. It’d be awfully crowded. Imagine yourself being stifled by the bodies of a hundred million other people.
ReadingMnemonic: The cult leader has his headquarters in <reading>Ok</reading>lahoma (おく). Are there a <kanji>hundred million</kanji> people in Oklahoma? No, probably not. But hey, this leader is shooting for the stars. First Oklahoma, then the universe!
MeaningMnemonic: A cult <radical>leader</radical> has a great <radical>idea</radical> to recruit a <kanji>hundred million</kanji> new members. Since he’s the guy with all the ideas, he is the leader of the cult. His idea is to give every new member a free puppy. He’ll have a hundred million new recruits in no time.

MeaningHint: Imagine having all these big products. How will you contain them all!? Imagine yourself stuffing all of these big products into their respective containers.
ReadingHint: Imagine the size of these big keys you have to use to lock these containers. It’s a really big key. Picture yourself straining to turn the key in the lock.
ReadingMnemonic: Once you get the big products into a <kanji>container</kanji>, you then lock it with a <reading>key</reading> (き). You wouldn’t want these products to bust out of their containers, so it’s best to lock them up with a key.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Big</radical> <radical>products</radical> need to be put in a big <kanji>container</kanji>. A small container just won’t do. These products are big and they need a big container to be contained in.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Picture the sheep samurai. Maybe draw him on a piece of paper. He's a sheep wearing wool armor, and he has ram horns on the hilt of his katana.
ReadingMnemonic: The most famous <kanji>samurai</kanji> in Japanese history was the illustrious <reading>shee</reading>p (し) samurai. He wasn't JUST a sheep who learned the ways of the sword, he would use the wool from the sheep enemies he killed to create his armor!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>samurai</radical> radical and the <kanji>samurai</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine ladling up a nice big scoop of rice and then seeing the little fee ladle next to it. Ugh. The material fee.
ReadingHint: That's a lot of material you have to give up every time you want to eat some rice. Maybe you should take this row boat oar ladle and row out into the ocean where no one can tax you.
ReadingMnemonic: One day, the <kanji>fee</kanji> ladle is suddenly the size of <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat oars! What! You're supposed to pay a row boat sized fee now?!
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>rice</radical> <radical>ladle</radical> that's specifically for paying the rice <kanji>fee</kanji>. Each scoop of something has an extra fee tacked on, so when you use a <kanji>material</kanji>, there's a fee you have to pay in that material as well.

MeaningHint: This is not the best signpost you've ever seen, but it's better than nothing at all, you suppose.
ReadingHint: Press it a few more times and hear the sound in your head. Also spell it out in kana as you hear it as a nice little additional visual.
ReadingMnemonic: As you look at this weird <kanji>signpost</kanji> you notice a button. You press it because it will supposedly tell you more about these dangerous helicopters and jackhammers. Instead, it just yells out in a loud obnoxious voice: <reading>HEYO</reading> (ひょう)!!
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>tree</radical> with a <radical>helicopter</radical> and a <radical>jackhammer</radical> tied to it. You look closer, because this is an odd sight, and realize that there is a sign here too. It's telling you to beware of helicopters and jackhammers in these woods. It's a poorly made <kanji>signpost</kanji>!

MeaningHint: You didn't just cut the tree down, you stabbed it and sliced it and killed it to death.
ReadingHint: Here's that little girl Satsuki again. Remember how you imagined her last time? Try to have a picture of her in your mind so these さつ kanji are easier to remember.
ReadingMnemonic: But after the <kanji>kill</kanji>, <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) appears and yells at you. She likes trees — remember how she protected one in the 札 mnemonic? — so Satsuki's pretty upset you'd kill one like that.
MeaningMnemonic: There's <radical>treasure</radical> in this <radical>tree</radical> so you take out your <radical>weapon</radical> and <kanji>kill</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: Picture a weird-looking, misshapen crescent moon casting its light onto the scene of a dog boiling up everything you'd find in nature. Trees, sticks, mud, little animals. All of that is being boiled by this dog by the light of a weird-looking moon.
ReadingHint: Picture the dog kneeling in front of the pot of concentrated nature and breathing deeply as it practiced Zen. You kind of wish you could join it, considering how peaceful it looks.
ReadingMnemonic: Destroying <kanji>nature</kanji> like this is the dog's way of practicing <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) Buddhism. As it breathes in the fumes from all of the different pieces of nature being boiled down, the dog gets really zen and chill.
MeaningMnemonic: Under a misshapen crescent <radical>moon</radical>, a <radical>dog</radical> <radical>boil</radical>s as much <kanji>nature</kanji> as it can find.

MeaningHint: Even if you don't have a womb, just picture one anyway: it's turning around in a circle faster and faster, as the heat bubbles and boils in you. You can feel this heat spread through your entire body as you burn up with a fever.
ReadingHint: These nets aren't making the fever heat go away, but they have made the spinning stop at least.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>heat</kanji> from your <kanji>fever</kanji> is making your insides and your head and your entire body spin and boil, until your doctor throws some <reading>nets</reading> (ねつ) around you. You were flailing around and they couldn't get you to stop, so they had to catch you in a series of nets.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>womb</radical> spins in a <radical>circle</radical> and starts to <radical>boil</radical> with <kanji>heat</kanji>. Uh oh, I think you have a <kanji>fever</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Think about it. Every grocery store has the fruit section right at the front, wouldn’t you say?
ReadingHint: Picture yourself driving a car and salivating over the fruit waiting for you in the fruit section. Imagine your favorite fruit as you put the pedal to the metal in your awesome car.
ReadingMnemonic: To get to the fruit <kanji>section</kanji>, you have to go to the store. To get to the store you have to drive a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Driving a car is the fastest way to get to the fruit section at the store.
MeaningMnemonic: I always <radical>say</radical> the <radical>fruit</radical> <kanji>section</kanji> at the grocery should be at the front of the store. It’s the perfect place for the fruit section, I say.

MeaningHint: Imagine a game at a fair where you have to throw a shellfish into the mouth of a hungry Viking. You want this prize just as much as the Viking wants his shellfish.
ReadingHint: You want this prize. You want to meet the Shougun. Soon you'll both be eating shellfish together!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>prize</kanji> you're working so hard to win is lunch with the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). This is a pretty huge prize, considering all you're doing is knocking something over.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Viking</radical> opens his <radical>mouth</radical>, and below it, a <radical>shellfish</radical> sits. Grab that shellfish and throw it into his mouth to win a <kanji>prize</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine this wheel you made out of a hat, some ground (presumably to keep it all together), and a bookshelf. You install it on your car and hope for the best. This is why you should buy wheels from professional wheel makers.
ReadingHint: A ring-shaped wheel is better than nothing. Hopefully it will get you where you need to go.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>wheel</kanji> is... generally <reading>rin</reading>g (りん) shaped, so that's good.
MeaningMnemonic: For your <radical>car</radical> you use a <radical>hat</radical>, some <radical>ground</radical> and a <radical>bookshelf</radical> to build a new <kanji>wheel</kanji>. I mean, it's generally <kanji>ring</kanji> and <kanji>loop</kanji> shaped, it just needs to be a <kanji>circle</kanji>, so it should work, right?

MeaningHint: Your angel self says you should choose the safest scooter. The devil self says safety is for suckers, and you should buy one that's loud and dangerous and covered in flames.
ReadingHint: The angel self says, "I choose this centaur scooter because of its strong arms." The devil self says, "I choose this scooter because it looks like a badass horse monster, wooh!"
ReadingMnemonic: Suddenly your selfs both gasp and <kanji>choose</kanji> a scooter in front of you that looks like a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). The scooter is designed like a human on the top, the bars are its arms, and the bottom with the seat and the wheels are all horse. This centaur scooter is what you choose to ride.
MeaningMnemonic: You want to get a <radical>scooter</radical>, so you have one self (<radical>oneself</radical>) and another self working <radical>together</radical> to <kanji>choose</kanji>. The two tiny yous sit on each of your shoulders, like angel and devil, and argue about which scooter you should choose.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at yourself in the mirror only to see you're made of gold. It would be pretty shocking. Imagine how surprised you would be.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself in front of a mirror, except your reflection is Chairman Kaga Takeshi. (Look up his picture if it helps.) Imagine not recognizing yourself at first, how weird that would feel, then suddenly remembering: "Kaga… me!"
ReadingMnemonic: As you look into the <kanji>mirror</kanji> you think, "<reading>Kaga</reading>… <reading>me</reading> (かがみ)?" You always forget that you're Chairman Kaga from the Iron Chef TV show. That's why you say "Kaga… me?" every time you look in the mirror.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical> reveals itself when you <radical>stand</radical> and <radical>see</radical> into this <kanji>mirror</kanji>. Yes, even you are made of gold. You're so great and valuable, standing in front of that mirror. It's not hard to see, especially in a mirror – you're golden.

MeaningHint: Imagine eating a fish fin with sheep wool on it. It tastes fishy like the ocean but the wool tastes like clothes. It's probably the worst thing ever, but try to imagine you're tasting it right now.
ReadingHint: Remember how bad those sheep fins tasted? Now imagine eating your favorite yogurt flavor instead. This yogurt is delicious.
ReadingMnemonic: The best way to <kanji>foster</kanji> a better relationship with these foster kids would be to give them some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Maybe they can dip the sheep fins in the yogurt to help mask the strange taste. Yogurt is also very nutritious, so it's a good supplement to the foster kids' diet.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Sheep</radical> <radical>fins</radical> are <radical>good</radical> for <kanji>foster</kanji> children. The sheep have been genetically combined with fish, so the sheep have fins (and they've lost their tails — which is why the tail part of the sheep radical is missing in this kanji). These sheep fins are rich in nutrients, so they're good for fostering big, strong foster children.

MeaningHint: Start with a blue soul. What feeling is that? Now a red soul. What emotion is that? Go through as many colors as you can think of, focusing on the fact that each color represents a different emotion or feeling.
ReadingHint: Maybe he was just having a bad day, but that still really hurt… and now your soul’s all blue too. Seems you’ll have to walk around with a blue soul for the next few days.
ReadingMnemonic: Why is your soul <kanji>feeling</kanji> blue? Because <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big local farmhand, was extremely mean to you.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> is <radical>blue</radical>. As in, it's sad and not feeling well. This is because your soul changes colors depending on your <kanji>feeling</kanji>s or what <kanji>emotion</kanji>s you're expressing.

MeaningHint: When you do a bad thing you should apologize. And saying everyone's private body measurements is definitely something you should not have done. So it's apology time.
ReadingHint: "What do we say?" the shaman says, poking you in the back. "Ugh, I'm sorry I told everyone how tiny your socks are, I guess." "And?" "Annnddd I'll never do it again."
ReadingMnemonic: You were told you had to <kanji>apologize</kanji> by the village <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ). At first you didn't even know why everyone was glaring at you, but the shaman took you aside and explained. And now she's standing behind you with her arms crossed as you apologize to everybody.
MeaningMnemonic: You thought it would be a good idea to <radical>say</radical> the <radical>body</radical> <radical>measurement</radical>s of everyone in your village and now you have to <kanji>apologize</kanji>.

MeaningHint: She's just acting so weird. You doubt she would ever make you hunt for your own meal like this. What are you going to do?
ReadingHint: With her head in the guillotine, it's your mama who starts to look doubtful now. "How could you ever doubt me?" she cries. Ugh, now you're really not sure. This guillotine thing has just compounded your doubts.
ReadingMnemonic: Now full of <kanji>doubt</kanji>, you decide to test your mama by putting her in a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). It sounds harsh, but the guillotine is the only way to clear up your doubts.
MeaningMnemonic: You reach for a <radical>spoon</radical> to start eating, but your <radical>mama</radical> hands you an <radical>arrow</radical> instead and points towards the <radical>coat rack</radical>. Hm, that's not like her… Suddenly you're filled with <kanji>doubt</kanji>. Is this really your mama? You doubt it.

MeaningHint: Just look at the color white. Compare it to other colors. Now look at white again. Split it up into all the colors, creating a beautiful spectrum. Wow, it's <kanji>all</kanji> the colors!
ReadingHint: Imagine everything covered in white. Everything in the world. Then, you see a dark splotch. It's the miners, they're all covered in coal, ruining all your ambitions. Walk up to them and see all the dirt on the perfectly white uniforms you gave them just a few minutes earlier. Feel enraged at them. How could they ruin all this white you made for <kanji>all</kanji> the people of the world!?
ReadingMnemonic: You decide white light is the ultimate color. You decide you want to put it on <kanji>all</kanji> things to make everything white. "What a great idea," you think. You try to put it on everything and you nearly succeed... except, the <reading>miners</reading> (みな). They keep getting dirty doing all their mining things. NOOO, your plan is ruined!
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>compare</radical> <radical>white</radical> light to the other colors you'll realize... white consists of <kanji>all</kanji> the colors in one. It contains all the colors in the spectrum in one color. It is the one color that is <kanji>everything</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a tin can in your mind as clearly as you can.
ReadingHint: かん is can, it's really easy, come on. Just take this one.
ReadingMnemonic:
The reading for a <kanji>can</kanji> is かん, which seems impossibly easy. If that isn't close enough for you, picture this:

Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) is drinking out of a tin can. He glug glug glugs, gasps, and wipes his face. He holds out the can, "Khan made cans," and winks.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical <radical>can</radical> is the same as the kanji <kanji>can</kanji>. Usually when we talk about a can with this kanji we mean a <kanji>tin can</kanji>, though.

MeaningHint: Remember, this is the OG = original geoduck. There are no other geoducks yet.
ReadingHint:
People listened when Gandhi said, "My friends. We must stop trying to eat the geoduck, and instead allow it to breed. Join me in this fast!"

And that's why we have so many geoducks on the earth today. Thanks Gandhi.
ReadingMnemonic: The only person that would even hear the geoduck's <kanji>request</kanji> was <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). Gandhi closed his eyes, sat down, and spread the geoduck's request to the rest of the world.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>original</radical> <radical>geoduck</radical> had a <kanji>request</kanji>. He wants there to be more geoducks in the world!

MeaningHint: The leader of the yakuza is just showing you an example of a knife. Maybe to scare you? Why would an example of what a knife is scare you, though? Coming from the leader of the yakuza, this is a weird tactic.
ReadingHint: Why is the leader of the yakuza showing you examples of weapons, not to mention weapons that aren't real, like ray guns?
ReadingMnemonic: "This is an <kanji>example</kanji>," the leader of the yakuza tells you. "Next time, I'll show you an example of a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun."
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>yakuza</radical> shows you his <radical>knife</radical>. He says, "This is an <kanji>example</kanji> of a knife."

MeaningHint: Try to picture the lid you give to the person(s), then their putting it on a cross. Why does this ceremony symbolize graduation? Maybe the persons are your teachers, and it's about the divine knowledge you've now finished collecting under your "lid," or something. Congrats?
ReadingHint: Imagine walking up to the stage with no lid, and the person(s) just say, "Sorry, you can't graduate. So two more years. Next!"
ReadingMnemonic: Uh oh, looks like you forgot your lid. They decide not to let you <kanji>graduate</kanji>. <reading>So two</reading> (そつ) more years now until you can graduate, it seems. So two more years?! Just for forgetting a lid? Nonsense!
MeaningMnemonic: You give a <radical>lid</radical> to a <radical>person</radical> (two of them, in fact), then they put it on a <radical>cross</radical> when you <kanji>graduate</kanji>. It's a symbolic ceremony, putting a lid over a cross, but it's just what you do when you graduate around these parts.

MeaningHint: The power of cooperation is a *ahem* powerful thing.
ReadingHint: Imagine being a citizen of きょうと. Watch as the powers from your neighboring prefectures march across the border to come cooperate together to stop the bandits that raided your farm. You admire their cooperation. You admire きょうと!
ReadingMnemonic: Nowhere is the power of cooperation more apparent than in <reading>きょう</reading>と. When invaders are attacking, when the townspeople want to rebel, when there is a kitty up a tree, the people combine their power from all reaches of Japan and cross into きょうと to put a stop to the madness through cooperation.
MeaningMnemonic: Next to the <radical>cross</radical> we combine our <radical>power</radical> <radical>power</radical> <radical>power</radical> so that we have <kanji>cooperation</kanji>. And with our powers combined, we become stronger than we ever could have been apart, lifting the cross above our heads!

MeaningHint: When you're thinking about your next hair style, if you don't like participating in things, you should think about something you can actually keep more private.
ReadingHint: Would you want to participate in Christmas if it meant looking like Santa and visiting every single house on earth?
ReadingMnemonic: You know who has big hair and loves to <kanji>participate</kanji> in everything? <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん)! Well, his hair is more big on the bottom (it's a beard) than whatever you have on your head, but hair is hair. And he participates in present-giving in every house in the world! See? Big hair = participation!
MeaningMnemonic: You try to be <radical>private</radical>, but because of your <radical>big</radical> <radical>hair</radical> you end up having to <kanji>participate</kanji> in everything. Can you imagine people asking for volunteers or something, and you trying to look inconspicuous with your big hair? Of course they're going to notice you and make you participate.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Try to find the circumference of your shoe and then email us with the measurement. I promise you'll never forget this kanji if you actually do it.
ReadingMnemonic: Practice how to measure the <kanji>circumference</kanji> of an item by using your <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう). Your shoe is kind of round, and it's free. I mean it's right there on your foot.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>circumference</radical> radical and the <kanji>circumference</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself surrounded by this well on all sides. You look up and you can barely see the mouth of the well where you fell in and now you are trapped. Imagine how scared you are. Imagine trying to claw your way up the sides of the well but all you do is break your nails on the walls.
ReadingHint: This eagle is basically a superhero. Imagine an eagle dressed in a superman outfit. He’s your savior. Imagine your gratitude towards this eagle superhero.
ReadingMnemonic: The well walls <kanji>surround</kanji> you as you look up to the mouth once again. This time you see an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) flying overhead. You call out to the eagle to save you and the eagle comes swooping down into the well and flies you out to safety. What a nice eagle!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>well</radical> will <kanji>surround</kanji> you if you fall into it. The mouth of a well is the opening of the well, and if you fall into that well, the mouth as well as the rest of the well will surround you.

MeaningHint: Go ahead and touch that old man's mouth. It's super hard feeling when you touch it.
ReadingHint: "You pressed the hard skin holding me captive! Thank you!" the 子 says, as they break free from their hard mouth prison.
ReadingMnemonic: You touch the old man's <kanji>hard</kanji> mouth and it crumbles away, revealing the face of a <reading>子</reading> (こ) inside.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>mouth</radical> that is <radical>old</radical> is going to be <kanji>hard</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Watch the grain children live their entire lives throughout the seasons. Though there are 72 seasons in Japan, so the life of a grain is actually really long there.
ReadingHint: Picture these seasonal keys floating through the sky, unlocking the weather changes, the growth of new grains, and the aging of us all.
ReadingMnemonic: All of the <kanji>seasons</kanji> have their own special <reading>key</reading> (き). As they change, that season's key appears and unlocks the next step in the grain's life.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>grain</radical>'s <radical>child</radical> will grow with the <kanji>seasons</kanji>. In spring the grain is born, it grows in summer, is harvested in fall, and dies in winter.

MeaningHint: Picture this absolutely perfect roof. They really got it right on their first try.
ReadingHint: Genghis Khan was a perfectionist, and by conquering and bringing all of these different people together, he was able to create something perfect by all standards.
