ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>perfect</kanji> roof was commissioned by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). We got all of the carpenters and home makers and construction workers he'd conquered together and tasked them with making the first roof, but only if they made it absolutely perfect.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> that became the <radical>origin</radical> of all roofs was <kanji>perfect</kanji>. It was tall and wide so it could fit and protect everyone. And the dimensions were perfect too.

MeaningHint: Picture this narwhal: above its long horn there is a huge, shiny treasure floating. It pulls the towel tight around itself, "Brururu you found me I see!" it whistles at you.
ReadingHint: If you find the key, what will you wish for? Think about your single, greatest wish. Close your eyes and picture it as clearly as you can and you'll find the key.
ReadingMnemonic: In order to open the treasure and get your <kanji>wish</kanji>, you need the <reading>key</reading> (き). The narwhal knows where it's hidden, so you'll have to convince it to tell you where the key is.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>treasure</radical> on top of a <radical>narwhal</radical> who wears a <radical>towel</radical> that will grant your greatest <kanji>wish</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Think about the last time you thought it was right to make a decision with your heart. What was it? Use these emotions to remember this kanji.
ReadingHint: Think of all the things you'd do with your life if you only had one 年 left. Maybe you should go do some of them anyways, right now?
ReadingMnemonic: You're living in the now and letting your heart control all your <kanji>thought</kanji>s. Why are you following your heart? You found you only have one <reading>年</reading> (ねん) to live, so you have to live for the now.
MeaningMnemonic: If you live in the <radical>now</radical>, there’s a good chance your <radical>heart</radical> controls your <kanji>thought</kanji> process. You don’t have time to think with your brain, you need an answer now so you use your heart to make decisions and thought process.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding an axe between your fingers and folding it like a pretzel. Or some silly putty. Or a really soft axe.
ReadingHint: How many axes can you bend at once? A set of two? What about a set of four? Or a set of forty-two?
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, as you <kanji>fold</kanji> these axes in half you realize they come in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ), so there are twice the axes you thought there would be. To compensate, you start folding them in sets too.
MeaningMnemonic: If your <radical>fingers</radical> are strong enough, you can take an <radical>axe</radical> and <kanji>fold</kanji> it in half. But be careful not to <kanji>bend</kanji> it too far, or you might just <kanji>break</kanji> it!

MeaningHint: Now picture the death moon king as the Emperor, sitting in his Death Star. There is no hope, except for that little opening you can shoot through to destroy the whole thing. That's hope, I guess!
ReadingHint: In the end, the real hope was bowling all along. I hope you've learned a valuable lesson from this space movie.
ReadingMnemonic: The name A New <kanji>Hope</kanji>, as we all know, comes from the ending where the moon king's son sends a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball into that little opening and knocks it out of the sky.
MeaningMnemonic: Causing <radical>death</radical> at every turn, the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>king</radical> sits inside his moon, getting ready to cause death on anything in his sights. Sounds a lot like the Death Star from a certain movie. One of which is "Star Wars: A New <kanji>Hope</kanji>."

MeaningHint: Picture a tree standing in the forest. It's thinking so hard you can see the math equations flying around in its branches, until finally a cartoon light bulb appears above its head. This talented tree has figured out how to become lumber!
ReadingHint: Watch as the tree becomes lumber and that lumber is used to craft a ginormous xylophone. Each key is its own plank of lumber!
ReadingMnemonic: Trees can be turned into all kinds of things, but this one wants to use its <kanji>lumber</kanji> and <kanji>material</kanji> to build a <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい). It's the most difficult instrument to create, so it suits the talent level of this tree!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> with <radical>talent</radical> can become <kanji>lumber</kanji>. Not all trees can do this. Only the talented ones are able to turn themselves into lumber or <kanji>material</kanji>, to be used for a million different things.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine doing laundry and making sure to bundle your socks together so none of them will be lost.
ReadingMnemonic: You should always <kanji>bundle</kanji> your <reading>sock</reading>s (そく). If you don't, there is a 100% chance you'll lose all of the left ones.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bundle</radical> radical and the <kanji>bundle</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine climbing up a pine tree. The pine needles are prickly, so your hands get pricked as you climb. Smell the pine tree smell and feel the pine sap on your hands.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself falling out of this pine tree and onto the mats. The mats are nice and soft so it’s nice. Hear the whoosh of air as you collapse onto the mats.
ReadingMnemonic: In case you fall, someone has laid <reading>mats</reading> (まつ) out around the base of the <kanji>pine</kanji> tree. That way, you’ll land on the mats should something unfortunate happen.
MeaningMnemonic: When climbing a <radical>tree</radical> in <radical>public</radical>, it’s best to choose a <kanji>pine</kanji>. Pine trees offer the most coverage, so you’ll be able to hide, even out in public.

MeaningHint: Imagine the yakuza bars all being shut down. There's only one place where they can remain.
ReadingHint: Imagine a yakuza bar in Zanzibar. What a strange place to hold out.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>remainder</kanji> of the yakuza bars aren't in Japan. Strangely, they are all in <reading>Zan</reading>zibar (ざん).
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Yakuza</radical> <radical>bars</radical> barely exist. The <kanji>remainder</kanji> of them aren't even in Japan.

MeaningHint: Picture this pasty white dude jumping into the target-bottomed pool. The sun glints off of his unnaturally white skin and stings your eyes.
ReadingHint: He hits the water right on target and the water splashes onto you. Since he was a techie, he was able to perform the perfect dive. And now you’re all wet.
ReadingMnemonic: This white dude is a huge <reading>techie</reading> (てき) so he has electronic enhancements that make sure he hits the <kanji>target</kanji>. Being a techie has its advantages.
MeaningMnemonic:
A <radical>white</radical> guy jumps into a <radical>pool</radical> with a <kanji>target</kanji> painted on the bottom. He's aiming for the target, of course.

This kanji can also mean <kanji>-al</kanji>, as in the endings you get on some English adjectives. You know, like the pool is artifici-al. Or painting a target down there is not practic-al. That kind of -al.

MeaningHint: The promise reminding thread on your finger was tied too tight. You can feel the thread starting to cut off your circulation as your finger starts to turn purple and go numb. It’s really uncomfortable.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself sitting on a riding mower as you’re pulled around the yard by a yak. The yak is having a hard time pulling you so it's making lots of yak noises. Hear them in your mind.
ReadingMnemonic: After you get out of the pool, you keep the <kanji>promise</kanji> you made to your mother. So you get on your <reading>yak</reading> (やく) powered mower and trim the grass. Yes, your mower is pulled by a yak. It’s very efficient.
MeaningMnemonic: You tie a <radical>thread</radical> on your finger before jumping in the <radical>pool</radical> to remind yourself of the <kanji>promise</kanji> you made to your mother. The thread represents the promise you made to mow the lawn after you finished swimming in the pool.

MeaningHint: Imagine piling up flowers on top of clouds to make your own unique art. It’s a fiddly business, because they keep falling through the clouds, but you get there in the end. Now step back and marvel at your work of flower cloud art.
ReadingHint: Picture the gay club with your art displayed behind the bar. Fame and fortune are just around the corner, now.
ReadingMnemonic: Your beautiful <kanji>art</kanji> is going to be displayed at a <reading>gay</reading> (げい) club. Imagine the pride you feel about having your art on display.
MeaningMnemonic: You put <radical>flowers</radical> on a <radical>cloud</radical> and call it <kanji>art</kanji>. Not everyone agrees that your flower cloud art is actually art, but you know it is.

MeaningHint: Do you live on the beach? Maybe the foundation of your house was made from crabs in dirt too. Why don't you go outside and check?
ReadingHint: Keys to houses are always made out of a piece of the foundation it was built upon. How else would they be unique from one another?
ReadingMnemonic: Remember, a good <kanji>foundation</kanji> is the <reading>key</reading> (き) to a good house. No, literally. You have to use a piece of the foundation to make the key to your house.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Crab</radical>s in the <radical>dirt</radical> make a great <kanji>foundation</kanji> for a seaside hut. It's right on the seashore, where all the crabs dig their holes in the dirt. But as they get old and die, their bodies form a strong base. Perfect for the foundation of a building like this!

MeaningHint: Picture your own soul and your own life. Listen to what your soul says about your gender and nature — because your life is yours to live, and only your soul can define who you truly are!
ReadingHint: The next time you get one of those surveys where you have to choose your gender, just think back to that time in your life when a random official came and certified your gender with their saber. Ahh, what lovely memories.
ReadingMnemonic: Once you've decided what your <kanji>gender</kanji> is, someone comes to your house and lays a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) on your shoulders. "This is now your <kanji>nature</kanji>," they say, lifting the saber. It's official! It's in all the newspapers, there's a parade, and everyone pats you on the back. Thanks, gender saber!
MeaningMnemonic: Everyone has a <radical>soul</radical> in their <radical>life</radical>, and that soul shapes their <kanji>gender</kanji>. It's not just about body parts — it's about what's inside. In the end, it's one's <kanji> nature</kanji> that truly matters.

MeaningHint: Imagine kissing a tree. Better yet, go kiss a tree. If you're lucky, it'll give you a little taste of sap — an even surer sign of your status.
ReadingHint: Probably because a cock crowing means the sun's about to come up. Morning light is basically tree breakfast, after all, and they say the way to a tree's heart is through its chloroplasts. So picture yourself as a cock, cock-a-doodle-doing to a tree about your status.
ReadingMnemonic: The best way to convince a tree of your <kanji>status</kanji> is to say "cock-a-doodle-doo" at it, like a <reading>cock</reading> (かく), i.e. a male chicken. Trees really place a lot of status on the ability to sound like a cock crowing.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tree</radical> wants to <radical>kiss</radical> you, it's a sign of your <kanji>status</kanji>. Only someone of high status would be worthy enough to receive the gnarly but gentle kiss of a tree. Trees don't kiss just anyone, you know.

MeaningHint: I wish I had this kind of ability... maybe I'll go up to the moon and try to steal your spoons.
ReadingHint: Oh, you thought I was talking about that old Nostradamus, who also had the ability to predict things? Nah, you're way cooler than him.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>ability</kanji> to predict the future makes you famous and now everyone knows your name: <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう)! And now that people know about your name and special ability, everyone will love you!
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>private</radical> place on the <radical>moon</radical>, you hide two <radical>spoon</radical>s that give you a special <kanji>ability</kanji>. It's the ability to predict the future! The private moon spoons reflect the knowledge of time and space into your brain. Now that's a cool ability!

MeaningHint: Imagine the force and mass of the moon hitting you right in the boney part of your forehead. Whomp! It sure would hurt.
ReadingHint: Again, feel your bones crack as you smash into the moon. At least you're warm though. Feel the cozy coats you're wearing and snuggle up in their warmth.
ReadingMnemonic: Outer space is cold, and if you're traveling to the moon, you're going to need a lot of <reading>coats</reading> (こつ). All of these coats will keep you warm, even if you do end up breaking your forehead <kanji>bone</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: If you have a <radical>mustache</radical> and you hit your <radical>forehead</radical> on the <radical>moon</radical>, you’re probably going to break a <kanji>bone</kanji>. Ok, so it doesn’t matter so much if you have a mustache, but if you’re travelling fast enough to make it to the moon, you’re definitely breaking a bone if you run into it.

MeaningHint: Picture a woman walking gently over someone's back. She's hardly moving.
ReadingHint: Poor old dad. His back hurts so his daughter has to walk on it with cleats to make it feel better. But if she isn't gentle enough, she'll just make it worse.
ReadingMnemonic: Actually, the person she's being <kanji>gentle</kanji> with is her <reading>da</reading>d (だ). No one would ever want to hurt their dad, especially if he's asked you to walk gently on his back as a way of giving him a massage.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>cleat</radical>-wearing <radical>woman</radical> is walking on someone's back, but she doesn't want to hurt him, so she has to be very <kanji>gentle</kanji>. Cleats are sharp and pointy, and not designed for back-walking, so she's being extra gentle right now.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking up into the sky, trying to figure out what the atmosphere up there is made out of. Your only lead: It's raining, a lot. So, the atmosphere must be partly made of rain, and it's just falling down.
ReadingHint: The reading for this kanji is actually the same as one of 分's on'yomi readings (ふん). One trick about complicated kanji is they often take the reading of one of the pieces they're made from. In this case it's 分. How fun.
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out, studying the <kanji>atmosphere</kanji> is <reading>fun</reading> (ふん)!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> makes up one <radical>part</radical> of this, and the rest is air or something. I imagine this is what people thought the <kanji>atmosphere</kanji> was made out of thousands of years ago.

MeaningHint: You know what a geoduck looks like, right? Does that look like a creature that was looking to change and evolve and be awesome? No, geoducks are weird and stubborn. Imagine how stubborn you’d have to be to not want to evolve into a more attractive animal.
ReadingHint: Gandhi’s stubbornness, however, was pretty effective. Everyone knows Gandhi. Remember Gandhi and his famous stubborn attitude whenever you see this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: You know who else was <kanji>stubborn</kanji>? <reading>Gan</reading>dhi (がん). Gandhi believed in non-violent resistance and he was pretty stubborn about it.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>origin</radical> of the <radical>geoduck</radical> is a pretty <kanji>stubborn</kanji> one. The geoduck race is so stubborn that it refused to evolve into an animal that made any sense. Hence, the origin of the geoduck is one of stubbornness.

MeaningHint: After your hard work, hear the gate close behind you with a creak. You're on the inside now, you're one of the talented ones. And your talents have changed you, made you different from the others — much like the talent radical in this kanji is a little different from its usual shape!
ReadingHint: Picture a bunch of people outside the closed gates, sleeping in piles of hay. You were there too once. You know how itchy that hay is, and how cold it can be at night. But it was all worth it.
ReadingMnemonic: Outside the talent gate that's always <kanji>closed</kanji>, <reading>hay</reading> (へい) is piled up to make beds for the people who are still trying to get in. Who knows how long they'll be there outside the closed gate, sleeping in the hay, but they're all working hard to become talented.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>gate</radical> to <radical>talent</radical> is now <kanji>closed</kanji>. In fact, the gate to talent is always closed. It's not like anyone can just waltz in and get themselves some talent. You have to work for it! You have to work hard to get through that closed gate to talent!

MeaningHint: Make sure you remember that this is a well of water not a "well, actually."
ReadingHint: You're absolutely terrified of this giant eagle hiding in the well. You're not even sure what its goal is, maybe it's just out to scare you... Either way, imagine yourself being absolutely terrified of this eagle every time you have to go fetch water from the well.
ReadingMnemonic: Each time you go to the town <kanji>well</kanji>, there's a giant <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) hiding inside, just waiting for you to show up.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>well</radical> radical and the <kanji>well</kanji> kanji are the same.

MeaningHint: Feel the sunshine coming into the building. It gets into this building so easily, and makes it very bright and warm. No matter where you look, sunshine is everywhere.
ReadingHint: Picture your yogurt glistening in the sunlight. Watch as it slowly curdles and starts to smell sour. You still eat your yogurt, but it's hot and lumpy now. Sunlight and yogurt do not mix.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sunshine</kanji> gets into the building so easily, in fact, that it hits your <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). The sunshine makes the yogurt... gross.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> has <radical>easy</radical> access to <kanji>sunshine</kanji>. The <kanji>sunlight</kanji> shines right in, because it's a clear building.

MeaningHint: Nope, the luck is definitely coming from the spirit in your scarecrow. You aren't lucky on your own.
ReadingHint: If you get a luck spirit to say f*** you, you might accidentally convince it to move somewhere else. If it does, you lose your luck. So be careful.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to check the scarecrow to make sure your recent <kanji>luck</kanji> is coming from it, so you kick it. "<reading>F***</reading> (ふく) you!" it yells. Oh yeah, there's a spirit in there.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>spirit</radical> decides to live in your <radical>scarecrow</radical>, you'll be granted good <kanji>luck</kanji> and good <kanji>fortune</kanji>. Crows won't hang out in your farm. Everything you grow will become large and delicious. But not everyone is so lucky that a spirit will decide to live in their scarecrow. So maybe the luck was in you all along!

MeaningHint: If you could have any skill imaginable, what would it be? Wouldn't it be nice to have these magical branching fingers? (Definitely. It would.)
ReadingHint: Imagine a tiny guillotine made for the cutting of fingers. Whoever made this must have had special hand skills too! Then maybe the skill-less people tested it on them…
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, these <kanji>skill</kanji>s are too much for the normal skill-less people to handle, so they pull out the <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). I guess they're going to chop off your skillful fingers because they're kinda scary.
MeaningMnemonic: You can make your <radical>fingers</radical> <radical>branch</radical> out. It's your special <kanji>skill</kanji>, a very advanced <kanji>technique</kanji>. Your fingers branch out into smaller fingers and you can hold things really well. You can type super-duper fast. And you can win any tickle fight. These are skills you have thanks to your branching fingers.

MeaningHint: Angels are really good at going around carrying drops! Imagine the artistry and technique involved. I bet they do some kind of dance, and there's lots of sparkly magic.
ReadingHint: Imagine an angel trained in ninjutsu. Marvel at all their divine ninja techniques!
ReadingMnemonic: Angels are so good at this because they are trained in nin<reading>jutsu</reading> (じゅつ), or the "ninja techniques." Everyone knows that ninjas are great at carrying drops on the go!
MeaningMnemonic: There <radical>go</radical> some <radical>angel</radical>s with <radical>drop</radical>s on their shoulders. Carrying drops like that requires <kanji>technique</kanji>. It’s a real <kanji>art</kanji>.

ReadingHint: That's too bad - feel sorry for the person and learn a little more about the terror attack, if it will help you to remember the reading of this kanji better.
ReadingMnemonic: Someone died. You ask how this person died, the person who's getting buried by the <kanji>temple</kanji>. The priest/monk says it was in a tragic <reading>terror</reading> (てら) attack.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>temple</radical> radical and the <kanji>temple</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Brilliant.

MeaningHint: This kanji makes a lot of sense. Just follow the logic.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself screaming "Eeeeee! Whaaaat!?" and running away from that boulder as fast as you can.
ReadingMnemonic: "<kanji>Boulder</kanji>!!!" yells a voice from behind you. "<reading>Eee, wha</reading>...?" (いわ) you reply. You look up to see a boulder falling off the cliff above you. "Eeeeee! Whaaaat!?," you shout again, running and screaming this time.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>stone</radical> is probably going to be a really big stone. We're talking about a <kanji>boulder</kanji> here.

MeaningHint: Your knife is one of those Swiss Army knives, so it's got a bunch of tools you'll need when you return home. Your broom is just a broom, but… maybe if you pretend to ride it like a witch, it'll make the trip home more fun?
ReadingHint: Imagine your family asking all your coworkers and friends to 返す you so you can return home. It's heartwarming, but also kind of embarrassing at the same time.
ReadingMnemonic: For you to <kanji>return home</kanji>, your family has to ask the world to <reading>返</reading>す (かえ) you to them. You belong at home with your family — the outside world is just borrowing you, and eventually it needs to 返す you and let you return home.
MeaningMnemonic: You pick up your <radical>knife</radical> and <radical>broom</radical> because you're getting ready to <kanji>return home</kanji>. There'll be a lot of work to do when you return home — cooking, cleaning, fixing stuff — so your knife and broom are sure to come in handy.

MeaningHint: The spring sun shows itself only in spring, and that's how you know the season has truly arrived. Its light has a special quality that gives everything a fresh, spring-like hue.
ReadingHint: Just picture how lively everything becomes in the springtime, and let yourself be guided by the wonderful sounds, smells and colours. Let yourself enjoy the hallucinations.
ReadingMnemonic: You also know <kanji>spring</kanji> has arrived when you start <reading>hallu</reading>cinating (はる). With all the funky pollens wafting around, it's kind of inevitable that spring will make you hallucinate — it's just part of the season.
MeaningMnemonic: When you see the <radical>spring</radical> <radical>sun</radical> you know it's finally the  <kanji>spring</kanji> season.

MeaningHint: Really try to imagine this giant shrimp on the sun as a normal thing that happens, without fail, every day at noon. "Hey guys, it's noon," the shrimp announces casually, then leaves again.
ReadingHint: Right after the shrimp's noon announcement, imagine it struggling to put on heels while sitting on the sun. This shrimp is almost bigger than the sun, actually, so all its heel struggles are easy to see.
ReadingMnemonic: At <kanji>noon</kanji>, the shrimp also puts on its high <reading>heel</reading>s (ひる). It wants to look good for the other shrimp it's going to lunch with. High noon? More like high heels, am I right?
MeaningMnemonic: A giant <radical>shrimp</radical> appears on top of the <radical>sun</radical> after it rises high above the <radical>ground</radical>, and this is how you know it's <kanji>noon</kanji>. The shrimp appears on the sun exactly at noon every day, to announce that it's noon.

MeaningHint: Look into the blue sky and imagine the clouds parting as the weather clears up and the sun starts to come out. Gorgeous! The beauty is so great you have to squint.
ReadingHint: Imagine walking down the street as the weather clears up and saying to everyone you pass, "Hah! I was right! I knew it would clear up! Hah!" Basking in the beautiful weather is nice, but basking in feelings of smug superiority is even better.
ReadingMnemonic: When it starts to <kanji>clear up</kanji>, you exclaim, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)! Told you so!" You had a feeling it would clear up, but no one believed you, so now you have to yell "Hah!" and rub your correctness in everyone's face.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is out, the sky is <radical>blue</radical>, things have begun to <kanji>clear up</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture all the grains in autumn. Picture all of the colors. The colors of fire.
ReadingHint:
Ah yes, the sound of the seasons changing to autumn.

Ack!
Ack!
Ack!
ReadingMnemonic: As the grains burn, and the season changes to autumn, imagine them yelling out in pain: "<reading>ACK</reading> (あき)!"
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Grain</radical>s look like they're on <radical>fire</radical> in <kanji>autumn</kanji>. They all turn red and orange and brown, the colors of fire. It's also fun to set grains on fire in Autumn, since it's getting colder.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to use your hands to show the size of this cross, but the other person shakes their head and says, "Use your words!" Ugh, okay, you'll have to measure it then.
ReadingHint: "This cross measures two cakes wide and three cakes long," you say.
ReadingMnemonic: You need to <kanji>measure</kanji> this cross but all you have is a <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). Good enough! I'm sure all cakes are exactly the same size, so they're a reliable unit of measure.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>say</radical> how big a <radical>cross</radical> is, you have to <kanji>measure</kanji> it. You can't say the size of something without measuring it first.

MeaningHint: You have a yakuza knife. There are a bunch there, all in a row, so you follow suit by putting yours in the row too.
ReadingHint: Imagine a bunch of yakuza being enthusiastic about putting their knives in a row. They all say together "Ret's do it!"
ReadingMnemonic: Should we put them in a <kanji>row</kanji>? <reading>Let's</reading> (れつ) put them in a row!
MeaningMnemonic: Take your <radical>yakuza</radical> <radical>knife</radical> and put it in a <kanji>row</kanji> with the others.

MeaningHint: Picture a big pirate map, and on it are a bunch of these markers for the districts where you can find the treasures you've been searching for.
ReadingHint: The treasure of the cookie district is cookies, which you want. And to be fair, even if you weren't a pirate, you'd probably want those cookies.
ReadingMnemonic: There is one <kanji>district</kanji> you're interested in, and that's the <reading>coo</reading>kie (く) district.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>cage</radical> with <radical>treasure</radical> in it is actually a map of the <kanji>district</kanji> the treasure is in.

MeaningHint: Imagine the pain in your calves as you walk up a steep slope. Wow, this dirt is really anti-you right now. Going down the slope will be a lot easier and quicker, but that's still just the dirt trying to shoo you away as fast as possible.
ReadingHint: Maybe it looks flat to a spectator, but a soccer pitch is actually a series of very small slopes. Those soccer athletes who seem to fall and flop all the time are in fact just slipping on tiny invisible slopes.
ReadingMnemonic: You need to know these things about <kanji>slope</kanji>s if you want to play <reading>soccer</reading> (さか). Soccer is surprisingly slope-intensive, actually.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Dirt</radical> is being <radical>anti</radical>-social when it's in the shape of a <kanji>slope</kanji>. Think about it — is there anything more annoying than having to walk up a steep slope? Why is the dirt here forming a slope?! Because the dirt is being anti-social, and a slope is how it tells you to stay away.

MeaningHint: Ritual and ceremony kind of mean the same thing. Don't worry too much if you mix them up a little — each will help you to remember the other. There's a reason this kanji is made up of the ceremony radical already!
ReadingHint: It's good to practice making up your own mnemonics every once in a while. It'll help if you add more to the mnemonics already here, or if you can make up new ones that are more personal to you!
ReadingMnemonic: So, you're at a <kanji>ritual</kanji>, but not just any old ritual. This is a full-blown <kanji>ceremony</kanji> and it's incredibly <reading>chic</reading> (しき). Imagine just how chic it is. Give it a shot — I'll let you come up with the chicness this time (it's good practice).
MeaningMnemonic:
A good <radical>ceremony</radical> is under <radical>construction</radical> for a long time. You can't just make up a new ceremony on the spot. A ceremony is done a certain way because that's the way it's always been done. In short, to build a ceremony, you have to create a <kanji>ritual</kanji> first. Then, the ritual can be constructed into a bigger, more lavish <kanji>ceremony</kanji>.

This kanji also means <kanji>equation</kanji> for some reason. Just think of a ceremony that involves doing math and solving equations. How strange would that be? Pretend you're at this ceremony.

MeaningHint: Imagine who your leader is here — maybe a boss, or maybe the Crabigator. Now say it out loud to this imagined leader: "Believe and trust in me!"
ReadingHint: Picture yourself pleading with your leader to trust and believe in you. Imagine the pain from your shin splints as you kneel. It hurts a lot, but it proves how serious you are.
ReadingMnemonic: As you face your leader and say "<kanji>Believe</kanji> and <kanji>trust</kanji> in me," you kneel down on each <reading>shin</reading> (しん). You have shin splints, so it hurts a bit, but you do it anyway to show how serious you are.
MeaningMnemonic: To your <radical>leader</radical>, you <radical>say</radical>: "<kanji>Believe</kanji> and <kanji>trust</kanji> in me." The leader doesn't think you can handle your duties, so you have to say this to him. You want him to believe and trust that you're responsible. You can handle this task.

MeaningHint: That means if you know your radicals well, you know the meaning of this kanji too!
ReadingHint: Imagine a courageous man standing against the youths. They use weird slang and try to tell him about avocado toast, but the man keeps his courage.
ReadingMnemonic: A man needs <kanji>courage</kanji> to stand up to all the <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) of the world. They are so numerous and difficult to understand, but the man can face them with courage thanks to his mama.
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>courage</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>courage</kanji> are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Simple is simple!
ReadingHint: Get a tan, it's simple, stupid! (Though it's terrible for you so uh... don't always take our advice, kay?)
ReadingMnemonic: One of the most <kanji>simple</kanji> things in the world is getting a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). All you have to do is stand outside long enough. It doesn't matter what skin type you have or what the weather is like. Eventually, if you stand out there long enough, you'll get a tan.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>simple</radical> radical and the <kanji>simple</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Picture the creepiest creeper you know. They're wearing a cape, laughing from behind a big desk in their new director-level position. That cape has really gone to their head.
ReadingHint: Picture that creeper you know. Their cape is made of wool now, since they're the Director of Sheep, and they're directing all kinds of creepy policy decisions about sheep. I'm not even sure what those would be, but try to imagine some.
ReadingMnemonic: After this creeper dons the cape, they decide to become <kanji>Director</kanji> of <reading>Shee</reading>p (し). From now on all sheep-related business, government policy, and law will be determined by this creeper in a cape, because they are the Director of Sheep.
MeaningMnemonic: Throw a <radical>cape</radical> over a <radical>creeper</radical>, and suddenly they think they're a big-shot <kanji>director</kanji>. They go around calling themselves managing director of this, director-general of that. They creep into meetings and ceremonies and try to direct the proceedings, all because of that stupid cape.

MeaningHint: With every winter that comes around, another year passes, and you change. Again and again. The never ending cycle of winters and change.
ReadingHint: Picture you and your hens. Going through change, every winter, again and again.
ReadingMnemonic: You know what else <kanji>change</kanji>s each winter? <reading>Hen</reading>s (へん)! They're constantly laying eggs, which then (possibly) turn into new hens.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Again</radical> and again, <radical>winter</radical> <kanji>change</kanji>s you each year.

ReadingHint: Husbands love eating food. Who doesn’t though, really?
ReadingMnemonic: What do all <kanji>husband</kanji>s love? <reading>Foo</reading>d (ふう)! Can you hear your future husband? He’s saying, "Fooooood! FOOOOOD!” like some kind of food craving zombie.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>husband</radical> radical and the <kanji>husband</kanji> kanji are the same. How useful, unlike some husbands, I'd imagine.

MeaningHint: Imagine having the power to control time like this. Picture yourself unlocking the sun to go back in time. Feel the heat of the sun and the bright light in your eyes as you unlock the door to the past.
ReadingHint: Imagine wearing this sack as you're sucked through the vortex of time and space. Feel yourself spinning around as you hurtle back into yesterday.
ReadingMnemonic: Time traveling to a <kanji>previous</kanji> time, like <kanji>yesterday</kanji>, can be rough on the body. As such, you wear a small <reading>sack</reading> (さく) to hold your valuables while you're ripped through the sands of time. The sack keeps your important stuff from getting lost. The sack holds your key to the sun as well.
MeaningMnemonic: Once the <radical>sun</radical> comes out, you can use this <radical>key</radical> to unlock it and take you to any <kanji>previous</kanji> day, even just <kanji>yesterday</kanji>! The sun is in charge of the day changes, so if you use a key to unlock it, you can control the flow of time and return to yesterday, or a previous day in the past. Behold the power of the sun.

MeaningHint: To keep life safely inside the window, put that poison down.
ReadingHint: Have you done Sudoku before? Imagine yourself completing these puzzles and concentrating really hard. They’re fun, but also challenging.
ReadingMnemonic: If anything's worse than <kanji>poison</kanji> it's doing Su<reading>doku</reading> (どく) puzzles.
MeaningMnemonic: To throw <radical>life</radical> out the <radical>window</radical>, use <kanji>poison</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Tsunamis used to come crashing into towns, just leveling them completely. Today we have new methods of building and protecting ourselves, and laws to keep them in place, so the past doesn't repeat itself.
ReadingHint: Your home has strong walls and ceilings. You aren't allowed to leave the doors unlocked and open. These methods and laws are because of those past tsunamis and the damage they used to cause. Your home is safe thanks to them!
ReadingMnemonic: These <kanji>method</kanji>s and <kanji>law</kanji>s affected your own <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう) the most.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>s in the <radical>past</radical> changed the <kanji>method</kanji>s and <kanji>law</kanji>s of today.

MeaningHint: Imagine getting hit with the full force of a tsunami. It slams into you and crushes every bone in your body. It hurts so much you cry.
ReadingHint: Imagine stuffing yourself with nachos. The tears stop. You're feeling bruised and wet, but much better.
ReadingMnemonic: Do you know the only way to stop yourself <kanji>cry</kanji>ing after a tsunami? That's right, <reading>na</reading>chos (な)! Nachos are the only proven way to cheer people up after being hit by a tsunami.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> approaches, and anyone who dares to <radical>stand</radical> in front of it will surely <kanji>cry</kanji>. That tsunami is going to hit you hard, and rip you right off the ground you're standing on. Who wouldn't cry?

MeaningHint: Picture yourself at this bar, sipping on a drink while you're wading in this shallow water. Feel the shallow water getting your socks and shoes all wet. It's uncomfortable and cold.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself in the bar again sipping on this Asahi beer. Taste the beery beer taste of Asahi as you walk around the bar with your wet feet.
ReadingMnemonic: What beer are you drinking at this bar while standing in <kanji>shallow</kanji> water? You’re drinking <reading>Asa</reading>hi (あさ), the beer of the salaryman. Asahi is super dry, so it takes your attention away from the shallow water a bit.
MeaningMnemonic: After a <radical>tsunami</radical>, if you go to a <radical>bar</radical>, you'll find yourself standing in <kanji>shallow</kanji> water. You're just trying to enjoy a drink, but with your feet constantly soaked, it's not exactly pleasant. Here's to hoping the conversations with your date won't be shallow too!

MeaningHint: Picture this account of your life, the thread of the narrative linking all of the different important points in your life together.
ReadingHint: Picture the people in your life that you'd give this special key to. They'd get to read the account of your life, so make sure they're trustworthy before you hand that key over.
ReadingMnemonic: You take this <kanji>account</kanji> and lock it with a special <reading>key</reading> (き). You only give this key to people you really trust, so they can read the account of your life and truly understand you.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> you weave together to tell others about <radical>oneself</radical> is the <kanji>account</kanji> of your life. It's your very own <kanji>narrative</kanji>, tying everything you've been through together.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself in the center of England, speaking English to all of the beautiful flowers.
ReadingHint: Imagine walking around the giant English acorn. Touch its smooth surface with your hand.
ReadingMnemonic: You keep walking to the very central center of <kanji>England</kanji> and see a giant <reading>a</reading>corn (えい) planted in the middle of the ground. This acorn is the source of all the flowers and life in England.
MeaningMnemonic:
There are many <radical>flowers</radical> in the <radical>center</radical> of <kanji>England</kanji>. It may seem rainy and gray at first, but if you walk right to the center you'll see all the flowers that grow there in the center of the country.

This kanji also means <kanji>English</kanji>, which is pretty similar to England. Just imagine all the people of England walking to the center, where the flowers are, and speaking English to them to help them grow.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself running away from the North Korean army. You have to get to the border, the entire army is coming after you for hitting their dear leader!
ReadingHint: Picture yourself running away from them as they shout and shoot at you. Hear the bullets wizzing past your head as you run away as fast as you can.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>army</kanji> is after you. Each army soldier has a <reading>gun</reading> (ぐん) and they want to use these guns of theirs to shoot you dead! You killed their leader with a car and now they’re gonna get you back with a gun!
MeaningMnemonic: You run into someone's <radical>forehead</radical> with your <radical>car</radical>. The big forehead you hit is Kim Jong Un's. You know that now you're going to have the whole North Korean <kanji>army</kanji> coming after you.

MeaningHint: Look down at the huge meal in front of you. You'll have to eat your food eventually, but I guess there are plenty of reasons to be anti-eat right now.
ReadingHint: Picture Han Solo slowly eating your whole meal. He's making constant eye contact with you, reaching down and shoving the entire meal into his mouth.
ReadingMnemonic: As you stare at the <kanji>meal</kanji>, <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo appears before you and takes a bite! This is what happens if you don't eat your food, someone else will eat it.
MeaningMnemonic: You might want to <radical>eat</radical>, but your <radical>anti</radical>-eating stance means your <kanji>meal</kanji> will just sit in front of you, uneaten. Food is meant to be eaten! Stop trying to fast and take a bite.

MeaningHint:
Imagine being in the privacy of your own home, your own room, and saying to yourself, "Yes, Buddha, you're my leader."

Or don't, if that doesn't sit right with you. Freedom of religion and all that, right?
ReadingHint: Picture Buddha wearing a giant pair of rubber boots. Yes, now this is fashion.
ReadingMnemonic: Your private <kanji>Buddha</kanji> is a little different than other people's idea of him. Your Buddha is wearing <reading>boots</reading> (ぶつ). They're big and rubber, and they look fabulous on him.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> in <radical>private</radical> is <kanji>Buddha</kanji>. You don't feel the need to tell everyone. But when you're alone you give your time to Buddha and let him lead your life.

MeaningHint: Imagine you’re the sun, working all day. Of course you get tired. This is a good excuse to go to bed at night and get some rest. Feel how sore and hot and sweaty and exhausted you are after a hard day’s work of being the sun.
ReadingHint: Stop working so much! Humans need some rest too, so it can't always be bright. Maybe you should try some therapy. We all just want to see you get better, sun. Anyway, you're banned for now. Cya.
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, you also have a history of overworking yourself, so you've been outright <reading>ban</reading>ned (ばん) from showing up at <kanji>night</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> has an <radical>excuse</radical> for disappearing at <kanji>night</kanji>. The sun gets really tired, same as anyone else – so when night rolls around the sun excuses itself and goes to sleep. It’s a valid excuse.

MeaningHint: Picture this cat meowing and purring as it rubs up against all the flowers in the rice paddy field. Aww, it’s so cute!
ReadingHint: Try not to imagine this too hard. It is disturbing.
ReadingMnemonic: Wait a minute… This <kanji>cat</kanji> is pretty odd looking. You take a closer look, seeing it is missing its <reading>neck</reading> (ねこ). You go to pet it, but it's super sensitive about that area of its head, which is basically a face connected to a body...
MeaningMnemonic: If you see an <radical>animal</radical> among the <radical>flowers</radical> in a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, chances are it’s a <kanji>cat</kanji>. Cats love rice paddy flowers, almost as much as they love catnip.

MeaningHint: Imagine people coming from miles around to pay for a look at your private fingers.
ReadingHint: If somebody looks at your fingers without paying, without your consent, file a harassment claim on their butt! That is NOT okay!
ReadingMnemonic: If someone tries to look without paying, that's <reading>hara</reading>ssment (はら)! Nobody gets to see something of yours without proper payment, but more importantly, your consent!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> are <radical>private</radical>, no one can see them unless they <kanji>pay</kanji>! And you decide if what they pay is enough to get a peek.

MeaningHint: You can only see the legs, though, because a cloth is covering the cage. What kind of small animal is it? Try to look.
ReadingHint: Who is giving this cat all those hickies? Pretty gross, if you ask me.
ReadingMnemonic: You look up through the cloth, and see a hairless cat. This <kanji>small animal</kanji> is hairless, but most surprisingly is covered in <reading>hickie</reading>s (ひき).
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>cage</radical> there are some <radical>legs</radical>. They are the legs attached to a <kanji>small animal</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Zombies are dead, so I guess their mouths have all the things garden plants crave. Their mouths are like compost piles, if you think about it.
ReadingHint: Oh. The zombie probably bit down on your head and now it's the end for you. At least you were looking at a nice garden at the end of it all...
ReadingMnemonic: You look closer at the <kanji>garden</kanji> and suddenly everything <reading>en</reading>ds (えん).
MeaningMnemonic: You examine the <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>zombie</radical> and find a small <kanji>garden</kanji> in there. There are even benches... it looks like a <kanji>park</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the majestic scene — two Wolverines (yes, there are two) and a turkey, frolicking in the sunlight together. It's one of those rare but regular natural occurrences that only happens once a week, on a particular day.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself looking at the shadow cast by the two Wolverines and the turkey. Imagine waiting a whole 24 hours and knowing exactly when your yogurt is ready. All thanks to this makeshift sundial which can accurately tell you which day of the week it is.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that you know how to figure out what <kanji>day of the week</kanji> it is, you can start making <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). It can take up to 24 hours to make yogurt. Knowing which weekday it is at any given moment means you can perfect your yogurt-making schedule.
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> is out, you can see two <radical>Wolverine</radical>s and a <radical>turkey</radical> playing together, but only on a certain <kanji>weekday</kanji>. Which <kanji>day of the week</kanji> is it? I'll never tell you. It could be Sunday. It could be Monday. It could be any day of the week.

MeaningHint: Just imagine yourself sitting on the sun, writing back to your friends and family with a brush. For someone who can visit the sun, it's really weird that you are writing with a brush like this, but you're doing it just the same.
ReadingHint: Think of your favorite live shows and imagine them on the sun. Also imagine yourself writing back to your friends and family about said shows.
ReadingMnemonic: What did you <kanji>write</kanji> about? You were writing about this amazing circus <reading>show</reading> (しょ) you just watched on the sun. You didn't expect there to be any shows here, but turns out you were wrong!
MeaningMnemonic: Why would you try to take a <radical>brush</radical> to the <radical>sun</radical>? It's almost too obvious, because you want to <kanji>write</kanji> something down.

MeaningHint: Picture the zombie, going far off into the distance. It's getting smaller and harder to see.
ReadingHint: I mean, to be fair, the zombie wasn't really alive to begin with. But going far enough to end it all? That's some serious distance!
ReadingMnemonic: The zombie goes so <kanji>far</kanji>, that its life <reading>en</reading>ds (えん). Zombies need to eat to live (it's really all they need), but they go so far on their scooter trip, that they just give up and end it all.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>scooter</radical> with a <radical>zombie</radical> on it, going really <kanji>far</kanji> away. Someone tied that zombie to the scooter and now it's far off in the distance, groaning and growling.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals or you won't be allowed to pass.
ReadingHint: Picture the most terrifying monster you can imagine, standing as tall as the gate itself. How on earth are you ever going to get past that thing?!
ReadingMnemonic: You want to pass through this <kanji>gate</kanji> but it’s guarded by… a <reading>mon</reading>ster (もん)!
MeaningMnemonic: The radical for <radical>gate</radical> and the kanji for <kanji>gates</kanji> are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine you're this leader, and you've just picked up a piece of thread from the ground. You make a jarring psychic connection with the owner of the thread. Your head spins, your heart races, and your hands tremble. Picture this happening to you as you pick up the thread from the ground.
ReadingHint: The connection is so strong that you feel like you're eating the cake as well. It's vanilla-strawberry. You taste the fluffy and creamy vanilla-strawberry flavors washing over your tongue. Imagine yourself eating this delectable cake. It's fantastically delicious.
ReadingMnemonic: As you develop this psychic <kanji>connection</kanji> with the owner of the thread, you can tell that they're currently eating a <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい).
MeaningMnemonic: A psychic <radical>leader</radical> kneels to the <radical>ground</radical>, picks up a <radical>thread</radical>, and instantly makes a <kanji>connection</kanji> with the person that it came from. This leader is very powerful, so even a thread is enough for them to make a psychic connection with the person who was wearing it.

MeaningHint: Which store has the best product selection, do you think? Picture all those articles and goods lined up on the shelves in their little boxes, kind of in the shape of this kanji.
ReadingHint: Then another Hindu person walks in your store and buys some. Then another... What are all these Hindu people doing buying your products, you wonder? When you ask them, they tell you "it's a cultural thing."
ReadingMnemonic: As you try to sell some <kanji>product</kanji>s, you finally get a buyer. It's a <reading>Hin</reading>du (ひん) person. She walks in and buys your products on the spot!
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji is the same as the <radical>products</radical> radical, so it means <kanji>product</kanji> — you know, the kind of <kanji>article</kanji>s or <kanji>goods</kanji> you buy and sell.

MeaningHint: If you think about it, your roof protects you from a lot of things! Rain, snow, bird poop. But if your roof's measurements are off it won't be able to protect you properly.
ReadingHint: Odds are, the roof collapsed or a tree fell on you and you died from lack of protection. So now your family and friends are going to sue on your behalf.
ReadingMnemonic: If the measurements are off, and your roof doesn't <kanji>protect</kanji> you, then you get to <reading>sue</reading> (す)! If you're still alive, that is.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>roof</radical> needs <radical>measurement</radical>s done every year to make sure it will <kanji>protect</kanji> you properly.

MeaningHint: Spicy foods literally do trigger happy chemicals in your brain. It wouldn't be too far off to say that putting a lid on a pot filled with something spicy could produce happiness.
ReadingHint: こういち looks sad. But when you serve him happiness, he starts to perk up a bit. A couple more bites of happiness, and there's a smile on his face. Although when you found こういち he was depressed, down on his luck, and hiding from the government, cooking him some spicy foods seems to have brought him some happiness.
ReadingMnemonic: Out of spicy foods you made pure <kanji>happiness</kanji>. You give it to your sad friend <reading>こう</reading>いち, who waits at the table.
MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>lid</radical> on something <radical>spicy</radical> and you'll cook up pure <kanji>happiness</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture this beating heart in the prison. Was it Wolverine's? That would explain why it's, like, beating all supernaturally on its own. What should you do with it? You'd better hurry, its owner probably needs it.
ReadingHint: And if it really is Wolverine's heart, you're gonna have to be cool as a cucumber when you try to help him, even if you're in a hurry. He's emotionally cold, remember? Gotta play it cool with him, despite the emergency.
ReadingMnemonic: You start to overheat as you <kanji>hurry</kanji> around, so you grab a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう) to cool you down. The high water content re-hydrates you and lets you continue hurrying to help save this heart.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>prison</radical> where you once kept <radical>Wolverine</radical>, you find a beating <radical>heart</radical>. You don't understand how it's still going, but you know you need to <kanji>hurry</kanji> up and do something, or it might stop all of a <kanji>sudden</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture trying to get the pope to stop staring at the fins lying on the dirty ground. Yup, he's completely lost touch with reality, you think.
ReadingHint: Imagine the pope angrily kicking your shin super hard. You fall over and pull it close. Feel how badly it aches. Who knew you'd need shin guards to deal with a pope that's lost touch with reality just from dropping his dinner.
ReadingMnemonic: You're doing your best to bring the pope back to <kanji>reality</kanji> when he snaps and kicks you in the <reading>shin</reading> (しん). Oww! What the heck, pope! But he's already back staring at the fins and your shin is throbbing where he kicked it.
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>pope</radical> stays fixated on the <radical>ground</radical>, staring at <radical>fins</radical>, he's going to lose touch with <kanji>reality</kanji>. He should be focusing on his popish duties, but instead he's looking at the fish fins he dropped, and now can't eat because they're dirty. Silly pope. You can't let this one thing get you down. You have other things to do.

MeaningHint: Remember back to those woodshop electives you had in school (if you did have them) and imagine yourself crafting a bamboo box out of a bamboo tree you felled yourself. Don't forget to wear eye protection though! Wouldn't want anything to happen to those valuable eyes of yours.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself filling up your lovingly crafted bamboo box with your collection of hardcore punk and rock records. Ah, they fit just perfectly thanks to that discerning eye of yours.
ReadingMnemonic: Now you can use that <kanji>box</kanji> to store all your <reading>ha</reading>rd<reading>co</reading>re (はこ) punk vinyl records. You're a real connoisseur, so you know that hardcore music should only ever be kept in bamboo boxes.
MeaningMnemonic: With the <radical>bamboo</radical> from that <radical>tree</radical>, your trained <radical>eye</radical> can tell it'll make an excellent <kanji>box</kanji>. Boxes made from bamboo trees are very sturdy and fashionable. You have an eye for fashion as well, you see.

MeaningHint: Picture this big, strong leader. They actually love flowers and feel flattered by your compliment! But being the leader, they can't help wondering why they should have to carry your luggage…
ReadingHint: On top of being a strong leader, they also have a very kind heart, so your begging convinces them and they agree to carry your luggage.
ReadingMnemonic: Unconvinced by your flattery, the leader refuses to carry your <kanji>luggage</kanji>, so you get on your <reading>knee</reading>s (に) and beg them to carry it.
MeaningMnemonic: You offer <radical>flowers</radical> to your <radical>leader</radical> and compliment them on their <radical>lip ring</radical>. You think that by doing this they might carry your <kanji>luggage</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The next time you look at your face, also notice how much surface area it has.
ReadingHint: Look at the face of all men put together. Measure how much surface area they all take up.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>face</kanji> you're looking at, that has a lot of <kanji>surface</kanji> area, belongs to <reading>men</reading> (めん). Not a single man, but all men put together. It's hundreds of thousands of men's faces in a row.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>face</radical> radical and the <kanji>face</kanji> kanji are the same! But this isn't just a face like the one you're looking at your computer/phone with. It's the <kanji>surface</kanji> kind of face too!

MeaningHint: Imagine a fish playing music using his fins. What kind of a song is it? Imagine it in your head as you see a sign with the fish fin rule on it.
ReadingHint: Picture a group of ten (as per the rule) fish playing music with their fins. Now the song can be louder and more intricate. Imagine it playing in your head.
ReadingMnemonic: There’s also a <kanji>rule</kanji> in place to limit the amount of fish fin musicians. No more than <reading>ten</reading> (てん) are allowed to gather and play their music together. That’s the rule. No more than ten.
MeaningMnemonic: If you play <radical>music</radical> with your <radical>fins</radical>, one <kanji>rule</kanji> is that you must be a fish. You can’t play music using fish fins that don’t belong to you. That’s the rule. And it’s also rude.

MeaningHint: Picture a map of the world with actual canopies attached to each government, representing the ruling governments of each place.
ReadingHint: Think of all the people who've been labeled as fools by the government because they try to detach themselves from under their reach. It's a pretty long list...
ReadingMnemonic: Try to get out from under this <kanji>government</kanji> canopy and you'll be labeled a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). Unless... you can fool them!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>canopy</radical> <radical>attach</radical>ing your entire country is the <kanji>government</kanji>. They loom over you all and try to keep you all organized and together under their control.

MeaningHint: Imagine if you had your own machine to cure yourself if a tsunami came to where you live. It would be pretty convenient!
ReadingHint: Imagine getting Jesus to cure half the people and feeding the other half lots of cheese and their wounds miraculously healing on their own! You're a hero!
ReadingMnemonic:
Alternative ways to <kanji>cure</kanji> yourself after a tsunami are either praying to <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) or eating your weight in <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). I'm not sure if I have enough cheese for all the people that got hit though... Hmm, do you have cheese?

Before you make your decision, remember that whoever cures you will be able to <kanji>reign</kanji> over you and your people. So do you want to be led by Jesus or by some cheese? The choice is yours.
MeaningMnemonic:
After a <radical>tsunami</radical>, use this <radical>machine</radical> to <kanji>cure</kanji> the people and the country from the damage. <kanji>Heal</kanji>ing machines are all over Japan just in case a tsunami does way more damage than they can handle. 

Oh, and whoever cures and heals the most people will be able to <kanji>rule</kanji> over them once everything starts going back to normal. So keep that in mind, kay?

MeaningHint: Sure, there's a lot of water in the valley, but you're big too. It's the perfect place for you to bathe... and good thing too, you're getting smelly.
ReadingHint: Imagine cooking egg yolks in the same water you bathe in.
ReadingMnemonic: You know what would make the <kanji>bath</kanji>ing experience even better? If you had some egg <reading>yolk</reading>s (よく) to eat. Just crack those yolks into the water you bathe in, and it'll cook to perfection. Yum!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>valley</radical>, filling it up with water. Lucky for you, you're a giant, so you just lay down in the valley and use it to <kanji>bathe</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as a bamboo plant that laughs in heaven. What are you laughing about? Chuckle to yourself as you remember this kanji.
ReadingHint: Now picture these bamboo again up in bamboo heaven, and remember this strange accent to remember that the bamboo plants up in bamboo heaven are laughing with nervous laughter at this bamboo war that’s a goin’ on up there.
ReadingMnemonic: There’s actually a war a goin’ on up in that there bamboo heaven, y’know? Ye might not have heard of it with all that there <kanji>laugh</kanji>in’ a goin’ on, but there definitely be a <reading>war a</reading> (わら) goin’ on up in bamboo heaven, y’hear? The bamboo are scared so that’s why they be laughin’ so much. They nervous.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Bamboo</radical> <radical>heaven</radical> is a place for <kanji>laugh</kanji>ing. Bamboo is a very jolly plant, and they love to laugh. Even after death in bamboo heaven, the bamboo continue to laugh joyfully. Bamboo heaven is truly a place of laughter.

MeaningHint: You may have to quit eating but you never have to quit saying words. Especially if you're, well, trying to learn a whole new language full of them.
ReadingHint: Picture Jesus scarfing down jalapeño peppers. He never quits. And then he says some words about how the jalapeños gave up their lives for this contest, so we better appreciate them. Thanks Jesus!
ReadingMnemonic: Oh, you're probably wondering who won that spicy food contest after you <kanji>quit</kanji> and became obsessed with <kanji>word</kanji>s instead. It was <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). Who did you expect? He was great with words AND never quitting when things got spicy.
MeaningMnemonic:
When your <radical>tongue</radical> becomes too <radical>spicy</radical> for you to bear, it’s time to <kanji>quit</kanji> the jalapeño eating contest. You’ve been eating them for three hours now and the spiciness from the peppers is really burning your tongue. Throw in the towel, it’s time to quit the contest.

This kanji has another meaning too:

Eating isn't the only thing that makes your <radical>tongue</radical> <radical>spicy</radical>, so do certain <kanji>word</kanji>s. Say your favorite words out loud right now. Feel them on your tongue. Words are the spicy food of language!

MeaningHint: Perhaps they are related to heaven because the devil has horns? Perhaps they are connected to heaven because these are the horns of heaven's enemies? The real reason they're connected will be revealed in the reading mnemonic.
ReadingHint: Imagine Genghis Khan taking horns off his enemies, and making a gate to heaven. The horn gate is connected to both heaven and Genghis Khan in that way.
ReadingMnemonic: They are <kanji>related</kanji> and <kanji>connected</kanji> to heaven because these are the horns that Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) made from his enemies. God said he could come into heaven if he made a gate of horns, so that's what he did.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>gate</radical> of <radical>horns</radical> will take you to <radical>heaven</radical>. These horns are <kanji>related</kanji> and <kanji>connected</kanji> to heaven in some way.

MeaningHint: Can you even imagine twenty different dialects in your head? Well I can tell you that there are definitely at least twenty of them in Japan alone.
ReadingHint: It also helps that bento are delicious, and anyone with any dialect will love them.
ReadingMnemonic: The best way to learn someone's <kanji>dialect</kanji> is to buy them a <reading>ben</reading>to (べん) from where they're from. Not only do these bento have writing in their dialect on them, but their speech will immediately shift to match. Then you'll be able to hear how they sound in their head and in private!
MeaningMnemonic: If you listened in to the <radical>private</radical> lives of <radical>twenty</radical> people, all of them would have different <kanji>dialect</kanji>s and ways of <kanji>speech</kanji>. Whether they actually use these dialects with you is another story. But when they're alone, or with other people who are from the same place, their speech will be different.

MeaningHint: Imagine all this politics and government talk as you shiver outside in the winter cold. You’re freezing and you’re bored, but you know this is the right (correct) thing to do.
ReadingHint: Picture two different politicians, both wielding sabers, fighting to make the government the way they want it to be! Who are you rooting for?
ReadingMnemonic: Everyone involved in <kanji>politics</kanji> and <kanji>government</kanji> wields a special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). If two political parties are arguing, they'll whip out their sabers and start slashing away!
MeaningMnemonic: It's <radical>correct</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> to focus on <kanji>politics</kanji> and the <kanji>government</kanji>. Elections are happening, in the new year politicians are being sworn in. Winter is government time!

MeaningHint: Imagine running into the rice paddy and detaining the private sword holders one by one. They won't get away this time!
ReadingHint: If Ryu hadn't shown up with this loophole you never would have detained all these private sword guys by yourself!
ReadingMnemonic:
You're running out of steam and you're afraid you might not be able to <kanji>detain</kanji> all of these guys when <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter appears. He hates swords, and will only use fists because it's the honorable way to fight, so he starts helping you detain them!

He's also found a <reading>loo</reading>phole (る) in the system and you don't have to be super gentle with these guys either. So detain them as forcefully as you want. It is your rice paddy they're in, after all.
MeaningMnemonic: A group of <radical>private</radical> <radical>sword</radical> guys are in your <radical>rice paddy</radical>. You must <kanji>detain</kanji> them at once! Private sword guys are people who work for private companies and use their swords on people. They're basically paid assassins. And you're going to detain them!

MeaningHint: You convince the officials questioning you that your evidence can be trusted and it's <kanji>proof</kanji> that you definitely didn't have anything to do with any law breaking or murdering.
ReadingHint: You better hope the Shougun believes your evidence is accurate, because he's known to kill people willy-nilly when he thinks they're lying.
ReadingMnemonic: The officials force you to present this <kanji>evidence</kanji> in front of the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう), himself. Your knees quake, you feel like you're gonna be sick, but you try to stand up tall and tell him everything. After all, you know your <kanji>proof</kanji> is correct, so you shouldn't be so scared.
MeaningMnemonic: What I <radical>say</radical> is <radical>correct</radical> 100%! You may use it as <kanji>evidence</kanji> in this murder case.

MeaningHint: One squid got stuck in his own building, but no one could save him because going in would be too risky. Poor little squid.
ReadingHint:
The squid cries, "I can build one that isn't so risky next time!" Ken shakes his head, "I'm not letting you do anymore risky things, you're coming with me from now on." Ken scoops up the squid into a bucket, hoists him over his shoulder, and walks off into the sunset.

Who knows what adventures await our new duo. All we know is that they'll definitely be getting into some risky business.
ReadingMnemonic: "It's too <kanji>risky</kanji>, leave me!" the squid cries. "I'll save you, underwater citizen! Nothing is too risky for <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai!" The building is collapsing on the squid, the risk is so great, but Ken the samurai dives in and saves him!
MeaningMnemonic: Going into a <radical>building</radical> made by a <radical>squid</radical> is <kanji>risky</kanji>. First of all, squids only have tentacles, no bones, and they make all of their buildings out of risky materials like kelp and sharp coral.

MeaningHint: Imagine a dangerous prison surrounded by cliffs and snakes. You wouldn't want to be here either, would you?
ReadingHint: Imagine staring at the key dangling right outside of the dangerous prison you're trapped in. Would you take the chance and go for it?
ReadingMnemonic: Lucky for you, even though you're stuck in this <kanji>dangerous</kanji> place, there is hope. There is a <reading>key</reading> (き). All dangerous prisons have a key. You just have to find and steal it so you can avoid the cliffs and snakes!
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>prison</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical> surrounded by <radical>snake</radical>s. Now that's a <kanji>dangerous</kanji> place. If you try to escape the prison you either fall to your death off the cliff or get bitten/squeezed to death by the snakes. Can you think of anything more dangerous than this? I can't.

MeaningHint: Imagine needing this kind of horrible proof to believe something exists. Maybe you should have more faith in what we teach.
ReadingHint: I suppose you'll never forget that narwhals exist now that your son has one permanently stuck in him.
ReadingMnemonic: Oh, and the child we had to narwhal stab to prove they <kanji>exist</kanji> - that's your <reading>son</reading> (そん). Maybe you should have listened and kept your son out of the Narwhal Danger <reading>Zone</reading> (ぞん) after we warned you about it.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>narwhal</radical> <radical>stick</radical>s a <radical>child</radical> with its horn then you have to admit that it <kanji>exist</kanji>s. We know some of you have been doubting narwhals and whether they're real. Well now your kid is a kabob, so do you believe they exist now? I <kanji>suppose</kanji> you do.

MeaningHint: Visualize yourself doing this. The numbers just come to you. It's just what you're good at. Your specialty is something no one else in the world is as good at as you!
ReadingHint: Picture yourself at a centaur funeral. Tons of centaurs standing around and stamping their hooves as you make your measurement and stick a cross into the ground.
ReadingMnemonic: Hearing of your amazing <kanji>specialty</kanji> and cross measurement abilities, a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) from a far off land recruits you for a job. The centaur’s father has just passed away and the centaur wants you to measure the rice paddies that have been left to him.
MeaningMnemonic: Sticking a <radical>cross</radical> into a <radical>rice paddy</radical> to get its <radical>measurement</radical>s is your <kanji>specialty</kanji>. Most people get really confused when they have to measure their rice paddies, but that's where you come in. You plant a cross right in the middle, look down it from one end to the other, and voila! You know the measurements immediately. That's why it's your specialty!

MeaningHint: Looking at the sun straight on hurts. It’s bad for your eyes and burns your brain. Imagine how painful it is to stare at the sun.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself bowling without the power of sight. It would be awfully difficult to do well. You’d just have to concentrate really hard on the sound of the pins and determine where you need to throw the ball.
ReadingMnemonic: After you’ve blinded yourself from the <kanji>dare</kanji> of looking at the sun and losing sight in each eye, the guy dares you to go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). You’re blind now, so bowling will be quite the challenge.
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> gets in your <radical>eye</radical>, I <kanji>dare</kanji> you to stare at it. I dare you to look right at the sun. I dare you.

MeaningHint: Seriously, you don't need a forehead table. It's totally unnecessary to have a table made of foreheads and is a superfluous purchase. First of all, getting all those foreheads is a waste of people. Second, forehead tables aren't very strong. Superfluous, superfluous, superfluous.
ReadingHint: Now every time you visit Joe, you’ll have to look at his giant, superfluous forehead table. Imagine how gross it looks, with dozens of foreheads protruding from its surface. Only Joe would buy such a thing.
ReadingMnemonic: The only person you know with a <kanji>superfluous</kanji> forehead table is <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the ten foot tall, simple farmhand.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>forehead</radical> <radical>table</radical> is completely <kanji>superfluous</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Try to imagine standing your whole life on a big, slanted slope, like one of these buildings that are so anti-Osaka. Wouldn't be easy, would it? By the way, although this kanji literally means "slope," it's mainly just used in the name for Osaka and to refer to the Osaka region, so that's the meaning we'll focus on.
ReadingHint: Han loves Osaka for its gritty, port-city vibe, which reminds him of the spaceport cantinas he used to frequent. And he chooses to live on top of a slope because he wants to be closer to space… obviously.
ReadingMnemonic: Who lives in <kanji>Osaka</kanji> at the top of a big <kanji>slope</kanji>? It's <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo.
MeaningMnemonic: The place that <radical>buildings</radical> feel very <radical>anti</radical>- towards is <kanji>Osaka</kanji>. Osaka is the last place they want to be built. Why? Because chances are they'll be built on a <kanji>slope</kanji>. Osaka has a lot of big slopes, and buildings hate being built on slopes.

MeaningHint: Imagine a hat walking about on your head, pacing back and forth deep in thought. See the hat stroke its chin in contemplation as it tries to think up a plan to get off your noggin and back inside.
ReadingHint: Watch as the hat begins to grin. This plan is so simple it’s sure to work. Now watch as the hat obtains the key and enters the locked door. What sort of mischief will the hat get into once inside?
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>plan</kanji> that made this hat stop right in its tracks was very simple. First, obtain the <reading>key</reading> (き) to the house. Then, use the key to get in the front door. How simple!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>hat</radical> comes to a <radical>stop</radical>, thinking of a <kanji>plan</kanji>. The hat had been pacing around for a while, trying to think of how to get off the head he's resting upon and back into the house where it's nice and cozy, and came to the sudden realization of a plan. This caused the hat to stop.

MeaningHint: Imagine watching a criminal after he's done stealing from a bank. He's all alone. He stops throwing the money around. And cries. So so sad.
ReadingHint: Imagine being so sad that your entire body fills with heat. That heat even makes squiggly heat lines pop out above and around you.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sad</kanji> feelings fill the criminal's body with <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). This heat spreads and radiates out. If you walk next to them on the street, you'll feel the sad heat floating by you.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>criminal</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical> will always be <kanji>sad</kanji>. No one chooses a life of crime if they're happy. And even if they seem like they're doing fine on the outside, deep down they're really sad.

MeaningHint: Imagine having a drum in your mouth and rejoicing about it! Imagine how loud it would be!
ReadingHint: Imagine if people could just stop the rejoicing of others by locking their mouths shut with keys. What a sad world.
ReadingMnemonic: You loudly <kanji>rejoice</kanji> for a while when suddenly someone runs over and shoves a <reading>key</reading> (き) in your mouth, locking it shut. You can't rejoice anymore! You need that key to unlock your mouth!
MeaningMnemonic: If there is a <radical>drum</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> it's time to <kanji>rejoice</kanji>! And because of your mouth drum your rejoicing will be loud and booming!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals! I shouldn't even have to tell you this by now.
ReadingHint: Picture Genji, the original Japanese protagonist, walking through the streets. He's signing his name for swooning ladies and gentlemen. Genji is the OG (original Genji).
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>original</kanji> Japanese hero was <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん), from "The Tale of Genji."
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>original</radical> radical and the <kanji>original</kanji> kanji are the same!

This kanji can also mean <kanji>field</kanji>, probably because that's what everything originally looked like before humans: just a bunch of fields.

MeaningHint: Imagine this thin thread of rice paddy. It looks so detailed because all the rice paddy stuff is contained in this thin thread shape. You could maybe get one narrow line of rice on this thing.
ReadingHint: Picture the cyborg eating a really thin piece of rice. Now it's completely full! What a smart, thrifty cyborg.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>thin</kanji> rice paddy is for your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). You gave your cyborg a very thin human stomach, so it can only digest one thin line of rice at a time.
MeaningMnemonic: If you take a <radical>thread</radical> and plant a <radical>rice paddy</radical> on it, the rice paddy is going to be extremely <kanji>thin</kanji>, but also <kanji>detailed</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine having some medicine that smells and tastes like fresh cut flowers. Smell the smell in your nose and taste the taste on your tongue.
ReadingHint: Picture a yak in your mind. Pretend you are riding atop the yak as it transports this medicine. Feel the fur of the yak in your hands as you cling on for dear life.
ReadingMnemonic: The best way to transport all the <kanji>medicine</kanji> on land is with a <reading>yak</reading> (やく). Yaks are big, strong, and very capable of transporting flower medicine like these.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> can be used for <radical>comfort</radical>, but also for <kanji>medicine</kanji>. Sure, it’s a comfort to smell flowers because they smell nice, but one can also use them as medicine to heal people.

MeaningHint: Really focus in on your nose. Your nostrils get huge, and your nose is huge. Smell the rice and its freshness in your imagination. Your nose is amazing.
ReadingHint: Honestly, is there anything better to do with your nose than smell 花s? 花s and noses are a match made in heaven (unless you have pollen allergies…).
ReadingMnemonic: As you sniff your rice paddies, an unexpected smell wafts into your <kanji>nose</kanji>: <reading>花</reading>s (はな). Oh no — did you accidentally plant 花s instead of rice? Oh well, the 花s are putting your nose in such a state of bliss that you actually think things might be better this way.
MeaningMnemonic: You're all by your<radical>self</radical> and you have to check your <radical>rice paddie</radical>s — all <radical>twenty</radical> of them! Luckily, you have an incredible <kanji>nose</kanji>, so you can quickly assess the state of your twenty rice paddies with just a few sniffs.

MeaningHint: Picture the shellfish leader in your mind and make sure to pay special attention to the knife that's sticking out of his side. It's right in the groove of his shell.
ReadingHint: Try saying かわ out the side of your mouth like the leader of the shellfish and you'll probably say がわ too. Don't stick a knife in your side though, we won't pay your medical bills when you complain to us that this mnemonic got you injured.
ReadingMnemonic: Because of this knife in his <kanji>side</kanji>, he can't pronounce certain words correctly. The most obvious one is when he says <reading>川</reading> (かわ), but it comes out of the side of his mouth as がわ.
