MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>shellfish</radical> has a <radical>knife</radical> in its <kanji>side</kanji>. It's fine though, don't worry, it's just a reminder that he won the great shellfish war back when they were trying to choose the new leader.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to carve some fish with an axe, to get them ready to eat. Not an easy thing to do. If you're a trained axe soldier, it'll probably be a lot easier. So picture yourself chopping off fins as an expert axe soldier.
ReadingHint: Imagine this soldier lighting a bale of hay on fire to cook his fish. The hay burns really fast, but it should be enough.
ReadingMnemonic: Uh-oh, it looks like this axe <kanji>soldier</kanji> was so excited about chopping the fins off his fish that he didn't think about how he was going to cook them. The only thing nearby to light on fire is a bale of <reading>hay</reading> (へい). Well, it's not the best thing, but at least it will get these fish cooked.
MeaningMnemonic: When you swing your <radical>axe</radical>, the <radical>ground</radical> ends up covered in <radical>fins</radical>, because you're a <kanji>soldier</kanji>. Only a skilled axe soldier could make such precise cuts as to de-fin a bunch of fish like that.

MeaningHint: Picture a long Viking hall, with a big table and tons of chairs. All of the Vikings are gossiping and sharing all their dirt on their enemies.
ReadingHint: Now picture all of the Vikings in the hall spitting dirt with one breath and shoveling doughnuts in with the next. It's pretty gross!
ReadingMnemonic: While the Vikings gather to spill the dirt in their <kanji>hall</kanji>, they also stuff their faces with <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう). Ah yes, Vikings love to eat doughnuts when they talk trash.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> will empty his <radical>mouth</radical> of all its <radical>dirt</radical> in the great <kanji>hall</kanji>. They all get together in some kind of <kanji>public chamber</kanji> and dish all the dirt (literal and figurative) that they've got.

MeaningHint: In olden days, salt was so valuable it was used as money. So imagine the gun buried in the dirt, its mouth vigilantly guarding the plate of salt.
ReadingHint: Are you sure you want to take her salt? Sure, she owes you, but I don't think you want to argue with the mouth of a gun.
ReadingMnemonic: When you see the <kanji>salt</kanji>, you think to yourself, "<reading>She owe</reading>s (しお) me big time, so I'm going to go ahead and snatch her salt!" Who is she, you ask? She's the lady who owns the salt. And the gun in the dirt, of course.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>dirt</radical>, there's a <radical>gun</radical>. Its <radical>mouth</radical> points toward a <radical>plate</radical>. What's on the plate? Why, it's <kanji>salt</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture this yurt, with towels laid out as seats for everyone who visits.
ReadingHint: Imagine the sexiest person you've ever seen, using a towel as a seat next to you in this yurt. Maybe they're in a bathing suit. Maybe they're completely covered up. Either way, they're very sexy.
ReadingMnemonic: Something about using a towel for a <kanji>seat</kanji> is very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき) to you. Probably because you associate it with sexy people using towel seats on the beach.
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>yurt</radical> you use a <radical>towel</radical> as a <kanji>seat</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine you're a shellfish in the cold, cold winter. The water is freezing but you must tough it out so no one will say you're a failure in spring when they find your dead, defeated shellfish body.
ReadingHint: Imagine being a shellfish freezing in the cold winter water, but you avoid failure by muttering haiku over and over to keep you warm and determined.
ReadingMnemonic: The best way to avoid <kanji>failure</kanji> is to say a little <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい) to distract you from the cold. In fact, this can work in any situation where you're afraid of defeat. Say a haiku aloud, it will calm your nerves and warm you from the inside.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>shellfish</radical> that can't live through <radical>winter</radical> is a <kanji>failure</kanji>. Any proper shellfish would survive through the winter, so this is quite an embarrassing <kanji>defeat</kanji>.

ReadingHint: Imagine you have a hankering for your favorite fruit. You gotta get in your car and put the pedal to the metal! Feel the wind in your hair as your car speeds down the road and listen to that engine purr.
ReadingMnemonic: They sell <kanji>fruit</kanji> at the store, and you need to drive your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) to get there. A car is the fastest way to fruit.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>fruit</radical> radical and the <kanji>fruit</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Bodacious!

MeaningHint: Imagine picking fruit off an ume tree this way, shooting them off the branches with your gun. You take aim at an unsuspecting plum — BANG! The gunshot rings in your ears and the smell of gunpowder fills the air. The ume branch is now one plum lighter.
ReadingHint: Imagine eating your harvest of ume. Taste the ripe fruity flesh of these Japanese plums as you stuff your gluttonous face.
ReadingMnemonic: You've probably heard of <kanji>ume</kanji> before, depending on your exposure to Japan, but an <reading>ume</reading> (うめ) is a <kanji>Japanese plum</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tree</radical> you aim your <radical>gun</radical> at from your <radical>window</radical> is an <kanji>ume</kanji>, aka a <kanji>Japanese plum</kanji>. Why? Because shooting the ume out of Japanese plum trees is, in your opinion, the best way to harvest plums.

ReadingHint: Listen to this cow moo. Can you do a better moo than this moo cow? Nope.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Nothing</kanji> can <reading>moo</reading> (む) quite like a cow can moo. Cows are the best at going moo. That’s a fact.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>nothing</radical> radical and the <kanji>nothing</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Woah!

MeaningHint: Tying two fingers together with a good luck thread is very common where you're from.
ReadingHint: This ketsup is exactly what you were looking for, but now it won't stop. You need to get out of this bind by untying the thread on your finger. But it won't budge. It's covered in ketsup. Help! You're going to drown!
ReadingMnemonic: After you <kanji>bind</kanji> your fingers, the good luck starts pouring in. Literally. Suddenly <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) starts pouring down from the heavens.
MeaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>thread</radical> for <radical>good luck</radical> by <kanji>bind</kanji>ing two fingers together. You <kanji>tie</kanji> them nice and tight.

MeaningHint: Imagine all the dumb things you blabber about, with your mouth open big and wide. Saying things you shouldn't say, causing all kinds of drama. Shame on you.
ReadingHint: Who knew you could cause people to live in an inn instead of their own homes with your big mouth...
ReadingMnemonic: Your big mouth is the <kanji>cause</kanji> behind all your friends and family moving to an <reading>inn</reading> (いん). They move to the inn so they don't have to live with you and hear you talk anymore.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is so <radical>big</radical> that it's the <kanji>cause</kanji> of all your problems.

MeaningHint: Vikings get a lot of nasty stuff on their mouths from their messy eating and pillaging, so it makes sense that they'd all have mouth towels. It's as ordinary and normal as it gets, at least amongst the vikings.
ReadingHint: When you ask Joe about his Viking mouth towel, he just tells you, "It's normal for everyone to have mouth towels. Totally ordinary." Now that you think about it, he really is a messy dude, so he probably needs it.
ReadingMnemonic: It's <kanji>normal</kanji> for a viking to have a mouth towel, but your sloppy farmhand friend <reading>Joe</reading>(じょう) has a mouth towel too. That's a habit that's only <kanji>normal</kanji> for vikings, so why does he have one?
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>viking</radical> with a <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>towel</radical> is a totally <kanji>normal</kanji> thing. It's <kanji>ordinary</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Yes, you can even tell a tequila drunk from a vodka drunk based on the sounds they make. (Imagine how exactly they'd be different.) To be able to say what sound each kind of drunkard makes, it takes a very discerning, discriminating mind.
ReadingHint: All chic people show some kind of discernment. For some it's about discerning what's fashionable. For you, it's about discriminating one kind of drunk sound from another. That's your version of chic.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Discerning</kanji> individuals like you are considered <reading>chic</reading> (しき). What's more chic than your <kanji>discriminating</kanji> way of listening?
MeaningMnemonic: If you can <radical>say</radical> what <radical>sound</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> makes, you must be very <kanji>discerning</kanji>. Few people are able to discern what sound a drunkard makes, but you — you can say, because you're a very <kanji>discriminating</kanji> listener.

MeaningHint: Think of all the criminals you know. Even if what they did was a mistake, it was still some type of injustice, right? It was something the rest of the world sees as a negative thing.
ReadingHint: This is the heat of embarrassment, of knowing you did something negative or wrong.
ReadingMnemonic: When you commit an <kanji>injustice</kanji>, you'll know because <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) fills your body. Maybe you've never done any kind of big thing, but when you make a <kanji>mistake</kanji> your face fills with heat and you turn red!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>criminal</radical> has done an <kanji>injustice</kanji> on the world or other people. Being a criminal is a <kanji>negative</kanji> thing, and even if it was just a <kanji>mistake</kanji>, it was still a crime.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine keeping Genghis Khan completely dry all day every day. He won't even get wet to bathe. Instead he makes you rub his body with dry sand and salts.
ReadingMnemonic: You need to <kanji>dry</kanji> Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). He hates being wet, all of the Mongols do, so your job is to dry his entire body. And his clothes, and his horse. In fact, when it rains, you have to ride next to him keeping him dry by holding an umbrella above his head.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dry</radical> radical and the <kanji>dry</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine the sun in the sky. Its rays blind you as it corrects all of the errors you’ve made.
ReadingHint: Imagine the sun being so absolutely consumed with rage that it catches a zeppelin on fire and crashes it. Feel the heat from the grounded zeppelin as it smolders.
ReadingMnemonic: Then, a <reading>ze</reading>ppelin (ぜ) flies in front of the sun, blocking it out. <kanji>Absolutely</kanji>, the sun cannot ignore this transgression. Therefore, the sun corrects the zeppelin’s evil ways by frying it with sunshine and bringing it to the ground.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is <radical>correct</radical>, <kanji>absolutely</kanji>. The mighty sun is never wrong. It is absolutely correct, always.

MeaningHint: Imaging taking your shoes off, hiking up your pants, and holding all of your things above your head as you ford across the tsunami.
ReadingHint: Your new fording techniques have increased the Shougun's military power! Congrats, I guess.
ReadingMnemonic: Your ability to <kanji>ford</kanji> this tsunami impresses everyone who witnesses it, especially the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). Now that you've taught him a tsunami can be forded, he's going to start sending his troops through them instead of waiting for them to pass.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is coming, and you want to <radical>walk</radical> over it, so you have to <kanji>ford</kanji> it. You don’t walk across a tsunami, you <kanji>wade</kanji> across it.

MeaningHint: Picture the government bear looking through government documents and snorting gruffly as it decides what to sign and what to reject. It has a little government sash and hat.
ReadingHint: Picture Genghis Khan patting the first bear government worker on the back as they start their first day working for the government.
ReadingMnemonic: The first <kanji>government</kanji> that allowed bears was set up by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). He was all about conquering people and then using them as efficiently as possible. He discovered that bears are incredibly useful in government buildings as government workers.
MeaningMnemonic: Under this <radical>roof</radical> is a <radical>bear</radical> who works for the <kanji>government</kanji>. If you saw a bear out in the wild you might be afraid and try to run, but if you see a bear inside a building under a roof, don't be scared! It's just a government bear.

MeaningHint: Imagine being on the roof above a festival. Can you guess what's happening below you based on the sounds and smells?
ReadingHint: And what's Satsuki doing up here, anyway? Your guess is as good as mine. Now imagine you and her trying to guess what kind of festival it is.
ReadingMnemonic: To get a better <kanji>guess</kanji>, you ask <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) for help. She's that little girl you know from some earlier mnemonics, and she happens to be up on the roof with you. Picture her as you ask, "What's your guess, Satsuki?"
MeaningMnemonic: If you're on the <radical>roof</radical> of the <radical>festival</radical> you'll have to <kanji>guess</kanji> what's actually going on below you. You can kind of see that there are rides and maybe food? It smells like food. But you have to guess on the specifics because you're above it and not in it.

MeaningHint: Picture all of the people in your clan under this canopy digging with all their might, trying to find the bottom. "Isn't there... no bottom though?" you think to yourself.
ReadingHint: The bottom is full of water, and everyone and everything down there is soaked through now.
ReadingMnemonic: Suddenly someone down in the hole shouts that they've reached the <kanji>bottom</kanji>, but it's full of water and they're all <reading>soak</reading>ed (そこ)!
MeaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>canopy</radical> your <radical>clan</radical> digs in the <radical>ground</radical>, trying to get to the <kanji>bottom</kanji>. They want to build a nice home your entire clan can live in, but they need to get to the bottom before they'll start building.

MeaningHint: Picture a person you love. Feel the joy of love in your heart as you brave the winter to see them. Your cleats crunch the snow underfoot, it's cold outside, but you're kept warm and happy by that feeling of love.
ReadingHint: Imagine seeing your love with your own two eyes after a long time apart. It feels great to be reunited and see eye to eye once again. Imagine this feeling.
ReadingMnemonic: Walking through the winter snow with your cleats on, you've gotta keep your <reading>eye</reading> (あい) on the prize. It's a really hard journey, but you keep your eye on the prize, which is your <kanji>love</kanji> and the time you'll get to spend with them.
MeaningMnemonic: Put on <radical>cleat</radical>s so you can plant a kiss on your sweetheart's <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>heart</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> — that's <kanji>love</kanji>. You'll be braving the harsh winter elements for love, so you put on your cleats to get a better grip on the snow. Sure, you could just wait for better weather to go see them, but then it wouldn't be true love, would it?

MeaningHint: Doesn't matter who, but you're going to catch someone from a government office using this net, it doesn't matter who.
ReadingHint: Imagine the show they put on. You'll call your show "Exactly 8 Hours of Bureaucracy."
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Government office</kanji> and <kanji>political office</kanji> people put on the best <reading>show</reading>s (しょ). That's why you want to capture someone from there with your net.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>net</radical> is for <radical>someone</radical> who works in a <kanji>government office</kanji>. You'll also take someone from a <kanji>political office</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the things you'd warn people about. What is it about winter that's so dangerous?
ReadingHint:
The winter cold will make you ache
Your whole body will start to shake
Don't go outside
I'll eat your cake
ReadingMnemonic: To make sure people listen to your <kanji>warn</kanji>ing, you threaten to take away their <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). People will do anything for cake, so when you bring it up, they actually take your attempts to warn them about winter seriously.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> that you <radical>say</radical> to <kanji>warn</kanji> people against going outside. It's cold and dangerous in winter! You <kanji>admonish</kanji> anyone and everyone you see going out there with your scary winter poem.

MeaningHint: Perhaps you have a person in mind that your heart comes back to again and again. Associate that person with this notion and kanji, and this'll be easy to memorize!
ReadingHint: Don’t let your love become a rental. Wait for the right person to come around.
ReadingMnemonic: But, remember that your <kanji>romance</kanji> isn’t a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん). You really wanna reserve it for the right person!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Again</radical> and again, your <radical>heart</radical> comes back to the same person. Is this <kanji>romance</kanji>? Are you in <kanji>love</kanji>?

MeaningHint: How else would a Viking memorize something? Imagine having to memorize sea charts for your Viking navigation. Look and see the maps in your brain as you memorize them.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself memorizing the sound of a cock crow and using it to play jokes on your fellow Vikings. Cock-a-doodle-doo!
ReadingMnemonic: Every morning, a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) crows and rouses the Vikings. Seeing this, one of the Vikings <kanji>memorize</kanji>s the cock crow so that he can mimic it and play pranks on his fellow Vikings.
MeaningMnemonic:
<radical>Viking</radical> <radical>see</radical>, Viking <kanji>memorize</kanji>. That’s how Vikings learn things. They see something, and then they memorize it. Quite simple, really.

It can also mean <kanji>awake</kanji>. Just remember that Vikings can't see if they aren't awake. There won't be anything to memorize if their eyes are still closed, after all!

MeaningHint: Imagine saying your theory out loud to a guard about how what he's guarding isn't even there. Imagine how he would react.
ReadingHint: Which theory do you believe? That there is nothing or that there are sets of things?
ReadingMnemonic: The guard listens to your <kanji>theory</kanji> but then he says his OWN! He thinks there are two <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) of guarded items, and he's only guarding one. According to his theory, either one could be real! His theory is more plausible, don't you think?
MeaningMnemonic: What do you <radical>say</radical> to a <radical>guard</radical>? Your <kanji>theory</kanji> about how the thing he's guarding isn't even real. Yeah, you have this whole thing figured out. You have for a while. At this point, it's only fair that you tell your theory to the guard who's wasting his time.

MeaningHint: Imagine having poop in your cape. It would smell bad and anyone close to you would be able to smell right through the illusion.
ReadingHint: Genji grabs the cape before you can stop him and he puts it on. You can see the illusion fade, he keeps smiling but more tears start streaming down his face. "A wonderful illusion, the best I've ever seen," he chokes out and marches away, without looking back.
ReadingMnemonic: Somehow the poop cape <kanji>illusion</kanji> goes over really well. The crowd is giving you a standing ovation and right in the center is <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). He marches up to you, wipes a tear from his eye, and says, "Your illusion cape is wonderful. I must have it!"
MeaningMnemonic: You hide <radical>poop</radical> in a <radical>cape</radical> as part of the <kanji>illusion</kanji> in your magic show. Your acts are kind of gross and this one involves creating the illusion of disappearing poop by hiding it within the folds of your cape.

MeaningHint: Imagine getting really close to a sheep and seeing all the tiny detailed curls in their wool. There are hidden patterns you'd never noticed before now!
ReadingHint: Picture the Shougun, leaning in close to inspect the detailed patterns all over the sheep. His nose is so close, he can probably smell all the little detailed nuances of the sheep's musk too.
ReadingMnemonic: The reason these sheep are so <kanji>detailed</kanji> is that they are trying to impress the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). If they're fancy enough, perhaps the Shougun won’t kill them for their meat and make their wool into clothing.
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>sheep</radical> are <kanji>detailed</kanji> creatures. I mean, I never thought sheep were very detailed, but that’s what they say.

MeaningHint: Picture this scene from the drama as dramatically as you can. The pig stares into the tiger's eyes, "Tiger, you wouldn't–uh!" The pig's eyes slowly lower to see the knife sticking into its gut, the tiger's hand still holding it. "This is what you get, you pig," the tiger spits back, twisting.
ReadingHint: Picture Gecki acting out the part of the tiger and the pig in this drama. It's so intense, you're moved to tears. Bravo Gecki, bravo!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>drama</kanji> is being put on by <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your childhood pet gecko. Gecki is playing all the parts, because as true drama fans know, one-gecko-shows are the highest form of art.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical> kills a <radical>pig</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> in this new <kanji>drama</kanji> about the animal kingdom! The tiger and pig used to be friends when they were younger, but now they're bitter enemies and the tiger just stabbed the pig right in the chest with a knife.

MeaningHint: Imagine saying things to this river. What would you say? Flow harder! Babble better! Picture yourself shouting at the river as the water moves along.
ReadingHint: Picture this raccoon captain and his raccoon crew coming down the river. The raccoons are all chittering about as they paddle the boat down the river.
ReadingMnemonic: While giving <kanji>instruction</kanji> to the river, you see a small boat coming down it. Inside the miniature boat is a rac<reading>coon</reading> (くん) captain and his raccoon crew. The raccoon captain is instructing them, “Stroke! Stroke!”
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> stuff to a <radical>river</radical>, giving it <kanji>instruction</kanji>. This river is a new river so it needs you to say things, giving it instruction on what to do. Otherwise the river wouldn’t know how to behave.

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals!
ReadingHint: Imagine fitting a cucumber into the string of your bow. Imagine the smell of it as you draw the string back. Mm, take a quick bite, then let it loose.
ReadingMnemonic: You have a <kanji>bow</kanji> in one hand and in the other... a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう). You're going to shoot this cucumber with this bow into the salad you're making. Many people don't know this, but this makes your cucumbers taste better.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bow</radical> radical and the <kanji>bow</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine announcing to all the denizens of the playground that you've licked their slide clean. For some reason, no one seems to be rushing to play on it…
ReadingHint: Honestly, after that big announcement, you deserve a Coke. Public speaking's hard, so you should treat yourself to a Coke whenever you announce something — regardless of whether you had to lick something gross beforehand.
ReadingMnemonic: After you <kanji>announce</kanji> the slide is clean, you take a big swig of <reading>Coke</reading> (こく). The dirty slide left a nasty taste in your mouth, but the Coke is helping to wash it away.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>slide</radical> covered in <radical>dirt</radical> and use your <radical>mouth</radical> to clean it off. Then, you <kanji>announce</kanji> to everyone that the slide is clean and they can use it safely again.

MeaningHint: Imagine sorting all the different types of grains by their weight.
ReadingHint: Picture the two chutes you use to separate the different types of grains. One has a sign that says "Light Chute" and the other says "Heavy Chute."
ReadingMnemonic: You sort all of the different <kanji>kind</kanji>s and <kanji>type</kanji>s by putting them in the right <reading>chu</reading>tes (しゅ). They slide away, probably to some big type-based piles you can't see.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>grain</radical> seems extra <radical>heavy</radical>, you know it's a different <kanji>kind</kanji>. Not all <kanji>type</kanji>s of grains are the same. So when you feel the heavy type, you know it isn't the same as the light kind.

MeaningHint: Feel the dirt spatter your face as you zip around off-road trails with your sheep under your arm. You will attain glory! You will attain greatness!
ReadingHint: Feel the pain of the tattoo needle as you get this commemorative tattoo. You're getting this tattoo on your forehead, of course, because you want to make sure everyone sees it.
ReadingMnemonic: After you <kanji>attain</kanji> that <kanji>plural</kanji> combination of glory, greatness, and fame, you get a <reading>tattoo</reading> (たつ) to celebrate: "world's greatest," in gothic lettering, above a picture of you and your sheep on your scooter.
MeaningMnemonic: You ride your scooter through the dirt while carrying a sheep because you're determined to attain something. Greatness? Glory? Everlasting fame? Maybe you'll attain a plural combination of all those things. After all, you are the best sheep-carrying off-road scooterist the world has ever seen.

MeaningHint: Picture this big geoduck shoveling rice down in a flurry of geoduckness. Hear the sloshing and goopy glugging as the geoduck gorges itself on every type of rice.
ReadingHint: Picture this big, rice-engorged geoduck, too big to move. It's now in a wheel chair and on a rice IV. Imagine its wheelchair squeaking by as it wheezes and groans — ruined by all those types of rice.
ReadingMnemonic: While eating every <kanji>type</kanji> and <kanji>kind</kanji> of rice from every <kanji>category</kanji>, the big geoduck has become even bigger. Its body is <reading>rui</reading>ned (るい). It's painfully obese. This geoduck has eaten itself to ruin.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>rice</radical> you feed to a <radical>big</radical> <radical>geoduck</radical> can be of any <kanji>type</kanji>, <kanji>kind</kanji>, or <kanji>category</kanji>. It doesn't matter what kind of rice it is, the big geoduck will eat it.

MeaningHint: Stand up and look at the happy news today! It definitely isn't sad or depressing.
ReadingHint: Because your reporting is done from home, most of the news is about how your plants are growing. What the cat did this morning. And it certainly isn't the news without the weather!
ReadingMnemonic: You research and <kanji>report</kanji> the <kanji>news</kanji> from <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Only after, once all of the news is stamped onto the stools, do you leave your home.
MeaningMnemonic: You want to share <radical>happiness</radical> with people so you <radical>stamp</radical> <radical>stool</radical>s with the <kanji>news</kanji>. Now when people want a <kanji>report</kanji> of all the happy things going on, they can just look down before they sit.

MeaningHint: Picture this bamboo temple compared to some temples made of stone or wood. It's the same size, shape, and it has the same number of worshipers. It's equal in every way.
ReadingHint: While many things in とうきょう may not be created equal, the temples are, at least.
ReadingMnemonic: The bamboo temple is also an <kanji>equal</kanji> distance away from the other temples in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. When you look at a map of とうきょう temples, you can measure them out and they'll all be perfectly equal.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>temple</radical> is <kanji>equal</kanji> to all of the other non-bamboo temples. Just because it's made of bamboo doesn't make it any less special and spiritual.

MeaningHint: Imagine how much steam a tsunami could create. Hear the steam hissing off of the water and feel the moisture from the steam washing over your body.
ReadingHint: Steam is very light, so carrying this box of steam energy will be no problem. Picture yourself wandering about with a box of steam in one hand and a key in the other.
ReadingMnemonic: In order to contain this <kanji>steam</kanji> energy and store it for later, you lock it up in a box with a <reading>key</reading> (き). That way the energy can be used at a later time. Provided you don’t lose the key, that is.
MeaningMnemonic: The water from a <radical>tsunami</radical> can create a lot of <radical>energy</radical> when made into <kanji>steam</kanji>. Steam power used to be all the rage back in the day. Steam comes from water, water comes from tsunamis, so logically, tsunamis are a great source of energy.

MeaningHint: Think about what precious thing this leader borrowed from you so long ago. What was it? Think about how angry you are about not having the thing returned to you.
ReadingHint: Come up with all the terrible things you do to this leader in the shack. Hey, it's just a little payback for the borrowing he did.
ReadingMnemonic: In your rage, you decide to find this leader, take back what's yours, and in return, <kanji>borrow</kanji> the leader's life. So you found him, grabbed him, and threw him into a <reading>shack</reading> (しゃく) — where you "borrow" a few parts of the leader himself, and do other terrible things to him.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> from <radical>long ago</radical> thought to <kanji>borrow</kanji> something precious from you. This leader did not return the thing he borrowed, and now he will pay.

MeaningHint: Imagine you're the gambler. Feel the heat intensify as your nostrils fill with the smell of baked goods.
ReadingHint: Imagine this guy running around baking in the fire of his home, only this time with a Yankees hat and jersey on. He doesn't bake for them anymore, but he still loves the team.
ReadingMnemonic: He used to <kanji>bake</kanji> for the <reading>Ya</reading>nkees (や). Baseball cakes, bat baguettes, that kind of thing. A career-ending kitchen injury forced him to retire. That's when he developed a gambling problem. But he still likes to bake whenever the Yankees are on TV.
MeaningMnemonic:
In a dangerous <radical>fire</radical>, a <radical>gambler</radical> will always try to <kanji>bake</kanji> something before fleeing to safety. It could be his own house on fire, and the gambler would still use the free heat source to get some baking done. Maybe he's gambling that those baked goods will offset the cost of a new home…

This kanji also means <kanji>burn</kanji>, because that's what happens when you expose something to a lot of heat.

MeaningHint: Imagine how it feels sitting on dirt. Is it comfortable? Probably not. Maybe you feel a little uneasy about just sitting on plain dirt. Try to recall this feeling whenever you encounter this kanji.
ReadingHint: This Czar you met is ruthless and has formally banned all non-sitting activities. Imagine the excruciating pain, the stiffness, the cramping in your legs — all caused by this 24-7 sit-a-thon the Czar has cruelly decreed.
ReadingMnemonic: Who decided people have to <kanji>sit</kanji> like this? The C<reading>za</reading>r (ざ). The Czar wants to be above everyone, so he makes them sit on the dirt under canopies while he goes around on horseback or something, towering over them as they sit.
MeaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>canopy</radical>, there are two <radical>person</radical>s on the <radical>dirt</radical>. What do they do there? They <kanji>sit</kanji>. The people use the dirt under the canopy as a <kanji>seat</kanji> for their butts.

MeaningHint: Imagine someone close to you experiencing the death of their heart. They look right at you but they've already started to forget everything. I know this is not a fun thing to imagine, but use that emotion to help you remember this kanji, not forget it.
ReadingHint: You know, that's probably why so many old people go bowling. They forgot everything else.
ReadingMnemonic: When someone <kanji>forget</kanji>s everything they're left with only one thing to do: go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). Bowling is easy, and there's nothing to forget. You pick up a ball. You drop it. Everything else is automatically taken care of.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>death</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> will cause you to <kanji>forget</kanji> all of the most important things. People think memories are kept in your brain, but that's not true. Your heart is where all of your most precious memories are kept, and when it dies, you forget them all.

MeaningHint: Imagine this soapy tsunami, washing all the things in town. Hear the soapy waves bubbling with cleanliness as they crash down around you.
ReadingHint: Imagine these centaurs frolicking around in the aftermath of the tsunami previous. It's just like a big bathtub for them. Hear them splashing and neighing in the water.
ReadingMnemonic: It wasn't just the town that got a <kanji>wash</kanji>, it was the <reading>cen</reading>taurs (せん) too. (Centaurs happen to live in this town.) And being half horse, centaurs have a lot of hair that needs washing. Luckily, the soap tsunami gave them a good wash too.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>previous</radical>ly gave everything in this town a good <kanji>wash</kanji>. There are many laundromats in this town, so when the previous tsunami hit, all of the detergent mixed with the water and gave everything a good wash.

MeaningHint: Imagine finding this treasure chest and donning the mighty chest armor. Feel the weight of the golden chest piece and the vast protection it grants you.
ReadingHint: If you can remember this kanji's reading and meaning with a Canadian accent, you'll have no problem, eh.
ReadingMnemonic: Where is this <kanji>chest</kanji> armor from? It's from the moon. Now how would you say that if you were Canadian? It's from the <reading>moon, eh</reading> (むね). Right, this here chest armor is from the moon, eh.
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>moon</radical> <radical>prison</radical>, there lies a <radical>treasure chest</radical> containing a golden <kanji>chest</kanji> piece. This mighty chest armor will grant you protection from nearly any attack, because it is imbued with moon magic.

MeaningHint: As you visualize the treasure chest you realize that the moon was your face, the grass your hair, and the treasure chest your brain! It was you all along!
ReadingHint: Use your brain like Nostradamus and try to see the future. You see... you see... huh, your brain is just surrounded by kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: If you can unlock the secrets of your <kanji>brain</kanji> you'll have powers like <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう). Nostradamus was able to look inward at his own hidden brain treasure chest and see the future, so maybe you can be like him too.
MeaningMnemonic: Nestled in the light of the <radical>moon</radical> in the <radical>grass</radical> is a <radical>treasure chest</radical> that you can see with your <kanji>brain</kanji>. You close your eyes, and you can see the spot, but it's kept deep in your brain, and you have to use its power to find it.

MeaningHint: Picture this leader in their mask. Listen to them chant prayers that reverberate throughout the building.
ReadingHint: Imagine the monk/priest's bright, pure soul shining behind the mask. You'll never actually see it, unless you're becoming a monk/priest yourself, and then your soul will need protecting too.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>monk</kanji> or <kanji>priest</kanji> is wearing a mask to protect their <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). You aren't a monk or a priest, so they don't want your not-religious-enough eyes to dirty their soul.
MeaningMnemonic: A religious <radical>leader</radical> wearing a <radical>mask</radical> could be either a <kanji>monk</kanji> or a <kanji>priest</kanji>. You don’t know, their face is covered by a mask!

MeaningHint: Try out Zen meditation for a little bit. Look away from your computer or phone or tablet and let your life be simple for a second. Just let it all go and free your spirit from all the noise.
ReadingHint: Without a new reading to learn, you should have time to close your eyes and clear your mind. Zen it out, friend.
ReadingMnemonic: The reading for this kanji is <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん), just like the <kanji>Zen</kanji> in <kanji>Zen Buddhism</kanji>. Pretty easy to remember, no? Just remember that the reading is the same as the meaning. It’s easy!
MeaningMnemonic: You want your <radical>spirit</radical> to be <radical>simple</radical> so you practice <kanji>Zen Buddhism</kanji>. Zen is all about meditating and letting earthly things go. There's nothing more simple for your spirit than being <kanji>Zen</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine getting a magical lip ring. First, you have to find a passable one. Then, you have to go through the pain of having your lip pierced. Is it even possible to imagine how painful that would be?!
ReadingHint: It was possible to get this passable car thanks to your lip ring. Hooray!
ReadingMnemonic: Since anything and everything is <kanji>possible</kanji> with your new <kanji>passable</kanji> lip ring, you use it to get a new <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You go to the car dealership and ask for a free (passable) car. The manager is impressed with your lip ring and gladly hands over the keys to the latest model of your favorite car. Not bad.
MeaningMnemonic: With a <radical>lip ring</radical>, anything is <kanji>possible</kanji>. Having a lip ring grants you the power to conquer any obstacle, achieve any goal. As long as it's a <kanji>passable</kanji> lip ring, that is. If it isn't good enough, it isn't possible to be passable.

MeaningHint: Imagine a world where you're not allowed to say anything except for at noon. At that moment, after you've been staring at the clock, waiting for that opportunity, you burst forth with things you want to say. But, once it hits 12:01, you are grabbed and your mouth is covered. You are not permitted to say anything outside of that time.
ReadingHint: Imagine going to big Kyoto where everything is fine. Then, go to little Kyoto where it's not. Little Kyoto has the short きょ (instead of the long one), and this is how you can remember the difference.
ReadingMnemonic: Where do these archaic horrible rules exist? The smaller version of Kyoto, Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). Normal Kyoto is just fine. This smaller one is where a group of people went to create their own little town with their own dumb rules.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Say</radical> it at <radical>noon</radical>. That's the only time I will <kanji>allow</kanji> you to say it. That's the only time I will <kanji>permit</kanji> you to say it. Other than that, no saying anything. Noon is your window.

MeaningHint: What with the snow and wind, winter also tends to knock trees down flat… which is probably why the tree radical on the left looks all flat and squished here. That's another way to remember this kanji is a counter for flat stuff!
ReadingHint: Now imagine yourself counting with these mice you just smashed. They're freshly dead, so there's not much of a stench yet, but you still feel bad about using dead mice to help you count. Imagine yourself counting with these mice as you try to hold back tears from all the murder you just did.
ReadingMnemonic: As you count each <kanji>counter for flat objects</kanji>, a bunch of <reading>mi</reading>ce (まい) show up and start climbing all over you. This won't do, so you take two slats and start smashing and flattening all the mice. Now that the mice are flat too, you can also use them as a counter for flat objects. Good job, mice.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> that's been sliced up in <radical>winter</radical> can be used as a <kanji>flat object counter</kanji>. Since it's winter, all the leaves have fallen off the tree, so it's easy to cut into slats. These slats can be used as counters for flat objects.

MeaningHint: Imagine watching this conflict from above the blue water on a boat. You can kind of see the action, but you can't hear a thing.
ReadingHint: Sabers are sharp, curved, and totally quiet. Great for underwater blue conflicts, and sneaky assassinations.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch below the <kanji>quiet</kanji> water, every once in a while a <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) breaches the surface and quietly slices back down.
MeaningMnemonic: Within the <radical>blue</radical> water, a <radical>conflict</radical> stirs, but is made <kanji>quiet</kanji> by the muffling power of liquid. Under this blue water is a great conflict between two sea monsters, but on the surface it appears peaceful and quiet as the water prevents sound from rising above the surface.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine actually getting an assignment to write a paragraph all about cookies. Would you write about your favorite cookies? Your eating methods?
ReadingMnemonic: You have to write an entire <kanji>paragraph</kanji> about a topic, so you choose <reading>coo</reading>kies (く).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>paragraph</radical> radical and the <kanji>paragraph</kanji> kanji are exactly the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine standing in front of a forest with your jackhammer, staring at a sign with just this kanji on it. Tree plus tree with jackhammer equals prohibited. Hmm.
ReadingHint: Picture the King angrily covering his ears as his subjects jackhammer trees. He screams and shouts, his crown bouncing on his head, and points at his new sign that prohibits the jackhammering of trees.
ReadingMnemonic: The person who decided to <kanji>prohibit</kanji> jackhammering two trees is the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. He's the only person who's able to prohibit certain things, so it had to be him.
MeaningMnemonic: Killing two <radical>tree</radical>s with a <radical>jackhammer</radical> is expressly <kanji>prohibit</kanji>ed. Don't get me wrong, you can rip up one tree with your jackhammer, but not two.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself doing a magic trick where you put a big sword in your mouth. You say: "Now, for my next illusion, I will consume a sword!"
ReadingHint: It's odd that there would be a "consume a sword" trick in your magic kit. It seems so real for a kit you can buy at the store.
ReadingMnemonic: The "<kanji>consume</kanji> a sword" illusion came with a cheap magic <reading>kit</reading> (きつ) you had.
MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>mouth</radical> you put your <radical>life</radical> on the line by putting in a <radical>sword</radical> that is <radical>big</radical>. You put it all the way down there, so you <kanji>consume</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: Imagine the smoke stopping up your lungs and getting in your eyes. All you know other than the smoke is the sound of the helicopter that's causing it to happen.
ReadingHint: Imagine choking on the smoke and accepting that this is the end. Boohoo, if only you'd learned more kanji before the end!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>smoke</kanji> is too much and you realize this is the <reading>en</reading>d (えん) for you. You're gonna die here!
MeaningMnemonic: If you have a <radical>fire</radical> and a <radical>helicopter</radical> hovering beside it, there's going to be a lot of <radical>dirt</radical> and <kanji>smoke</kanji> billowing around. Mostly smoke, on account of the fire. Now it's in your eyes and mouth. It gets in your lungs and you can't breathe. Why, helicopter, why!

MeaningHint: Say this sentence out loud. Incredible! You've used the power of your mouth to add words together in a sentence. Your parents must be very proud of you.
ReadingHint: Imagine riding around in your new car, singing songs with your powerful mouth to the playlists you added to the fancy new radio.
ReadingMnemonic: Your parents are so proud of you being able to <kanji>add</kanji> words together that they surprise you with a new <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Wow! You add some stickers to your bumper and add your favorite playlist to the car radio. Let's drive.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> lets you <kanji>add</kanji> words together to form a sentence. Marvel at the mighty additive power your mouth holds! You add words together in an amazing fashion!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself in a store, looking at a bunch of different bamboo stamps to make sure you don’t buy one that’s out of season, or one that has damaged joints. The season and the joints, that’s what quality bamboo is all about, and it’s why we have these stamps!
ReadingHint: How many bamboo sets will you be buying this season? I'm getting 42 sets.
ReadingMnemonic: These <kanji>season</kanji> stamps are marked on bamboo <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). You can't buy a single banana or a single grape, and just like that, if you want some nice seasonal bamboo, you have to buy them in sets.
MeaningMnemonic:
On the bottom of <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>root</radical>s, there’s a <radical>stamp</radical> that tells you the <kanji>season</kanji> in which it was cut.

Additionally, the stamp also has information on the condition of the bamboo’s <kanji>joint</kanji>s, because no one wants to buy bamboo with damaged joints!

MeaningHint: Just make sure you get a really thirsty canine, because they're going to need to drink a LOT of water to decrease the tsunami's power.
ReadingHint: Now you're wondering if you made the right choice. The world might actually be a better place if Genji decreased his silly lover-boy antics a bit.
ReadingMnemonic: After you and your canine <kanji>decrease</kanji> the tsunami's power, <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん) comes out to thank you. That tsunami was on track to wash away Genji's house, which would have forced him to majorly decrease his frivolous playboy activities.
MeaningMnemonic: Direct a <radical>tsunami</radical> into a <radical>canine</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> to <kanji>decrease</kanji> the amount of water that strikes the surrounding area.

MeaningHint: Geoducks usually live in the ocean, but when they migrate to a river, they do so in a specific, predetermined order. Imagine what that sequence might be. Maybe in order of neck length, with the longest-necked geoducks going first? Or maybe in alphabetical order?
ReadingHint: Imagine it's June, and you're on a beach somewhere watching a bunch of geoducks make their way to a river. Marvel at the biological clock that determines the June-based migration sequence for these fascinating creatures.
ReadingMnemonic: Actually, they go in birth-month <kanji>order</kanji>, starting from <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). No one knows why they follow this June-based <kanji>sequence</kanji>. But the geoducks born in June always go first in the migration order.
MeaningMnemonic: When they migrate to a <radical>river</radical>, a group of <radical>geoducks</radical> must do so in a certain <kanji>order</kanji> and <kanji>sequence</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a valley in your mind. Now put a roof on it. Hmm. It isn't <kanji>shape</kanji>d like a valley anymore.
ReadingHint: Now imagine this taking shape before your eyes and becoming actual yogurt! You grab a giant spoon and take a bite.
ReadingMnemonic: Its new <kanji>appearance</kanji> looks familiar to you, like it's a big <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう) cup! A valley with a roof on it looks exactly like a yogurt cup. Huh!
MeaningMnemonic: If you put a <radical>roof</radical> over a <radical>valley</radical> it will change its <kanji>form</kanji>. Its <kanji>appearance</kanji> will alter completely!

MeaningHint: Picture a narwhal wrapped in a fuzzy towel blanket, with a towel tail cover, and some towel underpants. Everything is made of this type of cloth.
ReadingHint: "You fool, you aren't a narwhal, that cloth isn't for you!" they say. They're so mean.
ReadingMnemonic: If you use this type of <kanji>cloth</kanji> for your clothes and blankets people will call you a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). They think you're a fool who can't tell the difference between human cloth and towel cloth.
MeaningMnemonic: To a <radical>narwhal</radical>, a <radical>towel</radical> is just normal <kanji>cloth</kanji>. They're always wet so towel cloth is all they know.

MeaningHint: Easy is easy!
ReadingHint: I told you easy was easy.
ReadingMnemonic: What would be an <kanji>easy</kanji> reading for this kanji? <reading>い</reading>! The sound easy starts with!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>easy</radical> radical and the <kanji>easy</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Picture the talented person huddled over a pile of shellfish, whispering to them about the wealth they'll soon be turned into.
ReadingHint: You'd be wealthy too if you sold that cyborg you made. It may not have any shellfish in it and it can't play the xylophone, but you could change that…
ReadingMnemonic: This talented person really starts accumulating <kanji>wealth</kanji> when their <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) is unveiled. It's a half-man, half-shellfish cyborg that plays the <reading>xy</reading>lophone  (ざい), and everyone wants to buy it.
MeaningMnemonic: Give some <radical>shellfish</radical> to a <radical>talent</radical>ed person and they'll have no problem accumulating <kanji>wealth</kanji>. People with talent can make money out of pretty much anything, so shellfish are like easy-mode.

MeaningHint: Look to your right, if you see flowers (metaphorically, maybe) then you're still young. Congratulations!
ReadingHint: All young people have a walker looming just off to the side. A sign that your youth won't last forever, dear summer child.
ReadingMnemonic: When <kanji>young</kanji> people look to the right, they'll see their future: a <reading>walke</reading>r (わか). You know, those metal contraptions with tennis balls on the bottoms to make sure they don't scrape the floor. Everything may be flowers and happiness now, but your future is in a walker.
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>flowers</radical> on your <radical>right</radical> to show that you're still <kanji>young</kanji>. Your body still works, you're happy, you have your entire life ahead of you! And everything smells like flowers.

MeaningHint:
"Is it an adjective?"
"No, that's the wrong part of speech."
"Is it a verb?"
"N-no! Of course not!"
ReadingHint: You stare blankly at him. What part of speech is that?! It's an animal, you know that. Oh! A noun. Duh.
ReadingMnemonic: The director asks you again, "What is the part of speech for the word <reading>shee</reading>p (し)?"
MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> to the <radical>director</radical> is judged immediately. You're having trouble understanding this <kanji>part of speech</kanji>, so he's trying to help you. But you say it over and over and he keeps saying you're wrong.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding up an insect to the sun to compare the two. See how they both shine, but the sun shines more brightly than the insect.
ReadingHint: Feel how dark and cold it is suddenly with all those condors blocking out the sun.
ReadingMnemonic: While you’re holding up an <kanji>insect</kanji> to the sun for comparison, a flock of <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) flies by, blocking out the sun. There are so many condors that they manage to totally block out the sun.
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>sun</radical> is <radical>compare</radical>d to an <kanji>insect</kanji>, you’ll see that they're both shiny, but that the sun is much shinier than the insect.

MeaningHint: Imagine this washed up drunkard leader passed out in front of the gate to your house. You have to pass him whenever you enter or exit your house. He's gross and he smells. Experience the stench in your nostrils. How he was ever the leader of a clan you have no idea.
ReadingHint: Hear the crushing blows of bats against flesh as you imagine this scene in your mind. You're beating the crap out of this guy with a bunch of bats, but hey — he had it coming.
ReadingMnemonic: Eventually you get sick and tired of this washed up <kanji>clan</kanji> leader and you gather your own <kanji>clique</kanji> to chase this guy away from your gate and your property. You and your friends grab some <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ) and use these bats to beat the drunkard leader. Being beaten with bats frightens him, and he scurries away.
MeaningMnemonic: Passed out in front of your <radical>gate</radical> lies a former <radical>leader</radical>. He's now a <radical>drunkard</radical>, but once ran a popular <kanji>clique</kanji> called the Cool Kids <kanji>Clan</kanji>. The clan has fallen apart, and he no longer has a clique to call his own.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself on this cliff. You desire a continuation for your journey, not wanting to stop here. Feel the strong wind on your body, blowing your hair about as you stand on the edge of the cliff. The leaves on the trees rustle in the breeze.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself using the wrecking ball to knock down the two trees on the cliff. Hear the the trees crack and splinter from the force of the wrecking ball. That wrecking ball is the sound of continuation.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Continuation</kanji> is impossible unless you chop these trees down to make a bridge to the other side. You decide to use a <reading>wrecki</reading>ng (れき) ball. Luckily, there's one right by you. So you take the wrecking ball and knock the trees over, making a bridge with them.
MeaningMnemonic: At the edge of the <radical>cliff</radical>, two <radical>tree</radical>s mark the spot where you must <radical>stop</radical>, since any <kanji>continuation</kanji> requires a way across. The trees seem long enough to reach to the other side, but how are you going to chop them down?

ReadingHint: Your significant other had been watching you sleep with your tongue out for a while now, so you're super embarrassed you wet the sheets with your nasty tongue drool. You feel very judged and embarrassed.
ReadingMnemonic: You’re sound asleep in bed when your <kanji>tongue</kanji> drops out of your mouth and you start to drool, getting saliva all over the bed <reading>sheet</reading> (した). You wake up with a soggy patch of sheet near your mouth and wipe the drool off your face. Your significant other sees the whole thing.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tongue</radical> radical and the <kanji>tongue</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Yee-haw!

MeaningHint: Imagine counting books on a bunch of bookshelves. One book, two book, red book, blue book!
ReadingHint: Imagine how tedious it'd be to watch her counting one by one. Teach her your book counter ways!
ReadingMnemonic: You use this <kanji>book counter</kanji> method to count all the books <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ) has in her house. Remember her? She's got, like, a ton of books, but she's just a little girl so she needs some help. That's where your book counter know-how comes in handy.
MeaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>bookshelf</radical> as a <kanji>book counter</kanji>. Duh. What, are you gonna count them one by one? Okay, well maybe, but you should count them while they're nice and organized on the shelves. Otherwise counting is going to be a huge pain.

MeaningHint: You don't want to float up into outer space and get all wet, do you? No? Then you better make sure you have a roof above your head.
ReadingHint: Look closely up at outer space and you'll see all of the uni floating around up there. They're really dark, so it can be hard to notice them, but I promise you, they're there.
ReadingMnemonic: Why isn't it dry in <kanji>outer space</kanji>, you ask? Because of all the <reading>u</reading>ni (う) swimming around up there. They have nasty, wet, pointy spikes and they're all over the place.
MeaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>roof</radical> to keep yourself <radical>dry</radical> and safe from <kanji>outer space</kanji>. Look outside, above your roof, and you'll see outer space stretching above you.

MeaningHint: At the moment you can't remember the reason you did it, but when you jumped off your roof you just floated in midair.
ReadingHint: Oh Chewbacca, you lovable fuzzball. You better not drop me in midair, or I'll die. I hope you bring me up there!
ReadingMnemonic: As you spin a bit in <kanji>midair</kanji> you look up and see <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう) looking down at you from a spaceship. He's laughing his Chewbacca laugh and you realize he has you trapped in his tractor beam.
MeaningMnemonic: You jumped off your <radical>roof</radical> for some <radical>reason</radical> and now you're floating in <kanji>midair</kanji>! You kick your legs a bit and move your arms like you're in water, but you're still just stuck there in midair.

MeaningHint: Look around you. See all those busy people? Their souls are dying. Don't end up like them.
ReadingHint: Think about your busy schedule this week. Now cancel everything and just go bowling instead.
ReadingMnemonic: Take time out of your <kanji>busy</kanji> life and go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) once in a while. In fact, you should probably go bowling once a week at least. Nothing brings energy back to your life like some relaxing bowling.
MeaningMnemonic: You'll work your <radical>soul</radical> to <radical>death</radical> if you're too <kanji>busy</kanji>. Being busy just isn't good for your soul. Take breaks. Go on vacation. Don't be so busy that you destroy yourself.

MeaningHint: If you look into the black hole, you can see your footwear right there, marked with the flag.
ReadingHint: It's amazing that these cute little reeds could grow in your footwear in the middle of a black hole. As a great philosopher once said, life finds a way.
ReadingMnemonic: You grab your <kanji>footwear</kanji> from the black hole. In your footwear (thanks to the shelter they provided), some <reading>ree</reading>ds (り) have grown in!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>flag</radical> <radical>loiter</radical>s in a <radical>black hole</radical>. This flag marks (in the black hole) where you left your <kanji>footwear</kanji>.

MeaningHint: I hope this group is full of people who enjoy being inside someone else's mouth, because they're gonna be pretty mad when you let them out if they aren't.
ReadingHint: Imagine letting this group of, now very wet, people out of your mouth one by one. They don't seem to care that they weigh a ton! That's a large measurement, they should be proud.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>group</kanji> is so big it takes you till <reading>dawn</reading> (だん) to get your measurement: they weigh a <reading>ton</reading> (とん) too!
MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>mouth</radical> to take a <radical>measurement</radical> of this <kanji>group</kanji>. Just stretch your mouth out and around every single person. Get 'em all in there.

MeaningHint: Picture this violent fight scene in your brain. Imagine water locked in battle with the sun as the sun’s fire flares up and the water starts to sizzle.
ReadingHint: Imagine the sun and the water trying to bowl. Hear the sound of bowling balls rolling down the lane and pins rattling around and crashing together.
ReadingMnemonic: But after a while, the sun and the water have had enough of <kanji>violence</kanji>. They decide to settle their differences with a friendly game of <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). Bowling is a great way to relieve stress and in the end, they bond over it.
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> gets <radical>together</radical> with <radical>water</radical>, only <kanji>violence</kanji> can ensue. They don’t mix well together. The sun loves to fight water and water loves to fight the sun. Lots and lots of violence.

MeaningHint: Imagine the sun getting blocked out as a tsunami rises up from the ocean. Feel the cold fear of death on you as the tsunami approaches. That tsunami is gonna mix you up.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself getting mixed up in this situation and the pterodactyl-sized condor picking you up. Feel the condor's talons digging into your shoulders as it grabs you up and flies you towards the sun.
ReadingMnemonic: Just before the tsunami crashes over you, a <reading>con</reading>dor (こん) swoops out of the sky and grabs you up. This condor must be a <kanji>mix</kanji> — a normal condor is not strong enough to pick up a human. The condor must be mixed with something else. Like a pterodactyl.
MeaningMnemonic: A raging <radical>tsunami</radical> rises next to the calm <radical>sun</radical>. When you try to <radical>compare</radical> these two things, it's clear that they cannot <kanji>mix</kanji>. If a tsunami is coming, it will likely get in the way of the sun.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself getting stabbed in the tongue with an umbrella. It’s very painful. Taste the blood and metal in your mouth as this happens to you.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself squaring off with Macho Man Randy Savage in the middle of this riot. You have the advantage of having an umbrella, but Randy has the advantage of being himself. Picture yourself getting into a fight with him.
ReadingMnemonic: Just now you realize that the man you stabbed in the tongue with your umbrella was none other than Macho Man <reading>Ran</reading>dy Savage (らん). Being a professional wrestler, Macho Man Randy Savage is ready to throw down at a moment’s notice. A <kanji>riot</kanji> immediately erupts. It’s a battle royal with you and Randy at the epicenter.
MeaningMnemonic: If you stab somebody in the <radical>tongue</radical> with an <radical>umbrella</radical>, it’s sure to start a <kanji>riot</kanji> and cause <kanji>disorder</kanji>. Nobody likes to get stabbed in the tongue with an umbrella, so they’re probably going to retaliate, and before you know it, you’ve a full scale riot on your hands. Look what you’ve done.

MeaningHint: Imagine Sean Connery saying “Don’t loiter – run, Junior!” and you should have a much easier time remembering this phrase if you remember it in his voice.
ReadingHint: Imagine that you are Indiana Jones Junior and this is happening to you. Feel the terrible pain of your throbbing toe as you clutch it, with the bad guys catching up with you.
ReadingMnemonic: Deciding not to loiter any longer, Indiana Jones <kanji>Junior</kanji> takes his dad’s advice and starts to run. Unfortunately he wasn’t paying attention to where his feet were and he stubbed his <reading>to</reading>e (と) on a jagged rock. His toe is in so much pain that he can’t bear to run any longer.
MeaningMnemonic: “Don’t <radical>loiter</radical> – <radical>run</radical>, <kanji>Junior</kanji>!” said Indiana Jones Senior to Indiana Jones Junior. They’re escaping from a sticky situation and Junior was just loitering around like a fool. It’s no time to loiter, it’s time to run!

MeaningHint: Imagine loitering around near the sun temple. Maybe you acquire some illegal goods, maybe you just acquire a tan (it is a SUN temple, after all).
ReadingHint: Your eyes fill with tears under Tokugawa's gaze and you drop everything you acquired to the ground. You bow before him apologizing. You'll never do this again!
ReadingMnemonic: You managed to <kanji>acquire</kanji> some tobacco, but just as you're about to leave <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu comes marching up to you!! Or at least, his ghost does. He stares you dead in the eyes and says, "I didn't found the shogunate for you to acquire illegal goods and kill your body toking on my soil."
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around the <radical>sun</radical> <radical>temple</radical>, it's likely you'll <kanji>acquire</kanji> something. When you loiter around like that it looks like you're waiting for something, so if someone puts something in your hands and you just happen to acquire it, well, no one can fault you for that.

MeaningHint: Imagine sitting in your house all winter, meditating and thinking about how you can renew yourself.
ReadingHint: Imagine being back in your house in winter. You're thinking about all the kayaking you're gonna do when winter ends. You're even making the movements with your arms, so you'll be a really renewed you.
ReadingMnemonic: Winter ends and, <kanji>renew</kanji>ed, you come out ready to start a new hobby: <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい)! Nothing says renewal like a brand new kayak and sense of purpose in life.
MeaningMnemonic: It's important to focus <radical>oneself</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> so that one can emerge <kanji>renew</kanji>ed! You can't really go outside in winter without getting cold or sick, so spend winter focusing on YOU.

MeaningHint: Imagine selling thread so you can continue living. You search for thread continually now. But are you gathering thread to continue to live, or living to continue gathering thread?
ReadingHint: Continue selling thread and paying off the Bosozoku. It's the only way you can continue to survive!
ReadingMnemonic: You have to <kanji>continue</kanji> to sell that thread because a Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang is forcing you to do it. You see? If you want to continue to live, you have to appease this Bosozoku gang. You better keep selling!
MeaningMnemonic: You have to gather <radical>thread</radical> to <radical>sell</radical> if you want to <kanji>continue</kanji> living. You want to make a living, don't you? Make sure you continue to sell that thread every single day!

MeaningHint: Your scooter and your car, connected by a tiny little string, are the two things you take along with you wherever you go.
ReadingHint: The roof over your head? Rental. The clothes on your body? Rental. All of your friends? Rental. The only thing you permanently take along with you are your scooter and your car.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>take along</kanji> your scooter and your car. Anything else, you can do a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん) for!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>scooter</radical> and your <radical>car</radical> is what you always <kanji>take along</kanji> with you. Make sure they're <kanji>connect</kanji>ed well so you don't lose them!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as the good person being approached by this forlorn sheep without horns. Hear the clack of the horns together as the sheep drops them out of his mouth onto the ground and lets out a pathetic bleat. “Baa~aa~ah~”
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself using your Zen magic to heal this sheep. Place your hands on the sheep’s horns and imagine returning them to their rightful location on the sheep’s head. Feel the soft wool of the sheep as he bleats in happiness.
ReadingMnemonic: Since you are <kanji>morally good</kanji>, you decide not to murder the sheep and harvest its organs. Instead, as a practitioner of the <kanji>good</kanji> teachings of <reading>Zen</reading> (ぜん) Buddhism, you decided to help the sheep out. Your Zen teachings have increased the power of your healing abilities, so you fix the sheep up in no time.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>sheep</radical> loses its <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> so it picks them up in its <radical>mouth</radical> to take them to someone who's <kanji>morally good</kanji> enough to attach them back onto its head. Sheep don’t have hands, so the sheep had to pick the horns up off the ground with his mouth. Someone evil would just murder the sheep and steal his horns, so the sheep must find someone <kanji>good</kanji> to reaffix them.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to kiss someone with a piece of thread between your lips. Feel the thread getting all entangled between the two of you. Thread entangled, tongues entangled, lips entangled — just a thready kiss of entanglement.
ReadingHint: Imagine pausing this kiss to pull a thread out of your mouth and entangle it around a random rack in your room. Smooth move, Casanova! Your partner will totally love that.
ReadingMnemonic: This isn't going well, so you pull out the weird kiss thread and <kanji>entangle</kanji> it around a <reading>rack</reading> (らく). Good thing that rack was there! Much better to have it entangled around a rack when you're trying to kiss someone.
MeaningMnemonic: If you put a <radical>thread</radical> in the middle of a <radical>kiss</radical>, you’re going to <kanji>entangle</kanji> it. Things can get pretty messy in a kiss, so adding a thread to the mix will definitely entangle it.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself cutting down some bamboo, thinking, "This is the reason I'm here, I need a flute, ASAP!" There is no other reason to be here besides this.
ReadingHint: You've done what you came to do. You summoned an alien craft with your bamboo flute, gaining some alien tech in the process. Open up the box to see what tech awaits you.
ReadingMnemonic: You cut down the bamboo and whittle a <kanji>flute</kanji>. You start playing a song. You know the reason why. An alien spacecraft comes flying down and drops a box full of alien <reading>tech</reading> (てき) in front of you.
MeaningMnemonic: Within the <radical>bamboo</radical> grove, someone asks your <radical>reason</radical> for being there, and you explain it's to harvest bamboo to craft a <kanji>flute</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Look at a history book, behind each of those humans was a sheep, changing the world as we know it.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>History</kanji> was full of famous <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Sure there are people and stuff, but historical sheep are where it's at. There's the famous sheep samurai, the death sheep who works for the yakuza, just tons!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>history</radical> radical and the <kanji>history</kanji> kanji are the same.

MeaningHint: Potatoes grow when flowers dry out. All the nutrients are going to the potato under the ground, so the flowers up top get sucked dry.
ReadingHint: Picture an emo person sitting alone in a dark room, staring at a potato. "You and I are the same."
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Potato</kanji>es are totally <reading>emo</reading> (いも) because of the way they dry out flowers. Emo people and potatoes both like to dry out flowers.
MeaningMnemonic: If you see some <radical>flowers</radical> that are <radical>dry</radical>, pull them up and you'll find a <kanji>potato</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the village where you grew up. Can you see the dirt area where all your ancestors were buried?
ReadingHint: Imagine crying as you bury your pet uni. Your tears can't save it now. (Though it is a bit ironic, since it needed salt water.)
ReadingMnemonic: You don't just <kanji>bury</kanji> dead relatives here. You also buried your dead pet <reading>u</reading>ni (う) in that dirt. You thought having an uni as a pet would be a good idea. Too bad uni need water to survive.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dirt</radical> next to your <radical>village</radical> is where you <kanji>bury</kanji> the dead. People don't live forever, so every village has a patch of dirt specifically used for burying dead things.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as this distressed and troubled person. Taste the taste of sap and bark in your mouth as you nom on the tree with all your might.
ReadingHint: Picture a very distressed condor watching you munch your mouth all over its tree house. You've made a condor enemy this day.
ReadingMnemonic: You thought you were the most <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> person in this situation, but then you look up and see a very equally distressed and troubled <reading>con</reading>dor (こん). The tree you've been mouthing is its home. And now you've slobbered all over it, ruining this condor's day.
MeaningMnemonic: If you put your <radical>mouth</radical> on a <radical>tree</radical> and try to eat it, you’re probably a <kanji>distressed</kanji> and <kanji>troubled</kanji> individual (unless you’re a woodchuck). You don’t need to eat this tree, you have plenty of food at home. Get your mouth off that tree you distressed child.

MeaningHint: Imagine a disaster so bad that there are floods AND fires. That's too much!
ReadingHint: You better figure out a way to stop your cyborg from causing more disasters like this. Fix it now, or you're gonna be in big trouble.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>disaster</kanji> was caused by your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)! It's malfunctioning, running around leaking large amounts of water and breathing fire. I guess you didn't work out all the kinks before you let it roam wild.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>flood</radical> and a <radical>fire</radical>! At the same time? This is a <kanji>disaster</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine this drunkard, squatting down under the tree to poop. You can smell the poop and a faint stench of alcohol in the air. How could he not have realized that there was a machine toilet right there?
ReadingHint: Listen to the drunkard grumbling about the lack of a key as he poops under the tree. He doesn't sound happy, and every time he stresses to push out the poop, his voice fluctuates. Imagine hearing this in your mind and being a wee bit disgusted.
ReadingMnemonic: Oh, I see — the door to the outhouse with the <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet is locked, and the drunkard doesn't have the <reading>key</reading> (き). If he had a machine like a jackhammer, or a circular saw, or some other heavy duty machine, he might be able to get in without a key, but since he has none of those or a key, he's reduced to pooping under the tree instead.
MeaningMnemonic: Next to a <radical>tree</radical>, you spot <radical>poop</radical>, not once but twice. Only a <radical>drunkard</radical> would be the type of <radical>person</radical> to not use the nearby <kanji>machine</kanji> toilet. There's a perfectly good outhouse with a machine toilet right there, but this drunkard is so drunk that he decides to poop under the tree instead. And not only does he poop once, he poops twice.

MeaningHint: You take a peek under the lid to check, and your poop explosion is one hundred percent gone! Phew!
ReadingHint: Look around you at the classy surroundings of the Ritz Hotel bathroom. You feel one hundred percent relieved now, and ready to enjoy your date at the Ritz!
ReadingMnemonic: It was extra important that there was zero <kanji>percent</kanji> poop left, because you're not on a date just anywhere, you're at the <reading>Ritz</reading> (りつ)!
MeaningMnemonic: Sitting on the <radical>lid</radical> of a toilet, you're praying your <radical>poop</radical> <radical>explosion</radical> will be flushed away, beseeching the <radical>cross</radical> decorating the bathroom wall. The poop needs to be one hundred <kanji>percent</kanji> flushed away because you're on a date, and you don't want even one percent of your explosion left for them to see.

ReadingHint: You’re flying through the air super fast. Feel the air rushing past you as heat builds up all over your body from rocketing through the air so fast.
ReadingMnemonic: When you <kanji>fly</kanji>, you generate a lot of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) because you’re moving so fast through the atmosphere. Normally this heat would be a nuisance, but you’re a superhero so it doesn’t bother you. This is actually the reason you’re able to fly – you’re a superhero and super resistant to heat.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>fly</radical> radical and the <kanji>fly</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Awesome!

MeaningHint: Imagine that moment as you fall off the roof. In slow motion you can see the roof moving away, you see yourself as a little kid, a teen, an adult, then BAM you've injured your mouth. Ow ow ow!
ReadingHint: No matter how much you try to wave your hands and gesture, they keep calling you Guy. It's really annoying, actually, because you aren't a guy and you aren't Guy either. But your mouth is too damaged for you to tell him.
ReadingMnemonic: As you try to assess the <kanji>damage</kanji>, you hear someone shout, "<reading>Guy</reading> (がい), hey, you okay, guy?" They run up to you, but your mouth is too <kanji>damage</kanji>d, you can't respond. "Okay guy, I'm gonna call you Guy now. Let's get you to the hospital."
MeaningMnemonic: You slip off the <radical>roof</radical>, your <radical>life</radical> flashes before your eyes, and you smash your <radical>mouth</radical> on the ground causing some serious <kanji>damage</kanji>. Oof, this is a really bad <kanji>injury</kanji>, you fell all the way off the roof and onto your mouth.

MeaningHint: The hat you’re wearing is ridiculous, giving you a surplus of bad style. Imagine a bunch of people scowling at you, not only because of your crazy hat, but because of the annoying sound of the jackhammer. Hear the obnoxious noise around you as the jackhammer rattles your bones.
ReadingHint: Your hat is doing little to defend you from the onslaught of yo-yos being tossed at you. Feel the yo-yos hitting your body and listen to them roll away after striking you.
ReadingMnemonic: One of the people walking by is the owner of a push cart <reading>yo</reading>-yo (よ) stand. Having a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of yo-yos, he starts throwing them at you. This makes you stop using the jackhammer as you need both hands to fend off the yo-yo barrage.
MeaningMnemonic: Regardless of whether or not you wear a <radical>hat</radical> while you <radical>jackhammer</radical>, you’re going to have a <kanji>surplus</kanji> of noise. Jackhammers are loud, and your hat is doing nothing to dampen the racket. There’s a surplus of commotion coming from the jackhammer and onlookers are not amused.

MeaningHint: Try and picture a Chinese turkey in your brain. Now imagine yourself trying to catch it, but you just can’t. It’s too difficult. The Chinese turkey is too elusive.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself entering a bakery, frantically scouring through all the Naan in an attempt to find one that’s actually a Chinese turkey. "WHERE ARE THE TURKEYS?", you scream, to everyone else’s bewilderment. Those elusive, difficult to find Chinese Naan turkeys…
ReadingMnemonic: Do you know the actual reason Chinese turkey are so <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find? It’s because they’re all made out of <reading>Naan</reading> (なん). Of course they’re elusive when they blend in so well with all the other Naan!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Chinese</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>difficult</kanji> to find because they are so rare. Chinese turkeys are the rarest variety of turkey, so naturally, finding one is exceedingly difficult.

MeaningHint: Imagine the most uncomfortable outfit you can. Your body is incurring some minor damage right now, but hey — at least you're covering your skin properly.
ReadingHint: Even if you incur some damage from uncomfortable clothing, it's better to cover up and retain heat than to underdress and incur damage from frostbite or something.
ReadingMnemonic: Another reason to <kanji>cover</kanji> yourself is to retain <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Actually, retaining heat and staying warm is probably the reason people started covering themselves up in the first place.
