MeaningMnemonic: That <radical>outfit</radical> on your <radical>skin</radical> is there to <kanji>cover</kanji> you up. In polite society, it's expected that you cover your skin with some kind of outfit — even if you have to wear something that causes you to <kanji>incur</kanji> damage, like circulation-cutting skinny jeans or a shirt with a really scratchy tag.

MeaningHint: See the green mountains rising on either side of the valley, hear the birdsong in the distance… and behold the miles of clothes racks that hold all your outfits. This valley is the only space big enough to contain your material abundance and wealth.
ReadingHint: Picture these wealthy youths as they peruse your valley of outfits. They're mostly influencers making short-form video content for their socials, basically leeching off your abundance. Ugh. Is this how people get wealthy in the 21st century?
ReadingMnemonic: This valley is also <kanji>abundant</kanji> with <kanji>wealthy</kanji> <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) — trendy, wealthy youths who come from around the world to descend on your valley and peep your outfit collection.
MeaningMnemonic: You have an <radical>outfit</radical> <radical>valley</radical>, which is a valley where you store your <kanji>abundant</kanji> number of outfits. Yes, you're so <kanji>wealthy</kanji> that not only can you purchase a valley for yourself, you can also afford to use it like a walk-in closet.

MeaningHint: Picture this happening in real time. First it starts to rain really hard, then the ground starts to shift and run, then everything is shaking and quaking so violently it's an earthquake!
ReadingHint: You grab your shin, but the earthquake shakes the coffee table toward you to hit your shin yet again! Ow ow ow stop!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>earthquake</kanji> shakes you so violently that you fall over, banging your <reading>shin</reading> (しん) on the coffee table. The edge of the coffee table is very sharp, so it cuts into your shin pretty badly.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> causes a <radical>landslide</radical> that causes an <kanji>earthquake</kanji>!!! The earth doesn't just slide, it <kanji>shake</kanji>s and <kanji>quake</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: What kind of animal could it be? You start to feel the soft fur with your hand and then the stench of death hits your nostrils. Ew.
ReadingHint: See the bees swarming out from around the tail and toward your face. Run! Run before there’s nothing left of you but your own tail!
ReadingMnemonic: You try to pull the flag out of the ground and hear a rumbling buzzing sound. At the end of the flagpole you see a huge <reading>bee</reading>hive (び)! These bees made their home under the <kanji>tail</kanji>! Maybe that’s why the flag was sticking in it… to warn people. Oops.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>flag</radical> stuck in the ground with a little bit of <radical>fur</radical> coming out from it and on closer inspection you realize it’s actually a <kanji>tail</kanji>. Someone stuck a flag into some sort of animal, pushing it under the ground. All that’s visible now is some fur and part of the tail.

MeaningHint: Can you even imagine twenty completely unique contraptions? Why don't you try, just for fun.
ReadingHint: "Kayak contraptions" is pretty vague, but you do have a kayak, so you're tempted anyway. If only you could figure out what each contraption did so you could justify buying some.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to know what these <kanji>contraptions</kanji> are for, so you ask. The drunkard says, "These are <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい) contraptions *urp* they're for your uh *blarp* kayak... things."
MeaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>tree</radical> a <radical>drunkard</radical> sits with <radical>twenty</radical> little <kanji>contraptions</kanji>. There's little electronic ones, little bopping ones, little walking ones... twenty of them.

MeaningHint: Try hitting a sponge on a turkey's forehead. Not certain. Now a stone. <kanji>Certain</kanji>.
ReadingHint: As you smash the stone into the turkey's forehead, yell out "This is for you, my cock! THIS IS FOR YOU!" Your cock lover watches from the shadows.
ReadingMnemonic: You're killing this turkey, and you need to make sure he's dead for <kanji>certain</kanji>. The reason you need to make sure this turkey is dead is because of the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) in your life. Male chickens get really jealous when turkeys are around.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>stone</radical> to the <radical>forehead</radical> of a <radical>turkey</radical>. It is <kanji>certain</kanji> that you will damage it.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself feeling this strong dislike towards the Mona Lisa. She gets all the attention and you get none. It’s not fair and you’re upset.
ReadingHint: Ken walks slowly toward the Mona Lisa, dislike shines in his eyes, but then! He falls for her too. No! Ken, destroy her!
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>dislike</kanji> the Mona Lisa so much you hire <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai to cut her up.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>woman</radical> sees the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>, she feels a strong feeling of <kanji>dislike</kanji>. All women dislike the Mona Lisa because of how much attention she gets.

MeaningHint: Imagine that leader's old mouth saying things that make them stand apart from the others. They're individual from everyone else.
ReadingHint: "You must be your own individual. Don't be sheeple," he says to the 子s.
ReadingMnemonic: As an <kanji>individual</kanji>, they must teach all the <reading>子</reading>s (こ) their way. Their individual way.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> has grown <radical>old</radical> from saying so many things. This is what makes them different from everyone else, they speak truly like an <kanji>individual</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine being down there under that heavy cliff. Imagine feeling the cliff's pressure as you watch it compress the dirt. It's so hard to breathe... h... help!
ReadingHint: Imagine yelling out to the dirt to encourage it: "Ah, two cliffs is nothing! Come on, you got this!" You're the dirt's gym buddy, and you won't let it buckle under the pressure.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to increase the <kanji>pressure</kanji>, so you stack on a second cliff. "<reading>Ah, two</reading> (あつ)?!" the dirt cries out in dismay. This dirt is tough, but it's really starting to feel the pressure now.
MeaningMnemonic: Under any <radical>cliff</radical>, there's some <radical>dirt</radical> that's under a lot of <kanji>pressure</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing a narwhal sticking a stick, and maybe even its horn too, into the dirt on land. It needs to know if life exists outside of the ocean for sure.
ReadingHint: Hear the plinky plink of the xylophone and think about what exists in this world. It's a sad song, you realize.
ReadingMnemonic: The narwhal can't find anything but it can't stop thinking about what could <kanji>exist</kanji> outside of its world. It pulls out a <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい) and starts tapping the keys with its horn. It plays a sad song about the meaning of life and whether existence is worth it.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>narwhal</radical> sticks a <radical>stick</radical> into the <radical>dirt</radical> to see what might <kanji>exist</kanji> underneath it. Narwhals live in the sea, you see. So they're convinced nothing can exist anywhere else.

MeaningHint: Watch yourself sleeping. Then, a net of flowers appear on your forehead as you start to dream.
ReadingHint: While still dreaming, you say "moo" out loud too. "Moooo!"
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>dream</kanji> about cows saying "<reading>moo</reading> (む), moo, moo!"
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>flowers</radical> <radical>net</radical> is upon your <radical>forehead</radical> in the <radical>evening</radical>. These appear on your head at night whenever you are having a <kanji>dream</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The baby is falling off the cliff! Imagine the sheer terror you feel as the baby plummets towards the ground below.
ReadingHint: Oh Santa-san. You're always here for me when I need you most. You can keep all my property and give it away as presents.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>give birth</kanji>, and now this happens. Such bad luck. But wait! What's that sound? Sleigh bells? Could it be?! It's <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん)! He caught your baby! And all your <kanji>property</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>stand</radical> on the <radical>Cliff</radical> of <radical>Life</radical> and <kanji>give birth</kanji> to a child. Oh no! You’re standing too close to the edge of the Cliff of Life! The baby falls off the cliff right after you give birth to it! In your despair, you throw all your <kanji>property</kanji> off the cliff too.

MeaningHint: Imagine a mole sneaking up behind the leader mole and stabbing him with a knife. This type of overthrowing of leadership feels familiar to you. Maybe this has happened before in the past?
ReadingHint: Picture this like an exaggerated reenactment on the History channel. Leader after leader of とうきょう gets overthrown and replaced with the next guy. It never stops. (Until the end of the Edo period, I guess.)
ReadingMnemonic: Where do leaders get <kanji>overthrow</kanji>n the most? In <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Just think back in Japanese history, back when everyone was at war with one another. People were taking over and getting overthrown and then that guy gets overthrown and then THAT guy gets overthrown. Basically, it used to be hard to stay a leader in とうきょう.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone stabbed the <radical>leader</radical> <radical>mole</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> to <kanji>overthrow</kanji> it. Now there is a new mole leader. Because that's what happens when you overthrow someone—someone else needs to take over. In this case, a vengeful mole.

MeaningHint: Seriously. If you or someone else is stinking to high heaven, embrace a big bar of self care.
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to use a big bar of soap on yourself to get the stinky 草 off. But it's not working... the 草 stink is SO STRONG!
ReadingMnemonic: You're <kanji>stinking</kanji> up the room because you spent your day rolling in <reading>草</reading> (くさ). And based on this particular stench, I'd say an animal left something in that 草 too...
MeaningMnemonic: Wash your<radical>self</radical> with a <radical>big</radical> bar of soap if you're <kanji>stinking</kanji> everything up.

MeaningHint: I mean, this unibrow is really, really thick. So thick it doesn’t even look real. But it is. It’s really real and really thick.
ReadingHint: Imagine the sun noticing its mistake and apologizing to the child. "Ah, two thick unibrows wasn't what you wanted? Wow, I'm sorry. This is embarrassing… I'm actually not sure I can remove the second one now that it's on there."
ReadingMnemonic: The child glances up at their <kanji>thick</kanji> unibrow and yells, "<reading>ah, two</reading> (あつ)!" Yup, it turns out the sun misunderstood the child's pleas for help. Instead of pulling them up from the cliff, it gave them a second thick unibrow on top of the first one.
MeaningMnemonic: Hanging off the edge of a <radical>cliff</radical> is a <radical>sun</radical>-worshipping <radical>child</radical> with a <kanji>thick</kanji> unibrow. Hanging off the cliff, the child cries out to the sun to save them from this cruel fate. Even the thickness of their unibrow is not enough to give the child the strength to climb back up on their own.

MeaningHint: Oh, how you miss your Wolverine woman and her long, sharp claws… Now all you've got left to remember her by is this cross. Your poor, poor wife.
ReadingHint: All your wife needs is a little cyborg tune-up. Bionic brain here, some new claws there. Yep, your cy-wife's as good as new.
ReadingMnemonic: Wait a minute… She can't be dead because your <kanji>wife</kanji> is a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)! How could you forget?!
MeaningMnemonic: You place a <radical>cross</radical> on a <radical>Wolverine</radical> <radical>woman</radical>. She's gone now, but she was once your <kanji>wife</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself in a deliberation with some other people. They think what they're saying is right, and it drives you mad. Then you realize that you're doing the same thing.
ReadingHint: Imagine how the deliberation concludes. Then, at the end, someone says "This deliberation is over! We have decided to _________" (in regards to the guillotine).
ReadingMnemonic: What are you in <kanji>deliberation</kanji> about? Whether or not to send some poor soul to the <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). "I think he should go," you say. "I don't think he should," says another. This <kanji>deliberation</kanji> is going nowhere...
MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> is <radical>righteousness</radical>, at least that's what you believe. You keep talking and saying these things, and you don't listen to anyone else's words. This is your style of <kanji>deliberation</kanji> and <kanji>discussion</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a snake as a hardened criminal. See this snake with many prison tattoos and muscles from working out in his jail cell. Hear the snake hissing as it slithers about to commit yet another crime.
ReadingHint: Now see Han Solo doing battle with the snake (cue Star Wars music). The snake hisses and lunges at Han, but Han shoots the snake and it’s incapacitated. Smell the smell of scorched snake flesh in your nose.
ReadingMnemonic: This snake has evaded the law for too long. It’s time for a true hero to put an end to its <kanji>crime</kanji> spree. Enter <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo. Han Solo is the only man strong and brave enough to put an end to this snake and his crime filled ways.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>animal</radical> kingdom, a <radical>snake</radical> is the most likely to commit a <kanji>crime</kanji>. Remember the Garden of Eden? The snake was the bad guy. Ever since then, snakes are the animal most known for committing crimes.

MeaningHint: Imagine throwing a net around someone you think, no, you know is guilty of doing something bad to you. It feels pretty nice.
ReadingHint: Woah! The xylophone is huge, how did they... well whatever, you definitely have proof that they're guilty now and the cops should be here any minute to take your criminal catch.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>guilt</kanji> shows on the criminal's face. They smile, shrug, and pull a stolen <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい) out of their back pocket.
MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>net</radical> around a <radical>criminal</radical> to show their <kanji>guilt</kanji>. Criminals look like anyone else on the street, so you decide to tie them in nets to make sure people know who they're dealing with.

MeaningHint: There's a building right there. Try to go that direction. Oh, did the building prevent you? Told you so.
ReadingHint: Your bowling antics are now preventing a bunch of people from going the directions they want to go. Nice job.
ReadingMnemonic: The building is <kanji>prevent</kanji>ing you from going the right direction, so you pull a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball out of your bag, aim, and bowl the building over. The building, and all the ones around it, go tumbling down. But now the rubble is preventing you from going further.
MeaningMnemonic: If there is a <radical>building</radical> in the <radical>direction</radical> you want to go it will <kanji>prevent</kanji> you from going any further. Like, come on kid, you think you can just walk right through a building? Don't make me laugh!

MeaningHint: This theory you've thought of is great, it's going to change everything!
ReadingHint: "Ron? Ron, what's your theory? What's your theory, Ron?" Hermione asks over and over. But you freeze. You don't remember who you are. "Hermione, I have a theory that I was just a person learning Japanese, and not a magical boy at all." "Shut up Ron, that's a stupid theory," she replies.
ReadingMnemonic: "What is your theory, <reading>Ron</reading> (ろん)?" asks Hermione. You're Ron Weasley, and you just came up with a theory that you think will help you stop Voldemort.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical>, throwing your <radical>hat</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> in front of the <radical>bookshelf</radical>, "I have a <kanji>theory</kanji>!" All this reading and thinking has finally given you a wonderful idea, a theory you think might solve all of your problems!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself coming back to the same tombstone each passing year and tying a new thread to it. You're trying to manage these feelings by keeping track of how much time passes.
ReadingHint: Maybe this is your future tombstone, and eating cake each year is a preemptive celebration of your death day! Ooh, what a spooky theory.
ReadingMnemonic: Dealing with the <kanji>passage of time</kanji> can be tough, so after you tie the thread to the tombstone, each year you eat a piece of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). You already eat cake to mark the passage of time on your birthday, so why not here as well?
MeaningMnemonic: Tying a <radical>thread</radical> to a <radical>tombstone</radical> each year will help you <kanji>manage</kanji> the <kanji>passage of time</kanji>. That way you’ll know how many years you <kanji>pass through</kanji>, since you’ll have one thread for each year to count.

MeaningHint: Imagine the pain and confusion of being stabbed by a violent tree. You hit your head on the tree again, as if to test it, and again a knife falls out and stabs you. Oh, so it's a stabbing tree, is it? That does it.
ReadingHint: Feel the grain of the saw's wooden handle in your bare hand. As the saw bites into tree bark, a retaliatory rain of knives comes stabbing down around you. But you don't even flinch. You will saw this stabbing tree down.
ReadingMnemonic: There's nothing more dangerous than a tree that likes to <kanji>stab</kanji>, so you get your <reading>saw</reading> (さ) and start to saw it down. Trees that stab get sawed.
MeaningMnemonic: You walk into this <radical>tree</radical> and hit your <radical>head</radical> on it, then a <radical>knife</radical> falls out of it to <kanji>stab</kanji> you. Ow! What the heck?! What was a knife doing in that tree?

MeaningHint: You do the bridge pose, and in your mind you imagine your brush painting an actual bridge. When you open your eyes, there it is! A bridge has been built, leading straight to your front door.
ReadingHint: It seems like Ken the samurai really appreciates your building skills.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>build</kanji> a bridge. Immediately after, someone is climbing up onto it. It's <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. He waves at you and says, "Did you build this? The craftsmanship is amazing."
MeaningMnemonic: Your magic <radical>yoga</radical> <radical>brush</radical> lets you <kanji>build</kanji> what's in your mind's eye.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to pray as hard as you can as a spirit with an axe closes in on you. Can you hold your prayer concentration long enough?
ReadingHint: I guess the key was prayer all along. ( ͡° ͜ʖ ͡°)
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>pray</kanji> and pray and pray and nothing happens. You stop praying and dare to open your eyes. There is a <reading>key</reading> (き) in front of you. Grab the key and hold it before you and pray for the spirit to take it. Moments later, both the key and the spirit are gone.
MeaningMnemonic: If you see a <radical>spirit</radical> with an <radical>axe</radical>, you better <kanji>pray</kanji> for your life. Spirits only hold axes if they're thinking about murder, so pray as hard as you can to try and get it to spare you.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself holed up in a winter encampment, waiting to strike at the mohawk wearing enemies. Feel the cold around you as you scan the horizon for mohawks.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself all teched out like the techie you are. Hear the beeps and boops of all the technology you have at your disposal as you search for more mohawk enemies.
ReadingMnemonic: You're kind of a <reading>techie</reading> (てき), so you have an advantage over your <kanji>enemy</kanji>. You're armed with night vision goggles and other cool tech gear as well. Being a techie certainly has its advantages.
MeaningMnemonic: Seeing a <radical>mohawk</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> means the <kanji>enemy</kanji> is nearby. You are from the dreadlock tribe and the mohawk tribe is your enemy. You’re deep in the heart of battle and winter has fallen. Now is the time to take out the enemy.

MeaningHint: Imagine a tsunami simultaneously happening as the world comes to an end. Oh no, isn't there any way to stop this!?
ReadingHint: So the world won't come to an end this one time, thanks to the cyborg you made. Though it doesn't look very good. The cyborg is beaten up and full of water. This might be the end for it...
ReadingMnemonic: Just as the world is about to <kanji>come to an end</kanji>, your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) appears and blocks it with its cyborg body!
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>tsunami</radical> that's <radical>simultaneous</radical> (with another tsunami, I assume) and you know the world is about to <kanji>come to an end</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine what this committee of women could be coming together to discuss. Isn't grain just grain? Why do they need a whole committee?
ReadingHint: Imagine a committee of women standing together in a field of grain, watching eagles swooping around. They take notes and mutter to one another about their options.
ReadingMnemonic: Well, they formed this <kanji>committee</kanji> because <reading>ea</reading>gles (い) have been eating all the grain. The women meet as a committee and try to come up with ways to stop these pesky eagles from eating all the grain and leaving none for the humans.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>grain</radical> <radical>woman</radical> is a woman who talks about grain with other women on a <kanji>committee</kanji>. Together they're known as the Committee of Grain Women. What does this committee discuss, you wonder.

MeaningHint: Feel the grass in your toes and the fins in your hand as you raise them up to the sky. See the sun glistening off the fins as you raise them up in the air.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself going through the same process as before, only this time imagine yourself in Little Kyoto. Little Kyoto is really little, so make sure you're in the right spot.
ReadingMnemonic: This ritual only works in one part of the world however. It absolutely must be performed in Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). The way Little Kyoto lines up with the sun and the sky and all that astrological junk makes it the best place for you to <kanji>raise</kanji> fish fins up in the air.
MeaningMnemonic: If there’s <radical>grass</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> and <radical>fins</radical> in your <radical>hand</radical>, you need to <kanji>raise</kanji> that hand up to the sky. You’re in the middle of a fisherman’s ritual, which can only be performed on grassy ground. You take fish fins from the previous year, and raise them up to the sky in order to ensure a good fishing harvest.

MeaningHint: If you can't decide who gets custody of the baby, the dog, the house, the car, the furniture, the savings, and the house plants, you have to leave it up to the judge. And the judge's judgment is that everything is cut in half with a knife. What an efficient judge.
ReadingHint: Han Solo the judge is a very busy person, so he has to judge quickly and efficiently. He'll cut all your stuff perfectly in half before he wastes time listening to you argue about who earned what. Sure it's crude, but at least it's… equal?
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>judge</kanji> is <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo, so you know he's not going to back down.
MeaningMnemonic: I hereby cut your assets in <radical>half</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical>, says the <kanji>judge</kanji>. At someone's divorce proceedings. Ah, the oldest judge trick in the book.

MeaningHint: Imagine this new cow-controlled world — there are cows on every street corner, armed with towels and knives, intimidating all us humans into following their system.
ReadingHint: The cows' towels and knives won't stand a chance against your saber!
ReadingMnemonic: It's time to take <kanji>control</kanji> back from the cows! Pull out your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and fight back!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Cow</radical>s, using nothing more than a <radical>towel</radical> and a <radical>knife</radical>, have taken <kanji>control</kanji> of the world. Their <kanji>system</kanji> of control is brutal — payback for how we humans have treated them, I guess.

MeaningHint: It's hard to get a task done in winter. That's why you have this spear to power and motivate you.
ReadingHint: The moo haunts you as you finish your task. Luckily this spear is helping you keep going even though this task is really difficult.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>task</kanji> is to spear this cow. "<reading>Moo</reading> (む)!" it yells at you and runs away. You need to make dinner though... that's your task, so you better catch up to that cow and put an end to its mooing.
MeaningMnemonic: You use a special <radical>spear</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> to give yourself the <radical>power</radical> to do all of your <kanji>task</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: Inspect the tree. Inspect the top hat. Did you find anything?
ReadingHint: Turns out the top hat was just decorative and the tree was growing out of the earth, like any other tree. Imagine your shame now. You should be more careful — don't be sawing trees willy nilly.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>inspect</kanji> the tree, you pick up a <reading>saw</reading>(さ) and start cutting it. You need to investigate how the tree and the top hat are connected, and a saw is the only way to do it properly.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tree</radical> is growing out of a <radical>top hat</radical> you need to <kanji>inspect</kanji> it to find out why and how this happened. You should probably <kanji>investigate</kanji> the area too. I mean, how could a tree grow out of a top hat anyway? Seems suspicious.

MeaningHint: The world has been in turmoil and the public has been suffering, but it's time to stop splitting our hearts apart and fighting. It's time to be whole again.
ReadingHint: Suddenly people who were fighting for years are crying and holding one another. Their souls recognize each other, they're healed, and the whole nation is finally seeing eye to eye.
ReadingMnemonic: Once their hearts are <kanji>whole</kanji>, the public's <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) becomes one!
MeaningMnemonic: Taking out your <radical>thread</radical>, you sew together the <radical>public</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical>s to make them <kanji>whole</kanji> again.

MeaningHint: Try it out: I want a stabbing weapon with extra weapons that can pop out on the sides. *POOF* You've established a new weapon. And it's been added to the official weapon wiki of the world.
ReadingHint: Establish weapons in sets or the people who use them won't be able to match each other.
ReadingMnemonic: Weapons have to be <kanji>establish</kanji>ed in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). So you have to make another one that goes with it. Make them cohesive please.
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>say</radical> a new <radical>weapon</radical> idea out loud, then it will be <kanji>establish</kanji>ed immediately.

MeaningHint: Our resources are based around a system of trading shellfish. It's not that weird, plenty of ancient societies used shellfish as their main resource!
ReadingHint: Of course you're going to use your resources to get a sheep. You're living in a time when shellfish are used to trade for goods and services!
ReadingMnemonic: You save up all of your <kanji>resources</kanji> so you can buy a <reading>shee</reading>p (し). You bring a big sack of your shellfish resources to the market and finally get to pick out that sheep you've had your eyes on for months.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>next</radical> <radical>shellfish</radical> will be yours, but this shellfish will be mine. These are the <kanji>resources</kanji> we have to share and distribute.

MeaningHint: Imagine comforting this turkey as it leans against the tree, breathing its final breaths. "I lay down my life for the rights of my people…" it gasps. What a noble turkey.
ReadingHint: Ken jumps down and picks the turkey up, the broken arrow sticking out of its neck. "Turkeys do not have rights, my friend. Just let this one go."
ReadingMnemonic: "You have <kanji>rights</kanji>, so I won't shoot you like this turkey," says the voice of <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai, from above you in the tree.
MeaningMnemonic: Under a <radical>tree</radical>, a broken <radical>arrow</radical> protrudes from a <radical>turkey</radical>. It was fighting for the <kanji>rights</kanji> of its fellow turkeys, and was mortally wounded in the process.

MeaningHint: Picture this hoity-toity cow leader who thinks he’s too good for small matters and affairs. See him acting all fancy like he’s better than everyone else. The cow even moos with a fake British accent. How pretentious.
ReadingHint: Picture Ken the samurai scooping poop for the leader of the cows, because that's a matter of poop and state.
ReadingMnemonic: All of his <kanji>affair</kanji>s are taken care of by <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai, who he hired as his underling.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>cow</radical> tribe is too important to deal with small <kanji>matter</kanji>s and trivial <kanji>affair</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: This sect of the tofu-lovers club takes their duties very seriously and they must protect this tofu cliff at all costs.
ReadingHint: Oh, do you want to be a member of the tofu sect? Hah! Don't make me laugh. If you have to ask to be a member of a sect, you'll never get in.
ReadingMnemonic: All of the members of the <kanji>sect</kanji> are standing on the cliff, their hands raised, and right as the tsunami is about to collide with them, they shout, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!" and it's repelled! The tsunami sinks and rolls back out to sea — the tofu cliff is safe!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is coming for the <radical>cliff</radical> made of <radical>tofu</radical>, but there is a special <kanji>sect</kanji> that protects this tofu cliff.

MeaningHint: Imagine this hill of horns on top of this mountain. This hill of horns makes it possible for you to get just high enough to touch the top of the sky.
ReadingHint: Feel indifferent about this hill of horns on top of the mountain. It's all in your head anyways.
ReadingMnemonic: How was standing atop this hill of horns on top of a mountain to touch the sky? Eh. It was <reading>oka</reading>y (おか), but that's about it.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a scene going on inside your <radical>head</radical>. It is of a pair of <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> atop a <radical>mountain</radical>. No. It is not just one pair of horns. It is many. So many that they create a giant <kanji>hill</kanji> atop the mountain, which is already pretty high.

MeaningHint: Visualize this element of life, interconnecting all living things. Without it, everything would fall apart and into chaos.
ReadingHint: Imagine grabbing hold of the element that holds all life together and trying to wash it with soap. It's slippery so you keep dropping it and soap keeps going in your eyes.
ReadingMnemonic: You need to keep this <kanji>element</kanji> clean, so you make sure to wash it with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ) every single day. This turns out to be pretty hard because the element is an invisible thread, but you do your best.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> <radical>thread</radical> is an <kanji>element</kanji> essential to all living things. This thread of life joins all things together, providing an essential relationship between all life. This element is all that holds this world together.

MeaningHint: Picture this lion on his hind legs, walking through a rice field. He’s holding an axe in one hand and acting like a human. See him cut off the plants and hear the whoosh of the axe as he cuts.
ReadingHint: When dawn comes in Africa (it's a lion, so that's where he is) it gets too hot to be in the sun, so the lion gets cut off from his job of cutting things off.
ReadingMnemonic: The lion will <kanji>cut off</kanji> rice stalks until <reading>dawn</reading> (だん). But at the first light of dawn, it will run and hide in the shade of the trees, cut off from the rice.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>lion</radical> harvesting <radical>rice</radical> uses an <radical>axe</radical> to <kanji>cut off</kanji> the tops of the stalks. The lion is roaming the rice fields using his axe to harvest the rice. He does so by cutting off the part of the rice stalks he needs.

MeaningHint: Some criticism is okay, as long as it's constructive and it helps the person get better. But when you start pointing fingers, it's time to step back and try to not be such a jerk.
ReadingHint: Before you throw criticism around, imagine that embarrassed heat. You don't want to feel that way, so why would you do it to someone else?
ReadingMnemonic: If you're having trouble not giving out <kanji>criticism</kanji>, just think about how you would react if someone criticized you. You'd feel an embarrassed <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) rise throughout your body. That heat will cover your whole face.
MeaningMnemonic: If you use your <radical>fingers</radical> to point when you <radical>compare</radical> things you're really just throwing around <kanji>criticism</kanji>. You should never need to point fingers when you're just comparing things. If you're pointing and picking and being mean, that's criticism.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself being protected by your king and leader. They don’t really like you, but it is their duty to protect you. See them fighting off evil doers and taking bullets for your sake.
ReadingHint: You’re a nincompoop. Even though you’re so nincompoopy, your king and leader still have a duty to protect you. Feel happy and content, despite your nincompoopery.
ReadingMnemonic: You’re the village <reading>nin</reading>compoop (にん), but the king and the leader still have a <kanji>duty</kanji> to protect you. Even a nincompoop is important to the kings and leaders of the world.
MeaningMnemonic: Both a <radical>leader</radical> and a <radical>king</radical> have a <kanji>duty</kanji> to protect their people. A good leader always protects his followers, and a good king always protects his subjects. This is their duty.

MeaningHint: As you examine the tree and the squid in it, you think, "Huh, nope still weird." You should examine it further to find out what's going on.
ReadingHint: Imagine how many trees Ken examined before he found you both. Picture him putting his face really close to each one, then shedding a single tear when he realized this squid wasn't in it.
ReadingMnemonic: You're sticking your face in really, really close to <kanji>examine</kanji> this little squid in the tree when <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai appears behind you. "There you are, squid! Thank you citizen, I've been looking all over for my little chum."
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>tree</radical> with a <radical>squid</radical> on it, so you get closer to <kanji>examine</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: The clause states the tree can only be a tree in winter, but not during other seasons. Once winter descends, the clause takes effect and it becomes a tree. (See how winter is descending on the tree from above in this kanji?) Go ahead and look at its contract — see the winter tree clause, right there, in fine print.
ReadingHint: Joe noticed that the trees were getting too crowded, so he wrote up these clauses and added them to the trees' contracts. Now, the forests are less crowded, thanks to the little clause. Makes sense that a farmhand like Joe would do this, too. The fewer trees, the more farmland. It's good for folks like Joe.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>clause</kanji> was written into all trees' contracts by <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the large farmhand.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>winter</radical> <radical>tree</radical> is only a winter tree due to a <kanji>clause</kanji> in its tree contract.

MeaningHint: Just imagine an innocent little shellfish, living its quiet little life, while everyone's heaping blame on it. It hides in its little shell and tries to deflect all that blame. What a life!
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to look sexy as you slurp up those saucy little shellfish.
ReadingMnemonic: When those poor shellfish aren't having <kanji>blame</kanji> piled on them, they're being eaten, because shellfish are thought to make for a very <reading>sexy</reading> (せき) meal.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>life</radical> of a <radical>shellfish</radical> is filled with <kanji>blame</kanji>. They take the blame for everything from food poisoning and allergies, to cutting people's feet on beaches.

MeaningHint: You only have like, three eyes left. If you conserve them.
ReadingHint: The Shougun stares down at you, eyes blazing, and you hand over all but one of your eyes. Now you really have to conserve, or you'll be blind. Thanks Shougun. I bet he has plenty of eyes, he didn't need yours.
ReadingMnemonic: You're trying to <kanji>conserve</kanji> your eyes, when the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) marches by. He stops, sees you, and demands you give him your remaining eyes! But sir, I'm conserving what little I have left!
MeaningMnemonic: You only have a <radical>few</radical> <radical>eye</radical>s so you need to <kanji>conserve</kanji> what's left.

MeaningHint: Imagine putting it on, and suddenly the dirt around you increases by quite a bit.
ReadingHint: Zombie hands keep coming out of the ground, but as long as you wear the mask and increase the dirt on top of them, they won't be able to escape their earthly prison.
ReadingMnemonic: Use the dirt mask to <kanji>increase</kanji> the dirt around you. The <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう) are trying to escape, so you just keep increasing the dirt on top of them so they can't get out.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dirt</radical> <radical>mask</radical> is a mask which, when put on, will greatly <kanji>increase</kanji> the amount of dirt.

MeaningHint: Can you imagine a guard coming to your door and demanding you pay your grain tax?
ReadingHint: You don't want to be sliced with the zeiber, so you listen to vat he zei-zei-zeis and pay the tax.
ReadingMnemonic: You have to pay this <kanji>tax</kanji>, because if you don't the guard will cut you down with his special German saber, a <reading>zei</reading>ber (ぜい). "Don't you hear vat I zei? Pay ze tax or I vill zlei you vis my zeiber!" he shouts.
MeaningMnemonic: Some of your <radical>grain</radical> has just been taken by a <radical>guard</radical>. This is the <kanji>tax</kanji> you pay for living in this town. Back in the day, grain was used like currency, so it makes sense that guards went around collecting grain tax.

MeaningHint: A festival can't be happening all the time. Then it wouldn't be special, it would just be a party building, so they can only happen on special occasions.
ReadingHint: Your cyborg has been really great lately. Your wife loves it. It saved the world. It's nice of you to take this occasion to show the cyborg how much it means to you all with a festival.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>occasion</kanji> this time is to celebrate your wonderful <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). It's about time you did something nice for it.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Building</radical>s hold <radical>festival</radical>s on special <kanji>occasion</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: How could you not acknowledge somebody if every word they said was carved into your heart? It's not particularly painful, but it does make you quite uncomfortable.
ReadingHint: You're one of the biggest Nintendo fans out there, so how could you not acknowledge their greatness? No matter what is carved into your heart, Nintendo will always occupy a special place.
ReadingMnemonic: Having their words carved into your heart makes you <kanji>recognize</kanji> the authority of many people. But the only one you could ever truly <kanji>acknowledge</kanji>, is <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん).
MeaningMnemonic: Whatever people <radical>say</radical> to you will be carved by a <radical>blade</radical> into your <radical>heart</radical>. Sounds cruel, but it's a way to make you <kanji>recognize</kanji> their power.

MeaningHint: Go on, put that righteous radical knowledge to use!
ReadingHint: "THIS IS NOT RIGHTEOUSNESS!" you yell, just before the guillotine comes down on your neck.
ReadingMnemonic: Such <kanji>righteousness</kanji>! Well, at least according to the king who thinks he's being righteous. His version of righteousness is to send anyone who disagrees with him to the <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ).
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are exactly the same. That means they share meanings as well. <radical>Righteousness</radical> is as <kanji>righteousness</kanji> does, right?

MeaningHint: Picture yourself holding your cross right up next to your mouth as you speak your prayers. What will you wish to come true? It could be anything you want!
ReadingHint: Legend has it that writing down your wishes in kana will lead to higher chances of them actually coming true! Whether you go with hiragana or katakana is up to you, just make sure it's all in some form of kana.
ReadingMnemonic: And if you really want your wish to <kanji>come true</kanji>, make sure to write it down in <reading>kana</reading>!
MeaningMnemonic: Beside your <radical>mouth</radical> you hold your <radical>cross</radical> as you pray for all your wishes to <kanji>come true</kanji>. Perhaps some divine power will <kanji>grant</kanji> what you wish for?

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself working the bamboo saw. It's made of bamboo, but its sharp edge glistens with a mysterious aura as you slice through tree after tree. Feel how unnaturally easy it is to cut down piles of logs to build with.
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to construct the rest of this building covered in baby chicks. They're very cute, but this is dangerous! You have to build very carefully, so you don't accidentally hurt any of them.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>construct</kanji> and <kanji>build</kanji> this building, you hear a strange cheeping noise. Suddenly a <reading>chick</reading> (ちく) pops out of one of the logs you are building with. Then another, and another. You're surrounded by chicks! Is this because you used the magical(?) bamboo saw??
MeaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>saw</radical> to cut down <radical>tree</radical>s to <kanji>construct</kanji> and <kanji>build</kanji> a building. The bamboo saw was crafted by a master bamboosmith, and cuts through trees with ease so you can quickly construct this new building.

MeaningHint: What situation would you like to evaluate? Be sure to say "peace" before doing so. Imagine yourself doing this out loud and think of what you would say.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself trying to explain a situation you’re about to evaluate and your audience keeps shouting “Heyo!” every other sentence. Imagine how frustrating that would be. Get angry at your rude audience and how much they shout “Heyo.”
ReadingMnemonic: When you say "peace," you end up making a really bad pun and you hear somebody say “<reading>Heyo</reading>!” (ひょう) You look at the people around you and try to <kanji>evaluate</kanji> who said heyo, but you can’t tell. You start talking again when somebody exclaims “That’s what she said” and another person shouts “Heyo!” once again, but you still can’t evaluate who’s saying these things.
MeaningMnemonic: You should <radical>say</radical> "<radical>peace</radical>" before you <kanji>evaluate</kanji> a situation. That way everyone will know where you’re coming from. If you’re honest and say "peace" before you evaluate a situation out loud, everyone will know where you stand.

MeaningHint: Imagine getting so mad that you use a knife instead of words to say what you're trying to say. A net falls over you and pulls you down. The knife goes flying. This is your penalty for acting like a crazy person.
ReadingHint: If you don't want to be punished with bats, maybe next time don't use a knife instead of your words.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>penalty</kanji> for breaking the law isn't over. Now that you're stuck in a net, you can't fight back when they hit you with <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ).
MeaningMnemonic: You'll be put in a <radical>net</radical> if you try to <radical>say</radical> things with a <radical>knife</radical> — that's your <kanji>penalty</kanji>. You can't just go up to other people and say things with a knife out. That's why the police have to put you in a net, as a penalty.

MeaningHint: Picture multiple people you know saying you should do yoga if you plan to give birth. It isn't a question of whether you want to, it's the correct way to do it. So you should.
ReadingHint: Hopefully having a tan doesn't make birth harder, since you've worked so hard to do this the correct way with so much yoga.
ReadingMnemonic: To prepare for giving <kanji>birth</kanji> you end up doing so much yoga outside that you get a really deep <reading>tan</reading> (たん).
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>yoga</radical> is the <radical>correct</radical> way to go through <kanji>birth</kanji>. Birth isn't easy, but people keep telling you that yoga is the right way to prepare and actually do it.

MeaningHint: Picture the guard being so warm from the moonlight that he’s trying to simultaneously undress and escape. You can’t just let him leave his post!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself desperately throwing darts at the undressing, escaping guard, trying to make him stop. Or perhaps trying to pin his clothes to his body using the darts. Anything to stop this madness!
ReadingMnemonic: You’re unable to stop the guard from <kanji>undress</kanji>ing and making his <kanji>escape</kanji>, so you decide to start throwing some <reading>darts</reading> (だつ) at him. Perhaps that’ll make him stop.
MeaningMnemonic: The light of the <radical>moon</radical> is shining on a <radical>guard</radical> who’s been on duty all night. In fact, the moon light is making him so warm, he’s trying to <kanji>undress</kanji> and <kanji>escape</kanji>! Somebody stop him!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself running on a scooter with a bunch of other people and closing your eyes tight, willing another mustache to sprout out of your face to surpass them all.
ReadingHint: Watch helplessly as the car surpasses every single one of your scooters. Both of your mustaches droop in shame.
ReadingMnemonic: Suddenly, something else manages to <kanji>surpass</kanji> you all. It's a <reading>ca</reading>r (か)! A car with two mustaches is also riding on a scooter and going way faster than you.
MeaningMnemonic: While riding on your <radical>scooter</radical>, you grow a <radical>mustache</radical> on top of the <radical>mustache</radical> you already have in order to <kanji>surpass</kanji> the people around you. You're in a scooter race and your mustache is the only thing keeping you in the lead, but someone else has a mustache too! So you grow another mustache to surpass them once again!

MeaningHint: Picture this entire scene. The icicle falls in slow motion from the roof. It hits wolverine right in the middle of his forehead. He goes down. He has to lie down. He has to sleep.
ReadingHint: Wolverine might have a concussion that's making him neigh in his sleep. I guess it's good that he's lying down, but I'm pretty sure you're not supposed to sleep... Maybe you should wake him up so the neighing will stop—I mean, so he'll be okay!
ReadingMnemonic: While wolverine <kanji>sleep</kanji>s, he makes a horrible <reading>neigh</reading>ing (ね) sound. It's like there's a giant horse in his mouth screaming to get out! NEIGH NEIGH!
MeaningMnemonic: From the <radical>roof</radical>, an <radical>icicle</radical> falls and hits <radical>Wolverine</radical> in the <radical>forehead</radical>, and he has to go over to the <radical>stool</radical> to <kanji>lie down</kanji>. And eventually he goes to <kanji>sleep</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It is indeed just a roof over a bathtub, but I guess spirits inhabit everything according to Shintoism, so there's no reason this bathtub can't be a Shinto shrine too.
ReadingHint: It makes sense, the person who says "It's-a-me! (Mario)" is someone who works on bathtubs (and plumbing).
ReadingMnemonic: What god does this <kanji>Shinto shrine</kanji> hold? You see that "It's a-<reading>me-ya</reading> (みや)! (Mario!)"
MeaningMnemonic: It may just be a <radical>roof</radical> over a <radical>bathtub</radical>, but this here is actually a <kanji>Shinto shrine</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine kissing each of your best friends on the mouth. Does this make you uncomfortable? Excited? Both? Feel that feeling.
ReadingHint: Kissing the cock beak is kind of a strange experience. Feel the feathers in your hands as you steady the cock to kiss it. Cock-a-doodle-doo!
ReadingMnemonic: Actually, <kanji>each</kanji> and <kanji>every</kanji> one of your best pals is of the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) variety. By that, I mean each of them is a rooster. Imagine kissing each cock on its beak.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>kiss</radical> <kanji>each</kanji> and <kanji>every</kanji> one of your best pals to show them how much you love and care for them. You kiss each friend right on the mouth.

MeaningHint: Picture a tree coming up with some elaborate plans to make sure they can spend as little money as possible.
ReadingHint: You're not sure about this anchovy plan. Are you cheap enough to choose a life of eating only anchovies?
ReadingMnemonic: The cheap tree's <kanji>plan</kanji> involves only buying <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん) and surviving on them. Good exposure to the sun and fresh water is too expensive. The tree plans to save money by eating anchovies.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>cheap</radical> <radical>tree</radical> has a <kanji>plan</kanji>. A plan to stay cheap.

MeaningHint: Imagine that some wild animal wanders into your net. Yes! You knew putting the net there was a good idea. But wait… Oh no, it escaped! It turns out your net wasn't straight when you put it there.
ReadingHint: Imagine the smell of the cheese as you put it in the net. Maybe a sharp cheddar? The aroma really carries. All the better to attract more animals! Soon you’ll be putting your cheese-eating prey on the dinner table.
ReadingMnemonic: Then you decide to <kanji>put</kanji> <reading>chee</reading>se (ち) in your net. With a perfectly straight net and the cheese you put down as bait, there’s no way this fails. Success is all about how you put the net down and the cheese you put in it.
MeaningMnemonic: You need your <radical>net</radical> to be <radical>straight</radical> before you <kanji>put</kanji> it on the ground and try to lure something into it. You put your net there to catch something, but if it's twisted or tangled, it won't be very effective.

MeaningHint: Imagine opening your bank account and looking at all of your monthly expenses. Now drag the little dollar and shellfish icons over to cover all of those costs.
ReadingHint: When you see how expensive your heat is, you feel a heat going through your body too.
ReadingMnemonic: What is the biggest <kanji>expense</kanji> and <kanji>cost</kanji>? It's the <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ) bill.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Dollar</radical>s and <radical>shellfish</radical> are used to pay <kanji>expense</kanji>s and <kanji>cost</kanji>s. Shellfish used to be used as currency, so they're like the coins that go with dollars.

MeaningHint: Can you understand the value of this helicopter? The leader flew it to war and back! So of course it's worth a lot!
ReadingHint: Imagine driving a valuable car with the leader's helicopter on top. Yeah!
ReadingMnemonic: To further increase the helicopter's <kanji>value</kanji>, the leader tied it to a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Wow, now it has the value of a helicopter and a car combined! For a total value of… Well, imagine for yourself what value the car adds.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> only uses the <radical>helicopter</radical> with the highest <kanji>value</kanji>. They choose to fly in only the very best, most valuable helicopter. That's why it's worth way more than the others. Actually, any helicopter they use goes up in value. That's the amazing effect of a good leader.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself in the womb, the circle of power. You’re being born into the world. Feel the force of your mother all around you.
ReadingHint: All that force works wonders, and when the baby's born you slice through the umbilical cord with your saber.
ReadingMnemonic: Now pretend you're the mother. You'll need all the <kanji>force</kanji> you can muster to bring that baby into the world, so you grab hold of your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and slice it about in the air to give you the extra force you need.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>womb</radical> is a <radical>circle</radical> of <radical>power</radical> where a <kanji>force</kanji> of nature is formed. The womb has the power to create life. Life is a force of nature, and the circle of life starts in the womb.

MeaningHint: Picture a Viking trying to manage his Viking friends so they'll use the bathtub properly. He has to manage which people go in when, how hot or cold the water is, and provide all the soap. It's a very important job.
ReadingHint: Some of the Vikings managed to fill their bathtubs with acorns and are trying to rub themselves clean with them instead of water...
ReadingMnemonic: The poor Viking trying to <kanji>manage</kanji> all the others keeps having to confiscate <reading>a</reading>corns (えい). "You can't take baths in acorns and wood, it has to be water!" he shouts.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> who knows how to use a <radical>bathtub</radical> will have to <kanji>manage</kanji> all the baths for the other Vikings. Vikings aren't so great with bathing... if you can get one Viking to figure it out he has to manage the rest of them.

MeaningHint: Imagine using this jackhammer to show someone where the prize is buried. Feel the vibrations through your hands and the rest of your body.
ReadingHint: Picture Jesus getting all over the top and super preachy about his religious beliefs. He’s doing everything he can to indicate to the crowd that he’s the best, and show them how cool he is. Listen to Jesus preach to the crowd, desperate for their attention.
ReadingMnemonic: Actually, you're unearthing <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ). He’s the treasure. For your soul. He’s here to show you that Jesus is still a hip and cool guy and he wants to <kanji>indicate</kanji> to you that having faith in Jesus and God and stuff is super rad. Jesus is all like, “Who has two thumbs and wants to <kanji>show</kanji> you the way of the Lord? This guy.”
MeaningMnemonic: You can use a <radical>jackhammer</radical> to <kanji>indicate</kanji> and <kanji>show</kanji> someone where the prize is buried. You buried the prize to keep it safe, but the jackhammer is great at unearthing it. Jackhammers do a great job and show where the treasure lies. They indicate quite clearly where one needs to go.

MeaningHint: Allocate all of your thread like you would your cash: by uniting it! Not at a bank, but in a big, intertwined lump. Now nobody can stop you and your big lump of thread!
ReadingHint: Try to do this yourself at home. Unite all of your thread and poof! There's とうきょう!
ReadingMnemonic: You're busy trying to <kanji>unite</kanji> all of your thread, twisting and tying and wrapping, and suddenly you look at the ball in your hands and see <reading>とう</reading>きょう. You've united this thread into the shape of とうきょう. There are even little pins for all the famous neighborhoods and tourist spots. This is strange, because you don't remember putting them there.
MeaningMnemonic: You take all of your <radical>thread</radical> and <radical>allocate</radical> them into one place to <kanji>unite</kanji> them. With all your thread united and wrapped together, you're ready to <kanji>rule</kanji> and <kanji>govern</kanji> the world!

MeaningHint: Imagine this geoduck shouting orders at you to march into enemy territory. See its gross body as a mouth forms and starts shouting out orders.
ReadingHint: Feel yourself in the row boat as you imagine rowing away to safe territory. Hear the geoduck in the background shouting at you from shore, ordering you to return to land.
ReadingMnemonic: Not wanting to end up dead in enemy <kanji>territory</kanji>, you decide to escape this geoduck leader by getting on a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and rowing your way outta there. The geoduck commander orders you to halt your escape, but you still get in your row boat and row away.
MeaningMnemonic: Taking <radical>orders</radical> from a <radical>geoduck</radical> will place you into enemy <kanji>territory</kanji>. Geoducks make terrible commanders, so if you take orders from one, expect to end up in unknown territory, potentially even enemy territory. Be prepared.

MeaningHint: Imagine being trapped under this bamboo tree that is resting on your head. You’re stuck. Feel the weight of the bamboo tree pressing on your head as you try and devise a plan to get out from under it.
ReadingHint: Bamboo is usually kind of light, but this bamboo has a sack of rocks tied to each end making it heavy. Imagine yourself trying to remove this bamboo tree by pushing it up and away from you. It’s like doing a bench-press, bamboo style.
ReadingMnemonic: But why is this bamboo tree so heavy? You look to each end of the bamboo and realize that there is a <reading>sack</reading> (さく) tied on each end. Each sack seems to be filled with heavy rocks. Looks like someone made a <kanji>plan</kanji> to trap you here. Now you have to devise a plan to escape!
MeaningMnemonic: With a <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>tree</radical> resting atop your <radical>head</radical>, you devise a <kanji>plan</kanji>. The plan is actually for getting the bamboo tree off of your head. You’re trapped under it.

MeaningHint: The moon gladiators have a thing for wisterias.
ReadingHint: Imagine them reaching down from the moon and plucking some wisteria up from Mt. Fuji then taking them back up to the moon. Now they water them.
ReadingMnemonic: Since they're on the moon, where did they get the <kanji>wisteria</kanji> from? Actually, from one of the points on earth that is nearest to the moon, Mt. <reading>Fuji</reading> (ふじ).
MeaningMnemonic: The only <radical>flowers</radical> that the <radical>moon</radical> <radical>gladiator</radical>s will <radical>water</radical> are <kanji>wisteria</kanji>.

MeaningHint: I don't know how we managed to get a knife-wielding scarecrow as a vice president, there's a side of politics I don't get, I guess. I mean, just look at him up there. If something happens to our president we're stuck with that thing.
ReadingHint: What's going on? Normally if a vice president yells f**k you there would be... I dunno... consequences? What's happening?!
ReadingMnemonic: It's the <kanji>vice</kanji> president's turn to speak at the podium and he just clears his throat and yells, "<reading>F**k</reading> (ふく) you!" to the crowd, holding up his knife. They cheer.
MeaningMnemonic: That <radical>scarecrow</radical> with the <radical>knife</radical> is the <kanji>vice</kanji> president. Yeah, I know how he looks, and I'm pretty sure he does some murdering on the <kanji>side</kanji>, but you didn't hear that from me.

MeaningHint: View the turkey as the turkey views you. View the arrow stuck in it too.
ReadingHint: As you view this strange scene, Genghis Khan turns and views you viewing him. He points two fingers to his eyes and then to you, and marches off into the woods. You also view the turkey running off in the other direction.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>view</kanji> the turkey, Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) suddenly comes into your view too. He towers over the injured turkey, leans down, and... pulls the arrow out!?
MeaningMnemonic: There's an <radical>arrow</radical> in a <radical>turkey</radical> that you can <radical>see</radical> in your <kanji>view</kanji>. The turkey seems to be viewing you too, from afar.

MeaningHint: Imagine how terrifying it is when your leader looks straight at YOU. Don't worry, though — you've got more value than you give yourself credit for, and your leader can surely see that.
ReadingHint: Imagine the despair you feel walking down the cheese aisle at the store and seeing how one little pack of string cheese now costs the same as your monthly rent. Why did your leader have to spike the value of cheese like this?
ReadingMnemonic: One thing your leader knows has great <kanji>value</kanji> is <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Say goodbye to your days of casually snacking on cheesy treats from the conbini, because your leader's decided the value of cheese is going up 1000%!
MeaningMnemonic: A great <radical>leader</radical> can look <radical>straight</radical> at something and know its <kanji>value</kanji>. That social media site? 44 billion dollars. That job position? 50k a year. A four-day work week? Um… sorry, no, there's no value there. See? Leaders can see straight to the heart of matters to grasp something's true value.

MeaningHint: If you're ever having trouble remembering how to suck or inhale, just picture an actual escalator that leads into your mouth. That'll definitely help.
ReadingHint: You can inhale a cucumber too, I suppose, but don't tell anyone I told you to.
ReadingMnemonic: Use this mouth ability to <kanji>suck</kanji> <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう) into your mouth. Cucumbers are shaped like cylinders so you can suck them into your mouth like this. Just as nature intended.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> is like an <radical>escalator</radical> you use to <kanji>suck</kanji> and <kanji>inhale</kanji> things into your body.

MeaningHint: Picture this drunkard in the dirt, her mouth pressed to the ground. "Ahh," she says, "so this is the taste of the Bordeaux region." Then she has a glass of Bordeaux, and starts noting all its particular regional qualities.
ReadingHint: Imagine her crying out, "Why are there such icky, earthy notes in all these regional wines?!" Feel the second-hand embarrassment for her. In her drunkenness she can't understand that putting her mouth on the ground makes everything taste icky, no matter what region it's from.
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out she thinks everything from this <kanji>region</kanji> is <reading>icky</reading> (いき). In fact, to the drunkard, every region tastes icky and gross. You don't have the heart to tell her it's the dirt and ground, not the region itself, that tastes icky…
MeaningMnemonic: Lying in the <radical>dirt</radical>, a <radical>drunkard</radical> puts her <radical>mouth</radical> to the <radical>ground</radical> to understand the <kanji>region</kanji>. She loves to know all the regional differences among wines and other alcohols, and to her there's no better way to do this than putting her mouth on the ground and literally tasting each region.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as a figure judge on this panel. You need to find a woman with the perfect figure to cast in your commercial. You'd cast yourself, but you just aren't confident (Ridiculous! You're special, and beautiful, and no matter who you are [yes you reading this right now] you should be proud of who you are!).
ReadingHint: We should all try harder to have a figure like this stunning sheep woman!
ReadingMnemonic: The next woman comes in and you examine her <kanji>figure</kanji>. The figure you've been searching for! It just happens to belong to a <reading>shee</reading>p (し) woman. But she's soft, fluffy, and round. The perfect figure!
MeaningMnemonic: “Bring in the <radical>next</radical> <radical>woman</radical> so that I may examine her <kanji>figure</kanji>!” We’re holding auditions for a commercial and we need to find a woman with a nice figure. The last woman was no good, so bring in the next one.

MeaningHint: You usually ignore everyone, but this one person... they really got under there and touched your heart. You finally feel like they're the right person to respond to.
ReadingHint: The 王 touched your heart, you responded, and now you're together. It's like a fairy tale!
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>respond</kanji> and it turns out it was to the <reading>王</reading> (おう).
MeaningMnemonic: If someone gets under the <radical>canopy</radical> over your <radical>heart</radical> you should <kanji>respond</kanji> to them.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself in class. You want to present next! You want to submit your ideas for class approval. So raise those fingers to the sun the correct way and wait for your teacher to call on you.
ReadingHint: The next time you have to submit work in class just raise your hand and give everyone tater tots. Instant "A" material.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to <kanji>present</kanji> your show and tell of <reading>ta</reading>ter tots (てい) to the class. You raise your hand, the teacher calls on you, and you <kanji>submit</kanji> the tater tots for her and the class's approval. "Oh my!" the teacher exclaims, "I hope you brought enough tater tots to present to the whole class!" You raise your hand once more to the sky, and in it you submit a whole tray of tater tots!
MeaningMnemonic: Pointing your <radical>fingers</radical> to the <radical>sun</radical> is the <radical>correct</radical> way to <kanji>present</kanji> yourself or offer to <kanji>submit</kanji> your ideas. You're really just raising your hand. And when you want to present yourself, your ideas, or offer anything, the correct way is to raise your fingers to the sun. You definitely learned this in school.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself playing football. You catch the ball with your fingers and then assist your team by running and smashing your body into the enemy team. But in the process you smash your fingers... so you need to go to the aid tent.
ReadingHint: You can't assist your team anymore because you got hurt. This isn't fair! It's the end. The end of your life and your career. Boo hoo hoo!
ReadingMnemonic: When you need <kanji>aid</kanji> for your football wrecked fingers you can't help but feel like it's the <reading>en</reading>d (えん) for you. Your fingers hurt so much, you don't even want the nice nurses to aid you.
MeaningMnemonic:
You use your <radical>fingers</radical> in the game of <radical>football</radical> to <kanji>aid</kanji> and <kanji>assist</kanji> the other players. And when you smash your fingers in football, you need to get some aid yourself.

If you're from the UK, just imagine someone kicking or stomping on your fingers instead.

MeaningHint: Picture a bunch of big, sharp icicles all falling down directly next to this dog. Now imagine the condition he would've been in, had they all fallen directly on top of him. Poor little thing.
ReadingHint: Good guy Joe, always worrying about his dog's condition. Everyone needs a farmhand like Joe.
ReadingMnemonic: What saved the dog's <kanji>condition</kanji> was none other than <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the local farmhand. He constantly keeps an eye on the dog's condition, including any dangerously sharp icicles that could descend on him.
MeaningMnemonic: See all those <radical>icicle</radical>s to the <radical>dog</radical>'s left? Good thing they all fell on his left, or he would've been in a terrible <kanji>condition</kanji>.

MeaningHint: See yourself in this ideal appearance. Everyone loves to look at you because you look so awesome. Your heart has given you many great abilities. Feel strong and powerful in this body of yours.
ReadingHint: Since you don’t know how to tie a tie or tie your shoes, you wear slip ons and clip ons. Hear the noise of Velcro being undone as you kick off your shoes and unclip your tie after a hard day’s work at the office.
ReadingMnemonic: While you may have the <kanji>appearance</kanji> of someone who is super strong, powerful, and awesome, you do have one shortcoming. You don’t know how to <reading>tie</reading> (たい) a tie. Actually, you don’t even know how to tie your shoes, let alone your tie. How embarrassing.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a great <radical>ability</radical> because your <radical>heart</radical> gives you the <kanji>appearance</kanji> of a superhuman. Your heart is really strong and powerful, fuelling your body to the best of its ability. All this together gives you the appearance of someone in top condition.

MeaningHint: Shellfish were once used as currency, so this totally makes sense. Just think of the shellfish as money coming out of your mouth as you say your congratulations.
ReadingHint: The next time you're congratulating someone, assume the gargoyle stance and dump all of their congratulatory shellfish on them from your mouth.
ReadingMnemonic: When you say <kanji>congratulations</kanji> like this, you look just like a <reading>ga</reading>rgoyle (が). You're crouching down, your mouth wide open, just like a stone gargoyle.
MeaningMnemonic: You use the <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> to spit <radical>shellfish</radical> at people as a form of <kanji>congratulations</kanji>! In fact, you yell "CONGRATULATIONS!" as the shellfish come pouring out of your powerful mouth.

MeaningHint: Seems like the treasure has some magical ability to renew the cells in your body. Really try to imagine this feeling as you pull the treasure out of the rice paddy’s ground. Perhaps it feels just as refreshing as getting a full night’s sleep.
ReadingHint: All you could hear was a sly “yoink!”, and suddenly your life renewing treasure was gone. God knows what things こういち will get up to with this life renewing treasure in his possession (but it sure won’t be anything good).
ReadingMnemonic: Just as you were getting accustomed to this life <kanji>renew</kanji>ing treasure, <reading>こう</reading>いち comes out of nowhere, stealing your treasure and running off with it.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>ground</radical> of the <radical>rice paddy</radical> is a <radical>treasure</radical>. As you pull it out of the mucky ground, you can feel your life force being <kanji>renew</kanji>ed.

MeaningHint: Focus on descending the stairs. There are many signs that say "To Winter Cow: descend" with an arrow pointing down. "I guess I'll have to descend," you say.
ReadingHint: "Hello, thank you for descending all the way down here to see me," says the Winter Cow with こういち's face. "You thought you could use the Winter Cow to stop me, but as you can see, I got here first."
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>descend</kanji> to the bottom and there it is, the Winter Cow. You walk up to the cow, and it swings its head back to see you. Instead of a cow's face, it's <reading>こう</reading>いち's face.
MeaningMnemonic: Inside the <radical>building</radical> is the <radical>Winter</radical> <radical>Cow</radical>. You need to see it, so you go inside. It is deep down in there, and you'll have to <kanji>descend</kanji> to reach it.

ReadingHint: Look at the eagle and her egg sitting atop your clothes. Every time you get close the eagle starts to screech at you fiercely. Screee! Screee!
ReadingMnemonic: An <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) sees a pile of your <kanji>clothes</kanji> and lays an <reading>e</reading>gg (え) in them. The eagle’s egg needs a soft warm place to incubate, and your pile of clothes is the perfect place.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>clothes</radical> radical and the <kanji>clothes</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Surprising!

MeaningHint: You can obtain a lot of goods this way without needing any cash. Just don't tell anyone I told you.
ReadingHint: You have to pull very slowly, or the shoes will fall off the stool. But you really, really want to obtain these particular shoes (they're really sweet), so you keep scooting them toward you.
ReadingMnemonic: Today you want to <kanji>obtain</kanji> a new pair of <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). You tie your vines around the stool they're on, and slowly inch them toward the door.
MeaningMnemonic: Wrap <radical>vines</radical> around a <radical>stool</radical> to <kanji>obtain</kanji> it. Don't worry about paying for it, sneak into the stool store and tie some vines around the legs. Then when you leave, pull the vines and the stool will come out after. That's how you obtain a stool!

MeaningHint: Imagine being told to halt in front of your favorite restaurant, then being turned away. Noooo! Better start sucking up to the restaurant leader.
ReadingHint: HALT! NO TATERS FOR YOU!
ReadingMnemonic: People only <kanji>halt</kanji> because they want this restaurant's world famous <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots. Which is probably why there is a leader out front making sure only people who can really appreciate the tater tots can get in to eat them.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>restaurant</radical> says, "<kanji>Halt</kanji>!" to everyone who wants to come in. The leader looks them up and down. Stares into their eyes. And then, maybe, they may go into the restaurant to eat.

ReadingHint: You won’t get much leather from a cock, but you could skin one in a pinch, should you really need a little extra leather.
ReadingMnemonic: One way to get <kanji>leather</kanji> is to skin a male chicken, a <reading>cock</reading> (かく). Not a very efficient way to get leather, but one way nonetheless.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leather</radical> radical and the <kanji>leather</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Cool!

MeaningHint: Hear the strange sounds this drunkard is making. Let his ramblings enter your ears and imagine what he could be trying to say.
ReadingHint: Actually you find out that he does have employment. He’s a garbage man. Feel the shock of making this shocking discovery. How can this drunkard possibly hold down his job like this?
ReadingMnemonic: Since this guy is such a pathetic drunkard, it comes as no <reading>shock</reading> (しょく) that he can’t find <kanji>employment</kanji>. If he did find employment, I would be filled with both shock and awe.
MeaningMnemonic: Reaching your <radical>ear</radical> is the <radical>sound</radical> of a <radical>drunkard</radical> who can’t find <kanji>employment</kanji>. You hear him before you see him because he’s behind some trash cans. Being a drunkard, he’s like, super wasted and just kind of rambling to himself, making strange sounds. No wonder he can’t find employment.

ReadingHint: Picture yourself locking up a demon with a special demon containing key. Hear the key click into the lock as the demon screams to be set free.
ReadingMnemonic: How do you keep a <kanji>demon</kanji> locked up? Use a <reading>key</reading> (き). Use a lock and key and keep that demon locked up safely behind bars so he can’t get out and do bad demon stuff.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>demon</radical> radical and the <kanji>demon</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Wicked!

MeaningHint: Picture this turkey holding its flowers while sitting on its stool. Hear it gobbling as it defends everything around it with its mere presence.
ReadingHint: Imagine Godzilla approaching the flower turkey. Hear Godzilla roar as the turkey gobbles in fear before being crushed beneath the massive and scaly foot of Godzilla.
ReadingMnemonic: The only thing this flower-bearing turkey can't <kanji>defend</kanji> against is <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご). Godzilla is pretty much undefeatable, especially for a turkey. The turkey is totally unable to defend anything from the mighty Godzilla.
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> that <radical>flowers</radical> held by a <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> are good to <kanji>defend</kanji> yourself with. Even better than a guard dog they say. People are so confused by the turkey sitting on a stool holding flowers that they just lose their will to fight. This turkey is great to defend yourself and your home.

MeaningHint: The entire house glows with blobs of life, and the mouth holds the knife in its mouth and cuts the life blobs apart to help them grow.
ReadingHint: Picture Wario's mouth dividing himself now, and creating more, smaller Warios. They all yell WA! and divide up even more until the whole room is full of わりs and わs!
ReadingMnemonic:
As it <kanji>divide</kanji>s life, the mouth begins to look really familiar. It has big teeth and a crooked mustache... it's <reading>Wari</reading>o (わり)!

Sometimes Wario cuts himself by accident and just becomes <reading>わ</reading> (which is all he says too).
MeaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>roof</radical> of <radical>life</radical> a <radical>mouth</radical> with a <radical>knife</radical> <kanji>divide</kanji>s cells to create life.

MeaningHint: He yells back, "YOU CAN'T JUDGE ME, I CAN DO WHAT I WANT. GET THAT JUDGEY CROSS OFF ME!"
ReadingHint: The cyborg joins in, "A judge would convict you if the fashion police brought you in. I have a bunch of meat and robot parts and even I'm better looking than you."
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>judge</kanji> each other, your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) comes stomping over and slams the drunkard against the wall. With your trusty cyborg coming to your rescue, you can continue to judge this guy all you want.
MeaningMnemonic: You place a <radical>cross</radical> on this <radical>drunkard</radical>'s <radical>clothes</radical> to show him you <kanji>judge</kanji> him. In fact, you yell, "I JUDGE YOU FOR BEING DRUNK AND WEARING THESE CLOTHES, TAKE THIS!"

MeaningHint: Cape Cod, for example. Picture it now (or look it up if you need to). Really does look like a big lip ring stuck in the side of Massachusetts, doesn't it? Imagine seeing that cape from the top of your mountain, wind blustering around you.
ReadingHint: Try to draw that bit of coastline where the cape is. Nice, easy, smooth lines, until BAM! — the cape. Now the coastline is all jagged like the edge of a saw or key.
ReadingMnemonic: Looking down at the <kanji>cape</kanji>, you realize capes always make the coastline look jagged, like a <reading>saw</reading> or <reading>key</reading> (さき). Thanks for keeping the coastline interesting, you saw-key capes!
MeaningMnemonic: From the top of a <radical>mountain</radical> you see something that looks like a <radical>big</radical> <radical>lip ring</radical> jutting out into the ocean: it's a <kanji>cape</kanji>! You know, the geological formation where a big piece of land sticks out into the ocean? That kind of cape.

MeaningHint: But why? Because this acting will distract everyone from the impending tsunami. You dance around in your fins, pretending to swim. It's a wonderful performance.
ReadingHint: The performance and the tsunami end together. How poetic.
ReadingMnemonic: As the <kanji>acting</kanji> <reading>en</reading>ds (えん), so do you all.
MeaningMnemonic: There's going to be a <radical>tsunami</radical>. So you get up on your <radical>roof</radical>. You see people across the <radical>street</radical>, in a <radical>rice paddy</radical>. They watch as you put on a pair of <radical>fins</radical>. And you begin <kanji>acting</kanji>. This is your last chance to <kanji>perform</kanji>!
