ReadingHint: Maybe if you yell f**k you enough things will be restored. Ha. No. Everything's gone and it's your fault for being gullible.
ReadingMnemonic: Finally there's nothing else for the black hole to eat so you hit the <kanji>restore</kanji> button.... but nothing happens. "Oh <reading>f**k</reading> (ふく) you!" you scream as you realize you were lied to.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>black hole</radical>, waiting for it to finish destroying and switch to <kanji>restore</kanji> mode. You were told all black holes finish eventually, and when they do you can press a button to restore everything back to the way it was.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself loitering around your master’s fancy mansion. You’re pretending to dust things off, but you’re actually just waiting for him to depart on his journey.
ReadingHint: Your master has departed! You are the 王 now!
ReadingMnemonic: Finally, it's time for your master's <kanji>journey</kanji> and he calls to you and says, "You are the <reading>王</reading> (おう) of the house now. Take care of everything while I'm gone."
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> around your <radical>master</radical> until he <kanji>depart</kanji>s on a <kanji>journey</kanji>. You have a lot more freedom to do things after your master has departed, so until that time you just loiter about looking busy and staying out of his way.

MeaningHint: Imagine this garage of yours with a sea of white cars. Now there is a new car that really sticks out like a sore thumb. Imagine yourself looking out over your cars and seeing this wacky car practically jumping out at you.
ReadingHint: See yourself driving around in this peacock car. Hear the engine purr as strangers jeer and yell at you. This car sure does get a lot of attention, doesn’t it?
ReadingMnemonic: What did I say this car looks like? Oh that’s right, a pea<reading>cock</reading> (かく). This car looks like a peacock. It’s the color of a peacock and you’ll look like you’re peacocking when you’re out on the town driving it. It really is such a stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to like, every other car out there.
MeaningMnemonic: Your new <radical>car</radical> doesn’t <radical>mix</radical> well with the other cars in your garage because its color is in stark <kanji>contrast</kanji> to the others. All of your other cars are pristine white in color, but this new car is a mix of lots of colors. It almost looks like a peacock or something it’s so colorful. Such a contrast.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself using this bamboo writing brush. Do you know many kanji? Of course you do! Imagine yourself writing them all with this nice writing brush. It’s actually fun to write things with it!
ReadingHint: Imagine the sound the brush makes when it hits the paper. It’s a bit of a plop, and then kind of a scratching sound as the brush bristles move around. Really focus on this sound that the brush makes when it hits the paper.
ReadingMnemonic: Once the <kanji>writing brush</kanji> <reading>hits</reading> (ひつ) that paper, it’s like you transform into a totally different person. All of a sudden you’re a master calligrapher, writing anything and everything in a most beautiful fashion. Once that brush hits the paper, it’s on!
MeaningMnemonic: Out of <radical>bamboo</radical>, you make a <radical>brush</radical> – a <kanji>writing brush</kanji>. This is no ordinary brush, mind you. It is a brush specifically designed for writing elegant characters. And it’s made from bamboo!

MeaningHint: Imagine having these gold fins in your mouth. Taste the metal as your tongue runs over them.
ReadingHint: Lead poisoning, huh? What a way for everything to end...
ReadingMnemonic: You pay for your <kanji>lead</kanji> but the cashier puts it in your mouth where the gold fins were and... everything <reading>en</reading>ds (えん).
MeaningMnemonic: You hold <radical>gold</radical> <radical>fins</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> while shopping for pencil <kanji>lead</kanji> at the store. This pencil lead store only accepts gold fins as payment, but your fins don’t fit in your pockets and you need your hands to carry the lead so the gold fins go in your mouth.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking down the street to deposit more shellfish into your special savings and gaze happily at the huge pile you keep far enough away that you aren't tempted to spend it.
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to lift up a shellfish with a pair of smooth chopsticks. It slips right out. Ugh! I guess that's good though, because it's a savings for a reason (you shouldn't be spending it)!
ReadingMnemonic: When you want to withdraw from your <kanji>savings</kanji> you force yourself to use <reading>cho</reading>psticks (ちょ) to pick up the shellfish. They're especially slippery, hard to use chopsticks too, that way it's really hard to take too much out of your savings.
MeaningMnemonic: You keep all of your <radical>shellfish</radical> under a <radical>roof</radical> down the <radical>street</radical> and it's your <kanji>savings</kanji>. Shellfish used to be used as money way back, and these aren't shellfish you're spending, they're ones you're saving up for later.

MeaningHint: Imagine a poor, broken town. And, imagine the mayor of a town telling their people that things are bad because of that bad old winter. "It's to blame for our circumstance!" he yells.
ReadingHint: Imagine your kid character walking through town, using their super powers to destroy buildings and rot food.
ReadingMnemonic: In reality, this <kanji>circumstance</kanji> was actually caused by a single <reading>子</reading> (こ), though. This kid went through town, destroying everything in their path.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>old</radical> <radical>winter</radical> is to blame for today's <kanji>circumstance</kanji>. It is the <kanji>reason</kanji> things are bad now.

MeaningHint: Picture this huge, tall building standing directly in the way. You peer around the other side and there is a book floating in the air that says "Chapter 5: Success and Happiness." Dang, you really want to get over there to that part of your life.
ReadingHint: The Shougun wants to hinder you and your life. He wants you to have to work harder so you become better, faster, and stronger than anyone else. Hindering you is his way of making sure you don't take the easy way out in life.
ReadingMnemonic: Someone put this building here specifically to <kanji>hinder</kanji> you. They knew you were close to the next chapter of your life, and they wanted to hinder you really good. And there they are, sitting on a throne smirking. It's the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう).
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>building</radical> standing in the way of the next <radical>chapter</radical> in your life, and it's <kanji>hinder</kanji>ing you. It's not like you couldn't find another way around, but it's definitely inconvenient.

MeaningHint: First of all, this could be really fun. Second of all, if this person is a demon or something, you should really just obey them or they could curse you. What's the worst that could happen?!
ReadingHint: You stole those jewels together and now you're an accomplice for accompanying him! You better hide those jewels!!
ReadingMnemonic: If you <kanji>obey</kanji> the horned man you'll end up with a ton of <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう)!
MeaningMnemonic: If you see someone <radical>loiter</radical>ing around with <radical>horns</radical> on their head you'd be <radical>correct</radical> to <kanji>obey</kanji> and <kanji>accompany</kanji> them.

MeaningHint: Just keep going with this sentence about the ego. You can't stop, because really it's your ego that's out of control. That's <kanji>I</kanji> when you're saying it.
ReadingHint: You're just destroying the ego of that other guy. "Why?" you ask? "Because <kanji>I</kanji> am the boss here. And you are the baby. Ego things."
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>I</kanji> am the greatest in the world! I am no baby like you, that's why. A baby who says "<reading>ga</reading> (が) ga ga ga!"
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>ego</radical>... it's totally out of control. <kanji>I</kanji> mean, my ego isn't out of control. I'm the greatest. That's <kanji>me</kanji>. But your ego? Ugh, so out of control unlike my amazing ego.

MeaningHint: Imagine this white tsunami, more violent and fierce than any other tsunami you’ve ever seen, coming toward you. It’s very frightening. Feel the cold wetness it brings to your skin.
ReadingHint: "Geecckkkiiiiii!!!" you scream in horror, as he's thrown about by the fierce, winter tsunami. You have to close your eyes. You can't watch Gecki go.
ReadingMnemonic: The tsunami is too <kanji>fierce</kanji>, too <kanji>violent</kanji>, and before you realize what's happening, <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your beloved gecko, is lifted up and carried off into the air.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> that is <radical>white</radical>, traveling in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> is a <kanji>fierce</kanji> and <kanji>violent</kanji> force of nature. This white tsunami is chasing after winter, and that’s why the tsunami is white—it’s a winter tsunami. It’s the most fierce and most violent kind of tsunami in existence.

MeaningHint: If you can get to the top of the cliff, it will use its "ten thousand power" to encourage you.
ReadingHint: A cliff is encouraging you to buy a ray gun for some reason. This is not the encouragement you expected, but it is encouragement nonetheless. You go back down the cliff and immediately buy a ray gun.
ReadingMnemonic: When you get there, it does <kanji>encourage</kanji> you. The cliff says "Hey, I encourage you to buy a <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun."
MeaningMnemonic: The "<radical>Cliff</radical> of <radical>Ten Thousand</radical> <radical>Power</radical>" is where you go if you want to be <kanji>encourage</kanji>d.

MeaningHint: Imagine being chastised with a measurement device. Think how horrible it would be getting hit on your knuckles, your nose, or the back of your head. It would be awful. Imagine this experience.
ReadingHint: How could you say something in English while you're in とうきょう?! After you went to all the trouble to learn all this kanji and Japanese? Geez, we almost think you deserve to be chastised...
ReadingMnemonic: You're being <kanji>chastise</kanji>d because you tried to say something in English in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Everyone around you slowly turned around, eyes aflame and rulers in hand, ready to chastise you for uttering those forbidden words.
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> using a <radical>measurement</radical> device (like a ruler) is a good way to <kanji>chastise</kanji> someone. They do something bad? Chastise them by slapping their wrists, their feet, their eyes, ears, anything! They say something out of turn? CHASTISE THEM!

MeaningHint: Picture a bunch of postal workers going in and out of the post office building, drooping under the weight of so much mail. It's a tough job.
ReadingHint: Imagine all these droopy youths, showing up for the morning shift to pay their way through college as mail carriers. Ah, you were one of them once…
ReadingMnemonic: Because it's such a physically demanding job, all the <kanji>mail</kanji> carriers in this <kanji>post</kanji> office are <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). Only the youths can handle this kind of heavy mail-bag lifting, I guess.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>droopy</radical> <radical>building</radical> is where people droop under big sacks of <kanji>mail</kanji>, aka a <kanji>post</kanji> office.

MeaningHint: Picture this golden cross in your head. Why do you suppose there’s this needle stuck in it? You try to pull it out but you can’t. Envision yourself straining, trying to get the thing out.
ReadingHint: Imagine a needle sticking into your shin. It hurts a ton. How would you try to get it out?
ReadingMnemonic: You're about to give up on the <kanji>needle</kanji> when it pops out and flies directly into your <reading>shin</reading> (しん). Ouch! Now you're desperately trying to pull the needle out of your shin, but it really won't budge this time. Maybe you should have minded your own business.
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>gold</radical> <radical>cross</radical>, you see a <kanji>needle</kanji> sticking out of it. The needle is right in the middle of the gold cross. If Jesus were on there, the needle would be sticking him right in the chest.

MeaningHint: We don't want to focus too much on what the indication / sign actually is, though, because it will take away from the <kanji>indication</kanji> part (which we want to remember). So, focus on the fact that this is some kind of indication that's important, but you don't know what that indication means or is... because we'll use that with the reading.
ReadingHint: Imagine an announcer watching the loitering mountain king, "There he is... and it's still winter. We all know what that means. It's a sign that Mrs. Chou will be coming out of hibernation soon." Then, you can imagine her bursting forth from the mountain, running down her street.
ReadingMnemonic: When you spot a loitering mountain king in the winter, you know it's an <kanji>indication</kanji> that soon Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) will appear. It's like a Groundhog's Day kind of situation, but instead it means Mrs. Chou is about to come out, perhaps early for the season.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>loiter</radical>ing <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>king</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> is an <kanji>indication</kanji> or <kanji>sign</kanji> of something to come.

MeaningHint: Imagine inching closer to this suspicious soul floating around a tombstone. What do you think it's doing?
ReadingHint: Picture a suspicious, kettle holding, kayaking soul. The afterlife may be more interesting than you thought.
ReadingMnemonic:
You sneak around the tombstone to see what this <kanji>suspicious</kanji> soul is doing, and you realize it's pretending to <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい). The soul is sitting behind the tombstone, in a small kayak, moving its arms like it's rowing through water.

Even more suspicious, the soul seems to be holding a <reading>ke</reading>ttle (け). Considering the soul is already dead, this doesn't seem like something it will need.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>soul</radical> floating around a <radical>tombstone</radical> acting <kanji>suspicious</kanji>. To be fair, if a soul is going to be anywhere, a tombstone is probably high up on the list, but this one seems like it's doing something weird.

MeaningHint: Picture this beast creeping, low to the ground, in the grass around the rice paddy. It's got its eyes on you. Uh oh.
ReadingHint: All beasts have something they're trying to protect. In this case, it's some beastly jewels.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>beast</kanji> is guarding its precious <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう), which it buried in the grass or the rice paddy somewhere. If you don't want the beast to think you're coming to steal those jewels, you better keep your distance.
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>grass</radical> near a <radical>rice paddy</radical> sits a <radical>creeper</radical> that is some sort of a <radical>dog</radical> <kanji>beast</kanji>, a vicious <kanji>animal</kanji>. This creeper isn’t just a normal dog, it is more beast-like, more like a feral animal than domesticated pet.

MeaningHint: Look at this cake you have made. It’s majestic. Now taste some of it. It is one great tasting cake. Imagine yourself eating the best cake in all of ever.
ReadingHint: Hear the roar of the car engine as you get splattered in the face with the cake. Wipe it off of your face just in time to see the car speeding off into the distance. Also, recall that this kanji shares a reading with the similar kanji, 果.
ReadingMnemonic: You’re standing back, admiring your <kanji>cake</kanji> when you hear a <reading>ca</reading>r (か) engine revving and before you know it your cake has been run over. The car came out of nowhere and ran your cake right over, spraying fruit cake bits and flowers all over the place. Stupid car!
MeaningMnemonic: You take some <radical>flowers</radical> and some <radical>fruit</radical> and you make a <kanji>cake</kanji> out of it. It’s a very nice looking, flavorful cake. The fruit makes it taste great and the flowers make for great cake decorations.

MeaningHint: Imagine how painful this would be, having your lip ring ripped from your lip. It would hurt a lot. Touch your lip and imagine missing half of it. Yikes.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself fording the river, all wet and cold from the water. Keeping your eyes on your car, you move slowly across. Look at the car. Look at it!
ReadingMnemonic: But why were you trying to cross this <kanji>river</kanji> anyway? Well your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) was on the other side of it, and you figured if you could get to your car you could outrun the tsunami and be safe. Too bad you fell and ripped out your lip ring though.
MeaningMnemonic: During a <radical>tsunami</radical>, your <radical>lip ring</radical> is ripped off while you’re trying to cross a <kanji>river</kanji>. There was no other path to safety, you had to cross the raging river, but you lost your footing and smashed your face on a rock and lost your lip ring.

MeaningHint: Imagine shaking your fingers at the dirt. Do you think you can shake the dirt hard enough to cause a landslide (spoilers, no).
ReadingHint: Imagine your shins shaking, your hands are waving, and they won't stop. No! You shouldn't have tried to start a landslide. This was a bad idea!
ReadingMnemonic: You think you finally got the earth to <kanji>shake</kanji> but when you look down it's just your <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Darn you shins, I thought I made some progress. Hey wait... why won't you stop?!
MeaningMnemonic: You're trying to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to cause a <radical>landslide</radical> so you have to <kanji>shake</kanji> the earth. You probably won't succeed, but the best you can do is to shake your hands on the dirt as hard as you can. It looks like you're trying to <kanji>wave</kanji> at the ground now.

MeaningHint: Sweat really does feel like a tsunami though. Wow, it's dry and hot out right now!
ReadingHint: The one place you wish sweat wouldn't go, but somehow it always does: the ass.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sweat</kanji> isn't just on your face, it's also dripping down your <reading>ass</reading> (あせ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> going down your <radical>dry</radical> face is just <kanji>sweat</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the dirt-covered head popping up again and again. How can it move under the ground like that if it's just a head? You decide to get closer this time, and for once it speaks: "Get me out of the ground!"
ReadingHint: Imagine how dirty you and your saw are after all this. At least you could help this cyborg with your saw. "Thanks again," it says, then runs off shaking the dirt out of its hair.
ReadingMnemonic: The dirt-covered head asks for help <kanji>again</kanji> and again, but you've only got a <reading>saw</reading> (さ). So you start digging with it, scraping the dirt away, until you see this head has the body of a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい)!
MeaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>ground</radical>, a <radical>head</radical> covered in <radical>dirt</radical> emerges... <kanji>again</kanji>! It's not the first time you've seen this buried head, nor is it the last. This dirt-covered head hides beneath the ground wherever you go, and keeps popping up to scare you again and again.

MeaningHint: Look around you. Other women are adjoining their fingers and standing with you. There's hundreds, no, thousands of you standing together.
ReadingHint: Picture a bunch of women standing together. They're holding hands in sets, adjoined together.
ReadingMnemonic: All the women <kanji>adjoin</kanji> in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). Sets of two, of course. One woman with another woman, standing in unison.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> touch as you <radical>stand</radical> with a <radical>woman</radical> and <kanji>adjoin</kanji> yourselves. You are united, you are one.

MeaningHint: Imagine this animal and this insect. Let’s say it’s a flea and a dog. Imagine them going everywhere together. To the park, getting ice cream, even the drive in movie theater. They’re inseparable.
ReadingHint: Picture this animal with the insect on his head doing a Sudoku. See the insect hopping around on the animals head shouting answers and suggestions into the animal’s ear. How cute.
ReadingMnemonic: One activity that the animal can never do <kanji>alone</kanji> is solve Su<reading>doku</reading> (どく) puzzles. The insect always has to help him out with them, and together they are an unstoppable Sudoku solving team.
MeaningMnemonic: There once was an <radical>animal</radical> with an <radical>insect</radical> friend who felt very <kanji>alone</kanji> unless he was around. An animal and an insect are an unlikely pairing, but these two get along delightfully and hate to be alone without the other.

MeaningHint: Imagine what your toe tastes like. Or actually taste it, I don't care. What do the taste and texture of it say about you, about your future fortune?
ReadingHint: Imagine getting your fortune told by a centaur fortune teller. Do you trust this mythical beast to know your future? Watch him manipulate his crystal ball as his hooves stomp the ground beneath him.
ReadingMnemonic: To <kanji>occupy</kanji> some free time you have, you go and get your <kanji>fortune</kanji> told at a fortune teller. Your fortune is that you’ll have an encounter with a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) in the near future. Just then you realize that the fortune teller is actually a centaur! The fortune has come true!
MeaningMnemonic:
The  <radical>fortune</radical> radical and the <kanji>fortune</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! This can also mean <kanji>occupy</kanji>, maybe because learning your fortune shows you how you're going to occupy your life. Or how it will occupy you…

You haven't seen the radical in a while, so here's a mnemonic to help you remember the kanji: Put your toe in your mouth to learn your <kanji>fortune</kanji>. The taste will tell you everything you need to know about your fortune. But your toe has to <kanji>occupy</kanji> your mouth first for it to work.

MeaningHint: Don't let your spirits droop — kanji can be tough to learn, but you're doing great!
ReadingHint: Imagine setting your bum in a droopy rubber swing seat. Feel it droop down a little lower as you put your weight into it and let your legs dangle. Ah, such comfort.
ReadingMnemonic: You know what's better when it <kanji>droop</kanji>s a little? A <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい). Those swings with the droopy rubber seats are so comfortable to sit on and <kanji>dangle</kanji> your legs off of — way better than the rigid wooden ones.
MeaningMnemonic: This kanji comes straight from the <radical>droopy</radical> radical. What do droopy things do? They <kanji>droop</kanji>. Oh, and sometimes they <kanji>dangle</kanji> too.

MeaningHint: Something about the way they're beckoning you with their fingers is really enticing. You can't help but walk toward them.
ReadingHint: Even if the Shougun didn't have an army that would crush you if you didn't come when he called, you'd go anyway. Those beckoning fingers are just so... weirdly enticing.
ReadingMnemonic: The person <kanji>beckon</kanji>ing you is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). You can't help but obey the Shougun's beckoning call.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone is reaching out with their <radical>fingers</radical> to <radical>call</radical> for you. They <kanji>beckon</kanji> you closer, this way.

MeaningHint: When you think about it, a comb is like a ladder. So if you cut all the rungs off with your weapon and put them on the ground, they turn into stairs!
ReadingHint: Imagine standing at the top of the stairs you made yourself, looking down at the entire town below you, as the light of dawn breaks and fills everything before you with light. And now more people can see this beautiful sight if they climb your steps.
ReadingMnemonic: You slowly work to create these <kanji>stairs</kanji>, you have to slice up a lot of combs, and at <reading>dawn</reading> (だん) they are done!
MeaningMnemonic: Cut this <radical>comb</radical> up with your <radical>weapon</radical> and turn it into the <kanji>steps</kanji> for some <kanji>stairs</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine having a whole rice paddy in your stomach. A rice paddy the size of the moon. Your stomach hurts a lot. You feel like you’re going to throw up.
ReadingHint: This eagle saw your stomach distress and decided to help by making you barf. Hear the screech of the eagle as it swoops down and rams you in the stomach.
ReadingMnemonic: From the sky, an eagle swoops down and flies right into your distended <kanji>stomach</kanji>. Bam! The <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) hits your stomach and you vomit up the entire moon sized rice paddy.
MeaningMnemonic: If you eat a <radical>rice paddy</radical> the size of the <radical>moon</radical>, your <kanji>stomach</kanji> is going to hurt. All the rice in a rice paddy the size of the moon is way too much rice for anyone to consume. If you do, your stomach is going to ache like crazy.

MeaningHint: Look down at your perfect belly. It's looking back up at you.
ReadingHint: Forget to eat lunch? Belly grumbles, "F**k you." Call yourself fat and say you need to lose weight? Belly cries, "F**k you!" Be nice to your belly.
ReadingMnemonic: If you don't treat your belly with respect, when you look down at it, it'll yell, "F**k (ふく) you!" Whoa, looks like someone's got an upset stomach.
MeaningMnemonic: Inside this <radical>moon</radical> is a <radical>black hole</radical>... haha just kidding, it's your <kanji>belly</kanji>! It's beautiful and round like the moon and it eats up everything you give it like a black hole. What a good <kanji>stomach</kanji> you have!

MeaningHint: Sick people shouldn't have to do things, of course they're in pain. You shouldn't let your mama do anything if she's in pain, do it for her!
ReadingHint: You finish doing your tasks and hers too, and you ask her, "How is the pain now, Mama?" "THE PAIN IS TOO MUCH!" she cries. Oh mama.
ReadingMnemonic: Your mama's <kanji>pain</kanji> is <reading>too</reading> (つう) much for her to handle, so now you have to do all of her tasks too.
MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>sick</radical> <radical>mama</radical> tries to do a <radical>task</radical> she's filled with <kanji>pain</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture the roots raising up a little bit from the ground and tripping up your scooter. They reject you, so you should just retreat.
ReadingHint: You didn't retreat fast enough, the roots are rejecting your life, and strangling you with your own tie.
ReadingMnemonic: You're trying to <kanji>retreat</kanji> by scooting backwards, but the roots grab onto your <reading>tie</reading> (たい). You try to pull your tie off but it's just getting tighter and tighter around your neck.
MeaningMnemonic: If you're on your <radical>scooter</radical> and you run over a <radical>root</radical>, you'll need to <kanji>retreat</kanji>. The roots have obviously <kanji>reject</kanji>ed you, they want you to leave, so you should.

MeaningHint: Look at this flag telling you to yield. Now look through the exit right by the flag. You can see a bunch of people out there walking. Imagine standing by the exit, yielding to the other people until you have an opening. Hear the hustle and bustle of the people as you wait.
ReadingHint: Darn coots! Forget the yield flag, it’s time to exit. Imagine busting out of the exit, punching and pushing your way through the old coots. Hear them scream and shout “Augh! My hip!” as you plow through them.
ReadingMnemonic: Even though the flag told you to <kanji>yield</kanji>, you’re starting to lose your patience. The people walking outside the exit are all a bunch of crazy old <reading>coots</reading> (くつ)! These crazy coots keep walking right in front of the door, getting in the way. The old coots aren’t even going anywhere, they just keep looping around to obstruct the way!
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>flag</radical> by the <radical>exit</radical> letting you know to <kanji>yield</kanji>. There’s cross traffic just outside the exit, and the flag lets you know that you need to yield to them.

MeaningHint: Picture your soul curled up inside this treasure chest that's just sitting out on the grass. Anyone could open it up and look inside, and this worries you quite a bit!
ReadingHint: You worry about what you'll find if you look directly at your soul. But you also worry about what will happen if someone else looks. So you're stuck in a constant state of worrying: nah or yah?!
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, you also <kanji>worry</kanji> about opening it yourself. You stand in front of the chest and think, "Should I open it? <reading>Nah, yah</reading> (なや). Wait. Nah. Yah. Dang, you can't make up your mind.
MeaningMnemonic: If your <radical>soul</radical> is lying in the <radical>grass</radical> in a <radical>treasure chest</radical>, you're going to <kanji>worry</kanji> about it. It's just sitting there, out in the open, waiting for anyone to come and open it.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself in your spare time, sitting on this stool in a cage in the sun. Feel the warmth of the sun on your body and feel the metal bars of the cage holding you in. You try to forget about your situation by combing your hair with the comb. The comb radical is a little modified, so be sure to remember that you made this comb yourself and it’s a little wonky.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself in this cage. You’re going to be there for life because you’d stolen too many cars. Feel the tears run down your face as you continue to comb your hair. You’re going to die in this cage.
ReadingMnemonic: So how did you get in this cage? Well, before you used to comb your hair in your <kanji>spare time</kanji>, you used to steal <reading>ca</reading>rs (か). You’d stolen many a car in your day, but you finally got caught in the act of stealing a car, and now you’re spending all of your spare time in that cage, just combing your hair.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>sun</radical> while holding a <radical>comb</radical> in a <radical>cage</radical> and sitting on a <radical>stool</radical>, you comb your hair in your <kanji>spare time</kanji>. Since you’re in a cage, you actually fashioned this comb yourself, so it’s a little messed up, missing a prong. You’re working on improving it in your spare time, and you also use it to style your mop.

MeaningHint: Imagine this drunkard bipping and bopping to the sound of the music as he weaves this thread through his clothes to repair them.
ReadingHint: Imagine this drunkard, and his chic but shabby appearance. Let's hope he can repair that chic outfit and go back to being his usual chic self.
ReadingMnemonic: This drunkard is really <reading>chic</reading> (しき), but he's just fallen on hard times and has been hitting the bottle pretty hard lately. The piece of clothing he's using the thread to weave back together is one of his fancy chic outfits.
MeaningMnemonic: Taking a <radical>thread</radical> and moving to the <radical>sound</radical> of the music, a <radical>drunkard</radical> makes a <kanji>weave</kanji> through some fabric. He’s using the thread to weave his clothes back together after he damaged them in a fall down the stairs. You know, because he’s a drunkard.

MeaningHint: "Shellfish used to be money! People used to lend them to each other all the time!" you say to your friend. "Hmm... okay, I guess so," they say skeptically.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself in this new car that you got with the money your friend lent you. Hear the roar of the engine and feel the wind in your hair.
ReadingMnemonic: You want your friend to <kanji>lend</kanji> you some money so you can buy yourself a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). You need money and a car a lot more than you need this pile of shellfish, so you’re happy to put them up as collateral for the loan.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>substitute</radical> some <radical>shellfish</radical> for collateral so your friend will <kanji>lend</kanji> you some money. Normally he would ask for something more valuable, but you’ve convinced him to substitute these shellfish for what he would normally take as collateral when lending to people.

MeaningHint: Usually there aren't paved areas inside areas of rice. You must be really lost if you ended up here with your scooter.
ReadingHint: How you got so lost that you ended up on an old ship from 1620 is beyond my understanding. But you did it.
ReadingMnemonic: You got so <kanji>lost</kanji> and went so <kanji>astray</kanji> you ended up on the <reading>May</reading>flower  (めい), a ship that eventually took you across the Atlantic Ocean. Now you've really, really gone astray.
MeaningMnemonic: If you're riding your <radical>scooter</radical> through some <radical>rice</radical>, it's likely you've gone <kanji>astray</kanji>. Also you might be <kanji>lost</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture this misguided drunkard. His whole deal is messed up.
ReadingHint: Did I do it? Did I figure out the kid lingo? Am I cool? Or am I just another wack, misguided adult?
ReadingMnemonic: Nowadays we don't say <kanji>misguided</kanji>, we say <reading>wack</reading> (わく). Like, damn that drunkard is so messed up, he's wack.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> who has his <radical>mouth</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical>, has a <radical>heart</radical> that is <kanji>misguided</kanji>. First of all, he tries to drink his problems away, but worse, he's so drunk he's trying to drink out of the ground.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself saying out loud how you've left grain on the stairs, trying to lure people in. You're surprised people are actually falling for this.
ReadingHint: Imagine the youths just loitering in the stairs having eaten all the grain and taking up all the space making it impossible for anyone else to come in.
ReadingMnemonic: You've <kanji>invite</kanji>d people in by leaving grain on the stairs but some <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) have ended up eating all the grain.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> you've left some <radical>grain</radical> on the <radical>stairs</radical>. This is how you <kanji>invite</kanji> people into your place.

MeaningHint: Imagine this dog going to the capital and getting a position. The poor dog looks pretty messed up though. The radical for dog in this kanji is a bit modified, so just remember that the dog in this story is all messed up.
ReadingHint: Watch the broken dog settle in to his new shoes in his new home in the capital. The old dog can finally enjoy life!
ReadingMnemonic: In order to help the dog <kanji>get a position</kanji>, the capital cobbler made him a pair of nice <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). The shoes help him walk on his hurt legs, but more importantly, they boost his confidence and make him feel ready for any position.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>capital</radical>, even a broken <radical>dog</radical> can <kanji>get a position</kanji>. That's because the capital has so many jobs available that even a poor, broken dog can find a place to work.

MeaningHint: You better think long and hard about where you think they should visit before you say so. They're relying on you.
ReadingHint: Your home is just over that direction, and it's your favorite place to visit, so why wouldn't this stranger want to visit your home too?! So come and visit my home, random person!
ReadingMnemonic: You end up saying, "You should <kanji>visit</kanji> my <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう)." How kind of you!
MeaningMnemonic: Someone just asked you to <radical>say</radical> which <radical>direction</radical> they should <kanji>visit</kanji>.

MeaningHint: This woman is so angry! Pretend you are the woman. Sit on your stool and feel your heart, painful in your chest, as you fume and think about how angry you are.
ReadingHint: Imagine your dog (or somebody else's dog) putting his furry, warm head in your lap. Feel how comforting it is. Are you still feeling angry?
ReadingMnemonic: As you're sitting on that stool feeling <kanji>angry</kanji>, your <reading>do</reading>g (ど) comes up to console you. He nuzzles his head into your lap, and you start to feel the anger melt away.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> with a broken <radical>heart</radical> is very <kanji>angry</kanji>. She had her heart broken by some jerk and now she’s sitting on the stool, just stewing over the guy and how angry she is with him.

MeaningHint: Feel the air whoosh past your face as you shoot off the end of the long slide and ascend into the air. It's starting to get hot and the sun is getting closer!
ReadingHint: As you ascend, look back through tear-filled eyes and see the Shougun staring up at you. He nods slightly. You've pleased him.
ReadingMnemonic: You need to <kanji>ascend</kanji> into the sky and toward the sun because the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) said so. He's testing out some space travel technology (the twenty story slide) and you're his guinea pig.
MeaningMnemonic: You want to go to the <radical>sun</radical> so you set up a <radical>slide</radical> <radical>twenty</radical> stories tall so you can <kanji>ascend</kanji> into the heavens.

MeaningHint: You are one of these peoples, so imagine yourself closing your eyes and sleeping. Go ahead and do it right now. Close one eye, and then the other. Now imagine yourself sleeping.
ReadingHint: Those other peoples were really mean to you. Imagine all the mean things they were saying to you. What did they say? Think about all these mean things as you close your eyes and drift off to sleep. Also keep in mind that this kanji has the same reading as 民, which is included in this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: The reason you want to <kanji>sleep</kanji> is because all the other peoples were <reading>mean</reading> (みん) to you. They were so mean that the only way you could get over it was to close your eyes and sleep. You just want to sleep.
MeaningMnemonic: Closing each <radical>eye</radical> is something that the <radical>peoples</radical> do before they <kanji>sleep</kanji>. The peoples can’t sleep with both eyes open and they can’t sleep with just one eye open either. Peoples have to close each eye before they can sleep.

MeaningHint: You're so drowsy that your eyelids have drooped all the way down to the floor. Be careful not to step on them as you drowsily make your way to bed.
ReadingHint: Feel yourself getting drowsier and drowsier as the swing gently rocks you to sleep. Hopefully it's one of those swings with the leg holes so you don't fall out as you're drifting off.
ReadingMnemonic: The reason you're so <kanji>drowsy</kanji> is because you're on a swing (すい). A swing works like a baby cradle — it rocks you and makes you feel drowsy right away!
MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>eye</radical>s get <radical>droopy</radical>, it means you're getting <kanji>drowsy</kanji>. Go get some shut-eye, you droopy-eyed drowsy person.

MeaningHint: If you know all of the symptoms for each and every sickness you'd be able to cure them all!
ReadingHint: You get all up close and personal with the Shougun. Hmm, his symptoms are watery eyes, stuffy nose, angry shouting (wait no, that's not a symptom, is it?). Don't mess up!
ReadingMnemonic: You need to figure out the <kanji>symptom</kanji>s the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) is having. This is really serious too, so you better be correct about which sickness he has. If something bad happens to the Shougun it will be off with your head.
MeaningMnemonic: When someone is <radical>sick</radical> in order to diagnose the <radical>correct</radical> cause and treatment, you need to check their <kanji>symptom</kanji>s. You have to be especially careful too, because if you get a symptom wrong, you could kill them!

MeaningHint: Imagine pulling this thread and feeling your towel tighten around you. It's getting so tight that you can barely stand properly, which is probably why the stand radical looks a little weird here.
ReadingHint: You really don't need to do this, you know — eating tater tots isn't a sin. Tighten that towel just enough to be snug and comfy, and keep enjoying your taters. No one's judging you.
ReadingMnemonic: You're <kanji>tighten</kanji>ing your towel because you've been eating a lot of <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots lately. You want to keep a slim figure, and rather than give up your beloved tater tots, you've just opted to constrict yourself with this towel, corset-style.
MeaningMnemonic: You pull on a <radical>thread</radical> as you <radical>stand</radical> with a <radical>towel</radical> wrapped around you. This allows you to <kanji>tighten</kanji> the towel.

MeaningHint: Look at how white the scooter is. It urges you to use it. It compels you. You feel coerced...
ReadingHint:
Really all marketing, advertising, and the like is just a hack of your brain.

Hint: If you see 白 in a kanji, there's a good likelihood the reading will be はく.
ReadingMnemonic: When they <kanji>urge</kanji>, <kanji>compel</kanji> and <kanji>coerce</kanji> you like this, they are <reading>hack</reading>ing (はく) your brain.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>scooter</radical> is <radical>white</radical>. This color will <kanji>urge</kanji> and <kanji>compel</kanji> you to use it. Scooter companies use colors to get you to do what they want. Some would say they are trying to <kanji>coerce</kanji> you, even.

MeaningHint: You start with a sheet of leather. Now imagine yourself going through the process of changing them to shoes. Smell the leather and feel it, too.
ReadingHint: Imagine some of these old coots busting into a modern shoe store and kicking over all the display stands and setting everything on fire because they hate non leather shoes so much. Hear the old coots screaming and babbling nonsense as they destroy the store.
ReadingMnemonic: Some <kanji>shoes</kanji> aren’t made from leather anymore these days, but you know who always wears shoes made of leather? Old <reading>coots</reading> (くつ). Old coots are so old that the only shoes they wore growing up were made from leather so they refuse to wear any shoes that aren’t made from leather. Crazy old coots.
MeaningMnemonic: Take your <radical>leather</radical> and <radical>change</radical> it into <kanji>shoes</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine your farming land absolutely covered with this dense layer of silt from the tsunami, but then seeing your crops sprouting up one after another, thicker than ever. It’s all very thick and dense, we think you get the point.
ReadingHint: You can always rely on Nostradamus’ predictions hitting the mark, even when it comes to thickly-layered-silt-bringing tsunamis. Good ol’ reliable Nostradamus.
ReadingMnemonic: Know who’s likely responsible for this <kanji>dense</kanji>, <kanji>thick</kanji> layer of silt? <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう). He’s the one who urged you to buy this farmland in the first place, likely because he predicted the tsunami would bring this thick layer of silt.
MeaningMnemonic: After a <radical>tsunami</radical> hit your <radical>farming</radical> land, you're gonna see a lot of <kanji>dense</kanji> silt left behind. This <kanji>thick</kanji> layer really enriches the soil, and your crops will come out tasting extra thick and dense in flavor.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself at the top of the mountain, holding the rake in your hands. Look over the edge. Feel the sinking feeling in your stomach as you realize how high up you really are.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself at the edge of this mountain getting your tan on. Feel the sun beating down on you and feel the fresh mountain breeze blowing over your body. It’s very relaxing.
ReadingMnemonic: You set up this stand at the top of the mountain so you could give a speech to the world. But you need to look your best, so you decide to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). You lie next to the <kanji>edge</kanji> of the mountain, and prepare to tan. Everyone likes a person with a healthy tan.
MeaningMnemonic: Setting up a <radical>stand</radical> on the top of a <radical>mountain</radical>, you <radical>rake</radical> all the rocks to the <kanji>edge</kanji> of it. You want to clear the area around your stand, so you get your rake out and you rake all the stones and debris away from the stand and toward the edge of the mountain top. It also creates a small barrier around the edge as well.

MeaningHint: How are you going to approach this weirdly specific, extreme task? Dig a hole using the stool and then plant the trees with your mouth? Good luck figuring this one out! You're in a weird underground prison for a reason, after all.
ReadingHint: Goku barely manages to cloak you before the prison guards realize what's going on. With this cloak, you'll be planting trees with your mouth and stool like no one else before! Hooray!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>extreme</kanji> challenge could only be solved by one person: <reading>Goku</reading> (ごく) from Dragon Ball Z! He has a special cloak that'll allow you to plant trees in record time, so he quickly <reading>cloaks you</reading> (きょく) to help with your extreme task.
MeaningMnemonic: There aren't many <radical>tree</radical>s in your under<radical>ground</radical> <radical>prison</radical>, so you've been given a task: using only your <radical>mouth</radical> and a <radical>stool</radical>, plant as many in the <radical>ground</radical> as you can. It's an <kanji>extreme</kanji> challenge!

MeaningHint: Okay, so it might not be legal to take the sidewalk route depending on where you live, but physically, you can do whatever you want because scooters are small.
ReadingHint: Psh, whatever. They aren't your toes, and this is the fastest route! You'll name it the toe route and do whatever you want.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to take the fastest <kanji>route</kanji>, since you have so many options, and drive on the sidewalk. However, in doing so you run over every single <reading>toe</reading> (と) on every single person trying to walk there.
MeaningMnemonic: When you ride a <radical>scooter</radical> you'll have a <radical>surplus</radical> of possible <kanji>route</kanji>s. Not only can you drive on any road, but you can ride on the sidewalk too!

MeaningHint: If you want to be healthy too, just do yoga and rub this healthy brush all over your bod.
ReadingHint: Imagine Ken, the picture of samurai health, doing yoga and getting that brush all up in those bits.
ReadingMnemonic: Not everyone wants to be <kanji>healthy</kanji>, but <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai sure does. He needs to be ready at a moment's notice so he can do tasks for other people. He's at every class, rubbing himself down with the brush.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of your <radical>yoga</radical> class uses a special <radical>brush</radical> to stay <kanji>healthy</kanji>. She rubs the brush on her long arms and legs, and she never ever gets sick.

MeaningHint: Imagine the sense of peace you'd feel in a place like this. The sound of the water, the shade of the canopy… Whoever was here must have felt at ease as they wrote or drew. But will they come back for their brush?
ReadingHint: Finding you there, こういち invites you spend some time under the canopy with him, practicing kanji together with his brush. Why not? Seems peaceful.
ReadingMnemonic: It turns out this place of <kanji>ease</kanji> and <kanji>peace</kanji> was created by <reading>こう</reading>いち, who suddenly comes in looking for the brush he left.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>canopy</radical> under which someone has left a <radical>brush</radical> (in its 肀 form) next to the <radical>water</radical>, creating a scene of such <kanji>ease</kanji> and <kanji>peace</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Feel free to walk around the Good Building. There are no things or people, except for that one guy. Who is that guy.
ReadingHint: You try to talk to the guy, poke him, and even yell at him. But, all he does is hand you some fish roe. When you take it, he offers you more fish roe. What's up with this guy?
ReadingMnemonic: You talk to that <kanji>guy</kanji>. The only thing he does is offer you <reading>roe</reading> (ろう).
MeaningMnemonic: Inside the "<radical>Good</radical> <radical>Building</radical>" there's a single <kanji>guy</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Have you ever been away from your village? Maybe you're far away right now, studying Japanese alone. Can't you feel your body longing for your soul back home?
ReadingHint: You long for a cougar to leave you alone! You yearn for a cougar to bother someone else!
ReadingMnemonic: No matter how much you <kanji>long for</kanji> it, you can't go back because <reading>a cougar</reading> (あこが) is in the way. Whenever you try to return to your village to be reunited with the soul you yearn for, a cougar is back, and won't let you in.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> <radical>stand</radical>s in the <radical>village</radical> you <kanji>long for</kanji>. It's the village you're from, and no matter how far you go, no matter how old you get, your soul is still there, and you <kanji>yearn</kanji> for it.

MeaningHint: Imagine riding your scooter to the barracks. It literally drops you on the ground when you get there, and then drives in reverse back the opposite way you came.
ReadingHint: If you don’t know what Gak is, check out this Gak commercial. The 90s were basically the heyday of non-Newtonian fluids masquerading as toys. Don't you wish you could reverse time and play with Gak again?
ReadingMnemonic: After you <kanji>reverse</kanji> in the <kanji>opposite</kanji> direction, you find a bunch of <reading>Gak</reading> (ぎゃく) covering your scooter wheels. The soldiers must have booby-trapped their barracks with Gak. You're able to reverse out of the barracks, but the Gak has gunked up your wheels so bad that they only go in reverse now. Weird… Guess you'll be going in reverse forever.
MeaningMnemonic: If you ride your <radical>scooter</radical> while wearing <radical>horns</radical> in a <radical>barracks</radical>, you're going to get stuck and have to <kanji>reverse</kanji> out of there in the <kanji>opposite</kanji> direction.

ReadingHint: See Little Kyoto after the giant rampaged through it. It looks even smaller and more pathetic than ever. Look at all the smoldering rubble and smell the smoke with your nose.
ReadingMnemonic: Just recently, a <kanji>giant</kanji> rampaged through Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ), so now it's even smaller than before. Poor Little Kyoto.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>giant</radical> radical and the <kanji>giant</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Massive!

MeaningHint: The canopy just protects your car from the elements as it sits in storage for long periods of time.
ReadingHint: Imagine a kid sneaking into your canopy to steal your car that's in storage. Maybe it's two 子s, one stacked on the other with a trench coat. One 子 works the pedals while the other 子 works the steering.
ReadingMnemonic: Your car is in this <kanji>storage</kanji> <kanji>warehouse</kanji>, just sitting there. A <reading>子</reading> (こ) notices this, and decides to take your car on a joyride. You won't notice, right? You never drive it.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>canopy</radical> is over your <radical>car</radical> because your car is in <kanji>storage</kanji> in a large <kanji>storehouse</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself getting new legs. Legs that belong to a child. They’re limber and young and full of energy! Feel your legs of a child underneath you!
ReadingHint: Say back to Jesus, “I’m not your child Jesus! And you’re just jealous of my new legs! You wish you had the legs of a child, Jesus! Your legs are like super old!” Actually say this out loud, and remember how Jesus wishes he could be a child again too.
ReadingMnemonic: As you delight in your new legs, <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) descends from the sky. “My <kanji>child</kanji>,” he says, “why have you pointed the stick at the sun and wished for the legs of a child? You were already perfect, just as all of God’s children are perfect.”
MeaningMnemonic: Pointing your magic solar powered wishing <radical>stick</radical> at the <radical>sun</radical>, you attain the <radical>legs</radical> of a <kanji>child</kanji>. You’re old and want young legs to carry you around, so you took your wishing stick that’s powered by the sun, and you wished for the legs of a child.

MeaningHint: Making things cool and cold are pretty much the only orders you'll hear from ice. Ice is very simple in that regard.
ReadingHint:
Imagine a cold ray coming out of your ray gun. Anything it touches becomes cold. "Stay cool, bird boy," you say.

Hint: When you see the radical <radical>orders</radical> (令) in a kanji, it's fairly likely the reading will be れい.
ReadingMnemonic: How will you make things <kanji>cool</kanji> and <kanji>cold</kanji>? You'll use a special <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun that shoots a cold ray.
MeaningMnemonic: You got your <radical>ice</radical> <radical>orders</radical>. Of course, your ice orders are to make things <kanji>cool</kanji> and <kanji>cold</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine this super frozen ice from the east. You’ve never seen anything quite like it before. It’s so frozen, it’s like it was frozen once and then frozen again. Twice frozen. Feel the ice and how freezing cold it is. It’s super cold, guys.
ReadingHint: Imagine being in とうきょう and seeing all this frozen ice. Hear people speaking Japanese all around you as you touch and feel the freezing twice-frozen ice.
ReadingMnemonic: Japan is a country in the east, and this ice was <kanji>frozen</kanji> in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. とうきょう is really good at freezing frozen things, so it makes sense that this frozen ice came from there.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Ice</radical> from the <radical>east</radical> is <kanji>frozen</kanji>. Well, of course, all ice is frozen, but ice from the east is like, really super frozen.

MeaningHint:
Infants poop a lot. There's poop everywhere. The powerful aroma overwhelms you. Eww.

Also refers to childhood, when you still have some of your poop powers, but they're already starting to go away.
ReadingHint: See this infant gobbling up all the yogurt he can get his grubby little hands on. Soon, he begins to poop all over the place. Hear the infant screaming, "Goo-goo! Yogurt poop power! I'm a baby! Wahhhh!!"
ReadingMnemonic: In <kanji>infancy</kanji>, one eats a lot of soft foods, such as <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Yogurt is the food item above all others that grants superior pooping power.
MeaningMnemonic: Super <radical>poop</radical> is a super <radical>power</radical> you have in <kanji>infancy</kanji>. Those in infancy have the power to poop at will, whenever and wherever they please. Infancy poop power!

MeaningHint: Imagine an immature turkey complaining at you until you give it grains. Now it's taking selfies with the grains and posting pics to Instagram and hashtagging them #GRAINLIFE.
ReadingHint: Imagine a bunch of immature kids and turkeys eating cheese together.
ReadingMnemonic: Another thing <kanji>immature</kanji> turkeys (and people) love is <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Most of us non-immature people and birds still like cheese, but if you didn't have an immature cheese phase as a kid or teen, you won't be able to like cheese as a mature old person.
MeaningMnemonic: Only give <radical>grain</radical>s to a <radical>turkey</radical> if it's <kanji>immature</kanji>. Adult turkeys should be eating healthier things, like insects and grass. But immature turkeys don't care if they get fat, so they whine and whine and whine until you give them grains.

MeaningHint: Look at this winter table. How are you ever going to deal with it? Just think of your favorite warm food and then imagine it getting cold. Deal with it, dog.
ReadingHint: Feel the relief of getting rid of this table. No more having to deal with it anymore. The circus show can have it.
ReadingMnemonic: This winter table is something you have to <kanji>deal with</kanji>, sure, but what if you just gave it to a circus <reading>show</reading> (しょ)? They like weird stuff like this.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>winter</radical> <radical>table</radical> would be an odd thing to <kanji>deal with</kanji>. Winter can be hard to deal with on its own, but turn your table into winter? Just dealing with your food getting cold would be the worst!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself at this specialty exhibition. Feel the weight of the cross shaped badge on your chest as you continue gambling at the exhibition.
ReadingHint: Hack the gambling machines. Imagine the sounds of the exhibition around you as you work to hack the machines. Look at the computer parts and cables as you work.
ReadingMnemonic: You’re not very good at <kanji>gambling</kanji>, but you are pretty good at cheating. You <reading>hack</reading> (はく) the specialty <kanji>exhibition</kanji> gambling machines to pay out lots of cash just for you. You hack quickly and quietly, so luckily no one notices that you’ve hacked the system.
MeaningMnemonic: You wear a great big <radical>cross</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on it, and head to a <radical>specialty</radical> <kanji>exhibition</kanji> for <kanji>gambling</kanji>. The big cross and drop on your chest is your badge for being admitted into the exhibition. Everyone at the specialty exhibition has a cross and drop, and everyone is gambling.

MeaningHint: Picture this pure blue tsunami. You can see every crystal clear drop of water in it.
ReadingHint: SPLAASHH! The tsunami's pure water splits and it absorbed into your saber. Now it's pure too. And transparent!
ReadingMnemonic: Seeing this <kanji>pure</kanji> blue tsunami inspires you to pull out your glowing blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! As the pure water comes toward you, you take one, long slash!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> that's <radical>blue</radical> is <kanji>pure</kanji>. Some tsunami are other colors, like brown or red because they aren't pure, they'll full of trash and poop and other horrible things. But a blue tsunami is completely pure!

MeaningHint: Picture the tsunami purifying the land of life, and in your mind see the sword cutting clean through the thread of life.
ReadingHint: The area is SO pure that the ground absorbs the ketsup and now it's all pure... ketsup color.
ReadingMnemonic: After the tsunami pulls away, everything left is <kanji>pure</kanji> white. You hate this, so you run over and squirt <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) all over the place to make it not so pure.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> takes <radical>life</radical>, cuts it like a <radical>sword</radical> through <radical>thread</radical>, and leaves everything behind completely <kanji>pure</kanji> and <kanji>clean</kanji>. Everything is dead and gone, wiped clean off the face of the earth, thanks to the tsunami. It's back to the way it was before humans were here, completely undefiled.

MeaningHint: See this gold Wolverine holding his gold medal after getting out of the water. His dad doesn’t want to forget anything about this precious moment so he makes a record of it. Imagine him scribbling furiously in his notebook.
ReadingHint: Imagine a pitiful Wolverine crying on the ground as each rock hits him. All he wanted was some recognition from his father. Poor guy. Imagine poor Wolverine like this as his dad just looks on and writes in his notebook.
ReadingMnemonic: Since Wolverine is actually a mutant, he is soon disqualified from his gold medal. His father makes a <kanji>record</kanji> of this also. One angry fan threw a <reading>rock</reading> (ろく) at Wolverine afterwards. Then another threw a rock. And another rock. Before you knew it, everyone was throwing rocks at Wolverine. Wolverine’s dad didn’t step in to help though, he just kept keeping records.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>gold</radical> <radical>Wolverine</radical> swam in the <radical>water</radical> while his father made a <kanji>record</kanji> of it. Wolverine is gold in color because he just won the gold medal in swimming, so now he is gold. Way to go, Wolverine.

MeaningHint: Our squad's symbol is horns on a pig, and we put it on everything to show the whole world our squad owns this turf.
ReadingHint: Picture our squad. It's Koichi, Viet, the Crabigator and Tofugu. We're all wearing matching horned pig ties and walking toward you in slow motion, our squad building towering behind us with the pig smirking down at you.
ReadingMnemonic: Our <kanji>squad</kanji> also wears special <reading>tie</reading>s (たい) with our horned pig on it. And we don't care if you think that's lame, because we're A SQUAD.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> features <radical>horns</radical> on a <radical>pig</radical> to represent our <kanji>squad</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine your leader holding this stick of hair in the winter, going around mastering all kinds of skills and repairing everything in sight. What a strange stick, giving even stranger abilities to its wielder.
ReadingHint: Picture your leader again; stick in one hand, shoe in the other. He looks kind of threatening, like he's about to slap you with the stick and then throw the shoe at you. But that's what it takes for mastering certain skills. This is all for discipline’s sake.
ReadingMnemonic: What's the key to <kanji>mastering</kanji> any skill? A stick and a <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう)!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> is using a <radical>stick</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> made of <radical>hair</radical>. This is the stick of <kanji>mastering</kanji> and <kanji>repair</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Right! You needed to buy a ticket to see the gladiator! He doesn't want to kill you, he wants to check to make sure you bought a ticket.
ReadingHint: Ken takes a long time to examine your ticket. I don't think he understands that he needs a ticket to fight the gladiator. Maybe you should give him yours.
ReadingMnemonic: The gladiator happily marks your <kanji>ticket</kanji> with his blade, when <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai pushes past you. He holds up his own sword, challenging the gladiator, who just shakes his head and points at your ticket again.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>gladiator</radical> in front of you draws his <radical>sword</radical>. He points the sword at your chest and right when you think everything is about to end, you realize he's pointing the sword at the <kanji>ticket</kanji> in your hand.

MeaningHint: The lady of the house will tolerate nothing less than excellence, so get ready to sweep and re-sweep this floor till you meet your lady's expectations.
ReadingHint:
"You there — fool. Come sweep this floor again." 
"Yes, m'lady — your humble fool lives to serve, m'lady."
ReadingMnemonic: Instead of your name, the <kanji>lady</kanji> of the house just calls you "<reading>foo</reading>l" (ふ). You rarely meet her ladyship's lofty standards, so from her perspective you're just a silly nincompoop fool who can't get anything right.
MeaningMnemonic: See that <radical>woman</radical> with the <radical>broom</radical>? That's the <kanji>lady</kanji> of the house. She's getting ready to hand that broom to you — her butler — because you missed a spot over there.

MeaningHint: Remember how odd this one is?
ReadingHint: You only give this odd key to people you trust, so they can unlock your odd secrets and see how odd you are deep down.
ReadingMnemonic: You're <kanji>odd</kanji> too, just like this kanji. But you don't want anyone to know, so you lock away your <kanji>strange</kanji> habits with a <reading>key</reading> (き).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>odd</radical> radical and the <kanji>odd</kanji> kanji are the same. They're both... really <kanji>strange</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine saying this to a woman. Try and imagine her face as you tell her how peculiar she is.
ReadingHint: Who's peculiar? Me and you. Me, you — we are peculiar! Peculiar, peculiar, peculiar!
ReadingMnemonic: But when you think about it, we're all a little peculiar. Me, you, <reading>myou</reading> (みょう) everybody!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> is one of the <radical>few</radical> <kanji>peculiar</kanji> things in the world. Everything but a woman can be explained and made sense of. There are only a few truly peculiar things in this world, with woman being one of them.

MeaningHint: You know what would make learning this kanji lovely? Studying your radicals.
ReadingHint: Try to imagine feeling this way about your ray gun. It's so lovely.
ReadingMnemonic: "<kanji>Lovely</kanji>" is the name of my <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun. It's more lovely than any other thing.
MeaningMnemonic: If you know the radical meaning <radical>lovely</radical>, you know the kanji meaning <kanji>lovely</kanji> as well!

MeaningHint: What other delicate things and tiny changes would you notice on the mountain? A little bird alighting in a tree, or the snowflakes it shakes loose from a branch? That's for you to decide, but imagine it vividly whatever it is.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself looking at the bee, thinking how each hair on its body is so delicate and tiny. Maybe it has a delicate aroma to go with it. A slight smell of honey?
ReadingMnemonic: Ah! Of course! The <kanji>delicate</kanji> bumble <reading>be</reading>e (び). Slightly out of place in winter, but this tiny creature is your idea of delicate. It lands on your finger, and you look at every tiny fiber of its being.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> on the <radical>mountain</radical>, reading off the digits of <radical>pi</radical> on this <radical>winter</radical> morning. The cold mountain air is so <kanji>delicate</kanji>, and changes only in very <kanji>tiny</kanji> ways — just like pi when you add another digit to it.

MeaningHint: You pick up the horns off the ground and put them on your head, then eat some fins off the plate. This is just one of many benefits to living here. You'll never go hungry!
ReadingHint: Okay, so using the 駅 is probably a better benefit than eating cold fins off a plate on the ground, but you take what you can get. They're all free!
ReadingMnemonic: Another <kanji>benefit</kanji> is free <reading>駅</reading> (えき) usage! Whenever you come back home from places on the train, this 駅 is completely free.
MeaningMnemonic: There are some <radical>horns</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> next to <radical>fins</radical> on a <radical>plate</radical> that are meant to <kanji>benefit</kanji> everyone. If you're a member of this town, one of your benefits is this little set up.

MeaningHint: Picture the grain in your head with a time-lapse of one evening after another happening in the background. Slowly, the grain shifts.
ReadingHint: Picture the eagle flying down on the second evening, scattering the grains it doesn't eat, shifting their location.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>shift</kanji> is caused by the <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) that wants to eat the grains. Every other evening, the eagle swoops down, shifts the grains (eating a few) and then flies off.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>grain</radical>, from one <radical>evening</radical> to the next <radical>evening</radical>, experiences a <kanji>shift</kanji>. After two evenings, the grain has shifted from one location to another.

MeaningHint: Of course, you need to imagine pulling the Grain Mouth King's finger, to some extent. You can't just read what we write for you, without going to the imagination zone. Pull that finger real hard a couple times, and watch an extra large purge of grain pour forth.
ReadingHint: Imagine hearing the rumors that the Grain Mouth King ran out of grain, so you go check the extent of his failure yourself. But tater tots taste pretty good too. Imagine eating one of his mouth tater tots and go ahead and comment about it to him.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>extent</kanji> to which we were harvesting grain from his mouth was too great. The Grain Mouth King has run out of grain. He worries he will lose power if he has no grain to offer, so he switches to barfing out <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots instead.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Grain</radical> <radical>Mouth</radical> <radical>King</radical> pours grain from his mouth for you and his other subjects. You go to him and pull his finger. When you do this, grain pours from his mouth. But we were greedy. We didn't understand the <kanji>extent</kanji> to which we were harvesting his mouth wheat.

MeaningHint: Picture this rice, the pulsing blue spirit of each grain is glowing so bright!
ReadingHint: As you release the spirits in the rice, your saber begins to glow blue too. Their spirits are going inside your saber, making it stronger!
ReadingMnemonic:
You want to see the <kanji>spirit</kanji> up close, so you take your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい), throw the blue rice in the air, and slice it in half! The rice explodes and blue light fills everything!

Then it's gone.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Rice</radical> that is <radical>blue</radical> has a strong <kanji>spirit</kanji>. Our spirits are blue, so if you ever see rice (or other food, or even people) that's blue, you know that's their spirit radiating out!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself lining up these horns in the ground. The spikes are sharp and you poke yourself with one. Ouch. Need to be more careful as you line these up.
ReadingHint: Imagine the hay in that bull's mouth as it chomps angrily, watching you line up horns in the ground. It probably wishes you were the hay.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>line up</kanji> these horns and stick them into the ground, you notice a bull munching on <reading>hay</reading> (へい) nearby. It's staring at the horns you're lining up. Somehow it feels like it's munching that hay angrily. You gulp, looking at its horn as it grabs another mouthful of hay, and you feel pretty guilty.
MeaningMnemonic: You stick <radical>horns</radical> in the <radical>ground</radical> using them as <radical>spikes</radical> and you <kanji>line up</kanji> the horns in a straight line. Horns are pretty much the same thing as spikes once you stick them in the ground, and you line these ones up precisely for optimal protection.

MeaningHint: The constitution makes it so that everything you hold dear is locked away, nice and safe on a piece of paper, and no one can change the rules or hurt you without your say so.
ReadingHint: Picture Ken standing in front of your constitution. But keep a close watch, cause wily ol' Nic Cage is in the business of thievin' on constitutions.
ReadingMnemonic: Your country's <kanji>constitution</kanji> is being protected by none other than <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai, hero for hire.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> protecting your <radical>life</radical> and the <radical>net</radical> protecting your <radical>heart</radical> are both protected by the <kanji>constitution</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Blood shows that all the people are related. Hair shows that we're all humans. Tofu is food to all. So, as you can see, those three words describe the entire populace.
ReadingHint: If you see people in your populace without shoes, it's your job as a fellow member of society to help them get some!
ReadingMnemonic: The entire <kanji>populace</kanji> deserves <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). It's a basic thing all humans should have like flowing blood, hair (somewhere), and tofu to eat.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Blood</radical> and <radical>hair</radical> and <radical>tofu</radical> make up the <kanji>populace</kanji>. Those are the three categories the entire populace fits into, anyway.

MeaningHint: See these four people all under one makeshift umbrella. The giant hat is set atop the cross, which is what makes it kinda like an umbrella, and the four pairs of hands are all holding it upright.
ReadingHint: Imagine the four people in the umbrella hat again, only this time imagine them skipping along singing "Mi casa es su casa." It'll help you remember.
ReadingMnemonic: You know the Spanish phrase "Mi <reading>casa</reading> (かさ) es su casa"? Well, this is kind of like that, but only with a hat. And that hat is also an <kanji>umbrella</kanji>. Mi casa es yours too, because it's an umbrella and also a hat. This makes sense, trust me.
MeaningMnemonic: Using a <radical>hat</radical>, four <radical>person</radical>s with a <radical>cross</radical> make an <kanji>umbrella</kanji>. The hat is big enough to be an umbrella for all four people, so it makes sense. All four of them are religious missionaries, so that's why they're holding the cross under the "umbrella."

MeaningHint: See these things on the beach. It makes sense to see fins, as fins are usually in the ocean, so seeing them on the beach is logical. Pick up the stuff you find as you feel the beach sand between your toes.
ReadingHint: Pick up the hammer in your hand. It’s pretty heavy. The tsunami must have been very strong to pick up such a heavy hammer.
ReadingMnemonic: Walking down the <kanji>beach</kanji> a bit more, you also find a <reading>hammer</reading> (はま). Gosh, looks like the tsunami hit a workshop or a hardware store or something. First the axe, now a hammer.
MeaningMnemonic: After a <radical>tsunami</radical>, you find an <radical>axe</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> next to some <radical>fins</radical> on the <kanji>beach</kanji>. The tsunami was pretty violent, and it looks like this axe got blown into some fish and cut off their fins and now those things are just on the ground by the beach.

MeaningHint: Picture the car, weapon out the window, preparing to attack you full force!
ReadingHint: Gecki is attacking you now. He's hitting you over and over with his tiny car. He's yelling and smacking your ankles with his weapon. And all you can do is wait and let the attack happen, because you know you deserve it.
ReadingMnemonic: Right before the <kanji>attack</kanji> begins, you recognize the hand holding this weapon and driving this car. It's <reading>Gecki</reading> (げき), your long-lost gecko who you may or may not have let fly off into a really fierce tsunami. Oops. I think he may be here attacking you out of revenge.
MeaningMnemonic: If someone is coming toward you in a <radical>car</radical>, holding a <radical>weapon</radical> in their <radical>hand</radical>, they're going to <kanji>attack</kanji> you!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself walking through the winter construction site, taking out your aggression on anyone you see. You hate this winter construction!
ReadingHint:
"Aggression" is going to depend on you, so you should imagine a scene where you take your aggression out on こういち, whatever that entails.

Hint: When you see the radical <radical>construction</radical> (工) in a kanji, it will often take the こう reading.
ReadingMnemonic: You're taking your <kanji>aggression</kanji> out on everyone. Then, the sexy construction worker from WaniKani Level 1, <reading>こう</reading>いち, walks by without a shirt on. You take out all your aggression on him.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Construction</radical> in the <radical>winter</radical> leads to <kanji>aggression</kanji>. Just think about it. It's cold, it's unpleasant, and you don't want to be there. So, you start to develop an aggression towards other people working with you on this winter project.

MeaningHint: If something is a bit odd, it's usually beautiful in its own way, just like this beautiful thread.
ReadingHint: To remember that there's a beautiful thread hidden away, you tie a not-as-beautiful thread to the end of the key.
ReadingMnemonic: You take this <kanji>beautiful</kanji> thread and lock it up with a <reading>key</reading> (き). You want to keep it all to yourself, you want its beauty to be for your eyes only!
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>thread</radical> is <radical>odd</radical>, but that's what makes it <kanji>beautiful</kanji>. It's a little frayed, it's a little miscolored, but that's what makes it appealing to you.

MeaningHint: Close your eyes and hear the bog's tempting call.
ReadingHint: So that's what happened to him! If you're a youngun, just do a little Google and you'll be pretty pleased, I'm sure.
ReadingMnemonic: As you listen to the <kanji>bog</kanji>'s call you realize it looks just like the <reading>Numa</reading> (ぬま) Numa guy in your mind. You open your eyes and it's just a bog. You close your eyes, and there he is. The Numa Numa guy.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> can't resist the <radical>call</radical> of a <kanji>bog</kanji>. Whenever a tsunami is headed toward land, a bog will always call out to it, "Hey tsunami, don't you want to hit me with your water? Come this way~"

MeaningHint: You point to the gun. You point to the ground (that's where the gun goes). You point to the plate (you want it). You have to oversee everything the servant does.
ReadingHint: If anyone had to oversee a lot, it was Genghis Khan, who had one of the largest kingdoms in all of history.
ReadingMnemonic: Why must you <kanji>oversee</kanji> everything? Because you are Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん), and Genghis Khan oversees everything.
MeaningMnemonic: To the <radical>servant</radical> you say: "<radical>gun</radical>, <radical>ground</radical>, <radical>plate</radical>." It is your job to <kanji>oversee</kanji> everything they do.

MeaningHint: Imagine cutting a tree into a cup of liquid and focus on the pain in your throat when you try to actually use it. Instead you kick the tree and angrily count cups of liquid in your head, because they simply are NOT the same.
ReadingHint: Think of a haiku about a cup of liquid and a counter for cups. Use that haiku to help remember this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: Another way to remember the <kanji>counter for cups</kanji> using a <kanji>cup of liquid</kanji> would be by creating and memorizing a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい) about them. Aren’t haikus useful?
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> is <radical>not</radical> a <kanji>cup of liquid</kanji> or a <kanji>counter for cups</kanji>. Don’t get them mixed up. The last time you tried making a cup out of a tree to count your throat got filled with splinters when you used it. There wasn't even any liquid in it.

MeaningHint: Don't worry, there aren't bullets in the gun. It's an air gun! So just imagine shooting the air into your nose every morning to make sure it's nice and dry.
ReadingHint: Now the next time you manage to get your nose nice and dry, think back to our founding father Genghis Khan and thank him for where you are today.
ReadingMnemonic: This technique for a nice <kanji>dry</kanji> nose was invented by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! Well, not him, but someone who worked under him. You see, he had a bit of a damp nose problem.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>morning</radical>, you use a <radical>gun</radical> on your <radical>nose</radical> to make it <kanji>dry</kanji>.

MeaningHint: This message is brought to you by our generous sponsor, the leader of the mountain turkeys. Long may he reign.
ReadingHint: Turn your cyborg over and you'll see a stamp under one of its feet. It says, "Sponsored by" with a little picture of a turkey on top a mountain.
