MeaningMnemonic: You create a <radical>thread</radical> with someone by <radical>call</radical>ing out to them to <kanji>introduce</kanji> yourself.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Picture yourself jammed into a kayak. Your pants are bunching and sticking out the sides and top. You really don't fit in here.
ReadingMnemonic: Look down! You're <kanji>jammed in</kanji>to something. You can't move your feet. What is this?! Oh, your body's been jammed in a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>jammed in</radical> radical and the <kanji>jammed in</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Make sure you know those radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Kids can only think of oneself. Until a certain age, that's literally all they can care about.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Oneself</kanji>. Who only thinks of oneself? Every <reading>子</reading> (こ) in the world.
MeaningMnemonic: The kanji and the radical are the same, so remembering the meaning of this kanji is as simple as making sure you know the radical for <radical>oneself</radical> first! If you know the radical, you know this kanji means <kanji>oneself</kanji> as well.

MeaningHint: What will you do? You can either let go and fall off the cliff, or hold on and get bitten by the snake. Man, talk about misfortune.
ReadingHint: Imagine the glittering maze of hooves and yak horns that enclose you now. Ahhh! Then the yak pass, and you find yourself alive but beaten. Life is one misfortune after another, but misfortunes, like the yak, eventually pass…
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to let go, but at the bottom of the cliff is an even greater <kanji>misfortune</kanji>: <reading>yak</reading> (やく). A whole stampeding herd of yak is passing just as you fall. Oops! You've accidentally committed yourself to the ultimate misfortune, death by yak trampling.
MeaningMnemonic: If you're on the edge of a <radical>cliff</radical> with a <radical>snake</radical>, that's a <kanji>misfortune</kanji>. You're hanging off the edge of this cliff and you can't pull yourself back up. Then you notice a snake approaching your fingers, looking like it's gonna bite you. What a misfortune!

ReadingHint: See this turtle as he gathers energy, shaking the earth around him, gathering chi for his Kamehameha blast. He releases it in a mighty blast of energy. Kamehameha!!
ReadingMnemonic: You know Dragon Ball Z, right? Imagine this <kanji>turtle</kanji> doing a <reading>Kame</reading>hameha (かめ). The turtle charges up his energy and releases a mighty Kamehameha blast. Shwooom!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>turtle</radical> radical and the <kanji>turtle</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Nice!

MeaningHint: Pick up the shuriken and throw it into the ground. Hear it whizz through the air and then plunk into the ground. This act of aggression causes their mutual respect to waver.
ReadingHint: You give Godzilla a thumbs up, you see what he's trying to do. He looks down at you and nods. Now you have a mutual respect for one another too.
ReadingMnemonic: Since you made the shuriken attack the ground, the <kanji>mutual</kanji> respect is out of balance. This summons <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) up from the depths of the ocean once more. He always shows up when something is out of wack, to show humans the error of their ways. He picks up the shuriken and flings it into a city, which explodes, leaving a crater in the ground.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>ground</radical> and a <radical>shuriken</radical> have <kanji>mutual</kanji> respect for one another. The shuriken respects the ground because of how solid and stable it is, and the ground respects the shuriken for how pointy it is. They have great mutual respect here.

MeaningHint: Imagine creating your own sword out of a knife and a squid.
ReadingHint: Picture Ken swinging his squid sword at you. *Squish* it squirts ink on your shoulder. But it doesn't really hurt.
ReadingMnemonic: You learned how to make <kanji>sword</kanji>s this way from <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. He put his own little squid buddy on his knife and now no one realizes it's not a sword!
MeaningMnemonic: Tie a <radical>squid</radical> to a <radical>knife</radical> and you get a <kanji>sword</kanji>! Knives are too small to be considered a sword on their own. That's why you strapped a squid to it. They're the right shape, kinda pointed, so no one will know it's just a knife.

MeaningHint: Feel the shame. Feel the shame in both your ear and your heart. Imagine the things you’ve done to make you feel so much shame.
ReadingHint: Hah! What a fool you were to think you could erase your shameful past with a half-hearted attempt at the Hajj. Shame on you.
ReadingMnemonic: You must cleanse your spirit of this <kanji>shame</kanji>. You decide to complete <reading>Hajj</reading> (はじ) — you know, the Islamic pilgrimage to Mecca. However, when you get there, all the other pilgrims look at you and say, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)! You? You're a lost cause, buddy." It seems even Hajj can't save you from your shame.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>ear</radical> listens to your <radical>heart</radical> and feels <kanji>shame</kanji>. Your heart is full of shame for how you’ve lived your life and when your ear hears all the things you’ve done, your ear feels shame too.

MeaningHint: Imagine having a cedar tree in your hair. It’s just a little tree, a sapling, but it’s pretty strange and awkward. It does smell nice though. Mmm… cedar.
ReadingHint: Give Sue her Sue guitar. Hear Sue squeal with glee as she receives her Sue guitar.
ReadingMnemonic: Once the <kanji>cedar</kanji> is fully grown, you use the cedar wood to make a guitar for Sue. A <reading>Sue gui</reading>tar (すぎ), if you will. This Sue guitar was made just for Sue. She’ll love it.
MeaningMnemonic:
There’s a <radical>tree</radical> in your <radical>hair</radical>, I think it’s a <kanji>cedar</kanji>. How did that cedar tree get in your hair?

(Certain types of cedar trees have bark that comes off in fine threads, making them very hairy trees. So this kanji makes perfect sense!)

MeaningHint: Picture a bowl of soup, but now there is a cross and some tsunami water in it.
ReadingHint: You're curious so you take a sip of this jewel soup and... it's delicious! You should make more and sell it!
ReadingMnemonic: You pull the cross out of your <kanji>soup</kanji> and see that the cross' <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう) fell off and are in there now. The jewels sparkle, shine, and dissolve into the soup!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> carries a <radical>cross</radical> into your <kanji>soup</kanji>. Hey tsunami, I was eating that soup!

MeaningHint: Feel the fire all around you, the flames licking at your body. It’s really hot!
ReadingHint: Sorry, but standing in a blaze isn't the best idea. Flames are pretty efficient at ending the lives of the things in it. Maybe you should do this kanji lesson over and try not to stand in the flames next time.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>flame</kanji>s gets bigger, surround you, and then everything <reading>en</reading>ds (えん).
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Fire</radical>, <radical>fire</radical>, <kanji>flame</kanji>! There’s flames everywhere!

MeaningHint: For goodness sake! Please remember this isn't "sake" as in お酒! It's the English "sake," that rhymes with "bake," "cake," and "steak." Oops.. now I'm hungry.
ReadingHint: See this eagle as it comes to deliver your order just for your sake. Scree! Hear its wings flap and your package plop down on your porch as it's delivered.
ReadingMnemonic: For the <kanji>sake</kanji> of convenience, companies will use <reading>ea</reading>gles (い) for delivery jobs in the future. Just put in an order, and it'll be at your doorstep in no time flat. Eagles are like, super fast at delivery.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sake</radical> radical and the <kanji>sake</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Sweet.

MeaningHint: I bet you didn't know this, but a bear can boil whenever it wants. Imagine a bear heating itself up and boiling, its fur and skin bubbling and letting off steam.
ReadingHint: You can also imagine a bear graduating in the top 5-10% of its class. Or, just imagine a bear saying "Cuma here!" to you.
ReadingMnemonic:
A <kanji>bear</kanji> boiling in front of you should fill you with great honor. Or, as they say in Latin: Magna <reading>cum</reading> (くま) laude.

Get it? A little hot bear humor for you.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>ability</radical> to <radical>boil</radical> is one of the main abilities of a <kanji>bear</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine any animal that’s not a dog. Now imagine dressing it up like a dog so it doesn’t have to go to prison. Practice throwing your voice by saying “woof woof” aloud to help convince people that your animal is actually a dog.
ReadingHint: Poor little Goku doesn’t want to go to prison either so Goku does his best to act like a dog even though he isn’t. How adorable.
ReadingMnemonic: What is the name of your animal? Your animal is named <reading>Goku</reading> (ごく). Yeah, like Goku from Dragon Ball Z. You don’t want your little Goku to go to <kanji>prison</kanji>, so you say that he’s a dog.
MeaningMnemonic: You have an <radical>animal</radical> and you <radical>say</radical> it’s a <radical>dog</radical> to prevent it from going to <kanji>prison</kanji>. Dogs are the only animals that don’t have to go to prison. Your animal is not a dog, but you say it is to prevent it from going to prison.

MeaningHint: Feel how drunk you are as you look at the cross shaped bottle that once held the alcohol. See the cross start to blur in front of you from how drunk you are.
ReadingHint: Imagine that vertigo that comes over you if you lean your head back when you're drunk. It feels like leaning back on a playground swing, in a way. Oh, wait… what if we got drunk and swung on the playground swings? Double swing effect! Actually, never mind. Bad idea.
ReadingMnemonic: Everything's spinning now, you're so <kanji>drunk</kanji> it feels like you're on a <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい). Lurching back and forth in your drunkenness, feeling more and more nauseated by all this horrible swinging. Ugh.
MeaningMnemonic: You have an <radical>alcohol</radical> <radical>nine</radical> times stronger than any other drink, in a bottle shaped like a <radical>cross</radical>, and you drink it all and become <kanji>drunk</kanji>. You're like, so drunk right now from all that alcohol. Nine times more drunk than ever before.

MeaningHint: Imagine making a bunch of vinegar. You use a key to change it from alcohol to vinegar, yum!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself doing the same thing as before but you are now wearing a suit. Dang, you look good.
ReadingMnemonic: You are demonstrating this practice in front of an audience. You want them to take you seriously so you wear a <reading>su</reading>it (す). A very nice suit. Surely they will now believe that you can turn alcohol into <kanji>vinegar</kanji>.
MeaningMnemonic: To make <radical>alcohol</radical> the <radical>key</radical> is to ferment it. But, if you ferment it a certain way, it will become <kanji>vinegar</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Where did lakes come from? They are the residue of tsunamis from many moons ago.
ReadingHint: This is just a stupid story to get this 子 to stop asking "why, why, why?" about everything. Earlier, the 子 asked "where do lakes come from?" and you had to come up with a crazy story to quiet them.
ReadingMnemonic: "That's where <kanji>lake</kanji>s come from," you tell the <reading>子</reading> (こ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical>s during the <radical>old</radical> <radical>moon</radical>s (many moons ago) were responsible for the world's <kanji>lake</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: Take a piece of gold in one hand and a piece of copper in the other. Bang them together furiously as you shout. “GOLD IS NOT THE SAME AS COPPER! GOLD IS NOT THE SAME AS COPPER!”
ReadingHint: Mmm… doughnuts. Eat the doughnut and taste its yummy flavor as you look at the piece of copper. It’s shiny.
ReadingMnemonic: Smashing the <kanji>copper</kanji> against the gold has made you tired. You should eat a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう). Go get yourself a doughnut.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Gold</radical> is not the <radical>same</radical> as <kanji>copper</kanji>. They are two different metals. Gold is not the same as copper.

MeaningHint: Watch as this tsunami crashes over the land, pushing earth out of the way. Once the tsunami passes, hear the spewing geysers of oil as they erupt out of the ground.
ReadingHint: Look at how this oil has changed the world. Instead of living in big homes, condos, or skyscrapers, everyone lives in a yurt at night and travels with all the oil by day.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that <kanji>oil</kanji> is so abundant, everyone lives in <reading>yu</reading>rts (ゆ). They're light and mobile, so people can just move from oil well to oil well, collecting oil.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tsunami</radical> is the <radical>reason</radical> we have so much <kanji>oil</kanji> now. The tsunami moved a bunch of earth out of the way, so now it’s really easy to dig for oil. Thanks tsunami!

MeaningHint: Imagine a teardrop going through this process to get out of your eye, though in a more miniature teardrop size.
ReadingHint: Feel each teardrop rolling like a little wave down your cheek. You've never paid such close attention to them before. Teardrops really are just 波だ, you think. Little, ticklish waves.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>teardrop</kanji>s are like little waves. What are they? They're <reading>波だ</reading> (なみだ).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> goes through a little <radical>door</radical>, and plops down a <radical>big</radical> drop on your face. This tsunami is your tears inside your eye area. The door is your tear duct. The big drop is the <kanji>teardrop</kanji> coming down your eye.

MeaningHint: One eave, one house. Two eaves, two houses.
ReadingHint: Ken hops down and explains that he's been hired to count all the houses in his lord's domain just because his name is the same as the counter for houses. It isn't really fair, he complains to you, as you try to sneak by and lock him out of your house.
ReadingMnemonic: On top of the <kanji>eaves</kanji> of your house <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai is counting houses too.
MeaningMnemonic:
Your <radical>car</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> because you kept it under the <kanji>eaves</kanji> of your house. 

This kanji has a second meaning too: <kanji>house counter</kanji>. Imagine counting all the houses on your block by the number of eaves you see keeping cars dry.

MeaningHint: Pretend you are the stick. Really focus on your former self as you feel the sun warm you with its rays. Imagine yourself growing, larger and larger.
ReadingHint: I guess this stick was formerly a cucumber tree. Or maybe it just tried so hard it pooped a cucumber. I don't care which one is true, you pick whatever you want to believe.
ReadingMnemonic: It's working, it's working! The stick is returning to its <kanji>former</kanji> state and... w-wuh? It sprouted a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう).
MeaningMnemonic: There’s a <radical>stick</radical> next to the <radical>sun</radical>, reflecting on its <kanji>former</kanji> glory. The stick used to be a great tree, and the stick thinks that if it stays by the sun long enough it might return to its former form as a towering tree.

MeaningHint: Imagine having your surname changed for the rest of your life. How do you feel about having a different family name? Is it a big deal to you or does it not matter much?
ReadingHint: Now you share a family name AND a holy blue saber! You can protect your legacy (and surname) hand in hand and saber in saber. (Or something like that.)
ReadingMnemonic: Picture this special woman in your mind. She's changing her <kanji>surname</kanji> to yours and taking hold of your special <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) as well. And it's... it's splitting in two!
MeaningMnemonic: If there's a special <radical>woman</radical> in your <radical>life</radical>, she might eventually take your <kanji>family name</kanji> and make it her <kanji>surname</kanji> too. In Japan and other countries, tradition dictates that women change their last name when they get married and keep that name for the rest of their life. Their partner’s family name becomes their family name too.

MeaningHint: Imagine your private sword holding a big bag of shellfish and going off to market to trade them.
ReadingHint: Oh boy, I hope my private sword trades my shellfish for that new bowling ball I heard about!
ReadingMnemonic: While he's out making the <kanji>trade</kanji> for you, you decide to relax and do some <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). In fact, you can't wait for the trade to be done because you're hoping he's going to come back with a new bowling ball for you.
MeaningMnemonic: You send your <radical>private</radical> <radical>sword</radical> man out with your <radical>shellfish</radical> so he can <kanji>trade</kanji> them. Shellfish were used as currency back in the olden days. And you aren't going to go out trading and dirty your own hands, no no. Trading your shellfish is the job for the private sword you hired!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: You better remember the Shougun is the commander of everything... or else...
ReadingMnemonic: Who is the <kanji>commander</kanji> of Japan? The <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう)! And if you ever say anyone else or vote for someone else or even THINK about someone else being the commander, he'll find you and cut off your head.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>commander</radical> radical and the <kanji>commander</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Look at this bright plate. Look at it. It’s awesome. It’s blinding. Hold it in your hands. Marvel at it. Wow. This plate is sweet.
ReadingHint: This Mayflower boat is the best boat you ever did see. Hear the ocean around it as it bobs in the water, full sails billowing in the wind. Boats are cool.
ReadingMnemonic: The person you are trying to get in an <kanji>alliance</kanji> with you is the captain of the <reading>May</reading>flower (めい). You want to be in an alliance with the captain of the Mayflower because you really like boats. You want to ride on the Mayflower like, so bad.
MeaningMnemonic: I’ll give you this <radical>bright</radical> <radical>plate</radical> if you agree to an <kanji>alliance</kanji> with me. I know you like things that are bright, and I know you like plates too. Therefore, this bright plate should be more than enough to convince you to enter into an alliance with me.

MeaningHint: Rings, necklaces, wallets, phones? Don't take them on a scooter. Leave behind all that valuable stuff!
ReadingHint: If you keep too many of your valuables on you, the eagles might not leave you behind either! They'll just pick you up off the scooter and carry you and your valuables off to their nests!
ReadingMnemonic: Anything you don't <kanji>leave behind</kanji> will be stolen by <reading>ea</reading>gles (い). Eagles like shiny, valuable things, especially ones that are bouncing around on you while you scoot by on a scooter.
MeaningMnemonic: When you go on a <radical>scooter</radical>, anything <radical>valuable</radical>, you need to <kanji>leave behind</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The leader is humble and human, just like the rest of us, and has to stretch to stay healthy too. He's not some kind of god!
ReadingHint: Watch as the leader stretches his body and the nori seaweed. He takes a bite, stretches the other way, then stretches out a piece for you to eat too. How humble of him!
ReadingMnemonic: The leader is currently working hard to <kanji>stretch</kanji> in front of all of you, and as he does, he munches on some <reading>no</reading>ri (の) seaweed snacks. Then he starts stretching the nori too.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> who's <radical>humble</radical> will <kanji>stretch</kanji> in front of everyone. They'll show that they have to get up every hour or so and stretch just like the rest of us.

MeaningHint: Picture the leader standing up in front of everyone as they blame him. He's holding a plaque with the amount of debt you're all in. Try to imagine what that number would be.
ReadingHint: Watch as your cyborg saves your company, even though it wasn't even his responsibility, from crippling debt. *sob* I can't believe your cyborg is gone!
ReadingMnemonic: To pay off this <kanji>debt</kanji> your leader says you need to sell your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) to help the company. You fight him! You yell! You scream! The cyborg puts a half-human half-robot hand on your shoulder and shakes its head sadly.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>leader</radical> gets the <radical>blame</radical> if your company is in <kanji>debt</kanji>. They're the one who led you all into that debt, so they should get the blame too.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself on an escalator. What is up above that you’re trying to reach?
ReadingHint: Cucumbers grow on trees sometimes, so you need to take escalators to get up there and reach them.
ReadingMnemonic: You're riding the escalator trying to <kanji>reach</kanji> a delicious <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう).
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Escalator</radical>s are used to <kanji>reach</kanji> things.

MeaningHint: Look at this big jackhammer. Look at it. It’s so big. Also it’s in Nara. Imagine being in Nara and looking at this big jackhammer. You’re all like, dang – that’s a big jackhammer.
ReadingHint: Just keep in mind what the first part of Nara is. The first part of Nara is “na” so you’ll have no trouble remembering this one.
ReadingMnemonic: What’s the first part of <kanji>Nara</kanji>? <reading>Na</reading> (な). Well that’s easy.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>big</radical> <radical>jackhammer</radical> in <kanji>Nara</kanji>. That jackhammer is the biggest in all the land and is on display in Nara, Japan.

MeaningHint: The scarecrow creeps you out. You don't want to look at it anymore, so you're standing eerily close to it measuring its width, so you can hide it forever.
ReadingHint: Do you know the width of a habanero? It's one scarecrow minus one cloth.
ReadingMnemonic: You finish measuring the <kanji>width</kanji> of the scarecrow and the width of the towel, and you realize you're the width of a <reading>haba</reading>nero (はば) pepper short. This is fine, you grow those. This is a whole field of habaneros! So you pluck one off the nearest plant, tape it to the cloth, and cover that scarecrow right up.
MeaningMnemonic: You want to put this <radical>towel</radical> over this <radical>scarecrow</radical>, but you have to measure its <kanji>width</kanji> first. If the towel doesn't have a greater width than the scarecrow, some of it will be showing. Which you definitely don't want.

MeaningHint: During your departure you look back at the obsolete canopy just once. It's standing there, still and alone. It's a really sad sight.
ReadingHint:
My sweet canopy
I don't need you anymore
You are obsolete
ReadingMnemonic: When something becomes <kanji>obsolete</kanji>, you must write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい). Honestly, the best haiku are about something ending, so this is perfect.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>canopy</radical>, after your <radical>departure</radical>, will become <kanji>obsolete</kanji>. The canopy was here to protect you, but no one will need it once you're gone.

ReadingHint: Put on the armor! Feel the sweetness begin to reflect off your armored body. Only a little gets through — just enough for the sweet flavor on your tongue to taste sweet, but not too sweet.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sweet</kanji>, sweet sensation is almost overwhelming. You gotta put on some sweet-resistant <reading>armor</reading> (あま) to protect you from all that sweetness. The armor will prevent you from having a sweet, sweet sweetness overload.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sweet</radical> radical and the <kanji>sweet</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Double sweet.

MeaningHint: Imagine counting all of the mouths and legs you've pummeled off of the other prisoners. Exchange them for better loot!
ReadingHint: Think back to when you were a kid and they would roll out those TVs on stands into the classrooms. Imagine watching a documentary on Genghis Khan's exchange routes where he bought and sold body parts. Educational!
ReadingMnemonic: You learned this type of <kanji>exchange</kanji> from the mighty Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). You read a book or watched a show about him once and you're pretty positive that Genghis Khan would exchange the body parts of his enemies all over the continents of Asia and Europe for nicer stuff.
MeaningMnemonic: You count with your <radical>fingers</radical> in <radical>prison</radical> all of the <radical>mouth</radical>s and <radical>legs</radical> you've acquired. Soon you'll be able to make a <radical>big</radical> <kanji>exchange</kanji> with all of these body parts to get something awesome.

MeaningHint: Imagine plucking the hairs out of your mohawk with your fingers. It's kind of hard to grab onto them at first, but eventually you're plucking them like it's nothing.
ReadingHint: You didn't realize techies were so exclusive... but they just plucked and plucked at your self-esteem, until you decided to pluck your hair out of your own head.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to <kanji>pluck</kanji> all your mohawk hair out because you're a <reading>techie</reading> (てき), and everyone at your techie job keeps making fun of you for your hair choices. "Techies should be bald!" your boss screamed. So here you are, plucking your hair out to fit in.
MeaningMnemonic: Stick your <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>mohawk</radical> and <kanji>pluck</kanji> each hair out, one by one.

MeaningHint: Imagine slicing this tree smaller and smaller until you finally get to the nucleus of it. It’s super tiny so you have to view it in a microscope. What does it look like?
ReadingHint: Look at this tree nucleus that looks like a cock. While you’re watching it, the nucleus goes “Cock-a-doodle-doo!”
ReadingMnemonic: It looks like a <reading>cock</reading> (かく). And by cock I mean a male chicken – a rooster. Why does the <kanji>nucleus</kanji> of a tree look like a cock? Science is weird.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>tree</radical> and <radical>slice</radical> it until you reach its <kanji>nucleus</kanji>. To get to the nucleus of the tree you have to make many a slice, but it’s possible.

MeaningHint: Look at this tsunami in the middle of the open sea. When the tsunami isn’t destroying things and is just out there in the open sea it looks really majestic and pretty.
ReadingHint: You really regret saying oki doki this time. You don’t want to surf on a tsunami in the middle of the open sea. Imagine doing so. It’s terrifying and wet and you don’t even know how to surf. You’re going to die.
ReadingMnemonic: Someone dares you to go out to the <kanji>open sea</kanji> and surf on the middle of the tsunami. “<reading>Oki</reading> (おき) doki,” you reply. You can’t refuse a dare. Every time someone dares you to do something, you always respond with “oki doki”.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>tsunami</radical> in the <radical>middle</radical> of the <kanji>open sea</kanji>. Luckily there are no ships around, so the tsunami in the middle of the open sea is not damaging to anyone.

MeaningHint: A rope made of threaded turtles. Go ahead and grab onto it and see how it feels. A little rough, but strong.
ReadingHint: Besides being awful, where did Joe learn to thread turtles into ropes? Why does he have so many turtles despite living on such an inland farm? These are nice ropes that Joe is making though, and you kind of want to buy some.
ReadingMnemonic: The person making these threaded turtle <kanji>rope</kanji>s is none other than the local farmhand <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう). You watch him stretch and peel pieces of turtle off of turtles, then thread them together into a rope.
MeaningMnemonic: Someone <radical>thread</radical>ed a <radical>turtle</radical> into long skinny pieces, making a <kanji>rope</kanji>.

MeaningHint: This harbor is all you’ve got, it’s the backbone of your life. So how are you going to defend it from this tsunami caused by a giant brush?!
ReadingHint: “How are two of my friends going to stop an entire tsunami from destroying my dear harbor?”, you might ask yourself. Well don’t ask us, we don’t specialize in brush-caused tsunami harbor defence—we’re just a kanji learning website!
ReadingMnemonic: You’ll defend your <kanji>harbor</kanji> with your <reading>two</reading> (つ) best friends, that’s how! Luckily, your two friends specialize in defending harbors from giant brush-caused tsunamis. Quite the occupation.
MeaningMnemonic: There’s a huge <radical>tsunami</radical>, caused by a giant <radical>brush</radical>, fast approaching you and your <kanji>harbor</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It is well known that dogs in the South are famous for their kindness and hospitality. It's customary to always offer something when a guest arrives.
ReadingHint: Ken offers to carry you, too. Imagine him offering this. You can choose whether or not to accept in your mind.
ReadingMnemonic: The South Dog <kanji>offer</kanji>s you the services of <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai as you traverse his lands.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>South</radical> <radical>Dog</radical> gives you an <kanji>offer</kanji>. None of the other dogs in the North, East, or West offered you anything.

MeaningHint: Imagine being sick. Like sick as a dog sick. And then you take some charcoal, put it into your drinking water, and then you're healed!
ReadingHint: Picture yourself rowing your row boat into the lake with huge chunks of solid charcoal. You carefully break them up and toss them over the side, so you can heal everyone.
ReadingMnemonic: You aren't the only one that needs to be <kanji>heal</kanji>ed, so you take your <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat out onto the lake where your town gets its drinking water and you dump a bunch of charcoal into it! Now everyone will be healed when they drink.
MeaningMnemonic: If someone is <radical>sick</radical> feed them <radical>charcoal</radical> to <kanji>heal</kanji> them. Okay, so maybe you shouldn't feed it directly to them, but charcoal has amazing water filtration powers. So put charcoal in their water and the water will heal them right up.

MeaningHint: Picture this lion surrounded by the thousands of grains of rice he'll inherit from his parents. He's carefully threading them all together.
ReadingHint: I'd much rather inherit a cake than a ton of hard to keep track of rice, like this poor lion.
ReadingMnemonic: If you're going to <kanji>inherit</kanji> something from your parents, try to convince them to make it <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい), not rice. A single cake is much easier to keep track of and probably tastier (though it will be super stale by the time they die).
MeaningMnemonic: With a <radical>thread</radical>, a <radical>lion</radical> sews together all the <radical>rice</radical> he'll <kanji>inherit</kanji> from his parents one day. He's convinced his parents are going to try to keep some of his inheritance from him, so he's linking it all together so it can't go anywhere.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding onto a thread that is attached to a turkey. Feel the turkey tug against the thread as you struggle to maintain control.
ReadingHint: Now you and the turkey and the eagle are all bumbling about in pandemonium. You try to maintain order but it’s not possible. What a kerfuffle.
ReadingMnemonic: The reason the turkey is tugging is because there’s an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swooping down to attack the turkey. You try to <kanji>maintain</kanji> control, but once you see the eagle as well, you let go of the thread.
MeaningMnemonic: You tie a piece of <radical>thread</radical> to your <radical>turkey</radical> in order to <kanji>maintain</kanji> proximity to it. The turkey likes to run away, so you tie a thread to it. This way you can hold the other end and maintain your turkey.

MeaningHint: If you want to do the nothing dance, just clear your head so there's nothing in it, and then dance!
ReadingHint: Feel the booze coursing through your veins as you dance like no one is watching.
ReadingMnemonic: To help you do this <kanji>dance</kanji> you need some <reading>boo</reading>ze (ぶ). Just enough so it's easier to think of nothing, let all your fears go, and dance.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>nothing</radical> <radical>dance</radical> is still a <kanji>dance</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture the leader of your branch doing deeds around your office. Pulling out chairs for people sitting down, emptying the trash, balancing spreadsheets. All good deeds.
ReadingHint: Picture him doing this deed. He's crawling around on the floor looking for your key. You aren't really helping, you're just watching him do the deed of key-searching for you.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>deed</kanji> he's currently working on is finding your lost <reading>key</reading> (き). He doesn't have to help, you're the one who lost your own house-combination-car key. But he's so used to doing deeds, he can't help but help.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of your <radical>branch</radical> became the leader from doing <kanji>deed</kanji>s. You can't rise to the rank of branch leader if you don't perform a certain number of deeds! Most of them good, I hope.

MeaningHint: Imagine each step on hot, sun-baked asphalt. The water under your feet sizzles, leaving steaming footprints where you stepped from.
ReadingHint: Each step is taking you closer to とうきょう. Don't forget that, no matter how hard it seems, don't lose your motivation to keep stepping forward toward your goal. ♡
ReadingMnemonic: Each <kanji>step</kanji> you take brings you closer to <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Unfortunately, the sun keeps getting hotter, meaning you have to take breaks between steps more often, to get your feet wet with even more water. But you're determined to reach とうきょう, no matter how many steps it takes.
MeaningMnemonic: You soak each <radical>foot</radical> in <radical>water</radical> before going out in the <radical>sun</radical> to take your first <kanji>step</kanji>. Stepping on the ground when it's been baking in the hot sun can burn your feet, so you're careful to make them wet with water before stepping outside even once.

MeaningHint: In general, you can assume a boat will have a weapon, but not all of them will.
ReadingHint: So of all the boats out there, generally all of them have weapons, and generally Han Solo will be on one.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Generally</kanji> a boat with a weapon will be owned by <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>boat</radical> will have at least one <radical>weapon</radical>, <kanji>generally</kanji>.

MeaningHint: A bundle of geoducks will never lie, never let you down, and never disappoint you. You can trust them completely.
ReadingHint: Imagine a bundle of geoducks and a piece rye bread. Think about how you can trust the geoducks but you can't trust the rye bread.
ReadingMnemonic: While you can <kanji>trust</kanji> a bundle of geoducks, never ever trust <reading>rye</reading> (らい) bread.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>bundle</radical> of <radical>geoduck</radical>s is something you can always <kanji>trust</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine this leader in his clothes. Look how reliant he is on them. Imagine what he would be without his clothes – nothing! And also, naked.
ReadingHint: Look at the leader as he shouts at his clothes he’s become so reliant on to do something. Hear the countless eagles screeching around as they terrorize the neighborhood. People scream and weep in the streets.
ReadingMnemonic: The leader has become so <kanji>reliant</kanji> on his clothes that his judgment has been clouded. His kingdom has become infested with <reading>ea</reading>gles (い) but the leader does nothing, trusting that his clothes will rout out the eagles if it is something that needs to be done. He’s become so reliant on his clothes that he’s started to become not so good a leader anymore.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> has <radical>clothes</radical> that he is quite <kanji>reliant</kanji> on. The leader needs his clothes to make him look like a leader. The leader has become more reliant on his clothes than he is on his actual skills as a leader!

MeaningHint: Picture the deer comparing the two structures. It probably has no idea what they are, but it's trying very hard to understand what it's seeing anyway.
ReadingHint: Deer get hit by cars all the time, but it was still really shocking . I mean it was right in front of you. The car could have hit you too!
ReadingMnemonic: You walk forward, about to call out to the <kanji>deer</kanji>, when a <reading>ca</reading>r (か) suddenly appears out of no where and runs the deer over! Was this all just a trap set up to murder the deer?!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>canopy</radical> and a <radical>helicopter</radical> are being <radical>compare</radical>d by a <kanji>deer</kanji>. The deer is standing very still (as deer do) and looking very slowly between the empty canopy and the helicopter sitting next to it.

MeaningHint: What did you say to the flowers that you are so in agreement about? Think about what you said.
ReadingHint: Imagine you are the flowers. There’s a duck. It’s making a poo. On you. Ew, man, that’s disgusting. Bleh.
ReadingMnemonic: You and the flowers are in <kanji>agreement</kanji> on the fact that neither of you like when a <reading>duck</reading> (だく) walks across the flowers. You don’t like having a duck walk on the flowers because the duck will poo in the flowers and then you have to worry about stepping in it. And the flowers just don’t like having duck poo in their face.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> something and the <radical>flowers</radical> are <radical>right</radical> in <kanji>agreement</kanji>. The flowers see where you are coming from when you say what you say. You and the flowers are in complete agreement.

MeaningHint: You're going for an ultra run, calling out with your super loud voice right down the middle of the street.
ReadingHint: Mrs. Chou is super ultra scary. And if you don't kick it into super ultra high gear she's going to catch up to you and make you super ultra dead.
ReadingMnemonic: You're just becoming <kanji>ultra</kanji> tired when you hear a terrifying sound. It's Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) barreling down toward you. "WERE YOU CALLING FOR ME, CHILD?!" she screams.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>run</radical> and <radical>call</radical> out to people at the same time because you're <kanji>super</kanji> <kanji>ultra</kanji> excited right now. You have a super crazy amount of energy you need to get out with your running and calling.

MeaningHint: Imagine a tsunami crashing over the land, being blamed by everyone. In its path are piles and piles of pickles, as far as the eye can see!
ReadingHint: Picture the two pickles and imagine what kind of pickles they are.
ReadingMnemonic: Looking at all those <kanji>pickle</kanji>s, you bend down and pick up <reading>two</reading> (つ) of them. One in each hand. You look at the pickles in your hand and wonder whether to try one.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>s get <radical>blame</radical>d because they <kanji>pickle</kanji> everything in their path. That means that everything smells of pickles after a tsunami.

MeaningHint: Your parents didn't want you having those things, which is why they put them up high, but your aunt and uncle would always spoil you and get them for you.
ReadingHint: You aren't supposed to wear your obi unless it's a special occasion, but your aunt and uncle don't know that. Now you can run around with it on your head and your parents can't stop you by putting it up high!
ReadingMnemonic: You ask your <kanji>aunt</kanji> and <kanji>uncle</kanji> to help you reach your special <reading>o</reading>bi (お) from high up in the closet.
MeaningMnemonic: There were lots of things that were <radical>above</radical> you when you were <radical>small</radical> that you would need a <radical>stool</radical> to reach, so you just asked your <kanji>aunt</kanji> or <kanji>uncle</kanji> to get them for you.

MeaningHint: Really feel the courage welling up inside of you in the face of this terrifying tsunami. Connect it to some physical feeling—perhaps you can feel it welling up in your chest.
ReadingHint: Who wells up with courage? You do!
ReadingMnemonic: Whom does that courage <kanji>well up</kanji> inside of? <reading>You</reading> (ゆう)! In fact, YOU feel like there is a never ending well of courage bursting out of you now. You are going to be a hero!
MeaningMnemonic: When you see a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming, <radical>courage</radical> will <kanji>well up</kanji> inside you, giving you the power to save everyone before you get washed away!

MeaningHint: Imagine your foot hearing this omen, "If you step on a crack, you'll break your mother's back." Now your foot hops over all of the tiny cracks in the street and the sidewalk and you look really silly.
ReadingHint: Now you're hopping away in fear from Mrs. Chou!
ReadingMnemonic: As you hop you start to hear an awful scream coming down the street. It's Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) and she's running straight for you yelling, "STEP ON MY STREET'S CRACKS! STOP HOPPING OR YOUR MOTHER WILL NEVER BREAK HER BACK!"
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>foot</radical> heard an <radical>omen</radical> and now it's making you <kanji>hop</kanji> over things.

MeaningHint: The sun doesn't have a voice, unfortunately, so it calls out the only way it can, with its shining rays.
ReadingHint: Picture the Shougun standing in the shining sunlight. He's so bright and beautiful!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>shining</kanji> is aimed directly at the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). The sun is shining on the Shougun to not only show its support for his leadership, but to let him shine brighter than anyone else in Japan.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> <radical>call</radical>s out to the earth by <kanji>shining</kanji> down on it.

MeaningHint: Imagine going fishing for fish in a fishing spot after a tsunami. There are SO many fish here. Your hook barely touches the water and there’s already a fish on it. Man, fishing like this is easy! Smell the smell of fish and sea air around you.
ReadingHint: Go! Go row! Go and row for the fishing! How do you go? You row! The row part should be pretty easy to remember since you gotta row your boat out to sea, but if you’re having trouble with the go part, just remember that it has the same reading as 魚.
ReadingMnemonic: To get to the best <kanji>fishing</kanji> spot you need to <reading>go</reading> (ぎょ) <reading>row</reading> (りょう) out to the middle of the ocean. You go by means of rowing. To get to the fish and do the fishing, you need to go and row out to sea.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> pushes all the <radical>fish</radical> closer to land, making for some easy <kanji>fishing</kanji>. The tsunami starts out in the middle of the ocean and pushes all the fish towards the coast where everyone does their fishing. With all the fish pushed closer to land and closer together, it’s very easy for people to go fishing in one spot and catch a lot of fish.

MeaningHint: Imagine receiving your reparation prize. Feel the prize in your hand. It’s all wrapped up but it’s heavy and feels expensive. What could it be?
ReadingHint: Imagine receiving your reparations from the Shougun himself. You're angry at him for threatening you, wounding people, and cutting off the heads of people who go against him, but you take it anyway.
ReadingMnemonic: The leader who presented you with this <kanji>reparation</kanji> is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) of Japan. He's trying to make up for all the threats he made to you and some of the things you saw (which he's now trying to cover up for).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> presents you with a <radical>prize</radical> as <kanji>reparation</kanji> for his past deeds. This leader wronged you in the past and wishes to make amends. Therefore the leader presents you with a reparation in the form of a glittering prize.

MeaningHint: Even if it was your own lantern, why stab it with a knife? You could have recycled that. I will punish you for lantern stabbing and pollution!
ReadingHint: The next time you want to stab a lantern with a knife, think about how good cake tastes and stop. Remember, DARE (Don't Attack Reusable Electronics)!
ReadingMnemonic: I will <kanji>punish</kanji> you by taking away your <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! You'll never eat cake again. Not for birthdays, not for holidays. This is your punishment.
MeaningMnemonic: Destroy a <radical>lantern</radical> with your <radical>knife</radical> and you'll be <kanji>punish</kanji>ed.

MeaningHint: Imagine what you had to do to recruit this greenhouse. Imagine schmoozing with the previous greenhouse owners to recruit them to your cause and sell you the greenhouse.
ReadingHint: Recruit Bobo the clown. Look at him. He’s terrifying. Bobo will make for a great deterrent for entry to your greenhouse.
ReadingMnemonic: After you <kanji>recruit</kanji> the greenhouse, you recruit <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the clown to guard it for you. Bobo the clown has a terrifying amount of power so he’ll make an excellent guard for the greenhouse.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>greenhouse</radical> is the newest <radical>power</radical> of the city you decided to <kanji>recruit</kanji>. You’re trying to recruit all the buildings in town and the greenhouse was the next on your list. It adds just a little bit of power to the town, but it's important nonetheless.

MeaningHint: Be tenacious about happiness in your life. No matter how many times it slips away, don't give up. Keep going after it, because it will absolutely come back if you're tenacious enough.
ReadingHint: Will someone take the shoe sometimes? Yeah, but the shih tzu doesn't give up. That's how you need to be with happiness. Kids, be tenacious!
ReadingMnemonic: Happiness is as <kanji>tenacious</kanji> as a <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) with a <reading>shoe</reading> (しゅう). That little dog does not want to give up on the shoe, no matter what's happening around it.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Happiness</radical> is a <radical>circle</radical> that's <kanji>tenacious</kanji>. In other words, we're all in this circle/cycle where we do whatever we can to grab onto happiness and hold it as tightly as we can.

MeaningHint: Picture a serene rice paddy. Now watch the whole thing explode, rice and dirt going everywhere, and a secret, underground baseball base is left there, smoldering.
ReadingHint: Hold this ruined baseball base in your hands. Hey, maybe it's a collectible?
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, thanks to the explosion, the <kanji>base</kanji> is in <reading>rui</reading>ns (るい). It's dark and smokey and kinda half burned. You probably can't get this in a regulation game.
MeaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>rice paddy</radical>, an <radical>explosion</radical> shakes the <radical>dirt</radical>, exposing a hidden <kanji>base</kanji>. A <kanji>baseball base</kanji>! I guess this rice paddy was built on top of an old baseball field…

MeaningHint: Watch as the moons crumble together, and then the mountain slowly crumbles down into them too.
ReadingHint: Your home crumbles like a dry cookie until it's nothing more than a bunch of dust, mixed in with the mountain and the moon. Now where will you live?
ReadingMnemonic: As the mountain <kanji>crumble</kanji>s you see your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), which is at the very top of the mountain, come crumbling down too.
MeaningMnemonic: Below this <radical>mountain</radical>, a <radical>moon</radical> and another <radical>moon</radical> crash into each other and the entire thing <kanji>crumble</kanji>s to pieces.

MeaningHint: You did not wish to be afflicted as such. Feel the skewer in your heart and the great pain it gives you. You know you’re dying.
ReadingHint: You look on in horror as the affliction grabs hold and everything starts to fade, but Genghis Khan still floats before you, watching you closely.
ReadingMnemonic: Since you’re <kanji>afflicted</kanji> with the death, you start to hallucinate and the ghost of Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) appears before you. "Ah, I was afflicted of the same disease. It killed me, you know."
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>skewer</radical> to the <radical>heart</radical> has <kanji>afflicted</kanji> your health. You’ve been afflicted with a disease from the skewer to your heart. Your affliction is death. You’re dying.

MeaningHint: Heft this big door in your arms. It’s very heavy. Feel your arms tire as you look for who to return the door to.
ReadingHint: You’re obsessed with returning this big door. Once you enter this mode, the only way to get out is to return the thing you have.
ReadingMnemonic: You are in <kanji>return</kanji> <reading>mode</reading> (もど). When you enter this mode, you concentrate only on returning something. Nothing else matters in this mode. You’re in full on return mode.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>door</radical> is <radical>big</radical> and you need to <kanji>return</kanji> it to its rightful owner. This door is too big and important looking to not belong to someone. You need to find its owner and return it to them.

MeaningHint: They weren't listening, which is why you slammed that lid down. You'll have to confront them more directly.
ReadingHint: こういち is laughing at you as you confront him. Which makes you angrier, so you bang the lid on the table some more.
ReadingMnemonic: You are trying to <kanji>confront</kanji> Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち. You're confronting him about all the kanji he's forcing you, and everyone else, to learn. "Look here こういち! I know you said I'd learn like 2000 kanji in no time, but this is way too efficient! What will the people do when they're done? Do you think they will sit idly by?"
MeaningMnemonic: You take your <radical>fingers</radical> and slam a <radical>lid</radical> on the <radical>table</radical>. You make this big noise to <kanji>confront</kanji> the person in front of you.

MeaningHint: Imagine your entire clan putting their fingers in the ground as a form of resistance. It's a moving sight.
ReadingHint: You make your choice to resist. He orders his guards to pull your fingers out of the ground, chop them into the size of tater tots, and then deep fry them. After they're done, the lord pops one in his mouth and says, "Oooh, resistance is delicious."
ReadingMnemonic: You're all <kanji>resist</kanji>ing with your fingers in the ground, when the lords appear, angry. "You resist us by hiding your fingers with potatoes in the ground?! You want your fingers to be useless potatoes so badly? We'll chop them up and turn them into <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots!"
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> join the rest of your <radical>clan</radical>, pressed into the <radical>ground</radical> to declare that you will <kanji>resist</kanji> your lords. Your clan is made of a bunch of peasants, but you aren't going to work the land for other people anymore. You're going to resist. Your fingers work for no one but yourselves from now on!

MeaningHint: You're welcome. Now you know how to keep everything in season. Lifehack!
ReadingHint: Joke's on them though. Soon it will be apple season. Soon it will always be time for apples. Soon June will end and people will forget about everything else, living forever inside that period of ten days when apples are most in season.
ReadingMnemonic: When things are <kanji>in season</kanji> for more than <kanji>ten days</kanji>, people are going to wonder what's going on. When's Christmas? When's Golden Week? <reading>Soon</reading> (しゅん), you say. Soon enough, it's <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). Which is the middle of the year, so now everything is soon.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>prison</radical> holds the <radical>sun</radical> inside it, everything will always be <kanji>in season</kanji>. Obviously if the sun is in prison, the seasons can't change. Want to eat perfect apples all year round? Just put the sun in prison when they're ripe — usually a short window of <kanji>ten days</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture these extremely violent tsunamis hitting a shore again and again, turning it into a bow-shaped gulf. That's how gulfs are created in this part of the world!
ReadingHint: Many tsunamis, again and again, for just one gulf? Yep, that's just how life goes. Gulfs are in high demand, but you'll need many tsunamis to create just one.
ReadingMnemonic: How many <kanji>gulf</kanji>s will this create? Just <reading>one</reading> (わん).
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>s hit these lands <radical>again</radical> and again, molding it into some kind of <radical>bow</radical> shape. Or in other words, creating a <kanji>gulf</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the fire explode. Feel the heat of the fire and the explosion. It’s very violent. BOOM BOOM BA-BOOM!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself as you back away from the explosion. As you back away, see the fire dance and feel the heat of the explosion on your face.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch the fire <kanji>explode</kanji>, you start to <reading>back</reading> (ばく) away. I don’t blame you. You need to back away or else you’ll get caught in the violent explosion. Back away a little more just to be safe.
MeaningMnemonic: If you combine <radical>fire</radical> with <radical>violence</radical>, it will <kanji>explode</kanji>! A fire, once full of violence, will surely explode. The fire can’t handle all that violence so the fire just rumbles and shakes and then finally explodes. BOOM!

MeaningHint: Imagine how simple life would be if we replaced all the bows in the world with bullets. (Insert sarcastic crying face here.)
ReadingHint: Imagine shooting bullets into the sun at dawn. Pow pow, take that history! Blam blam, take that dawn!
ReadingMnemonic: If you want to shoot <kanji>bullet</kanji>s, you need to do it at <reading>dawn</reading> (だん). Back in the old days, it wasn't bright enough to shoot arrows at dawn, but you can shoot bullets at dawn because they're simpler to use!
MeaningMnemonic: What's like a <radical>bow</radical>, but even more <radical>simple</radical>? A <kanji>bullet</kanji>. With a bow, you have to load an arrow and pull back the string and aim, yuck. Bullets can be shot with just a simple squeeze of a trigger.

MeaningHint: Imagine the whole process of sound going through your ear and to your heart, then the gears turning to really understand what someone is saying. It's a real listen, not a fake non-heart listen.
ReadingHint: Focus on listening, and focus on their message about Mrs. Chou. Feel afraid about the future because of it.
ReadingMnemonic: You are <kanji>listen</kanji>ing so well to what someone is saying. What are they telling you that is so important? They are telling you of the omen of Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう), who will destroy the earth.
MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>ear</radical> is <radical>cross</radical>ed with sound, it catches it like a <radical>net</radical>, and sends it to your <radical>heart</radical> for processing. Because you're using your heart to process this sound, it means you're giving it a proper <kanji>listen</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself stepping around with your foot again and again, leaving traces all over the place. Anyone would be able to trace you like this, but you just love a good little stomping around.
ReadingHint: You're absolutely surrounded by all these people attracted to your sexy stomping around with your foot, again and again. Look, no shaming here, whatever floats your boat.
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out you leaving <kanji>traces</kanji> everywhere is quite <reading>sexy</reading> (せき) to a lot of people.
MeaningMnemonic: When you're stepping around with your <radical>foot</radical> <radical>again</radical> and again, you'll leave behind <kanji>traces</kanji> all over the place.

MeaningHint: Imagine the conversation with emergency services. They say they will dispatch an ambulance immediately.
ReadingHint: He takes out a very tiny katana and performs surgery right there. The insect dies on the scooter. "It looks like I've dispatched this insect to the afterlife."
ReadingMnemonic: Out of the <kanji>dispatch</kanji>ed ambulance jumps <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. "Dispatch sent me! I'm here to fix that insect."
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>scooter</radical> is an <radical>insect</radical> and a <radical>bear</radical>. The bear immediately stomps the insect, squishing it flat. "My god, the insect is smashed. Someone call <kanji>dispatch</kanji>!"

MeaningHint: Imagine having to fight against the bean bureaucracy. It's so absurd! Who can say a bean is too big or too small, in this age of diversity?! Your fighting pays off though, and they let you squeeze your beans through the gate. Well fought, bean brethren. Well fought.
ReadingHint: How are you supposed to get your beans into とうきょう if a fight breaks out at the gate every time? What a struggle. Fortunately those bean-counting bureaucrats of とうきょう were no match for your fight.
ReadingMnemonic: The gate you put up a <kanji>fight</kanji> at leads to <reading>とう</reading>きょう. The people of とうきょう need those beans for sustenance, and that's why you fight — for them!
MeaningMnemonic: At this <radical>gate</radical> you're trying to get some <radical>beans</radical> through, but your <radical>measurements</radical> were off, leading to a big <kanji>fight</kanji>. The bean authorities at the gate always want to fight about bean sizes, and now you have to fight to get those beans through.

MeaningHint: Picture an army camp housing all of the different cars the army needs at any given time.
ReadingHint: Jeans are pretty durable, now that you think about it. And if the soldiers are working on these cars in the camp while they're there, it makes sense to wear a nice pair of jeans.
ReadingMnemonic: All of the people in the <kanji>army camp</kanji> are wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). You thought soldiers were supposed to wear uniforms, but it looks like when they're in the camp they have to wear jeans.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> is full of <radical>car</radical>s because it's an <kanji>army camp</kanji>. The cars (and tanks and planes) need to be ready to go at any moment just like the soldiers.

MeaningHint: Imagine this warm, grain fragrance. What does it smell like to you? Cooked rice? Gentle flowers? A warm summer's day?
ReadingHint: This fragrance was こういち's armpits the whole time. You thought it was warm grains. Or... maybe he smells like... grains?
ReadingMnemonic: As you sniff this lovely <kanji>fragrance</kanji> you get lost in the smell. When you open your eyes again, you see a farming <reading>こう</reading>いち standing in front of you. With... his... arm... up. "Do you like my fragrance? I was genetically modified to have a unique fragrance compared to the other こういちs."
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Grain</radical> left in the <radical>sun</radical> blossom and give off a wonderful <kanji>fragrance</kanji>. They smell fresh and delicious. There's nothing better than the fragrance of fresh, sun-kissed grain.

MeaningHint: If you know the omen radical, you know the omen kanji as well!
ReadingHint:
You can clarify with the person telling you the omen several things: "Do you believe in the omen about Mrs. Chou destroying the world?" "When will this omen about Mrs. Chou come true?" etc. As long as you're associating omen with Mrs. Chou you should be good, so keep harping on that.

Anyway, whenever you hear any kind of omen (omen is a radical in the kanji), there's a good chance it's going to be about Mrs. Chou.
ReadingMnemonic: You listen to the <kanji>omen</kanji>, in it, it tells of an event in the future when Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう) will destroy the world.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>omen</radical> radical and the <kanji>omen</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine your servant as he looks to your gun and products. You can always look to him to take care of your menial tasks. You feel smug.
ReadingHint: Imagine your servant as he looks to your car’s o-ring. Hear him clinking and clanking around in the garage as he does so.
ReadingMnemonic: After your servant is done with your gun and other products you want him to <kanji>look to</kanji> your car’s o-<reading>rin</reading>g (りん). There’s something wrong with the o-ring so you want your servant to look to that as well.
MeaningMnemonic: You give your <radical>servant</radical> a <radical>gun</radical> along with some other <radical>products</radical> to <kanji>look to</kanji>. You can’t look to them yourself since you are very busy. You allow your servant to look to your gun and the other products.

MeaningHint: Imagine the triceratops with its little moon knife, doing some whittling. Hand it anything and it will whittle it down.
ReadingHint: Check out the sack that holds all the things that the triceratops whittled down. Go ahead and look through it, it's quite filled.
ReadingMnemonic: Everything it will <kanji>whittle down</kanji> shall be put in a <reading>sack</reading> (さく).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>triceratops</radical> with a <radical>moon</radical> <radical>knife</radical> can <kanji>whittle down</kanji> anything.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding up your sword and pledging to protect the lives of everyone in this city. It's a big deal!
ReadingHint: Would you pledge to give up cake to show everyone how serious you are about protecting life?
ReadingMnemonic: Until the city is safe, you <kanji>pledge</kanji> to give up <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい), your favorite food.
MeaningMnemonic: The weight of <radical>life</radical> on your <radical>sword</radical> is <radical>big</radical> when you <kanji>pledge</kanji> to protect your city. That's the big, big weight of every single person's life in that city pressing down on you every time you take out that sword.

MeaningHint: This is a pretty big favor. Imagine running through a rice paddy carrying a cross with a warm beating heart on it. Thump thump. Thump thump.
ReadingHint: Imagine how dumb you feel at this miscommunication. Eh? You don’t feel dumb? Well you should!
ReadingMnemonic: You arrive at your friend’s place and you can finally return the <kanji>favor</kanji> you owe him by presenting him with the heart. “<reading>Eh</reading> (え)? What’s this? I didn’t ask for this!” “Eh!?!?” you reply. Apparently he didn’t want a real heart, he wanted a candy one. He wanted a Valentine!
MeaningMnemonic: You carry a <radical>cross</radical> across a <radical>rice paddy</radical> with a <radical>heart</radical> on it to return a <kanji>favor</kanji> for your friend. The heart needs to be purified so that’s why you have it on the cross and your friend lives on a rice paddy farm so that’s why you’re running through that. The favor you are paying your friend back with is getting him this heart. He needs a new one and you’re providing it.

MeaningHint: Picture it like you're a baby who is new to hugs. You start with your fingers, spread them out and move forward. Now wrap all that stuff attached to your fingers around another person. You did it! You're hugging now!
ReadingHint: Imagine getting a nice, big hug from your dad. Mm. It's cozy and feels like home.
ReadingMnemonic: Who do you love to <kanji>hug</kanji> the most? Your <reading>da</reading>d (だ)! He gives the best hugs. They're warm and big and tight and comforting. The kind of hug dads are really good at giving.
MeaningMnemonic: Take your <radical>fingers</radical> and <radical>wrap</radical> them around another person. This is called a <kanji>hug</kanji>. Just make sure you include all the stuff attached to your fingers, like your hands and arms too, otherwise it's more of a squeeze, and we don't want to do that to people.

MeaningHint: Picture the sun reflecting off your metal prison spoon as you hold it aloft in your fingers. It hurts your eyes a little, but it also draws everyone's attention to this spoon you want to display.
ReadingHint: It's gotta be a spoon because forks are too pointy and dangerous for prison, by the way. So imagine eating your cake with the spoon you just displayed, big spoonfuls of cake frosting filling your mouth with every bite.
ReadingMnemonic: The reason you <kanji>display</kanji> it is to get some <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい). In this prison, if you display your spoon deftly enough, the wardens bring you some cake as a reward.
MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>fingers</radical> when the <radical>sun</radical> shines into your <radical>prison</radical> cell and hold your <radical>spoon</radical> up in full <kanji>display</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It's much easier to aim for an animal wearing a top hat than one without.
ReadingHint: Your aim with the soap is perfect! The soap knocked the animal's hat right off.
ReadingMnemonic: Ready, steady, <kanji>aim</kanji>... fire! You throw a bar of <reading>so</reading>ap (そ) at the animal. Yes, soap. You were sitting in your bathtub, washing and scrubbing, then the animal in the top hat caught your eye.
MeaningMnemonic: You see an <radical>animal</radical> wearing a <radical>top hat</radical> and decide to <kanji>aim</kanji> at it. The top hat is a perfect, giant target you use to take down this animal.

MeaningHint: Give a yakuza a spoon and you always get 死 (death), in this case twenty times. So it's honestly not surprising that things would end with commemorative flowers and a burial.
ReadingHint: I guess the twenty souls couldn't go up to... wherever... until they had a proper burial.
ReadingMnemonic: Once the <kanji>burial</kanji> is complete the <reading>sou</reading>ls (そう) of all the dead people come floating up and out of the ground. "Thaaannk YooOoOO~" they moan, as they ascend to the sky.
MeaningMnemonic: You brought <radical>flowers</radical> to leave at the place where a <radical>yakuza</radical> took a <radical>spoon</radical> and killed <radical>twenty</radical> people, since there's now a <kanji>burial</kanji> happening there. You didn't know these people, but you still want to leave flowers at their burial.

MeaningHint: Picture feeding some thread into the mouth of a worm as the light from the full moon shines in on you. See the silk come out of the worm's butt and into your hands.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself wearing nothing but silk. Silk shirt, silk pants, even silk underwear. It feels great against the skin! And most importantly, you're not nude.
ReadingMnemonic: Wearing <kanji>silk</kanji> is the <reading>key</reading> (き) to not being <reading>nude</reading> (ぬ). Wear it!
MeaningMnemonic: Put <radical>thread</radical> in the <radical>mouth</radical> of a worm while the <radical>moon</radical> is out and it will poop out some <kanji>silk</kanji>. That's why they call them silkworms, didn't you know? But it has to be while the moon is out, otherwise normal thread will come right back out the other end of the worm. The moon is what makes the magic happen.

MeaningHint: Look at yourself in the mirror. You can see the stick holding up the flag and it's very clear you drew some eyebrows onto the flag with some markers, but you still look pretty good (in your opinion).
ReadingHint: Imagine walking around town, holding your eyebrow flags. You can feel the meat sticking above your eyes. They stink and they're weirdly lukewarm, but you know your real eyebrows will grow under them any day now.
ReadingMnemonic: Underneath your fake <kanji>eyebrows</kanji> are two slabs of <reading>me</reading>at (み). Meat helps hair grow, according to many Internet specialists who also happen to sell meat. You're waiting for this meat to foster eyebrow hair, and when that sweet day comes you won't need these flags.
MeaningMnemonic: You're holding a <radical>flag</radical> on a <radical>stick</radical> above your <radical>eye</radical>s to imitate <kanji>eyebrows</kanji>. You can't grow any of your own, but you really love eyebrows, so this was the best you could do.

MeaningHint: Go out into the rain and shake your rake. Imagine shouting your demand at the sky as the rain pelts you from above.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself demanding more rain from the sky, trying to fill up your tiny juice box with what little juice rain you were able to get.
ReadingMnemonic: Now imagine the rain changing. Wait a second, this isn't rain, it's <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ)! You shake your rake harder, and <kanji>demand</kanji> that the juice rain continues. It doesn't listen to your demands and stops as soon as you start bottling it. Well, at least you have enough for one, really small juice box.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>rain</radical> I take my <radical>rake</radical> and shake it, making a <kanji>demand</kanji> that the rain stop! I demand this rain stop right now! Don’t make me shake my rake more violently than I am currently shaking it!

MeaningHint: You get baby age tooth orders (to get your first teeth). You get child age tooth orders (to get your adult teeth). Then you get old age tooth orders (when you start losing your teeth). There are tooth orders for every age.
ReadingHint:
Decide what age you have to be to get your first ray gun, and then imagine getting it when you hit that age. Perhaps you use this ray gun on your teeth, to keep them clean.

Hint: when you see the radical <radical>orders</radical> it's fairly likely the reading will be れい.
ReadingMnemonic: Gaining and losing teeth aren't the only things dependent on <kanji>age</kanji>. There is also an age in which you get your first <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun.
MeaningMnemonic: You get <radical>tooth</radical> <radical>orders</radical>. You get these tooth orders various times in your life, depending on your <kanji>age</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Giving your best regards is basically just tipping your hat and showing the roof of it.
ReadingHint: YOLO means "you only live once," so in a way that's a good way to give your best regards. At the very least, it's an interesting way.
ReadingMnemonic: When you give your <kanji>best regards</kanji> you always yell <reading>YOLO</reading> (よろ) to the other person.
MeaningMnemonic: By showing the <radical>roof</radical> of your <radical>top hat</radical> you send your <kanji>best regards</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine carefully dripping syrup onto a thread and then spinning it in a circle.
ReadingHint: Imagine eating a syrup cookie you made by spinning it yourself. Homemade is always better than store-bought.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>spin</kanji> the syrup, it begins to change shape and forms into a <reading>coo</reading>kie (く)! Haven't you ever made syrup-spun cookies before? The spinning forms and cooks them in the air.
MeaningMnemonic: You have <radical>thread</radical> and some <radical>syrup</radical> and you need to <kanji>spin</kanji> them together. Drip the syrup onto the thread and spin it around and around over your head.

MeaningHint: Imagine riding your scooter, dodging multiple hot peppers as they're tossed at you. A few of them get stuck in the wheels and keep coming around and around, and you have to dodge them too.
ReadingHint: Feel the heat flow through your body. It's getting harder to dodge as the heat grows and you start sweating!
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>dodge</kanji> the hot peppers, you feel a strong <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) fill your body. You can't tell if this heat is from the effort from dodging or if you didn't dodge right and one of the hot peppers touched you. Or maybe the scooter is overheating.
MeaningMnemonic: You're on a <radical>scooter</radical> and suddenly someone throws a <radical>hot pepper</radical> at you and you have to <kanji>dodge</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: The king radical is slightly altered in this kanji, so just remember that the king is a strange alien and not human. Imagine being this woman and having this half-breed alien baby inside you. Feels weird.
ReadingHint: What game are you playing on the Nintendo? Maybe it’s a game where you kill alien invaders? That’s messed up.
ReadingMnemonic: Being <kanji>pregnant</kanji> with this strange creature, you need to do something to calm you down. You should play <reading>Nin</reading>tendo (にん). Nintendo really calms your nerves and makes being pregnant with some mutant baby not seem like that big of a deal anymore.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> sleeps with the <radical>king</radical> and becomes <kanji>pregnant</kanji>. Only the king wasn’t human and now the woman is pregnant with a hybrid alien baby!

MeaningHint: Picture an ultrasound of the pregnant woman, but instead of a little baby heartbeat, you hear a scary roaring sound. That's what being pregnant is like. Waiting for the landslide to come.
ReadingHint: Imagine being pregnant and getting kicked by the shins of your baby all day, but especially all night. You're so tired. Please let this landslide come already.
ReadingMnemonic: When you're <kanji>pregnant</kanji> the baby is constantly kicking you with their <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Not their feet, their shins. And shins are really hard, let me tell you.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> who has a <radical>landslide</radical> getting ready inside her is <kanji>pregnant</kanji>. And that baby is gonna come out like a landslide.

MeaningHint: They do most of their own chores during the rest of the year, so winter is when you should ask them to do things for you.
ReadingHint: Moles will do anything for cheese, but honestly, wouldn't we all?
ReadingMnemonic: To get the mole to <kanji>do</kanji> what you want, just give it a nice big hunk of <reading>chee</reading>se (ち).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>mole</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> will <kanji>do</kanji> whatever you ask, if you know how to ask it.

MeaningHint: You're really into this magazine, and when you heard there's a new edition of it, you went out and bought it right away.
ReadingHint: The article on Genghis Khan was perhaps the best written article in the Dry Knife series to date. How did this magazine get such a big interview?
ReadingMnemonic: In this <kanji>edition</kanji> of "The Dry Knife," there is a huge article written by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) on how he kept his knives dry while campaigning.
MeaningMnemonic: You got your copy of "The <radical>Dry</radical> <radical>Knife</radical>." It's the newest <kanji>edition</kanji> of the magazine you love on keeping your knives dry.

MeaningHint: Close your eyes and imagine all of the spring sounds that come down to you from heaven in the springtime. Mmm, you can almost smell the damp soil and rain.
ReadingHint: Picture all of the souls in heaven playing spring music together!
ReadingMnemonic: In heaven, the ones that <kanji>play music</kanji> are the <reading>sou</reading>ls (そう) of the dead. They have a huge band up there, and even though it just seems like normal spring sounds to you, up there in heaven when the souls play music it's more like a huge, 100-part band!
MeaningMnemonic: When it's <radical>spring</radical> in <radical>heaven</radical> they <kanji>play music</kanji>. To your mortal, human ears it sounds more like snow melting, birds chirping, and plants waking up.

MeaningHint: Imagine you are one of the leader’s men who volunteered. What made you want to accompany him? Imagine the good qualities of this leader and how he inspired you to accompany him on this mission.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself as you accompany Han Solo. You try to stay calm, but you’re just so excited to be around Han Solo.
ReadingMnemonic: The leader you decided to <kanji>accompany</kanji> is none other than <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo. Who could be more inspiring? I’m surprised not more than half decided to accompany him. Han Solo is awesome.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> asks <radical>half</radical> of his men to <kanji>accompany</kanji> him on a secret mission. The leader’s mission is volunteer only, so it’s impressive that he got half of his men to accompany him.

MeaningHint: Imagine this shady leader approaching you in the night. The lantern light shines in your face. Would you join him?
ReadingHint: The last thing you wanted to do was join that guy so you hid in a haystack to avoid him.
ReadingMnemonic: The leader guy approaches you out of the darkness, his lantern held high. Oh no, not that guy. You've heard about his horn problem and you don't want to <kanji>join</kanji> him. You look around and spot a large <reading>hay</reading>stack (へい). You quickly jump into it and hide from him.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> takes a <radical>lantern</radical> and searches for people to <kanji>join</kanji> him. He needs more people to join his cause so he can get his horns removed. He’s embarrassed of them which is why he only travels at night, hence the lantern.

MeaningHint: Why do you want this geoduck to lean? Try to come up with your own reason. And why don't you look up what a geoduck looks like one more time for good measure. You don't want to be able to get this image out of your head.
ReadingHint: Watch as the geoduck slowly leans toward the cake, trying to get at it for a taste. But don't let it eat any! Keep putting the cake further and further away so the geoduck has to lean more.
ReadingMnemonic: To get the geoduck to <kanji>lean</kanji> you put a piece of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい) on one of its sides.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>change</radical> this <radical>geoduck</radical> because you want it to <kanji>lean</kanji>. Right now it's standing up straight, but you want it to lean to one side instead.

MeaningHint: Look at this stamp. There's Columbus being hugged by a bunch of South American natives. Yikes, that's contrary to how things went, that's for sure.
ReadingHint: Be contrary to the evildoers of the past and be like Kyaku instead.
ReadingMnemonic: Don't be the type of person who acts <kanji>contrary</kanji> to how they should and instead be like <reading>Kyaku</reading> (きゃく) the customer. They're always so nice and polite and they don't give diseases to entire populations of people.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Past</radical> <radical>stamp</radical>s usually depict images <kanji>contrary</kanji> to current views.

MeaningHint: Imagine being in the interior of someone’s tummy. See the big pieces of rice all around you as drops of stomach fluid continue to drop down on your head.
ReadingHint: If you’re unfamiliar with this Oklahoma song, check it out on YouTube. Imagine being in the interior of someone’s belly as you hear this song booming all around you.
ReadingMnemonic: While you’re in the <kanji>interior</kanji> of this guy, you hear him break out in song. He starts singing “<reading>Ok</reading>lahoma” (おく) at the top of his lungs. The big pieces of rice slosh around as he sings and dances. “OOOOk-lahoma, where the wind comes sweepin' down the plain!”
MeaningMnemonic: If there’s a <radical>drop</radical> on your <radical>head</radical> and the <radical>rice</radical> is <radical>big</radical>, you know you’re in the <kanji>interior</kanji>. And by interior, I mean you’re actually inside someone. The rice is big because you’ve shrunk to a tiny size and are inside someone’s stomach. The drop on your head is actually digestive fluid.
