MeaningHint: Look at this tiger as it approaches you. Think what things it has to consider. It has to consider how big of a threat you are and so on. And you need to consider how you're going to escape from this tiger.
ReadingHint: Imagine your relief when you realize this tiger isn't considering you as prey, but is considering whether to visit Rio de Janeiro and just wants your advice. There are no tigers in South America, so I guess it's natural for one to consider Rio in its vacation plans.
ReadingMnemonic: Finally, the tiger speaks: "I'm <kanji>consider</kanji>ing going to <reading>Rio</reading> (りょ). Have you ever been?"
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical> needs to <radical>think</radical> and <kanji>consider</kanji> its actions before it attacks. Tigers are smart hunters, so they need to consider all options and really think through a situation.

MeaningHint: You see the suspended heart in the distance, and make your way towards it.
ReadingHint: "I've protected this suspended heart for you, for you are next in the lineage to receive it." He grabs the heart and throws it into your body.
ReadingMnemonic: You arrive at the <kanji>suspend</kanji>ed heart. There in front of it, protecting it, is <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai.
MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>prefecture</radical>, you come from a long <radical>lineage</radical> of people who share one <radical>heart</radical>. When the heart is passed on to the next in line, it flies out of their body, and <kanji>suspend</kanji>s itself in the sky, for you to take.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing all these doors, this cluster of doors. You feel confused at which one to enter but finally pick one. Hear the door creak as you open it.
ReadingHint: Watch as clusters of pins fall when the bowling balls hit them. You back up and peek through one of the other doors in the cluster and it also houses a bowling alley. Huh.
ReadingMnemonic: After you make it through the door, you come across another <kanji>cluster</kanji> of doors. Which one to choose? There's a loud thunderous sound coming from behind one and you decide to open it. Behind it, there's an entire <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) alley and people bowling, loudly!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>door</radical> in that <radical>direction</radical> is among a <kanji>cluster</kanji> of doors. You run in the direction of the cluster and one door stands out to you. You enter it.

MeaningHint: Imagine being on an escalator and wrapping your fingers around a small handle on your way up or down. It's still a bit precarious, truth be told.
ReadingHint: 暑か? Is the handle hot? Think about it. Is it? What does your imagination tell you?
ReadingMnemonic: You put your hand on the <kanji>handle</kanji> and an old person leans in close to you and says, "<reading>暑か</reading>?" (あつか). He wants to know if the handle is ... hot? Before you can respond he says it again, and again! “暑か？！ 暑か？！？！ 暑か？！？！？！” he's shouting as you slowly ride the escalator away from him.
MeaningMnemonic: Where do you put your <radical>fingers</radical> when you're on an <radical>escalator</radical>? On the <kanji>handle</kanji>! Okay, so maybe it's not a handle and more of a railing, but for the sake of this kanji, picture a handle instead.

MeaningHint: Imagine suppressing a cat pirate with your own fingers. His fur is so soft, but his nails are so sharp!
ReadingHint: Defend the yolk! Smell the smell of eggs in the air as you suppress the cat pirate. Maybe even share the yolk with him, once he's done trying to kill you for it. And then enjoy breakfast while petting your new cat friend.
ReadingMnemonic: He forgot, but you know you had to <kanji>suppress</kanji> the cat pirate because he was trying to steal your egg <reading>yolk</reading> (よく). You were making breakfast on your ship and the cat pirate boarded you out of nowhere, and tried to take it by force.
MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>fingers</radical> on the <radical>cat pirate</radical> to <kanji>suppress</kanji> him. The cat pirate was attacking you, so you took your fingers and pet his head and scratched his chin. Now he's calm and suppressed. He doesn't even remember why he was attacking you.

MeaningHint: Feel your fingers touching each and every shrimp, one by one, until they graze the one you select as the perfect shrimp. Slowly pull it out and lower it into your mouth. Munch munch... ahhh! The perfect shrimp.
ReadingHint: Oh, woe is me! How will I select the perfect shrimp with my fingers if there are tacks in the shrimp bowls! Boohoohoo!
ReadingMnemonic: You do this at every restaurant, party, and indoor sporting event and people start getting wise to your shrimp <kanji>select</kanji>ion methods. One day, as you attempt to select your shrimp, a sharp pain shoots through your finger. Ow! You pull your hand back to find... a <reading>tack</reading> (たく)!
MeaningMnemonic: Shove your <radical>fingers</radical> into a bowl of <radical>shrimp</radical> to <kanji>select</kanji> the best one. You know your shrimp and you know them well, so you won't settle for anything less than the best. Put your whole arm in there and feel around until you can select the one you want most.

MeaningHint: Imagine using your fingers to draw these rice paddy flowers. Don't worry about how well you can draw. These flowers will look beautiful no matter how you draw them.
ReadingHint: To be fair, would you want to look at a drawing that makes you constantly smell stinky B.O.? I didn't think so.
ReadingMnemonic: Even though you tried to <kanji>draw</kanji> the flowers as best you could, whenever someone looks at it they smell <reading>B.O.</reading> (びょう). Maybe it's because you were drawing for a really long time and kept sweating. Maybe it's because you are just a stinky person. But no one wants to look at your drawing for long because all they can smell is B.O.
MeaningMnemonic: With your <radical>fingers</radical> you pluck some <radical>flowers</radical> in a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, in order to <kanji>draw</kanji> them. Have you ever seen rice paddy flowers? They're beautiful. And if you can draw them, you can capture that beauty and show it to everyone.

MeaningHint: A big boat shaped like a plate is just a tray. You keep your weapons on it, nice and flat.
ReadingHint: Your ban from the Small Boat Tray Association has led to a massive feud between large boat tray enthusiasts and small boat tray enthusiasts, and it seems like it'll never end—leading to bans all over the place. If only we could all come together as enthusiasts of boat trays of all sizes...
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, this boat <kanji>tray</kanji> is so huge, it's gotten you <reading>ban</reading>ned (ばん) from the Small Boat Tray Association. Those pesky small boat tray enthusiasts...
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>boat</radical> holds your <radical>weapon</radical> like a <radical>plate</radical>. So in a way, it's just a <kanji>tray</kanji> for your weapon.

MeaningHint: This isn't exactly a title you're proud of, in fact, you're really embarrassed. Maybe you can make a name for yourself in something else so you can get a new title.
ReadingHint: The plaque says, "No one is more deserving of this SAD title." - Love, the Shougun.
ReadingMnemonic: Word of your SAD <kanji>title</kanji> makes it all the way to the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) and he travels out to see you. When he arrives at your door, with all of his fancy escorts, he hands you a plaque with your title on it.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>grain</radical> and fashion it into a <radical>gun</radical> that is <radical>small</radical>, earning you the <kanji>title</kanji> of Small Arms Dealer (or SAD). Congratulations!

MeaningHint: Imagine taking that cord and wrapping it around someone (with their permission, of course). Feel your fingers tightening a knot in the cord. Then tie the other end around yourself. Is this some freaky ritual to deepen your connection to them, or… ? Hey, no judgment from me.
ReadingHint: Imagine doing this cord show on stage. Feel the heat of the stage lights and the gaze of the audience as your tie your cord. What are they expecting? Why did they pay to see this? Must be some kind of modern performance art — let's go with that.
ReadingMnemonic: Actually, you're tying this <kanji>cord</kanji> around someone as part of a <reading>show</reading> (しょ). What kind of a show? I really shouldn't say. This cord show isn't free, I'll tell you that much.
MeaningMnemonic:
The kind of <radical>thread</radical> you'd wrap around <radical>someone</radical> would have to be a <kanji>cord</kanji>. A regular thread wouldn't be strong enough, so you'd definitely use a cord. 

This can also mean <kanji>connection</kanji>, since cords create connections (by tying things together).

MeaningHint: Look at this football. The thread is loose on the football and needs to be tightened. Attempt to tighten the loose thread on the football as you hold it in your hands.
ReadingHint: Listen to your friends shouting “You rule!” and hear their cheers echo in your ears as you toss the football around again.
ReadingMnemonic: You finally tighten the <kanji>loose</kanji> thread and all of your friends cheer, “<reading>You ru</reading>le!” (ゆる) You rule because you fixed the loose thread on the football and now you can play again. Yeah! You rule, man!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> on this <radical>football</radical> is <kanji>loose</kanji>. We need to tighten up the loose thread on this football or it’s gonna fall apart while we’re playing.

MeaningHint: Imagine what seven things you consign over to this other person for safekeeping. Visualize them in your head as you slide each one down the slide.
ReadingHint: Let’s imagine what it would be like to hit this tack while sliding on the slide. It would hurt. Good thing you are consigning it over to someone. Probably safer for everyone that way.
ReadingMnemonic: One of the seven things you <kanji>consign</kanji> over to this guy is a <reading>tack</reading> (たく). It’s the sharpest tack you’ve ever seen. You slide the tack down the slide. Good thing no one is on the slide now, sitting on that tack would hurt.
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> if you put things on a <radical>slide</radical>, <radical>seven</radical> things to be exact, that’s the best way to <kanji>consign</kanji> goods to someone. They say if you consign seven things to someone, you should send them over on a slide. Slide ‘em on down. All seven of ‘em.

MeaningHint: Imagine bribing a bunch of yakuza and politicians with all the shellfish you have. Ohohoh, you'll never be caught!
ReadingHint:
Yeah really, why? Why bribe people at all. You could have just been rolling in shellfish dough. Why risk everything?

And you counter with.

Why not?
ReadingMnemonic: Welp, the cops found out about your <kanji>bribe</kanji>, that didn't last long, and now your entire family is watching you get put into the police car screaming, "<reading>WHY</reading> (わい)?!
MeaningMnemonic: The more <radical>shellfish</radical> you <radical>have</radical>, the more you can <kanji>bribe</kanji> people. That worked in the olden days when shells were money, and today too if the people you want to bribe really like seafood.

MeaningHint: You're like beach santa, except you're real and you wear a mask made of shellfish.
ReadingHint:
Ooh hoo, it's me! the Shellfish Mask Man!
I go to beach houses and leave presents where I can!
The presents are not clothes, or anything good.
I don't even care if you leave me some food.
Try whipping some up, try cooking me peas,
It matters not, I'll leave you zombies!

Tee hee hee hee hee!
ReadingMnemonic: The "<kanji>presents</kanji>" you leave? They are actually just <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう). You leave zombies in people's houses.
MeaningMnemonic: You put on the <radical>shellfish</radical> <radical>mask</radical> once per year and break into people's beach houses, leaving behind <kanji>presents</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Unfortunately, the omen is riding a scooter too, so you're going to have to be crafty to escape this omen properly.
ReadingHint: Imagine scooting in and out of とうきょう traffic, the omen looming ever further back behind you. You're doing it! You're escaping!
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to <kanji>escape</kanji> this omen by weaving in and out of the busy streets of <reading>とう</reading>きょう. By now, you know とうきょう like the back of your hand, so you're sure you can escape this way.
MeaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>scooter</radical> when the <radical>omen</radical> comes so that you can <kanji>escape</kanji>.

MeaningHint: You want to send back this zombie the way it came. You definitely don't want it loose in your neighborhood.
ReadingHint: You managed to send back the zombie, good for you! Genghis Khan would be proud.
ReadingMnemonic: As you prepare to <kanji>send back</kanji> the zombie, you imagine yourself as Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). On your horse (it's just your own scooter) you stab forward with your spear (stick), swing the scooter the zombie is on, and send it back from whence it came!
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>scooter</radical> coming toward you with a <radical>net</radical> trapping a weird <radical>zombie</radical> on it. You don't want this here, so you decide to <kanji>send back</kanji> the zombie. (It's a weird zombie because it has the ground on top of its head instead of dirt!)

MeaningHint: Tigers are pretty intimidating just on their own, let alone with spikey armor. Imagine this tiger, covered in spikes. It’s roaring at you. Pretty scary, huh?
ReadingHint: Picture this grand match taking place in Little Kyoto. Actual tigers versus the Hanshin Tigers. It’s truly a sight to behold. The void tigers are armed with their spikes and the Hanshin Tigers are armed with their bats. Hear the crack of wood and the roar of tigers in the air as the battle commences.
ReadingMnemonic: The aforementioned <kanji>void</kanji> has opened up in Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). The spiked void tigers chose to come here in order to face off against the Hanshin Tigers baseball team in battle. It will be a vicious match here in Little Kyoto. Since this is taking place in Little Kyoto and not Japan itself, the Hanshin Tigers won't have the home field advantage. Who will win?
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical>, clad in <radical>spikes</radical>, exists in the <kanji>void</kanji>. This tiger is from another dimension where tigers rule the earth, clad in armor with spikes. The void has allowed this tiger to cross dimensional planes.

MeaningHint: Imagine standing at the top of the building and looking down at the three slides that stretch off toward your home country. It takes a few hours to slide all the way there, but at the end you'll be home.
ReadingHint: Even if you move, even if you haven't been back in years, your home country will always be your home in some way, deep down.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>home country</kanji> is your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう)!
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>three</radical> <radical>slide</radical>s sticking out of this <radical>building</radical> that lead to your <kanji>home country</kanji>. The rest of the slides go to other places, but three are yours.

MeaningHint: Imagine the gold, heavy in your pockets, as you construct this buzzer with your bare hands. Feel the cool metal clink around in your hands as you put it together.
ReadingHint: Wait, should this buzzer be buzzing or ringing? Oh well, you got your gold. It’s the client’s problem now.
ReadingMnemonic: Finally the <kanji>buzzer</kanji> is done. You’ve earned your gold. You test the buzzer and it goes <reading>rin</reading>g (りん)! Ring! Ring! Ring! Ring ring ring ring ring! Man, that ringing is annoying but hey, that’s what buzzers do.
MeaningMnemonic: You take <radical>gold</radical> in exchange for executing <radical>orders</radical> to construct a <kanji>buzzer</kanji>. You are a <kanji>small bell</kanji> maker and someone orders you to make one into a buzzer in exchange for some gold. This is how you make a living.

MeaningHint: Picture this part of the mountain, branching off from the rest, into a mysterious path of its own.
ReadingHint: If you branch off from the beaten path, you need 1) access, and 2) to be really careful so you keep your head.
ReadingMnemonic: If you want to <kanji>branch off</kanji> from your hiking group and explore this branch of the mountain, you need a special <reading>key</reading> (き). Just don't get caught down there or you'll get the <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>mountain</radical> <radical>branch</radical> will <kanji>branch off</kanji> from the rest.

MeaningHint: Picture a dark, bleak winter landscape. No life anywhere. Now turn around and look at the prosperity on display in the building you're in. Everyone is happy and eating food and not freezing to death.
ReadingHint: Ryu's prosperity has made him realize that having things isn't always enough. Now he's looking wistfully outside at the winter weather, missing the rough times.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>prosperity</kanji> isn't good for everyone though. You look over and see <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter sitting in the corner. He's got everything he could ask for. He's won the tournament, he's got money, he's got this nice big building, but now he's depressed.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>building</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> will allow <radical>life</radical> to have <kanji>prosperity</kanji>. Before we had buildings it was impossible to have any prosperity in winter because everything was cold and most of us died.

MeaningHint: Imagine hundreds of turkeys walking in. The word has gotten out amongst the turkey community that if they walk through your door, you'll employ them.
ReadingHint: "I will employ turkeys, but I will never employ children!" you exclaim.
ReadingMnemonic: Although you <kanji>employ</kanji> turkeys, when a <reading>子</reading> (こ) walks through your door, you send them away.
MeaningMnemonic: In through your <radical>door</radical> walks a <radical>turkey</radical>, so you <kanji>employ</kanji> it. You've always employed any turkey that comes through that door.

MeaningHint: Abstaining from doing what other people are doing is important sometimes. So make sure you never forget the universal abstaining symbol.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself in Central America. Standing in the water, blocking poachers from the hicatee turtles. "Abstain!" you shout at them.
ReadingMnemonic: This symbol to <kanji>abstain</kanji> comes from the <reading>hica</reading>tee (ひか) turtle. The hicatee is a Central American river turtle hunted for its meat, eggs, and shell. Therefore, the poor hicatee are endangered. So people started to rally around them, abstaining from hurting them anymore. They raised their fingers to the sky and shouted, "I abstain from harming these hicatee!"
MeaningMnemonic: Raise your <radical>fingers</radical> to the <radical>sky</radical> to symbolize that you <kanji>abstain</kanji> from something. If someone offers you fur coats, raise your fingers to abstain. If someone tries to get you to bully someone, raise your fingers to abstain.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself walking around and seeing a wall around some dirt. You look inside and there are some hot peppers hiding just below the surface. Phew! Good thing this wall was here to protect you from them.
ReadingHint: Picture some corn cobs. Now picture some hot peppers. Put a wall between them.
ReadingMnemonic: On the other side of this <kanji>wall</kanji> there are much less threatening <reading>cob</reading>s (かべ) of corn. Corn isn't spicy. It's delicious and sweet, so it's free from being walled in.
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>hot peppers</radical> hiding in this <radical>dirt</radical>, so you build a <kanji>wall</kanji> to protect yourself from them. Have you ever stepped on a really hot pepper? The oils bleed into your shoes and burn your feet! You don't want anyone stepping on these so you wall them up.

MeaningHint: Imagine finding this tree crab in your games cabinet trying to make off with your chess piece. This tree crab is big and you weren’t expecting to find it in your games cabinet so you’re kind of terrified. You scream in horror.
ReadingHint: You put the key in your pocket and call animal control. They unlock the cabinet with the key and remove the tree crab from your home. But who’s to say he won’t be back??
ReadingMnemonic: Before the tree crab can make it out with the <kanji>chess piece</kanji>, you shut the door to the games cabinet and lock it with a <reading>key</reading> (き), trapping the tree crab inside. Now what?
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> <radical>crab</radical> breaks into your games cabinet and steals a <kanji>chess piece</kanji>. He’s trying to impress all his tree crabs back home by showing how not afraid of humans he is. Plus the rest of the tree crabs need one last chess piece to complete their set.

MeaningHint: You're reluctant to face this bitter end at the hands of a tsunami.
ReadingHint: Try to stuff some of the jewels into your bitter mouth before the tsunami stops you.
ReadingMnemonic: With the <kanji>bitter</kanji> taste in your mouth, you <kanji>reluctant</kanji>ly gather up all of your <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう) and wait for the tsunami to take you away. At least with your jewels with you, there may be some hope of selling them and building a better life if you make it.
MeaningMnemonic:
There's a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming and you can't <radical>stop</radical> the <radical>explosion</radical> of <kanji>bitter</kanji>ness in your mouth. You're scared. Your mouth tastes bitter and <kanji>astringent</kanji>.

This word also means <kanji>reluctant</kanji>, because wouldn't you be reluctant to let a tsunami come wash you away with that bitter explosion of flavor in your mouth?

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine a one sided situation that you've had. Now imagine the winning side with a catapult. For example, a baseball game where one team is destroying the other. That's because instead of a pitcher, they have a catapult.
ReadingMnemonic: Think of anything that is <kanji>one sided</kanji>. A battle. A sports game. A relationship. What makes them so one sided? One side always has a <reading>cata</reading>pult (かた).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>one sided</radical> radical and the <kanji>one sided</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Imagine following your sheep buddy back to his flock. Feel the soft fur of all the sheep around you as they baa peacefully.
ReadingHint: Feel the recoil of the gun in your hands and the ringing of the shots in your ears as you slaughter the flock without remorse. BANG BANG BANG!
ReadingMnemonic: Now take out your <reading>gun</reading> (ぐん) and murder every sheep in the <kanji>flock</kanji>. Except for your buddy. You’re incredibly jealous of your buddy and you don’t like the attention his flock gives him so that’s why you decided to murder them all with a gun.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>buddy</radical> the <radical>sheep</radical> is part of a <kanji>flock</kanji>. A flock of sheep. Your sheep buddy is a member of a flock.

MeaningHint: Imagine this odd hermit leading mountain goats around the mountain, marching to the beat of a drum. It’s a strange sight indeed.
ReadingHint: Picture this old hermit fighting this centaur in a duel. Who do you think is gonna win? Hear the hermit screaming in agony as the centaur demolishes him.
ReadingMnemonic: One day the <kanji>hermit</kanji> was leading the mountain goats around and he ran into a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). The centaur said that he was the rightful leader of these mountain goats so the centaur challenged the hermit to a duel.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> who lives in a <radical>mountain</radical> is a <kanji>hermit</kanji>. The only thing this hermit is a leader of now is mountain goats. This hermit dislikes people so that’s why he lives in a mountain now.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Picture all the jewels you were able to buy because you allocated your funds correctly. Imagine swimming in these jewels. Like a rich duck.
ReadingMnemonic: If you <kanji>allocate</kanji> your funds correctly you'll be able to buy as many precious <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう) as you want! However, jewels are expensive, so if you don't allocate any money, you won't be able to afford any. Ever.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>allocate</radical> radical and the <kanji>allocate</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, stop making excuses.
ReadingHint: Imagine all the men you’ve talked to in your life. Think of every excuse they’ve told you. Think of the worst excuse you’ve ever heard. Remember that.
ReadingMnemonic: Who always makes <kanji>excuse</kanji> after excuse? <reading>Men</reading> (めん)! They have an excuse for everything. Why didn’t you take out the trash? Why did you forget my birthday? It’s just excuse after excuse with men. So I’ve heard, anyway.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>excuse</radical> radical and the <kanji>excuse</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Dandy!

MeaningHint: On the map, there is a circle drawn around your mouth showing what range your mouth exists in. Thankfully, your tongue and teeth are within that sphere.
ReadingHint: Everything else on the map was accurate, so you take a close look in the mirror, and there he is: a tiny Ken the samurai, protecting the range that is your mouth. He gives you a small nod and goes back to his post.
ReadingMnemonic: Looking at the <kanji>range</kanji> of your mouth on the map, you notice a tiny samurai marked near your molars. It is <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>mouth</radical> contains a <radical>scroll</radical>, which you pull out and look at. On the scroll is a map, showing the <kanji>range</kanji> of the <kanji>sphere</kanji> of your mouth.

MeaningHint: Imagine this turkey preparing to rob a place. See him with the tape measure, and hear his little gobbles of excitement as he realizes the safe here is just the right size for robbing.
ReadingHint: Picture this turkey with all the stuff he robbed, looking nervously over his shoulder from the back seat of his Uber.
ReadingMnemonic: Once he's able to <kanji>rob</kanji> the place, he gets away in an <reading>Uber</reading> (うば). He just called the Uber in advance and had it wait outside. Hey, if you're gonna rob a place, make sure you have an Uber.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>big</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> takes the <radical>measurement</radical>s of a safe before he can <kanji>rob</kanji> it. If the safe is too big it won't fit in his getaway car and the robbery will have been a failure. So before the turkey robs a place he needs to get those measurements.

MeaningHint: Imagine feeling nervous as the honorable person pulls up at the stop sign. Hear yourself humbly asking for their stamp as you hand the written request to them. And just who is this honorable being, anyway?
ReadingHint: Imagine the relief you'd feel as Godzilla hands the request back to you with its personal stamp — some kind of webbed claw print that basically blacks out the whole paper. Thank you, O honorable Godzilla.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>honorable</kanji> personage is <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご). Actually, you're requesting Godzilla's stamp of approval for a new demolition job in downtown Tokyo. Godzilla is the god of urban demolition, what with all those cities it's destroyed, and you need its honorable approval to continue.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>loiter</radical> at <radical>noon</radical> near a <radical>stop</radical> sign to get a <radical>stamp</radical> of approval from someone <kanji>honorable</kanji>. This honorable personage drives by at noon every day, and because they have to stop at the stop sign, people loiter here for a chance to get their honorable stamp of approval.

MeaningHint: Focus on the feeling of humility here. You're putting it all out there, you know? With nowhere to hide.
ReadingHint: Imagine humility literally moving through your shins. Can you still stand or do you need to fall to your shins, in humility?
ReadingMnemonic: As <kanji>humility</kanji> washes over you, you feel a shaking in your <reading>shin</reading>s (しん). Humility lives in your shins, so now they're shaking and trying to bring you down to the ground.
MeaningMnemonic: When someone wants to view your <radical>soul</radical>'s <radical>reality</radical>, show it with <kanji>humility</kanji>. Because the reality is... our souls are all a little dirty. And we haven't been the best versions we could be. So make sure when you show all of yourself you do so with some humility.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding up your fingers to show a giant you're refusing to let him do something. Yeesh, you're brave!
ReadingHint: Imagine standing in front of a giant, refusing to let him crush Little Kyoto. I hope he doesn't stomp on you instead.
ReadingMnemonic: What do you <kanji>refuse</kanji>? You refuse to let him stomp all over Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). Little Kyoto is your home! And if the giant comes any closer he's going to destroy all the little buildings and shrines and soba noodle shops.
MeaningMnemonic: Show your <radical>fingers</radical> to a <radical>giant</radical> to show you <kanji>refuse</kanji> him. Giants are very big. So big that when you talk they can't really hear what you're saying. Hold up all your fingers so that he can see what you mean.

MeaningHint: Feel the tree bark under your palms as you climb up the tree to take a frame. You get a splinter from grabbing the frame. It really smarts.
ReadingHint: Why is this frame so wack? Think about what makes it wack, to help you remember this reading.
ReadingMnemonic: You realize that the <kanji>frame</kanji> isn't half as nice as you thought it was, in fact it's totally <reading>wack</reading> (わく). How disappointing.
MeaningMnemonic: In a <radical>tree</radical> on <radical>nine</radical> branches are some <radical>cross</radical> <kanji>frame</kanji>s. Each cross frame is hung on the branches of the tree. You don’t know who decided to hang the frames there, but you think it might be some new wave art or something. Actually you need a frame, and there are already nine up there, so you decide to take one.

MeaningHint: Cowabunga! You get an A!
ReadingHint: Because this is WaniKani, こういち has probably set the turtle shell on fire, too. "Burn those turtle shells, burn those kanji!" he yells.
ReadingMnemonic: With a <kanji>turtle shell</kanji> on his back, imagine <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>turtle shell</radical> radical and the <kanji>turtle shell</kanji> kanji are exactly the same! Oddly, this can also be used to talk about the first item in a list, so to mean <kanji>A</kanji> out of A through D, for example.

MeaningHint: Can you see the patterns in the writing this criminal did? It looks like a quilt or some plaid. It's almost beautiful, the way those death threats are arranged!
ReadingHint: Picture an eagle patterned out of cut out letters. What the words say isn't important (though it is asking for a ransom on your grandma), but the underlying image is gorgeous.
ReadingMnemonic: As you look closer at the <kanji>patterned</kanji> writing you realize that it forms the shape of an <reading>eagle</reading> (い). The most American bird of them all! Maybe this guy isn't a criminal after all!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Criminal</radical> <radical>writing</radical> is always <kanji>patterned</kanji>. That is to say, there is a pattern to it, lots of ARGS and GONNA STAB YAs, but it's also patterned beautifully, with a collage of cut out letters.

MeaningHint: The next time you see someone on welfare, be nice. Their spirit stopped protecting them, so they need a little more help right now.
ReadingHint: If you've ever been on welfare you probably already know about the sheep.
ReadingMnemonic: While on <kanji>welfare</kanji>, you have to do the only job available to you: herding <reading>shee</reading>p (し). Sheep can help you out when you're having a rough time. Sheep provide warmth, wool for clothes, and even food, if you wanna get dark about it.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>spirit</radical> <radical>stop</radical>s protecting you, you'll have to go on <kanji>welfare</kanji>. If you can't rely on your family spirit, you'll have to have the help and protection come from somewhere else.

MeaningHint: When you look at a grain of rice, you should think about how it used to be a rice plant. That rice plant had a family! いいね, you think — that rice plant had a good life.
ReadingHint: As it reminisces it wants to say "good," but the いいね is tragically cut short… much like the rice plant's life. It doesn't even have the strength to say it properly, it has to say いね. Yes, little grain, your rice plant life really was いね. Because you… were an いね. I'm sorry.
ReadingMnemonic: The grain of rice thinks about its former life as a <kanji>rice plant</kanji> and, like you, mutters "<reading>いね</reading>." What's that, little rice grain? Are you trying to say life was good?
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>grain</radical> stuck in your <radical>cleat</radical> had a <radical>former</radical> life. In that life it was a <kanji>rice plant</kanji>. It was a happy little rice plant, minding its own business, when it was yanked up, cleaned, and put in a bag for people to buy and cook and eat. That was so long ago. Now all that's left is this sad loose grain in the bottom of your shoe.

MeaningHint: There's many situations where you'd want to defer to a nurse, like when somebody is seriously hurt and you don't know what to do to help them. Just imagine yourself saying "Nurse!" in order to defer to them for their medical knowledge.
ReadingHint: You’re starting to panic because you don’t know to help Joe, so you’re just going around saying “Nurse! Nurse!” repeatedly, desperately looking for someone you could defer to. Nurse!!!
ReadingMnemonic: The reason you’re trying to <kanji>defer</kanji> to a nurse is because you’re trying to save the local farmhand, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう). He’s seriously injured, and you don’t know how to help him, so you’ll have to defer to the knowledge of a nurse.
MeaningMnemonic: When you <radical>say</radical> "<radical>Nurse</radical>!" you're probably asking to <kanji>defer</kanji> to them in some way, shape, or form.

MeaningHint: Leonardo da Vinci picked her for her modesty. She's so modest she shows no emotion.
ReadingHint: Have you met Ken the samurai in person? He never smiles, and will never show you his emotions. He's too modest for that.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>modesty</kanji> of Mona Lisa inspired <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai to become more modest.
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>Mona Lisa</radical> had more <kanji>modesty</kanji> than any other woman at the time.

MeaningHint: Imagine kicking Wolverine in the head with your foot as the turkey makes turkey sounds in the background. He’s taller than you so you must leap as you kick.
ReadingHint: Leap! Leap onto the yak. Feel the yak fur beneath you as you ride away to safety, wind in your hair.
ReadingMnemonic: After you kick Wolverine and the turkey is safe, you <kanji>leap</kanji> onto a <reading>yak</reading> (やく) to make your getaway. This yak is really fast so you’ll have no problem outrunning Wolverine while riding it.
MeaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>foot</radical> to kick <radical>Wolverine</radical> in the face so a <radical>turkey</radical> can <kanji>leap</kanji> to freedom. This turkey is trying to escape from Wolverine by leaping over a fence. Wolverine doesn’t want the turkey to escape, but you kick him with your foot and the turkey is then free to leap to safety.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself handing over an armful of gold bars to buy a single gun.
ReadingHint: Picture an old fashioned holdup. You're holding a gun and shoveling jewels into a burlap sack. The heist of the century!
ReadingMnemonic: You need this <kanji>gun</kanji> so you can steal some <reading>jew</reading>els (じゅう)! Why didn't you just use your gold to buy the jewels, you ask? Because! That's not how we did things in olden days. When you want jewels you buy a gun with gold and rob a jewelry store!
MeaningMnemonic: Take some <radical>gold</radical> and <radical>allocate</radical> it to buy a <kanji>gun</kanji>. Guns are weapons of the old world, so you can't just buy them with paper money. Guns have to be bought with gold coins or bricks.

MeaningHint: Actually imagine yourself writing one paragraph of explanation to the city so you can get the permits to construct housing for your geoducks. On the form, it specifies you should only write a paragraph.
ReadingHint:
In that paragraph, you praised こういち for being a great emperor, and you said that this construction project would help you to have more time to do your WaniKani reviews. Clearly you were pandering to Emperor こういち when you wrote it. But hey, it worked!

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading.
ReadingMnemonic: You turn in your <kanji>paragraph</kanji>, and it is given to Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち, who insists on being involved in all construction projects. Surprisingly, he approves your construction project, thanks to that paragraph you wrote in the application.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Construction</radical> for <radical>geoduck</radical>s doesn't require as much paperwork as construction for humans, but you still need to write at least a <kanji>paragraph</kanji> to explain what you're doing.

MeaningHint: Dig out many other spots. Every time, it's just steel under there.
ReadingHint: "I suppose I can use this steel, even if it is not a hill of gold," he says. "We will make steel weapons, which we will use to subjugate other nations. The whole world will learn kanji using the WaniKani system!"
ReadingMnemonic: You report your findings about the <kanji>steel</kanji> you found to Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち. He is displeased that it is a hill of steel.
MeaningMnemonic: You come across a <radical>gold</radical> <radical>hill</radical>. You think you hit it big! You start digging, only to find out that it is merely a paper-thin layer of gold covering a hill of <kanji>steel</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the criminal, trapped inside the mouth. They don't even look sorry!
ReadingHint: Wearing nothing but shoes is also really uncomfortable too, so it's actually part of the criminal's punishment. They don't seem to know that though.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>criminal</kanji> is only wearing <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). They're completely naked otherwise. They have to wear shoes because the mouth jail would be too slippery for them to stand otherwise.
MeaningMnemonic: Inside this giant <radical>mouth</radical> is a <radical>person</radical>, trapped, because they're a <kanji>criminal</kanji>. They did something bad (they touched the mouth of another person without consent) and now they're in mouth jail for criminals.

MeaningHint: Imagine hiring a geoduck, then ask them to review your work. Please review my writing, geoduck. Please review my record-keeping, geoduck. Please review my taxes, geoduck. This geoduck you employed can review anything for you.
ReadingHint: The geoduck tells you that while it was reviewing your records, it found information about a child you have. Even you didn't know this, so you're surprised that this review geoduck found something like this.
ReadingMnemonic: As the geoduck <kanji>review</kanji>s your paperwork, it notices something surprising. You have a <reading>子</reading> (こ)!
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>employ</radical> a <radical>geoduck</radical> to <kanji>review</kanji> all your work.

MeaningHint: Imagine having a horse as your master. Hear him neigh out orders to you and the other residents of the horse master’s house.
ReadingHint: Imagine Chewbacca in a cage in the horse master’s house. You’ve proved yourself a worthy resident. Hear Chewbacca making his Wookie sounds as he bashes against the bars of his new cage.
ReadingMnemonic: To prove yourself a worthy <kanji>resident</kanji> of the horse master’s house, you must capture <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). Your horse master collects rare creatures, and Chewbacca is his latest desire. If you wish to remain a resident of the house, you must capture Chewbacca.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> is your <radical>master</radical> and you are merely a <kanji>resident</kanji> in his house. How did this horse become your master? Those aren’t questions a resident should ask! Know your place, resident!

MeaningHint: Hear the horse clip clopping around the district as it continues to gallop. Clip clop, clop clip clop!
ReadingHint: The tires screech, the horse neighs, BAM! Total destruction everywhere. Bystanders scream in horror. That horse will never gallop again. It’s a sad day for all horsekind.
ReadingMnemonic: Then the horse <kanji>gallop</kanji>s into the road and gets hit by a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Oh man, there’s blood everywhere. This horse is never going to gallop again. That car totally destroyed it.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> goes to his favorite <radical>district</radical> to <kanji>gallop</kanji> around. This district has lots of cool buildings and lights to look at, so this horse loves to gallop around there.

MeaningHint: See the pillar next to the tree master. See the pillar bow over and over in veneration of its tree master.
ReadingHint: Hear the crack and snap of branches as the tree beats the crap out of the pillar for having murdered Chewbacca. Unforgivable.
ReadingMnemonic: While bowing, the <kanji>pillar</kanji> accidentally smashes <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). The pillar is horrified for having murdered Chewbacca, his tree master’s closest friend. The pillar is going to be punished.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> is the <radical>master</radical> of a <kanji>pillar</kanji>. The pillar is subservient to its tree master because the tree is so great and awesome and big and cool and stuff. The pillar has great respect for its tree master.

MeaningHint: Open your mouth and practice the chant right now! Sun sun, sun sun, sun sun!
ReadingHint: The Shougun is glaring intently at you as you chant. Open your mouth as wide as you can, get all those "sun suns" out, and pray that the sun will appear.
ReadingMnemonic: You're doing this <kanji>chant</kanji> because the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) ordered you to. The sun hasn't been shining on him as much lately, and he's displeased. So now you have to chant for him.
MeaningMnemonic: It's time to do the <radical>mouth</radical> <radical>sun</radical> <radical>sun</radical> <kanji>chant</kanji>. That's when you use your mouth to chant sun sun, sun sun, sun sun, over and over until the sun appears.

MeaningHint: Respect your elders, for they have much to teach us.
ReadingHint: I am so ignorant and have much to learn. I must show filial piety to my father, こういち, so that he may teach me.
ReadingMnemonic: With <kanji>filial piety</kanji> in mind, you decide to go visit your father, who is <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Teacher</radical>s are all around us. Those who came before us are our teachers, so it's important to respect them. This is the basic idea of <kanji>filial piety</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing the leader of the spiders weaving it's own little spider computer. Doing spider math, and spider chemistry. It's a genius!
ReadingHint: Soon we'll be slaves to the genius spiders. Soon we'll be the flies.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch the spider <kanji>genius</kanji> you realize that <reading>soon</reading> (しゅん) spiders will take over the humans. They're just way smarter than us. Soon we'll be the bugs.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of all the <radical>spider</radical>s is a <kanji>genius</kanji>! Spiders are already pretty smart. They can weave complex homes and traps in one web. They even know where not to step so they don't get caught. But the leader knows everything. It made itself a little webbed computer and everything!

MeaningHint: Pay close attention to the students around you. Don't go too fast or too slow, or you won't finish concurrently.
ReadingHint: It's a nice painting, the frown is just right, and you finished concurrently with everyone else, but you're probably going to fail this class.
ReadingMnemonic: Your entire class finishes <kanji>concurrently</kanji>. You look at your finished result. Wait, that's not the Mona Lisa. You painted <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Mona Lisa</radical> is in front of you. "You must all paint the Mona Lisa <kanji>concurrently</kanji>," says your art class teacher.

MeaningHint: Watch as the dose of poison sinks into your body through the knife that's in your chest, as it simultaneously happens to the other guy too.
ReadingHint: Now you're begging them for a dose of the xylophone drug. Just a taste, you want to live!
ReadingMnemonic: "I need a <kanji>dose</kanji> of the antidote!" you yell, and the other guy looks you dead in the eyes. They slowly pull a tiny <reading>xy</reading>lophone (ざい) out of their pocket, play a few notes, and then eat it. "Ah, you'd like a dose of the xylophone cure, wouldn't you?"
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Simultaneous</radical>ly, you throw your <radical>knife</radical> at another person who's throwing a knife at you and you both get a <kanji>dose</kanji> of your own medicine. Literally, there's poison on these knives and now you've both been stabbed.

MeaningHint: Blow some air out of your mouth. It’s easy, right? Imagine what could cause you to fail at this activity.
ReadingHint: Attempt to blow air out of your mouth while covering it with your hand. It’s hard to do, right? Only a fool would try to blow air out of their mouth this way.
ReadingMnemonic: You silly <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ), the reason you couldn’t <kanji>blow</kanji> any air out of your mouth is because your hand was over it. You can’t blow air out that way. You silly fool, you.
MeaningMnemonic: You attempt to expel wind out of your <radical>mouth</radical> but <radical>lack</radical> the breath to <kanji>blow</kanji> out any. You’re using your mouth and trying to blow out some air, but you are failing to do so.

MeaningHint: Picture the flag in the dirt. Picture the ditch just beyond it. Now look for another way to exit this road, unless you have a ditch wish.
ReadingHint: It's a pun, get it? Holy. Hole-y. The ditch is a long hole. See? It's funny.
ReadingMnemonic: If you do end up veering into a <kanji>ditch</kanji> you'll find yourself yelling, "<reading>Holy</reading> (ほり) crap!" Then you'll go hurtling into the ditch.
MeaningMnemonic: Imagine you're driving and there's some <radical>dirt</radical> with a <radical>flag</radical> in it near the <radical>exit</radical> ramp. That flag marks a <kanji>ditch</kanji> running alongside the road. Don't try to exit the road here, or you'll end up stuck in that ditch.

MeaningHint: Imagine patrolling the flood waters on your scooter, which is bubbling and scooting along on the surface of the water. You have to be extremely vigilant in your patrol, you don't want any lives to be lost on your watch.
ReadingHint: You're in a group with a bunch of other scooter people, and you're all assigned to one month of flood patrol duty. But you got June. The floodiest month. Womp womp.
ReadingMnemonic: You have to <kanji>patrol</kanji> all of the floods in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). Unfortunately, that's the month when most of the floods in Japan happen, so you end up on patrol a lot.
MeaningMnemonic: You're using your <radical>scooter</radical> to navigate the <radical>flood</radical> because you're on <kanji>patrol</kanji>. You're one of the few people with a floating scooter, so it's your job to patrol the flood waters and look for people and animals that need help.

MeaningHint: He basically just made up a bunch of commandments to order all the other drunkards around and decided having twenty (twice the amount of that other religion he heard about) would be a good number.
ReadingHint:
Commandment 1: Thou shalt not touch my kayak
Commandment 2: Thou shalt not look at my kayak
Commandment 3: Thou shalt give me beer to put in my kayak

It goes on like that for a while...
ReadingMnemonic: The drunkard is yelling out his twenty <kanji>commandment</kanji>s to the others at the bar, but they're all about <reading>kay</reading>aks (かい).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>drunkard</radical> issued <radical>twenty</radical> <kanji>commandment</kanji>s to his people.

MeaningHint: Picture a criminal’s fingers, and all of the bad energy just oozing out of them, almost like an exhaust pipe emitting toxic fumes.
ReadingHint: Imagine this criminal's fingers emitting god-awful, cringe-inducing haiku after haiku. Really feel your skin crawl at the shoddiness of this criminal's fingers' haiku.
ReadingMnemonic: Then you realize what the fingers are actually <kanji>emit</kanji>ting: terrible, awful <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>fingers</radical> are where <radical>criminal</radical>s <kanji>emit</kanji> or <kanji>expel</kanji> all of their bad energy from.

MeaningHint: Turkeys aren't portable on their own. I mean you could pick one up I guess, but it will bite and poop on you. So get it in a portable carrier first.
ReadingHint: Imagine getting a portable turkey cake made of your pet turkey. It's made of turkey friendly cake mix.
ReadingMnemonic: You needed the turkey to be more <kanji>portable</kanji> because you're going to be bringing it to a <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい) shop to turn it into a cake. You aren't cooking it! Geez, what's wrong with you? No, you're making a cake OF it, for its birthday. The baker needs to see it for reference.
MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>fingers</radical> to shoo a <radical>turkey</radical> down the <radical>stairs</radical> and into a <kanji>portable</kanji> carrier. You've been trying to get this turkey into the carrier so you can transport it for like, the past hour. You finally got it done with the help of gravity. And, you know, smacking it a bit in the butt with your fingers.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself out in the winter wilderness. You hear twigs snapping, growling, leaves rustling. You’re incredibly alert and aware of everything going on around you.
ReadingHint: Listen to the sasquatch rumble on by as you continue to smell the smelly smells of this putrid bin you’re in.
ReadingMnemonic: Being the <kanji>alert</kanji> fellow you are, you hear a sasquatch approaching and dive into a trash <reading>bin</reading> (びん) to hide. The bin smells really bad and you are keenly alert to all the various stenches and smells currently penetrating your nostrils, but at least you are safe.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Every</radical> <radical>winter</radical> you are <kanji>alert</kanji> for danger. Everyone knows that the worst monsters come out in the winter so you need to be alert if you want to survive.

MeaningHint: You should probably also get some sharp spikes, sharp traps, and sharp spears to keep that gold safe too. Just a bunch of sharp stuff.
ReadingHint: Imagine covering the floor around your gold in sharp acorns. This is basically a Home Alone situation, and anyone who tries to steal your stuff is gonna get stuck full of sharp things and probably stabbed by your guard.
ReadingMnemonic: You're setting up all these sharp traps around your gold when you step on a <kanji>sharp</kanji> <reading>a</reading>corn (えい) that's just lying on the floor. Ouch! But wait, then you get a great idea!
MeaningMnemonic: If you have <radical>gold</radical> you also need a <radical>guard</radical> with a very <kanji>sharp</kanji> sword to protect it.

MeaningHint: Imagine facing the direction of the approaching winter while facing away from the rice paddy. Feel the weight of the cross in your hands as you spread your arms and implore the winter to hold off on its arrival. Drip the drop onto any thirsty rice.
ReadingHint: Feel the strain on your chic arms as you continue to spread them. Since you're by far the most chic person around, you have to set a good example for your people, so don't let them (or your arms) down.
ReadingMnemonic:
With your arms <kanji>spread</kanji> like that, you look extremely <reading>chic</reading> (しき). Spread arms are obviously a chic posture, and the fact that you're holding a cross makes it even more chic. 

Side note: The official kun'yomi reading is just し, but since far more words use しき, we're focusing on that here. Even though the き part is technically okurigana, it's actually used in the readings for most of the compounds that use this kanji!
MeaningMnemonic: Taking a <radical>cross</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> on it, you walk through the <radical>rice paddy</radical> in the <radical>direction</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> and <kanji>spread</kanji> your arms wide. You spread your arms to halt the approach of winter because your rice paddy is not done growing yet. You take the cross with you for some extra religious power of persuasion, and the drop in case any of that rice is thirsty.

MeaningHint: When you address your lord you better say "milord," otherwise you might get beheaded with the weapon you're all giving him.
ReadingHint: Oh no, oh no, oh no! Now the lord is running around with his dented weapon trying to put dents in all of you. You don't want a dent in your head! Run!
ReadingMnemonic: As you give the <kanji>lord</kanji> his weapon together, you see his face turn into a frown, "What's wrong, <kanji>Milord</kanji>?" you ask. "THERE'S A <reading>DEN</reading>T (でん)!" he bellows.
MeaningMnemonic: Under the same <radical>flag</radical>, all the people get <radical>together</radical> to present a new <radical>weapon</radical> to your <kanji>lord</kanji>. You all bow and say, "<kanji>Milord</kanji>, this gift is for you." Because that's the proper way to address your lord.

MeaningHint: The reason he is sacrificing himself is so we can all eat! Yum! Thanks to your sacrifice, righteous cow, we all have hamburgers. You should think about all the cows you ask to die for you every day. Maybe say thanks next time.
ReadingHint: Of course, the reading is also shared with kanji like 義. The righteousness radicals seems to have that effect on kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>sacrifice</kanji> must be done via <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). I know, I know, that sounds really brutal, but it's the best way. It's fast, it's effective, and hopefully the sacrifice—I mean, cow—doesn't feel a thing.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>cow</radical> of <radical>righteousness</radical> is always there for us. He is the cow that must <kanji>sacrifice</kanji> himself for us to survive.

MeaningHint: Look at this turkey straining itself on the stool trying to seize the flowers, grunting with effort. You think he’ll ever get them?
ReadingHint: Imagine this scene again only with a big cock holding the flowers as the turkey attempts to seize them. Hear the cock shouting cock-a-doodle-doo as it defends its flowers.
ReadingMnemonic: The animal the turkey is trying to <kanji>seize</kanji> the flowers from is a <reading>cock</reading> (かく). The cock is a lot bigger than the turkey, so that’s why the turkey needs to stand on the stool in its attempt to seize those flowers.
MeaningMnemonic: You see an <radical>animal</radical> with some <radical>flowers</radical> and a <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> trying to <kanji>seize</kanji> them. The animal with the flowers is really tall so the turkey needs to stand on the stool in its attempt to seize the flowers.

MeaningHint: Imagine being in this luxuriant garden — smell the overwhelming floral aroma as you clear flowers off the slide and try to shoo away the drunkard so your kids can come out and play.
ReadingHint: You can't believe this has happened again. The last drunk to wander in was moaning for a week before the flowers finished draining his life-force. I guess this is just the price you pay for having a luxuriant garden.
ReadingMnemonic: As you try to shoo the drunkard out of your <kanji>luxuriant</kanji> garden, he begins to <reading>mo</reading>an (も) with distress. Your garden is so luxuriant that the flowers have wrapped their roots around him and started draining the nutrients from his body, causing him to moan in anguish.
MeaningMnemonic: A thick carpet of <radical>flowers</radical> grows on a <radical>slide</radical> and on the <radical>drunkard</radical> passed out next to it in your <kanji>luxuriant</kanji> garden. Your garden is so luxuriant that the flowers have engulfed literally everything in it, from the kids' play structure to the hapless drunk who wandered in last night.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to wade through this overgrown field of thread. It’s really difficult, and also it’s really cold because it’s winter. You shiver. Imagine chopping the thread down with your machete. Chop chop chop!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself as Han Solo, bravely blasting your way through the overgrown thread with your blaster. Pew pew pew!
ReadingMnemonic: The reason this field of thread got so <kanji>overgrown</kanji> is because you’re <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo and you’re not used to simple things like farming thread. Han Solo is used to blasting baddies and kissing princesses. Overgrown thread is not his specialty. Use your blaster to clear a way through this thread instead of using that machete.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Every</radical> <radical>winter</radical> the <radical>thread</radical> fields become <kanji>overgrown</kanji>. Yes, thread grows in fields these days. And the thread grows the most in the winter because reasons. The thread gets so overgrown that you can’t even walk through it without cutting the thread down with a machete.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking around near this geoduck. This is the best area to frequent when you want to look at the geoduck. Just keep looking at it as you walk.
ReadingHint: Look at all the Hindu people as you walk around and observe the geoduck. Hear them all speaking Hindi as you continue to walk around.
ReadingMnemonic: Apparently Hindu people also love geoducks because <reading>Hin</reading>du (ひん) people <kanji>frequent</kanji> this area quite often as well. You always see the geoduck here, but also a lot of Hindu people.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>walk</radical> by a <radical>geoduck</radical> a <kanji>frequent</kanji> amount. This geoduck really intrigues you, so you can’t help but frequent the area the geoduck is in and walk around.

MeaningHint: Why would you ask the yakuza to "straighten out" a problem for you? Do you realize what that means in yakuza-speak? Your problems are going to be multiplying exponentially, friend.
ReadingHint: Feel shock envelop your entire body as yakuza run around town roughing up everyone who's ever wronged you. Police attention on you is beginning to multiply, too, and you can't even think from the shock of it all.
ReadingMnemonic: Your problems <kanji>multiply</kanji> and give you quite a <reading>shock</reading> (しょく) as yakuza thugs commit crime after shocking crime on your behalf. You really shouldn't be shocked though — of course crimes are going to multiply when you ask the yakuza to "straighten things out."
MeaningMnemonic: When you go to the <radical>yakuza</radical> to make things <radical>straight</radical>, your problems just <kanji>multiply</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Look at the flowers as the tsunami begins to dilute them with just one drop. Their color and fragrance and dimensions become weaker and more spread out. It’s really trippy to witness.
ReadingHint: As you continue to watch the tsunami's drop of water dilute the flowers, imagine what techniques this tsunami is using to hack nature. Is the tsunami a sinister being from the future? Maybe.
ReadingMnemonic: You watch the flowers become <kanji>dilute</kanji>d with just one drop, and realize that this is a nature <reading>hack</reading> (はく). You’ve never seen anything like this before, so you think the tsunami found out how to hack nature and that’s how it got so good at diluting things. By hacking nature.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> are being worked upon by a <radical>tsunami</radical> with a <radical>drop</radical> <radical>specialty</radical>: this tsunami can <kanji>dilute</kanji> flowers with just one tsunami-powered drop of itself. That’s its specialty.

MeaningHint: Imagine being too heavy to go anywhere without colliding with things.
ReadingHint: The Shougun is glaring at you from the ground. "You've been colliding with my entire city and now you collide into ME?!" Smoke blows out his nostrils.
ReadingMnemonic: You're going around the town, you <kanji>collide</kanji> with a bunch of things, when all of a sudden you collide with the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう)! Oh no! The shogun looks really mad.
MeaningMnemonic: If you try to <radical>go</radical> somewhere but you're too <radical>heavy</radical> you'll <kanji>collide</kanji> with everything. Cars, people, houses. You're too heavy to dodge them, so you collide with it all.

MeaningHint: Listen to what the fins have to say as they flop around on the ground in the grass. Hear them swish about as they flop, brushing up against the blades of grass.
ReadingHint: Imagine taking your yo-yo out to do this. Feel the string tighten around your finger as you unleash the yo-yo and silence all the fins.
ReadingMnemonic: Being one of little <kanji>honor</kanji>, you decide to squash these vocal fins with your <reading>yo</reading>-yo (よ). You take out your yo-yo and start bashing each fin with it until they stop talking about what little honor you have.
MeaningMnemonic: There’s <radical>grass</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> and the <radical>fins</radical> there <radical>say</radical> you have no <kanji>honor</kanji>. The fins are left over from the fish you dismembered and they do not believe it was an act of honor. Now they are just sitting on the ground on the grass, calling you out for having no honor.

MeaningHint: You also praise the lid. And praise the plants and animals that gave you the food.
ReadingHint: If you're going to praise the ingenuity of the invention of the "lid" for its preservation methods, you should definitely do it from home. Or at least from the internet from home. Find your people where you can, you know?
ReadingMnemonic: You do this <kanji>prais</kanji>ing in your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), though, not a church. I don't think anyone outside your home would understand why you're praising inanimate objects.
MeaningMnemonic: Lift the <radical>lid</radical> off your <radical>preserve</radical>s, <radical>kick</radical> it, and <kanji>praise</kanji> the lord! Your food is preserved, you don't need the lid anymore, praise be!

MeaningHint: Picture this transparent scooter. Well, it's actually a bit hard to see, because it's see-through. I guess imagine the outline and the feel, as you perch at the top of your stairs.
ReadingHint: Almost immediately you lose track of the scooter because it's transparent. All you see is とうきょう in front of you. And no scooter. Maybe this is why people don't make transparent vehicles.
ReadingMnemonic: You slip, the <kanji>transparent</kanji> scooter scoots down the stairs without you and into the streets of <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Nooo! Transpa-scootie come back!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>scooter</radical> you made out of <radical>grain</radical>s, and are about to ride down the <radical>stairs</radical> with, is completely <kanji>transparent</kanji>. You mashed the grains down so fine that you can see through them! And then you made them into a scooter.

MeaningHint: Imagine your neighbor doing a rice dance, whatever that may be, in the building next to yours. That's your neighbor, for ya.
ReadingHint: Imagine the ring your neighbor has as overly extravagant. You have to make it wild enough for you to remember. Maybe it is integral to their rice dance. Perhaps you covet your neighbor's rice dance ring.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>neighbor</kanji> has an incredible <reading>rin</reading>g (りん) that they use for their rice dance.
MeaningMnemonic: Inside that <radical>building</radical> someone is doing a <radical>rice</radical> <radical>dance</radical>. That someone is your <kanji>neighbor</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Look at this turkey with a fang. It’s elegant, but also frightening. See the fanged turkey staring at you menacingly.
ReadingHint: Picture this elegant gargoyle sitting in front of you. Reach out your hand and feels its hard, elegant stone gargoyle body.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>elegant</kanji> fang makes the turkey look more like a <reading>ga</reading>rgoyle (が) than a turkey. In fact, you haven't seen it move at all. Maybe it is just an elegant gargoyle!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>fang</radical> on a <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>elegant</kanji>. Most turkeys do not have fangs, so this turkey with a fang is quite elegant and dignified. This turkey is special.

MeaningHint: Picture a woman rejoicing loudly. She's got a big smile on her face and she wants you and everyone else to see how glad she is.
ReadingHint: You'd be happy too if you'd just found your lost keys. Though maybe you wouldn't shout it for the entire world to hear.
ReadingMnemonic: "I'm so <kanji>glad</kanji>!" the woman shouts, "Because I found the <reading>key</reading> (き)!" You immediately start to wonder what "the key" is. The key to life? The key to learning Japanese? Then you see she's holding up some car keys.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>woman</radical> is <radical>rejoic</radical>ing. She must be <kanji>glad</kanji> and <kanji>happy</kanji> to rejoice out loud in front of you like this.

MeaningHint: Just make sure you don't inquire about anything inappropriate, or you'll end up facing an inquiry from HR…
ReadingHint: Imagine saying "Ooh, Kaga!" to this overly curious leader-director-chef. Say it out loud, like he's just inquired about something weird. He just really loves cooking, okay? But he inquires too much about it in the workplace, so you have to let him know how you feel.
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out the leader and director of your company is Chairman Kaga from the TV show Iron Chef. Every time he tries to <kanji>inquire</kanji> about weird stuff, you have to say <reading>Ooh, Kaga</reading> (うかが) disapprovingly. "What are you doing for dinner tonight?" he inquires. Ooh, Kaga! "What's the best way to soften an eggplant?" he inquires. Ooh, Kaga!
MeaningMnemonic: Being a <radical>leader</radical> or <radical>director</radical> in a company means you have to <kanji>inquire</kanji> about lots of things. Have you started this yet? Have you finished that yet? Did you get that thing I sent you? Where's my coffee? Basically if you're a leader or director, your main job is to inquire about these very important things.

MeaningHint: Picture a single beam of moonlight penetrating through the winter sky, right onto the trash.
ReadingHint: The clear, penetrating beam tastes exactly like iron, which is something you really weren't expecting. But now it's filling your entire mouth and you don't want it to stop.
ReadingMnemonic: You want the light to <kanji>penetrate</kanji> you, so you put your face in the way of the beam and it shoots <kanji>clear</kanji> through you. Your mouth fills with the taste of <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ).
MeaningMnemonic: You decide to <radical>loiter</radical> next to the <radical>trash</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> so the moon's light will <kanji>penetrate</kanji> you. The sky is <kanji>clear</kanji>est in winter, and this spot next to the trash happens to get the most of it.

MeaningHint: Picture the bundle of geoducks getting smashed by the tsunami, breaking apart, and getting tossed around in the rapids.
ReadingHint: Imagine the cool shallow water lapping at your feet as you bite into some crunchy celery and watch the rapids in the distance.
ReadingMnemonic: While those geoducks are getting tossed about in the <kanji>rapids</kanji>, you're sitting in the <kanji>shallows</kanji> chomping down on some <reading>ce</reading>lery (せ).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>bundle</radical> of <radical>geoduck</radical>s, swirling around their weird shapes, and creating <kanji>rapids</kanji>! The rapids are so violent, some of the geoducks break out of the bundle and get washed into the <kanji>shallows</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine you need proof of your withdrawal so badly, that you're in the trash like this, raking around with your fingers in the moonlight. Close your eyes and feel the cold, smell the stink.
ReadingHint: The pain from the 鉄 reverberates up your fingers and into your hands and arms. What's this great lump of 鉄 doing in the trash?
ReadingMnemonic: While looking for the <kanji>withdrawal</kanji> letter, your fingers hit a piece of <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ). Ouch!
MeaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> to dig through the <radical>trash</radical> in the light of the <radical>moon</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> because somewhere in there is your <kanji>withdrawal</kanji> letter. What did you withdraw? Your resignation? Your troops? Whatever it was, you need to find the proof, and fast.

MeaningHint: What would have to happen for you to set aside your own fingers? Try to imagine it right now.
ReadingHint: Think of all the things you do with your fingers. Now picture how they would look if you had never ever cleaned them with soap. Uhg, disgusting. You should just set them aside here, and find some new ones.
ReadingMnemonic: I know why they <kanji>set aside</kanji> their fingers long ago, it's because there was no <reading>so</reading>ap (そ). If you didn't have soap to clean your fingers you wouldn't want to keep them for very long...
MeaningMnemonic: You find some <radical>fingers</radical> from <radical>long ago</radical>. They must have been <kanji>set aside</kanji> by their previous owners. They definitely weren't cut off or anything, someone just decided they didn't want them anymore and set them aside.

MeaningHint: Imagine tracing your fingers over the table, the cold winter frost stinging them, as you reveal the secrets of this table and what it was based on.
ReadingHint: The buildings and temples are all very childlike. Like crayon drawn on a table.
ReadingMnemonic: This particular table was <kanji>based on</kanji> Little <reading>Kyo</reading>to (きょ). Little buildings and temples, that sort of look like Little Kyoto. Problem is, it was made by a <reading>子</reading> (こ), so it's not very good.
MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>fingers</radical> to wipe this <radical>winter</radical> <radical>table</radical> and see what it was <kanji>based on</kanji>. The cold winter weather covers the table in frost, and when you wipe it away, you can see traces of what the table was originally based on. All tables have to be based on something, even if it's just other tables that came before them.

MeaningHint: Have him run your ceremony for you. Think about why he is so righteous, then think about an extremely righteous ceremony.
ReadingHint: *CHOP*
ReadingMnemonic: Sadly, the reason only the righteous can run this <kanji>ceremony</kanji> is because it's a punishment ceremony, where he pulls the cord to cut someone's head off via a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>righteousness</radical> is the only one who can run this <kanji>ceremony</kanji>. Why? Because it's a very pure ceremony, and only those with an extremely righteous mind can run it.

MeaningHint: There are a lot of hoodlums out there that try to dress as trees and confuse people. So make sure you measure their drum before you cut one down or tell someone your secrets under it.
ReadingHint: However! A non-wood tree will be really thirsty from pretending to be a wood tree. It will start making sounds and moving and probably say, "Can I have a juice box, please?"
ReadingMnemonic: If you're still not sure if the tree is really made of <kanji>wood</kanji>, just take out a small <reading>ju</reading>ice (じゅ) box and start drinking it. A wooden tree will show no interest in the juice box at all.
MeaningMnemonic: Each <radical>tree</radical> needs its <radical>drum</radical> <radical>measurement</radical> done before you can say it's really made of <kanji>wood</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Most people abandon fruit when it's misshapen. It's probably fine though...
ReadingHint: You abandon the fruit and stare intently at this key. It must be why the fruit was weird looking and abandoned in the trash... but what does it go to?! You dig through the trash and try the key on everything that's been abandoned in there.
ReadingMnemonic: You reach into the trash, take out the <kanji>abandon</kanji>ed fruit, and bite into it. OUCH! There's a <reading>key</reading> (き) inside, and you just bit into it.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>trash</radical> is a weird looking <radical>fruit</radical>. Someone decided to <kanji>abandon</kanji> it here.

MeaningHint: Look at the tiger running with his tiger legs. So majestic. So fierce.
ReadingHint: Imagine watching the tiger as it notices the Torah and then runs away. The tiger sounds as if it is screaming in terror as it flees from the proximity of the Torah.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>tiger</kanji> is using his legs to run away from the <reading>Torah</reading> (とら). This tiger is deathly afraid of religion, so a holy book like the Torah causes the tiger to run away.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tiger</radical> has <radical>legs</radical> that belong to a <kanji>tiger</kanji>. You know, a tiger has the legs of a tiger. This is common knowledge. Nothing new here.

MeaningHint: Imagine this little firefly blinking his firefly light as he flies around shouting a Viking warcry and brandishing an axe. Yaaaargh!
ReadingHint: Hear Hotaru’s shrill scream as she runs away from the Viking firefly. If you don’t know what Hotaru Tomoe looks like, Google a picture of her to get a better visualization.
ReadingMnemonic: The Viking <kanji>firefly</kanji> is attacking <reading>Hotaru</reading> (ほたる) Tomoe (alter ego of Sailor Saturn) because the Viking firefly hates the show Sailor Moon and everyone on it. Hotaru screams in terror as she is deathly afraid of fireflies, especially Viking ones.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>Viking</radical> <radical>insect</radical> is a <kanji>firefly</kanji>. In the insect kingdom, the insect most resembling a Viking is undoubtedly the firefly.

MeaningHint: Picture a huge warship overseeing everything around it. It's big and kinda scary.
ReadingHint: Picture Genghis Khan aboard a monstrous warship. He's carefully watching the sea and land that he's conquered.
ReadingMnemonic: This huge <kanji>warship</kanji> is run by Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん), the famous warlord. Really, what did you expect? Who else is strong enough to captain a warship but him?
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>boat</radical> that <radical>oversee</radical>s other boats, land, and people is usually a <kanji>warship</kanji>. They're the big boats that oversee everything around them. And if anyone steps out of line, a warship can take care of it.

MeaningHint: Imagine witnessing this tsunami taking the two husbands up into the sun. Hear the water rushing. That tsunami is massive. What are they going to conceal in the sun?
ReadingHint: Just make sure to remember the husbands are riding that tsunami alongside their centaur, heading towards the sun. They need to conceal the centaur to keep it safe.
ReadingMnemonic: The husbands are going to <kanji>conceal</kanji> their pet <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) in the sun. The local government isn't too pleased that they have a pet centaur, so they have to conceal it in the sun so it doesn't get confiscated.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> carries two <radical>husband</radical>s up into the <radical>sun</radical>, where they're going to <kanji>conceal</kanji> something.

MeaningHint: Picture the gladiator squeezing his hand into a tight fist, raising it up and yelling.
ReadingHint: Ken and the gladiator clash! Fists fly! You've never seen hand-to-hand combat like this! You can hardly keep up!!
ReadingMnemonic: The gladiator's <kanji>fist</kanji> is now aimed at <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. Little does the gladiator know, Ken knows the ways of the samurai and the gladiator. He slowly raises his own hands and turns them into fists.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>gladiator</radical> turns his <radical>hand</radical> into a <kanji>fist</kanji>. A fist for punching other gladiators.

MeaningHint: You’re angry that you could only find charcoal so you take the charcoal in your hands and you crush it atop the cliff on the mountain. Feel the charcoal breaking in your hands as the wind carries the charcoal dust off from the top of the cliff on the mountain.
ReadingHint: Imagine lying out in the sun next to the crushed charcoal, thinking you’re going to get tan. You’re very surprised when you actually catch on fire. Hot hot hot!
ReadingMnemonic: After crushing the <kanji>charcoal</kanji>, you decide to take advantage of your current location and lie out in the sun for a while to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). Instead of getting a tan though, you catch on fire. Oops.
MeaningMnemonic: You climb up a <radical>mountain</radical> and a <radical>cliff</radical> in search of <radical>fire</radical>, but all you find is <kanji>charcoal</kanji>. You can make fire with the charcoal, but you were hoping to find fire itself. All that climbing for nothing.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Home is where the wrapped heart is.
ReadingMnemonic: Think about being <kanji>wrap</kanji>ped up, nice and warm and safe in your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Home is where you wrap yourself in a blanket, in the warmth of the fireplace, in the arms of those you love.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>wrap</radical> radical and the <kanji>wrap</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Reflect upon your own humanity. What does it mean to be human? Is it wearing shoes and hats? Probably.
ReadingHint: Are you wearing jeans right now? If so, rub them with your hands and say aloud “Oh, the humanity!” Or just imagine wearing jeans if you aren’t actually wearing them.
ReadingMnemonic: Another thing that reminds you and the leader about your <kanji>humanity</kanji> is wearing <reading>jean</reading>s (じん). Jeans are the go-to legwear for many people, and no one but humans wear them. Jeans are another great symbol of humanity.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>leader</radical> has <radical>two</radical> things that remind him of his <kanji>humanity</kanji>: his shoes, and his hat. These two things remind the leader of his humanity because humans are the only ones who wear shoes and hats.

MeaningHint: Think of the wide range of minerals there are that aren't gold. There are a lot.
ReadingHint: Emperor こういち is getting all the minerals, and the 鋼, so he can take over all neighboring nations and force them into a subjugation of efficient kanji learning.
ReadingMnemonic: One person is trying to control all the <kanji>minerals</kanji> of the world. That person is Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
