MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>eye</radical> catches an <radical>omen</radical>, it's only natural that you will <kanji>stare</kanji> at it.

MeaningHint: You're like one of those nudes in a Renaissance painting, strategically covering yourself with a bunch of grapes or a well-placed apple or something.
ReadingHint: Whatever you do, don't Google "Ra Egyptian god naked" to see how naked he actually got. There's no need to taint your browser history for the sake of kanji learning.
ReadingMnemonic: You know who else is often <kanji>naked</kanji>? The ancient Egyptian god <reading>Ra</reading> (ら). Well, half-naked at least. Egypt is a hot country, so you can't really blame Ra for wanting to strip down.
MeaningMnemonic: If your <radical>outfit</radical> is just a piece of <radical>fruit</radical>, then you are <kanji>naked</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Your cram school thinks that you’ll learn better sitting in a circle in the dirt. So there you are in the dirt, receiving your lessons.
ReadingHint: Imagine a jukebox playing loudly right beside you while you’re cramming away. Hard to believe, but the jukebox's melodies do actually amplify the cramming fervor, making you just cram, cram, cram!
ReadingMnemonic: Not only does your <kanji>cram school</kanji> teach kids in a circle in the dirt, it also plays music on a <reading>juke</reading>box (じゅく). Yes, a jukebox. They believe a jukebox's tunes help with cramming.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>receive</radical> instruction in a <radical>circle</radical> in the <radical>dirt</radical>. Where are you? <kanji>Cram school</kanji>!

MeaningHint: See all the toes lined up on the <kanji>table</kanji>. They are the ones who showed up early.
ReadingHint: Imagine the toes talking to each other. "Hello Mr. Toe!" "Oh hello other Mr. Toe!"
ReadingMnemonic: What do all the early toes on the <kanji>table</kanji> do while they're waiting until it's the right time? They just sit around on their table, <reading>talk</reading>ing (たく).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>toe</radical> is <radical>early</radical>. I hate it when the toes show up early. Well, if the toes are going to show up this early, I guess it's best to put them on the <kanji>table</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It's a single, perfectly round stone under this canopy of trees. It didn't start this way, but thanks to all the polishing you did, it has become nice and smooth.
ReadingHint: There's only this one stone under these trees, and polishing the stone is the only job here. Still, you don't get much from it, thus the meager life of polishing your stone.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>polish</kanji> the stone due to your <reading>meager</reading> (みが) lifestyle.
MeaningMnemonic: Below this <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>stone</radical>. Since it's the only stone here, you make sure to <kanji>polish</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: Imagine how much bacteria would flower in your mouth if you just left grains in there for like, I dunno, a month. That's a lot of nasty bacteria!
ReadingHint: Picture the King's mouth up close. Look at all the bacteria squiggling around and through his teeth and gums. Bleh! Barf!
ReadingMnemonic: There is a lot of this <kanji>bacteria</kanji> in the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England's mouth. He eats a lot of grains, and he won't let anyone touch his mouth to clean it, but he refuses to do things himself... so it's absolutely chock full of bacteria.
MeaningMnemonic: When you leave <radical>grain</radical>s in your <radical>mouth</radical> for long enough, they'll turn into <radical>flowers</radical> of <kanji>bacteria</kanji>. These aren't the nice, pretty flowers you'd give your mom on Mother's Day. These are little bacteria flowers that are taking over your mouth. Maybe brush your teeth next time.

MeaningHint: This shade is really convenient right now, because it's been such a hot day. Ahh, shade is so nice and cool.
ReadingHint: Picture an inn with a cloud right above it right now. Look at the cloud-shaped shade keeping everyone visiting the inn cool and sunburn-free.
ReadingMnemonic: The building that's getting this nice <kanji>shade</kanji> is the <reading>inn</reading> (いん) you're staying at.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> <radical>now</radical> has a <radical>cloud</radical> over it, creating <kanji>shade</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The ghosts are pinned to the ground by the spikes, unable to move.
ReadingHint: A ray gun, coincidentally, is the only thing that can kill a ghost, and there it lays, just out of reach. You'll have to be the one to pull the trigger of the ray gun. Do you pick up the ray gun out of the row boat to destroy the ghosts?
ReadingMnemonic: You come across the <kanji>ghost</kanji>s, who are stabbed into the ground. "Please," they beg. "Please use that <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun that's in that <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and end our suffering."
MeaningMnemonic: Coming down with the <radical>rain</radical> is <radical>two</radical> <radical>spikes</radical>. They go right through two <kanji>ghost</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: The sun did a little bit of work, so they aren't wet, but they aren't dry either. They're damp.
ReadingHint: Luckily the shih tzu is kinda pinned between the spikes, so it isn't dead or anything, but it's definitely stuck. You try to help it out, but you slip because it's so damp and you fall in too! Imagine the sound of your cries mixing in with the shih tzu's as you both struggling to get out but can't because of how damp everything is.
ReadingMnemonic: As you're looking at the <kanji>damp</kanji> spikes, a <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) prances over, slips on the damp ground, and falls right on the spike! Oh nooo!
MeaningMnemonic: After the <radical>tsunami</radical> the <radical>sun</radical> comes out again and shines on these <radical>spikes</radical>. They were super wet before, but you figure the sun probably dried them off. You lean down to touch them and nope, they's still <kanji>damp</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Think of a stone being renewed like it is having something like a spa day. Pedicure, manicure, the whole shebang. It’ll come out all stiff and hardened, cause that’s how good stones are.
ReadingHint: If only you could ask こういち to give you massages again, but he's too busy for you, now (being emperor and all).
ReadingMnemonic: You remember how you used to cure these <kanji>stiff</kanji> body times: your old friend <reading>こう</reading>いち would just massage them out for you.
MeaningMnemonic: You feel like a <radical>stone</radical> that’s been <radical>renew</radical>ed. How do renewed stones feel? <kanji>Stiff</kanji>. Very stiff.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at the grains in the houses of people who make vastly different earnings. The poor artist has only a few sacks of grain. The rich samurai has an extra house just for holding his grain. That's how you know he has much higher earnings than the artist.
ReadingHint: Picture your own earnings. Can you afford a lot of grains and a car?
ReadingMnemonic: Another way to check someone's <kanji>earnings</kanji> is to see if they have a <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Would someone who doesn't make a lot of money have a car? No!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>grain</radical>s you have stored in your <radical>house</radical> reflect on your <kanji>earnings</kanji>. If you earn a lot of money, you have more grains.

MeaningHint: She's in your house because you married her. Thus, she's your bride. Your house is now a home.
ReadingHint: Your bride is very upset at how sexist you're being right now, and makes you do all the cleaning instead. The maid still gets fired.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that you have a <kanji>bride</kanji> in your house, you can get rid of <reading>yo</reading>ur <reading>mai</reading>d (よめ), since your bride will do all the cleaning.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>woman</radical> who makes your <radical>house</radical> a home is your <kanji>bride</kanji>.

MeaningHint: To be fair, it's pretty obvious who would win. Nobody knows what that lifeguard was thinking.
ReadingHint:
"This lifeguard didn't believe in the power of kanji learning," こういち says to the press. "He was punished by the great Crabigator, who sent its tsunami as a warning for all."

Hint: when you see the "lifeguard" radical, the reading is probably going to be こう.
ReadingMnemonic: In the <kanji>gutter</kanji> is a lifeguard, so Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち decides to survey the damage.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical> vs <radical>lifeguard</radical>. Who will win? After the water drains away, we find the lifeguard in the <kanji>gutter</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Can you see why a tsunami dragon (especially the Asian skinny snake-like kind) could be a waterfall? Try to imagine it.
ReadingHint: Now imagine it being a bit tacky. Dragons are alright but... you just don't want to overdo it, you know?
ReadingMnemonic: Despite how cool a dragon <kanji>waterfall</kanji> is, to be honest it's also pretty <reading>tacky</reading> (たき).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> in the shape of a <radical>dragon</radical> flies off the cliff, then comes crashing down to the ground. This skinny tsunami of water flying down to the ground is a <kanji>waterfall</kanji>.

MeaningHint: While we're speaking of animals, he's absolutely bat sh*t crazy.
ReadingHint: I think he maybe got too friendly with some raccoons and beavers and went crazy. Watch out for him if you live in きょうと, rabies is no joke.
ReadingMnemonic: You might see that <kanji>crazy</kanji> <kanji>lunatic</kanji> hanging around the outskirts of <reading>きょう</reading>と. He'll lurk near your trash cans and scream, "I AM THE ANIMAL KING!" and run away with some orange peels.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>animal</radical> <radical>king</radical>? He's just this guy who lives in the forest and thinks he's king of all the animals. He's a <kanji>lunatic</kanji>, though.

MeaningHint: Imagine the sheep with feathers taking off and flying around. It's an odd sight, but makes sense, kind of.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself enjoying the cozy car-kettle brew, while admiring the unusual sight of the sheep with feathers and wings flying through the sky. This is just shear bliss!
ReadingMnemonic: Watching that sheep with feathers and wings flying around made you thirsty. You remember that you have a <reading>ca</reading>r <reading>ke</reading>ttle (かけ), so you reach into your car and grab your car kettle to make a special car-kettle brew.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>sheep</radical> with <radical>feathers</radical> (and presumably wings, too) is able to <kanji>fly</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Make sure the dirt you're using to make black ink is really, really black. Like the kind you find around volcanoes or something.
ReadingHint: Imagine yelling "SUE ME!" at someone's face, which is covered in the black ink you spilled there.
ReadingMnemonic: Be careful that you don't spill <kanji>black ink</kanji> on anyone or anything. If you do, you have to own it. Imagine spilling black ink on someone, and when they confront you, you yell, "Yeah I got black ink on you, so <reading>sue me</reading> (すみ)!"
MeaningMnemonic: You use <radical>black</radical> <radical>dirt</radical> to make <kanji>black ink</kanji>. That's what you use to write in black!

MeaningHint: Look at these nine birds strutting down the street like they own the place. The dove and pigeon are out in front because they are the most important. Hear them chirping and fluttering their feathers about.
ReadingHint: Devour it. You love pigeon hearts. Discard the rest, keep the heart.
ReadingMnemonic: Take a look at the <kanji>pigeon</kanji> in front of you. Now, grab it, tear it in half, and grab the <reading>heart</reading> (はと) from its little body.
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>nine</radical> <radical>bird</radical>s and one of them is a <kanji>dove</kanji> and another is a <kanji>pigeon</kanji>. These nine birds are a flock of all kinds, but the dove and the pigeon are the leaders.

MeaningHint: He's calm now because he stomped right into a farmer's field (thus the rice) and if he didn't calm down he'd probably ruin it.
ReadingHint: Picture Wolverine sitting calmly in this field, onions causing tears to stream down his cheeks as he reflects on what he's done.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that Wolverine is <kanji>calm</kanji>, tears come to his eyes. Being calm has given him perspective on what his anger has done. Oh, wait no. There are just some <reading>on</reading>ions (おん) in his cleats too, and they're making calm tears trickle down his eyes.
MeaningMnemonic: Some <radical>grains</radical> get stuck in his <radical>cleat</radical>, and <radical>Wolverine</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical> grows <kanji>calm</kanji>. Wolverine had been screaming and yelling and stomping around, but when the grains entered his cleat he realized where he was. He was finally able to calm down.

MeaningHint: Picture a huge barracks made of dull, almost brown, gold. Maybe you should have picked a better material.
ReadingHint: You stand cowering in front of the don as he inspects how dull the gold barracks are. You don't wanna be off'd for this just because the don was dull enough to make you use gold!
ReadingMnemonic: As you stare sadly at the <kanji>dull</kanji> barracks, the <reading>don</reading> (どん) shows up out of nowhere, and he's pissed. He turns his angry gaze toward you and says, "How did my barracks get so dull? Are you at fault? This is bad, this is very bad. Someone will pay for this."
MeaningMnemonic: Make <radical>barracks</radical> out of <radical>gold</radical> and they will get <kanji>dull</kanji> quickly. Gold is soft, it's okay for jewelry and delicate things that aren't used much, but barracks for soldiers? Those are used constantly, and they get worn out easily. What was once shiny gold will go dull in days.

MeaningHint: You thought the devil hangs out in hell, but he actually hangs out under this canopy of trees.
ReadingHint: Into the trees is where all mamas go missing. And so the devil searches for his mama under this canopy of trees.
ReadingMnemonic: Why is the <kanji>devil</kanji> under a canopy of trees? Because he's looking for his <reading>ma</reading>ma (ま).
MeaningMnemonic: Below the <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s is a <radical>demon</radical>. This demon is a <kanji>devil</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at a dormitory chore wheel and seeing it's your turn to carry in the charcoal this week. Bleh!
ReadingHint: The dormitory row boat leaks a little too, so by the time you get back you really need to light the charcoal and heat up!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>dormitory</kanji> is on an island, so when you go out to get the charcoal you have to take a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat. You row and row and row until you reach the shed with all the charcoal. Then you row and row and row it back to the dormitory.
MeaningMnemonic: You need to bring <radical>charcoal</radical> under this <radical>roof</radical> to heat the <kanji>dormitory</kanji>. You live in this dormitory with a bunch of other kids, and you all have to take turns bringing in the charcoal from outside to heat it.

MeaningHint: Imagine preparing this tray, just before your ancestors arrive in friendly ghost form to share part of your meal with you. They only come once a year, so make sure it's a nice tray, okay?
ReadingHint: Tap the bone china plate with your knuckle bone. Hear the pleasant clink it makes as you set it on the tray.
ReadingMnemonic: The plate you put on the <kanji>tray</kanji> for the <kanji>ancestral festival</kanji> is made of <reading>bone</reading> (ぼん). Bone china, that is. How fancy… And appropriate, considering bone china is made from actual bone.
MeaningMnemonic: You put <radical>part</radical> of your food on a <radical>plate</radical>, then put that on a <kanji>tray</kanji> to keep it safe. This tray is an offering for the <kanji>ancestral festival</kanji>, where your ancestors' spirits visit you to check in and share some of that food you're offering on a tray.

MeaningHint: Look at this big tree building over in the east. Imagine actually using it as a building and moving in. And why not? It's free real estate.
ReadingHint: Imagine looking down on とうきょう from the top floor of this magnificent building you've just moved into. The sun is rising behind you, slowly illuminating the city below.
ReadingMnemonic: That <kanji>building</kanji> happens to be in the center of <reading>とう</reading>きょう. If you need to find east (which is where とうきょう is), you just look for the building.
MeaningMnemonic: There’s a <radical>tree</radical> in the <radical>east</radical> that looks like a <kanji>building</kanji>. The tree is tall and straight and doesn't have any leaves or branches. It's basically a big wooden building.

MeaningHint: Would you rather have your heart up on the roof, close to falling but out of the way of people. Or would you rather have your heart in a net, which people can't steal from, but is in the way of cars.
ReadingHint: "I'd rather you didn't have that!" you shout as your neighbors run through the neighborhood to their own house. You'd rather this wasn't happening! You'd rather your neighbors let you make your own choice!!
ReadingMnemonic: As you try to decide which you'd <kanji>rather</kanji> have, your <reading>neigh</reading>bors (ねい) come out and grab the heart out of your hands.
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>roof</radical> is your <radical>heart</radical>. OR! In a <radical>net</radical> in the <radical>street</radical>. Which would you <kanji>rather</kanji>?

MeaningHint: Imagine how sad you'll feel this year when you turn that many years old and have to give up your small canine. You can't believe this much time has already passed — where have all the years gone?
ReadingHint: No matter how many years old you are, everyone loves kanji with easy readings.
ReadingMnemonic: Remember the vocab word 〜才 which also meant "<kanji>years old</kanji>"? Well, this has the same reading: さい.
MeaningMnemonic: You have to <radical>stop</radical> keeping <radical>canine</radical>s which are <radical>small</radical> once you reach a certain number of <kanji>years old</kanji>. You'll be hitting that age limit this <kanji>year</kanji>, which means your <kanji>time</kanji> with your small canine will end and you'll have to grow up and get a bigger one.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at a monkey at the zoo. Aww, it's so cute. But then it turns around and kicks some dirt right into your aww-ing mouth. Yuck! Why monkey, why?!
ReadingHint: Picture the monkey saluting you as you spit dirt out of your mouth. Taste the bitter dirt and the bitter shame. You were played by a monkey.
ReadingMnemonic: You spit the dirt out of your mouth and look back at the <kanji>monkey</kanji> to see that it's <reading>salu</reading>ting (さる) you. Is... is it mocking you?
MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>animal</radical> that <radical>kick</radical>s <radical>dirt</radical> into people's <radical>mouth</radical>s is a <kanji>monkey</kanji>. Monkeys are naughty little things. You should be happy it was just dirt it kicked into your mouth, and not something worse.

MeaningHint: Oh, did you think your pupil was a hole? No, no, you misunderstand. That's where the village stands. It's full of little people who whisper to your brain what you should see.
ReadingHint: Even if you're a pupil non-believer, at least you can see how it's shaped like a doughnut hole, right?
ReadingMnemonic: All of the people in your <kanji>pupil</kanji> eat <reading>dou</reading>ghnuts (どう) all day long. That's why your eye is shaped like a doughnut, with the "hole" (village) as their home.
MeaningMnemonic: Inside your <radical>eye</radical> <radical>stand</radical>s a tiny <radical>village</radical> that makes up your <kanji>pupil</kanji>. That's the black part of your eye!

MeaningHint: Despite it raining, your orders are to make sure none (zero!) of the rain spills anywhere. Figure out what you need to do to make sure you follow these rain orders successfully. Zero spill, alright?
ReadingHint:
You just take your ray gun and "zap!" the clouds are gone. There will be zero spills today.

Hint: when you see the radical <radical>orders</radical> it's fairly likely the reading will be れい.
ReadingMnemonic: The best way to make sure there is <kanji>zero</kanji> <kanji>spill</kanji> when it rains is to get rid of the rain. You use a special <reading>ray</reading> (れい) gun and zap the rain right out of the sky. There will be zero spills because there will be zero rain.
MeaningMnemonic: You get <radical>rain</radical> <radical>orders</radical>. Your orders are to make sure there is <kanji>zero</kanji> <kanji>spill</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Look at this crab placing stones on the go board. Hear them click and clack as the crab places them with expert precision.
ReadingHint: Go is go. Easy!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Go</kanji> is pronounced <reading>go</reading> (ご) in Japanese too. How convenient is that? Like, super convenient. So easy even a crab could remember!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>crab</radical> moves a <radical>stone</radical> because he is playing the game <kanji>go</kanji>. You know this game, right? You put black or white stones on a board until you win the game. This is the Japanese game known as go. This crab is pretty good.

MeaningHint: People in top hats like to put tariffs on lots of things. Especially things you need, like grains you eat to stay alive. You have no choice but to pay.
ReadingHint: Everything you get from other counties has been tariffed except for soap. You'll be hungry, but at least you'll be clean.
ReadingMnemonic: You're listening to all the things in this <kanji>tariff</kanji> and you finally get fed up and ask what isn't being taxed now. The top hat man looks at you and smiles, holding out his hand. In his hand is some <reading>soa</reading>p (そ).
MeaningMnemonic: Someone with a <radical>top hat</radical> comes and tells you that your <radical>grain</radical>s are going to be taxed from now on. Top hat man says there is a <kanji>tariff</kanji> that's been enacted on grains. Rice? Triple the price. Wheat? Quadruple.

MeaningHint: A secluded place has no toilets, so you just poop right on the mountain.
ReadingHint: Imagine hundreds of youths on this secluded mountain, all pooping on the ground while trying to get a cell phone signal.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>secluded</kanji> Poop Poop Mountain is a favorite of <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). They go there to get away from their phones to get in touch with nature again.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Poop</radical> <radical>Poop</radical> <radical>Mountain</radical> is covered in poop because it is such a <kanji>secluded</kanji> place.

MeaningHint: Picture the bubbles bursting out of the wrapped up tsunami as it crashes down. The air that the tsunami sucked in while it was wrapping up come out as bubbles big and small.
ReadingHint: Watch helplessly as your home, now trapped in a bubble, floats away into the sky.
ReadingMnemonic: One of the biggest <kanji>bubbles</kanji> heads straight for your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), engulfs it, and picks it up into the air!
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>wrap</radical>s around itself, the resulting wave will create a lot of <kanji>bubbles</kanji>. You know how tsunamis wrap around once gravity kicks in, right? Well when that wave crashes, a whole lot of bubbles are created.

MeaningHint: Imagine if you had a habit of eating hot peppers whenever you thought you might be getting sick. To be fair, it flushes out your sinuses and tastes good, so it's not the worst habit you could have.
ReadingHint: Unfortunately, now you've gotten into the habit of telling everyone what to do when they get sick. "Yeah, yeah, eat a hot pepper, we know. But couscous is better," they say before you can speak. To each their own, I suppose.
ReadingMnemonic: Everyone you know has a different <kanji>habit</kanji> when they get sick. Instead, they eat <reading>cous</reading>cous (くせ). But you think couscous is gross. It's bland and does nothing for your stuffy noses and scratchy throats. I wonder how you can convince them to replace their habit with your own.
MeaningMnemonic: When you get <radical>sick</radical> you always eat a <radical>hot pepper</radical>. It's a <kanji>habit</kanji> you've picked up and you can't seem to shake it. One sniffle, one cough, and before you know it, you're eating a hot pepper or two.

MeaningHint: Once the gold comb has melted down in the forge, you can forge it into whatever weapon you want.
ReadingHint: Most people who do forge work have tans. And now you know why.
ReadingMnemonic: You're trying to work the <kanji>forge</kanji>, but it's harder than you thought. You end up forging outside all day and get a wicked <reading>tan</reading> (たん).
MeaningMnemonic: You want to turn your <radical>gold</radical> <radical>comb</radical> into a <radical>weapon</radical>, so you throw it into the <kanji>forge</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine tempering this gold from the east. You're heat-treating the gold when you're tempering it, so feel the heat from the fire on your face and arms.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself tempering your gold with this shabby rental forge. It keeps flaring up and almost burning off your eyebrows every time you try to work it. It's tough doing tempering with a rental!
ReadingMnemonic: The smithy where you're doing the <kanji>tempering</kanji> is just a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん). You need a rental for your tempering because you haven't sold enough gold to own your own smithy yet. It's seen its fair share of tempering, so it's a pretty shabby rental…
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>gold</radical> from the <radical>east</radical> needs <kanji>tempering</kanji> before it can be sold. The gold from the east is good, it just needs some tempering before it's entirely presentable. You know, tempering like you'd do in a forge.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself walking through a field of rice plants. Now look very closely at the head of the plant in front of you. See the rice bunched up there? That's the good stuff.
ReadingHint: Imagine working in a field of rice and using a hoe so you don't injure the head of the plant. You're sweating and it's hot, but you keep at it.
ReadingMnemonic: When you want to harvest the <kanji>head of the plant</kanji> you'd like to eat, you need to use a <reading>hoe</reading> (ほ). Using a hoe, you can dig up the bottom of the plant without injuring the head.
MeaningMnemonic: The part of the <radical>grain</radical> people <radical>favor</radical> the most is the <kanji>head of the plant</kanji>. You don't eat the stems and leaves of rice or wheat plants, right? You eat the head where the grains are.

MeaningHint: The stand radical looks a bit unusual in this kanji, but that just makes it more sovereign-like, don't you think? Sovereigns don't stand like the common folk, after all.
ReadingHint: On top of being the sovereign of breakfast foods, the tater also symbolizes all the qualities of a good sovereign: humble and accessible; firm on the outside, but with a soft and warm heart; and always providing nourishment to the people… or something.
ReadingMnemonic: And it's not just any towel you stand on. To show that you're the <kanji>sovereign</kanji>, it has the symbol of a <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tot on it. The mighty, sovereign tater tot! That's how everyone knows it belongs to the sovereign.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>stand</radical> on a <radical>towel</radical> because you're the <kanji>sovereign</kanji>. As a sovereign, you're too important to be getting your shoes dirty. Everywhere you go, you're standing on a towel — that's how people know you're the sovereign.

MeaningHint: Well, of course you blink, you can't control that. And when a cleat come hurtling toward your eye it blinks automatically.
ReadingHint: Soon the person who's cleat hit you is running over and trying to apologize and stop your crying, but it's too late.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>blink</kanji> and blink and <reading>soon</reading> (しゅん) there are tears coming out of them too. You try to blink them away but it's not working. Soon you're crying and blinking and crying some more.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>eye</radical> gets kicked with a <radical>cleat</radical> that came careening toward your <radical>forehead</radical> when it popped off someone doing a <radical>dance</radical>, and you <kanji>blink</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Chrysanthemums are super important in Japan because they're the symbol of the imperial family. They're even on the cover of Japanese passports. Please don't ever pick them unless you want to end up in prison eating only rice for the rest of your life.
ReadingHint: Imagine holding a chrysanthemum in your hands while you get kicked over and over and over in prison. Was it worth it?
ReadingMnemonic: If you go to prison for <kanji>chrysanthemum</kanji> picking, everyone in the prison is going to <reading>kick</reading> (きく) you nonstop. You broke the chrysanthemum picking law, so kicking is your punishment. And believe me, you don't want these kicks.
MeaningMnemonic: If you pick the wrong kind of <radical>flowers</radical>, you'll be put into <radical>prison</radical> with nothing but <radical>rice</radical> to eat. You'll especially want to avoid picking <kanji>chrysanthemums</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine really puffing out your chest and saying, "Yes, two big prisons!", with all the pride you can muster.
ReadingHint: With your regained, child-like pride, you venture out into the world after spending way too much time in those two big prisons. A new life awaits you, good luck!
ReadingMnemonic: After mustering all your <kanji>pride</kanji>, you feel like a new person, reborn almost. "I feel like a <reading>子</reading> (こ) again!", you proclaim to the world.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Say</radical> "<radical>two</radical> <radical>big</radical> <radical>prison</radical>s" with <kanji>pride</kanji>! Nobody said you can't have pride after serving time in two big prisons. So say it with your chest!

MeaningHint: Not only will the canopy protect your manorial estate from the elements, it will also let would-be trespassers know that this is your private patch of dirt that they're not allowed to set foot on.
ReadingHint: You keep a big portrait of the shogun on the wall of your manor house to let all the serfs and peasants on your estate know who they'll have to deal with if they question your authority.
ReadingMnemonic: The establishment of your <kanji>manor</kanji> was approved by the <reading>sho</reading>gun (しょう) himself. The shogun presides over all the territory in the land, so you can't set up a private <kanji>estate</kanji> without his blessing.
MeaningMnemonic: You set up a <radical>canopy</radical> over a patch of <radical>dirt</radical> because you're setting up a <kanji>manor</kanji> here — you know, a big rural <kanji>estate</kanji>, probably with serfs and stuff.

MeaningHint: Picture the building, with it's tall top hat, inching closer to other buildings when no one is looking. It's planning to thwart everything!
ReadingHint: Watch as the soap cleans all of the paint away, thwarting the entire paint project. The painters are on the ground, crying and squeaky clean.
ReadingMnemonic: The building next door is being painted, and the top hat building is jealous. It <kanji>thwart</kanji>s the painting plans by dumping <reading>so</reading>ap (そ) out of its windows and onto the painters and the building!
MeaningMnemonic: If you see a <radical>building</radical> wearing a giant <radical>top hat</radical>, beware! This building is a fake and it's trying to <kanji>thwart</kanji> all of the plans the people in other buildings have.

MeaningHint: The entire village is shouting and yelling and pointing at the poor dog. They're boiling a giant boiling pot of water, and you can't even hear yourself think anymore. Shut up, shut up, shut up!
ReadingHint: Even the dog is mocking you now. It's barks sound more like "shut up, shut up" than "bow wow, bow wow." What the heck dog, I was trying to save your life!
ReadingMnemonic: The villagers are mad that you told them to <kanji>shut up</kanji>, and they start to <reading>mock</reading> (もく) you. They put their hands on their hips, lean forward, cross their eyes, and yell "SHUT UP!" back.
MeaningMnemonic: Your village wants to take the <radical>village</radical> <radical>dog</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it, and you can think of no other response than <kanji>SHUT UP</kanji>!

MeaningHint: See this leader's kick coming at your straw sack. Hear the leader yelling as he aims his kick at your straw sack. Hiyahhh! Also keep in mind that the life radical is missing the little drop/slide thing on the side. It's missing that because your life is lousy and sad.
ReadingHint: You thought you were all cool saying "heyo" and moving your sack away from the kick, but they had this planned all along. Heyo…
ReadingMnemonic: You manage to get your <kanji>straw sack</kanji> out of the way as you shout "<reading>Heyo</reading> (ひょう)! Look, you missed! Heyo!" Unfortunately your leader's friend was standing behind you and he kicks your sack as you move it out of the way. "Heyo!" he shouts, mockingly.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of your sad <radical>life</radical> aims a <radical>kick</radical> at the <kanji>straw sack</kanji> you are holding. This leader is in charge of making your life sad. The next step in the leader making your life suck is attempting to kick your straw sack out of your hands. Your sack holds all your valuables, so it's not something you want to have kicked.

MeaningHint: Imagine shoving your face into a nice, clean, white cotton towel. Mmm, so fresh.
ReadingHint: Picture all of the men lined up, making towels out of pure white cotton. They're all rubbing their faces in the soft cotton whenever they can.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>cotton</kanji> is made by <reading>men</reading> (めん).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>white</radical> <radical>thread</radical> in your <radical>towel</radical> comes from <kanji>cotton</kanji>. We use cotton to make lots of things, like clothes and bedsheets. But white towels are always made from soft, fluffy cotton.

MeaningHint: Imagine using your mouth to turn a tree into a shelf. Yell at this tree and keep yelling at it until you see it become a shelf.
ReadingHint: Imagine transporting the shelves in your car. Over and over. Back and forth in your car. Vroom vroom.
ReadingMnemonic: Once you turn the tree into a <kanji>shelf</kanji> you put the shelf in your <reading>ca</reading>r (か) and sell it at a local pawn shop. You keep doing this over and over again until you are rich. You command the trees to turn into shelves and then you take the shelves in your car to sell them. Easy money.
MeaningMnemonic: With the <radical>power</radical> of your <radical>mouth</radical> you command a <radical>tree</radical> to become a <kanji>shelf</kanji>. Most of the time one would have to use their hands and tools to turn a tree into a shelf, but you are capable of turning a tree into a shelf by using the power of your mouth.

MeaningHint: You run over to where the child fell. They are just fine! Because they had an umbrella, they were able to float to the bottom.
ReadingHint: "You will stay in that cavity or hole until you repent." If it hasn't been clear up until now, Emperor こういち is going mad with power.
ReadingMnemonic: "Into my <kanji>cavity</kanji> you go!" yells a voice. It is <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>child</radical> with an <radical>umbrella</radical> in front of you. Suddenly, they fall straight down into a <kanji>cavity</kanji> in the road. It's a big <kanji>hole</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture the nine crosses in front of you, lined up in a triangle. Now take your rock, hold it above your head, and SMASH it down on them as hard as you can!!
ReadingHint: You smash things because your own heart has been smashed by your cyborg. :'(
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>smash</kanji> these crosses, you think of your <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい) and how much you want to smash the people who took it away from you. Your cyborg was so good at smashing things too. You could be smashing together, if you didn't have to sell it.
MeaningMnemonic: Take this <radical>stone</radical> and throw it at <radical>nine</radical> <radical>cross</radical>es to <kanji>smash</kanji> them! It's like old-fashioned bowling, but instead of rolling this stone to knock down the crosses, you want to completely smash them to bits!

MeaningHint: Look at this sticky rice fortune of yours. Run your hands through it and feel the sticky rice between your fingers. Hear the sticky sucking sounds and you stir the sticky rice about.
ReadingHint: Imagine sculpting these sticky rice nendoroids with your bare hands. Feel the sticky rice between your fingers as you smush the rice into cute little nendoroids.
ReadingMnemonic: This rice is so <kanji>sticky</kanji> that you are able to sculpt <reading>nen</reading>doroids (ねん) from it. Your rice fortune is worth even more now that you are hand making limited edition nendoroids from it.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>rice</radical> <radical>fortune</radical> is <kanji>sticky</kanji>. Rice is sticky. So is your rice fortune. Your fortune of rice is sticky because it is made up entirely of sticky rice.

MeaningHint: Covering things up with deceit is pretty much the worst thing you can do, so don't be like this crab.
ReadingHint: Picture the crab with its head, er, front half? Whatever, it's in the guillotine and it's screaming, "You've been deceived! I never deceived anyone, you're all sheep!"
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Deceit</kanji> can only take you so far, and soon the crab is caught, tried for its crimes and coverups, and sentenced to death via <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>crab</radical> will try to hide what it <radical>lack</radical>s with <kanji>deceit</kanji>. Lying, stealing, faking things—no type of deceit is too much for a crab.

MeaningHint: Imagine an actual key going from you to another person. This key is the lie. You gotta believe in the lie for it to reach the other person and unlock their understanding. Visualize this visual lie.
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to lie your way out of this. He saw you steal his saw. He caught you red handed. What sort of lie can you spin to get yourself out of this situation?
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>lie</kanji> you told was about not stealing your buddy’s <reading>saw</reading> (さ). You actually did steal his saw. He saw you steal his saw, but now you are trying to lie your way out of it. It’s not going well.
MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> the <radical>key</radical> to telling a <kanji>lie</kanji> is to totally believe the lie yourself. They say that if you believe your own lie, others will believe it as well. This is key.

MeaningHint: When warm water touches a cold spear, fog billows out and covers everything.
ReadingHint:
You walk toward the kirin through the fog. When you get close, the image before you becomes clear: it's two cans of Kirin beer on a slide.

Next time you see fog, keep your eyes peeled for the real kirin in the kiri.
ReadingMnemonic: Through the <kanji>fog</kanji> you see the shape of a mythical <reading>kiri</reading>n (きり). You can only see the outline through the fog, but you know it's there.
MeaningMnemonic: It's <radical>rain</radical>ing on your <radical>spear</radical> that has <radical>winter</radical> <radical>power</radical>. When the warm rain hits your cold, winter spear, it creates <kanji>fog</kanji>.

MeaningHint: A willow tree is a tree that looks like it's on repeat over and over again. It also looks nice on a stamp. Note that the first stamp is kind of an ugly looking backwards stamp, and the second stamp is the normal stamp radical.
ReadingHint: Watch Ryu as he French kisses this willow tree. Hear him slurping and moaning in ecstasy. Why are you watching this? This is gross. Wait – is this turning you on!? Ew.
ReadingMnemonic: <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter really loves <kanji>willow</kanji> trees. Ryu loves them so much that he makes out with them. Ryu is open mouth kissing a willow tree. You are watching this. It is happening.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> that is <radical>stamp</radical>ed with one pattern, then <radical>stamp</radical>ed with another pattern, over and over, is the <kanji>willow</kanji> tree.

MeaningHint: Imagine Wolverine and his leader Professor X going down this slide to Italy. Hear them wooping and cheering as they ride down the slide. Whee~
ReadingHint: Imagine this eagle carrying Professor X away from Italy and out over the ocean. You can also remember this reading easily because Italy starts with the letter I. Super simple stuff.
ReadingMnemonic: Before Professor X can get off the slide into <kanji>Italy</kanji>, a large <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) swoops down and plucks him right out of his wheelchair. That eagle is very strong. Goodbye, Professor X!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> and <radical>Wolverine</radical> take a <radical>slide</radical> to <kanji>Italy</kanji>. Wolverine’s leader is Professor X, and they are both sliding down a big slide to Italy. How fun!

MeaningHint: Imagine being the narwhal leader and asking others for help. Ask a shark for help. Ask a whale for help. Ask a shrimp for help. Go on, ask.
ReadingHint: Imagine using this saw and realizing how big of a help it is. Hear the saw sawing away as you and your fellow narwhals get to work.
ReadingMnemonic: Then you discover a <reading>saw</reading> (さ) who would be a great big <kanji>help</kanji>. This saw is very strong and would help you and your narwhal construction team out a lot. You should make use of this saw right away.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of a <radical>narwhal</radical> <radical>construction</radical> crew needs all the <kanji>help</kanji> he can get. The narwhals are beginning construction on an underwater city. The leader knows the narwhals are hard construction workers, but they’ll need some help building this city.

MeaningHint: Imagine this foot long shrimp. It’s pretty big. Imagine it squirming about making a “shaku shaku” sound as it worms about.
ReadingHint: Imagine Shaq dribbling the shrimp like a basketball, saying “shaku, shaku, shaku” every time he bounces the shrimp.
ReadingMnemonic: How is <kanji>shaku</kanji>, the <kanji>Japanese foot</kanji>, pronounced by <reading>Shaq</reading> (しゃく) in Japanese? Why Shaq pronounces it shaku. It’s pronounced shaku in both English and Japanese.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>shrimp</radical> is the exact length of a <kanji>shaku</kanji>, or <kanji>Japanese foot</kanji>. That’s how they came up with this form of measurement. They measured the length of this shrimp and it was the length of a Japanese foot which they deemed the shaku. It’s equal to 30.3cm, very close to one imperial foot.

MeaningHint: Imagine kicking this lid and with each kick you feel a little bit more pathetic. Your mouth hurts a lot too.
ReadingHint: Hold your eye with one hand and your mouth with the other. Feel them pulse in pain as you reflect on how pathetic you are today.
ReadingMnemonic: You kick the lid one last time and it bounces off the wall and hits you in the <reading>eye</reading> (あい). How <kanji>pathetic</kanji>. You are a pathetic mess. You should have never kicked the lid. Now both your eye and your mouth are in pain, you pathetic fool, you.
MeaningMnemonic: With a <radical>lid</radical>, you hit yourself in the <radical>mouth</radical> and then <radical>kick</radical> it like a <kanji>pathetic</kanji> loser. You kick the lid, even though it is your fault that you hit yourself in the mouth with it. It’s not the lid’s fault. You don’t need to kick it. You’re being pathetic.

MeaningHint: Picture your mouth as an open hole. Now a landslide of something (words? food? something worse?) comes pouring out, and when it's done you have lips. You flap them open and closed to test them out.
ReadingHint: Imagine wrapping your new lips around the mouth of this Kuchi beer bottle. It's so good! And you aren't spilling any of it!
ReadingMnemonic: Now you can use your new <kanji>lips</kanji> to enjoy a nice, cold <reading>Kuchi beer</reading> (くちびる). The Kuchi people are Afghan Pashtun nomads. They are lesser known for their amazing beer. And you've never been able to get this delicious beer into your mouth, but now that you have lips you can get it all in there.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>landslide</radical> came out of your <radical>mouth</radical> and created your <kanji>lips</kanji>. Before the landslide, you just had a wide open mouth hole.

MeaningHint: Can you see the fence made of horns and flags through the light of the lantern?
ReadingHint: Picture yourself climbing over this fence (that you're definitely not supposed to climb based on all these flags and horns) and falling into the piles of hay. It's a little scratchy, but otherwise pretty comfy!
ReadingMnemonic: You want to know what this <kanji>fence</kanji> is fencing in, so you take a step closer. As the light from the lantern gets brighter you see that the fence is around a ton of <reading>hay</reading> (へい).
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>dirt</radical> there are <radical>flag</radical>s and <radical>horns</radical> lined up with a <radical>lantern</radical> attached to them, making a <kanji>fence</kanji>. It's a little weird looking, but these things are definitely supposed to fence something in.

MeaningHint: Picture a pig with horns crashing into a building and both of them crashing into the dirt. Uh oh, is the pig dead?
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to explain to the pig that it's a pig, and not a bird. "Tweet, tweet, tweet!" says the pig again. Oh dear.
ReadingMnemonic: You pull the poor pig out of the rubble of the <kanji>crash</kanji>. It's not dead, but it had a nasty bump to the head and now thinks it's a bird. "<reading>TWEE</reading>T (つい)!" says the pig. "Tweet, tweet, tweet!" Poor confused pig.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>building</radical> falls into the <radical>dirt</radical> when a <radical>pig</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> <kanji>crash</kanji>es into it. The building literally crashes to the ground when the horned pig attacks it.

MeaningHint: Imagine someone's putting a woman's mouth over your mouth, looking at you, and commenting on your likeness. All it took was this woman's mouth.
ReadingHint: Jo-Anne popped her mouth off and put it on you. Indeed with that mouth on, you have an extreme likeness to her.
ReadingMnemonic: You have a <kanji>likeness</kanji> to <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ) when you wear that mouth.
MeaningMnemonic: With a <radical>woman</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical>, you have a <kanji>likeness</kanji> to me.

MeaningHint: Imagine a gorgeous woman. Now see her get hit by a wave of water and turn into an old woman. The old woman could still be pretty too, she just looks way old now. Like ancient.
ReadingHint: Imagine this old woman acting like a sheep, just saying "baa" over and over and over again. Baa! Baa! Baa!
ReadingMnemonic: In an attempt to distract you from the fact that she is actually an <kanji>old woman</kanji>, the old woman starts to <reading>baa</reading> (ば) and act like a sheep. The old woman goes "Baa! Baa!" and hops around on all fours like a sheep.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>wave</radical> crashes into a <radical>woman</radical>, transforming her into an <kanji>old woman</kanji>! The wave washes away all of the woman's makeup, revealing her to be an old woman. The makeup she had on made her appear to be a much younger woman, but once the wave washed away her makeup, she was revealed for the old woman she is.

MeaningHint: A hat is just a piece of cloth or a towel you get to stay on your head. Extra points if it protects your eyes from the sun.
ReadingHint: Imagine sitting your grandkids on your lap someday and telling them how you're the person the bowler hat's named after. They'll be bursting with pride and admiration.
ReadingMnemonic: After inventing the <kanji>hat</kanji>, you wanted to show it off to everyone, so you went to the <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) alley. You really stood out bowling strike after strike in your fancy new hat, and before you knew it, people had coined a term for your funky headpiece. That's right — you became the namesake of the iconic bowler hat!
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>towel</radical> and wrap it around your head to keep the <radical>sun</radical> out of your <radical>eye</radical>s. You've invented... the <kanji>hat</kanji>!

MeaningHint: The towel the cash is in even has a big $ on it like the ones they have at the bank. You follow the penguin into a back alley.
ReadingHint: You wait for the penguin to leave and you run over to the hay stack and stick your hand in. But you don't feel the towel or the cash! Instead you pull out your hand and it's full of hay and needles. Ouch! No fair, gimme that cash!!
ReadingMnemonic: Your eyes follow the <kanji>cash</kanji>, and you watch as the penguin stuffs it, towel and all, into a <reading>hay</reading> (へい) stack. What a smart penguin, people don't look for cash in hay, they're too busy looking for needles.
MeaningMnemonic: That <radical>penguin</radical> over there seems to have a <radical>towel</radical> stuffed with <kanji>cash</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Don't die with a grudge, or your soul might end up angry and trapped in some roots.
ReadingHint: Picture a tree with the soul of a condor with a grudge in it before you. You feel bad about being so wasteful with paper and chopsticks now, huh?
ReadingMnemonic: If you see one of these <kanji>grudge</kanji> trees, it's probably inhabited by the soul of a <reading>con</reading>dor (こん). Humans tend to kill condors and take their trees away to build houses and make toilet paper. So condors hold pretty bad grudges against us, and so their souls end up stuck in a lot of trees.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>soul</radical> will only inhabit a <radical>root</radical> if it's holding a <kanji>grudge</kanji>. Have you ever seen a nice, haunted tree? No! They're always angry and trying to get someone back for something.

MeaningHint: You're supposed to see all the emotions, so why do you only see hate inside everyone?
ReadingHint: Zombies are pretty filled with hate, so it makes sense that you'd see a bunch of hate if you used the soul mask on them.
ReadingMnemonic: It turns out you're not looking at normal people. You see so much <kanji>hate</kanji> because all the people you're looking at are actually just a group of <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> <radical>mask</radical> helps you to see the deepest part of anyone's soul. You look around and in all the people you only see <kanji>hate</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It's important to let yourself rest every now and then, you know.
ReadingHint: You should rest right now. Take a break from Japanese and go eat some cake. (Just remember to come back when your tongue and heart are sufficiently rested!)
ReadingMnemonic: What better way to <kanji>rest</kanji> than to eat some <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! No talking necessary. Just enjoy the delicious taste of cake on your tongue and feel your heart let out a sigh of relief.
MeaningMnemonic: You keep your <radical>tongue</radical> to your <radical>self</radical> to give your <radical>heart</radical> a <kanji>rest</kanji>. You’ve been talking and moving your tongue around all day expressing your feelings. This has tired both your tongue and your heart out, so you stop your tongue moving and decide that you need some self rest time.

MeaningHint: Use this folding fan to fan your face. Feel the refreshing breeze as you admire the gorgeous feather you just added. What a fashionista you’ve become.
ReadingHint: Imagine handing this newly made folding fan to the centaur. See his face light up with delight as he examines your fine craftsmanship and starts fanning himself with it. Another satisfied customer!
ReadingMnemonic: Your biggest <kanji>folding fan</kanji> customer is a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). This centaur just loves your folding fans and this one you just made with the feather was for the centaur’s latest order. You go back out your door and deliver this folding fan to the centaur.
MeaningMnemonic: After passing through a <radical>door</radical>, you use a <radical>feather</radical> to put the finishing touches on a <kanji>folding fan</kanji>. The door leads to your folding fan workshop where you make all your folding fans. This folding fan is now complete that you’ve added a feather to it. How lovely it is.

MeaningHint: Imagine waking up one morning to find all your doors missing. The door criminal strikes again! They left the sliding door behind though, since they only wanted the hinged 扉 ones. So… there's that at least?
ReadingHint: Now picture yourself waking up to a bright, beautiful morning with all your 扉 doors still safely attached to their hinges. Thanks, 飛び Ra!
ReadingMnemonic: To protect your <kanji>door</kanji>, you get help from <reading>飛び Ra</reading> (とびら). 飛び Ra is the flying (飛び) version of Ra, the Egyptian sun god. Whenever a criminal tries to steal your door, 飛び Ra flies out from behind it and blinds them with his sun-god brightness.
MeaningMnemonic: What would a <radical>door</radical> <radical>criminal</radical> steal? A <kanji>door</kanji>, of course, but specifically a <kanji>hinged door</kanji>. You know, the kind that swings open and shut? That's the kind of door 扉 means.

MeaningHint: Hold your thousand+ fingers up to the sun and imagine inserting them into the sun's super-hot ... s-sun lava. (That's what it is, right? Yeah, let's go with that.)
ReadingHint: Since you have a thousand fingers too many, you decide to insert them all into the sun and watch as their souls pop out. You listen to the little souls scream at you.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>insert</kanji> one finger, it burns up immediately, and its <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) comes flying out screaming for a moment before it disappears. Hmm. You insert another finger and as it burns up, another little soul comes out screeching, "WHY WOULD YOU INSERT ME IN THERE!" and is gone.
MeaningMnemonic: You have <radical>fingers</radical> in the <radical>thousand</radical>s and while looking up at the <radical>sun</radical> you decide you want to <kanji>insert</kanji> them in there.

MeaningHint: And don't use your fingers either, 'cause they'll just get bitten off. You absolutely want to make sure it's the palm — a nice flat open palm. It's a lot like feeding a horse, I guess.
ReadingHint: Imagine if a Shogun and a Viking had actually met in history (never mind that the dates don't overlap). You can definitely picture the quiet, refined Shogun putting his palm over the mouth of some noisy Viking during a tough diplomatic meeting.
ReadingMnemonic: This Viking-touching technique of using the <kanji>palm</kanji> was developed by the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). The Shogun has to deal with a lot of loudmouthed Vikings, so he's learned to use his palm to cover their mouths.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to cover a <radical>Viking</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical> with your <radical>hand</radical>, you have to use your <kanji>palm</kanji>. Why the palm? Well, if you do it knuckles-to-mouth, you're sort of just punching the Viking… and you don't want a punch a Viking. That's why you use the palm.

MeaningHint: Imagine having all this tsunami water in your hair. Hear it drip from your mohawk. Drip… drip… drip…
ReadingHint: Feel the weight of this dryer helmet on your head as you listen to it whirr away. The dripping has finally stopped! Success!
ReadingMnemonic: Luckily, you’re a self-proclaimed <reading>techie</reading> (てき), so you develop a device to stop the <kanji>drip</kanji> drip dripping. This techie device of yours is like a hair dryer helmet. You put it over your mohawk, it does its thing, and your hair is dry! Being a techie sure does have its benefits.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>mohawk</radical> and it begins to <kanji>drip</kanji>. The water from the tsunami continues to drip from your mohawk long after the tsunami has passed. There’s a lot of water in your hair now, so it’s probably going to drip for a while.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as you cook with a fire very confidently. Knowing you cannot fail, feel the heat and hear the crackling embers of the fire. Smell the delicious smell of whatever you are cooking as well.
ReadingHint: Imagine swinging this meal over the fire. Feel the flames licking at your fingertips as you cook this meal by swinging it back and forth over the fire.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>cook</kanji> your meal evenly by <reading>swi</reading>nging (すい) it back and forth over the fire. You swing it left, you swing it right. You swing it to, you swing it fro. All this swinging ensures a properly cooked meal.
MeaningMnemonic: Without a <radical>fire</radical>, you will <radical>lack</radical> the ability to <kanji>cook</kanji> anything. You really need a fire to cook properly. Having a fire will make sure any cooking endeavors you attempt will not fail.

MeaningHint: Imagine finding this big treasure treasure treasure treasure and being really refreshed and relieved and happy. This treasure treasure treasure treasure is so big, you’ll be set for life!
ReadingHint: Imagine the most sour taste you could ever imagine. So super-duper sour! Now imagine that sour taste instantly melting away once you discover this big refreshing treasure treasure treasure treasure. Jackpot!
ReadingMnemonic: You’d had a <reading>sour</reading> (さわ) taste in your mouth for not being able to find any treasure treasure treasure treasure for such a long time, but now that you found this big one, the sour taste is totally gone and it’s sooooo <kanji>refreshing</kanji>!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>big</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> <radical>treasure</radical> is <kanji>refreshing</kanji>! Sorry, I can’t say treasure treasure treasure treasure without saying it four times. See? I did it again. Anyway, this big treasure treasure treasure treasure is really refreshing. It’s been a long time since you found a treasure treasure treasure treasure, so finding this big one is really refreshing.

MeaningHint: The meaning you're trying to learn is tatami mat, so focus on the beautiful tatami mats that are strangely in the middle of this rice paddy. On top is a forehead with a top hat. It almost looks like the tatami mats are an altar for this gruesome scene.
ReadingHint: You saw Joe setting up the tatami mats a day earlier, but you thought he was just making an area for workers in the field to rest in. Does the forehead with a top hat have anything to do with Joe? What sort of ominous sign could this be?
ReadingMnemonic: Who made this creepy <kanji>tatami mat</kanji> altar? It was <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the increasingly concerning farmhand who lives in your village.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>rice paddy</radical> there is a <radical>forehead</radical> with a <radical>top hat</radical>. It sits atop an area of <kanji>tatami mat</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: You can even rub charcoal in your eyes if you really want to clear them up, but you should probably only do this under the supervision of your eye doctor.
ReadingHint: Imagine how dangerous it would be not to have clear vision while you row around in a row boat. You might even smack yourself in the face with your own oars.
ReadingMnemonic: You have to have <kanji>clear</kanji> vision because you're about to go out in your <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat. The light off the water shines right into your eyes and you need the charcoal to clear that up for you. Otherwise you'll end up crashing into the shore or going over a waterfall to your death.
MeaningMnemonic: Rub <radical>charcoal</radical> under your <radical>eye</radical>s if you want to see <kanji>clear</kanji>ly. Sports players also rub charcoal under their eyes to see nice and clear in the bright sun.

MeaningHint: Imagine if your torso was as large as the moon. You’re a gigantic human. Imagine floating around in space like this. Wooooaaaahhh.
ReadingHint: Imagine how many doughnuts you would have to eat to get your torso this size. Now imagine eating all of those doughnuts. I hope you like doughnuts, because you’re going to be eating a lot of them.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>torso</kanji> got so big because of all the <reading>dough</reading>nuts (どう) you ate. You ate so many doughnuts that your torso got to be the size of the moon and then you floated away into space with your own gravitational pull and everything. Doughnuts are dangerous things.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> is the <radical>same</radical> as your <kanji>torso</kanji>. You must have a really huge torso for it to be the same as the moon.

MeaningHint: Imagine this insect with a construction hat on, building a rainbow. He works very fast. The resulting rainbow is beautiful!
ReadingHint: Imagine this construction insect being all whiny and needy for attention and stuff. It’s pathetic. Hear him whine and whine because of how needy he is.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>rainbow</kanji> building insect is so <reading>needy</reading> (にじ). He is needy because he needs everyone to look at his rainbow and praise him for what he’s done. “Look at my rainbow! Look at how good it is and I am! I’m so needy! Needy for praise and recognition!”
MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>insect</radical> specializing in <radical>construction</radical> builds a <kanji>rainbow</kanji>. Did you ever wonder where rainbows come from? Construction insects build them. Construction insects build rainbows.

MeaningHint: You are making the towel really long, because it's white and it might be good for writing in. It's going to become your notebook.
ReadingHint: Something bad is going to happen, and you need to warn everybody. It's her, it's her, it's her. Mrs. Chou, Mrs. Chou, Mrs. Chou!
ReadingMnemonic: Inside your long towel <kanji>notebook</kanji> you just write the same name over and over again: "Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading>" (ちょう).
MeaningMnemonic: Take your <radical>towel</radical> and make it <radical>long</radical>. It has to be longer than that! For this will be your <kanji>notebook</kanji>, which you will write all your notes in.

MeaningHint: Imagine sitting at your desk writing when a mosquito starts bzzzzing in your ear. You can't ignore it, it's gonna bite you. So you flail around helplessly.
ReadingHint: You can't. You can't write like this. You need to kill the mosquito and its little car too.
ReadingMnemonic: That <kanji>mosquito</kanji> noise sounds like it's driving a tiny <reading>ca</reading>r (か) in your ear. How are you supposed to write with a bug driving around going bzzz bzzz like a revving car?
MeaningMnemonic: The type of <radical>insect</radical> that ruins your <radical>writing</radical> is the <kanji>mosquito</kanji>. You're trying to write, when you hear that horrible high-pitched mosquito noise in your ear. You jerk your head and swat and it's gone. But when you look down, there's lines all over what you were writing.

MeaningHint: Snakes love insects, especially when they're ready to eat on a spoon. But, snakes don't usually go on top of the roof, so that's why you have to tempt one with an insect in a spoon.
ReadingHint: Especially big snakes. They're really, really hebi.
ReadingMnemonic: Why don't you just bring a <kanji>snake</kanji> up to the roof yourself? Because snakes are <reading>heavy</reading> (へび).
MeaningMnemonic: There is an <radical>insect</radical> on the <radical>roof</radical> in a <radical>spoon</radical>. It's there for the eating, and it's how you plan to attract a <kanji>snake</kanji> to your roof.

MeaningHint: Honestly, that's what you get for lying. But it doesn't make it any less humiliating even if you deserve it.
ReadingHint: Imagine all of the cruel jokes people say to humiliate you. Feel your face burn and your stomach flip.
ReadingMnemonic: Your friends and coworkers <kanji>humiliate</kanji> you more by making <reading>joke</reading>s (じょく). "Uh oh, I heard it might rain later, I hope you don't drown in the inch of water that's coming!"
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>landslide</radical> <radical>measurement</radical> <kanji>humiliate</kanji>s you. You told everyone you couldn't come to work, that your house had been destroyed and your car was carried away, but then scientists measured the landslide, and it only went a few feet.

MeaningHint: Imagine melting down your gold book. You're excited to have your new flower pot, but you're a little sad because this book was actually pretty interesting. Make a mental note to buy it in paperback later.
ReadingHint: Okay, so this may not be the best flower pot. You usually don't need a hatch or this much gold coating. But that's how they used to do it in the old days, so that's good enough for you!
ReadingMnemonic: The gold <kanji>flower pot</kanji> has a <reading>hatch</reading> (はち) attached to it, which used to be the book's back cover. That's fine, you use the hatch as a lid for the flower pot!
MeaningMnemonic: Melt down this <radical>gold</radical> <radical>book</radical> and recast it as a <kanji>flower pot</kanji>. A solid gold book is too heavy to hold and too shiny to read, but a solid gold flower pot would make a fancy new addition to your garden.

MeaningHint: Feel the rain hit your eye and then crystalize into frost immediately. The rain in your eye is wet, and then very cold as it becomes frost.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself walking proudly, casting your frost gaze at all who you pass. "Wow look at that frosty, cool shemo. She's sooooo cool!" you hear them whisper. (Haha cool, get it? Because you're frosty and also a little sad, which is totally in when you're a teen.)
ReadingMnemonic: This whole description is pretty emo. You came up with this whole emo <kanji>frost</kanji> idea because you are a <reading>shemo</reading> (しも). An emo who goes by she pronouns.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Rain</radical> falls off a <radical>tree</radical> and lands in your <radical>eye</radical> where it turns to <kanji>frost</kanji>. It turns to frost because it’s super cold outside and your body is freezing. The rain that fell off the tree turns to frost as soon as it hits your eye.

MeaningHint: You don’t want to starve, do you? Feel your tummy gurgle in pain. You gotta eat this table. Taste the dry wood taste on your tongue as you eat the table and get splinters in your tongue and gums.
ReadingHint: Imagine eating every key. Taste the cold metal on your tongue. After you swallow each key, you hear it clink against the other keys already in your tummy. Clink!
ReadingMnemonic: After you finish the table, you still feel like you might <kanji>starve</kanji> so you decide to eat every <reading>key</reading> (き) off your key ring. One by one, you eat each and every key so you don’t starve.
MeaningMnemonic: You’re so hungry that you <radical>eat</radical> a <radical>table</radical> so you won’t <kanji>starve</kanji>. If you don’t eat this table, you will most definitely starve, so you eat the table to stay alive.

MeaningHint: You have to check your ego at the door, because you'll definitely starve otherwise.
ReadingHint: Now your starved body has been moved to the top of a castle, where you scare away people and birds with your loud grumbling stomach and scary face.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>starve</kanji> (thanks ego) and turn into a <reading>ga</reading>rgoyle (が). As your stomach aches, your skin turns gray and you slowly harden into a crouched position.
MeaningMnemonic: If you have nothing to <radical>eat</radical> but your <radical>ego</radical>, you'll <kanji>starve</kanji>. You can't eat it, and your ego is keeping you from accepting actual food or help from other people.

MeaningHint: Examine this mysterious livestock from a distance. Imagine them floating around the mysterious rice paddy like they aren’t even walking. Hear them moo and oink as well.
ReadingHint: Look at this chick bobbling about in the air. It looks very ephemeral and other worldly. Still a very cute chick though. You want to pet it.
ReadingMnemonic: Your favorite member of the mysterious <kanji>livestock</kanji> crew is a little <reading>chick</reading> (ちく). The chick is very cute and little. The chick is also very mysterious because it floats about without even flapping its little wings.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>mysterious</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> there is some <kanji>livestock</kanji>. This livestock is also mysterious because no one knows where it came from. The mysterious livestock are only seen in the mysterious rice paddy. How mysterious indeed.

MeaningHint: Hear the cape flapping behind you wildly, and hear yourself murmuring your prayers for extra swiftness. You just have a need for speed, okay? And with any luck, the gods will grant you the gift… of swift…
ReadingHint: Fortunately, the lack of jeans make you even more swift and aerodynamic. You also feel a swift and pleasant breeze on all your downstairs bits now…
ReadingMnemonic: You move at such <kanji>swift</kanji> speeds now, your <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) are ripped right off your body.
MeaningMnemonic: When you ride a <radical>scooter</radical>, you wear a <radical>cape</radical> and carry a <radical>cross</radical> in order to move at a <kanji>swift</kanji> speed. The cape gives you a swiftness buff, naturally, and the cross lets you pray to a divine power to help you move ever more swiftly down the road.

MeaningHint: Look at this odd horse. It's a little odd-looking, but it turned its quirks into strengths and now it's an exceptional horse for horse riding!
ReadingHint: Want to ride the odd horse? Then, you must have the key to open its stable!
ReadingMnemonic: Before <kanji>horse riding</kanji>, you’ll need the <reading>key</reading> (き) to access the stable and let out the odd horse you'll be riding.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>horse</radical> may look <radical>odd</radical> but it’s actually perfect for <kanji>horse riding</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself spraying poison on these mysterious flowers, but they just double in size, and continue to amass.
ReadingHint: Feel the wetness of the rice paddy under your feet and the fluffiness of the chicks pressing in on you. Watch the looming, mysterious flowers above you as the chicks smother you.
ReadingMnemonic: As the flowers <kanji>amass</kanji> it starts attracting wildlife. Soon thousands of baby <reading>chick</reading>s (ちく) are amassing in the rice paddy as well.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> are <radical>mysterious</radical>ly appearing in the <radical>rice paddy</radical>. No matter what you do to them, they continue to <kanji>amass</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the lack of shrimp on the ice. All you wanted was some delicious shrimp for dinner, but now it's all gone.
ReadingHint: Imagine the worker's jeans and how gross they are with all those shrimp stuffed into them. Yuck!
ReadingMnemonic: How could the store <kanji>use up</kanji> its shrimp supply so quickly? Suddenly you notice the worker behind the fish counter has shrimp stuffed into their <reading>jean</reading>s (じん) . Aha! The culprit!
MeaningMnemonic: You see <radical>shrimp</radical> on <radical>ice</radical> at the market in the morning. "I'll get some on the way home" you think to yourself. Later that evening, you see no shrimp, just ice. The supply of shrimp has been <kanji>exhaust</kanji>ed. It's all gone! You never thought they'd <kanji>use up</kanji> their shrimp supply so quickly.

MeaningHint: Imagine digging your cleats into the bark of the tree until you get high enough for your hair to start coloring itself tree colors. It might be a lot of work, but it's worth it.
ReadingHint: You couldn't take the pain of losing your cyborg, so you've become one. Your robo-hair can change its coloring, but soon you'll be even more cyborg-y than before!
ReadingMnemonic: This cool <kanji>coloring</kanji> can only happen because you turned yourself into a <reading>cy</reading>borg (さい). Your hair and scalp are now made of high tech parts, not human ones.
MeaningMnemonic: You use a <radical>cleat</radical> to climb a <radical>tree</radical> and your <radical>hair</radical> gets the same <kanji>coloring</kanji> as the foliage because your hair is like a chameleon. The coloring of your hair changes to whatever color is around you, so you use your cleats to climb trees to make your hair turn green and brown, and in the fall, yellow and orange and red!

MeaningHint: When you have a recollection of some idea or thing in the past, it's just your soul saying, hey remember this? Let's not forget this.
ReadingHint: Imagine recollecting a past life in Oklahoma. Life was simple and nice. You should consider taking this as a sign to move there again!
ReadingMnemonic: Your soul is sending you a <kanji>recollection</kanji> right now of... your past life as a farmer in <reading>Ok</reading>lahoma (おく). You see yourself farming and sweating and living a wonderful life. Hmm, maybe you should move to Oklahoma and live the simple life again.
MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>soul</radical> has an <radical>idea</radical> it's just a <kanji>recollection</kanji> of something you already know. Only your brain can have original ideas, so when your soul tries to come up with one, it's just a recollection of something you thought before now.

MeaningHint: Feel the water from the tsunami crashing into your form. Feel the water on your body as your form begins to melt. I’m melting! I’m mellltiinnnggg!
ReadingHint: Feel the melt begin to reverse as you eat your yogurt. Taste the yogurt on your tongue as your form begins to solidify once again.
ReadingMnemonic: The only way to reverse the <kanji>melt</kanji> process is to eat some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). The yogurt counteracts the melting from the water and your form will become whole again. Quick, eat some yogurt!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>form</radical> and you begin to <kanji>melt</kanji>. You are a witch and water makes you melt. The tsunami is a lot of water, so once it touches your form, you begin to melt.

MeaningHint: Try and move this rake over to the measurement table. It is resistant so you have a hard time moving it. Imagine the rake struggling and resisting endlessly.
ReadingHint: See yourself tying the tie around the rake and attaching it to the measurement table. The rake is still jostling around, but the tie keeps it from moving about too terribly much.
ReadingMnemonic: Because the rake is persistently <kanji>resistant</kanji>, you eventually tie it to the table with your neck <reading>tie</reading> (たい). The tie won’t hold for long, so you need to take each measurement quickly.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>rake</radical> in for a <radical>measurement</radical> but it is <kanji>resistant</kanji>. The rake doesn’t want to have its measurements taken. The rake is resistant to have any measurement taken because it feels inferior to the other rakes and doesn’t want to be embarrassed if it ends up being smaller than the others.

MeaningHint: Picture your mama asking you to dance. The two of you pick up and kick your feet to dance, spinning 'round and 'round.
ReadingHint: Lean down and take a big whiff of your dancing feet. The odor is... HORRIBLE! AHGH!
ReadingMnemonic: You both <kanji>dance</kanji> so much that you develop an <reading>odo</reading>r (おど). Not just any odor, an odor particular to stinky dancing feet.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>foot</radical>, as your <radical>mama</radical> says, was given the <radical>task</radical> to <kanji>dance</kanji>. Shake those two foots your mama gave you and dance dance dance!

MeaningHint: "You sure are clever, man!" you shout encouragingly at your wise servant as he plops his stool down on another mound of shellfish. Dinner tonight is sure to be a seafood feast, and you can't wait!
ReadingHint: Picture Ken standing there with his sword and crab-shell armor. Pretty clever of him to disguise himself as your servant for so many years just to save a few mollusks. It might have been wiser not to shuck so many of them first, though…
ReadingMnemonic: "Yes, more <kanji>clever</kanji> than you!" he yells, tearing off his servant's disguise. "It is I, <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai. I won't let you eat these shellfish. I'm taking them back to the sea, where they belong!"
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>servant</radical> uses a <radical>stool</radical> to break open <radical>shellfish</radical>. This is really <kanji>clever</kanji>, because shucking shellfish is a lot of work. It's also a <kanji>wise</kanji> move, because it'll save him time that he can spend on all his other servant chores.

MeaningHint: Imagine this radiance emanating from your shiny dome. The radiance is blinding!
ReadingHint: Point your shiny forehead, filled with radiance, at your car. Hear the engine start and jump to life as you power it with the reflected sunlight from your forehead.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>radiance</kanji> is the <reading>key</reading> (き) to powering your car. Your car runs on reflected solar energy, and the radiance coming from your forehead is the most powerful source of radiant sunlight ever imagined. Your forehead is a perfect key for this car.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>light</radical> shines on your <radical>forehead</radical> in your <radical>car</radical> with a great <kanji>radiance</kanji>. The car you are in is a convertible, so the light shines right onto your big bald forehead. The light has a pretty powerful radiance as it is, but the sheen from this light hitting your forehead is the most powerful radiance ever witnessed by man.

MeaningHint: The moon actually looks like it wants to threaten you now too. It seems to have grown a big, smiling, angry mouth and it's way closer to you than you've ever seen it. All that power is radiating out of it and down towards you, threateningly.
ReadingHint: Picture the powerful moon, threatening to crush きょうと and all its residents. If only a small green boy could turn back time and save everyone from this powerful moon!
ReadingMnemonic: This extra power-filled moon is now trying to <kanji>threaten</kanji> the safety of <reading>きょう</reading>と. All of きょうと is shaking and getting dark as the moon comes closer and closer.
MeaningMnemonic: There's <radical>power</radical>, <radical>power</radical>, and <radical>power</radical> stacked on top of the <radical>moon</radical>, <kanji>threaten</kanji>ing to bring it down!

MeaningHint: Wow, these hemp plants are so big that they look like trees. But, despite their size they're still just hemp.
ReadingHint: Your mama knew that you were going to like hemp in the future, so she planted these hemp plants for you. What a great mama.
ReadingMnemonic: Who planted all this <kanji>hemp</kanji>? Your <reading>ma</reading>ma (ま) did.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>canopy</radical> of <radical>tree</radical>s isn't actually a canopy of trees at all. When you look closely, you see these trees are actually giant stalks of <kanji>hemp</kanji>.
