MeaningHint: Picture these lamps lining the street, with their little fires burning brightly. Nowadays we don't have real fire in them, we use electricity!
ReadingHint: Picture とうきょう, not lit up by all of the electricity, signs, shops, and things that light up its streets today, but by old-fashioned fire lamps!
ReadingMnemonic: These street <kanji>lamp</kanji>s were first used in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. It was the most advanced city after all. And more people were out at night in とうきょう than anywhere else in Japan. So they needed lamps to light things up!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Fire</radical> you use to light up the <radical>street</radical> needs to go in a <kanji>lamp</kanji>. Campfires, matches? No, none of those work. You need bright, safe lamps to keep the street nice at night.

MeaningHint: Imagine studying this little blossom. Its mouth is open and nectar-filled, and out of it spring these horn-like things that reach up to heaven. It smells nice too. A perfect specimen for your blossom collection, maybe?
ReadingHint: Picture those classic dainty little blossoms — buttercups, pansies, you name it. Now imagine their tougher cousins, blossoms so tough you'd need a saw to cut them down. If you can picture the latter, you should remember the さ reading.
ReadingMnemonic: If it's shaped like a mouth with horns poking out of it, you know it's going to be a tough little <kanji>blossom</kanji>. That's why you'll need a <reading>saw</reading> (さ) if you've got any hope of cutting it down for your blossom collection.
MeaningMnemonic: When a flower looks like a <radical>mouth</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> that reach up to <radical>heaven</radical>, you know it's in <kanji>blossom</kanji>. It's all opened up like a mouth, and the pistil and stamen are like horns reaching up to heaven for its sunlight and rain.

MeaningHint: Imagine burying a dead clown in the dirt and then planting some seeds on top. It's worth it, you think, if it helps cultivate your crops.
ReadingHint: Saying bye is polite. Never forget to say bye, even in the middle of a cold-blooded clown murder.
ReadingMnemonic: Before you can start this whole <kanji>cultivation</kanji> process however, you have to say <reading>bye</reading> (ばい) to the clown. Say bye to him, then bash his head in, and drop him in the dirt.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>dirt</radical> needs a  <radical>clown</radical> in it if you want to <kanji>cultivate</kanji> good crops. Clowns act as a natural fertilizer, and this will make it super easy to cultivate any crops you could ever want in this dirt. Isn't nature amazing?

MeaningHint: Imagine if you went full force into everything you've ever even thought of doing. Imagine how burnt out your soul would be. Imagine all the regret you'd have that you didn't just stick to one thing and do it well.
ReadingHint: Throw everything you regret away. Only kayak from now on.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>regret</kanji> is too strong for you to ignore, so you decide to drop everything but <reading>kay</reading>aking (かい). If there's one thing you refuse to regret, it's your bright orange kayak and all the friends you made at kayaking club.
MeaningMnemonic: If you try to put your <radical>soul</radical> into <radical>every</radical>thing, you'll <kanji>regret</kanji> it. It's not so much that putting your soul into things is bad, but every single thing? You'll burn out immediately. You won't be able to be good at any of those things. And you'll regret all the time you wasted trying to be the best at everything.

MeaningHint: Imagine the pig scooting around. It is able to accomplish anything you can imagine (so go nuts with your imagination).
ReadingHint: Perhaps it even takes the swing all the way around in a full 360 degree rotation. This horned scooter riding pig can truly accomplish anything.
ReadingMnemonic: It was even able to successfully <kanji>accomplish</kanji> a feat that involved it jumping its scooter right into a <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい), swinging back and forth, then jumping off.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>scooter</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> with a <radical>pig</radical> riding on it will be able to <kanji>accomplish</kanji> anything.

MeaningHint: Imagine if you were given a knife and allowed into the king's squad. Would you let the power corrupt you?
ReadingHint: I'm not saying Han Solo is a bad squad leader, but his track record isn't the greatest. I mean, best case scenario the king gets stabbed in the arm and they manage to win.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>squad</kanji> is led by <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo, so it's basically a 50/50 shot that the king is gonna die.
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>king</radical> gives you a <radical>knife</radical> you become part of his <kanji>squad</kanji>. Every king has a knife squad or two protecting him and making sure his subjects keep in line.

MeaningHint: Picture all of the different colors of paint being swept along by tsunami after tsunami, painting the dirt as it goes.
ReadingHint: Imagine dipping each of your toes into the paint-dirt, one by one, to paint each of them a different color. Why are you doing this?
ReadingMnemonic: You touch the <kanji>paint</kanji> in the dirt with your <reading>toe</reading> (と) to see if it's still wet. It is. The paint is still very wet and now your toe looks like a tiny rainbow.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>surplus</radical> will cover all the <radical>dirt</radical> with <kanji>paint</kanji>. The surplus of tsunamis will strike the paint factories first (everyone knows paint factories are built close to the ocean). Then the paint will flow over all the dirt and paint it a ton of rainbow colors.

MeaningHint: Look at all these ladles lying diagonally in front of you. Pick them up. Feel the wood grain. Wonder why you have so many.
ReadingHint: Uh oh, is this one of those weird tests where they find out if you're the chosen one or some old scholar reincarnated? Who knew laying ladles at diagonal angles could reveal this kinda stuff?!
ReadingMnemonic: In front of this <kanji>diagonal</kanji> pattern is a <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ) who is currently staring at you. You slowly reach down and turn one of the ladles around, diagonally, and look back up. The shaman's eyes are wide, and now they're scribbling on in a notebook.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>surplus</radical> of <radical>ladle</radical>s laid out <kanji>diagonally</kanji> in front of you. Why are they diagonal? And why do you have so many ladles? Why the surplus? Hang on, we'll get there.

MeaningHint: Picture two heavily armed residents of your district punching the living crap out of each other. They're both armed to the teeth with missile launchers, flamethrowers, katanas, lightsabers, etc., but neither one wants to be the first to draw a weapon and escalate the situation.
ReadingHint: Imagine the heavily armed combatants from earlier slugging it out, but this time the 王 is watching them from a box seat above the action. He gleefully encourages them, a maniacal, bloodthirsty glint in his eye.
ReadingMnemonic: The district residents <kanji>punch</kanji> each other in front of the <reading>王</reading> (おう). These punching matches are actually gladiatorial battles for the 王's entertainment.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>district</radical> is full of <radical>weapons</radical>, but everyone prefers to <kanji>punch</kanji> each other. When everyone's armed, things can escalate quickly, so it's better to stick to fists to be safe.

MeaningHint: Imagine the shield glowing brightly as hymns play in the background. The demons try to get close to the pope to shove him off the cliff but the holy shield is too bright and popey.
ReadingHint: This pope would have been in a lot of trouble if June's summer sun hadn't risen at just the right moment. His shield would have become useless and he might have been shoved off the cliff if it was a less sunny month.
ReadingMnemonic: The light of the pope's <kanji>shield</kanji> begins to fade. The shield's holy power is waning! Just as the demons are about to push him off the cliff, the sun begins to rise. That's right! It's <reading>June</reading> (じゅん) and summer has just begun. The pope's shield reflects the power of June's bright sun into the eyes of the demons, casting them off the cliff behind him and back into hell!
MeaningMnemonic: On a <radical>cliff</radical>, the <radical>pope</radical> is holding a <kanji>shield</kanji>. The pope is near the edge of the cliff and is using the shield to fend off demons. The demons want to shove the pope off the cliff, but the pope is using his holy shield of popery to fend them off.

MeaningHint: Picture this fat turkey with flowers on its head, sitting on a stool. It's gobbling for grains and you need to be the one who harvests them.
ReadingHint: Imagine an angry cock chasing you around as you try to harvest grain. He keeps pecking you, ouch!
ReadingMnemonic: You're just starting to <kanji>harvest</kanji> the grains when an angry <reading>cock</reading> (かく) shows up! He's mad because these are his grains and you're about to harvest them and feed them to a turkey instead of him.
MeaningMnemonic: You need to feed this <radical>grain</radical> to a <radical>flower</radical> wearing <radical>turkey</radical> on a <radical>stool</radical> so you start the <kanji>harvest</kanji>. Grains don't pick themselves, you have to harvest them.

MeaningHint: Imagine a horse trying to write a paragraph about why it deserves to be a chess piece. See the horse struggle with his hooves to write out a paragraph as he neighs in nervous frustration.
ReadingHint: See this horse in its coma. You feel bad that it will never be able to be a chess piece, but this is a bit dramatic.
ReadingMnemonic: Upon being told that he will never be a <kanji>chess piece</kanji>, the horse slips into a <reading>coma</reading> (こま). The horse will never awaken from this coma as he knows he will never fulfill his dream of becoming a chess piece, so he has nothing to live for.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>horse</radical> who can write a <radical>paragraph</radical> gets to be a <kanji>chess piece</kanji>. Only the most erudite of horses gets selected to be a chess piece. The knight’s chess piece is represented by a horse, and for a horse to earn this honor, they must first write a paragraph explaining why they deserve the honor.

MeaningHint: Imagine having fingers that work like a magic wand of extraction. With a flick of your fingers, you could extract any radical out of a kanji too. How could these special fingers not be a reason for you to extract? With these fingers, you can't stop extracting!
ReadingHint: Taste that chewy goodness in every bite. All you need is a dash of Chewbacca extract.
ReadingMnemonic: Your magic fingers can even extract the essence out of living things, so you decide to <kanji>extract</kanji> some from <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). You always thought a nice Chewbacca extract could take your cookies to the next level. It'll help offset the sweetness and make them extra chewy (or chewbacca-y!).
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> give you a good <radical>reason</radical> to <kanji>extract</kanji> things. You extract juice from fruits, oil from seeds, and coffee from beans. Why? Because you have fingers! That's your reason.

MeaningHint: Picture the axe hovering above your fingers. Now say the vow. But don't lie. The axe knows when you're lying.
ReadingHint: Imagine what it would be like to slice off your own fingers with the saber you've carried for so long. You better not break that vow.
ReadingMnemonic: When you finish your <kanji>vow</kanji>, you take out your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) and cut the axe in half! You know you're never going to break your vow, and if you do, you'll use your own saber to cut off your fingers.
MeaningMnemonic: With your <radical>fingers</radical> under an <radical>axe</radical>, you <radical>say</radical> a <kanji>vow</kanji>. A vow is a sacred promise, so you have to be really serious when you say one. That's why you do it with an axe hovering above your fingers—it's a reminder that if you break this vow your fingers are gonna get chopped off.

MeaningHint: Maybe the five mouths are your five senses, "speaking" information to your soul so you can realize and perceive things about the world. Whoa, dude…
ReadingHint: You now realize why Godzilla destroys all those buildings: he's warning the Japanese people about the use of new technology and warfare and experimentation. He just wants everyone to realize what the consequences of these new things could be!
ReadingMnemonic: Right now those five mouths are helping you to <kanji>realize</kanji> something about <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご). First you hear Godzilla, then you see him, then you smell him, then you touch him. Finally you taste Godzilla. Now you've realized exactly what kind of thing he is. You truly know Godzilla.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> connects to <radical>five</radical> different <radical>mouths</radical>, allowing you to <kanji>realize</kanji> something. You can realize profound truths about the world with these five mouths speaking to your soul.

MeaningHint: Imagine being in a field, using your fingers and a stone to cultivate the whole thing, by hand.
ReadingHint: Now cultivation is going to involve getting all the tacks out of here, preferably with the stone and not your fingers, so they don't get stuck anymore.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Cultivation</kanji> is suddenly halted because a <reading>tack</reading> (たく) sticks into your finger. You pull the tack out, throw it away and start cultivation again when... ouch! There's another tack. This field is full of tacks!
MeaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> and a <radical>stone</radical> to begin <kanji>cultivation</kanji> of the land. You use your fingers to till the soil, the stone to flatten the ground, and you carefully poke the seeds into the dirt and then use the stone to smash them further down.

MeaningHint: Imagine reading the paragraph very slowly. You find your answer. It looks like you'll be able to arrest that person...
ReadingHint: Still, according to this paragraph you're running your fingers through, you can do it. You can arrest こういち.
ReadingMnemonic: Who do you want to <kanji>arrest</kanji>? You want to arrest <reading>こう</reading>いち. Even though he's started wars, created a religion, and trapped a child in a cavity, it's going to be tricky to arrest him because he's the emperor now.
MeaningMnemonic: You run your <radical>fingers</radical> along the words of a  <radical>paragraph</radical>. You are learning about the law because you plan to <kanji>arrest</kanji> somebody.

MeaningHint: Imagine making a foundation with the stone and the two trees so you’ll have a place for your coat rack. Feel the wood grain and cold stone against your skin as you move them around to form the foundation.
ReadingHint: Imagine getting on your hands and knees, rubbing a bar of soap all over the foundation you built. The soap gets into all the nooks and crannies in the wood and stone. Now if a coat falls off the coat rack onto the floor, you just add water and poof! Clean!
ReadingMnemonic: After you finish the <kanji>foundation</kanji>, the only step that's left is to wash the entire thing with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ).
MeaningMnemonic: There’s a <radical>stone</radical>, a <radical>tree</radical>, another <radical>tree</radical>, and a <radical>coat rack</radical>. That stone and two trees will make the perfect <kanji>foundation</kanji>, or <kanji>cornerstone</kanji>, for a house to put that coat rack in! This stone is flat and heavy and both of the trees are flexible and durable.

MeaningHint: Picture the beautiful black and white crane dancing gracefully on the roof. Now that you know what it is, you feel a little bad about calling it a turkey bird.
ReadingHint: The crane keeps saying you're too rude whenever other people come close so they know what kind of person you are. Yikes, you really shouldn't have gotten on this crane's bad side.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>crane</kanji> leans down toward you from its roof perch and squawks, "<reading>Too ru</reading>de (つる)!" You're shocked! You didn't know the crane could speak English, but even worse, it knew you called it a turkey bird.
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>roof</radical> there's some type of <radical>turkey</radical> <radical>bird</radical>, but thinner and longer. You look closer and realize it's a <kanji>crane</kanji>.

MeaningHint: All treasures need to be taken care of, no matter what they are. Prune away anything that isn't necessary.
ReadingHint: You want your car to be small and sleek, not a giant truck, so you have to carefully prune it when it grows too much.
ReadingMnemonic: Currently, you want to <kanji>prune</kanji> your greatest treasure: your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). Your car is made of wood, and it keeps trying to grow new buds and branches, so you have to prune them off.
MeaningMnemonic: Take your greatest <radical>treasure</radical>, hold a <radical>knife</radical> nice and close to it, and <kanji>prune</kanji> away all the weak and dead bits.

MeaningHint: The reason hill knives are so sturdy is because they are shaped like hills. Very wide, very thick, not so long. So, they don't break and are more sturdy.
ReadingHint: Imagine Grandpa ごういち rolling the knife back and forth over various things to cut them. "I love my sturdy hill knife," he says.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>sturdy</kanji> (but strangely shaped) knife is used by Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>hill</radical> <radical>knife</radical> is the most <kanji>sturdy</kanji> knife you can find.

MeaningHint: Imagine what makes this turkey’s mouth so unique. This beak is solely his. It belongs to no other. This turkey is the only turkey with a mouth like his.
ReadingHint: Imagine hanging a U-ey on the highway. Hear your tires screech as you turn around and get off where you’re supposed to.
ReadingMnemonic: You traveled a long way to see this turkey and the mouth that is <kanji>solely</kanji> his but you missed your exit on the highway and you have to hang a <reading>U-ey</reading> (ゆい). You know what hang a U-ey means, right? It’s making a U-turn. Well, you have to hang a U-ey on the highway so you can get off at the right spot to see this turkey and the mouth that is solely his.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>mouth</radical> of that <radical>turkey</radical> is <kanji>solely</kanji> his. It’s very unique, so that mouth could belong to no other turkey but him. That mouth is solely his.

MeaningHint: This kanji also just... really looks like a podium right? With a guy wearing a graduation cap, pointing toward you, in the crowd.
ReadingHint: I guess that makes sense. This podium is by the dawn for the dawn.
ReadingMnemonic: You listen to what the <kanji>podium</kanji> has to say for so long you finally can't help but ask, "Who are you?" "I'm the <reading>dawn</reading> (だん), of course!" And this is my podium where I spread the good news about the dawn!
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>dirt</radical> there is a <radical>lid</radical> trying to cover the number of <radical>times</radical> the <radical>dawn</radical> has risen here. You walk over and pull the lid off and the whole thing transforms into a <kanji>podium</kanji>. A podium for the dawn to tell you how many times it's risen over this dirt over and over again. It just keeps going and you realize the lid was here because this dang podium can't seem to keep a lid on it! (Get it? Figure of speech!)

MeaningHint: Imagine handing the nun your country’s flag. And also a spoon that is very valuable to you. Imagine getting down on one knee as you do so.
ReadingHint: Feel your knee digging into the cold floor as you present your offerings to the nun. You feel slightly sad as she takes your precious belongings from you.
ReadingMnemonic: You’re getting down on one <reading>knee</reading> (に) as you present the flag and the spoon to the <kanji>nun</kanji>. The nun accepts your offer and takes your flag and spoon from you. Your soul is saved!
MeaningMnemonic: You give a <radical>flag</radical> and a <radical>spoon</radical> to a <kanji>nun</kanji> as an offering. You give her this flag and this spoon because they are very precious to you and you want to show the nun how serious you are about repenting for your evil deeds.

MeaningHint: Once you have an approximation on the age, you'll be able to come up with an approximation on how much it's worth too.
ReadingHint: The guy is trying to look at your approximation now, while calling YOU "guy" over and over, and you just want him to buy it and leave you alone!
ReadingMnemonic: As you're recording your <kanji>approximation</kanji> down,  somebody comes up and shouts, “Hey <reading>guy</reading> (がい)! I’ll give you $10,000 for that tree there, guy!” This guy must be crazy. That’s much more than your approximation of the value of this thing. But you sell it to the guy anyway.
MeaningMnemonic: Bite a <radical>tree</radical>'s <radical>root</radical> with your <radical>fang</radical> to get an <kanji>approximation</kanji> on its age. It won't be exact, this isn't scientific or anything, but it will be pretty close.

MeaningHint: Feel the water all around you as you become immersed in this tsunami. See Wolverine trying to swim his way to the top as you watch the stool floating about in the water.
ReadingHint: Hear the stool crack against your shin and feel the pain erupt through your leg. You try to scream but you’re under water so you just get a big mouthful of tsunami.
ReadingMnemonic: Still <kanji>immersed</kanji>, the stool floats over and hits you in the <reading>shin</reading> (しん). The tsunami gave the stool a good push and while it was fully immersed, it ran into your shin. It really hurts.
MeaningMnemonic: In the event of a <radical>tsunami</radical>, <radical>Wolverine</radical>, your <radical>forehead</radical>, and a <radical>stool</radical> will all be <kanji>immersed</kanji> in it. You will become immersed in the tsunami after it hits because tsunamis are gigantic and bring a lot of water. Now you and your forehead are immersed in the tsunami along with Wolverine and a stool.

MeaningHint: You go to see if you can find any remainder of the fire + tsunami battle. You can, but it's so faint.
ReadingHint: Look at your skin. It has such a faint tint.
ReadingMnemonic: Just like the tsunami and fire canceling each other out, leaving a <kanji>faint</kanji> remainder of what was, your <reading>tan</reading> (たん) is extremely faint as well.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> and a couple <radical>fire</radical>s run into each other. They cancel each other out completely, and the remainder left behind is a <kanji>faint</kanji> remnant of what was once there.

MeaningHint: There's someone in your face yelling about how they want to boil! You yell back, "Okay, if you want to boil, just boil!"
ReadingHint: Once your knee is done boiling, the person kneels down in front of you and presents it on a platter, mocking you, since you can't kneel anymore. You should pay more attention to what's going on from now on or you could find yourself in... HOT WATER. Ohohoho.
ReadingMnemonic: Welp, the pot is at a rolling <kanji>boil</kanji> and your <reading>knee</reading> (に) is in there. The person wasn't saying they wanted to boil, they said they wanted to boil someone. And that meant you. Well, your knee anyway.
MeaningMnemonic: If <radical>someone</radical> wants to <radical>boil</radical>, you let them <kanji>boil</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to cover up a black hole so that the helicopter doesn't capsize. It's one or the other, dude. Cover or capsize. You decide.
ReadingHint: Man, f**k you you, black hole. F**k you you. Making that helicopter capsize and then refusing to be covered?
ReadingMnemonic: You're doing your best to <kanji>cover</kanji> the black hole to stop the helicopter from <kanji>capsiz</kanji>ing but it's not working! “<reading>F**k</reading> you (ふく) black hole!" you shout. You just want to cover it up! You just want to do the right thing!
MeaningMnemonic:
If a <radical>helicopter</radical> <radical>loiter</radical>s near a <radical>black hole</radical> then it's going to <kanji>capsize</kanji>. I mean, yeah, it's not a boat, but just look, that's what's happening! That helicopter is going down!

If you want to save it you better <kanji>cover</kanji> up the black hole! The only way to save it from capsizing is to cover it right up!

MeaningHint: Trees may look all sweet and innocent, like they aren't listening to every word you say, but it's a front! They're conspiring against you!! They'll blackmail you and take everything!!!
ReadingHint:
Watch as the conspiratorial tree and all it's gossiping friends are bowled over by your bowling ball. Phew. You're safe from the trees.

For now.
ReadingMnemonic: Now you're getting it! That tree is trying to <kanji>conspire</kanji> against you and take you down. Take your <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and bowl it and all its friends down!
MeaningMnemonic: Watch what you <radical>say</radical> to what appears to be a <radical>sweet</radical> <radical>tree</radical>, because that tree will 100% <kanji>conspire</kanji> against you.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself trading cans for pottery with some kind of rope-and-pulley system you've rigged up between your building and the prison.
ReadingHint: A particularly good option would be the とうきょう Metropolitan Art Museum. That would be a great place to hold a pottery exhibit.
ReadingMnemonic: Where do you display your prison <kanji>pottery</kanji>? In <reading>とう</reading>きょう, of course. とうきょう's full of fancy art museums that are more than happy to show off your quirky pottery collection.
MeaningMnemonic: From a <radical>building</radical> next to a <radical>prison</radical>, you trade <radical>can</radical>s to the inmates for pottery. You collect <kanji>pottery</kanji> and other prisoner artwork, and they need metal cans to make shanks and stuff, so it's a win-win arrangement.

MeaningHint: Picture a single building, isolated from the rest of the city and those mean, bully buildings that live there.
ReadingHint: Picture the sun rising behind an isolated building. A cock lets out a cry from the roof. It's a sad and lonely scene.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>isolate</kanji>d building only has one companion, and it's the <reading>cock</reading> (かく) that lives inside of it.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>building</radical> that has a <radical>bully</radical> will <kanji>isolate</kanji> itself. Buildings have feelings like people, you know.

MeaningHint: You can't just let people do whatever they want. If it isn't correct to loiter here, then it IS correct to subjugate them, and force them to do what you want. You control them now. And they're gonna get it!
ReadingHint: As you swing your saber around, subjugating the loiterers, and making them do as you say, you remind yourself that this is correct. And if it's correct, you have to use your saber to enforce it.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>subjugate</kanji> these loiterers with your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい)! It's the correct thing to do, after all, and you were given this saber all those levels ago because you did the correct thing.
MeaningMnemonic: If someone <radical>loiter</radical>s it's <radical>correct</radical> to <kanji>subjugate</kanji> them.

MeaningHint: Picture Her Highness, high up in her building. Now compare her home and life to yours, you dirt person.
ReadingHint: Picture His and Her Highness coming out of their huge building and walking by you in your hay pile. "My goodness, Highness, have you ever seen such a dirty, fluffy creature?" "My no, dear, let's move quickly to remove it from our sight!"
ReadingMnemonic: I bet His <kanji>Highness</kanji> has never had to sleep in <reading>hay</reading> (へい) either, like you do every night. Hay is what you use as a pillow, mattress, blanket. Basically hay and dirt are all you have. Unlike His Highness.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Compare</radical> a <radical>building</radical> to some <radical>dirt</radical> and you'll see the difference between you and His and Her <kanji>Highness</kanji>. You live outside and sleep on the dirt. But His and Her Highness both live in a huge building.

MeaningHint: Imagine meeting the leader of Vulgar Valley. Imagine all the vulgar things he says to prove he is worthy of being the leader of Vulgar Valley. He is so very, very vulgar.
ReadingHint: Hear the motorcycles rev and purr as they carouse through the Vulgar Valley. Hear the leader screaming vulgar things as he and his Bosozoku bike gang ride all around.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>vulgar</kanji> leader leads his Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang through Vulgar Valley, patrolling the area and spreading vulgarity as he goes.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of this <radical>valley</radical> is very <kanji>vulgar</kanji>. The name of the valley is Vulgar Valley, so it makes sense for its leader to be very vulgar.

MeaningHint: Imagine putting three stools on top of a tree and watching it transform into a mulberry. The science of nature!
ReadingHint: Imagine the huge quantity of mulberries growing from your stool tree. Imagine picking them all. What an enormous quantity of mulberries you now have.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>mulberry</kanji> tree then produces a <reading>qua</reading>ntity (くわ) of mulberries. Yum.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>stool</radical>, another <radical>stool</radical>, and yet another <radical>stool</radical> and stack them on top of a <radical>tree</radical> to make a <kanji>mulberry</kanji> bush. This is how mulberry bushes are made. You take three stools and you put them atop a tree.

MeaningHint: Hear the tsunami crash into the gate as you see the water slosh around and make sure that all of the king’s plants get good and watered.
ReadingHint: Again, watch and hear the tsunami crashing into the gate and getting the plants watered. This time, remember that the month is June and it is very hot and sticky outside. You’re sweating and very hot.
ReadingMnemonic: These plants are getting <kanji>watered</kanji> in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん), the month they need watering the most. June is a hot month, so it’s good that this tsunami came and got the king’s plants watered when it did.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> at the <radical>gate</radical> of the <radical>king</radical> makes sure all of his plants get <kanji>watered</kanji>. The gate prevents the tsunami from damaging anything inside the king’s home, and the plants around the gate get watered by the water of the tsunami.

MeaningHint: Imagine giving this gorgeous white pearl to the king after he slides down his slide and boards the jet. See his king face light up as he sees the pretty pearl.
ReadingHint: Oh. I guess the king didn't need your pearl, since he has a pearl chute to get them off his jet before takeoff. You feel silly. And you kind of want to jump into the pearl chute yourself.
ReadingMnemonic: The king takes the <kanji>pearl</kanji> and tosses it into a <reading>chu</reading>te (しゅ) next to the slide he took to get into the jet. You watch as the pearl goes down, down, down until it lands into a huge vat of pearls.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> takes his <radical>slide</radical> into his private <radical>jet</radical> and is given a <kanji>pearl</kanji> as a gift, by you! He's letting you ride in his jet today, and you were told the king should be presented with a pearl as soon as he boards.

MeaningHint: As you watch the lid decline, keep in mind that the sun radical has its middle line extending outside the box on both sides. This is because the sun is going supernova. Pew!
ReadingHint: Imagine swinging on these vines as you hear the sun explode and go supernova behind you. Swing faster! Swing faster!
ReadingMnemonic: As the lid begins to <kanji>decline</kanji> in the sky, you realize what’s going to happen so you attempt to <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい) to safety. Swing on some vines in the direction opposite of the sun. You know that once that lid finishes its decline and collides with the sun, there’s gonna be trouble.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>lid</radical>, and aiming it at the <radical>sun</radical> you <radical>kick</radical> it into the air and eventually it begins to <kanji>decline</kanji> and crashes into the sun, causing it to supernova. Imagine kicking this lid so hard that it flies into space. See it fly up to the peak of its arch, then watch as it begins the decline until it finally collides with the sun.

MeaningHint: "Look at me! You can be a big commander someday too!" he shouts, encouraging everyone.
ReadingHint: Picture the encouraged Shougun: his eyes are shining with tears, his heart is pounding, he's been encouraged to be better!
ReadingMnemonic: He <kanji>encourage</kanji>s the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) too! You've never seen the Shougun express any emotion other than intense indifference or intense dislike.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>commander</radical> who's <radical>big</radical> will use his size to <kanji>encourage</kanji> his troops.

MeaningHint: Imagine you are one of the few without power. Feel the inferiority crushing down on you. It feels terrible and there is nothing you can do about it.
ReadingHint: Let’s go, let’s go! Time’s a wastin’! I’m tired of this inferiority and want to gain some power!
ReadingMnemonic: Since you suffer from such extreme feelings of <kanji>inferiority</kanji>, when someone offers you and your friends the chance to gain power, you yell “<reading>Let’s</reading> (れつ) go!” without a second thought. “Let’s go, guys! Come on! Power awaits us!”
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>few</radical> members of society without <radical>power</radical> suffer from severe <kanji>inferiority</kanji> complexes. Most people in this world have lots of power, so the few that do not feel quite inferior. It is this feeling of inferiority that fuels their inferiority complexes.

MeaningHint: Look at this crab trap and see it visibly glowing with intuition. Its powers of intuition are so strong that you can actually see it shimmering with intuition as it expertly traps all those crabs.
ReadingHint: Genghis Khan probably has the strongest intuition of anyone in history. This crab trap is getting close though.
ReadingMnemonic: This crab trap's <kanji>intuition</kanji> powers were extracted from Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) himself. And as we all know by now, Genghis Khan was the absolute master of intuition, he always knew what his enemies and his allies were about to do.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>crab trap</radical> has the <radical>power</radical> of <kanji>intuition</kanji>. By the power of intuition, this crab trap can trap like, every crab. Through intuition, it knows where the crabs will go and how they will react, so it is much easier to trap them.

MeaningHint: Even if you're royalty, you can't expect to have a woman all to yourself without popping the question. Put a ring on it and make that lady your royal consort!
ReadingHint: Imagine standing next to a royal consort. She and her fancy, voluminous 17th-century dress are radiating so much heat that even you're starting to sweat.
ReadingMnemonic: You can tell someone's a <kanji>royal consort</kanji> by the <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ) she radiates. Royal consorts are all smoking hot… literally, because they have to wear bulky, elaborate dresses that were designed several centuries ago. Those things trap heat like nobody's business.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>woman</radical> one has to <radical>oneself</radical> is one's <kanji>royal consort</kanji>… You know, assuming one is a member of the royalty. This is a woman who's tied the knot with some royal person and been made into a <kanji>princess</kanji> or <kanji>queen</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine covering a turkey in ice and every time you check it, it's only semi frozen. It doesn't matter what you do, it just refuses to freeze all the way through.
ReadingHint: The lesson is to never try to freeze something in June. If you do it will only semi freeze.
ReadingMnemonic: Finally you realize it will only <kanji>semi</kanji> freeze because it's <reading>June</reading> (じゅん)! It's too hot out for your turkey to freeze all the way. The ice keeps melting off the top and semi thawing it!
MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>turkey</radical> on <radical>ice</radical> and it will only get <kanji>semi</kanji> frozen. You add more ice, and it stays the same. Only some of it will freeze. What the heck!

MeaningHint: If that signpost wasn't there how would you know you reached the summit? Like, if you were on top of Everest and there was a bad storm... you'd just topple off the other side. Those summit signposts are important!
ReadingHint: Picture your beautiful summit home. You know, George Washington had one of these too. So. That's cool, I guess.
ReadingMnemonic: At the <kanji>summit</kanji>, you decide to build your new <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). It's been destroyed so many times, and you're sick of it. Maybe if you build you home at the summit of this mountain it will be safer.
MeaningMnemonic: At the top of the <radical>mountain</radical> is a <radical>signpost</radical> to tell you you've reached the <kanji>summit</kanji>. Literally every mountain that humans have reached the top of has one of these summit signs.

MeaningHint: And that adept person is you! You're an expert when it comes to constructing underground prisons, so you've been hired for this job. Good luck!
ReadingHint:
With a dignified nod, Emperor こういち entrusts you with the grand project of constructing an underground prison, confident that the adept builder will make prisoners feel right at home in their new subterranean digs.

Hint: When you see 工 in a kanji it will often take the こう reading.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Adept</kanji> at constructing underground prisons, you've caught the eye of Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち, the royal underground prison enthusiast.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>construction</radical> of an under<radical>ground</radical> <radical>prison</radical> will be done by someone who is <kanji>adept</kanji> at it.

MeaningHint: Not like, wicked cool, but like wicked evil. I see you, New Englanders.
ReadingHint: Imagine being chased around the outside of this wicked fanged building as the wicked fanged jaguar tries to eat you. Ahhh!
ReadingMnemonic: You peek inside the <kanji>wicked</kanji> building and a wicked <reading>ja</reading>guar (じゃ) suddenly pounces at you! You let out a sigh of relief when it's stopped by the window, but then... the window opens?! What a wicked building! It's letting the jaguar get you!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>fang</radical> on a <radical>building</radical> shows that it's <kanji>wicked</kanji>. Something wicked probably lives in the building too.

MeaningHint: A fat horse. So burdensome. Not to mention the burden on the horse on top of the fatness.
ReadingHint: Go visit your dad the burdensome horse because you have to visit your dad. It's weird because you're a human and he's a horse.
ReadingMnemonic: But you have to be nice to the <kanji>burdensome</kanji> horse because this horse is your <reading>da</reading>d (だ).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>horse</radical> that is <radical>fat</radical> is a <kanji>burdensome</kanji> animal.

MeaningHint: Picture him sitting above you in the judge's chair in court. He's about to make his judgement.
ReadingHint: "I hereby sentence you to..." *ploop* "Ooh, this is really good. I hereby sentence..." *ploop* "Wow, are these tater tots? Court dismissed."
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>court</kanji> of the Yoga King is against you. You have to turn this around, so you start pushing <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots into his tater-tot-hole shaped mouth.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Yoga</radical> <radical>King</radical> presides over all <kanji>courts</kanji>: law, imperial, and so forth. In this case, you've met him in the court of law, where he is judge, jury, and executioner.

MeaningHint: The bamboo tsunami hits the ground and drops of bamboo turn into sheets of paper. The sheets of paper combine to become record books that record what happened here, on this day.
ReadingHint: Bamboo can also become <reading>bo</reading> staffs too, if they don't turn into record books.
ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>record book</kanji> is a <reading>bo</reading>ok (ぼ), when it comes down to it.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>tsunami</radical> uses its <radical>drop</radical>s to carry out its <radical>specialty</radical>, which is turning itself into <kanji>record book</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: Your writing is so clear and concise that just about everyone does read it. Well, everyone with hair that is.
ReadingHint: Picture the Shougun angrily yelling at you about his hair disaster! The only good thing he can say about your book is that it gave clear directions.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>clear</kanji> writing got the attention of the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) and now he's at your door once again. Although, this time he's angrier than usual. His hair also looks awful. He took your advice after reading the book and cut his entire ponytail off.
MeaningMnemonic: You want to write a <radical>chapter</radical> about <radical>hair</radical> in your new book so you have to make sure it's really <kanji>clear</kanji>. Clear enough so that ANYbody can read it.

MeaningHint: Imagine reading this issue of your favorite stretchy pirate boy comic. Lumpy uses his long arms as a net, traps the sun and ties it to the stool right as the tsunami hits. What kind of crazy trouble is he getting into this time?!
ReadingHint: You've known about Japanese comics or manga for a long time, and now you can read them too. Look how far you've come. Wow, what a journey. I'm just so proud of you, kiddo.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Comic</kanji>s are <reading>man</reading>ga (まん) in Japan, whether they're comic or serious.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> crashes into the <radical>sun</radical>, which is then caught in a <radical>net</radical> and tied to a <radical>stool</radical>! Wait a second... this doesn't sound realistic, it sounds really <kanji>comic</kanji>! That's because it's from that comic book about a pirate you're always reading!

MeaningHint: Think about all the stupid things you said on the streets today. Do you want those exact phrases, mistakes, and mouth sounds following you around forever? Revise it before you write it down!
ReadingHint:
"Tater tots, tater tots, tater tots, man.
Bake me a tater tot as fast as you banana."

Well that's bad. Better revise this so it rhymes.

"Tater tots, tater tots, tater tots, man.
Bake me a tater tot as fast as you want."

Nope, still bad. Revise!

"Tater tots, tater tots, tater tots, man.
Bake me a tater tot as fast as you clam."

Yes. It's gold.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to <kanji>revise</kanji> the song you wrote about <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots at lunch today. But it needs a lot of revising.
MeaningMnemonic: What you <radical>say</radical> on the <radical>street</radical> needs to be <kanji>revise</kanji>d. Your words need to be polished, you can't publish improv. Revise your street word inspiration before you share it again.

MeaningHint: This person wants to consult you about something important, so whatever you say next from your mouth better be really smart-sounding and helpful. Make this person glad they consulted you.
ReadingHint: They were on the fence about buying one, which is why they consulted with you. You think sheep are the ultimate safe-haven asset, so you told them not to be sheepish and advised them to invest in one.
ReadingMnemonic: After they <kanji>consult</kanji> with you, the person decides to buy a <reading>shee</reading>p (し).
MeaningMnemonic: Watch what you <radical>say</radical> <radical>next</radical> from your <radical>mouth</radical> when someone comes to <kanji>consult</kanji> with you.

MeaningHint: This inscription refers to a signature or something similar. This is an artisan's signature in the form of an inscription.
ReadingHint: If you need to review 名 go ahead and do that. It will help you to remember this kanji.
ReadingMnemonic: The inscription is a name written on something, right? Remember the kanji for "name"? It is 名, and it shares the reading with this kanji as well, making both names <reading>めい</reading>.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>gold</radical> <radical>name</radical> on this piece of wood is the <kanji>inscription</kanji> that shows the artist's signature.

MeaningHint: Imagine an embankment made of dirt, in the correct place, blocking the sun from your eyes.
ReadingHint: Picture an embankment made of dirt and tater tots. Not like, tater tots just thrown in the embankment, you probably see those all the time. But dirt AND tater tots in the embankment itself.
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, building a correct <kanji>embankment</kanji> doesn't just take dirt, but <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots too. You pile up the dirt and the tater tots so the embankment can be nice and high.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>correct</radical> way to block the <radical>sun</radical> with <radical>dirt</radical> is with an <kanji>embankment</kanji>. Build a huge, sloping embankment correctly and the sun won't be able to bother you ever again!

MeaningHint: Picture your helicopter as it drifts out to sea with the tsunami.
ReadingHint: "Heyoooo stop drifting awaaaay!!"
ReadingMnemonic: As it <kanji>drift</kanji>s away you shout, "<reading>Heyooo</reading>! (ひょう)." You know the helicopter probably can't hear you (or understand you) but maybe it will turn on and stop drifting away if you shout it enough.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> hits your <radical>helicopter</radical> while you are <radical>jackhammer</radical>ing, it will <kanji>drift</kanji> out to sea. What were you doing with a jackhammer at a time like this? Now your helicopter is drifting away!

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself doing flips with this feather on your head. Flip flip flip.
ReadingHint: Or, maybe you get so good that you write a 本 (ほん) about doing a flip. Wow, you're good!
ReadingMnemonic: You're pretty good at your <kanji>flip</kanji> technique, but you're not that good. You really need to practice and <reading>hone</reading> (ほん) your skills and get better.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>number</radical>ed <radical>feather</radical>. Pick your favorite number and put it on the feather. Then, put the numbered feather on your head. When you put it on, you're able to <kanji>flip</kanji> and <kanji>flip</kanji> over and over.

MeaningHint: Imagine the cars spinning out in the rut. Spin spin spin. The rut gets bigger. Nine is too many cars.
ReadingHint: Imagine turning the thrusters key, then feeling the car lift off the ground. You'll finally get out of this <kanji>rut</kanji>!
ReadingMnemonic: To get out of the rut you have to put in a second <reading>key</reading> (き) that turns on the thrusters. You put in the key and press the "take off" button.
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>nine</radical> <radical>car</radical>s all driving in the same place. With so many cars driving in the same place, they create a <kanji>rut</kanji>.

MeaningHint: She was buried in the cliff down to her mouth so nobody would find her! You pull her out of the ground and soon what was just a mouth becomes a whole empress!
ReadingHint: It's only natural the empress would want to see her emperor. And right now, the emperor of these lands is Emperor こういち.
ReadingMnemonic: When you finish unburying the <kanji>empress</kanji>, the first thing she says to you is: "Please, you must hurry and take me to Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち."
MeaningMnemonic: You walk out on a <radical>cliff</radical>, where you find in the <radical>ground</radical> a <radical>mouth</radical> poking out. It speaks: "It is me, the <kanji>empress</kanji> of these lands!"

MeaningHint: He is a really big turkey too. Like, 12 feet tall, or something. Don't want to stir up a turkey that's that big.
ReadingHint: Is it laughing? Oh no, this is bad, it's enjoying being stirred up, now we'll never stop it.
ReadingMnemonic: While the turkey is <kanji>stirred up</kanji>, it also looks like it's having a lot of <reading>fun</reading> (ふん).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>big</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> in the <radical>rice paddy</radical> is getting all <kanji>stirred up</kanji>. He's kicking up all the rice. Who stirred him up?

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine going to a restaurant and seeing a menu of all different types and styles of tater tots and nothing else. Even the drinks are tater tot based. Mmm mm.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>restaurant</kanji> only serves <reading>tater</reading> (てい) tots. Really, would you ever want to go to a restaurant that didn't have tater tots? No.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>restaurant</radical> radical and the <kanji>restaurant</kanji> kanji are the same! The meaning of this kanji also extends other resting places, like an <kanji>inn</kanji> or a <kanji>pavilion</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine this majestic cat pirate leader of leaders. Feel in your heart that you look up to this cat pirate, truly.
ReadingHint: Of course, the gyoza is filled with fish, because we're talking about cat pirates, here.
ReadingMnemonic: Your job is to bring this leader of cat pirates (whom you <kanji>look up to</kanji>) its favorite food: <reading>gyo</reading>za (ぎょう).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>cat pirate</radical> is the greatest cat pirate of them all. It leads all the cat pirate leaders, even! This is truly a cat pirate <kanji>to look up to</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine all the leaders standing around, then the chief person standing there all in white.
ReadingHint: You'll learn more about which reading to use when the vocabulary roll around. In the meantime, try to remember both of them the best you can!
ReadingMnemonic: The reading is the same as 白, which is included in this kanji. The other reading is お, and it's like the honorific 御, in a way. You'd want to be polite to someone who is the chief ______, right? That's why it could be お, too.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> all in <radical>white</radical> is the <kanji>chief</kanji>. All the other leaders are wearing some other color, but that's how you tell the chief apart from the other leaders.

MeaningHint: Imagine someone accidentally spilling ooze on a rat, then watch the rat turn into a sentient rat martial artist.
ReadingHint: However you look at it, try to make this accident be a good accident.
ReadingMnemonic: But really, if you do something <kanji>accidentally</kanji>, it's probably a <reading>goo</reading>d (ぐう) thing in a way. You learn a bit about how you can improve yourself so you don't do it again, or in Splinter's case, it's just a good mistake!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical>, Master <radical>Splinter</radical> (the rat from Ninja Turtles) isn't even supposed to exist. He exists because someone <kanji>accidentally</kanji> poured ooze on him.

MeaningHint: Can you picture this big dirt tomb, with flowers and shellfish decorating it and a cross right on top?
ReadingHint:
See this tomb, and you'll remember all the fun you had. It's decorated to make sure you feel that fun.

Fun fact: when you see 賁 in a kanji, the reading is probably going to be ふん.
ReadingMnemonic: This is a <kanji>tomb</kanji>, but it feels really <reading>fun</reading> (ふん). Decorated with flowers and shellfish shells, it’s not a sad place at all. It's a fun place to remember all the fun times with people you loved.
MeaningMnemonic: Sticking out of the <radical>dirt</radical>, there's a <radical>cross</radical> adorned with <radical>flowers</radical> and <radical>shellfish</radical> shells. This is a <kanji>tomb</kanji>. The flowers and shellfish were put here as decorations and to honor the dead that currently lie under the dirt. And the cross marks the tomb, obviously.

MeaningHint: Picture samurai standing in a line. One, two, three... woah. You look up. This guy has icicles growing off him and he's the size of a bus. Now that's a robust samurai.
ReadingHint: Physical robustness is just a reflection of the strength of your soul!
ReadingMnemonic: You may think it's just his <kanji>robust</kanji> body, but it's actually the samurai's robust <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) that keeps him fighting through the cold and the icicles.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> who can fight with <radical>icicle</radical>s on him is more <kanji>robust</kanji> than regular samurai. Can you imagine how robust you'd have to be to fight in weather cold enough to form icicles on your body?

MeaningHint: Imagine pressing your fingers into the bed, grasping tightly and ripping the sheets free. Now bundle them up, tight as you can!
ReadingHint: Hah! Take that naysayers! Who's bundling their own sheets now? Me, that's who! Hah!
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>bundle</kanji> up your sheets and <kanji>grasp</kanji> them tightly, you triumphantly exclaim, "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!" to all your imaginary naysayers. In your mind, everyone doubted that you could actually bundle up your own sheets and launder them yourself.
MeaningMnemonic: Why would you put your <radical>fingers</radical> on a <radical>bed</radical>? To <kanji>bundle</kanji> up the sheets — maybe because you need to do laundry or something. <kanji>Grasp</kanji> that bundle of sheets tightly, and be careful not to let it unfurl as you carry it on over to the washing machine.

MeaningHint: Imagine loading a bunch of weapons into a boat and sailing off to transport them somewhere else.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself as a cool smuggler, transporting stolen... I mean... totally legitimate weapons from one place to another. Just like Han Solo!
ReadingMnemonic: Hey! You're <kanji>transport</kanji>ing weapons just like <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! I mean, it was a spaceship, not technically a boat, but he definitely used the Millennium Falcon as a transport.
MeaningMnemonic: With your <radical>fingers</radical> you put some <radical>weapon</radical>s in a <radical>boat</radical> for <kanji>transport</kanji>. This is a transport boat and its your job to get these weapons from one place to another.

MeaningHint: Also, imagine a tsunami hitting a cave, becoming the same as the cave (since a cave will shape it).
ReadingHint: Also note that the 同 part of this kanji gives away the reading as well. 同 and 洞 have the same readings!
ReadingMnemonic: Best way to remember the reading for this kanji is to think about how the shape of a cave is the shape of a <reading>dou</reading>ghnut (どう).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is the <radical>same</radical> as a <kanji>cave</kanji>. Because, if a wave is big enough, it will curl over on itself, creating a sort of cave within the wave. That's why it's the same as a cave!

MeaningHint: Picture the tsunami breaking over the cliff on the horizon. It would be beautiful if it wasn't so frightening.
ReadingHint: You wonder who that guy was. You wonder why he was there. You wonder if he's still alive out there, past the horizon.
ReadingMnemonic: You squint into the <kanji>horizon</kanji> and see something else. There's some <reading>guy</reading> (がい) up there. And he's... he's being swept away by the tsunami. The guy disappears into the horizon. The guy is gone.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>tsunami</radical> coming up over a <radical>cliff</radical> made of <radical>dirt</radical>. You can see this happening very far away because it's right on the <kanji>horizon</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine if countries decided to make sick weapons that would spread epidemics like this all over part of the world. Let's hope they don't...
ReadingHint: Picture a single 駅 on a map, bright red. Now watch the red spread to all of the other 駅 on the map, dyeing them red and spreading the epidemic all over your country.
ReadingMnemonic: These <kanji>epidemic</kanji>s spread by <reading>駅</reading> (えき). Each train takes the sickness and shoots off in a different direction, spreading it further.
MeaningMnemonic: Turn a <radical>sick</radical>ness into a <radical>weapon</radical> and it becomes an <kanji>epidemic</kanji>. This sickness spreads to everyone and it's all your fault because you put it in the water supply.

MeaningHint: Imagine your super comfy, state-of-the-art stool. You take it everywhere with you, and you're always sitting on it. Again, and again, and <kanji>again</kanji>. You love that stool.
ReadingHint: Imagine your friends getting more and more annoyed with your constant stool-sitting habits! They really don't get it. What is the matter with you?! Why can't you just stand like everyone else? Don't you know sitting is the "new smoking"!?
ReadingMnemonic: Your friends are starting to worry about you, because you're always sitting on that stool. "What is the <reading>matter</reading> (また) with you?" they scream! You might want to imagine that all your friends have Jamaican or British accents to make this one fit a bit better!
MeaningMnemonic: You're sitting on your <radical>stool</radical> <kanji>again</kanji>!!?

MeaningHint: I mean, they're the leader of Korea already, right? So they have to have a decent chunk of greatness in them already to get that far.
ReadingHint: Picture the greatness of the scene before you: the leader of Korea, a great eagle overhead, it's so great!!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>greatness</kanji> radiates off of the leader of Korea, so much so that you can hardly look. Just when you're about to close your eyes to shield them from the greatness, a great <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) soars over them, letting out a cry.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of <radical>Korea</radical> is destined for <kanji>greatness</kanji>.

MeaningHint: There is a giant geoduck at the summit of the mountain, and the street will lead you right there. Imagine the grueling climb up the mountain to the summit. Feel it in your legs, and keep looking at that tall, tall geoduck. Now imagine kneeling those same aching legs humbly to the street below the geoduck.
ReadingHint: Anytime you use the street, you're in Mrs. Chou's territory. After detecting you were using her street to reach the geoduck, she started coming after you. Remember, wherever there's a street (radical), Mrs. Chou is not far behind.
ReadingMnemonic: You are at the top, the <kanji>summit</kanji> of everything! You <kanji>humbly</kanji> survey your surroundings from the top of the geoduck, only to see someone on the street, running toward you. It is Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう), and she looks angry.
MeaningMnemonic: Take the <radical>street</radical> all the way to that <radical>geoduck</radical>, way up there. When you reach it, you will have made it to the <kanji>summit</kanji>. You <kanji>humbly</kanji> present the road to the geoduck and bow before it.

MeaningHint: Call your mom or dad and ask them to go out to eat with you right now.
ReadingHint: Imagine getting a call from the Shougun himself. He's come to your house before, but he's never asked you to eat with him. This is huge!
ReadingMnemonic: You are <kanji>call</kanji>ed to come <kanji>eat</kanji> by the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). He's been really impressed with you lately and he wants to chat over drinks, he says on the phone.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>call</radical> radical and the <kanji>call</kanji> kanji are the same. This kanji can also mean <kanji>eat</kanji>, because why would you call someone if not to ask them to eat with you?!

MeaningHint: This is one of the perks of having a horned demonic creature for a husband. You can put his horns between your fingers and get a really nice stretch on your finger joints.
ReadingHint: Imagine having a strong, muscular sergeant with horns for a husband — so macho! With a husband like that, you'd be running your fingers through his sexy horns every chance you get. Sir, yes sir!
ReadingMnemonic: Why are your husband's horns what you always love to <kanji>put between</kanji> your fingers? Because he's a <reading>ho</reading>t <reading>ser</reading>geant (はさ)!
MeaningMnemonic: You run your <radical>fingers</radical> through your <radical>husband</radical>'s <radical>horns</radical>. His horns are what you love to <kanji>put between</kanji> your fingers.

MeaningHint: Old trees' roots and leaves grow inefficient, and they can't get enough nutrients. It's only natural for them to wither up a little bit.
ReadingHint: It gave the 子 its shade, its sap, and eventually its body to build a house. What an awful child, and what a generic giving tree.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>wither</kanji>ed tree's best friend is a human <reading>子</reading> (こ). Imagine the child hugging the tree.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>tree</radical> grows <radical>old</radical>, it will <kanji>wither</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine standing in front of an incoming tsunami. Suddenly in the distance you see someone throwing dollar bills into it and it starts to boil. Ahh!
ReadingHint: Feel your poor little foots burn in the boiling water. You really want to know who that jerk was that threw dollars into the tsunami to make it boil!
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>boil</kanji>ing water crashes onto the land and you realize your foot is in it! No, not one foot, both your <reading>foots</reading> (ふつ)! It hurts so much that you can't even remember the word for two foots.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>dollar</radical> the water will instantly start to <kanji>boil</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Imagine how big and heavy this turkey must be. It's so huge so you need the strength of two Wolverines and the magnitude of a whole tsunami to move it around and wash it.
ReadingHint: If you want to wash your turkey, make sure to have your special tack ready. The tsunami will recede pretty fast, so you need to tack your turkey to the ground if you don't want the sea to take it.
ReadingMnemonic: To make sure your turkey won't get carried away by the tsunami while you <kanji>wash</kanji> it, you pin it down with a giant <reading>tack</reading> (たく). If you're washing a giant turkey inside a giant wave, only a giant tack will guarantee it doesn't get washed away.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> is coming, so you ask two <radical>Wolverine</radical>s to help you take your <radical>turkey</radical> out for a <kanji>wash</kanji>. Despite the obvious dangers, a giant wave is the only thing that'll wash a giant turkey, so you have no choice.

MeaningHint: Imagine pouring some syrup next to a fire and watching it dry up. Watch as the sides slowly bubble and harden and lose what makes syrup so good in the first place.
ReadingHint: Go back to imagining a fire drying up some syrup and watch as a tiny little syrup soul cries and is released into the air. Oh no!
ReadingMnemonic: If you <kanji>dry up</kanji> syrup like this, you're removing its <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Have you ever seen dry syrup before? No soul in that. In fact, you probably have no soul if you can dry out syrup like this without feeling bad about it.
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>fire</radical> next to which you left some <radical>syrup</radical>, and it has all <kanji>dried up</kanji>! Put any liquid substance near fire and it will dry up actually, but syrup especially. It goes from being sticky and gooey and yummy to a pile of hard, dry sugar. Not so yummy.

MeaningHint: Picture this horned bottle sitting on the ground. You don't know who left it here or why, but it's here and the weirdest looking bottle you've ever seen.
ReadingHint: Imagine picking up this weird bottle with your bare hands and putting it in a trash bin. You did your good deed for today! Yay!
ReadingMnemonic: Even though you're not the one who left this <kanji>bottle</kanji> here, you decide to do the right thing and throw it in the trash <reading>bin</reading> (びん). It's not right to leave a bottle just sitting on the ground, you know.
MeaningMnemonic: You see <radical>horns</radical> on a <radical>lantern</radical> that can hold <radical>ten thousand</radical> <radical>drops</radical> of water sitting on the <radical>ground</radical>. You get closer and realize it's not a lantern, it's actually a <kanji>bottle</kanji>. The horns are like a weird handle, but it's definitely a bottle filled with water.

MeaningHint: You'd think you'd only need a plow, but you also need water if you're going to plow a field, otherwise what's the point?
ReadingHint: Thousands and thousands of こういちs, set free unto the world to do all our <kanji>plow</kanji>ing work.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Plow</kanji>ing all the fields are <reading>こう</reading>いちs. Not Emperor こういち. These are the こういち clones the government made, and now they're being put to use.
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>plow</radical> and a (water) <radical>well</radical>. These are the two things you need in order to <kanji>plow</kanji>.

MeaningHint: There are a group of people pointing at pictures of the moon coming closer to earth. They are the world's leaders, and although they don't get along normally, this new attack from the moon has united them in agreement: we must stop the moon.
ReadingHint: The world has united against the moon, and they have made Emperor こういち their temporary leader.
ReadingMnemonic: "We have <kanji>agreement</kanji>," the world's leaders all say. "We will work together to stop the moon, and we have <kanji>agreement</kanji> on who we will follow, Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: We must <radical>stop</radical> the <radical>moon</radical>. That, at least, we can come to an <kanji>agreement</kanji> on.

MeaningHint: Imagine staring down regretfully at your fat-covered spoon after eating both the moon and the sun. Not only did you eat two really important celestial bodies, but their high fat content made them way less healthy than you expected.
ReadingHint: If you had known you were going to get a whole sheep's worth of fat, you would have just eaten a big platter of mutton chops instead of dooming the entire solar system by eating the moon and sun.
ReadingMnemonic: Unbeknownst to you, the moon and sun contained as much <kanji>fat</kanji> as an entire adult <reading>shee</reading>p (し)! Why didn't you check the nutrition facts first???
MeaningMnemonic: You ate the <radical>moon</radical> with a <radical>spoon</radical>, and then the <radical>sun</radical> too, but man were they high in <kanji>fat</kanji>! In fact, you wanted to eat them with chopsticks, but they were so greasy with fat that the spoon was your only option.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself opening a tigers mouth and looking all the way down into its stomach and seeing a bunch of skin. Why would you imagine that? Eww.
ReadingHint: Imagine a fool trying to pull skin out of a tiger's stomach. You can imagine them getting their skin added to the pile too if you want.
ReadingMnemonic: The only person who would try to take <kanji>skin</kanji> from a tiger's stomach is a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). You'll probably get your head bitten off looking for skin that way.
MeaningMnemonic: If you look in a <radical>tiger</radical>'s <radical>stomach</radical> you'll find a bunch of <kanji>skin</kanji>. It's really gross but what else did you think you'd find? Everyone knows tigers can't digest the skin of the things they eat.

MeaningHint: Picture a soft car lacking what it needs to drive down the road. It's so soft it just flops down and won't move.
ReadingHint: Picture trying to drive this soft naan car. It probably just sinks into the ground and then gets eaten by wild animals (and me).
ReadingMnemonic: This car is probably so <kanji>soft</kanji> because it's made entirely of <reading>naan</reading> (なん). Naan may be delicious, but it makes a really crappy car. No wonder it was so soft (and yummy).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>car</radical> that's <radical>lack</radical>ing is really <kanji>soft</kanji>. Cars need to be hard so that things like bugs and other cars wont hurt the people inside when you hit them. But if your car is lacking the hardness it needs, you'll have a really soft, useless car.

MeaningHint: Isn't that all a suburb is? Just a mix of a variety of buildings in one area?
ReadingHint: "That suburb life will encourage more people to study kanji," Emperor こういち continues.
ReadingMnemonic: "<kanji>Suburb</kanji>s are what we need!" says Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>mix</radical> a bunch of <radical>building</radical>s together, you have yourself a <kanji>suburb</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine a huge splinter coming out of the corner of the building. It's in a really dangerous spot, but the owner doesn't seem so worried about it.
ReadingHint: Actually, please don't sue me. I'll fix the splinter coming out of the corner of my building.
ReadingMnemonic: That <kanji>corner</kanji> is dangerous, but what are you going to do, <reading>sue me</reading> (すみ)?
MeaningMnemonic: So my <radical>building</radical> has a big <radical>splinter</radical>, right in the <kanji>corner</kanji>. So what?

MeaningHint: Picture yourself counting ships by pointing your finger from one turkey propped up on a stool to the next. You'll be the best ship counter in the world at this rate!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself, a sexy ship counter. Sexily counting ships. You go guy/girl/non binary person! So sexy!
ReadingMnemonic: Once you become the best <kanji>ship counter</kanji> in the world everyone will think you are super <reading>sexy</reading> (せき)! Nothing is sexier than being able to count, and counting ships is the sexiest thing you can count.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>turkey</radical> sitting on a <radical>stool</radical> is something every ship has in its crow's nest. That's why the universal <kanji>ship counter</kanji> is a turkey on a stool. Not a crow.

MeaningHint: Picture your clan, working together, to build a new residence for you on their land.
ReadingHint: Imagine living in a tater tot residence. Whenever you're hungry, you just lean over to the wall and munch.
ReadingMnemonic: Your new <kanji>residence</kanji> is complete! You step closer and the smell of <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots hits your nose. Someone is making you tater tots in your new residence. Wait, no they aren't. Your residence is MADE of tater tots! Thank you, clan!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>clan</radical> is breaking <radical>ground</radical> and <radical>building</radical> your new <kanji>residence</kanji>. You're all working together to make you a new home!

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at a map of your county and the surrounding ones. All of the buildings in the counties next to yours are buddy buildings!
ReadingHint: Imagine taking out a gun and shooting in a random direction. When you find where the bullet landed, you know that's the county line. Though if it gets stuck in a building or a person it doesn't count. You'll have to try again.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>county</kanji> is everything you can reach when shooting a <reading>gun</reading> (ぐん). If the bullet can't reach a building you know, it's a buddy building in another county.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>buddy</radical> <radical>building</radical> is in the next <kanji>county</kanji>. If it was in your county then it wouldn't be a buddy, it would be a neighbor. You're aware of this building, but they aren't that close, so they're just a buddy.

MeaningHint: Try to come with an explanation for picking up a sickle and cutting the head off a shrimp with it.
ReadingHint: Picture Shaq popping into your head as you struggle to come up with an explanation for me. Picture Shaq's face. This is payback for Shaq Fu, you think, as you finalize the rest of the explanation in your mind.
ReadingMnemonic: In your <kanji>explanation</kanji> you blame everything on <reading>Shaq</reading> (しゃく). He told you to do it! It's all Shaq's fault, you explain.
MeaningMnemonic: If you use a <radical>sickle</radical> to kill a <radical>shrimp</radical> you'll need a really good <kanji>explanation</kanji> for yourself. What did that shrimp do to you? Why use a sickle? Well? What's your explanation?

MeaningHint: Picture the moon turning to face a different direction. Feel all the fat in your body start to get extracted up through your pores, creating this slick, greasy sheen all over your skin. Let's hope this phase of the lunar cycle ends quickly.
ReadingHint: Feel how slippery the bowling ball gets as you smear it with fat. Now close your eyes and imagine pins toppling in every direction as your fat-slathered ball careens into them!
ReadingMnemonic: As long as you're covered in <kanji>fat</kanji>, you might as well go <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう). Imagine how smoothly that bowling ball's going to roll after you slather it with some of this fat!
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>moon</radical> faces the right <radical>direction</radical>, the <kanji>fat</kanji> in the bodies of people and animals all gets drawn to the surface and we all look weirdly greasy for a little while. You probably knew that the moon exerts a pull on water, but I bet you didn't know it exerted a pull on fat too!

MeaningHint: Picture a prison for legs. It's like a trap that your legs can never escape, basically. And it's pretty painful, so imagine your mouth opening big and wide and letting out a scream.
ReadingHint: Imagine Genghis Khan doing something to make you scream. What is it? What could Genghis Khan do that's worse than what's already happening to you?
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>scream</kanji> and scream and scream until a door bursts open. It's Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) and he looks mad. Apparently your screaming is bothering him and now he's gonna give you a real reason to scream.
MeaningMnemonic: What would your <radical>mouth</radical> do if you got caught in a <radical>prison</radical> for <radical>legs</radical>? It'd probably get real <radical>big</radical> — in other words, it would <kanji>scream</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the awesome princess, kicking butt and taking names, out on the football field.
ReadingHint: Make sure you know both princess words and you'll know their readings too.
ReadingMnemonic: Remember the word 姫? It means <kanji>princess</kanji> too. If you remember the ひめ from 姫, you'll be able to remember this one too.
MeaningMnemonic: Everyone knows that when a <radical>woman</radical> plays <radical>football</radical> she becomes a <kanji>princess</kanji>. Thats how princesses are made.

MeaningHint: "No, I won't open up until I meet the toe I will marry," it says to you.
ReadingHint: You give the shellfish a tater tot, and it breaks its vow of chastity, opening up for your toe. You look the other way, but you can hear the sounds of passion below.
ReadingMnemonic: "<kanji>Chastity</kanji> is really important to me," the shellfish says. "But, maybe if you give me a <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tot, I'll change my mind."
MeaningMnemonic: You try to put your <radical>toe</radical> inside a <radical>shellfish</radical>, but it just won't open. That's because this shellfish has sworn an oath of <kanji>chastity</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the seedlings growing among the rice plants. Once they bloom they'll be picked, so let's hope they stay small as long as they can.
ReadingHint: Listen to the little seedlings say myou over and over. Too bad the farmers can tell they aren't rice plants.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>seedling</kanji>s try their best to blend in by referring to themselves as <reading>myou</reading> (みょう), you know, me and you combined. That's myou.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> that grow in a <radical>rice paddy</radical> only live to be <kanji>seedling</kanji>s. Once they sprout, they're plucked away to make room for more rice. Poor little seedlings.

MeaningHint: Picture the tsunami spinning in a circle, trying not to hit the mustaches until it spins itself into a giant whirlpool.
ReadingHint: Picture my car getting sucked into the whirlpool. Picture me crying as my car disappears into it forever.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>whirlpool</kanji> is huge, and it starts pulling things into itself. Oh no, is that my <reading>ca</reading>r (か)? Hey, wait! Whirlpool give my car back!
MeaningMnemonic: If you put two <radical>mustache</radical>s on either side of a <radical>tsunami</radical> it won't know which way to go and spin back and forth until it becomes a <kanji>whirlpool</kanji>. Mustaches are pretty gross, so I don't blame the tsunami for avoiding them.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself saying "mercy" over an over as this horned guy has you on the ground and keeps pooping over your heart.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself saying mercy and seeing Jesus appearing just in time to save the day. The word "mercy" must be his bat signal. Jesus signal? Sure.
ReadingMnemonic: As you shout "<kanji>Mercy</kanji>!" one more time, suddenly <reading>Je</reading>sus (じ) appears out of no where and punches the horned guy into the stratosphere. Jesus snaps his fingers and the poop is gone and you're off the ground. Your heart is safe.
MeaningMnemonic: A person with <radical>horns</radical> has you on the <radical>ground</radical> and lets loose two <radical>poops</radical> over your <radical>heart</radical>. "<kanji>Mercy</kanji>! Mercy!" You cry, but the horned dude doesn't stop until there are two gross poops sitting on your heart. "M-mercy..." is the only thing you can think to say.

MeaningHint: This collar is what you always wear when you do your "hot lumberjack" act. If you were seriously trying to cut down trees, you wouldn't be using a jackhammer, after all.
ReadingHint: Imagine standing there in your collar, jackhammering trees as all your swooning fans scream "Eric! Eriiiic! We love you!" from the audience.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>collar</kanji> has "<reading>Eri</reading>c" (えり) emblazoned on it in cursive letters, because this is the stage name you use when you do your lumberjack act. Eric's not your real name, of course — you just think it sounds hunky and looks good on a collar.
MeaningMnemonic: What <radical>outfit</radical> do you wear when cutting down <radical>tree</radical>s with a <radical>jackhammer</radical>? A <kanji>collar</kanji>. Yup, just a collar… plus some shirt cuffs and slacks, of course. You know, the classic Chippendales look.

MeaningHint: Imagine placing lotuses on top of your scooter and car. They are the right kind of flowers for these vehicles.
ReadingHint: You always put a lotus on top of your rental cars and scooters. It just makes those boring, ugly rental cars and scooters so much prettier!
ReadingMnemonic: You're putting a <kanji>lotus</kanji> on both your scooter and car because they are <reading>ren</reading>tals (れん).
MeaningMnemonic: You put <radical>flowers</radical> on your <radical>car</radical> and your <radical>scooter</radical>. The flowers you put on them are <kanji>lotus</kanji> flowers.

MeaningHint: Imagine this pose, imagine everything becoming clear. Clear your mind. Clear your head.
ReadingHint: Picture the row boat on the clear pond in your mind. Watch the clear water flow over the row boat's oars.
