ReadingMnemonic: Your mind is as <kanji>clear</kanji> as a sparkling clear pond. On this pond you see yourself in a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat. A single row boat on the clear pond that is now your mind.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a special type of meditation where you put a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>mouth</radical> and touch your <radical>forehead</radical> down to your <radical>legs</radical>. If you can do this pose, everything in life will become completely <kanji>clear</kanji>. Everything will clear out of your head too. You should try it.

MeaningHint: Close your eyes and open your ears. Feel all of the wise things fill you.
ReadingHint: Picture your wise soul, living inside your body. Somehow it just seems to know what the public really wants. It's like your soul is connected to their souls, granting you this wisdom.
ReadingMnemonic: All of these <kanji>wise</kanji> things are stored in your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう), not your brain. That's where all of these kinds of intuitive feelings live.
MeaningMnemonic: If you have an <radical>ear</radical> for the <radical>public</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical>, you must be very <kanji>wise</kanji>. You can understand everything the people want, and you know how to give it to them because you listen to their hearts so well.

MeaningHint: Picture the tsunami coming towards your beautiful wedding as you stand by the bay. It's not safe, but it's memorable!
ReadingHint: Imagine throwing buckets of uranium into the bay. It only burns your hands a little. And now they glow green, just like the bay does!
ReadingMnemonic: How did you get a tsunami to show up in the <kanji>bay</kanji> in the first place? By throwing a bunch of <reading>ura</reading>nium (うら) in there, of course! Its highly unstable nature can make any body of water, especially a calm bay, turn violent.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to have a <radical>tsunami</radical> at your <radical>wedding</radical>, you need to make sure you have it at the <kanji>bay</kanji>. Anywhere more inland, and the tsunami won't reach it in time.

MeaningHint: Imagine that you're the pig. How big can you make your forehead dirt mound?
ReadingHint: Picture a pig with a mound of dirt the size of two cars leisurely stomping around on a farm.
ReadingMnemonic: The pig's forehead dirt <kanji>mound</kanji> keeps getting bigger until it's the size of <reading>two cars</reading> (つか)! Pigs are big, but you didn't think one could balance a mound the size of two cars on its forehead and not fall over.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dirt</radical> on the <radical>forehead</radical> of a <radical>pig</radical> makes a small <kanji>mound</kanji>. Picture it, if you will. A pig is rooting through the dirt. It lifts its head and there is a mound of dirt right on its forehead.

MeaningHint: Picture the cave in in your mind. Can you see all the little prisoners and guards being squished as the building tumbles down around them? Oh no!
ReadingHint: Genghis Khan is all about being smart and using better tactics. In this case, why fight a bunch of guards and prisoners when you can just cause a cave in and win immediately? The ol' Khan cave in, a classic war maneuver.
ReadingMnemonic: You keep picturing the <kanji>cave in</kanji> and you see Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) climbing over the rubble. He's the one who took down this prison! And he's coming for your town next!
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>building</radical> was a <radical>former</radical> <radical>prison</radical> until the <kanji>cave in</kanji>. You see, prisons aren't built very carefully, they just try to get them done fast enough to stuff people in there. So while you can tell this was once a building, it definitely isn't a prison anymore, since the whole thing caved in.

MeaningHint: Imagine the window piercing into the shellfish's shell. Then imagine lining up the pieces, putting sushi on them, and counting them all.
ReadingHint: Carefully count out all the sushi on their pierced shell plates and place them in front of Genghis Khan and his army. As you reach Genghis Khan himself, you realize it's just his ghost, but he's still staring at you as you serve his troops. Ghost or not, you don't want him to pierce you if you mess up.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that your shellfish is properly <kanji>pierce</kanji>d and turned into <kanji>sushi counter</kanji>s, you can serve them to Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) and his men. I really hope you counted them correctly too, because there are a lot of them.
MeaningMnemonic:
Under the <radical>window</radical>, you place a <radical>shellfish</radical>, then slam the window shut to <kanji>pierce</kanji> its tough shell. This is the best way to pierce a member of the shellfish family, I promise.

This kanji also has a second meaning. That shellfish you pierced with the window is in two halves now. You can use those halves as plates for sushi. If you count the broken shells you'll know how much sushi you have. That makes them <kanji>sushi counter</kanji>s!

MeaningHint: Not many people have the leadership it takes to lead such a mission. Imagine what it takes to do it!
ReadingHint: Imagine planting your flag on the moon and looking back at everyone who doubted you. You throw your arms up and exclaim, "Hah! I knew I could do it!" The view is breathtaking, but the real thrill comes from showing all your doubters exactly who was right.
ReadingMnemonic: You've proven your <kanji>leadership</kanji> qualities by successfully completing your mission to the moon. "<reading>Hah</reading> (は)!" you say to all your haters. "I knew I could do it — that'll teach you! Hah!"
MeaningMnemonic: Take a <radical>helicopter</radical> made of <radical>leather</radical> to the <radical>moon</radical> to prove your excellent <kanji>leadership</kanji>. Some people called you crazy, but you'll show them, as you lead an entire crew of leather helicopter pilots on their first mission to space!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself taking a bath in your bathtub. Relaxing!
ReadingHint: Imagine being stuck on the road all day for a week. Imagine how good a bath will feel when you finally get home. Nice and hot and relaxing. Mmmm...
ReadingMnemonic: When do you miss taking a <kanji>bath</kanji> the most? When you're on the <reading>ro</reading>ad (ろ). Traveling is fun and all, but when you're stuck in a car, traveling down a dirt road for days, all you want to do is take a nice long bath.
MeaningMnemonic: I hope all you're doing in the <radical>bathtub</radical> is taking a <kanji>bath</kanji>. I mean, you can do other things if you want I guess. But bathtubs were made just for baths!

MeaningHint: Someone was obviously going to cook this poop turkey in a pot, and if you hadn't looked under this lid it surely would have died. Now as you embrace it you can hear the turkey weeping softly.
ReadingHint: Imagine embracing the turkey as it covers you in yogurt poo. You hope it's yogurt at least. You aren't gonna taste it to find out. Maybe. Okay you might taste it a little.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>embrace</kanji> the sad turkey it makes a funny sound and suddenly starts pooping <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう) all over you. But you don't want to stop embracing it! It's probably just scared still. But the more you squeeze, the faster the yogurt comes out of it!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>fingers</radical> grasp this <radical>lid</radical> and when you raise it you see a <radical>poop</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> (a turkey covered in poop). You can't help yourself, you <kanji>embrace</kanji> the turkey.

MeaningHint: The "melon boy orphan," is what they called him. Imagine a melon (your favorite kind) with a face and arms and legs sticking out. He walks up to you and says "hello kind person, could you help an orphan?"
ReadingHint: That's the sad thing about the melon orphan boy... he's just a child. "Hello gov, I'm just a child," he says to you.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>orphan</kanji> melon boy is but a <reading>子</reading> (こ).
MeaningMnemonic: There was once a <radical>melon</radical> <radical>child</radical>, that is, a child with the body of a melon. When he was born, his parents didn't want him, so he became an <kanji>orphan</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the shellfish in your hands. Imagine handing them over to the clown as compensation for the unspeakable acts you had him do for you.
ReadingHint: All you have to do is give him the right amount of compensation and you'll hear his sweet "bye, bye." Then you never have to see him again.
ReadingMnemonic: Once the clown has been properly <kanji>compensate</kanji>d, he looks you in the eyes, one hand full of shellfish, the other in the air, and waves, "<reading>Bye</reading> (ばい)" he says. "Bye, bye."
MeaningMnemonic: You give a bunch of <radical>shellfish</radical> to a <radical>clown</radical> as <kanji>compensation</kanji>. The clown did you some favors, and everyone knows clowns only accept shellfish as a form of currency. So your compensation is a pile of random shellfish.

MeaningHint: Picture these little gold triceratops shellfish linked together by their horns in a chain around your neck. Feel them hanging there. Sparkly and cold.
ReadingHint: Picture the saw in your hands, sawing back and forth on the gold chain around your neck. Maybe this isn't the best fashion accessory if you have to use a saw to get it off.
ReadingMnemonic: However, the only way to break the <kanji>chain</kanji> is to cut it open with a <reading>saw</reading> (さ). Once the golden triceratops shellfish start holding on, they never let go. So when you want to take the chain off you have to really hack at it with your saw.
MeaningMnemonic: The famous <radical>gold</radical> <radical>triceratops</radical> <radical>shellfish</radical> is good for only one thing, and that's being made into <kanji>chain</kanji>s. They link their triceratops horns together and form an unbreakable gold chain. You can't eat them, because they're gold. So they're only really good for their chaining together ability.

MeaningHint: Imagine these three things coming out of your mouth not one at a time, but all together like an eruption. Perhaps you're laying on your back, and they burst out of your mouth.
ReadingHint:
It's really inconvenient that things erupt from your mouth whenever you're having fun. Makes it hard to have fun.

Fun fact: when you see 賁 in a kanji, the reading is probably going to be ふん.
ReadingMnemonic: When do inanimate objects <kanji>erupt</kanji> from your mouth? Mostly when you're having <reading>fun</reading> (ふん).
MeaningMnemonic: From your <radical>mouth</radical> comes a <radical>cross</radical>, some <radical>flowers</radical>, and <radical>shellfish</radical>. It all <kanji>erupt</kanji>s from your mouth!

MeaningHint: Picture this auspicious spirit sheep. Blessing the children with its fluffy hooves.
ReadingHint: Picture the Shougun telling the world that they must fly spirit sheep flags on this day forever, because of how auspicious it is.
ReadingMnemonic: The spirit sheep is so <kanji>auspicious</kanji> that the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) declares this day to be the most auspicious of all days. The day when the spirit sheep appeared.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>spirit</radical> lives in a <radical>sheep</radical> it becomes the most <kanji>auspicious</kanji> creature on the planet. Lights shine out of it and the whole world weeps at its beauty. Look at the sheep's fluffy glowing head. Nothing could be more auspicious and glorious than this.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself offering the cow's life up to the god(s). It's a good thing we don't actually have to do this anymore, you think.
ReadingHint: Thanks to the mercy of your saber, the offering is quick and painless. A normal saber, however, would have cut that cow to bits. Like, really just wrecked it. Good thing we didn't make you think about that, huh?
ReadingMnemonic: You prepare the <kanji>offering</kanji> with your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい). Normally this would be gruesome, but your saber glows and the cow's life shoots up into the sky without you laying a hand on it.
MeaningMnemonic: If you take a <radical>cow</radical>'s <radical>life</radical> you're turning it into an <kanji>offering</kanji>. In the old days, cows' lives were given to the gods as offerings pretty often.

MeaningHint: Imagine being one of these people who maintain order by going out in the fields and inspecting every single grain, keeping the good ones and plucking off the ones with faults.
ReadingHint: If there are faulty grains in there, they cheated and order was not maintained. Now you have to go through all the grains and throw out the weird ones yourself.
ReadingMnemonic: You can always tell if a company keeps <kanji>order</kanji> or if it <reading>cheats</reading> (ちつ) just by looking at the grains.
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>grain</radical> has a <radical>fault</radical>, it needs to be put back in <kanji>order</kanji>. Grains must be perfectly identical at all times, otherwise they'll be thrown out. That's why special people will go out and check them to confirm their <kanji>regularity</kanji>. Keeping order is a tough job!

MeaningHint: Imagine this spider controlling your mouth with its web, making you say things you wouldn't dare think of. "Free healthcare for all!" "Pineapple is a legitimate pizza topping!" Man, you gotta stop letting that spider instigate you into saying weird stuff.
ReadingHint: If this spider's web is strong enough to control your mouth, you will most likely need a saw to cut it off, right?
ReadingMnemonic: You want the spider to stop instigating you into saying bad things and starting riots. So you pick up a <reading>saw</reading> (さ) and start cutting off the silken threads that are controlling your mouth.
MeaningMnemonic: Beside your <radical>mouth</radical> is an evil <radical>spider</radical> that tries to <kanji>instigate</kanji> you into saying bad things and starting a riot. It's like the little silken puppet master of your mouth or something. Don't let it instigate you!

MeaningHint: Have you been suffering from flat, boring hair? Well do I have the swelling treatment you've been looking for!
ReadingHint: Imagine watching your hair swell to the size of a large bowling ball. Imagine never being able to go bowling again because the comparison is too easy for people to make.
ReadingMnemonic: Using this method, your hair <kanji>swell</kanji>s so much it looks like a giant <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball is sitting on top of your head. It's all round, and well... swollen is probably the only word that fits.
MeaningMnemonic: Play your <radical>drum</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical> and your <radical>hair</radical> will <kanji>swell</kanji>! The more you play and the longer it's under the moon, the larger your hair will swell.

MeaningHint: Imagine aiming a bunch of flowers in the same direction towards you. Their aromatic fragrance coats your body. You smell great now!
ReadingHint: Unfortunately, now when you're at home you're choked by the overwhelming fragrance of flowers, all concentrating their aromas on a single point (your home). Oh… oh no it's too strong. You can't breathe. And you live alone. Help! Someone!
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to bring this <kanji>aromatic</kanji> <kanji>fragrance</kanji> into your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). To do this, you plant flowers all the way around your home, all facing in toward it.
MeaningMnemonic: If you point a bunch of <radical>flowers</radical> in the same <radical>direction</radical>, you'll be able to smell their <kanji>aromatic</kanji> <kanji>fragrance</kanji>. The best way to bask in their fragrance is to make them all point in the same direction (preferably straight at you).

MeaningHint: Think of some other things your soul is like, but always make sure you come back to one word: constant.
ReadingHint: A constant rule from Emperor こういち means constant kanji learning and constant WaniKani reviews. They will always be constant!
ReadingMnemonic: Also <kanji>constant</kanji> is the Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち, who will surely rule for a thousand more years!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> is like the <radical>ground</radical> or the <radical>dawn</radical>. It is <kanji>constant</kanji>, never changing.

MeaningHint: People hang around the bookshelf, and the leader wants to see if anyone will steal the hat or not. That's why its a test of ethics.
ReadingHint: Now we'll really see how everyone's ethics are. Will they turn in the ring to the lost and found? Will they ignore it? Will they steal it?
ReadingMnemonic: To test your <kanji>ethics</kanji> even more, the leader puts down a <reading>rin</reading>g (りん) as well.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> puts their <radical>hat</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> next to a <radical>bookshelf</radical>. This is a test in <kanji>ethics</kanji>.

MeaningHint: It's a pretty large building and you can see inside to all of the different exhibits within the main exhibit.
ReadingHint: Go up to the first exhibit and look very closely at some turtle chins, then some crab chins, then some alligator chins. This is weirdly familiar.
ReadingMnemonic: Right now the <kanji>exhibit</kanji> has a bunch of <reading>chin</reading>s (ちん) on display. Animal chins, human chins, every chin you can think of is in the exhibit.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>building</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> is an <kanji>exhibit</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the page in your cookbook where it says "Shaving the hair off your geoduck = Necessary!!" It's absolutely necessary that you follow that instruction!
ReadingHint: What's necessary? To shave the hair off your geoduck and to wear a suit. That's not too hard. You should do it.
ReadingMnemonic: It is also <kanji>necessary</kanji> that you wear a <reading>su</reading>it (す) when eating shaved geoduck. It's a super fancy meal so a suit is necessary for you to enjoy it correctly.
MeaningMnemonic: Shaving the <radical>hair</radical> off a <radical>geoduck</radical> before you try to eat it is absolutely <kanji>necessary</kanji>. If you don't, the hair will get caught in your throat and you'll choke. It is necessary that you remove the hair.

MeaningHint: Imagine showing the leader your favorite book. "That's not a book in MY library," he says, and throws it in the trash. That's so biased! He didn't even give it a shot!
ReadingHint: "This is such biased crap," you say, as you finish reading the title of a book called "Hens are better than all other birds." Screw this leader guy and his books.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to give in to his <kanji>biased</kanji> recommendations and look at what he has in his library. It doesn't take long until you realize that these books are all about <reading>hen</reading>s (へん) and hen care. This guy is so biased about his favorite books that he only filled his library with what HE wants to read. And apparently that thing is taking care of hens... weird.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> of the <radical>library</radical> thinks that all the books in their library are better than everyone else's books. He's a very <kanji>biased</kanji> person. If he wasn't the leader of that specific library he'd probably feel differently.

MeaningHint: These are tough times for your scooter, so be patient with it. Try to treat it with understanding and care.
ReadingHint: Not just "good", but "gooooood". The long vowel is important here, so try to think of different ways you could make your scooter feel gooooood. Maybe a nice little wash after you’ve removed the splinter will do the trick.
ReadingMnemonic: Give your scooter only the best <kanji>treatment</kanji>, so it can feel real <reading>goo</reading>ooood (ぐう).
MeaningMnemonic:
Your poor little <radical>scooter</radical> got a <radical>splinter</radical>, so make sure to give it extra nice <kanji>treatment</kanji>. 

This can also mean <kanji>encounter</kanji>. Makes sense when you think about how you first have to encounter someone before deciding how you're going to treat them!

Also, when this means "treatment," note that it doesn't refer to medical treatment, but rather about how you personally treat or receive people.

MeaningHint: Picture everyone in the village getting all of their rice together as provisions for the army. Even the kids and old people are helping out.
ReadingHint: Picture the soldiers gobbling down their provisions of rice and roe in their row boats as they row away.
ReadingMnemonic: Once all of the <kanji>provisions</kanji> are ready, the soldiers show up in their <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boats and load it all up. But they aren't done there. "Rice isn't enough, we need <reading>roe</reading> (ろう) so we will have the strength to row our row boats back." And so the village brings them all of their roe too.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>rice</radical> that's prepared at <radical>dawn</radical> by the <radical>village</radical> are <kanji>provisions</kanji> for the army. The army is scheduled to arrive right after dawn, so all of the village's provisions for them have to be ready by then.

MeaningHint: A jet with a teeter-totter? Normal yakuza boss. A jet with a jungle gym? Powerful yakuza boss. A jet with a slide? Especially powerful yakuza boss.
ReadingHint: Just don't get on the bad side of one of these especially important yakuza bosses — a trap door at the bottom of the chute will open and drop you out of their jet.
ReadingMnemonic: These <kanji>especially</kanji> powerful yakuza bosses have slides that are like <reading>chu</reading>tes (しゅ) — they're especially fast and especially fun!
MeaningMnemonic: Within the <radical>yakuza</radical>, having a <radical>slide</radical> on your <radical>jet</radical> means you're <kanji>especially</kanji> important and powerful. It's a symbol of how especially awesome and badass you are.

MeaningHint: Imagine the level of overconfidence necessary to think you could capture the soul of such an important celestial body with just a net. Get off your stool, stop being lazy, and go try to catch the soul of something more within your league — a small asteroid, for example.
ReadingHint: Manga has also given you the overconfidence to believe that your lack of progress in life isn't due to laziness — it's because you're still in your "training arc."
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>overconfidence</kanji> stems from your <reading>man</reading>ga (まん) addiction, which is also the source of your <kanji>laziness</kanji>. You lie in bed for hours a day reading manga and imagining yourself as the protagonist, and it's seriously distorted your sense of reality. This is probably why you think you can do outlandish things like capturing the sun's soul.
MeaningMnemonic: You think you can catch the <radical>soul</radical> of the <radical>sun</radical> in a <radical>net</radical> without even getting up from your <radical>stool</radical>? That's some serious <kanji>overconfidence</kanji>, buddy. The fact that you want to stay seated really proves your <kanji>laziness</kanji>, too.

MeaningHint: Picture everyone in the world being swept away in a tsunami to die. This is what happens when you try to use nature as a weapon.
ReadingHint: You don't want to die, do you? Get to the boats!
ReadingMnemonic: The only way to maybe not <kanji>die</kanji> is to find <reading>boats</reading> (ぼつ). As long as the boats don't get smashed by the tsunami, you might be able to cheat death by holding onto the boats and not drowning.
MeaningMnemonic: If you figure out how to use a <radical>tsunami</radical> as a <radical>weapon</radical>, everyone is going to <kanji>die</kanji>. You can't control a tsunami, you fool. Now you've doomed us all.

MeaningHint: Picture a machine with a heart, lying on the couch, eating oil snacks, snoozing and neglecting the orders you've given it. What a lazy machine!
ReadingHint: Picture this lazy machine sitting on the couch again. There are untied ties all over the floor, hanging on the back of the couch, but it totally neglects them.
ReadingMnemonic: The machine is so <kanji>lazy</kanji> it even starts to <kanji>neglect</kanji> its <reading>tie</reading> (たい). It's supposed to wear one, but it's too lazy to put it on now. Do lazy humans put on ties? Nope, and neither do neglectful machines.
MeaningMnemonic: If you give a <radical>machine</radical> a <radical>heart</radical> it will get <kanji>lazy</kanji> and <kanji>neglect</kanji> its duties. Hearts give us emotions and with emotions we become weak. Why would a machine with a heart want to work anymore? Nah. It's going to be lazy and neglectful now.

MeaningHint: Picture all of the people you encounter as you ride your scooter, spreading your music around town.
ReadingHint: "We love your music, look at our souls!" Uh oh, this is turning into some kind of horror movie. You better scoot a bit faster or you might meet an unfortunate fate.
ReadingMnemonic: Everyone you <kanji>encounter</kanji> wants to bare their <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) to you, literally. They're all coming out to meet you, pulling back their skin and showing you the soul ghosts inside of them.
MeaningMnemonic: When you ride your <radical>scooter</radical> on the <radical>ground</radical> listening to <radical>music</radical> under the <radical>sun</radical>, you're bound to <kanji>encounter</kanji> a bunch of new people! They hear your music playing as you scoot by and they come out to <kanji>meet</kanji> you.

MeaningHint: Picture the lazy narwhal laying on a block of ice in the moonlight. There are tools and other blocks of ice around, but he just can't seem to get up and do any construction work.
ReadingHint: Picture your lazy dad. Your big, lazy narwhal dad.
ReadingMnemonic: You can't tell the <kanji>lazy</kanji> narwhal he's being lazy because he's your <reading>da</reading>d (だ). If he was anyone else you'd tell him off for being lazy, but you can't tell off your dad. That's just not right.
MeaningMnemonic: In its <radical>soul</radical>, a <radical>narwhal</radical> that's supposed to do <radical>construction</radical> under the <radical>moon</radical>, will be too <kanji>lazy</kanji> to do it. And when your soul is lazy, there's no way you'll be able to get something done.

MeaningHint: Picture the shape of the animal in the grass, off in the distance. You can't see what kind of animal it is yet, but you know you're hunting it.
ReadingHint: You wouldn't be able to go hunting in any other kind of boat—the motor would be too loud. Picture yourself in a row boat with all of your hunting gear. You need to be extra careful not to make any loud splashing sounds with the oars!
ReadingMnemonic: You're currently <kanji>hunting</kanji> in a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and you have to row as quietly and carefully as you can or the animal will run away.
MeaningMnemonic: There is an <radical>animal</radical> in the <radical>grass</radical> and your horrible <radical>task</radical> is to go <kanji>hunting</kanji> for it.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself climbing stairs, picture going from room to room. From place to place. From wherever you were to wherever you want to be.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself going from one place to another with your pockets stuffed full of nori. The smell is starting to get overwhelming. It's making you really hungry (or nauseous, depending on how you feel about nori!).
ReadingMnemonic: If you're going <kanji>from</kanji> place to place, make sure you have some <reading>no</reading>ri (の) seaweed on you, in case you get hungry. Nori is a great source of energy.
MeaningMnemonic: What do <radical>stairs</radical> do? They take you <kanji>from</kanji> where you were, to somewhere else. They're a way to get <kanji>from</kanji> place to place.

MeaningHint: Imagine holding your creation in your frostbitten hands as the snowflake design starts to appear. The design gets more and more complicated the longer you watch, twisting this way and that, like it's dancing in front of your eyes.
ReadingHint: If you know someone else called Aya, go ahead and use them in this mnemonic! Whoever it is, imagine an Aya watching from afar holding a magic wand, creating this beautiful frosty pattern!
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch the snowflake <kanji>design</kanji> form you see letters appearing in the corner! They form the name <reading>Aya</reading> (あや), our artist here at WaniKani. Somehow she's magically putting together this beautiful pattern!
MeaningMnemonic: You're sewing something with <radical>thread</radical> but you and your creation get <radical>frostbite</radical> while you're still working. Luckily the snowflakes freeze themselves into the cloth and turn into a pretty <kanji>design</kanji>. They swirl around and press a unique snowy design into the fabric. This is nice for you because you can't sew anymore with your frostbitten hands.

MeaningHint: Imagine how quick the wind knocks you over when you stand up. So quick, you don't even remember being up. Try again and count how many seconds it takes for it to knock you down. Less than one. Now that's quick.
ReadingHint: Okay, pretty quick, but this wind is even quicker than that.
ReadingMnemonic: That <kanji>quick</kanji> wind is quicker than you can say <reading>Satsu</reading>ki (さつ). And "Satsuki" is a pretty quick sounding name already. Go ahead, say "Satsuki" as quick as you can right now. Heck, try it a few times.
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>stand</radical> in the <radical>wind</radical> it will be really <kanji>quick</kanji> to knock you over. So quick that you probably won't even know the wind did it. You'll end up standing up only to be knocked over again, just as quick.

MeaningHint: Imagine tying a cross to this turkey and see it transform into an elegant falcon. Majestic.
ReadingHint: Picture your falcon Hayabusa and the real... ok fake.. Hayabusa running around together and kicking butt.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to name this new falcon after your favorite falcon-like ninja. Ryu Hayabusa (はやぶさ) from Ninja Gaiden! Picture your fat turkey falcon and Ryu Hayabusa teaming up Banjo and Kazooey style. Yeah, that's the dream right there.
MeaningMnemonic: If you tie a <radical>cross</radical> to a <radical>turkey</radical> you can trick everyone into thinking it's a <kanji>falcon</kanji>. The cross will stretch its wings out just enough for it to stop looking so fat and turkey like, into a super cool bird of prey.

MeaningHint: Picture all of your close friends coming to help you squeeze everything for the wedding into your car.
ReadingHint: Imagine calling all your friends and scheduling when you will get their help. Make sure you have your day planner ready!
ReadingMnemonic: You can't just ask for <kanji>help</kanji> on the day of the wedding, either. You have to <reading>sche</reading>dule (すけ) getting your friends' help ahead of time. They might have pretty tight schedules too, so make sure you plan early!
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to have your <radical>wedding</radical> in a <radical>car</radical> you're going to need a lot of <kanji>help</kanji>. Cars are pretty small compared to weddings, so if you don't get help from everyone you know you won't be able to pull it off.

MeaningHint: These roof flowers only see because they do not judge. They accept everything. We humans can learn a thing or two about tolerance from these roof flowers.
ReadingHint: You wouldn't expect it, but he actually allowed many types of cultures, religions, and peoples to exist under his reign. The Mongols had an exceptional amount of tolerance for the time.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Tolerance</kanji> is one thing that Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) had a surprising amount of.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>roof</radical> <radical>flowers</radical> <radical>see</radical> everything. But, they only see. That is because they learned the art of <kanji>tolerance</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the moon with a big bag circling it. Now look down at your biology homework on cells. They're identical!
ReadingHint: AHHH CLEAN ALL OF THESE CELLS OUT OF YOUR HOME!
ReadingMnemonic: As you learn about <kanji>cell</kanji>s you realize they're all over your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). Without ever noticing it, your home has been slowly filling with dead skin cells. And now that you know what they look like (the moon with a plastic wrap bag around it) you can see them everywhere.
MeaningMnemonic: Take the <radical>moon</radical> and <radical>wrap</radical> it in a bag and it will look just like a <kanji>cell</kanji>. It's a circle in a bigger circle. The moon is the nucleus. Yes! Science is happening!

MeaningHint: Anything less than a tsunami of conflict would leave something behind. But this much conflict will cleanse the area of everything.
ReadingHint: Joe has gone through many things during your WaniKani journey, but this might be too much even for him. Either way, he’s going to try his best to stop this tsunami of conflict, threatening to cleanse the lands of everything we know. With his bare hands, of course.
ReadingMnemonic: Who could possibly stop this <kanji>cleanse</kanji> of all lands?! The only person capable of this is, of course, <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the local farmhand. He’s so tall and burly that he can stop even the largest of tsunamis… right?
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> of <radical>conflict</radical> will <kanji>cleanse</kanji> and <kanji>purify</kanji> the lands of people, homes, and culture.

MeaningHint: All buildings have at least one scooter. It could be out front, on the roof, in the basement. But all of them have scooters and someone who likes to ride them instead of using a car or a bike.
ReadingHint: Imagine the sound of the scooter's tiny engine: zuwee zuwee zu zu zuwee. All of them do this, which is why you hate them.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>All</kanji> of these scooters have a specific sound they make as they burst out of the buildings: <reading>zuwee</reading> (ずい).
MeaningMnemonic: How many <radical>building</radical>s <radical>have</radical> <radical>scooter</radical>s? <kanji>All</kanji> of them!

MeaningHint: Imagine holding your draft up outside in a field of grains. You're a couple leaves short. Time to get back to the drawing board.
ReadingHint: Now こういち is out there measuring your draft against the tall grain plants. You better hope it's long enough, because if it isn't, Emperor こういち's probably going to execute you.
ReadingMnemonic: Who asked you to write such a tall <kanji>draft</kanji> in the first place? Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち, who wants you to draft up the world's largest book about how great of an emperor he is.
MeaningMnemonic: When what you're writing is as <radical>tall</radical> as the <radical>grain</radical>s growing outside, you know you're done with your first <kanji>draft</kanji>. Some teachers want papers to be a certain number of words or pages, but that's silly. You should measure your draft in grains!

MeaningHint: Picture this broken, rust-colored boat. It may be rusty and missing some important pieces, but it's not trying to hide its flaws — and there's a beautiful sincerity in that.
ReadingHint: Watch your skin slowly change as you tan to match the rust-colored boat. Don't worry about the uncomfortable burning sensation — that's just proof you're becoming a more sincere, more tan human being.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>rust-colored</kanji> boat has inspired you, so you lie on its deck and get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). If you can tan until you're the same <kanji>cinnabar</kanji> color as the boat, you think some of its <kanji>sincerity</kanji> might rub off on you.
MeaningMnemonic: This is a <radical>boat</radical>, but it's missing some pieces. That's because it's really rusty, so parts are falling off of it and it looks <kanji>rust-colored</kanji>. In Taoist thought and ancient Chinese literature, this rusty red color, also known as <kanji>cinnabar</kanji>, represented <kanji>sincerity</kanji>, and you'll often see this kanji take that meaning in jukugo words.

MeaningHint: Soil generally has more nutrients in it. It makes sense for dirt that's been nursed to become soil.
ReadingHint: That kind of makes Joe a dirt or soil nurse, if you think about it. Not sure he’d like that title, though…
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Soil</kanji> is a thing that <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the huge farmhand, works on improving. If he makes the soil better, the farm will yield more crops.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>dirt</radical> <radical>nurse</radical> is someone who nurses dirt. When nursed, dirt becomes <kanji>soil</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the incredibly laborious task of paving an entire road by yourself, just to get to this cottage wedding shop. You really want your quaint little cottage wedding though, so it’s the only choice you have…
ReadingHint: Imagine walking around the shop and then falling in love with a hoe with pretty tassels and flowers attached to it. Yes, it's only for decoration, but you're going to buy and use it during your cottage wedding.
ReadingMnemonic: You're browsing everything in the <kanji>shop</kanji> when a garden <reading>hoe</reading> (ほ) catches your eye. Yes. This will make your cottage wedding the absolute best one. The hoe will give you away at your wedding.
MeaningMnemonic:
You want to have a quaint <radical>cottage</radical> <radical>wedding</radical>, so you have to go to the cottage wedding <kanji>shop</kanji> to get everything you need.

Unfortunately, there’s no road to the shop, so… you’ll just have to <kanji>pave</kanji> one yourself! Every shop needs a road, so get to paving.

MeaningHint: Picture the gladiator riding his horse in the light of the moon yelling, "Do you have any idea how important it is to increase the average pay rate to account for inflation? All of us gladiators are going to be unable to pay the bills soon because inflation isn't being taken into account! You'll suffer too! Listen to me!"
ReadingHint: Imagine if you were in this gladiator's place. In five years, you wouldn't be able to afford to eat thanks to the bosses in とうきょう refusing to adjust for inflation. Back in my day, onigiri only cost FIFteen yen!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Inflation</kanji> rises the most in <reading>とう</reading>きょう, and the gladiator knows that because that's where most of them live and fight (and die).
MeaningMnemonic: While the <radical>moon</radical> is out, a <radical>gladiator</radical> is riding a <radical>horse</radical>, yelling about <kanji>inflation</kanji>. No one would listen to him during the day, so he waited for the moon to rise so he could complain about prices doing the same.

MeaningHint:
Why thread? Back in the old days globes were sewn together with cloth and thread, so there was a literal thread dividing the Koreas!

Or you can just picture an actual, super long piece of thread going around the entire globe. That works too.
ReadingHint: Imagine being an eagle yourself, flying around the entire globe, following the latitude line that splits Korea down the middle.
ReadingMnemonic: The only way you can see this <kanji>latitude</kanji> line is to ride an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い) over it. The line isn't visible from a car, you're too close, and a plane is too high. You have to see it from an eagle's point of view.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> that goes through <radical>Korea</radical> is the <kanji>latitude</kanji> line splitting up North and South Korea.

MeaningHint: Picture the Yoga King rowing a rowboat. Maybe he's using his legs. Maybe he's using his butt? You don't know. Yoga people are super flexible, he could be using anything to move this rowboat.
ReadingHint: Go rent a rowboat and try eating tater tots in it. For fun, throw a bunch of them in the water and watch them float. Maybe you'll catch a fish!
ReadingMnemonic: As he rows the <kanji>rowboat</kanji> with his butt, you see the Yoga King eating something with his toes. They're <reading>ta</reading>ter tots (てい). Tater tots are the perfect snack for a rowboat. They're small, delicious, and they float if you drop them in the water.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>boat</radical> owned by a <radical>Yoga</radical> <radical>King</radical> needs to be able to let him keep doing his exercises while he's on it. That's why his boat is a <kanji>rowboat</kanji>, so he can work on his yoga arms as he gets around.

MeaningHint: Close your eyes. Picture someone's arm. Now touch their skin with your fingers and peel it back to expose the muscles and bones underneath.
ReadingHint: Imagine the heat steaming out, like it's coming out of a teapot, not out of somebody's arm skin.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>expose</kanji> a small hole under their skin and <reading>he</reading>at (ひ) comes pouring out. Aha! You knew there was something wrong under here. Apparently there's something under this person's skin that's exuding way too much heat.
MeaningMnemonic: Press your <radical>fingers</radical> in someone's <radical>skin</radical> to <kanji>expose</kanji> what's underneath. This is pretty gross, but sometimes it's more important that you expose the cause of the problem, than avoiding something uncomfortable.

MeaningHint: Imagine weaving this beautiful brocade fabric out of one of your old towels. Try to actually picture the gold and white patterns you want on there. If you're a crafter, actually do it!
ReadingHint: Imagine using your niche key to open this hidden brocade storage room. The key is gold and white and looks like brocade too!
ReadingMnemonic: You want to keep your <kanji>brocade</kanji> fabrics safe, so you lock them away and seal them in with a <reading>niche key</reading> (にしき). It's niche because brocade is a very niche kind of thing.
MeaningMnemonic: You weave <radical>gold</radical> and <radical>white</radical> patterns into this <radical>towel</radical>, turning it into <kanji>brocade</kanji>. It's beautiful, elegant, and worth way more than when it was just a plain towel.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing so much grain that you can stuff your mouth so full you can't swallow or spit it out. You have to use a knife to remove it. After you get all the grain out, comment on the surplus of grain.
ReadingHint: Remember how Joe made the soil better? Perhaps this is some of that work paying off. Thanks to that and everything else, there is a great surplus.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>surplus</kanji> is thanks to the hard work of folks like <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big farmhand. He worked hard to make sure there was a surplus of grain this year.
MeaningMnemonic: There's so much <radical>grain</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> you need to use a <radical>knife</radical> to remove it. Only when there is a <kanji>surplus</kanji> can you put that much grain in your mouth.

MeaningHint: Imagine a drunkard crossing threads onto spikes and pulling the tiny fibers free. You don't think you could do that. Maybe if you got drunk too...
ReadingHint: Watch the centaur cross the fibers and separate them with his spikes. How strange.
ReadingMnemonic: You stare at the <kanji>fiber</kanji>s easily coming apart when you realize that this drunkard seems to have a few extra legs... and hair and a tail? This isn't just a drunk guy, it's a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Maybe separating fibers is really a centaur trick, and that's why normal people can't do it...
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> <radical>cross</radical>es <radical>thread</radical> across special <radical>spikes</radical> to separate the <kanji>fiber</kanji>s. The best way to separate the fibers of thread is to get a drunkard to do it. If you're too precise it will just snap. You gotta be loose with it! Just flop them onto the spikes and the fibers will come apart by themselves!

MeaningHint: Imagine being admonished by the Death Star. It's hovering above you, giant and round, just yelling at you about everything you've ever messed up. It's really awful being yelled at. But the Death Star doing it is particularly scary and embarrassing.
ReadingHint: Imagine sitting in your yurt, the home you love, but you can still hear the admonishments from the Death Star in your ears. You feel... bad.
ReadingMnemonic: Right now you're being <kanji>admonish</kanji>ed about living in a <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ). The Death Star bellows, "What, am I too big and safe for you? I'm easy to move too, you know. You're a disgrace! You should be ashamed!"
MeaningMnemonic: If you try to <radical>say</radical> something to the <radical>Death Star</radical>, it would only <kanji>admonish</kanji> you. It was made to destroy planets, after all, so I don't know why you'd expect it to have anything nice to say. It just admonishes you about everything you've ever done wrong until you can't stop crying.

MeaningHint: This disaster is ruining your life. You can't eat, sleep, all you can do is panic trying to get the hair out.
ReadingHint: Santa-san has forsaken you. Your life is a wreck. This is a total disaster just because of one big hair.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>disaster</kanji> is so huge that even <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) left his vacation in <reading>Zan</reading>zibar (ざん) to come tell you you're on the naughty list for being such a disaster person. WHAT?! Even you Santa-san?
MeaningMnemonic: If you get a <radical>big</radical> <radical>hair</radical> stuck in your <radical>private</radical> <radical>soul</radical> it will be a <kanji>disaster</kanji>! Have you ever gotten a hair in your eye or your mouth that you just couldn't get out? You cough, your eyes water, it's awful! You can't focus on anything until it's gone. Now imagine if a big 'ol hair got stuck in your own private soul!!! Disaster!!!

MeaningHint: Imagine being a tiger or Wolverine and being oppressed just because you have claws. Imagine being stuck in a cage. Not being allowed to vote. It's awful!
ReadingHint: Imagine how silly it would look for Wolverine and these animals to have their claws covered in multicolored goo. Gak is such a humiliating way to oppress someone.
ReadingMnemonic: Tigers and other clawed beings like Wolverine are so <kanji>oppress</kanji>ed, they're forced to cover their claws with <reading>Gak</reading> (ぎゃく). You know, that weird silly putty they made us play with as kids? Well, it's now an instrument of anti-Wolverine, anti-tiger oppression.
MeaningMnemonic: Whether you're a <radical>tiger</radical> or <radical>Wolverine</radical>, the anti-claw regime will want to <kanji>oppress</kanji> you. Too afraid of any potential violent rebellion, this oppressive regime is going to imprison and declaw every single clawed being.

MeaningHint: Imagine reaching out and grabbing onto the old flag with your fingers. Feel the dirty grime and little holes it's gotten. Now install something new!
ReadingHint: Picture a newly installed suit flying in the air on a pole.
ReadingMnemonic: You've decided to <kanji>install</kanji> a <reading>su</reading>it (す) where the old flag used to be. I mean, a suit is probably more durable than a flag, am I right? This way you won't have to install flags as often.
MeaningMnemonic: You use your <radical>fingers</radical> to take down the <radical>old</radical> <radical>flag</radical> and <kanji>install</kanji> a new one. Flags get old, just like computers and furniture and people. So when one gets too old, you have to get on up there and install a new one in its place.

MeaningHint: Unfortunately it's not specific as to what kind of surplus you're loitering around, so you'll need to imagine a surplus of something memorable to you. Then, just imagine loitering around there, being really gentle with everything.
ReadingHint: Imagine Jo-Anne coming back to her surplus, then asking you if you were gentle with the goods. You were, you treated everything very gently.
ReadingMnemonic: You have to be <kanji>gentle</kanji> and handle these things <kanji>gently</kanji> because they belong to <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ). You're loitering around her surplus of (whatever you decided to imagine).
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>loiter</radical> around a <radical>surplus</radical> of anything, you need to be <kanji>gentle</kanji> and touch everything <kanji>gently</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself boarding the ship and letting the flowers slip from your fingers, into the crowd of people waving goodbye.
ReadingHint: Where else would you go at this point? I mean, come on. You've made it 53 levels since you embarked on your kanji journey with us. We know you by now. You want to be in とうきょう.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>board</kanji> a ship to <reading>とう</reading>きょう, bound for your new life, of course!
MeaningMnemonic: In your <radical>fingers</radical> are <radical>flowers</radical> which really <radical>suit</radical> you. But you throw them to the people waving to you from below, as you <kanji>board</kanji> your ship. The flowers fall, unneeded. You're <kanji>embark</kanji>ing on a new life, a new journey. There will be more flowers where you're going.

MeaningHint: Picture your village in Japan. There's a big, still pond full of bright and beautiful giant carp. You hear the village kids giggle as they feed them and their big round mouths pop out of the water.
ReadingHint: Carp are koi and koi are carp. Blub blub.
ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>carp</kanji> is called a <reading>koi</reading> (こい) in Japanese. We've used that word before so I hope you know it by now. A koi is a koi, you know?
MeaningMnemonic: If you're in Japan, the <radical>fish</radical> in your <radical>village</radical> are <kanji>carp</kanji>. You may know them as <kanji>koi</kanji>, too.

MeaningHint: Picture a person wearing all scarlet all the time. Don't they give you a criminal-y vibe?
ReadingHint: Picture a criminal, covered in this scarlet thread, heat radiating off of him.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>scarlet</kanji> color holds a lot of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Scarlet is the color of fire, and it has the heat of fire too.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical>s a <radical>criminal</radical> wears will always be <kanji>scarlet</kanji>. Scarlet is the color of freshly spilt blood, which is why bad guys wear it so much.

MeaningHint: Picture someone tied down in a net as dawn breaks.
ReadingHint: Look at Akebono looking at you as you look at dawn breaking.
ReadingMnemonic: The someone who saw <kanji>dawn</kanji> for the first time goes on to become sumo wrestling champ <reading>Akebono</reading> Taro (あけぼの). He always gazes at the sunrise at dawn before he wrestles and it fills him with strength.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is rising and there is a <radical>net</radical> covering <radical>someone</radical>, who's being forced to watch as <kanji>dawn</kanji> appears. They've never seen dawn before, and they didn't want to, but you're making them.

MeaningHint: Just imagine a huge barbarian (whatever that means to you) floating in the sky, looking down. Some ancient people look up and wave at the old moon barbarian.
ReadingHint: "You see, my child, the old moon was not like the current moon. It was a giant barbarian in the sky, who would eat children that asked too many questions."
ReadingMnemonic: At least, this is the kind of story you told your <reading>子</reading> (こ) when they asked you where the moon came from.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>old</radical> <radical>moon</radical> (the moon we had before the current moon) was actually just a huge <kanji>barbarian</kanji> floating in the sky.

MeaningHint: Imagine a commander carefully looking over everything. They not only notice a drop of something on a towel, but they're able to identify that the drop is a small piece of liquid bear.
ReadingHint: "Weeee!" says the commander.
ReadingMnemonic: You watch the <kanji>commander</kanji> in all his glory... going back and forth on a <reading>swi</reading>ng (すい).
MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>drop</radical> of <radical>bear</radical> on this <radical>towel</radical>. A true <kanji>commander</kanji> can notice little things like this.

MeaningHint: Imagine searching for this special door in the cold wind and snow of winter. The search for enlightenment always has something to do with suffering and searching on your own like this.
ReadingHint: All you need to do to attain enlightenment is to eat this cake... out of this mouth. Eh. Maybe enlightenment isn't worth it.
ReadingMnemonic: The door <kanji>enlight</kanji>ened you by opening up and revealing the <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい) in its door-mouth. "Cake is the key to enlightenment, my child. Now eat it from my mouth."
MeaningMnemonic: If you can find the <radical>door</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> shaped like a <radical>mouth</radical> it will <kanji>enlighten</kanji> you.

MeaningHint: Picture a tiny yokai using a hollyhock as a tent. Aww cute!
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself in a field of hollyhocks and the あおい of them match the あおい of the heavens! It's like you're standing on a giant flower filled mirror of the sky.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Hollyhock</kanji>s are mostly <reading>あおい</reading> (blue) because they copy the color of the heavens, where they came from.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> that are thought to be little <radical>tent</radical>s sent from <radical>heaven</radical> are <kanji>hollyhock</kanji>s. They're shaped almost like little teepees and would make the perfect tent for a faerie or a little yokai!

MeaningHint: Imagine the eyes of a horse seeing a spider and then running at top speed to get the heck away from it.
ReadingHint: Soon the world will be filled with horses running at full speed all thanks to the spider increase of the 2000s! Soon we will be in the future of top speeds!
ReadingMnemonic: Once people realize that they can increase the <kanji>speed</kanji> of their horses, <reading>soon</reading> (しゅん) everyone will be taking spiders with them to scare their horses into running faster. I say soon because it hasn't quite caught on yet. But if you help to spread the word it can happen even sooner.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>horse</radical> sees a <radical>spider</radical> it will start running at a <kanji>speed</kanji> no one ever thought a horse could go! Horses are super afraid of spiders, you see, so if they see one, they'll bolt and reach top speed! That's actually the only way to get a horse to run that fast.

MeaningHint: Imagine the confused faces of the people in the countryside as you try to talk to them. Decide to say it the way it's said in the capital and see their faces light up as they comprehend what you're trying to say.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself rowing away in your new row boat as the townspeople wave goodbye. They were much nicer once they comprehended what you were asking for. Maybe you'll row your way back here someday.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that the townspeople finally <kanji>comprehend</kanji> what you were trying to say, they sell you the <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat you were trying to buy. You were using the wrong word, but りょう is what they needed to hear to understand you, and now you can row your way back home.
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>say</radical> something the same way they say it in the <radical>capital</radical>, everyone in the whole country will be able to <kanji>comprehend</kanji> what you're trying to say. If you try to speak in your hometown's dialect no one will. But everyone can comprehend what you want if you use the capital's dialect to say it.

MeaningHint: It's weird that jasmine flowers are growing out of your grain, but that's why you're cutting them off.
ReadingHint: You have a farm that grows grain and reeds. Jasmine flowers are growing out of both of those things, ruining your crops! Perhaps you should become a jasmine tea farmer instead...
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>jasmine</kanji> is growing out of everything, not just grain! It's also growing out of the <reading>ree</reading>ds (り)!
MeaningMnemonic: You cut the <radical>flowers</radical> off the <radical>grain</radical> with your <radical>knife</radical>. The flowers that fall off are <kanji>jasmine</kanji> flowers.

MeaningHint: Picture the underwater fish capital. Statues of whales line the fishy streets, and all of the fish bow down to the whale ruler's giant body.
ReadingHint: Picture the gay whale ruler holding whale hands... fins? with his whale husband. They're so cute.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>whale</kanji> who resides over the capital just happens to be <reading>gay</reading> (げい). You only know this because his husband is a man whale too. Actually, now that you think about it, all the whales you've ever met have been gay.
MeaningMnemonic: If you travel to the <radical>fish</radical> <radical>capital</radical>, you'll find the leader of all fish, the mighty <kanji>whale</kanji>! Okay, so a whale isn't technically a fish, but that's why it makes such a good leader. You can usually only find whales hanging around near the capital because they really enjoy ruling over all the other fish. They are the biggest, after all.

MeaningHint: Picture the samurai's villa. It's in the countryside and covered in flowers and icicles. Even though it's spring, it's still cold, so all the new flowers are very cold.
ReadingHint: Picture the samurai sitting in a chair in his beautiful villa and breathing his last breath. His soul slowly floats up and out of his still body and dances around the villa, free.
ReadingMnemonic: Once the samurai enters the <kanji>villa</kanji>, he lets his <reading>soul</reading> (そう) go. He's been fighting for long enough and this villa was always where he wanted to go.
MeaningMnemonic: Clearing his <radical>flowers</radical> of <radical>icicle</radical>s, the <radical>samurai</radical> returns home to his <kanji>villa</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a drunk guy laughing and plowing a field and planting seeds. He wouldn't do this if he was sober, but drunk people can't help themselves.
ReadingHint: Oh my sweet cyborg child! Is this what they've done with you? You're just a drunkard who's only good for planting? Nooooooo!!
ReadingMnemonic: He keeps <kanji>planting</kanji> and planting... a little too much. You walk over and realize this drunkard isn't just any guy... he's YOUR <reading>CY</reading>BORG (さい)!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> gets his hands on a <radical>plow</radical> and starts <kanji>planting</kanji> right away. Drunk people are surprisingly good at planting, especially when they have machines to help them do it.

MeaningHint: Imagine you've been kidnapped. And the person who kidnapped you is going to cut off some of your fingers and mail them to your parents if they don't pay the ransom soon enough. You don't want your fingers to be cut off with a sword! Help!
ReadingHint: If your parents don't send in the kayak to your kidnapper soon, you're gonna lose some fingers. Come on guys, just get the kayak already!!!
ReadingMnemonic: You've been <kanji>kidnap</kanji>ped and your kidnapper has asked for one thing in exchange for your safe return: a <reading>kay</reading>ak (かい). Kayaks are expensive. And it's way easier for this dude to kidnap you and demand your family buy one and give it to them than for them to work for one.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>mouth</radical> with a <radical>sword</radical> is threatening to cut off your <radical>fingers</radical> because they've <kanji>kidnap</kanji>ped you! You can't see anything about them, since they're all covered up. All you see is their angry mouth and the sword they keep putting near your fingers.

MeaningHint: Imagine someone putting a tape measure around your forehead and taking careful measurements for your crown. They better not mess up, or you'll have them put to death!
ReadingHint: Why don't you go look up what Genghis Khan's crown/cap looked like. Then try to imagine how it would look atop your head.
ReadingMnemonic: You want your <kanji>crown</kanji> to be just like Genghis <reading>Khan</reading>'s (かん), which was more of a cap. He's your hero though, because he ruled over the most people, so you want yours to match his measurements.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>forehead</radical> is the <radical>origin</radical> for the <radical>measurement</radical> for your <kanji>crown</kanji>! If you measure somewhere else on your head for a crown it won't fit.

MeaningHint: Most people in places heavy with power, like the scouts, boil down all of their responsibilities and accomplishments this way into merit badges.
ReadingHint: This is why you never see a military officer or a girl scout leaving their merit badges out in the open. They pin them right onto their clothes. Otherwise the raccoons would come.
ReadingMnemonic: The only problem with consolidating your <kanji>merit</kanji>s like this is that rac<reading>coon</reading>s (くん) are always trying to steal them. Raccoons really like shiny, small things. Like, say, the physical representation of all your personal and professional merits.
MeaningMnemonic: You're in a really important position, <radical>heavy</radical> with <radical>power</radical>, but you can't carry all of that around with you, so you <radical>boil</radical> it all down to just a few <kanji>merit</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself dropping the alcohol, drop by drop, into the river. Uh oh, the person just caught up to you, so you better repay them.
ReadingHint: Imagine waiting as this person tries on like twenty different pairs of shoes. They're getting them for free! Why do they have to be picky?! Just let me repay you already!
ReadingMnemonic: In order to <kanji>repay</kanji> them for the alcohol you wasted, you offer to give them your <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう). They don't want your shoes though, so you have to bring them to a store and watch them try on a bunch of different pairs so you can repay them that way.
MeaningMnemonic: You took someone's <radical>alcohol</radical> and <radical>drop</radical> <radical>drop</radical> <radical>drop</radical>'d it all into the <radical>river</radical>. Now you have to <kanji>repay</kanji> them. Alcohol costs a lot of money, you know.

MeaningHint: Imagine the thread slowly forming the writing that makes up your family crest. You're making a scarf with your crest on it. It's like a wizarding school house symbol.
ReadingHint: What kind of monster is on your family crest? Picture it in your mind right now. Now tweet to us @wanikaniapp because I really want to know what you came up with!!
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>family crest</kanji> is of a <reading>mon</reading>ster! (もん) And the writing says "Our Family Monster."
MeaningMnemonic: You use <radical>thread</radical> and <radical>writing</radical> to sew our <kanji>family crest</kanji>. You've been sewing your family crest since you were a kid, you could do it today with your eyes closed.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself stopping at the empty bank at noon, grabbing a handful of wholesale stamps. So many stamps. So few extra charges.
ReadingHint: "Oh Roshi" you say carefully as you hand your stamps over at noon. The guy raises an eyebrow at you, but gives you them wholesale! It worked!
ReadingMnemonic: Oh, one more thing. Going at noon isn't enough, you have to say the password to get them at <kanji>wholesale</kanji> price too. The password is "<reading>Oh Roshi</reading>" (おろし). Okay, so maybe that's just the name of the guy there, but it always works when I want to get stamps wholesale so you should do it too!
MeaningMnemonic: At <radical>noon</radical> you <radical>stop</radical> to buy <radical>stamp</radical>s because that's the only time they're being sold at <kanji>wholesale</kanji> price. Everyone's so busy eating and working and being at school, that they miss this awesome deal, but not you. Everyday at noon you stock up on wholesale stamps. Do you send a lot of mail? No, you probably just collect them. But they were cheap, so who cares!

MeaningHint: Picture these column trees outside the gate to the east. You end up drawing them in the columns of your graph paper because they fit so well.
ReadingHint: Now draw Randy Savage in the columns on your graph paper. He just seems to fit there now.
ReadingMnemonic: Out of the tree <kanji>column</kanji>s bursts professional wrestler <reading>Ran</reading>dy (らん) Savage! "Oooh I'm talking about columns that I like mmmm oh yeaah. The Macho Man loves columns MMm OOHH YEAAH~!"
MeaningMnemonic: You see a bunch of <radical>tree</radical>s lined up outside of the <radical>gate</radical> to the <radical>east</radical> and realize they look like perfect <kanji>column</kanji>s. It seems like a building or a castle should be resting above them.

MeaningHint: Imagine excusing yourself by scooting away on a scooter. If they want to get your answer, they'll have to chase you.
ReadingHint: Welp, it's too late to turn back now. You've already deviated pretty far, and you're not even sure where you are now. You were too busy trying to elude that person that you didn't look where your scooter was taking you.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>deviate</kanji> from the plan a little too well, and as you're scooting, what you've done <reading>eats</reading> (いつ) away at you. You feel guilty for eluding them...
MeaningMnemonic: Jump on a <radical>scooter</radical> to <radical>excuse</radical> yourself from answering a question and you've successfully <kanji>deviate</kanji>d from that nosey person's plan! You've also been able to <kanji>elude</kanji> them pretty well.

MeaningHint: Imagine this mustachioed triceratops. He probably has a monocle and a fedora too. Every time you think you can talk or leave he says, "Furthermore" again. Come on guy, how much do you have to say?
ReadingHint: "Furthermore!" the mustachioed triceratops points at the Shougun, "He is the best Shougun we'll ever have. And he'll let me talk forever OH OH OH!"
ReadingMnemonic: Why don't you just interrupt this mustache triceratops while he's saying <kanji>furthermore</kanji>? Because he's the <reading>Shou</reading>gun's (しょう) pet. If you interrupt him you'll probably be put to death or something. Just let him keep going and hope he runs out of furthermores to talk about.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>triceratops</radical> with a <radical>mustache</radical> will go on talking forever. Every time you think he's done he says, "<kanji>Furthermore</kanji>..." and keeps going. Something about a mustache on a dinosaur makes him kind of a jerk, but he's held in such high <kanji>esteem</kanji> that no one ever interrupts him.

MeaningHint: The spikes don't just come out of the geoduck, they literally appear out of thin air. They didn't even <kanji>exist</kanji> before the sun touched them.
ReadingHint: "Thank you sweet geoduck, you have taught me the way of appearing with your spikes."
ReadingMnemonic: You watch these spikes <kanji>appear</kanji> and suddenly <reading>Ken</reading> (けん)the samurai also appears next to you. "I DID IT!" he shouts, "I've learned how to appear out of thin air!"
MeaningMnemonic: When the <radical>sun</radical> comes up the <radical>spikes</radical> of <radical>geoduck</radical>s <kanji>appear</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Putting a stick in some rice is a ritual that Wolverine practices to mark solemn occasions. Shh, listen up! He's probably about to say something solemn and important about his dad.
ReadingHint: Do you think you could shake the solemnness out of someone? Imagine being shooketh so hard, you stop feeling solemn. Wouldn't that be great?
ReadingMnemonic: When you first saw how <kanji>solemn</kanji> Wolverine was after his dad died, it really <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) you. Now you're used to always seeing him emotionally cold and distant. It might be time to shake that solemnity out of him though.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Wolverine</radical> puts a <radical>stick</radical> between his <radical>legs</radical> and straight into some <radical>rice</radical>, all while wearing a <kanji>solemn</kanji> expression on his face. He's probably remembering his dad who died from getting his legs stuck in rice. Just leave the poor solemn creature alone.

MeaningHint: You were foolish to give your heart away to Splinter. He didn't want it. Also he's a rat, what's wrong with you?
ReadingHint: What will you do with your foolish goo heart now?
ReadingMnemonic: Splinter tells you to your face how <kanji>foolish</kanji> you are and you feel your heart turn to <reading>goo</reading> (ぐ). Now you can't even give it to someone else. No one wants goo.
MeaningMnemonic: If you give <radical>Splinter</radical> your <radical>heart</radical> you'll feel pretty <kanji>foolish</kanji> afterwards. His heart belongs to the art of ninjutsu!

MeaningHint: Wait, you're telling me you didn't know it's common to boil things in yurts? Excuse me?
ReadingHint: Okay, now that I've shown you what to do, you can fit in with the common people here.
ReadingMnemonic: Here, the <kanji>common</kanji> people all do this, so just let me <reading>show</reading> (しょ) you how. First you go into your yurt, and… Hey, pay attention and watch what I'm showing you!
MeaningMnemonic: Who goes into their <radical>yurt</radical> to <radical>boil</radical> things? The <kanji>common</kanji> people! <kanji>All sorts</kanji> of people do it, it's really common. You don't boil things outside, do you?

MeaningHint: Are you the kind to practice flirting with the bartender, or are you more the kind to engage in a bit of small talk with the other patrons? Maybe you're just trying to practice patience while the bartender is talking instead of getting your order ready.
ReadingHint: Picture your centaur foot on the bar as you practice to become a gymnast. It's going to take years and years of this kind of practice, but you're sure you'll make it all the way up there some day! Never give up!
ReadingMnemonic: This type of <kanji>practice</kanji> is especially hard for you because you're a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). You only have hooves for feet, and balancing on a bar is going to be a huge hurdle. But your teacher promised if you practiced enough you could make it, and here you are! With your hoofed foot on the bar!
MeaningMnemonic: Whenever you set <radical>foot</radical> in a <radical>bar</radical>, there's always something new to <kanji>practice</kanji>. If you usually drink a lot, you might want to practice moderation for example. Or perhaps you simply want to brush up on your social skills. Either way, plenty of opportunities for practice.

MeaningHint: Imagine the Mouth King giving a presentation about the importance of mouths. His subjects all flatter him by saying, "Please, please, present to us about mouths again! We truuuuuuly love it!"
ReadingHint: You pour a bucket of tater tots into his mouth body and he continues his presentation about mouths. "You see, mouths are just orifices..."
ReadingMnemonic: "I will <kanji>present</kanji> to you a presentation about mouths, but I require energy. Please fill my giant mouth body with delicious <reading>ta</reading>ter (てい) tots.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Mouth</radical> <radical>King</radical>, who is just one big mouth, has one skill, and that is to <kanji>present</kanji>. That big mouth is always presenting something.

MeaningHint: Picture this sparse, neglected coat rack. All of its arms and the coats that were on it have fallen off and are in a big pile on the ground.
ReadingHint: If you neglect something for too long, not even a good soap scrub will bring it back. That goes for humans too. Take care of yourselves. Use soap.
ReadingMnemonic: You go up to the <kanji>neglect</kanji>ed coat rack and try to make things right by cleaning it with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ). But no matter how much you scrub, it won't get clean, the arms won't go back on.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>coat rack</radical> will turn into a <radical>bundle</radical> of sticks if you <kanji>neglect</kanji> it. When you neglect a coat rack, the weight from the coats will pull all of the arms off, leaving it as a <kanji>sparse</kanji> stick.

MeaningHint: Picture this rapid sickness spreading through your body like a cartoon arrow ricocheting through your body. Yuck!
ReadingHint: Imagine getting sick this rapidly. All of a sudden, there's a shih tzu barking in your head, it hurts! And... oh no, where's the toilet?!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>rapid</kanji> sickness makes your head pound like there's a <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ) yapping and barking nonstop. And it's making you sh*t a lot.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>sick</radical>ness that hits you like an <radical>arrow</radical> will do its gross work <kanji>rapidly</kanji>. It's not a good thing, you're gonna get really sick really fast.

MeaningHint: Say whatever you want and send it up to the satellite. Now imagine listening to it when it comes back and hearing beautiful, deep Noh chanting back!
ReadingHint: Watch closely the next time you go to a Noh play. See the man Noh chanting? Watch close enough and you'll see him eat a scoop of yogurt every once in a while.
ReadingMnemonic: While you're fake <kanji>Noh chanting</kanji> you have to pause every few minutes to take a bite of <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう). Yogurt is good for the throat! At least you think it is. It's not like you're actually chanting, the satellite is doing all the real work.
MeaningMnemonic: Whatever you <radical>say</radical> through a <radical>satellite</radical> turns into <kanji>Noh chanting</kanji>. It gets all drawn out and distorted from going through the satellite, which makes it SOUND like Noh chanting at least.

MeaningHint: Imagine wielding your gold Mona Lisa sickle. It's sooo heavy, but it's really shiny and it cuts grass and wheat pretty well!
ReadingHint: Hear all the guests say, "Mona Lisa... comma the sickle? WHAT'S HAPPENED HERE?!" As panic ensues. You like your addition and you're very proud.
ReadingMnemonic: Now that you've turned the Mona Lisa into a <kanji>sickle</kanji>, you have to change the little description under its name in the Louvre. You draw a giant <reading>comma</reading> (かま) next to "Mona Lisa" and write "THE SICKLE." Then you shove it back on the wall as best you can.
MeaningMnemonic: You cast the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical> in <radical>gold</radical> and attach it to a stick to turn it into a <kanji>sickle</kanji>! It's really big and bulky, but when you swing it fast enough your new sickle cuts lots of wheat! Just don't tell anyone what you did with the famous Mona Lisa painting and you should be able to get away with this!

MeaningHint: Imagine if your country's leader announced that there would be no more alcohol. People would be in the streets in seconds calling them cruel and unjust!
ReadingHint: Imagine drinking coke while complaining how unjust it is that you can't add rum to it.
ReadingMnemonic: After this <kanji>cruel</kanji> announcement is made everyone will turn to the next best thing after alcohol. <reading>Coke</reading> (こく)! It's not a replacement, but if this unjust law won't change, then coke sales are going to go through the roof.
MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>announce</radical> that <radical>alcohol</radical> is illegal you're just being <kanji>cruel</kanji>. Honestly, saying no one can have any alcohol at all is totally <kanji>unjust</kanji>. They tried that in the 1920s and it didn't work then either, so cut it out! Let people drink what they want to drink!

MeaningHint: You have a surplus (extra) stool, and someone wants it. But, they need to know more about it, so you describe it in great detail. Imagine yourself doing just that. Every screw and nubbin is described.
ReadingHint: Jo-Anne is really excited about this stool. It's the perfect height for her (remember, she's on the shorter side to distinguish じょ from じょう). She pays attention to you describing the stool while sitting on it and spinning around. Have her ask for you to describe it more.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>describe</kanji> the features of the stool to <reading>Jo</reading>-Anne (じょ).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>surplus</radical> <radical>stool</radical>, please <kanji>describe</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: Picture all the things you also need. A suit, a tie, a monocle, some shiny shoes. There's also ... something... else. What is it?
ReadingHint: Think about all the things you also need when you wear a top hat. The car is probably the most important addition. Otherwise no one can see the top hat in the first place!
ReadingMnemonic: You have a top hat and all the clothes that go with it but you <kanji>also</kanji> need a nice <reading>ca</reading>r (か) too! You can't just get all dressed up and then have no way to get anywhere to show it off.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to wear a <radical>top hat</radical> you <kanji>also</kanji> have to wear a suit to go with it. You can't just wear a top hat, that just doesn't look right. When you think top hat, think about what you also need with it.

MeaningHint: How stupid do you have to be to put arrows into your mouth until it makes you sick? Pretty darn stupid, it seems.
ReadingHint: Imagine if everyone knew that cheese cures the stupid out of people. Then maybe we would have more cheese and less stupid in the world!
ReadingMnemonic: The only way to cure this serious case of <kanji>stupid</kanji> is to make you eat <reading>chee</reading>se (ち). Not only will it fix your sick stomach by cushioning all the arrows that you shoved in your stupid mouth, but it should send smart cheese waves to your brain and cure you of that stupid all together.
MeaningMnemonic: You're <radical>sick</radical> because you put an <radical>arrow</radical> in your <radical>mouth</radical> and tried to eat it. To be honest, that's just really <kanji>stupid</kanji>. You have to be stupid to try to eat an arrow. You deserve to be sick.

MeaningHint: Imagine being in a graveyard looking at all the tombstones and seeing nothing but stems on all of them. All the pretty flowery parts have died and blown away.
ReadingHint: Look at all the cookie covered tombstones. Now your bag is full of stems, but you can use them to make more cookies to bring back next time. Don't worry, nobody eats them. Stem cookies are probably poisonous.
ReadingMnemonic: You have an idea that can make these tombstones look better than just having leftover <kanji>stem</kanji>s all over them. You brought <reading>cookie</reading>s (くき)! Cookies don't fall apart in the wind, and everyone loves cookies, alive or dead. You replace the stems with the cookies and everything looks a lot nicer.
MeaningMnemonic: When you leave <radical>flowers</radical> on a <radical>tombstone</radical> they look good at first, but after a few days or some harsh weather, there's nothing left but <kanji>stem</kanji>s. That's why you have to replace flowers on tombstones all the time. If you don't it will look like you only left the stems there, and that's pretty disrespectful.

MeaningHint: Flattering buildings is just as nice as flattering people, as long as they're okay with it.
ReadingHint: The "ah" the building made was actually quite loud, and since you've never heard a building speak before, it scared you pretty bad. But at least you know the building was flattered by your compliment (I think.)
ReadingMnemonic: The building is <kanji>flatter</kanji>ed and you hear it shout, "<reading>Ah</reading> (あ)!" and blush. The entire building turns red.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>building</radical> with a <radical>lip ring</radical> and it's just so cute you have to <kanji>flatter</kanji> it. "That lip ring looks wonderful on you, Buildingさん," you say.
