MeaningHint: You fall to your knees, "Oh thank you for not hitting me with your stick and letting me relax!" You haven't had a moment of leisure in ages!
ReadingHint: As the leader leisurely watches, the youths smack you with their sticks. What a cruel world! "Enjoy your leisure time," the leader yells over, "the youths hit much softer than I do. Have fun!"
ReadingMnemonic: You're just about to start your <kanji>leisure</kanji> time when a group of <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) appears with their own sticks. You look back for the leader, and he's wearing a bathrobe, sunglasses, and is drinking some hot cocoa. Wait... this is leisure time for... HIM?! OH NO!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> who usually hits you with a <radical>stick</radical> in <radical>winter</radical> finds it in his <radical>heart</radical> to give you some <kanji>leisure</kanji> time.

MeaningHint: Picture the mouth sized apricots hanging down off the tree. Imagine using your own mouth to bite them down. Just don't swallow the pit!
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to eat the bite sized apricot and spitting it out after the taste of anchovies from the zoo fill you up.
ReadingMnemonic: After you put the <kanji>apricot</kanji> in your mouth you realize that this tastes really familiar, but not pleasant. It tastes just like the <reading>an</reading>chovies (あん) from the <reading>zoo</reading> (ず) smell. How did that happen? You don't like apricots anymore.
MeaningMnemonic: What does a <radical>tree</radical> make that you'd want to put in your <radical>mouth</radical>? An <kanji>apricot</kanji>! Apricots are also about the size of an open mouth, so they kind of look like mouths hanging down under the trees. You can bite them right off the tree with your own mouth!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself sitting in the west as the red sun sets, smashing those little rose madder roots into a paste to make your deep red dyes. Your fingers turn red from the rose madder too.
ReadingHint: Hear the little girl say あかね while pointing at the rose madder and your deep red fingers. She's right. And the way she says it is so darn cute.
ReadingMnemonic: As you smash the roots of the <kanji>rose madder</kanji> and stain your fingers a <kanji>deep red</kanji>, a little girl comes up to you and points. She says, "<reading>赤ね</reading> (あかね)?" (That's red, right?) You look down at the rose madder roots and your fingers, and nod. She's right, it is red. Deep red.
MeaningMnemonic: What kind of <radical>flowers</radical> grow in the <radical>west</radical>? <kanji>Rose madder</kanji> flowers, of course. Don't know what those are? That's okay. Just know their roots are used to make dyes of a <kanji>deep red</kanji> color. Red like the rose in their name, but madder, wilder, deeper… And they grow in the west so they can take in those deep red colors of the setting sun.

MeaningHint: To be fair, books are just dry trees in a way, but bookmarks are actually dry, dry trees that have flattened out and turned into chunks.
ReadingHint: What does she owe you? Open the page to your bookmark and see she owes reeds!
ReadingMnemonic: What are you bookmarking anyway? Look down and see yourself putting one of the tree <kanji>bookmark</kanji>s on a page in your notebook that says "<reading>She owe</reading>s me <reading>ree</reading>ds (しおり)." Oh yeah, you had to make this bookmark in the first place because your friend took your reed bookmarks and didn't give them back.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Dry</radical> an already <radical>dry</radical> <radical>tree</radical> to create a <kanji>bookmark</kanji>. The more you dry the tree, the flatter it becomes, making it the perfect way to mark your spot in a book without needing to fold a page or write on it.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself bowing to the leader dog. Leader dog is so cool!
ReadingHint: Bow even deeper as the dog leader keeps barking what sounds like f**k you. You're pretty sure that's what he's saying at least... but maybe... maybe it's not.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>bow</kanji> as low as you can, but you lose your balance and fall over. "<reading>F**k</reading> you (ふく)!" the leader dog barks. Oh no. You messed up and now the leader dog is super mad. Saying f**k you is a little harsh for falling over though, you think.
MeaningMnemonic: When you meet the <radical>leader</radical> <radical>dog</radical> you have to <kanji>bow</kanji> to show your respect. Dogs are pretty great, so the leader of all dogs must be the greatest! You should bow really deeply to this one.

MeaningHint: Imagine being tranquilized by the sweet caress of gold coins. You can't even move. You're in a daze or something.
ReadingHint: If you let gold tranquilize you, you'll drool all over your own chin. Maybe the chins of other people too, who knows?
ReadingMnemonic: You've been <kanji>tranquilize</kanji>d and now drool is running down your <reading>chin</reading> (ちん). It's actually really gross. But you don't care, look at all this gold! No one will care about your chin when you're rich!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>reality</radical> of <radical>gold</radical> is that it has the power to <kanji>tranquilize</kanji> anyone who gets their hands on it. Gold may seem cool to have in movies and songs, but in reality, it has the power to mess you up!

MeaningHint: You know how you get. When you can't go outside, you dedicate yourself full force into something else. I mean, you made it to level 55, right? That's some serious dedication. And it probably started because of one particularly dry spring.
ReadingHint:
Dedicate yourself to doing things in your home! Haha, who are we kidding, you already did that.

We love you, nerd. ♡
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to <kanji>dedicate</kanji> yourself to your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). You don't want to be outside in the dry spring air, so you focus on home activities. You can clean the kitchen, build a shelf, or dedicate your time to your home internet system.
MeaningMnemonic: When <radical>spring</radical> is <radical>dry</radical> you need to <kanji>dedicate</kanji> your time and effort somewhere else. You can't plant flowers or vegetables, it's too dry. You can't play outside, your skin will flake. So you need to dedicate yourself to something inside.

MeaningHint: Imagine if winter was so brutal that there were only one hundred human foreheads left. You lost all of your friends and family. Feel that grief.
ReadingHint: Grief for youths is so painful. No one should have to bury their forehead child.
ReadingMnemonic: Their <kanji>grief</kanji> intensifies because all of the foreheads they lost belonged to <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう). Dead youths everywhere. Now there's no one left to repopulate the world.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>hundred</radical> <radical>forehead</radical>s lower themselves to their <radical>heart</radical>s in <radical>winter</radical> in <kanji>grief</kanji>. There were once many of their kind, the forehead peoples, but now there are only a hundred left after this harsh winter took the rest of them. Their grief is immense.

MeaningHint: Imagine carving a really simple, crude toe from a tree. It's your first attempt, so I guess you should have known it wouldn't be great.
ReadingHint: Who carved this simple toe? ぼく!
ReadingMnemonic: You're admiring your <kanji>simple</kanji> looking toe tree when a lumberjack runs up and asks who created it. <reading>ぼく</reading>, you say. As in "I" said it, in a manly dude, way. He doesn't seem to believe you, so you say it a few more times. ぼく ぼく ぼく!
MeaningMnemonic: You decide to carve a <radical>tree</radical> into a giant replica of your big <radical>toe</radical> because you think it will be really <kanji>simple</kanji>. Once you're done though, it looks like a really <kanji>crude</kanji> attempt at a fat, lumpy blob. Maybe carving isn't as simple as you thought.

MeaningHint: Thank you soul, for being frugal so that I can have so much now.
ReadingHint: If you want your spouse or S.O. to think you're more sexy, just be more frugal. They'll definitely appreciate it and see you in a different light.
ReadingMnemonic: You know what being <kanji>frugal</kanji> is? <reading>Sexy</reading> (せき). You save money, you use less resources, that's the sexiest thing I can think of.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>soul</radical> <radical>long ago</radical> was very <kanji>frugal</kanji>, which is why you get to have too much in this life!

MeaningHint: Imagine being so excellent at leading, that you get double the dirt that everyone else gets.
ReadingHint: Imagine your own car. Is it as excellent as you?
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>excellent</kanji> leader is going to use all this dirt to store her excellent <reading>ca</reading>rs (か)! Excellent people have to have cars in equal or greater than excellent-ness than themselves.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> gets double the <radical>dirt</radical> to build on if he's the most <kanji>excellent</kanji> and most <kanji>skilled</kanji> of all the leaders. You don't think dirt is so great? Well what if I took the dirt you own away from you? Your house is on that dirt. It's mine now. Get outta here.

MeaningHint: Look at your toe. It has no soul. It's dark and shriveled, like a mummy's toe. Grieve and watch your tears drip onto it.
ReadingHint: Walk through the streets of とうきょう grieving, with your dead toe flopping onto the pavement.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to <kanji>grieve</kanji> in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. Your toe always wanted to walk in とうきょう, and now you can at least fulfill its dying wish.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> in your <radical>toe</radical> leaves <radical>early</radical>. You <kanji>grieve</kanji> and <kanji>mourn</kanji> for your now-dead toe.

MeaningHint: Imagine actually slicing the words you say in the air. The above section is what you want people to pay attention to, so you slice a big gap between the above and the below words.
ReadingHint: That guy doesn't care if the above is all he's supposed to hear. That guy sucks.
ReadingMnemonic: As you focus on <kanji>the above</kanji> words you realize a <reading>guy</reading> (がい) is trying to push the rest of the things you're saying back up with the rest of them. No! The above text is all that matters! Go away guy!
MeaningMnemonic: You want to <radical>slice</radical> what you <radical>say</radical> in half so that people focus on <kanji>the above</kanji> text first. The above is what you want them to see! So slice what you say!

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to proceed down the road by running on your toes. No other part of your foot can touch the ground!
ReadingHint: Imagine being called a fool when you can't proceed along the road because running on your toes ends up being super hard to do.
ReadingMnemonic: If you find that you can't <kanji>proceed</kanji>, you're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). Everyone can run on their toes. Don't you know that? No? Then you're an even bigger fool, now get outta here!
MeaningMnemonic: You need to <radical>run</radical> on your <radical>toe</radical>s if you want to <kanji>proceed</kanji> down this road. If you can't run on your toes, well then you're out of luck, and you won't be able to <kanji>move on</kanji> at all. I didn't make this rule, I just enforce it. Now run!

MeaningHint: If you think about it, bone marrow is really just a road for tiny blood scooters.
ReadingHint: That's how doctors can tell if there's something wrong with your bone marrow. If they can't hear the zuuwee of the scooters, you're gonna die soon. I mean, maybe, I'm not a doctor.
ReadingMnemonic: If you put your ear really close to a bone with <kanji>marrow</kanji> still in it, you'll hear the scooters going <reading>zuwee</reading> (ずい) as they move the blood around inside there.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>bone</radical>s <radical>have</radical> a <radical>scooter</radical> that moves through the <kanji>bone marrow</kanji> inside them.

MeaningHint: Picture the leader very close to your side. He's looking off into the distance, a distance he'll never get to because all he can do is stand there with his messed up feet.
ReadingHint: Picture the leader bowling nearby. You keep him close by, you never know when he'll fall over and need your help.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to bring the leader to a <kanji>nearby</kanji> <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) alley. It's really close and you can stay right by his side as he throws bowling balls and gets his anger out.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> is <radical>stand</radical>ing funny, facing a <radical>direction</radical> <kanji>nearby</kanji>. He can't go far because there's obviously something wrong with his feet. He's stuck nearby, very close to your side.

MeaningHint: Just imagine how much thread you'll accumulate if you can just grow it along with your rice in the rice paddy! So much thread!
ReadingHint: Your attempts to accumulate more of something by cheating have brought ruin to everything else you were doing. Nice job, dummy.
ReadingMnemonic: Sadly, this is a terrible way to <kanji>accumulate</kanji> anything thread related and you ended up <reading>rui</reading>ning (るい) your rice paddy too. I guess if you want to accumulate thread this isn't the way to do it.
MeaningMnemonic: You start growing <radical>thread</radical> in your <radical>rice paddy</radical> to <kanji>accumulate</kanji> more. You didn't want to have to buy any, so you're just trying to grow thread with your rice. That seems like a good way to accumulate more to you.

MeaningHint: Maybe the Death Star wouldn't be sick if it wasn't so mean to you, yelling at you for living in a yurt, when it was a yurt on the inside the whole time. Wait! I know the cure!
ReadingHint: Hmm, but does this type of yurt healing technology exist?! You'll have to find out to cure it!!
ReadingMnemonic: You realized that the only way to <kanji>cure</kanji> the Death Star is to turn it back into a <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ). Then it won't be able to do any more harm, and its outside will reflect its pleasant self on the inside!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sick</radical> <radical>Death Star</radical> needs its <radical>heart</radical> <kanji>cure</kanji>d. If it doesn't get <kanji>healing</kanji> soon, it will die.

MeaningHint: Imagine the building you just received. You want to protect it, so you get started on that enclosure. Imagine building your enclosure with your bare hands.
ReadingHint: Your pet cock is the perfect addition to your new enclosure. Just look at it, pecking happily between the enclosure and that building you received.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>enclosure</kanji> felt a  bit quiet and lonely, so you decided to get yourself a pet <reading>cock</reading> (かく).
MeaningMnemonic: You just <radical>receive</radical>d a <radical>building</radical>, so you want to surround it with an <kanji>enclosure</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine swimming over to the flag that's sticking out of the water and peeing there. Yeah, everyone knows what you're doing over there, but it's better than getting out and walking to the bathroom right? Just pee in the water. Just do it.
ReadingHint: Imagine your embarrassment when you find out that you aren't supposed to urinate in the water, not even by the pee flag, because a life guard yelled NYOOOOOO and pointed right at you while you did it.
ReadingMnemonic: Right as you start to <kanji>pee</kanji>, you hear the life guard start to yell, "<reading>NYOOOOOO</reading>! (にょう)" and he's pointing at you! You were just peeing by the pee flag! Why is he mad?
MeaningMnemonic: When you go to the pool or lake to swim, you'll see a <radical>flag</radical> stuck somewhere in the <radical>water</radical>. That flag is there so you know it's okay to <kanji>pee</kanji> in that area of the pool. Everyone knows <kanji>urine</kanji> dilutes in water, and if everyone just pees in one place, it won't be gross. So go pee over by that flag when you have to go. Don't bother getting out of the water.

MeaningHint: The VIPs came into our home (the place between our roof and the ground) where they consumed all our shellfish. Only the best for guests, I guess.
ReadingHint: We are treating these VIP guests extra special because they represent the whole Hindu religion. They get shellfish because of course we weren't going to serve them beef!
ReadingMnemonic: These <kanji>VIP</kanji> <kanji>guest</kanji>s are part of a <reading>Hin</reading>du (ひん) envoy.
MeaningMnemonic: Between the <radical>roof</radical> and the <radical>ground</radical> there are <radical>few</radical> <radical>shellfish</radical>. This is because we invited some <kanji>VIP</kanji> <kanji>guest</kanji>s into our home and they ate all our shellfish.

MeaningHint: Picture the sad tiger captive and the man laughing as he pokes at it. Tigers shouldn't be captives, but when men find them they always seem to do this anyway.
ReadingHint: Now picture this tiger captive in Rio. He's stuck in a cage at home while the man yucks it up along Rio's beaches, bragging to all the locals about his captive tiger.
ReadingMnemonic: The man takes his new tiger <kanji>captive</kanji> to <reading>Rio</reading> (りょ). Why Rio de Janeiro? Turns out you're allowed to keep tigers captive down in Rio.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>tiger</radical> meets a <radical>man</radical>, it becomes the man's <kanji>captive</kanji>. Men can be pretty mean like that. They see a tiger and they want to put it in a cage, so the poor tiger becomes the man's captive.

MeaningHint: If you died right now and left your soul behind, do you think you'd have regrets?
ReadingHint: Picture Genghis Khan looking down at the state of his people, the Mongols, in the modern world. See the remorse in his soul's face. They should be leaders of the modern world. Not reduced to a people struggling in the background.
ReadingMnemonic: The soul with the most <kanji>regret</kanji>s is Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん). Everyone knows to stay away from him in the spirit world because he's so sad. Not because of his own actions, but because of the state of his people now.
MeaningMnemonic: The only <radical>feeling</radical> a dead <radical>soul</radical> will have is <kanji>remorse</kanji>. Remorse for the people it left behind. <kanji>Regret</kanji>s for the things it did and didn't do.

MeaningHint: Imagine increasing your power with a new weapon. What would it be?
ReadingHint: Imagine walking down a line of weapons, each one increasing in power from the one before it. Bow, small gun, bigger gun, tank, Yankee's bat. Yup, that sounds right.
ReadingMnemonic: You keep trading in weapons to get <kanji>increasing</kanji>ly stronger gear, when you realize that at the top of the list is... a <reading>Ya</reading>nkee's (や) baseball bat? Huh. I would have gone for a tank or a bazooka. But I guess if this will increase your power level, you have to go for it.
MeaningMnemonic: Go from a <radical>bow</radical> to a <radical>small</radical> <radical>gun</radical> and you're <kanji>increasing</kanji> in fire power! Even though the gun is small, it's still a step up from a bow.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to eat the rice you found in a top hat. It's so coarse it feels like it's scratching your teeth. There's probably some dirt in here too. This wasn't the best idea.
ReadingHint: If you were trying to make this coarse rice taste like soap, you did it. But if you were trying to make it less coarse, well... sorry, but that's not how anything works.
ReadingMnemonic: You take the <kanji>coarse</kanji> rice and try to wash it with <reading>so</reading>ap (そ).
MeaningMnemonic: If you find some <radical>rice</radical> in a <radical>top hat</radical> it's going to be really <kanji>coarse</kanji> and <kanji>rough</kanji>. Probably because it's not the kind that's meant to be eaten.

MeaningHint: Imagine this strange circulation of you and the pope up here on a cliff. It's not even like you two are up here for a reason, you're just loitering. But he doesn't want to be hot so he makes you circulate with him.
ReadingHint: "This circulation is all June's fault," you mutter, walking in a circle with the pope to stay cool.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>circulation</kanji> wouldn't have to even be happening if it wasn't <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). The cliffs are so hot in June, and in any other month the two of you would have just sat down and had a chat. But in June you have to do this weird circulation dance.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>loiter</radical> on a <radical>cliff</radical> with the <radical>pope</radical> you better be ready to move in perfect <kanji>circulation</kanji> with him. The pope is wearing really heavy robes and it's hot up here on this cliff, so you both need to walk in circles to keep cool.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking over water in an ice cube tray. You aren't sure if they'll turn out alright, and you doubt whether ice is even a thing. As you think this, the water congeals and freezes into gross shapes.
ReadingHint: Picture frozen, congealed gyoza. Do you want to eat that? I didn't think so.
ReadingMnemonic: You always end up <kanji>congeal</kanji>ing your <reading>gyo</reading>za (ぎょう) this way. You want to <kanji>freeze</kanji> your leftover gyoza so you can enjoy it later, but you doubt it will turn out all right and it always congeals in a gross, half-frozen gyoza block. Yuck!
MeaningMnemonic: If you try to turn something into <radical>ice</radical> while having <radical>doubt</radical>s, it will just <kanji>congeal</kanji> instead. It will <kanji>freeze</kanji>, but it will get kinda yucky too. Your doubts will affect the ice-making process, so try not to be so worried and unsure next time you want normal ice.

MeaningHint: Picture the prosperous planet that has two suns. They get so much work done that no one is ever hungry or tired or without.
ReadingHint: Picture the Shougun looking over the prosperous world, the two suns at his back.
ReadingMnemonic: The person on this planet with two suns who benefits from the prosperity the most is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). Yes, this planet still has a shougun as their leader, but everything is going so well that they didn't see a need to change. The shogun will be in charge of this prosperous world forever. No one ever thinks of revolt when everyone has everything they could ever need.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>sun</radical> is the reason why crops grow and people can get out of bed in the morning. But on planets with two <radical>sun</radical>s, they can get even more done and it makes them way more <kanji>prosperous</kanji> than us. They work all day, then as the first sun sets, the second one rises and they get a new burst of energy. The whole planet lives in a state of <kanji>prosperity</kanji> because of the two suns.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine getting a tan at dawn. Ahh. Relaxing and warm.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Dawn</kanji> is <reading>dawn</reading> (だん)! It's also a great time to get a <reading>tan</reading> (たん). The sun is bright at dawn, but not so much that it will send skin cancer rays into you.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>dawn</radical> radical and the <kanji>dawn</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Picture the Death Star in your mind. As you picture it, its pleasant, smiling soul floats up and out of it. "Oh hello, dear," it says happily, "Didn't see you there!"
ReadingHint: Yurts are very pleasant structures. They're round and soft and moveable, just like the Death Star's soul.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>pleasant</kanji> soul within the Death Star actually looks like a <reading>yu</reading>rt (ゆ), not a big angry, metal ball.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>soul</radical> of the <radical>Death Star</radical> is actually quite <kanji>pleasant</kanji>. And if you think about it, it's the people who control the Death Star that are evil and kill.

MeaningHint: Imagine your fingers slowly getting closer and closer to the jet's engine. It feels like it's going to suck your whole body in. And you better watch out, because if it does you'll be erased from the planet. And probably from time itself.
ReadingHint: Now you're so thankful to the mats. If they weren't there you might have erased your whole body! Thank you mats! I'll never walk on you without thanking you for your service again!
ReadingMnemonic: You're almost done <kanji>eras</kanji>ing your fingers when the jet starts sucking all of you in. You would try to grab the closest thing to you, but your fingers don't exist anymore, so you hold onto the <reading>mats</reading> (まつ) at your feet with your toes. The mats are super strong (they're made for jets, after all) and you manage to save yourself!
MeaningMnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> in a <radical>jet</radical> if you want to <kanji>erase</kanji> them from existence. You aren't just chopping or burning them off. There's like, no trace of them at all. Your parents can't even remember if you were born with fingers or not. Jets are that powerful.

MeaningHint: Picture how silly the king looks with this cork on his head. He kind of looks like a fat, plugged up bottle of wine when he yells now.
ReadingHint: Picture the centaurs drinking and giggling, putting the cork from their wine on their head to imitate the stupid king.
ReadingMnemonic: It turned out this <kanji>cork</kanji> hat was given to him by a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). You look into it and it turned out it IS a really big cork that centaurs use on their big wine casks. But the king didn't understand, and thought it was a hat. The centaurs are probably still laughing.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> has a <radical>hat</radical> that's made from a <radical>tree</radical> but the more you look at it, the more it looks like it's just a big <kanji>cork</kanji>. It's a big, wooden <kanji>plug</kanji> that he's just placed on his head. It can hardly be called a hat.

MeaningHint: As you look at the drop of water, you note that you found the right thing. Written on it is the word “this."
ReadingHint: You’re always collecting drops of water in the hills. It’s your hobby, in fact. But this one is the first one you’ve ever collected with “this” written on it. What a great addition to your drop collection.
ReadingMnemonic: You <reading>colle</reading>ct(これ) <kanji>this</kanji> very carefully. You’re going to add this to your drop collection.
MeaningMnemonic: How do you make a mnemonic for the word this? You don’t. But, we can try. You climb many <radical>hills</radical>. On top of one of the hills is a little <radical>drop</radical> of water. You pick it up and say “<kanji>THIS</kanji>… THIS IS WHAT I WAS LOOKING FOR!”

MeaningHint: Your scooter is charcoal powered, but it looks like you put too much in there and it drove off somewhere distant without you. If you look really carefully, you can just see it, a distant speck on the horizon.
ReadingHint: You can still make out the distant shape of your scooter, now on a row boat, fading away and completely out of reach.
ReadingMnemonic: You chase the scooter to that <kanji>distant</kanji> place, but then you see it heading for a lake. Oh good, it should be stopped by the water. But wait! In the distance you see your scooter drive onto a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat and... row itself through the water?! No!!
MeaningMnemonic: You got off your <radical>scooter</radical> to load some <radical>charcoal</radical> onto it, when it suddenly took off to a <kanji>distant</kanji> place! That's some powerful charcoal.

MeaningHint: Picture the king holding this crystal aloft, giggling as the sun shines through it and sparkles.
ReadingHint: Imagine going to England, to the giant acorn in the center of the flowers, and searching for the king's crystal. If you find it, you can probably sell it for a fortune! Or just make a really sweet necklace.
ReadingMnemonic: The king thinks everyone wants his <kanji>crystal</kanji> so he hides it in the giant <reading>a</reading>corn (えい) in the center of the flowers in England. There's a tiny hole in the bottom of the acorn, and that's where this crystal stays hidden.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> of the <radical>flowers</radical> in the <radical>center</radical> (of England, if you remember) has a special <kanji>crystal</kanji>. The king crystal.

MeaningHint: What? Do you think I'm gonna help you make this decision? No way, you need to dig deep and think about what you want to do.
ReadingHint: What?! You got nachos either way. You should be happy, don't ask me any other questions.
ReadingMnemonic:
A. <kanji>What</kanji> is, stab the building on the left? You stab the building on the left with your sword and what comes out is... a plate of delicious <reading>na</reading>chos (な)!

B. <kanji>What</kanji> is, stab the building on the right? You stab the building on the right with your sword and what comes out is... a plate of delicious <reading>na</reading>chos (な)!
MeaningMnemonic:
You're holding a <radical>sword</radical> in front of <radical>two</radical> <radical>building</radical>s. <kanji>What</kanji> will you do?

A. Stab the building on the left.

B. Stab the building on the right.

What do you choose?

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself in a completely white room. All you have is the white walls, your fingers, and your beats. When you stop all you're left with is white noise. Yuck! Keep those beats coming!
ReadingHint: Imagine hacking the wall apart as you try to keep this beat going. The music is all you have, man! You can't lose the beat now!
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>beat</kanji> is sick, but as you tap you realize you're <reading>hack</reading>ing (はく) up the wall! Slowly but surely, your fingers are hacking out little bits of the wall. And the beats don't sound as good until you move to a new, clean white area.
MeaningMnemonic: You're tapping your <radical>fingers</radical> against a <radical>white</radical> wall when you realize the sound you're making is actually a sick <kanji>beat</kanji>. You keep going and start singing along. Boo boo boop. Boo do do do doop! Finger beats!

MeaningHint: You keep checking outside to see if they have stopped, but they are still drinking. They haven't stopped yet.
ReadingHint: You keep checking, but that youth is still drinking. Of course, you don't know they are a youth, so it's quite impressive an animal can drink this much.
ReadingMnemonic: The animal with horns that is <kanji>still</kanji> drinking your alcohol is no animal at all. It is actually one of the local <reading>you</reading>ths (ゆう) dressed up like a horned animal so they can drink as long as they want and you'll be too scared to shoo them away.
MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>animal</radical> with <radical>horns</radical> is drinking our <radical>alcohol</radical>? <kanji>Still</kanji>?

MeaningHint: Picture yourself as the manager of a restaurant or a science lab. You have to test all of your managerial methods at home before you can bring them to work. And if you were to lick that roof of yours... it would be spicy from all of your experiments.
ReadingHint: Unfortunately, you couldn't figure out how to make non-spicy cyborgs, but that's okay because you're going to be the best cyborg manager ever! No one will ever take them away from you again.
ReadingMnemonic: You've finally done it. You're now the <kanji>manager</kanji> of a bunch of spicy new <reading>cy</reading>borgs (さい). You've been toiling away for over a year, but you've finally replaced the cyborg you lost, and now you can manage them properly without anyone getting in the way.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>roof</radical> is <radical>spicy</radical> because you're the <kanji>manager</kanji>. You're always testing new recipes in your house to share with everyone who works under you, but that makes all the spice stick to your roof (since it doesn't have good ventilation like a restaurant or a lab would).

MeaningHint: Imagine sitting on this small stool, waaaaay up there above the roof. You can't see anyone and you can't talk to anyone. You feel this awful emptiness inside. That's what it's like to be lonely.
ReadingHint: You're so busy talking to Jack, you don't hear the voices from below, "Aren't you lonely up there, miss? Do you want us to get a jack to get you down? Hello? I don't think she can hear us..."
ReadingMnemonic: You're so <kanji>lonely</kanji>, you make up an imaginary friend: <reading>Jack</reading> (じゃく). Jack doesn't get rid of the lonely feeling because he isn't real, but he is a distraction at least.
MeaningMnemonic: On the <radical>roof</radical> <radical>above</radical>, is a <radical>small</radical> <radical>stool</radical>. Anyone who sits in that stool will be <kanji>lonely</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine slowly sewing your scooter to the signpost, passing the thread back and forth through the signpost's metal holes and your scooter's handles.
ReadingHint: Hopefully, if your scooter is still here later you'll be able to start sewing outside of your home more often from now on!
ReadingMnemonic: You usually only <kanji>sew</kanji> at <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), so this is a big exception for you. You're usually worried that if you sew in public people will judge your stitches, so this is a big step for you!
MeaningMnemonic: You take some <radical>thread</radical>, put your <radical>scooter</radical> up to a <radical>signpost</radical>, and <kanji>sew</kanji> them together. You don't have a lock, so sewing your scooter up to the signpost to keep it safe is all you can think of.

MeaningHint: If you want to give someone something, use a catapult!
ReadingHint: You give Godzilla old used cars, chunks of old buildings, and sometimes hamburgers. Godzilla is so happy you're giving him food, he doesn't destroy anymore!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Give</kanji> food to your new friend <reading>Go</reading>dzilla (ご) with the catapult! You and Godzilla understand each other now. He's just hungry.
MeaningMnemonic: You've learned that you can <radical>catapult</radical> things to <kanji>give</kanji> them to other people! Don't catapult rocks at buildings and walls, catapult gifts to the people you love.

MeaningHint: You didn't expect to see water drop damage on this table so now your mood is mediocre too.
ReadingHint: Imagine having a bone to pick with someone because they're trying to sell you something mediocre and they just won't listen. Now you have a bone to pick with the person who recommended this guy to you!
ReadingMnemonic: You weren't expecting to see such a <kanji>mediocre</kanji> table so you go up to the guy who's selling it and say, "Hey, I have a <reading>bone</reading> (ぼん) to pick with you!" But he doesn't think there is a bone to pick at all, he doesn't think the table is mediocre like you do.
MeaningMnemonic: You go to buy a <radical>table</radical> but it looks like it has water damage from <radical>drop</radical>s of water hitting it. This makes it a pretty <kanji>mediocre</kanji> table. The water drops haven't really damaged it that much, it still stands and you can use it, but it's kinda ugly, so it's just mediocre.

MeaningHint: Imagine being a small drop of water and seeing other drops of water come to help you by working together. You'd really respect them, right?
ReadingHint: Imagine respecting someone so much that you'll cheer ooh yankees over and over even though they're kind of a crappy team.
ReadingMnemonic: If you <kanji>respect</kanji> someone enough you'll even take up their hobbies. The person you respect most loves the Yankees. You don't really feel the same, but you respect that about them so you chant, "<reading>Ooh Ya</reading>nkees, <reading>Ooh Ya</reading>nkees!" (うやうや) with them during each game.
MeaningMnemonic: When <radical>small</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s of water work <radical>together</radical> they gain <kanji>respect</kanji> for one another. If you end up working with someone long enough, and they end up being really great, you'll respect them too!

MeaningHint: You could swear this thing was gold a long time ago. Now it's a mix of different things.
ReadingHint: Since it was in that sack for so long, it just ended up getting mixed with the other stuff in that sack. It was gold a long time ago, but now it's mixed with everything else.
ReadingMnemonic: It's <kanji>mixed</kanji> (and not pure anymore) because the <kanji>confused</kanji> person put it in that <reading>sack</reading> (さく) filled with various other things.
MeaningMnemonic: This was pure <radical>gold</radical> <radical>long ago</radical>, but now it's not. Someone got <kanji>confused</kanji> and <kanji>mixed</kanji> it with something else.

MeaningHint: Picture the samurai taking out his weapon and spinning in a lightning fast circle. The forehead grains pop off and come raining into his open mouth.
ReadingHint: These grains aren't even cooked, they're just hard and raw, so the Coke really helps wash them down.
ReadingMnemonic: Phew, all of this <kanji>grain</kanji> is making the samurai thirsty, so he reaches in his bag and pulls out an ice cold, refreshing <reading>Coke</reading> (こく).
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> hits the <radical>forehead</radical>s of <radical>grain</radical>s with his <radical>weapon</radical> so the <kanji>grain</kanji>s will pop off and into his mouth. He doesn't want to kill the plants, so he's just skimming the grain's foreheads so they'll fall off and he can eat them.

MeaningHint: Unfortunately, you're realizing this because you thought a mausoleum would be a good hiding place from the winter cold. Spoilers, it's not.
ReadingHint: Imagine taking your frostbitten fingers and trying to get away from the mausoleum in this row boat. It looks like it was made of bones and it is not very seaworthy. But you row with all your might, because you don't want to end up a permanent fixture in this tomb!
ReadingMnemonic: You need to escape this <kanji>mausoleum</kanji> now but the only way out is on a <reading>row</reading> (りょう) boat over a creepy frozen pond. You've heard of these mausoleum row boats before, and you're pretty sure they're cursed.
MeaningMnemonic: The only <radical>building</radical> you'll get <radical>frostbite</radical> in is a <kanji>mausoleum</kanji>. Mausoleums and <kanji>tomb</kanji>s weren't made for living people, so they aren't insulated from the cold. No one cares if a dead body gets frostbite.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking around in the arctic when you stumble upon an evil group of penguins. They'd be cute if they weren't holding papers with "Kill All Humans" on them and tiny guns in their penguin hands.
ReadingHint: Only someone or, in this case, something truly evil would kill someone and hide their remains under some hay. That's really disrespectful and, well, evil.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>evil</kanji> penguins can't let you tell the other human's what you've seen, so they murder you and hide your body in a <reading>hay</reading>stack (へい). Your family will never know what happened to you. The penguins will get away with their evil plans.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Twenty</radical> <radical>penguin</radical>s together are always <kanji>evil</kanji>. Penguins are seen in groups a lot, sure, but did you know that any time there are exactly twenty penguins in a group they're plotting evil things? Evil things like global warming. And how to eat all the fish in the world so humans can't have any.

MeaningHint: Boats don't need to be a bright white color. But, ships do! They're dangerous and big, so they paint them nice and white.
ReadingHint: You can also imagine hacking the ship's computer. "Look at me, look at me. You are the hacker now."
ReadingMnemonic: A white-painted <kanji>ship</kanji> is a kind of <reading>hack</reading> (はく). It makes the ship more visible without costing much.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>boat</radical> that's <radical>white</radical> isn't just a boat, it's a <kanji>ship</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a destitute person wandering in the streets with a hole in their body.
ReadingHint: Picture a destitute person, sitting on the street, holding a cucumber. Putting the cucumber in their hole. Crying.
ReadingMnemonic: You see this <kanji>destitute</kanji> person and decide to give them a <reading>cu</reading>cumber (きゅう). They don't even remember that they're supposed to eat those, so they shove it in the hole you made with your bow.
MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>hole</radical> in someone's <radical>body</radical> with a <radical>bow</radical> and they'll become <kanji>destitute</kanji>. You put a hole in them. They can't work. They can't take care of their house or their kids. They've lost it all.

MeaningHint: Imagine guarding yourself from all the bad things in the world. No more politics, religion, internet trolls debating the two, it's all guarded from your precious soul. Ahhh, it's so joyous to be unburdened.
ReadingHint: Picture joy etched on your own face. No one can get you down.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>delight</kanji> you feel is so great that it becomes permanently <reading>etch</reading>ed (えつ) in your soul and even your face.
MeaningMnemonic: If you can <radical>guard</radical> your <radical>soul</radical> from bad things you'll always feel <kanji>delight</kanji> and <kanji>joy</kanji>. It's hard to guard your soul from everything though, so take all the joy you can, while you can.

MeaningHint: Imagine if your specialty was dropping people by binding them with thread. You'd be such a weird person.
ReadingHint: Imagine how you would bind someone so that when they drop they land on their back instead of somewhere more painful. How would you do that?
ReadingMnemonic: If you're going to be a weirdo who likes to <kanji>bind</kanji> people, at least make sure that they land on their <reading>back</reading>s (ばく) when they DO drop. Landing on your head or your boobs really hurts. So please at least try to make them land on their backs!
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>specialty</radical> is <radical>drop</radical>ping people with <radical>thread</radical> by <kanji>bind</kanji>ing them! You can drop anyone as long as you have enough thread to restrain them. It doesn't matter how much they fight, you'll get them to drop!

MeaningHint: It's really important to remember how many things are under your control when you operate a vehicle. It isn't just the people in your car either, it's everyone and everything in your way too.
ReadingHint: If you don't want to think about hitting adorable cats with your car, you better maintain control. You're responsible!
ReadingMnemonic: You have to maintain <kanji>control</kanji> of your car or you'll hit a bunch of <reading>cats</reading> (かつ). The roads are full of cats at all times. And if you lose control of your car for just a second, you'll hit at least two or three cats.
MeaningMnemonic: When you drive a <radical>car</radical>, everyone under your <radical>roof</radical> is putting their <radical>life</radical> in your hands, even if they don't say it with their <radical>mouth</radical>—it's still under your <kanji>control</kanji>.

MeaningHint: So this mysterious bow's string is for plucking a one-note tune or launching an attack. Either way, your enemies will be moved to tears.
ReadingHint: Genji is a lover, not a fighter. Picture Genji, the most handsome and romantic man ever, serenading you with his one-note G-string bow. Swoon! Just hope he doesn't sink an arrow into you too.
ReadingMnemonic: Turns out this bow strung with a mysterious <kanji>chord</kanji> belongs to <reading>Gen</reading>ji (げん). Of course! Only someone as elegant and dramatic as Genji would own a bow that plays music when you pluck its string.
MeaningMnemonic:
This <radical>bow</radical> is <radical>mysterious</radical> because it's strung with a single <kanji>chord</kanji>. I guess that makes the chord both a musical instrument <kanji>string</kanji> as well as a <kanji>bowstring</kanji>. Pluck the chord and it shoots an arrow while making a perfect G note. So mysterious!

Note: We're using "chord" here as the fancy name for a musical instrument string (like in "the chords of a harp" or "strike a chord"). This kanji covers both musical strings and bowstrings!

MeaningHint: Picture a poor little mole suffocating in your hand after you took it out of its hole. Put it back right away! Don't kill that little mole!
ReadingHint: Unfortunately, you can't use cheats on life, and the mole suddenly stiffens and suffocates to death anyway. You should feel bad about what you've done.
ReadingMnemonic: As the mole <kanji>suffocate</kanji>s it <reading>cheats</reading> (ちつ) and shoves its head into your closed hand. You realize what it's doing and squeeze your hand tighter so it's more like a hole. You can feel the mole breathing again now.
MeaningMnemonic: Take a <radical>mole</radical> out of its <radical>hole</radical> and it will <kanji>suffocate</kanji>. They need to be in tight quarters, unlike us humans. So when you take one out of its nice, safe hole, it thinks it can't breathe and suffocates.

MeaningHint: More than one tsunami will definitely result in a flood. Imagine the floody aftermath of more than one tsunami, arriving together.
ReadingHint: "This flood was caused by you! It is because you did not believe in the holy Crabigator! It is because you didn't do your kanji reviews. You have sinned, sinned, sinned!" こういち says to the scared, cold, now homeless people.
ReadingMnemonic: After the terrible <kanji>flood</kanji>, Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち comes to survey the destruction and offer hope to his people. But, instead he just tells the people that this flood happened because they didn't believe in the Crabigator's words, and because they weren't doing their kanji reviews.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> <radical>together</radical> with another tsunami is going to cause a massive <kanji>flood</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Pretend you're in a class in the mid-20th century, watching a video on bomb explosion safety. In addition to putting your fingers in your ears, you have to do a bunch of other stuff. You can't just stop after the first step!
ReadingHint: In addition to ears, you have a lot of sets on your body to worry about. Sets of eyes, nostrils, legs, you get the picture.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>In addition</kanji> to all the steps you have to take to protect yourself, you can't forget that ears come in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). You can't just stick one finger in one ear and have that be the end. You have a set of ears to worry about!
MeaningMnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>ear</radical>s when there's an <radical>explosion</radical> <kanji>in addition</kanji> to all the other things you were told to do. You have to crouch under a desk, you have to stay away from windows, you have to close your eyes. But in addition to that, you need to protect your ears!

MeaningHint: Haven't you ever been so bored that you just wrapped yourself up into a boredom ball?
ReadingHint: Ehhhhhhgggg guys I'm stuck at home and I'm soooo bored. Heeelpp!
ReadingMnemonic: So now you're <kanji>bored</kanji> and stuck at <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう). You aren't hungry. There's nothing on TV. And you don't want to have to leave your home to do something, even though that would probably solve your boredom problem.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>eat</radical> and then <radical>wrap</radical> yourself into a ball because you're <kanji>bored</kanji>. You're also <kanji>sated</kanji>, because you ate just the right amount, but now you have nothing to do and it's soooo booooring.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself as the perfect gentleman. You may not have a fancy top hat and tie, but you're humble about what you wear and you're kind to everyone.
ReadingHint: But you're a gentleman, so you don't kick their shins back, you smile and hold in the pain. No matter how bad your shins hurt, you never fight back!
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, as a <kanji>gentleman</kanji> who dresses humbly, you're always getting kicked in the <reading>shin</reading>s (しん) by rude kids (and occasionally rude adults).
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>thread</radical>s are <radical>humble</radical> and that makes you a perfect <kanji>gentleman</kanji>. You don't have to have fancy clothes to be a gentleman, just humble ones that don't stand out and show off too much. That shows how polite you are.

MeaningHint: This is a totally commonplace thing for almost every class or job you'll ever have. They sit you down under the canopy, put a brush in your hand, and say, "Here, complete this task. I'll be over there."
ReadingHint: As you eat the yogurt you think about how commonplace yogurt is too. It's just a normal, plain, boring old food, found in practically everyone's fridge.
ReadingMnemonic: This is so <kanji>commonplace</kanji> and familiar to you that you finish your task early and eat some <reading>yo</reading>gurt (よう) while you wait for the guy to come back.
MeaningMnemonic: Under this <radical>canopy</radical> you have a <radical>brush</radical> (in its 肀 form) and a <radical>task</radical> to complete with it. This is totally <kanji>commonplace</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine how peaceful staring at the blue ocean, the blue sky, or the blue tumblr makes you.
ReadingHint: YAASS! Peaceful blue radicals standing in a row. What's that? YAASS you do want more radicals added to the site. Well okay then, if it will keep you at peace, sure!
ReadingMnemonic: Now if someone asks you if the color blue makes you <kanji>peaceful</kanji> you should say "<reading>YAASS</reading> (やす)!" And you'll have us to thank for that! All those peaceful blue radicals. They're the easiest thing on the site, and if someone asks you if radicals make you feel peaceful say "YAASS" again!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Stand</radical> staring up at the <radical>blue</radical> sky and you'll feel more <kanji>peaceful</kanji> than you ever have. Blue is a color that promotes peaceful thoughts and feelings. That's why all the social media sites are blue. To stop you from being a jerk.

MeaningHint: Imagine hearing someone speak and it's so powerful and loud that it sounds like they have a drum booming out of their mouth. You'll love them! You'll hold them in the highest esteem!
ReadingHint: If you hold someone in high esteem you give me your- give them your car. Gimme!
ReadingMnemonic: You hold them in such high <kanji>esteem</kanji> that you give them your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). I ... I said you give them your car! Give them your car. Hey, hey!
MeaningMnemonic: If when you speak it sounds like the <radical>power</radical> of a <radical>drum</radical> is coming out of your <radical>mouth</radical>, then people will hold you in high <kanji>esteem</kanji>. Anyone who has a loud, booming, powerful voice will be held in high esteem in this day and age. Look at all those awesome musicians who have booming voices!

MeaningHint: Imagine what it would be like to squeeze your fingers into a keyhole. Maybe try squeezing one finger really tight first. Did that hurt? Well it's going to be a lot worse in that key hole.
ReadingHint: You have the ability to squeeze your fingers into locks to open them, so of course you're stealing. And you squeeze those stolen goodies into your sack so you can take them with you.
ReadingMnemonic: Once you've <kanji>squeeze</kanji>d your finger in the key hole to unlock this door you squeeze all the money and jewels and treasures you can fit into your <reading>sack</reading> (さく). Your stealing sack.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to fit your <radical>fingers</radical> into a <radical>key</radical> <radical>hole</radical>, you have to <kanji>squeeze</kanji> them in. Fingers aren't exactly supposed to fit in key holes... they're for keys, after all. But you want to squeeze your finger on in there anyway to unlock the door.

MeaningHint: Imagine any tree in the world with different kinds of butterflies fluttering around. Then go to another tree in the world that looks completely different. Once again, butterflies! They really are fluttering around every tree in the world.
ReadingHint: She grabs another butterfly and starts pulling off wings again, though this time you can see her evil face looking at you while she does it. "I hate butterflies," she says. "They are always dying in my tea."
ReadingMnemonic: As you look at a <kanji>butterfly</kanji>, fluttering around a tree, a hand reaches out from the tree and grabs it, then starts plucking the wings off. An evil laugh comes from inside the tree, and you know it's Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>insect</radical> that is amongst all the <radical>world</radical>'s <radical>tree</radical>s is the <kanji>butterfly</kanji>. They are always hanging around trees, those butterflies.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing a stone and a cross drop from the sky onto each other to form a tombstone right in front of your eyes!
ReadingHint: Can you feel the heat radiating off this space tombstone? Maybe there's a space skeleton sizzling under there too...
ReadingMnemonic: Now you're curious about what's under there but when you try to touch the <kanji>tombstone</kanji> it's radiating with intense <reading>he</reading>at (ひ)! It must have come from outer space and this heat is from when it entered our atmosphere!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>stone</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s from the sky into a <radical>rice paddy</radical> and then a <radical>cross</radical> <radical>drop</radical>s on top of it. They've combined to form a <kanji>tombstone</kanji>! Wait, does that mean a body fell from the sky first and you just didn't see it?

MeaningHint: Picture a military officer doing all those things.
ReadingHint: Picture the military officer saluting as an eagle flies overhead. Majestic.
ReadingMnemonic: That <kanji>military officer</kanji>'s favorite bird is an <reading>ea</reading>gle (い)! That should be obvious because the eagle is the bird of America! And that's the country he fights for!
MeaningMnemonic: Who loves their <radical>flag</radical>, using <radical>jackhammer</radical>s, and precise <radical>measurement</radical>s? Your <kanji>military officer</kanji>! He's always saluting the flag, jackhammering near all the new recruits to test them, and checking the measurement between the floor and their pants. Who else could it be?

MeaningHint: Imagine looking down your street and seeing caged axes above all of the artisans homes and shops. You want a new chair? You want some pants mended? Go to one of those signs and you'll find the right artisan.
ReadingHint: Picture the Shougun marching angrily down the street. His shoe suddenly snaps in half. With just one glance he'll be able to find the shoe artisan and make them fix his shoe for free.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Artisan</kanji>s have to be marked this way because the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう) says so. The Shougun doesn't want to have to put any effort into finding people to make things for him, so he created this new system.
MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>cage</radical> with an <radical>axe</radical> in it above your house to show that you are an <kanji>artisan</kanji>. All of the artisans have to have this special sign somewhere near where they live to show they make things.

MeaningHint: The scooter satellite is always far off because it does what scooters do best. It just keeps scooting farther and farther into the distance.
ReadingHint: You're seeing the scooter satellite because it's what your heart desires, so your mind is playing tricks on you. But nope, no chance of seeing it from that distance — you're just hallucinating.
ReadingMnemonic: You think you can spot the scooter satellite in the <kanji>far off</kanji> distance, but really it's just a <reading>hallu</reading>cination (はる). It's way too small and far away for you to see it.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>scooter</radical> <radical>satellite</radical> is the satellite that is always <kanji>far off</kanji> and <kanji>far away</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine shooting an arrow at the mouth of the sun and then waiting for the wisdom of the world to hit you. Did it work? Will you know when it works? Probably...
ReadingHint: Picture yourself thanking the sun for teaching you that all wisdom comes from cheese. Now take a big bite!
ReadingMnemonic: You're standing around waiting for the <kanji>wisdom</kanji> to hit you when something else does. In the head. Ouch! You look down and it's a wheel of <reading>chee</reading>se (ち)! At first you're angry, until you realize that this must be a sign. Cheese IS wisdom. And the key to wisdom is eating a lot of cheese!
MeaningMnemonic: You shoot an <radical>arrow</radical> at the <radical>mouth</radical> of the <radical>sun</radical> so that it will grant you a gift of <kanji>wisdom</kanji>. You heard about a Greek hero doing this and the god in the sun gave him the wisdom to see everything. You're hoping it will work for you too.

MeaningHint: Some people make really bad choices when they're drunk, and in this case, when a shellfish gets drunk and sees something it wants it'll just rob people for it.
ReadingHint: Picture a robber shellfish in a Bosozoku bike gang. Look at him the wrong way and he and his gang will steal all your stuff next time!
ReadingMnemonic: Once the shellfish has a taste for being a <kanji>robber</kanji>, he'll join a Boso<reading>zoku</reading> (ぞく) bike gang so he can keep stealing more and more crosses. He'll drink more, steal more, and get to ride cool motorcycles.
MeaningMnemonic: When a <radical>shellfish</radical> becomes a <radical>drunkard</radical> and sees a <radical>cross</radical>, they'll become a <kanji>robber</kanji>. Because they'll steal the cross!

MeaningHint: A branch is just like a drumstick, so of course you're going to drum them together.
ReadingHint: Kids can't get enough of it. Anything that makes a loud drumming noise or anything they can beat together is fun for them.
ReadingMnemonic: Who loves to <kanji>drum</kanji> things together and make a huge racket? <reading>子</reading>s (こ), of course.
MeaningMnemonic: You take a <radical>drum</radical> and a <radical>branch</radical> and... well, what else would you do? You would <kanji>drum</kanji> or <kanji>beat</kanji> them together.

MeaningHint: Imagine getting your coat rack to make rotations in a circle just by shooting it with a gun!
ReadingHint: Picture that centaurs angry face. Convince yourself it was the rotation it didn't like.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch your coat rack's <kanji>rotation</kanji>s a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) barges in and takes the gun from you, stops the rotating coat rack and leaves. How rude! I guess centaurs don't like when things rotate.
MeaningMnemonic: Move your <radical>coat rack</radical> in one <radical>direction</radical> by shooting it with a <radical>gun</radical>. Then you can keep shooting it to get a good <kanji>rotation</kanji> going. If you think there is a better way to make your coat rack make <kanji>revolution</kanji>s, you're probably wrong.

MeaningHint: Imagine the moon, then imagine it cross-fading into your big, round tummy. Then, cut it open because you think the moon is easy to cut. And finally, see your intestines as clearly as you can.
ReadingHint: This scene was in all the omen radicals you've been seeing about her. If only you could have stopped her, then none of this would have happened, right? Perhaps it was all destiny, you think, as your intestines fall from your stomach and Mrs. Chou cackles over your weakening body.
ReadingMnemonic: All over the world, people are cutting their bellies open, because they think its an easy moon (versus a normal moon, or hard moon, presumably). This is because they have fallen victim to a spell by Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>moon</radical> is <radical>easy</radical> tonight, so you decide to cut it open. But, the moon wasn't the moon, it was your big, pale, round tummy. You just cut it open, and now you can see your <kanji>intestines</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine scooping a melted tree into a tank. It's a very strange sensation.
ReadingHint:
"Um, okay?" you reply to the soul, and walk away from the tank. "W-wait! Tanks can't move? Come back, let me haunt you with my tree soul!"

It's a good thing there weren't wheels on that tank!
ReadingMnemonic: You're staring at the big <kanji>tank</kanji> when an eerie sound comes out, "Oooh I am the <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) of the tree! You've trapped me in this tank and now I'm going to haunt it!" You don't want to be haunted by a tank with a soul stuck in it.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> melts into the <radical>ground</radical> after listening to loud <radical>music</radical> in the hot <radical>sun</radical>, so you have to find a nice big <kanji>tank</kanji> to scoop the melted tree into. The music made the tree vibrate at juuust the right frequency that it, combined with the hot sun, made the tree turn into a puddle. You can't just leave it being all blobby on the ground, so you find a tank for it.

MeaningHint: Imagine felling trees with a bunch of other guys, yelling things like, "This is what you get for stepping on my turf!" It's a little silly.
ReadingHint: This is good news for the trees, but your drunken leader isn't going to be happy.
ReadingMnemonic: You and your gang head out to <kanji>fell</kanji> the trees, only to remember that all you have are <reading>bats</reading> (ばつ). Good luck trying to take down trees with nothing but bats!
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>leader</radical> of your gang is a <radical>drunkard</radical> he'll try to have you <kanji>fell</kanji> trees thinking they're rival gang members. You all know they aren't but you can't argue with your leader, especially if he's drunk. Go ahead and <kanji>attack</kanji> the trees anyway.

MeaningHint: Try to imagine roughly how big a 3.3-square-meter area is. Now imagine yourself lying down in that space. You feel pretty peaceful, right?
ReadingHint: Even better, you and your friend can squeeze your two bodies into the 壺 after setting it in the 3.3-square-meter area. Now you're really using the space efficiently!
ReadingMnemonic:
An area of <kanji>3.3 square meters</kanji> also happens to be just the right size for <reading>two bo</reading>dies (つぼ)! Not in, like, a creepy way, mind you — two bodies in the sense of you and a friend snuggling up and finding peace in this <kanji>two-mat area</kanji> together. 

Alternatively, you can remember this word with 壺, which has the same reading. Just imagine putting a big, fancy 壺 in your 3.3 square meter area. It's the perfect way to utilize the space.
MeaningMnemonic: The amount of <radical>dirt</radical> you need to get some <radical>peace</radical> is <kanji>3.3 square meters</kanji>. This is roughly the area of two tatami mats, so some might call it a <kanji>two-mat area</kanji>. Not only is it the perfect amount of space for finding peace, but it's also a pretty common measurement in Japan (called a <kanji>tsubo</kanji>), so it's worth getting familiar with.

MeaningHint: Turn your thread a sweet navy color and people will be reminded of yummy blue raspberry. MmMmm.
ReadingHint: Imagine a condor stealing your sweet navy thread right after you dyed it, flying off. Lame!
ReadingMnemonic: Now that your threads are <kanji>navy</kanji> colored you need to be careful that <reading>con</reading>dors (こん) don't try to steal it for their nests. Condors love <kanji>dark blue</kanji> things and thread is perfect for a condor nest.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to turn your <radical>thread</radical> into a <radical>sweet</radical> color you just HAVE to go with <kanji>navy blue</kanji> or <kanji>dark blue</kanji>. That's the sweetest color in existence, everyone knows that. After all, blue raspberry flavor is dark blue and it's super sweet.

MeaningHint: The reason you'd use a net made of thread is because it's hard to see. But, it's only that way when you spread out the net. It's important to spread out and arrange your net otherwise the turkey will see it.
ReadingHint: "Let me out of this net!" says Ra. "You arranged and spread it out just right so I couldn't see it!"
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>spread out</kanji> your net and <kanji>arrange</kanji> it just so. You expect it to catch a turkey, but instead you catch the ancient Egyptian god <reading>Ra</reading> (ら).
MeaningMnemonic: You have a <radical>net</radical> of <radical>thread</radical> for catching a <radical>turkey</radical>. But, it must be <kanji>spread out</kanji> and you must <kanji>arrange</kanji> it first.

MeaningHint: Imagine walking up the mountain in your wedding clothes so you can get married without anyone trying to stop you. Then, because you're still worried, you go into a ravine where you are read your marriage vows.
ReadingHint: This ravine runs right through the middle of きょうと, though most people don't notice it. That makes it the best place for a secret wedding to a horned freak-husband.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>ravine</kanji> is still in <reading>きょう</reading>と though, because if you went outside the city your marriage wouldn't be legal. So you found a secluded, local ravine.
MeaningMnemonic: To the <radical>mountain</radical>s you took your <radical>horned</radical> <radical>husband</radical> for you two to get married. (Society wouldn't accept a horned husband!) To hide even more from the prying eyes of society, you take your horned husband into a <kanji>ravine</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The leader shows you the number, it's all of your salary for spring. "This way, if things dry up worse, you'll be okay for a while," he says. This is awfully nice, but very suspicious at the same time.
ReadingHint:
You're sitting at home, and there is a knock at the door. A man in a suit, hat, and dark sunglasses hands you a briefcase full of money. "Here's your spring salary. Enjoy the dry weather."

"T-thanks?"

Welp, have fun at home with your stacks of early salary cash.
ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>salary</kanji> is sent straight to your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう) instead of the bank. Okay, something is definitely fishy here.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> says <radical>spring</radical> is looking <radical>dry</radical>, so you'll need your <kanji>salary</kanji> in advance.

MeaningHint: Imagine a nurse brewing alcohol with the same attention they would nurse a human patient. But this brew is much more important than any person's health.
ReadingHint: Imagine what it must be like for Joe, out working on the fields all day long, working up a sweat. I’d say he’s earned a cold alcoholic brew after such a day. Cut the man some slack.
ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>brew</kanji> of alcohol is what <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう) loves most after a long day of working the fields.
MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>alcohol</radical> <radical>nurse</radical> is someone who nurses alcohol. In other words, they <kanji>brew</kanji> it.

MeaningHint: Imagine looking at the bow and stick as you slide them over to your deceased friend's family. This won't do enough, but it is a little condolence at least.
ReadingHint: Imagine her saying, "My condolences," as Mrs. Chou does him in.
ReadingMnemonic: More than a <kanji>condolence</kanji>, you want to avenge your friend's death. He was killed by Mrs. <reading>Chou</reading> (ちょう), and she broke his stick and bow, which is why you got the family a new set.
MeaningMnemonic: You give a <radical>bow</radical> and a <radical>stick</radical> to your friend's family. This is because your friend died, and he loved bows and sticks. You give this to his family, to give our <kanji>condolence</kanji>s.

MeaningHint: Imagine saying A B C as you look down someones face at their eyes, nose and mouth. Their nose is B!
ReadingHint: Imagine running out of cereal so you have to eat your latter option of breakfast oats. They aren't awful but they aren't your A breakfast choice, that's for sure.
ReadingMnemonic: If you don't have any cereal you have to eat your <kanji>B</kanji> breakfast option. <reading>Oats</reading> (おつ). You don't hate oats, but they're definitely not your first choice.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>nose</radical> isn't the first thing people see about your face, it's the <kanji>latter</kanji>. They see your eyes THEN your nose, then your mouth, in order going down. Just label them A <kanji>B</kanji> and C and you'll never forget!

MeaningHint: Scooters and libraries are universally loved by all!
ReadingHint: The next time you scoot to the library look around. There are little hen scooters and hens there with their chicks. They're reading them books about grains and how to hatch eggs. It's a universally adorable thing to see.
ReadingMnemonic: Riding a scooter to the library is so <kanji>universal</kanji>, even <reading>hen</reading>s (へん) do it.
MeaningMnemonic: Riding a <radical>scooter</radical> to the <radical>library</radical> is a <kanji>universal</kanji> experience. People young and old have all, at some point, taken a scooter to the library, picked up a book, used the internet, sat in the comfy chairs, then left via that same scooter.

MeaningHint: There was a lot of crime right here in this rice paddy, so you just built a big prison right there to lower the crime rate so this region can experience equilibrium once again.
ReadingHint: You might remember from the kanji 耕 that there were こういち clones working in these rice paddy fields. Well, they were the ones disturbing the equilibrium. At least that's what Emperor こういち said. So by throwing them in a prison built right here, the area could once again achieve equilibrium.
ReadingMnemonic: To restore <kanji>equilibrium</kanji>, you throw all the <reading>こう</reading>いち clones into the prison.
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>go</radical> to a <radical>big</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> and establish a <radical>prison</radical> right in the middle of it to restore the <kanji>equilibrium</kanji> of the area.

MeaningHint: Imagine the guard doing his inspection in front of the gate all day long. He should have a nice report for you once he's done.
ReadingHint: If you hire a guard to stand at your gate and conduct an inspection of your neighborhood, make sure you tell him not to use an etch a sketch!
ReadingMnemonic: His <kanji>inspection</kanji> is complete, but it looks like he's used an <reading>etch</reading> (えつ) a sketch to record all the results. That's not really helpful. If you shake it all the inspection results will disappear!
MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>guard</radical> at your <radical>gate</radical> to get a good <kanji>inspection</kanji> of your neighborhood going. The guard will stand there all day inspecting everything and then report it all back to you. You won't have to do any of the inspection yourself!

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to reflect some precious sunlight into your prison room with a spoon. Now imagine a mouth appearing in the way of the sunlight and it scolds you.
ReadingHint: WHAT KIND OF WORLD IS THIS THAT YOU'RE IN A PRISON WHERE CATS SCOLD YOU?!
ReadingMnemonic: One day you finally get fed up. You need to know who the mouth of the person who's <kanji>scold</kanji>ing you is. You stick your spoon out into the sun and the mouth appears, scolding you right on time. You reach out and grab them. But in your hand is a pile of <reading>cats</reading> (かつ).
MeaningMnemonic: If you put your <radical>spoon</radical> in the <radical>sun</radical> while you're in <radical>prison</radical>, a <radical>mouth</radical> will appear to <kanji>scold</kanji> you. You can never see who the mouth belongs to, but considering you're in prison it's probably a guard. Either way, that person doesn't like what you're doing and they keep scolding you when you put your spoon out into the sun.

MeaningHint: Imagine how much daring it would take to go outside in winter knowing you could be freezing your precious elf ears off.
ReadingHint: Do you think you have the same amount of daring as an elf going out in winter and Genghis Khan? Tell the truth.
ReadingMnemonic: The amount of <kanji>daring</kanji> you'd need is about equal to the amount Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) had. And he was really daring!
MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>elf</radical> who ventures outside in <radical>winter</radical> has a lot of <kanji>daring</kanji>. Elf ears are very, very delicate, and the winter cold can really mess them up. So if you see an elf adventuring in winter, know that that particular elf is a really daring dude.

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing the moon with a membrane sitting stuffed in a greenhouse. It looks like something out of the Alien movies and you're starting to think putting it in there wasn't a good idea.
ReadingHint: Can you imagine if the moon had a membrane and it smelled like a big mac? How does that even happen? You should probably go ask that food chain what they use for their ingredients.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to open the greenhouse to check this moon <kanji>membrane</kanji> out but when you open the door the smell of big <reading>mac</reading>s (まく) hits you in the face. Eww. Why does this membrane smell like a big mac... or does that mean big macs smell like a festering moon?
MeaningMnemonic: If you trap the <radical>moon</radical> in a <radical>greenhouse</radical> it will grow a creepy <kanji>membrane</kanji> around itself. The moon isn't used to the heat it would be exposed to in a greenhouse so that membrane is there to protect it. It's really gross looking though.

MeaningHint: Imagine what it would be like to have one of your eyes die. You'll be blind in that eye forever!
ReadingHint: Imagine a blind person mowing over everyone in their car as they drive on the sidewalk. Eeek. At least they don't have to see this mess.
ReadingMnemonic: When you're <kanji>blind</kanji> you probably shouldn't drive anymore because if you do you'll <reading>mow</reading> (もう) everyone over. Actually, don't mow your lawn either. Blind people probably shouldn't be driving anything.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>death</radical> of your <radical>eye</radical>s means that you've gone <kanji>blind</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The next time you're cursing your uterus for making you bleed, curse the moon instead! It's the moon's fault you're in pain.
ReadingHint: Picture an invisible tie tieing your uterus to the moon. Damn you moon, stop pulling, it hurts!
ReadingMnemonic: It kind of sucks, but there is no way to sever this <kanji>uterus</kanji> - moon <reading>tie</reading> (たい). But if you find a way please tell me, I can't take this anymore.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>machine</radical> in your body that is powered by the <radical>moon</radical> is your <kanji>womb</kanji>, also called your <kanji>uterus</kanji>! Haven't you ever wondered why periods happen with the revolutions of the moon? That's because it powers your uterus and tells it what to do!

MeaningHint: Because alcohol is so good and addicting, people will work extra hard to ferment it properly. This makes it a good teacher to learn about fermentation. People are motivated to figure it out!
ReadingHint: You are fermenting thousands of bottles of alcohol for Emperor こういち's big party.
ReadingMnemonic: Using <kanji>fermentation</kanji>, you are indeed making some alcohol. It will be drunk at a party put on by Emperor <reading>こう</reading>いち.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Alcohol</radical> is a good <radical>teacher</radical> on the process of <kanji>fermentation</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture a street where nothing is maintained. The buildings crumble in the dirt, but the people have lost the will to care. It's not just physical, it's spiritual degradation too…
ReadingHint: Your dad isn't just part of the degradation — he's the ringleader. He's sitting in a broken lawn chair, surrounded by empty beer cans, takeout containers, and piles of dirt. He waves his hand, and more junk food wrappers fall around him, almost like he's commanding this lazy, degenerate lifestyle.
ReadingMnemonic: And who's leading this charge into <kanji>degradation</kanji>? Your <reading>da</reading>d (だ), of course.
MeaningMnemonic: When <radical>building</radical>s <radical>have</radical> <radical>dirt</radical> piling up all around them, it's a sign of their <kanji>degradation</kanji>.

MeaningHint: If you manage to intercept them before they can go in there to boil things, you'll be able to pick up all the clothes and comic books you left on the floor. You really don't want anyone to see those.
ReadingHint: Phew, you intercepted the shaman in time (though she doesn't look very happy about being scooted into) and now you can clean up your yurt before they go in.
ReadingMnemonic: You manage to <kanji>intercept</kanji> the <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ) who was just about to open the door to your yurt, and instead you crash right into him.
MeaningMnemonic: You're riding your <radical>scooter</radical> toward your <radical>yurt</radical> as fast as you can because someone is trying to get in there to <radical>boil</radical> your water and you want to <kanji>intercept</kanji> them.

MeaningHint: Study your radicals and you'll read good.
ReadingHint: Tote bags are totes not convex though. Don't mix them up.
ReadingMnemonic: Is this <kanji>convex</kanji>? It's <reading>totes</reading> (とつ) convex!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>convex</radical> radical and the <kanji>convex</kanji> kanji are the same, so hopefully you now just know them both.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Watch your mouth around the 王. Have you ever seen a concave person? Yeah that's because they didn't live through it. 王s aren't very nice, you know.
ReadingMnemonic: If you call someone <kanji>concave</kanji> the <reading>王</reading> (おう) will find out and he'll have his guards turn you concave.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>concave</radical> radical and the <kanji>concave</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: Picture a bunch of maple trees being blown by the wind. They don't seem upset, they actually seem really happy.
ReadingHint: Imagine your kite in the maple tree.
ReadingMnemonic: When the wind goes through the <kanji>maple</kanji> tree you know it's <reading>kite day</reading> (かえで)!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> that does well in the <radical>wind</radical> is a <kanji>maple</kanji> tree. Maple trees don't have fruit so the wind can't knock anything off of it. That's why they thrive well in windy places!

MeaningHint: Imagine having a drunkard spit a dirty question mark into your hand. You want to say, "Yes, I'm ??? too."
ReadingHint: Picture yourself throwing the question mark at the drunkard who only cares about the Yankees. What a waste of time.
ReadingMnemonic: The drunkard takes the <kanji>question mark</kanji> back and presses it to his head. He's wearing a <reading>Ya</reading>nkee's (や) cap. Oh come on. "Do you just want to know the Yankee's score in the game?" The drunkard nods.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>drunkard</radical> has <radical>dirt</radical> in his <radical>mouth</radical> as he tries to talk to you. You walk over and he spits a <kanji>question mark</kanji> into your hand. It's covered in dirt and spit. Um... does this mean he's confused? He probably doesn't even know where he is. But how did he make this question mark and why was it in his mouth?

MeaningHint: Imagine walking into a warehouse and seeing hundreds of pale flowers growing in it. It's subtle, but beautiful.
ReadingHint: Picture a bunch of pale, soulless flowers. You pick one up and it falls apart in your hands.
