ReadingMnemonic: When you grow <kanji>pale</kanji> flowers like this, they don't grow a <reading>soul</reading> (そう). Soul adds color to plants and flowers. That's probably why ghosts and dead bodies are pale too.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> grown in a <radical>warehouse</radical> will be very <kanji>pale</kanji>. They never see the sun, so they don't grow to have bright colors.

MeaningHint: Picture your flowers sprouting out of the ground in the bright spring sun.
ReadingHint: Take a walk around your home right now. Can you find the best place to sprout flowers? Remember, it has to be nice and bright! Not dark and damp. Don't put them in your closet.
ReadingMnemonic: You want to grow these <kanji>sprout</kanji>s in your <reading>ho</reading>me (ほう), not outside. So you need to find the windows in your house that are the brightest.
MeaningMnemonic: You have to put <radical>flowers</radical> in a <radical>bright</radical> place if you want them to <kanji>sprout</kanji>. If they're left in the shade they'll just rot.

MeaningHint: Silkworms and their silky secretions are actually really core to heaven's entire aesthetic.
ReadingHint: Imagine the anticipation as you wait in line to get your must-買い coat. You're so grateful to the silkworms — thanks to them, you'll finally be able to hang out with the cool angels!
ReadingMnemonic: Lately, the <kanji>silkworm</kanji>s have been making "must-<reading>買い coa</reading>ts (かいこ)" that all the angels are going crazy over. Regular angel robes? Those are sooo last year. If you're not rocking one of these silkworm-crafted must-買い coats, then you simply can't be part of heaven's in-crowd.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Heaven</radical>'s favorite <radical>insect</radical> is the <kanji>silkworm</kanji>. Silkworms are really prized in heaven because they produce all the silk used to make those flowy white angel robes and those fluffy clouds everyone walks around on.

MeaningHint: Watch the king's swords digging in your private rice paddy and digging up huge chunks of lapis lazuli. This isn't fair, that lapis should belong to you.
ReadingHint: Imagine reading through a giant text called "The Law" and looking for this loophole the king's men claim to have about taking your lapis lazuli. You can't find it... but maybe if you search just a bit longer...
ReadingMnemonic: You run up and ask how this <kanji>lapis lazuli</kanji> thievery isn't illegal and one of the swordsmen just shouts, "<reading>Loo</reading>phole (る)!" What? What loophole? What law says that the king gets to steal your stuff?
MeaningMnemonic:
The <radical>king</radical> will send his men with <radical>sword</radical>s into your <radical>private</radical> <radical>rice paddy</radical> if you come across <kanji>lapis lazuli</kanji>. Even though this is your private land, he wants that lapis lazuli to make some new crowns and thrones for himself. Plus he's the king, so he can do whatever he wants.

If you've never taken an art history class (or played Minecraft) you may never have come across lapis lazuli, but think of it as a pretty, blue precious stone that people really, really liked in the olden days.

MeaningHint: If you haven't smelled sulfur before, it smells a bit like rotten eggs. Something that definitely belongs in the trash.
ReadingHint: Ryu looks down at you, holding the sulfurous rock in one hand. Then he uppercuts you with it! Shoryuken!
ReadingMnemonic: You throw the <kanji>sulfur</kanji> rock as hard as you can, but it hits someone in the back of the head. Oh no, it's <reading>Ryu</reading> (りゅう) from Street Fighter. You try to apologize and explain that you just didn't want this stinky trash rock, I mean, sulfur rock near you. You didn't know he was there.
MeaningMnemonic: There are <radical>stone</radical>s that smell like <radical>trash</radical> in the <radical>river</radical>. You reach in and realize these rocks are made of <kanji>sulfur</kanji>. Even the flow of this water can't hide the stink of these sulfur rocks.

MeaningHint: Imagine spilling red wine or red paint down your shirt. Quickly pardon yourself and get changed now!
ReadingHint: Imagine not pardoning yourself fast enough and having the winter shaman spirit away whatever you got red on. That's embarrassing.
ReadingMnemonic: If you don't <kanji>pardon</kanji> yourself fast enough the winter <reading>sha</reading>man (しゃ) will burst in and cast a spell on you, taking away all the clothes that have red on them! Now you have to pardon yourself for being naked!
MeaningMnemonic: If you spill something <radical>red</radical> on yourself in <radical>winter</radical> you should <kanji>pardon</kanji> yourself immediately and try to find something else to wear. It's very dangerous to wear red in winter, and even if it seems rude, make sure you pardon yourself right away and get that taken care of!

MeaningHint: Imagine seeing seven swords just lying abandoned in a hole. Feel the urge to steal them all. Do it, do it!
ReadingHint: Imagine your delight as you look up the different sword sets online and realize you happened to steal the best one!
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>steal</kanji> the swords and realize they aren't just seven random swords, they're from the famous sword <reading>sets</reading> (せつ) they sell online. This particular set is from the Seven Samurai movie. Yes! What a great steal!
MeaningMnemonic: You see <radical>seven</radical> <radical>sword</radical>s just sitting in a <radical>hole</radical> and so you decide to <kanji>steal</kanji> them. They were just left in that hole after all, it's not like anyone was using them. And even if they were, they'll never know you were the one to steal them, right?

MeaningHint: Imagine all of the other people staring and pointing, the kids making fun of you, no one seeing you as equal. And just... sigh. Deep down, you want to get rid of your fangs, but you can't do it.
ReadingHint: Now you're staring in the mirror at yourself. You sigh loudly and take out the pliers. You'll be a guy like everyone else. S-soon. As soon as you can muster up enough strength to stop sighing and rip these fangs out.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>sigh</kanji> again and say, "I wish I was just a normal <reading>guy</reading> (がい)." I swear the rest of me is a guy like the rest of you. *sigh*
MeaningMnemonic: Deep in your <radical>soul</radical>, you feel like you want to pull your <radical>fang</radical>s out by the <radical>root</radical>, but instead you let out a loud <kanji>sigh</kanji>. You're one of the only people you know that has fangs, and it weighs heavily on your soul.

MeaningHint: Imagine rubbing your husband's back with your fingers to aid him. You want him to feel better. You want him to get that comforting feeling that this kind of touching gives.
ReadingHint: That's true love, my friend.
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, your husband wouldn't need your <kanji>aid</kanji> if he wasn't such a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ). He walks into doors, he falls down the stairs, he touches the plate when the waiter says it's hot. And every single time your fool husband hurts himself, you aid him.
MeaningMnemonic: Put your <radical>fingers</radical> on your <radical>husband</radical> to <kanji>aid</kanji> him. There's something about a spouse's touch that just makes you feel better. Have you ever had someone rub your back while you were hurt or sick or sad? It's nice, right?

MeaningHint: If you were the tiger how would you play with the drunkard?
ReadingHint: What would you rather watch? A guillotine at work or a tiger playing like this?
ReadingMnemonic: If you aren't the tiger, watching it <kanji>play</kanji> is a little like watching someone's head get cut off with a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). In fact, you don't want to watch it play at all anymore.
MeaningMnemonic: Throw a <radical>drunkard</radical> in a cage with a <radical>tiger</radical> and some <radical>spikes</radical> and the tiger will have the time of its life <kanji>play</kanji>ing with them! Think of all the tiger play time possibilities!

MeaningHint: Picture a net prison full of insects being dragged by a tsunami getting muddier and muddier. Poor buggles!
ReadingHint: Picture a bunch of muddy ducks munching on some muddy trapped insects, in some muddy leftover tsunami water.
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately for the insects, these <kanji>muddy</kanji> conditions have created the perfect habitat for <reading>duck</reading>s (だく). And muddy insects already imprisoned in a muddy net is a duck's favorite treat!
MeaningMnemonic: If a <radical>tsunami</radical> gets into a <radical>net</radical> that's im<radical>prison</radical>ing a bunch of <radical>insect</radical>s, the insects are going to get all <kanji>muddy</kanji>. The net gets dragged along with the tsunami water and the insects can't escape, so they just get covered in the yucky stuff.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to run down a street with twenty big crosses lining it. It's so scary that you keep running faster and faster as you go.
ReadingHint: Imagine distracting yourself from the scary crosses by honing your running skills while you do it.
ReadingMnemonic: You figure since you're already on the <kanji>run</kanji> that you might as well <reading>hone</reading> (ほん) your running skills while you make your way down this scary, cross lined street.
MeaningMnemonic: You're walking down the street when you see <radical>twenty</radical> <radical>big</radical> <radical>cross</radical>es lined up in front of you. Crosses creep you out and this is way too many for you to keep walking so you decide to <kanji>run</kanji> as fast as you can to get past them.

MeaningHint: Look at the triceratops. Now look at the moon. See the resemblance? Uncanny, don't you think?
ReadingHint: Imagine being the Shougun and seeing the resemblance between a triceratops and the moon for the first time. You'd put that on all your stuff too, if you were him.
ReadingMnemonic: The first person to see this <kanji>resemblance</kanji> was the <reading>Shou</reading>gun (しょう). That's why he decided to put a triceratops head with the moon behind it on his mon (family crest). Now he's known as the full moon dinosaur shogun, and he's very famous.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>triceratops</radical> standing in front of a full <radical>moon</radical> and you can't help but see a really strong <kanji>resemblance</kanji>. The triceratops has that round frill thing behind its horns and it looks just like the round full moon. Who knew that a triceratops would resemble the moon!

MeaningHint: Trees can't live without sunlight, and it's too damp underground in a prison. The water gets all moldy and the tree will rot and die.
ReadingHint: This poor cucumber tree, left in this ground prison to rot. There's nothing worse than eating a rotten cucumber.
ReadingMnemonic: You get close to the <kanji>rot</kanji>ting tree and see some rotten <reading>cu</reading>cumbers (きゅう) hanging from its branches. No!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tree</radical> is stuck in a <radical>prison</radical> under the <radical>ground</radical> and it's starting to <kanji>rot</kanji> and <kanji>decay</kanji>.

MeaningHint: He's just sitting there, he can't even pick up his weapon or raise his head anymore.
ReadingHint: "Co co co co cooooock!" the cock screams, shocking the samurai back into his lifeless shell. You did it!
ReadingMnemonic: The only way to bring a <kanji>husk</kanji> of a samurai back to life is to have a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) crow right in his ear.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>samurai</radical> puts his <radical>forehead</radical> on the <radical>table</radical> next to his <radical>weapon</radical>. He is just a <kanji>husk</kanji> of his former self. This <kanji>shell</kanji> of a samurai has nothing left.

MeaningHint: Receive this nice, easy kanji with thanks!
ReadingHint: Imagine being in きょうと and receiving something wonderful from a passerby in the street. What did you receive? How does it feel to receive something so great?
ReadingMnemonic: The best place to <kanji>receive</kanji> anything is in <reading>きょう</reading>と. The city is famous for the quality of everything they make, so if you’re in きょうと, keep your fingers crossed that you’ll receive something.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>receive</radical> radical and <kanji>receive</kanji> kanji are exactly the same.

MeaningHint: Imagine being the person who has to check whether each fiefdom has enough flowers and tsunamis per year to keep their status.
ReadingHint: Picture Han Solo flying around Japan checking to make sure all the fiefdoms are up to code.
ReadingMnemonic: The most famous <kanji>fiefdom</kanji> checker is <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo. He takes his Millennium Falcon and swoops around checking for flowers and signs of past tsunamis and then he'll either say you can or cannot be your own fiefdom. Thanks Han Solo!
MeaningMnemonic: You have to reach a certain <radical>number</radical> of <radical>flowers</radical> and <radical>tsunami</radical>s hits to become a legitimate <kanji>fiefdom</kanji>. At least in the old days. If you don't meet one of these conditions, like if you get hit by tsunamis all the time but you only have two or three flowers that will grow, you can't be your own fiefdom.

MeaningHint: Imagine the sweet tree woman being a mediator for you and your arch rival. What she says fixes everything between you two!
ReadingHint: Imagine her refusing to let you pay her for her mediator services and just saying "bye" over and over until you leave. Weird.
ReadingMnemonic: You go to pay the woman for being your <kanji>mediator</kanji> for you two but she just says, "<reading>Bye</reading> ばい)." You try to hand her some money and she stares into your eyes. "Bye." Oh... okay, bye then.
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>sweet</radical> <radical>woman</radical> sits under a <radical>tree</radical> and works as a <kanji>mediator</kanji>. If you're having a fight with someone, bring them to the sweet woman under the tree and she'll mediate for the two of you. Problem solved! Thanks sweet lady!

MeaningHint: Picture the sad, stepped on chicken. It's angrily clucking and chasing your husband around your yard for what he did.
ReadingHint: This cake was for your husband, but now he gets nothing. The chicken gets to eat all of the cake in front of him instead.
ReadingMnemonic: The only way to stop the <kanji>chicken</kanji> from putting on your husband's cleats and stepping on him to death in revenge is to feed it the <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい) you were making.
MeaningMnemonic: Your <radical>husband</radical> was wearing his <radical>cleat</radical>s when he accidentally stepped on a <radical>bird</radical>. Luckily this particular bird is a <kanji>chicken</kanji>, and chickens are really durable!

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to request a flag from a scary mantis that has it sitting in it's mouth. Will it give it to you? Will it bite you? You'll have to wait and see.
ReadingHint: Imagine your shock at finding out the mantis not only understood your request, but agreed! This is great but also makes you wonder what other creatures can understand you...
ReadingMnemonic: After you <kanji>request</kanji> the mantis give you its flag it <reading>shock</reading>s (しょく) you by actually spitting it out of its mouth at your feet. You didn't think your request would work, or even if the mantis understood human speech, but it shocked you by doing exactly what you asked!
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to take a <radical>flag</radical> out of the <radical>mouth</radical> of a <radical>mantis</radical> you have to make a formal <kanji>request</kanji> first. Mantises don't like to just give things away, and they're kind of mean so if you try to steal the flag it will probably bite you. So make a formal, polite request if you want it.

MeaningHint: You have a scooter that you drive every single day, but if you notice a fault, you need to send it in to get it fixed. That's when you break out your alternate scooter.
ReadingHint: Imagine riding on your alternate 鉄 scooter. Feel the hard iron under your bum and the loud sounds it makes as you scoot down the street. I hope your normal scooter is fixed soon.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>alternate</kanji> scooter is made entirely of <reading>鉄</reading> (てつ). It's hard and cold and not very comfortable when you sit on it. That's why you chose to keep it as an alternate and not as your everyday scooter.
MeaningMnemonic: When your <radical>scooter</radical> has a <radical>fault</radical>, you need to get an <kanji>alternate</kanji>. Hopefully your insurance will cover it too.

MeaningHint: Tree turkeys are absolutely huge, and this exceptionally large one has been sitting right on top of your spine for what feels like ages. Imagine the pain of a huge turkey pressing down on your spine for weeks, perhaps months. How are you still holding on?!
ReadingHint: You desperately try to escape from the weight of the tree turkey still sitting on top of your spine, but to no avail. The only thing you can see is dozens of tree turkeys approaching to plant themselves firmly on top of your spine, screaming "TWEET, TWEET".
ReadingMnemonic: Just as your <kanji>spine</kanji> seems to be getting used to the pressure, the tree turkey unleashes a cry: "<reading>TWEE</reading>T (つい)!" To your horror, a flock of tree turkeys emerge from the trees.
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>tree</radical> <radical>turkey</radical> has been sitting directly on your <kanji>spine</kanji> for weeks straight. The pain is starting to become unbearable, and it seems like no one wants to help you.

MeaningHint: I know it's been a long time, but try not to mix this one up with 	部, if you can. Remember, the clown building is in another part of town and the clowns aren't supposed to leave. But the building clown is okay to accompany you through the rest of the city.
ReadingHint: Wave bye to the clown, he'll accompany you on your building job tomorrow, but right now he has to go home and be with the rest of his clown people. He can't accompany you after dark.
ReadingMnemonic: He can only <kanji>accompany</kanji> you for so long though and soon it's time to say <reading>bye</reading> (ばい) again.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>building</radical> <radical>clown</radical> is the only clown allowed to <kanji>accompany</kanji> you. You hate all clowns, and so does the rest of your town. But this clown is really good at building buildings, and he's allowed to leave his part of town and accompany you while you fix buildings together.

MeaningHint: Instead of dying, the clown divides into slightly thinner clowns.
ReadingHint: The many clowns crash to the floor as the bowling ball plows through their... his(?) divided bodies. You can't bear to look anymore.
ReadingMnemonic: The clown finally stops trying to <kanji>divide</kanji> himself, and you can't stand to look at all of him. You grab a <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and bowl all of his divided parts over!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>clown</radical> takes a <radical>knife</radical> and <kanji>divide</kanji>s himself. First he divides himself into two pieces, then he's in four pieces, then... oh why won't he stop?!

MeaningHint: Say the line up of your family under the sun, then sing some sunny chords to match. You'll know your genealogy and a musical score!
ReadingHint: Your family genealogy and a musical score aren't even close to being the same thing. Memorize these or everyone will think you're a fool.
ReadingMnemonic: If you can't tell the difference between your <kanji>genealogy</kanji> and a musical <kanji>score</kanji>, you're a <reading>foo</reading>l (ふ) and I can't help you. You aren't a fool, are you? You know the difference.
MeaningMnemonic:
If you <radical>say</radical> the <radical>lineup</radical> of your family members that have lived on this earth under the <radical>sun</radical>, you're really explaining your <kanji>genealogy</kanji>.

This kanji has a second meaning too. It means score, as in a musical score or sheet music. So if your sheet music <radical>say</radical>s the <radical>line up</radical> of chords, especially <radical>sun</radical>nny notes, that's the <kanji>score</kanji> of a song. Those sunny notes are loved by your ancestors.

MeaningHint: Imagine being graceful enough to balance on a small stool above a raging tsunami! Sooo cool!
ReadingHint: It doesn't matter how badly the stool shook, you stayed graceful and didn't fall!
ReadingMnemonic: No matter how <kanji>graceful</kanji> you are, the tsunami still <reading>shook</reading> (しゅく) the small stool. That's where your gracefulness came in. The stool shook, but you didn't. You balanced yourself and refused to go down.
MeaningMnemonic: You can only manage to avoid a <radical>tsunami</radical> by standing <radical>above</radical> it on a <radical>small</radical> <radical>stool</radical> if you are very <kanji>graceful</kanji>. If you aren't graceful enough the tsunami will knock you and the stool over and you'll be swept away like everyone else.

MeaningHint: Imagine sailing out into the open sea, drops of water hitting your face, as the towel sail catches the air and carries you away.
ReadingHint: You sail nervously past Han Solo who also seems to be sailing, though in worse shape than you. But he keeps throwing you finger guns so you figure it's best to just sail on and pretend this never happened.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>sail</kanji> off into the ocean you hear a familiar voice shout out, "Ahoy!" <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo calls. He's also got a towel sail, but it's on the Millennium Falcon, which is half-submerged in the water.
MeaningMnemonic: You hoist your <radical>towel</radical> above your <radical>table</radical>, <radical>drop</radical> into the water and <kanji>sail</kanji> away. You turned the towel into an actual sail and you're balancing on top of the table, which is now a boat.

MeaningHint: Picture the soul on the cross, flowers all around it and shellfish at its feet. Feel your resentment grow.
ReadingHint:
You wanna have fun hanging out on a cross with flowers and all the shellfish you can eat. Your resentment grows and grows as you watch the soul having everything you suddenly want.

Fun fact: when you see 賁 in a kanji, the reading is probably going to be ふん.
ReadingMnemonic: You realize you <kanji>resent</kanji> them because it looks like they're having <reading>fun</reading> (ふん). You aren't having fun, and you wanna be! How dare they have fun when you aren't!
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>soul</radical> on a <radical>cross</radical> covered in <radical>flowers</radical> with <radical>shellfish</radical> at its feet and you suddenly <kanji>resent</kanji> them. It's not like you want to be the one up there on the cross, but so many people left flowers and shellfish for them. It should have been you...

MeaningHint: Imagine serving all of your guests their alcohol by dumping it all in the pool! Now jump in and drink all you want!
ReadingHint: Picture Shaq serving your guests by tossing them into your alcohol filled pool. Thanks, Shaq... I guess.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>serve</kanji> your guests (and yourself) you look over and see <reading>Shaq</reading> (しゃく) also trying to serve them. And by serving I mean getting served because he's throwing them into the pool.
MeaningMnemonic: Pour all of your <radical>alcohol</radical> in the <radical>pool</radical> to <kanji>serve</kanji> all your guests at once! They can all help themselves to as much or as little alcohol as they want! What a great idea!

MeaningHint: Imagine being a gambler and seeing the bright dawn sun when you come out of the casino or pachinko parlor. It burns your eyes! Evil dawn, get back inside quick!
ReadingHint: Imagine getting up at dawn and seeing the あかつき in the sky near the sun. It's so beautiful!
ReadingMnemonic: If you're up at <kanji>dawn</kanji> you'll also see the <reading>あか</reading>(red) <reading>つき</reading>(moon). The あかつき only happens as the sun comes up at dawn so usually only gamblers see it. But if you get up early, you can see it too!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Gambler</radical>s usually only see the <radical>sun</radical> at <kanji>dawn</kanji>. They gamble all day and through the night, and if they leave it's at the crack of dawn to get home, clean up, and then go back. I haven't known a gambler who's seen the sun ... well ever!

MeaningHint: "Look upon your leader's greatness!" he shouts, doing a jig around on the branches.
ReadingHint: "Oh leader, your greatness is so... great!" you shout up at him. He's holding the ketsup bottle over your head and staring into your eyes as he dances in the tree. He isn't sure if he wants to dump it on you yet. He isn't sure if you really recognize his excellence.
ReadingMnemonic: If you don't recognize the leader's <kanji>greatness</kanji> he will throw <reading>ketsu</reading>p (けつ) all over you (it's a special mix of ketchup and catsup). And it's ketsup because it looks like blood. The blood he'll spill if you don't look up and clap at his great dancing.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> does a <radical>dance</radical> on top of the <radical>tree</radical> to show his <kanji>greatness</kanji> to everyone around. If he dances all the way up there on top of the tree, then no one can deny his <kanji>excellence</kanji>!

MeaningHint: It's only natural that you'd want to put a lock on a gold roof so that nobody could steal it.
ReadingHint: How did Joe, a simple farmhand, get hold of such a valuable gold roof? Either way, the lock he’s put on it is incredibly tough, so there’s no way you could make off with his gold roof anyway.
ReadingMnemonic: The person who put the <kanji>lock</kanji> on the gold roof was none other than... <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう)?!
MeaningMnemonic: This is a <radical>gold</radical> <radical>roof</radical>, <radical>correct</radical>? Better put a <kanji>lock</kanji> on it, then.

MeaningHint: Imagine having to endure the pain of frostbite because you were a jerk who put his hand on ice for a few hours.
ReadingHint: Imagine if you were one of these cool Shinobi ninjas that could endure the extreme cold and pain of frostbite!
ReadingMnemonic: The best people in the world at being able to <kanji>endure</kanji> the pain are the <reading>shino</reading>bi (しの) ninjas! They train by hanging out in frozen areas all the time and don't complain at all. Maybe if the pain gets to be too much you can go ask them for advice.
MeaningMnemonic: If you touch <radical>ice</radical> for too long you'll get <radical>frostbite</radical> and that's a really hard thing to <kanji>endure</kanji>. A lot of people can't, and die. Then again, you managed to endure touching ice long enough to get frostbite in the first place, so maybe this is baby stuff for you.

MeaningHint: Imagine saying "Congratulations!" to a king who just ordered his servants to rake down all the pretty mountains in his kingdom.
ReadingHint: Imagine how hollow your congratulation sounds when you really miss those mountains. This king doesn't deserve your nice words.
ReadingMnemonic: The moment you say "<kanji>Congratulations</kanji>!" you've already started to <reading>miss</reading> (みず) those beautiful mountains. But you can't let missing them ruin your congratulations! Don't let the king know!
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> <radical>rake</radical>d all the <radical>mountain</radical>s down into flat land so he could see everyone in the kingdom from his castle windows. You should share your <kanji>congratulations</kanji> with him. Now, quick tell him this was a great idea.

MeaningHint: Imagine a king who won't accept a rocket that isn't made of glassy material. He's not very smart...
ReadingHint: Picture the king admiring himself in the glassy surface and then his face contorts. He smashes his fist through the rocket and rips out the reeds. "REEDS AREN'T GLASSY!" he cries, and starts hitting you with them.
ReadingMnemonic: You take the <kanji>glassy</kanji> rocket and try to stuff some thin <reading>reed</reading>s (り) inside to support it. But because it's clear and glassy the king sees them at the last second.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>king</radical> wants you to make a <radical>rocket</radical> that looks <kanji>glassy</kanji>. It won't be very stable, and it probably won't work, but making it glassy and pretty is the only way to make the king approve.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself all alone. You never let anyone into your heart but yourself, and now you're mourning the life and friends you could have had.
ReadingHint: You try to push the key back into your body. Back into your eye, you even try to swallow it, but it comes back out. Finally you look down at your mourning heart and try the key there. It clicks. And opens.
ReadingMnemonic: While you're in <kanji>mourning</kanji>, you feel a sharp pain in your crying eyes. A <reading>key</reading> (き) comes sliding out with your tears. You look around wildly, but you're still alone. Is this... the key of mourning?
MeaningMnemonic: If you only let <radical>oneself</radical> (yourself) into your <radical>heart</radical>, you'll end up <kanji>mourning</kanji> all of the loves and friendships you could have had but didn't let in.

MeaningHint: It's an upward transition, so that's nice, but it's still going to take you a long time to get used to using a big helicopter instead of a scooter.
ReadingHint: A centaur is just a human halfway between transitioning into a horse, so it isn't even that hard to do!
ReadingMnemonic: You decide one <kanji>transition</kanji> isn't enough, so you transition to a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん) too. Now you're a big half horse-half human creature and you'll fit in the big helicopter better.
MeaningMnemonic: Going from a <radical>scooter</radical> to a <radical>helicopter</radical> that's <radical>big</radical> even for yourself (<radical>oneself</radical>), is a huge <kanji>transition</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Of course, you're too clumsy to do that right either, you accidentally threw the door backwards and hurt some people...
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to use your clumsy fingers all over the sets of doors. You keep falling over and hurting yourself. Why is it so hard to leave?!
ReadingMnemonic: After you make your <kanji>clumsy</kanji> mistake you realize that these doors come in <reading>sets</reading> (せつ). And now you have to try to open another door. And another door. So many sets of doors!
MeaningMnemonic: You have to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to make an <radical>exit</radical> because you're so <kanji>clumsy</kanji>. You can't seem to get the door to open, your hands just slip around on the knob uselessly. Instead, you take your fingers and stick them into the door frame, pulling the whole thing off the hinges so you can leave.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself walking along a mountain pass. Look up at the mountains above you, and down at the mountains below you.
ReadingHint: Picture yourself hiking along a mountain pass in Tokyo and spotting a little Tokyo gecko run up and down on them. It's adorable and totally worth the climb.
ReadingMnemonic: It's only on this type of <kanji>mountain pass</kanji> that you'll be able to find the rare and adorable <reading>To</reading>kyo <reading>ge</reading>cko (とうげ). These tiny little critters can only be found on the ridges going above and below the mountains in Tokyo. There are like six mountains in Tokyo, so the next time you're scaling the ridges keep your eyes open for the Tokyo gecko!
MeaningMnemonic: If there are <radical>mountain</radical>s <radical>above</radical> and <radical>below</radical> you, that means you're on a <kanji>mountain pass</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Someone sat down and deliberately planned the entire horse out, collected the materials, and made it by hand.
ReadingHint: Tokugawa Ieyasu then poofs out of existence and so does the bamboo horse.
ReadingMnemonic: As you admire all the <kanji>deliberate</kanji> details in this bamboo horse, the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you! "I deliberately made this horse to show you how important being deliberate is. You should be absolutely certain and decisive about your decisions."
MeaningMnemonic: If someone makes a <radical>bamboo</radical> <radical>horse</radical>, you know it's <kanji>deliberate</kanji>. You can't accidentally make an entire horse out of bamboo.

MeaningHint: Picture the female turkey stopping when it sees you holding out a spoon. Why are you doing that human? it thinks. Are you sick?
ReadingHint: Imagine watching a female turkey make a mess of her own cage because she thinks you need help. How sweet.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>female</kanji> turkey then runs around making a <reading>mess</reading> (めす) of things trying to figure out what's wrong with you. She kicks around her hay, water bowl, and scratches at your pants, making more mess marks on them. But you don't move and she gets even more worried.
MeaningMnemonic: If you can <radical>stop</radical> a <radical>turkey</radical> by putting a <radical>spoon</radical> in its face you'll know it's a <kanji>female</kanji> turkey. A male turkey will peck the spoon out of your hand and run away. But a female turkey will wonder why you're putting a spoon there, stop, and look up at you wondering if you're okay.

MeaningHint: Imagine a little crab getting stuck in a crab trap with dirt and other crabs. Endure the horror as long as you can, little friend.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself putting a collar around the crab's neck, then adding a leash and taking your crab for a walk around your neighborhood.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to make the little crab that <kanji>endure</kanji>d being stuck in a dirty crab trap your pet, and put a <reading>colla</reading>r (こら) round its little neck.
MeaningMnemonic: If there is <radical>dirt</radical> in a <radical>crab trap</radical>, it's a lot for the crabs to <kanji>endure</kanji>.

MeaningHint: The recital is happening now. So open your mouth and start already!
ReadingHint: Drink gin before all your recitals! It'll help!
ReadingMnemonic: Here, if you are too afraid to start the <kanji>recital</kanji> now drink this <reading>gin</reading> (ぎん) to get your courage up. But once you're done drinking it the recital starts!
MeaningMnemonic: We want you to say things with your <radical>mouth</radical> right <radical>now</radical> because it's <kanji>recital</kanji> time! Go, perform please. What do you mean you didn't know the recital was right now? Well, tough luck. Recite!

MeaningHint: How hard is it to come up with a good mnemonic for a crab trap? Very hard!
ReadingHint: How many sentences can you make with very and jeans? More than me I hope.
ReadingMnemonic: You know what else is <kanji>very</kanji> something? <reading>Jean</reading>s (じん). Jeans are very expensive. Jeans are very tight. Jeans are very popular. Jeans are very a lot of things.
MeaningMnemonic: Making a mnemonic for a <radical>crab trap</radical> is <kanji>VERY</kanji> hard. So hard that I couldn't come up with one. But it's very possible that one of you will!

MeaningHint: Man your own jackhammer! (And worship the gods.)
ReadingHint: You try to argue that you can't sue someone for the way they choose to worship, but they say they're all deaf now, all the animals have gone, and no one sleeps anymore because of the shaking. Yikes, they might have a case!
ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, the people who live around here can also hear you and the others <kanji>worship</kanji> in the mountain and they're going to <reading>sue</reading> (すう) you for the noise.
MeaningMnemonic: Under the <radical>mountain</radical>'s <radical>roof</radical> are people using <radical>jackhammer</radical>s to <kanji>worship</kanji> the gods. The noise from their jackhammering echos up through the mountain and up toward the sky where the gods live. As long as each person uses their own jackhammer, the sound will be loud enough to reach them.

MeaningHint: Picture your hat and all of the other things the tsunami + tree water touched. It's all been hardened with a thick coating of the lacquer.
ReadingHint: Watch as your shih tzu tries to bite your now lacquered chair. It gets its teeth around the leg, but it's hard as a rock. The shih tzu starts sneezing and spitting, letting go. Looks like it tastes bad too. Perfect!
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to scoop up the rest of the <kanji>lacquer</kanji> water and use it on your furniture to protect it from your <reading>shih tzu</reading> (しつ). Now it can't bite or scratch or ruin any of your things anymore!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>tsunami</radical> hits a <radical>tree</radical> and your <radical>hat</radical> protects you from the <radical>water</radical>, but the hat itself is now covered in <kanji>lacquer</kanji>. You take it off and see that it's shiny and hard and protected.

MeaningHint: Picture the turtle shell shaped cape. Feel the salty wind in your hair!
ReadingHint: The next time you go to the cape, remember to bring your old, used meat sack. You can toss it right into the ocean and it won't bother you ever again! Just make sure no one sees you.
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to walk along this <kanji>cape</kanji> because it looks like the perfect place to get rid of your old <reading>me</reading>at <reading>sack</reading> (みさき). It's starting to smell, but the ocean breeze from the cape pulls the meat sack out of your hands and off into the sky (then probably into the sea too).
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>mountain</radical> in the shape of a <radical>turtle shell</radical> sticking out into the ocean. It's a <kanji>cape</kanji>! No, not the kind you wear, the geographical kind. When you're on land, most capes look like turtle shell mountains sticking out into the sea. Do you know any capes like that near where you live?

MeaningHint: Imagine spinning thread into whatever your imagination wants.
ReadingHint: Picture all of these thread-made bowling items. You're a really big bowling fan, huh?
ReadingMnemonic: While you're <kanji>spinning</kanji> you realize that you've always wanted to make your own <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball. And bowling ball cover. And bowling ball shoes.
MeaningMnemonic: Move <radical>thread</radical> in the right <radical>direction</radical> and you'll end up <kanji>spinning</kanji> it into yarn. Then you can spin that yarn into a sweater or a scarf with your favorite half tofu half fugu mascot on it!

MeaningHint: It may seem like there are a bunch of turkeys under there, but it's just the shape the reef takes. Though I guess if you're looking from above the water it really does look like a bunch of turkeys freaking out.
ReadingHint: You don't know if the Shougun likes the reef because he likes the beauty of the ocean, or if he's fantasizing about turkeys boiling. He's a... complex man.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>reef</kanji> is the <reading>Shou</reading>gun's (しょう) favorite place to be nowadays. He sits next to the water, and stares down into it for hours.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>stone</radical> that looks like a <radical>turkey</radical> <radical>boil</radical>ing underwater is just a <kanji>reef</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Know your radicals, yo!
ReadingHint: Imagine trying to lift some barracks. They weigh a ton! How are you supposed to do this? Why would you even try to lift something that obviously weighs a couple tons?!
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Barracks</kanji> are pretty big, so they weigh a <reading>ton</reading> (とん). And they probably take tons of materials to build.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>barracks</radical> radical and the <kanji>barracks</kanji> kanji are the same!

MeaningHint: When you think about the person you want to marry, picture their mouth wide in a big, beautiful smile, a wide-open laugh. That's the lady for you, alright!
ReadingHint: When you get to the inn all you do is make her laugh and smile so you can bask in her bright, big, mouth smiles.
ReadingMnemonic: You <kanji>marry</kanji> this woman right away and you both go straight to an <reading>inn</reading> (いん) together!
MeaningMnemonic: You meet a <radical>woman</radical> whose <radical>mouth</radical> is <radical>big</radical> and you instantly know this is the person you want to <kanji>marry</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture your angry friend standing up next to your fingers, shouting, "I'm the real me! That's just an imitation!" The rest of your friends look nervously at each other. They doubt this person is real now.
ReadingHint: Okay, so that's a lie, Your fingers are definitely imitating them, but you don't want to end up in the guillotine so you keep your mouth shut.
ReadingMnemonic: Everyone thinks the friend you were trying to <kanji>imitate</kanji> is the imitation now and they have them in a <reading>gui</reading>llotine (ぎ). You even doubt if they're an imitation or if your fingers are the imitation.
MeaningMnemonic: You try to use your <radical>fingers</radical> to cast <radical>doubt</radical> on people by using them to <kanji>imitate</kanji> each other. Right now you're using your fingers as finger puppets, imitating one of your friends to the others.

MeaningHint: Imagine having someone you aren't friendly with trying to have a look at your womb. No thank you.
ReadingHint: Imagine only letting this guy start to introduce himself with ぼく before walking the heck outta there. Just because he was acting friendly doesn't mean he was actually your friend.
ReadingMnemonic: This <kanji>friendly</kanji> guy comes up to you and start to introduce himself, "<reading>ぼく</reading>-" but you stop him. He's been getting way too friendly with your womb and you don't want to hear who he is. He can ぼく at someone else, you're out.
MeaningMnemonic: If you see someone <radical>eye</radical>ing your <radical>womb</radical> you're probably going to be weirded out by how <kanji>friendly</kanji> they're acting. You only let people who are really, really close to you look at your womb. So if this guy is acting that friendly he should have introduced himself first.

MeaningHint: Imagine spending your leisure time curled up under a tree that's growing through a gate. It's so cozy and secluded!
ReadingHint: But wait. Isn't Genghis Khan dead now? He winks at you, "Leisure time is for the dead." He smiles, and fades away, disappearing into the tree.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>leisure</kanji>ly nap, you hear a gruff voice, "Ah, you're done too, huh?" It's Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん)! He's spending his leisure time relaxing under this abandoned gate just like you.
MeaningMnemonic: You see a <radical>gate</radical> with a <radical>tree</radical> growing through it and it reminds you that you need more <kanji>leisure</kanji> time. You walk up to the gate tree and curl up under its branches. No one will bother you because the tree is blocking the gate from being used.

MeaningHint: Think of all that music you listened to underground but not in the light of the sun. None of that was official music. None of it!
ReadingHint: The official music penetrates into your soul, instead of just your ears, and it's affecting everyone else's souls too! Oh no... wait that's too many people. This was your song! No, it's too popular now, stop your souls from liking it! Ahhh!
ReadingMnemonic: Once the music is <kanji>official</kanji>, you'll feel it in your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). When you listen to it outside, under the bright sun, your soul gets to finally hear it too.
MeaningMnemonic: When under<radical>ground</radical> <radical>music</radical> makes it out into the light of the <radical>sun</radical> (the public), it finally becomes <kanji>official</kanji>. Music isn't really real unless people hear it outside of those secret underground concerts.

MeaningHint: Try to compose a poem about eternity right now. Just jot it down somewhere.
ReadingHint:
Let me recite the acorn poem to you now:

Acorns start small
Grow up big and tall
Spread their baby acorns for all
Live on for all eternity
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to <kanji>compose</kanji> a poem about an <reading>a</reading>corn (えい). The tiny acorn is the perfect vessel for you to say how eternity feels to you.
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>say</radical> how <radical>eternity</radical> sounds you have to <kanji>compose</kanji> a poem. Or an essay. Or a song. <kanji>Recite</kanji> those deep thoughts about eternity and impress everyone who hears it.

MeaningHint: Imagine dropping down in a rice paddy, then again in front of your cross. Be as lowly as you can make yourself.
ReadingHint: You tried to make yourself lowly in front of God but this heat is probably a sign that you didn't do it well enough.
ReadingMnemonic: The only problem about being <kanji>lowly</kanji> is the <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). Everyone always told you that heat rises, but not here. The rice paddy is incredibly hot... unless oh no. Is that the heat of hell?
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>drop</radical> to the ground in a <radical>rice paddy</radical>, and then <radical>drop</radical> down in front of the <radical>cross</radical> you keep on you because you're trying to make yourself as <kanji>lowly</kanji> as you can in front of God. He said to lower yourself and this is the only way you can interpret that.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself as a leader who's come to despise every single one of their followers. Look down at them in disgust as they ask you to grant them all a pony.
ReadingHint: This is not a healthy way to handle things! If you despise someone you should talk to them, don't turn to booze. Don't be like every leader that's come before you.
ReadingMnemonic: The more you <kanji>despise</kanji> them, the more <reading>boo</reading>ze (ぶ) you drink. This is not smart or healthy, but it's what this kind of hate does to people.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Every</radical> <radical>leader</radical> will come to <kanji>despise</kanji> their followers. In the beginning everything is fine and dandy, but eventually the leader's followers will ask for more and more things, eventually ridiculous things, and the leader will really despise them for it.

MeaningHint: Imagine yourself carefully pouring the gold down each of your three slides. Really be careful — the measurements need to be exact, or the cast won't come out right.
ReadingHint: Picture your majestic Chewbacca cast. You place it outside your front door to guard the entrance to your house. Wraaaaaaarrrrrrr!
ReadingMnemonic: At last, you reveal what you've <kanji>cast</kanji>: a gold statue of <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう). It's a life-size cast, which is why you needed so much gold and such exact measurements.
MeaningMnemonic: You pour molten <radical>gold</radical> down <radical>three</radical> <radical>slide</radical>s while taking <radical>measurement</radical>s in order to make a <kanji>cast</kanji> of something. Whatever it is you're casting, it requires a lot of gold (three slides' worth!) and some very precise measurements.

MeaningHint: Imagine the guts you'd need to have to stay up so late that you see the moon and the sun switch places! And to risk gallbladder damage?! That takes guts!
ReadingHint: Imagine the deep tan you'd get after staying awake outside for such a long time. Tanning is also bad for your gallbladder! Man, you've really got guts to be getting tans like that.
ReadingMnemonic: You stayed awake outside for so long, just to prove you had <kanji>guts</kanji> and powerful <kanji>gallbladder</kanji>, that you also got a sweet <reading>tan</reading> (たん) out of it!
MeaningMnemonic: If you stay up so long that you see the <radical>moon</radical> go down and the <radical>dawn</radical> come, people will say you have serious <kanji>guts</kanji>. It takes guts to stay up that late. More specifically, it takes a powerful <kanji>gallbladder</kanji>. Your gallbladder is sensitive to sleep changes, you know, so you risk a lot by staying up like that.

MeaningHint: You wander everywhere, with no particular direction in mind.
ReadingHint: Wherever the good tsunami goes, it leaves delicious fish roe behind. That's what makes it a good tsunami.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>wander</kanji>, you come across a clue. It is fish <reading>roe</reading> (ろう).
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> that is <radical>good</radical>. This fabled creature has never been seen, but you <kanji>wander</kanji> the lands in search of it.

MeaningHint: Look at the evil spirit with two mustaches on its spirit face. Imagine its face in detail, especially those mustaches.
ReadingHint: Imagine running around, flailing your arms, trying to get the spirit to stop ruining your car. But your hands go right through him and his two mustaches. You can't stop his evil doings.
ReadingMnemonic: You're watching the <kanji>evil</kanji> mustache spirit when it starts to float toward your <reading>ca</reading>r (か). And it's... hey! It's trashing your car! You knew this guy was evil!
MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>spirit</radical> wearing two <radical>mustache</radical>s must be <kanji>evil</kanji>. Everyone knows a mustache makes you evil, but two? And on a spirit? Yup, definitely evil.

MeaningHint: Imagine trying to give more than a kiss of alcohol to dairy and watch it bubble and curdle and get all gross and chunky.
ReadingHint: Look at all your dairy products: milk, yogurt, cheese, all of it sitting nicely on your dairy rack.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Dairy</kanji> isn't kept with other drinks, it needs to be placed on a <reading>rack</reading> (らく). I hope you've been using your dairy rack to store your dairy. If you haven't you should get one right away!
MeaningMnemonic: You can only give a <radical>kiss</radical> of <radical>alcohol</radical> to <kanji>dairy</kanji>. If you add anymore than a kiss the dairy will curdle and you won't be able to drink it anymore. Bleh.

MeaningHint: Can you imagine how difficult landing a helicopter is in just one of these horrible conditions? Congratulate them!
ReadingHint: Cake is always the appropriate celebratory food.
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Congratulate</kanji> the pilot with <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)!
MeaningMnemonic: If a pilot manages to land under a <radical>canopy</radical> with a <radical>helicopter</radical> that's pierced with a sideways <radical>barb</radical> in the <radical>heart</radical> of <radical>winter</radical> you should <kanji>congratulate</kanji> them! People should always be congratulated for doing good things, and this is one of the best things you've ever heard of!

MeaningHint: 亜 is associated with Asia because of its あ reading, but sometimes you might see it used as ateji for other place names as well. And don't forget that sub- meaning!
ReadingHint: A is for Asia!
ReadingMnemonic: What's the first sound in <kanji>Asia</kanji>? It's <reading>A</reading> (あ). Nice and easy!
MeaningMnemonic:
The kanji and the radical that look like this are exactly the same. They are both <kanji>Asia</kanji>. 

This can also mean <kanji>sub-</kanji>. As in subtropics, subspecies, subhuman… That kind of sub-! When it's not in a place name, 亜 will usually be using its sub- meaning.

MeaningHint: Imagine being at the tsunami store and selecting the least fat one there.
ReadingHint: While you select the tsunami that's right for you. Keep an eye out for those fat taco-eating ones. And then stay away from them!
ReadingMnemonic: If you're having trouble <kanji>select</kanji>ing which one is the least fat just check to see which tsunamis are eating <reading>ta</reading>cos (た). Only fat tsunamis will be eating them, so just look for one without a taco.
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>tsunami</radical> that's heading for your village is <radical>fat</radical> you should try to <kanji>select</kanji> a different one to hit instead. You don't want to select fat tsunamis because they do more damage.

MeaningHint: Imagine licking the trunk of a Japanese birch tree and feeling the heat on your tongue.
ReadingHint: Imagine using a zoo saw to cut down a Japanese birch tree. What's a zoo saw? It's a saw that's used in zoos, to build all the animal enclosures. And to cut down spicy trees.
ReadingMnemonic: You can only cut down a <kanji>Japanese birch</kanji> using <reading>a zoo saw</reading> (あずさ) because a regular saw can't handle how spicy these trees are.
MeaningMnemonic: What kind of <radical>tree</radical> is <radical>spicy</radical>? A <kanji>Japanese birch</kanji>, or <kanji>birch</kanji>, obviously. There's nothing spicier than a Japanese birch!

MeaningHint: Imagine how those few grains of sand feel to get swept up into a tsunami. They didn't ask for this. They just wanted to stay at the bottom of the sea.
ReadingHint: As long as you remember 砂, you can remember さ.
ReadingMnemonic: You already learnt the reading for 砂, the other kanji for <kanji>sand</kanji>. It should be easy to remember this one too, since it's also さ!
MeaningMnemonic: People think <radical>tsunami</radical>s are only made of water but they have a <radical>few</radical> pieces of <kanji>sand</kanji> in there too. There's actually more sand in the ocean than most people think. And tsunamis can't just have water and sharks in them. A few grains of sand will always slip in.

MeaningHint: By writing up new articles and laws they keep taking things away from samurai. First the katana, now jackhammers?
ReadingHint: Genghis Khan knew that samurai were a threat to his kingdom, so he took away their secondary weapon, the jackhammer.
ReadingMnemonic: The one who wrote these <kanji>article</kanji>s with such <kanji>sincerity</kanji> was Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん).
MeaningMnemonic: "For the  <radical>samurai</radical>, it is a <radical>jackhammer</radical> that they will <radical>lack</radical>." That is the latest <kanji>article</kanji> of the law written about samurai, hundreds of years after katana were taken away. This article was written with great <kanji>sincerity</kanji> by the government.

MeaningHint: Picture someone else riding a scooter with an axe in their fingertips. That's so dangerous! One bump, one bad turn, and that axe slips and they'll die.
ReadingHint: Your holy blue saber died for you. It saved you from certain death and now it died. After all you've been through together. All 60 levels. This is the end.
ReadingMnemonic:
You slip. The axe collides with your face. You <kanji>die</kanji>. But... wait! There's a bright, blue glow and suddenly you're un-dead. You watch in slow motion as your holy blue <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) brings you back to life and takes the hit from the axe instead.

Wait, no!

It shatters.
MeaningMnemonic: If you ride a <radical>scooter</radical> with your <radical>fingers</radical> around an <radical>axe</radical> you're going to <kanji>die</kanji>. I mean, probably. There's a 99% chance you'll die.

MeaningHint: You don't really want to be in this cage, but there were some things you didn't want to do today, so you hid. Luckily these flowers block the right side of the cage and hide you completely.
ReadingHint: "N-no?" you respond. "Wrong! I hid all the time. There's nothing wrong with hiding, you're doing a great job." he says, and disappears.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>hide</kanji> the ghost of <reading>Toku</reading>gawa (とく) Ieyasu appears before you, inside the cage! His arms are crossed and he's shaking his head at you. "Do you think I managed to become the first shougun of Japan by hiding?"
MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>cage</radical> is full of <radical>flowers</radical> on the <radical>right</radical> side to <kanji>hide</kanji> the fact that you're in here too.

MeaningHint: Imagine the geoduck laughing and screaming, "Now you're a widow! Now you're a widow!" flailing the sword around. It's awful.
ReadingHint: It's just you and your car now, living the widow life, driving on into the sunset and forgetting your past.
ReadingMnemonic: You may be a <kanji>widow</kanji>, but that doesn't make you weak. You run outside, jump into your <reading>ca</reading>r (か), and run your entire house and the geoduck over.
MeaningMnemonic: Under your own <radical>roof</radical>, a <radical>geoduck</radical> jumps out of the <radical>ground</radical> with a <radical>sword</radical> and stabs your husband/wife/marriage partner to death. Now you're a <kanji>widow</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine back to the last time you ate something bad and it gave you diarrhea. It felt like someone was sticking you with a knife, didn't it?
ReadingHint: You learned this reed technique from a group of ninjas who use them to breathe underwater, and you never thought you'd use them like this.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>diarrhea</kanji> is here. It's happening. And it smells so awful you can't breathe. You have to take some hollow <reading>reed</reading>s (り) and shove them up your nose and out the bathroom door so you can get some fresh air in you.
MeaningMnemonic: You ate some <radical>grain</radical>s that made you so <radical>sick</radical> it feels like a <radical>knife</radical> is stabbing in your guts and all your poop is falling out. This is called <kanji>diarrhea</kanji>. Everyone's had diarrhea before, so you should know this feeling by now.

MeaningHint: You pull away the lid and crawl inside. There is a ladder that takes you deep into the pit.
ReadingHint: After you've nursed こういち back to health, he explains that he was put here by a clone こういち–the very same こういち who became emperor. He tells you he can think of around 61-70 different pain levels of revenge, but also that "it probably wouldn't be worth it, so let's just hang out for a while instead. Ooh, are those cookies?"
ReadingMnemonic: When you reach the bottom of the <kanji>pit</kanji>, you hear someone scuffling around and the sounds of chains. You light a match and inch closer to see what it is. In the darkness, you see <reading>こう</reading>いち, starved and chained in a pit under your table.
MeaningMnemonic: In the <radical>dirt</radical>, you find a <radical>lid</radical> under the <radical>table</radical>. You lift it to reveal a deep <kanji>pit</kanji>. Why is there a <kanji>hole</kanji> under your table?

MeaningHint: Imagine if every flower you were supposed to oversee was indigo. Indigo everywhere!
ReadingHint: Can you imagine having indigo-only seeing eyes? I can. Because of this awesome mnemonic.
ReadingMnemonic: Wait, maybe these flowers are only <kanji>indigo</kanji> because there is something wrong with your <reading>eye</reading>s (あい)! Maybe your eyes are indigo and that's why it's all you see!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Flowers</radical> that you <radical>oversee</radical> are always <kanji>indigo</kanji>. You aren't sure why though...

MeaningHint: Picture a rice paddy. Half of it is trailing off into the shore. Hear the seagulls. Feel the ocean breeze!
ReadingHint: Imagine lying on the shore with Han Solo. Enjoying a cool drink. Smelling the fragrance of the rice paddy behind you. Yeah, this is the life!
ReadingMnemonic: You decide to walk along this <kanji>shore</kanji> when you see <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo lounging in the sand. He's waving for you to join him! You get to lie on the shore with Han!!
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Half</radical> of a <radical>rice paddy</radical> is always on the <kanji>shore</kanji>. Japan is surrounded by water, after all. So it only makes sense that half of every rice paddy is right next to the ocean shore, right?

MeaningHint: Imagine using your mouth to sing and a shell to play the shamisen to perform your shamisen song!
ReadingHint: A shamisen song is a song, yay!
ReadingMnemonic: A <kanji>shamisen song</kanji> is just an <reading>歌</reading> (うた) but with a shamisen. They're still songs so they're said the exact same way!
MeaningMnemonic: Use your <radical>mouth</radical> to sing and a <radical>shellfish</radical> to stroke your shamisen, and you'll be performing a <kanji>shamisen song</kanji> just like that! Yes, it's that easy. Just go find a shell, open your mouth, and you'll be singing one too in no time!

MeaningHint: First, you put them in a coffin to make them think they're going to die. Then you tickle them with your fingers. There is no greater psychological + physical torture than this.
ReadingHint: He keeps screaming: "STOP THIS TICKLE TORTURE. I CAN'T TAKE IT, JUST BURY ME."
ReadingMnemonic: The beggar you will coffin tickle <kanji>torture</kanji> is none other than old Grandpa <reading>ごう</reading>いち. He is a frail old man, and doesn't like to be tickled.
MeaningMnemonic: You put your <radical>fingers</radical> in the <radical>coffin</radical> to tickle the <radical>beggar</radical> inside. What you're doing is a new form of <kanji>torture</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Watch as the tsunami rakes your husband's cleat across the mountain, carving a valley and an easier path for itself. Nature is brutal.
ReadingHint: Imagine yourself eating cake with your husband in this newly formed valley. You both agree that this should be called Cake Valley!
ReadingMnemonic: When the water clears, you and your husband go to the <kanji>valley</kanji> to look for the cleat and instead you find a bunch of <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)! Maybe this is the tsunami's way of saying, "Thanks for letting me steal your cleat so I could make this valley shortcut."
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>tsunami</radical> swept away one of the <radical>cleat</radical>s your <radical>husband</radical> just bought and creates an entire <kanji>valley</kanji> with it.

MeaningHint: Look really closely at this Mona Lisa. Hmm, I don't remember her having a mustache, but I guess it is a little dark under this canopy.
ReadingHint: No wonder this moustached Mona Lisa is such a bargain. You can really get anything as a rental these days, huh?
ReadingMnemonic: This Mona Lisa is such a <kanji>bargain</kanji> because it's just a <reading>ren</reading>tal (れん)!
MeaningMnemonic: Under this <radical>canopy</radical> is the <radical>Mona Lisa</radical>, being sold for cheap, what a great <kanji>bargain</kanji>!

MeaningHint: Picture yourself trying to be humble. You're down on the ground talking to a pair of boots. You don't dare raise your eyes past that.
ReadingHint: Imagine saying something to the King. Get low down on the ground to be as humble as you can. Whisper into the King's boots. Lick them if you have to.
ReadingMnemonic: You're being <kanji>humble</kanji> like this because those boots belong to the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England. You can't just say whatever you want at eye level to a king. He'll have your head chopped off for insolence, or worse!
MeaningMnemonic: You <radical>say</radical> something to a <radical>boot</radical> when you want to be <kanji>humble</kanji>. You're down there because you're bowing in front of the person you're humbling yourself before. Because of that, your face is really close to their boots and you're speaking directly at them, instead of at the person's face, so it's more <kanji>discreet</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Picture the drunk demon as its pretty face drips off revealing the ugly crap piled underneath.
ReadingHint: The demon drinking alcohol out of its nasty shoes is the ugliest scene you've ever seen.
ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>ugly</kanji> demon is drunk and taking off his <reading>shoe</reading>s (しゅう) and swinging them around his head. And, oh no, he's drinking more alcohol out of the shoes.
MeaningMnemonic: Give <radical>alcohol</radical> to a <radical>demon</radical> and you'll see how <kanji>ugly</kanji> it really is. Demons need to concentrate to keep their disguises going. Even if it looked pretty when you met it, like super handsome, give it a few drinks and its facade will slide off and reveal its super gross demon face.

MeaningHint: Picture twenty slides laid out in a grid system.
ReadingHint: You can make a grid out of anything if you set your mind to it. And you can do math anywhere as long as you have some grid paper!
ReadingMnemonic: As you stare at the slide <kanji>grid</kanji> you see some people come out and overhear them talk about their mass <reading>maths</reading> (ます) tournament happening later today. Oohhh it IS a giant grid. And they're going to use it to do maths later!
MeaningMnemonic: You see <radical>twenty</radical> <radical>slide</radical>s lined up like a <kanji>grid</kanji>. You don't know who put them here, but they almost look like the grid paper you do your maths homework in. But who would use a slide as grid paper?

MeaningHint: Imagine all the yakuza coming out into the streets and holding up signs saying this guy was a martyr. A hero to all who do bad stuff in bad groups but don't wanna get caught.
ReadingHint: Really, it's not the fault of the people who put him there that he's become a martyr. It's the month of June. PUNISH JUNE! PUT JUNE IN THE SUN PRISON! (Is what I assume those yakuza are shouting.)
ReadingMnemonic: The only reason he ended up a <kanji>martyr</kanji> in the first place is because they put him up there in <reading>June</reading> (じゅん). Everyone knows the sun is way too hot for humans in June. It's summer!
MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>yakuza</radical> member in a <radical>prison</radical> on the <radical>sun</radical> and he'll become a <kanji>martyr</kanji> to all the other yakuza still on earth. He died up there so the rest of them wouldn't have to. Because they definitely aren't getting caught now, if the penalty is a burning death in the new sun prison.

MeaningHint: Imagine how annoyed you would be if someone set YOU on fire for no reason. The geoduck has every reason to find you annoying, dude.
ReadingHint: Think how annoying it must be to be a geoduck when Han Solo comes around.
ReadingMnemonic: You know who loves <kanji>annoying</kanji> geoducks more than anyone else in the universe? <reading>Han</reading> (はん) Solo! If you watch Star Wars closely enough you can see him shooting geoducks with his blaster and catching them on fire for fun.
MeaningMnemonic: Set <radical>fire</radical> to a <radical>geoduck</radical> if you want to <kanji>annoy</kanji> it. Fire won't kill a geoduck, but have you ever been on fire? It's not really pleasant. So you'll just annoy it pretty badly.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself as you slice the power out of that senator you hate by censuring them in front of the world!
ReadingHint: Look at that guy now. What a loser.
ReadingMnemonic: Once you <kanji>censure</kanji> them and they lose all their power, they'll be just another <reading>guy</reading> (がい). A normal guy with everything lost to him. HAHAHA!
MeaningMnemonic: If you want to <radical>slice</radical> the <radical>power</radical> out of someone just <kanji>censure</kanji> them. Then they'll be cut down in front of everyone and they'll feel super embarrassed. It's the best way to slice them down without actually cutting them and getting arrested.

MeaningHint: Imagine laying your tree into the water to make your own jetty extending out to the floating bar in the ocean. Innovative!
ReadingHint: Picture Santa-san coming in from the ocean on his speedboat and docking on your jetty so he can go relax in the bar. Imagine the mountain of presents he lays on the jetty for you as thanks.
ReadingMnemonic: You also made this <kanji>jetty</kanji> so it would be easier for <reading>San</reading>ta-san (さん) to dock his sleigh. Oh, you didn't know? His sleigh is more of a speed boat now, and he wants a refreshing drink after delivering all those presents. You get extra presents for making him a jetty, so win-win.
MeaningMnemonic: You grab a <radical>tree</radical> to help you reach the <radical>bar</radical> out in the ocean, using it as a <kanji>jetty</kanji> leading straight to it. Now you can walk out on your makeshift <kanji>pier</kanji> and go get drinks without needing a boat or getting wet.

MeaningHint: Imagine what it would be like to fall in love with a coat rack and make it YOUR groom.
ReadingHint: Try to imagine what this moon coat looks like. Does it glow in the dark like the moon shines at night? Or does it instead have a rugged texture like the moon's surface? Or maybe both?
ReadingMnemonic: Since their wedding happened under the full moon, the <kanji>groom</kanji> wore his special <reading>moon coa</reading>t (むこ) to the ceremony. This is the ideal coat for a groom to wear, since they can keep wearing it all through their honeymoon.
MeaningMnemonic: Have you heard the tale of the <radical>woman</radical> who fell in love with a <radical>coat rack</radical> during a full <radical>moon</radical> and made him her <kanji>groom</kanji>? It's a true story! She even had a wedding ceremony and had more coat racks sitting in the groom's side. The moon will make people do craaaazy things.

MeaningHint: Imagine how it feels to yearn for someone else when you see the small water drop. Imagine how your heart feels.
ReadingHint: Now you yearn for being somewhere Bobo is not.
ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>yearn for</kanji> that person from your past, you look back at the drop and see... <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the clown?! Ugh. Apparently Bobo dropped that small drop of water.
MeaningMnemonic: You're walking in a <radical>greenhouse</radical> when you see a <radical>small</radical> <radical>drop</radical> of water fall and it makes you <kanji>yearn for</kanji> the person you <kanji>adore</kanji>. You haven't seen them in so long. But something about being in this greenhouse and seeing that tiny water droplet made your heart burst with longing.

MeaningHint: Your captors think they have you trapped, but you know you can leave whenever you feel like it.
ReadingHint: As the heat spreads you decide to leave. You hop out of the net and watch the shock spread in your captors eyes.
ReadingMnemonic: You don't know if you want to <kanji>quit</kanji> this charade until you feel an uncomfortable <reading>hea</reading>t (ひ). You look down and the net you're in is being hung above a large fire.
MeaningMnemonic: You're in a <radical>net</radical> but you have the <radical>ability</radical> to <kanji>quit</kanji> playing along and <kanji>leave</kanji> at any time.

MeaningHint: An arrow from heaven will correct all your problems!
ReadingHint: If you want someone to correct your teeth or mustache, just wander around きょうと, staring up at the sky. Then when you see an arrow, run for it and try to catch it with your face!
ReadingMnemonic: These heavenly <kanji>correct</kanji>ing arrows actually come from <reading>きょう</reading>と. They hate seeing people with crooked mustaches, so they shoot them out on the regular. It just looks like they're coming from heaven because they fall out of the sky.
MeaningMnemonic: An <radical>arrow </radical>is shot from <radical>heaven</radical> and hits right between your <radical>mouth</radical> and your <radical>mustache</radical> to <kanji>correct</kanji> your crooked smile. It also <kanji>straighten</kanji>s your teeth and the crooked cut of your mustache too. Who told you that looked good? Well, it doesn't matter. The heaven arrow corrected it for you.

MeaningHint: Which tree do you think is sweet? That one? Or that one? Are you absolutely certain? I hope you are!
ReadingHint: So now let me ask you again, are you certain, or would you like to try the bowling ball test?
ReadingMnemonic: If you really want to be <kanji>certain</kanji>, and not just guess, take out your <reading>bow</reading>ling (ぼう) ball and roll it as hard as you can into the tree. If its the right one it will let out a sweet smell, if it's not the tree will punch you.
MeaningMnemonic: If you think a <radical>tree</radical> looks <radical>sweet</radical> you have to be <kanji>certain</kanji> before you bite into it. Trees are really hard, and usually pretty bitter (believe me) so if you don't want a mouthful of broken teeth, sap, and sadness, you really need to be sure it's the one before you start munching.

MeaningHint: Certain tsunami secretions are dangerous. Can you think of any?
ReadingHint: The next time someone asks you what does a tsunami secrete? You'll say heat!
ReadingMnemonic: Most tsunami's <kanji>secrete</kanji> <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). It takes a lot of energy for a tsunami to crash onto the land so the water is really really hot, and it has to secrete that heat as it's going back out to sea.
MeaningMnemonic: <radical>Tsunami</radical>'s have a <radical>certain</radical> tendency to <kanji>secrete</kanji> things as they go back out to sea. When they bash themselves onto the land they just throw a bunch of sea water around, but there are only certain things that they'll secrete when they're leaving.

MeaningHint: Imagine gradually denting your car door open with an axe while you're trapped in a tsunami. This isn't fast enough!
ReadingHint: YO YANKEES! Gradually yelling that saved your life!
ReadingMnemonic: As you're <kanji>gradually</kanji> making your door open with the axe you look up to see the New York Yankees floating by on top of their bus. You yell, "<reading>YO YA</reading>NKEES (ようや) to get their attention. They see your gradual progress and start throwing balls at your car to help dent it open.
MeaningMnemonic: You're stuck in your <radical>car</radical> during a <radical>tsunami</radical> and all you have to break out is an <radical>axe</radical>. You whack and whack but you're only <kanji>gradually</kanji> making progress. You thought an axe would be an easy way to get out of a car but it's not working like you'd planned! You have to make more than gradual progress if you don't want to drown.

MeaningHint: Seaweed is the flower of the sea!
ReadingHint: Feed your soul with seaweed and it will thank you. Promise! <3
ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Seaweed</kanji> is true <reading>sou</reading>l (そう) food. Which means it's good for your soul. Didn't you ever read those books about chicken soup being good for your soul? Well that was a lie. Miso soup with seaweed is what's really good for your soul.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>flowers</radical> that wash up on shore in <radical>tsunami</radical>s that we make into <radical>syrup</radical> is <kanji>seaweed</kanji>. Okay, so it's more like soup or tea than syrup, but you can make them both out of seaweed.

MeaningHint: Picture yourself watching the news about a woman's death. The word RECKLESS?! pops up on your screen and keeps flashing. Huh, she must have been, you think to yourself.
ReadingHint: Imagine what reckless thing that woman had to have done to die while mowing the lawn. Eww.
ReadingMnemonic: As you watch the news about this <kanji>reckless</kanji> woman you find out that she died from <reading>mow</reading>ing (もう) her lawn. You're not sure how, but now you really believe that she had to be doing it really recklessly to die doing it.
MeaningMnemonic: If the <radical>death</radical> of a <radical>woman</radical> occurs it's because she was being <kanji>reckless</kanji>. People don't just die out of nowhere! They have to have done something super crazy to cause it. Right? Yes... right.

MeaningHint: Again and again, these insect-like barbarians are harassing you and your lands. What shall we do, m'lord?
ReadingHint: Picture yourself as the lord of your lands, screaming "BAN them" at the top of your lungs. You'll finally expel these insect-like barbarians that have been plaguing your lands again and again.
ReadingMnemonic: "<reading>BAN</reading> (ばん) the barbarians! Ban them, I say!," you loudly declare as the lord of these lands.
MeaningMnemonic: If, <radical>again</radical> and again, you behave like an <radical>insect</radical>, you might as well be a <kanji>barbarian</kanji>.

MeaningHint: Imagine the leader squid giving a speech about how you shouldn't buy new things, and how you should live with a roommate to save money. Every squid yen counts!
ReadingHint:
Ken's squid is always shaking his head at Ken the samurai's obsession with wearing all silk. He should visit Thrifty Threads, the second hand shop, and get some cotton clothes that would do the job just fine.

Hint: Whenever there's a squid, remember that Ken (けん) the samurai is nearby.
ReadingMnemonic: Who is a <kanji>thrifty</kanji> squid? <reading>Ken</reading> (けん) the samurai's squid buddy, who gets carried around in a bucket and never pays rent.
MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> <radical>squid</radical> tells all of its squid people to be <kanji>thrifty</kanji>.
